1. A 17-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her father after she had loss of consciousness and collapsed on the sidewalk during an afternoon run. Her father says that his daughter is overweight and has been trying to lose weight for the past 2 months. In addition to modifying her diet, she has been running 2 to 3 miles daily during the past month. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On questioning, her father says that his daughter is a "good kid" but occasionally attends parties, where alcohol is served; he is unsure whether she has used illicit drugs. On arrival, she is somnolent but arousable and responsive. She is oriented to person but not to place or time. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 85 kg (187 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Her temperature is 39°C (102.2°F), pulse is 140/min, respirations are 40/min, and blood pressure is 105/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent. Examination shows cool, clammy, dry skin. The pupils are equal and reactive to light. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Congestive heart failure</br>b. Heat exhaustion</br>c. Illicit drug use</br>d. Sepsis</br>e. Vasovagal syncope</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (provided by user): b. Heat exhaustion  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Setting &amp; activity: “Afternoon run,” “2–3 miles daily,” warm outdoor environment → heat-related illness is on the differential.<br /></br>2. Risk factors: obesity (BMI 30), rapid increase in exercise over 1 month → poorer heat dissipation and higher metabolic heat production.<br /></br>3. Presentation: collapse with transient LOC, persistent hyperthermia (39 °C), tachycardia (140/min), tachypnea (40/min), borderline systolic BP (105 mm Hg) → points to systemic volume depletion and sympathetic surge.<br /></br>4. Skin: “cool, clammy” (i.e., moist from sweating that has begun to evaporate) → patient is still able to sweat; this favors heat exhaustion over heat stroke.<br /></br>5. Neuro status: somnolent but arousable, oriented only to person (mild CNS symptoms) → more than simple vasovagal, but not the severe delirium, seizures, or coma typical of heat stroke.<br /></br>Cognitive approach for test-day:<br /></br>• In any exertional collapse, first separate cardiac/electrical causes (sudden death, arrhythmia) from thermoregulatory collapse.<br /></br>• Core temp ≥38 °C plus volume-depletion signs + intact sweating → think heat exhaustion.<br /></br>• Core temp ≥40 °C + severe CNS dysfunction + hot, dry skin → think heat stroke.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise linkage:<br /></br>a. Exertion in heat + obesity → increased heat production and impaired dissipation.<br /></br>b. Sweating leads to salt/water depletion → intravascular volume loss → ↓ cerebral perfusion → syncope/collapse.<br /></br>c. Continued vasodilation + tachycardia but preserved sweating keeps skin “cool, clammy.”<br /></br>d. Core temp usually 38–40 °C (here 39 °C) → diagnostic of heat exhaustion, not yet heat stroke.<br /></br>Take-home concept: Heat exhaustion = hypotension + tachycardia + temp &lt;40 °C + profuse sweating and only mild CNS symptoms.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): mental status, core temperature method, skin findings (wet vs dry), environmental history, hydration status, vitals trend, treatment response.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Excessive heat production + inadequate cardiovascular compensation → massive cutaneous vasodilation and sweating.<br /></br>• Sweat = hypotonic; loss of NaCl + water → hypovolemia → decreased venous return → cerebral hypoperfusion → syncope.<br /></br>• Core temp remains below 40 °C because evaporative cooling still functional.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management (FYI for exam and wards)<br /></br>1. Immediate: move to cool shaded area; remove excess clothing.<br /></br>2. Passive or active cooling: cool mist fans, ice packs to groin/axilla/neck.<br /></br>3. Re-hydrate: oral sports drink if awake; IV 0.9% saline if altered or vomiting.<br /></br>4. Monitor for progression to heat stroke; check electrolytes (Na⁺, K⁺) and CK if prolonged exertion.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Ex‐hauss-tion” = E for Evaporative sweating still working.<br /></br>• Heat Stroke = “STROKEs the CNS” (seizures, coma) and temperature “STRIKES 40 °C.”<br /></br>Visual mnemonic: picture a runner pouring sweat (wet shirt) but still conscious—Exhaustion; next frame, no sweat, red hot skin, unconscious—Stroke.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>– Would cause exertional dyspnea, crackles, peripheral edema, S3, not sudden hyperthermia.<br /></br>– Skin typically cool but NOT febrile to 39 °C.  </p></br><p>c. Illicit drug use<br /></br>– Stimulants (MDMA, cocaine) can produce hyperthermia and collapse, but pupils usually markedly dilated, patient often agitated not somnolent, and sweating often absent late (similar to heat stroke). No supporting tox history or autonomic hyperactivity beyond tachycardia.  </p></br><p>d. Sepsis<br /></br>– Fever and tachycardia fit, but collapse immediately after intense exercise plus cool clammy skin and absence of infection source argue against. Sepsis usually presents with warm/flushed skin early (“warm shock”) or cool mottled skin late; respiratory rate often elevated but not directly exertion-linked.  </p></br><p>e. Vasovagal syncope<br /></br>– Typically precipitated by standing/emotional stress, NOT by hyperthermia or exercise. Vitals rapidly normalize; no persistent fever or tachypnea after event, and skin is usually pale &amp; sweaty but temperature normal.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Exertional collapse + core temp 38–40 °C + profuse sweating = heat exhaustion. Recognize early and treat with cooling and rehydration to prevent progression to life-threatening heat stroke.</p>
2. A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 2 hours after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure that lasted 5 minutes. He has no other history of seizures. His wife reports that the patient has been increasingly forgetful during the past 3 months. He also has had increasingly frequent jerk-like movements of the arms and legs. Three times during the past week, he did not recognize his wife when he saw her enter a room, but he recognized her voice. He has hypercholesterolemia treated with atorvastatin. He drinks three to four beers nightly. Examination shows involuntary jerking of the head, trunk, and all extremities. There are fasciculations of all extremities. On mental status examination, he has a normal mood and affect. He is alert and is able to repeat six digits forward. He recalls two of five parts of a name and address after 5 minutes. When shown a sketch of a pretzel, he identifies it as a snake. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease</br>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type</br>c. Dementia, frontotemporal</br>d. Dementia, Lewy body</br>e. Subdural hematoma</br>f. Thalamic glioma</br>g. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Dementia, frontotemporal (frontotemporal dementia [FTD] with motor-neuron-disease variant)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that narrow the differential:<br /></br>1. “Increasingly forgetful during the past 3 months” → rapidly progressive cortical dementia.<br /></br>2. “Increasingly frequent jerk-like movements of the arms and legs” + “involuntary jerking of the head, trunk, and all extremities” → cortical myoclonus.<br /></br>3. “Fasciculations of all extremities” → lower-motor-neuron (LMN) involvement, classically seen in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).<br /></br>4. Prosopagnosia/visual agnosia: “did not recognize his wife when he saw her,” “pretzel identified as a snake.”<br /></br>5. New-onset generalized seizure in an elderly patient with the above picture.<br /></br>6. Age 72 and no history of alcohol withdrawal or confusion at baseline.</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: Rapidly progressive dementia + cortical myoclonus + LMN signs (fasciculations) + focal temporal/parietal lobe deficits (prosopagnosia) is the classic triad of the “FTD-ALS” spectrum. Up to 15 % of patients with FTD show overt motor-neuron findings, and the dementia progresses over months (faster than Alzheimer disease but slower than Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: Any time dementia is paired with fasciculations, think of frontotemporal lobar degeneration with motor neuron disease before jumping to prion disease.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Is this delirium?  No; patient is alert, oriented, stable mood.<br /></br>Step 2 – Is this classic Alzheimer disease?  No; too rapid (3 months) and prominent movement disorder.<br /></br>Step 3 – Look for parkinsonism or hallucinations → would suggest Lewy body but neither is present.<br /></br>Step 4 – Are LMN signs present?  Yes (fasciculations) → steers toward FTD-ALS complex.<br /></br>Step 5 – Myoclonus alone could be CJD, but LMN signs strongly argue against prion disease.<br /></br>Conclusion → Frontotemporal dementia with motor-neuron involvement.</p></br><p>Take-home concept: In a patient with rapidly progressive dementia, myoclonus, and fasciculations, the most likely etiology is frontotemporal dementia on the ALS spectrum.</p></br><p>Documentation (what you’d write): “Rapidly progressive cognitive decline with prosopagnosia, diffuse myoclonus, and generalized LMN fasciculations concerning for FTD-MND variant; neurology consulted, MRI brain and EMG ordered.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Frontotemporal dementia = tau or TDP-43 proteinopathy causing degeneration of frontal and temporal lobes.<br /></br>• When TDP-43 aggregates extend to anterior horn cells, an ALS phenotype emerges (weakness, fasciculations, hyperreflexia).<br /></br>• Typical course: insidious behavioral or language change ± motor signs; progression over months–few years (faster than AD, slower than CJD).<br /></br>• Seizures and cortical myoclonus occur in ~10–20 %.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Management &amp; Diagnostics (high yield)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. MRI brain: frontal/temporal atrophy, particularly anterior temporal lobes.<br /></br>2. EMG: fibrillation potentials/positive sharp waves confirming LMN disease.<br /></br>3. Exclude reversible mimics: CBC, CMP, B12, TSH, RPR, HIV, CSF 14-3-3 if prion suspected.  </p></br><p>Treatment (symptomatic)<br /></br>• No disease-modifying therapy.<br /></br>• SSRIs or trazodone for behavioral symptoms.<br /></br>• Riluzole or edaravone if significant ALS component.<br /></br>• Antiseizure medication for myoclonus/seizures (levetiracetam favored).<br /></br>• Multidisciplinary care (speech, PT/OT, caregiver support).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “Frontotemporal = Front door + Tempo drum.”  Imagine a drummer at the front door whose arms/legs twitch (myoclonus) and muscles ripple (fasciculations). Every time you picture the front door you remember FTD-ALS.<br /></br>• FAST mnemonic for rapidly progressive dementia:<br /></br>  F – Fasciculations → FTD<br /></br>  A – Ataxia → CJD<br /></br>  S – Startle myoclonus → CJD<br /></br>  T – Tremor/Parkinsonism → Lewy body  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD)<br /></br>   • Rapidly progressive dementia + myoclonus fits, but fasciculations/LMN findings are NOT typical. Visual agnosia is less common; ataxia and startle myoclonus would be expected.  </p></br><p>b. Dementia, Alzheimer type<br /></br>   • Usually slower (years), lacks early myoclonus and LMN signs; prosopagnosia occurs late.  </p></br><p>d. Dementia, Lewy body<br /></br>   • Requires at least two of: visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition, REM sleep-behavior disorder. None are documented here; LMN fasciculations are absent in DLB.  </p></br><p>e. Subdural hematoma<br /></br>   • Can cause seizures and dementia, but would expect focal neurologic deficits, headache, history of fall/anticoagulation; myoclonus/fasciculations not typical.  </p></br><p>f. Thalamic glioma<br /></br>   • Rare; would produce progressive focal deficits (sensory loss, pain syndromes) rather than diffuse cortical cognitive deficits and fasciculations.  </p></br><p>g. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome<br /></br>   • Requires thiamine deficiency; triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, confusion; chronic amnestic state has confabulation, but LMN signs/myoclonus are absent and vision recognition is preserved.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Dementia + cortical myoclonus + lower-motor-neuron fasciculations = frontotemporal dementia on the ALS spectrum, a distinct entity from Alzheimer, Lewy body, or prion disease.</p>
3. A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a second opinion regarding a positive Chlamydia trachomatis DNA hybridization test done by her primary physician 2 weeks ago. Patient has been in a mutually monogamous heterosexual relationship for the past 7 years. She does not doubt her partner’s fidelity, and she disbelieves the results. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Pelvic examination shows normal vaginal discharge, an IUD string at the cervical os, and normal cervical mucus. There is no pelvic tenderness. The physician is aware that the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection in this patient’s age group is less than 1 percent; the sensitivity of the test is 80 percent, and the specificity is 97 percent. Which of the following best explains the validity of the test results to this patient?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a.    Accuracy</br>b.    Negative likelihood ratio</br>c.    Negative predictive value</br>d.    Positive likelihood ratio</br>e.    Positive predictive value</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Positive predictive value (PPV)</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should leap out on test-day:<br /></br>• Routine screening test, but the patient belongs to a population with a VERY LOW PREVALENCE (&lt;1%).<br /></br>• Test characteristics are given (sensitivity 80 %, specificity 97 %).<br /></br>• The patient wants to know “how valid” her positive result is—that is, How likely is it that she actually has Chlamydia?  </p></br><p>High-yield cognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Ask yourself, “What number tells a patient the probability she truly has the disease after a POSITIVE result?” → PPV.<br /></br>2. Remember that PPV and NPV are highly prevalence-dependent. With very low prevalence, PPV drops dramatically (lots of false-positives).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any time the stem mentions prevalence plus sensitivity/specificity and someone questions a POSITIVE result’s truthfulness, you should immediately think of PPV.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Pre-test probability = prevalence ≈ 1 %.<br /></br>2. Apply the test:<br /></br>   • True-positives = Sensitivity × Diseased = 0.80 × 1 %<br /></br>   • False-positives = (1 – Specificity) × Non-diseased = 0.03 × 99 %<br /></br>3. PPV = True-positives / (True-positives + False-positives).<br /></br>   Using 10,000 women to make numbers concrete:<br /></br>   • Diseased: 100; true-positive = 80.<br /></br>   • Non-diseased: 9,900; false-positive = 297.<br /></br>   • PPV ≈ 80 / (80 + 297) ≈ 21 %.<br /></br>4. Interpretation: Despite a “positive” test, the chance she truly has Chlamydia is only ~1 in 5. That is exactly the information the patient is asking for.  </p></br><p>Take-home: PPV answers, “If my test is positive, what’s the probability I really have the disease?”</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-world): Counsel patient about possibility of false-positive, consider repeat NAAT or alternate assay, discuss partner testing, symptoms, and risk factors.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common bacterial STI; however, prevalence in monogamous, &gt;30-year-old women is extremely low (&lt;1 %). Nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) are highly sensitive/specific but not perfect; therefore, Bayes’ theorem dictates that low pre-test probability → low PPV.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review  </p></br><p>• Confirmatory testing: Repeat NAAT or a different molecular assay can be considered when clinical suspicion is low and PPV is expected to be poor.<br /></br>• Treatment if confirmed: Azithromycin 1 g PO ×1 or Doxycycline 100 mg PO bid ×7 d; treat partner; abstain for 7 d.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“P-P-V = Post-Positive Verdict” – the post-test probability after a positive result.<br /></br>“SnNout / SpPin” are prevalence-independent, but PPV/NPV are Prevalence-Dependent → think ‘P’ for Prevalence.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Accuracy – (TP + TN) / total tests. Useful for lab QC but does NOT tell an individual patient the probability she truly has the disease after her result.  </p></br><p>B. Negative likelihood ratio – (1 – sensitivity) / specificity. Applies to a NEGATIVE result, not a positive one.  </p></br><p>C. Negative predictive value – Probability of being disease-free after a NEGATIVE test. The patient had a positive test.  </p></br><p>D. Positive likelihood ratio – Sensitivity / (1 – specificity) = 0.80 / 0.03 ≈ 27. A PLR tells you how many times more likely a positive result is in someone with disease than without disease, but you still need to apply Bayes’ theorem to convert this to an absolute probability. PPV already gives the final probability in plain language.  </p></br><p>E. Positive predictive value – Correct. Directly answers, “Given my positive result, what’s the chance I really have Chlamydia?”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────</br>Key takeaway: In low-prevalence settings, even good tests have low PPV; always interpret results in the context of pre-test probability.</p>
4. A 7-month-old girl is brought to the physician because of a 2-day history of rapid breathing, sweating, pallor, decreased feeding, and increased fussiness. The mother says the patient had the same symptoms 3 weeks ago, but they were less severe and resolved spontaneously in less than 1 day. The patient is alert and fussy. She is at the 50th percentile for length and weight. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 220/min, respirations are 48/min, and blood pressure is 62/42 mm Hg. Capillary refill time is 3 seconds. Examination shows pink skin. The liver edge is palpated 3 cm below the right costal margin. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows narrow complex tachycardia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring</br>b. Echocardiography</br>c. Intravenous adenosine therapy</br>d. Intravenous digoxin therapy</br>e. Intravenous propranolol therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Intravenous adenosine therapy  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key findings that immediately narrow the differential to paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (SVT):<br /></br>• Age: 7-month-old – infants commonly develop AV-reentry-mediated SVT.<br /></br>• Heart rate: 220/min – in an infant, &gt;220–230/min strongly suggests SVT rather than sinus tachycardia.<br /></br>• Narrow-complex rhythm on ECG – rules out ventricular tachycardia.<br /></br>• Episodic history (“same symptoms 3 weeks ago … resolved spontaneously”) – classic for paroxysmal SVT.<br /></br>• Signs of mild hypoperfusion (irritability, poor feeding, borderline capillary refill, low BP 62/42 mm Hg). Skin remains pink and patient is alert → “stable” by ACLS/PALS criteria (no altered mental status, shock, or pulselessness).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach for exam day:<br /></br>1. See extreme tachycardia in infant → check rate cutoffs (SVT &gt;220).<br /></br>2. Look at ECG descriptor (narrow vs wide).<br /></br>3. Assess stability (mental status, pulses/BP, perfusion).<br /></br>4. If stable narrow-complex SVT → 1st-line = vagal maneuvers → IV adenosine. If unstable → synchronized cardioversion.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic to the answer:<br /></br>1. Diagnose SVT: narrow complex + rate 220 in a 7-mo.<br /></br>2. Determine stability: despite hypotension, patient is alert and perfused (pink, cap refill 3 s). PALS labels this “stable.”<br /></br>3. Acute termination of SVT in a stable patient:<br /></br>   • Give vagal maneuvers (ice to face, Valsalva) – often implied/assumed in the ED; IV adenosine is next and is the tested intervention.<br /></br>4. Therefore, IV adenosine is the best immediate management.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In hemodynamically stable infants/children with narrow-complex SVT, IV adenosine (rapid push 0.1 mg/kg, max 6 mg; may repeat 0.2 mg/kg) is the drug of choice to acutely terminate the arrhythmia.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real encounter): rate/rhythm, blood pressure trends, perfusion exam, response to vagal maneuvers, dose/time of adenosine, rhythm post-conversion.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Most infant SVT is due to an accessory pathway (orthodromic AV re-entrant tachycardia).<br /></br>• AV node is part of the circuit; blocking it transiently (adenosine → ↑ AV-node K+ efflux → hyperpolarizes and transiently blocks conduction) terminates the loop.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Acute SVT algorithm (PALS):<br /></br>1. Rapid assessment → identify SVT.<br /></br>2. Stable: vagal → adenosine.<br /></br>3. Unstable: synchronized cardioversion 0.5–1 J/kg → 2 J/kg if no response.<br /></br>4. Chronic prevention after two episodes or high-risk substrate: beta-blocker (eg, propranolol), digoxin, or catheter ablation (definitive).  </p></br><p>Helpful numbers to memorize:<br /></br>• Infant SVT rate &gt;220–230/min.<br /></br>• Adenosine push with rapid 5–10 mL NS flush through large-bore IV closest to heart; very short half-life (~10 s).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring<br /></br>   • Useful for documenting intermittent arrhythmias, not for acute termination. Does nothing for current episode.  </p></br><p>b. Echocardiography<br /></br>   • Performed electively to rule out structural heart disease but is not emergent therapy.  </p></br><p>d. Intravenous digoxin therapy<br /></br>   • Digoxin enhances vagal tone and slows AV conduction chronically. Onset is too slow for acute SVT termination and carries risk of ventricular arrhythmias in accessory-pathway tachycardias like WPW.  </p></br><p>e. Intravenous propranolol therapy<br /></br>   • Beta-blockers are another prophylactic option once rhythm is converted, but they have slower onset and more hemodynamic side-effects than adenosine in the acute setting.  </p></br><p>Pitfall to avoid: assuming any hypotension equals “unstable.” Stability is judged by airway, breathing, and mental status. An alert, perfused infant with narrow-complex SVT still gets adenosine first.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SAFe-A” for stable narrow-complex SVT:<br /></br>• Stable →<br /></br>• Adenosine Fast push →<br /></br>• if Fails, Synchronized cardioversion →<br /></br>• Evaluate for accessory pathway afterward.  </p></br><p>Visual trick: Picture a fire alarm (SVT) wired through a single junction box (AV node). Hitting the master switch (adenosine) turns off the junction box for a second, breaking the circuit and silencing the alarm.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Stable infant with narrow-complex tachycardia &gt;220/min = SVT → terminate acutely with rapid IV adenosine.</p>
5. A 67-year-old woman is brought to the physician because of a 2-week history of increasingly blurred vision. Two months ago, she was hospitalized for 10 days for management of tuberculous meningitis; treatment with ethambutol, isoniazid, prednisone, pyrazinamide, pyridoxine, and rifampin was started at that time. She had been feeling well since discharge. Her only other medication is lisinopril for hypertension. She has no other history of serious illness. Her blood pressure is 125/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a best-corrected visual acuity of 20/50 bilaterally. Visual field examination shows decreased vision in the center of each field. Peripheral vision is normal. A mild-to-moderate decrease in color vision is noted bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Toxicity from which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s blurred vision?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ethambutol  </br>b. Isoniazid  </br>c. Prednisone  </br>d. Pyrazinamide  </br>e. Pyridoxine  </br>f. Rifampin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>a. Ethambutol  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Medication timeline: Patient started four-drug TB therapy (INH, RIF, PZA, EMB) 2 months ago.<br /></br>2. Key ophthalmic findings:<br /></br>   • Progressive bilateral blurred vision over 2 weeks<br /></br>   • Central visual field loss (central scotoma) with preserved peripheral vision<br /></br>   • Decreased color perception (dyschromatopsia)<br /></br>3. No other neurologic findings ⇒ localizes problem to the optic nerve, not brain parenchyma.<br /></br>4. Classic NBME/Step pattern: “Bilateral, painless, central visual loss + impaired color vision after starting TB therapy” → think ethambutol optic neuropathy (“Eye-thambutol”).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see central scotoma + color vision loss in any patient on TB meds, scan the answer choices for ethambutol first.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step approach:<br /></br>A. Identify drug exposures.<br /></br>B. Match characteristic adverse effect profiles.<br /></br>C. Central scotoma &amp; color vision loss are hallmark of toxic optic neuropathy.<br /></br>D. Ethambutol is the only first-line TB drug whose signature toxicity is retrobulbar optic neuritis leading to these exact visual changes.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Ethambutol causes dose-related, reversible (if caught early) optic neuropathy that presents with painless, symmetric loss of visual acuity, central scotomas, and red-green color blindness.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In real practice you must record baseline and serial (monthly if dose &gt;15–25 mg/kg/day or renal disease) visual acuity and color vision testing for any patient on ethambutol.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Ethambutol chelates copper &amp; zinc, interfering with mitochondrial function in retinal ganglion cells → demyelination of optic nerve fibers.<br /></br>• Risk ↑ with high doses, prolonged therapy, renal insufficiency.<br /></br>• Usually develops within weeks to months; may improve after discontinuation, but can be permanent if unrecognized.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>1. Immediate discontinuation of ethambutol; substitute streptomycin, fluoroquinolone, or second-line agent per susceptibility.<br /></br>2. Baseline/periodic: Snellen acuity, Ishihara color plates, central field testing (Amsler grid).<br /></br>3. No specific medical therapy reverses the damage; prevention &amp; early recognition are key.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>• “Eye-thambutol” or “EMB = Eye Muscle Blindness.”<br /></br>• Think “Color gets EMB-arrassed” → red-green color loss.<br /></br>• Visualize EMB on a Snellen chart turning the letters gray in the middle.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Isoniazid – Classic toxicities: peripheral neuropathy (pyridoxine deficiency), hepatotoxicity, seizures, lupus-like syndrome. Optic neuritis is exceedingly rare.<br /></br>c. Prednisone – Long-term use may cause posterior subcapsular cataracts, glaucoma, or central serous chorioretinopathy, but not acute central scotoma with color loss after only 2 months.<br /></br>d. Pyrazinamide – Causes hepatotoxicity and hyperuricemia/gout; no optic toxicity.<br /></br>e. Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) – Given precisely to PREVENT INH neuropathy; toxicity only at massive doses (sensory neuropathy), not optic neuritis.<br /></br>f. Rifampin – Hepatotoxicity, thrombocytopenia, flu-like syndrome, orange body fluids; no characteristic optic nerve effect.  </p></br><p>Common Test Trap: Some students remember “INH can cause neuropathy” and mistakenly pick it. Note that INH neuropathy is peripheral (stocking-glove) and prevented with B6, whereas EMB toxicity is optic and affects central vision first.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Point to Remember<br /></br>Central, painless visual loss with color desaturation in a patient on TB therapy = Ethambutol optic neuropathy.</p>
6. A 45-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis comes to the physician for a routine examination. His medications are spironolactone and furosemide. He no longer drinks alcohol. He is alert and fully oriented. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 108/60 mm Hg. Examination shows scattered spider angiomas over the chest and upper abdomen. The abdomen is distended; there is dullness to percussion over the flanks. Which of the following is the most appropriate screening test for this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Colonoscopy</br>b. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br>c. Measurement of serum ammonia concentration</br>d. Measurement of serum ferritin concentration</br>e. Serum CA 19-9 assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues</br>• Known alcoholic cirrhosis → automatically places the patient at high risk for portal-hypertensive complications, especially esophageal varices.<br /></br>• Currently compensated (normal mentation, stable vitals, no ascites tense enough for paracentesis, on diuretics) → guidelines address screening in all patients with cirrhosis, compensated or not.<br /></br>• “Routine examination” → question asks, “What should you be doing now as part of evidence-based surveillance?”  </p></br><p>Pattern to recognize</br>Any patient with newly diagnosed or established cirrhosis must be screened for:</br>1. Esophageal varices → EGD at diagnosis and every 1–3 years.<br /></br>2. Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) → abdominal ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (not one of the answer choices).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a cirrhotic patient shows up in any NBME/Step2CK question that mentions “routine evaluation,” the two reflex thoughts should be “variceal screening (EGD)” and “HCC screening (ultrasound).” If only one is offered, pick it.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step approach<br /></br>1. Recognize condition: alcoholic cirrhosis.<br /></br>2. Ask: what life-threatening but preventable complications should I look for?<br /></br>   • Variceal bleeding → high mortality, prophylaxis available (non-selective β-blocker or variceal band ligation).<br /></br>3. What is the guideline-recommended screening test?<br /></br>   • Diagnostic EGD to visualize and grade varices.<br /></br>4. Choose that option.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: All patients with cirrhosis—regardless of etiology, symptoms, or Child-Pugh class—require screening EGD at the time of diagnosis; repeat every 1–3 years depending on findings and disease status.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Screening EGD ordered for variceal surveillance per AASLD cirrhosis guideline. Will start prophylactic nadolol if medium/large varices identified.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Portal hypertension (↑ resistance through fibrotic liver) → collateral formation in lower esophagus.<br /></br>• Varices enlarge silently until they rupture → massive upper GI bleed.<br /></br>• Non-selective β-blockers (propranolol, nadolol) ↓ portal venous inflow; endoscopic band ligation eradicates varices.<br /></br>• Thus early detection changes management → improved survival.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line prophylaxis AFTER screening EGD<br /></br>• Small/no varices → repeat EGD in 2–3 yr (compensated) or 1 yr (decomp).<br /></br>• Medium/large varices → start non-selective β-blocker (propranolol 20 mg BID; titrate) OR band ligation if β-blocker contraindicated; repeat EGD every 6–12 mo.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Cirrhotic ABCs”<br /></br>A – AFP + Abdominal ultrasound q6mo for HCC<br /></br>B – Beta-blocker/Banding → but only AFTER<br /></br>C – Camera down the esophagus (EGD) to look for varices first.</p></br><p>Mnemonic image: Picture a “camera” (EGD) entering a beer bottle (alcoholic cirrhosis) before a “band” wraps around the neck of the bottle (= band ligation of varices).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Colonoscopy<br /></br>• Indicated for colorectal cancer screening based on age/family history, not specifically because of cirrhosis. Portal hypertensive colopathy exists but routine colonoscopy is not recommended solely for cirrhosis.</p></br><p>C. Measurement of serum ammonia concentration<br /></br>• Ammonia does not correlate with presence/grade of hepatic encephalopathy or predict bleeding risk; not used for screening in asymptomatic patients.</p></br><p>D. Measurement of serum ferritin concentration<br /></br>• Ferritin screens for hemochromatosis, not needed in a patient with established alcoholic cirrhosis unless iron overload suspected; would not change near-term management.</p></br><p>E. Serum CA 19-9 assay<br /></br>• Marker for pancreatic and biliary cancers; not recommended for HCC screening (that would be AFP + ultrasound). No indication in routine cirrhosis follow-up.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Every patient with cirrhosis needs an initial EGD to look for esophageal varices because early identification allows life-saving prophylaxis against catastrophic upper GI bleeding.</p>
7. A 19-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after he was involved in a motor vehicle collision. He was the unrestrained driver. A cervical collar and spinal board were placed at the scene. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On arrival, he reports shortness of breath and difficulty breathing, especially in the right side of the chest. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.5°F), pulse is 126/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 78 percent. Examination shows right-sided supraclavicular fossal distention with inspiration. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Aortic dissection</br>b. Cardiac contusion</br>c. Clavicular fracture</br>d. Esophageal rupture</br>e. Pericardial hemorrhage</br>f. Pneumothorax</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Pneumothorax  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• “Unrestrained driver” + high-speed motor vehicle collision → high-energy blunt chest trauma.<br /></br>• “30 min after” injury → acute decompensation rather than late complication.<br /></br>• “Shortness of breath … especially in the right side of the chest” → unilateral process.<br /></br>• Vital signs: hypoxia (SpO₂ 78 %), tachypnea (30/min), tachycardia (126/min), borderline-low BP (110/70). These are classic early tension physiology findings.<br /></br>• “Right-sided supraclavicular fossal distention with inspiration” → the right apical pleura is bulging upward with each breath, a subtle description of unilateral hyperinflation.  </p></br><p>Why this narrows the differential<br /></br>After major chest trauma the two life-threatening causes of sudden hypoxia + unilateral chest findings are pneumothorax (simple or tension) and massive hemothorax. Hemothorax produces dullness, not apical bulging. The vignette’s description of apical fullness during inspiration is a textbook clue for accumulating intrapleural air.  </p></br><p>Test-taking perspective<br /></br>1. Immediately ask: “Does this scenario demand an airway/ breathing/ circulation (ABCs) diagnosis?” Yes—severe hypoxia and unilateral chest findings.<br /></br>2. Look for buzzwords indicating tension physiology: severe dyspnea, unilateral hyperexpansion, tracheal deviation, JVD, or in this question “supraclavicular fossal distention.”<br /></br>3. Pick pneumothorax without overthinking; do not be distracted by other thoracic injuries.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Mechanism: rapid deceleration + unrestrained → rib or pleural injury.<br /></br>2. Presentation: sudden unilateral dyspnea + ↓O₂ sat → suspect PTX.<br /></br>3. Physical clues: unilateral apical bulge ⇒ lung on that side not ventilating, pleural cavity filling with air → pneumothorax (likely tension).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Unilateral chest trauma + acute hypoxia + ipsilateral chest hyperexpansion (including bulging supraclavicular fossa) = pneumothorax until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Respiratory distress, unilateral absent breath sounds/hyperresonance (would be documented).<br /></br>• Immediate needle thoracostomy performed prior to imaging if tension physiology suspected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Blunt/penetrating chest trauma can tear visceral pleura. If the tear acts as a one-way valve, each inspiration forces air into the pleural space but prevents exit → tension pneumothorax. Intrapleural pressure rises, ipsilateral lung collapses, mediastinum shifts, venous return drops → tachycardia, hypotension, hypoxia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Emergent: Needle decompression (14-16 G) in 2nd intercostal space mid-clavicular line or 5th intercostal space anterior axillary line, followed by tube thoracostomy. Imaging is obtained only when patient is stable; do NOT delay treatment for chest x-ray if tension suspected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“COLLAPSED” for tension PTX features:<br /></br>Chest pain / dyspnea<br /></br>One-sided hyperresonance<br /></br>Low BP<br /></br>Leads to tracheal deviation<br /></br>Absent breath sounds<br /></br>Persistent tachycardia<br /></br>Elevated JVD or apical bulge<br /></br>Decompress immediately  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Aortic dissection – Classically tearing chest/back pain in hypertensive older adults; CXR shows widened mediastinum, not unilateral apical bulging or isolated hypoxia.  </p></br><p>b. Cardiac contusion – Presents with arrhythmias, chest pain, elevated troponins; respiratory exam usually unremarkable. No unilateral hyperinflation.  </p></br><p>c. Clavicular fracture – Localized pain over clavicle, crepitus; does not cause severe hypoxia or supraclavicular fossa distention.  </p></br><p>d. Esophageal rupture – Severe chest pain, crepitus, subcutaneous emphysema, fever; usually after forceful vomiting (Boerhaave) or penetrating trauma; not an immediate cause of profound hypoxia.  </p></br><p>e. Pericardial hemorrhage (tamponade) – Beck triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD. Breath sounds normal; oxygen saturation often preserved early.  </p></br><p>Key discriminators: only pneumothorax causes unilateral chest expansion and acute hypoxia after trauma.</p>
8. A previously healthy 67-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-hour history of severe upper abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Her symptoms began when her 4-year-old grandson jumped on her abdomen while she was lying on the sofa. She is in acute distress. Her temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 150/60 mm Hg. Decreased breath sounds are heard in the lung bases bilaterally. Cardiac examination shows no other abnormalities. Abdominal examination shows diffuse tenderness to palpation; bowel sounds are decreased. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 11 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count 8500/mm³</br>Serum</br>    Ca²⁺ 7.5 mg/dL</br>    Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL</br>    Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL</br>    AST 30 U/L</br>    ALT 29 U/L</br>    Amylase 348 U/L</br>    Lipase 189 U/L (N=14–280)</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a.   Gallstone impaction</br>b.   Intestinal contusion</br>c.   Liver laceration</br>d.   Splenic laceration</br>e.   Traumatic pancreatitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>e. Traumatic pancreatitis</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How NBME wants you to think</br>1. Immediate mechanism: “4-year-old grandson jumped on her abdomen” = blunt abdominal trauma that compresses retroperitoneal structures.</br>2. Timing: Severe epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting beginning minutes–hours after the blow.</br>3. Objective findings that scream PANCREAS:</br>   • Amylase 348 U/L (↑)<br /></br>   • Lipase 189 U/L (upper-normal/ mildly ↑)<br /></br>   • Ca²⁺ 7.5 mg/dL (↓) – classic for fat saponification in acute pancreatitis.<br /></br>   • Decreased breath sounds at bases – pleural effusions/atelectasis frequently accompany pancreatitis.</br>4. No clues for solid-organ hemorrhage: Hgb stable at 11 g/dL, vitals relatively preserved, no peritoneal signs.</br>Putting the clues together → acute pancreatitis precipitated by direct pancreatic injury = Traumatic pancreatitis.</p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: In any trauma stem, if you see epigastric pain + elevated amylase/lipase, immediately think of pancreatic contusion/laceration. Don’t let other abdominal organ options distract you unless the enzyme pattern or vitals point elsewhere.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)</br>Step 1  Identify the mechanism: blunt abdominal blow → suspect pancreatic and duodenal injury because they are crushed against the vertebral column.<br /></br>Step 2  Match labs to organ: Only pancreatitis reliably elevates amylase/lipase and causes hypocalcemia.<br /></br>Step 3  Rule out competing choices with targeted data:</br>       • No ALT/AST spike → not liver.<br /></br>       • Normal bilirubin/ALP → gallstone disease unlikely.<br /></br>       • Stable Hgb, no Kehr sign → not splenic/liver bleed.<br /></br>       • Bowel sounds ↓ but no free air/perforation signs → intestinal contusion less likely.</br>Core takeaway: Elevated pancreatic enzymes after blunt trauma = traumatic pancreatitis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were real:</br>• Mechanism of injury and timing of pain onset<br /></br>• Serial abdominal exams, pain severity, vitals<br /></br>• Serum amylase/lipase, calcium trend<br /></br>• Plan for contrast-enhanced CT abdomen to grade pancreatic injury<br /></br>• Supportive management (IV fluids, analgesia, NPO) ± surgical consult if duct disrupted</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Direct compression of the pancreas against the spine → parenchymal bruising or ductal disruption.<br /></br>• Enzyme leakage triggers autodigestion → fat necrosis → Ca²⁺ deposition (hypocalcemia).<br /></br>• Inflammatory mediators cause third-spacing → ↑ BUN/Cr, pleural effusions, SIRS.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment</br>Diagnostics:</br>1. Serum amylase &amp; lipase (already elevated).<br /></br>2. Contrast CT abdomen: identifies laceration/ fluid collections, guides need for ERCP or surgery.<br /></br>3. Consider MRCP/ERCP if main pancreatic duct injury suspected.</p></br><p>Treatment:</br>• Initial: Aggressive isotonic IV fluids (e.g., Lactated Ringer), NPO, IV opioids.<br /></br>• Monitor Ca²⁺; replace if symptomatic.<br /></br>• Antibiotics NOT routine—reserve for infected necrosis.<br /></br>• Surgical/debridement only if duct disruption, hemorrhage, or infected necrosis develops.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids</br>“Pancreas gets PUNCHED” mnemonic for traumatic pancreatitis:</br>P – Post-trauma epigastric Pain<br /></br>U – ↑ amylase/lipase Units<br /></br>N – Nausea/vomiting<br /></br>C – Calcium low<br /></br>H – Hemorrhage rare at first (stable Hgb)<br /></br>E – Effusions at lung bases<br /></br>D – Diagnostic CT confirms injury</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Gallstone impaction<br /></br>   • Gallstone pancreatitis usually shows ALT &gt;150 U/L and ↑ bilirubin/ALP; no traumatic trigger.</p></br><p>b. Intestinal contusion<br /></br>   • Presents with delayed obstruction, seat-belt sign, or free intraperitoneal fluid; enzymes normal.</p></br><p>c. Liver laceration<br /></br>   • RUQ tenderness, hypotension, rapidly falling Hgb, AST/ALT markedly ↑; lungs typically clear.</p></br><p>d. Splenic laceration<br /></br>   • LUQ pain, Kehr sign (left shoulder), hypotension/tachycardia, ↓ Hgb; no amylase elevation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Blunt abdominal trauma + elevated pancreatic enzymes ± hypocalcemia = traumatic pancreatitis. Always correlate mechanism with lab pattern to avoid solid-organ injury traps on NBME/Step 2 questions.</p>
9. Researchers would like to review the evidence on the association between cigarette smoking and cardiovascular disease. They review a sample of 500 studies published during the past 50 years and note that the increased risk for cardiovascular disease in patients positively correlates with the number of cigarettes smoked daily. The researchers also note that all of these studies are observational. They conclude that this type of study design cannot determine a causal link between smoking and cardiovascular disease, but these studies support one of the criteria for causality. Which of the following best represents the causation criterion met by this type of association?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Biologic plausibility</br>b. Dose-response relationship</br>c. Specificity of the association</br>d. Strength of the association</br>e. Temporal relationship</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Dose-response relationship  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrase: “the increased risk for cardiovascular disease…positively correlates with the number of cigarettes smoked daily.”<br /></br>• “Positively correlates” means as exposure (cigarettes/day) goes up, outcome (CVD risk) goes up. That is the classic pattern of a dose-response (a.k.a. biological gradient).<br /></br>• The vignette also reminds you that ALL the included studies are observational ⇒ cannot alone establish causality, but they can satisfy individual Bradford-Hill criteria.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: Whenever you see “more of the exposure → more of the disease,” immediately think of the dose-response criterion on exams asking about causal inference.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify what part of Bradford-Hill is explicitly demonstrated.<br /></br>2. “Biologic plausibility,” “specificity,” “strength,” and “temporal sequence” would each need other information (mechanism, single outcome, large RR, or exposure preceding disease).<br /></br>3. Only “dose-response” is directly supplied by the phrase “positively correlates with the number of cigarettes smoked daily.”<br /></br>Take-home: A graded increase in risk with increased exposure satisfies the Dose-Response Relationship criterion for causation.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Smoking accelerates atherosclerosis via endothelial dysfunction, increased LDL oxidation, inflammation, and hypercoagulability.<br /></br>• Greater cigarette burden intensifies these processes, leading to proportionally higher CVD incidence—hence the observed dose-response.</p></br><p>Diagnostic / Treatment Pearls<br /></br>• For real patients, quantify tobacco burden in pack-years; document in H&amp;P.<br /></br>• Smoking cessation is first-line intervention to reduce CVD risk; pharmacologic aids: varenicline, bupropion, or NRT (patch/gum).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “BEST DRT”:<br /></br>B – Biological plausibility<br /></br>E – Experiment (reversibility)<br /></br>S – Strength<br /></br>T – Temporality<br /></br>D – Dose-response<br /></br>R – Replication (Consistency)<br /></br>T – Type-specificity<br /></br>Link: “More Dose, more Disease” → Dose-Response.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Biologic plausibility – Requires mechanistic explanation (e.g., nicotine causes endothelial injury). Vignette provides no mechanistic data.<br /></br>c. Specificity – One exposure → one disease (rarely met in medicine; smoking causes many diseases). Not supported.<br /></br>d. Strength of association – Needs a large relative risk or odds ratio (e.g., RR &gt;10). Vignette only says “increased,” not how strong.<br /></br>e. Temporal relationship – Exposure must precede outcome (established in prospective data). Cross-sectional correlation does not confirm temporality.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Students often choose Strength or Temporality whenever causality is mentioned. Always anchor your choice in the explicit information given—here, a graded correlation = Dose-Response.</p>
10. A 23-year-old woman comes to the physician to learn the results of a PPD skin test performed 48 hours ago. The test is required before she begins her first year of nursing school. She feels well. She emigrated from Thailand at the age of 15 years. She received the BCG vaccine during infancy. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She has no known exposure to anyone with tuberculosis or respiratory symptoms. She does not drink alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. The PPD skin test shows 15 mm of induration. An x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Isoniazid therapy</br>b. Repeat PPD skin test in 2 weeks</br>c. Rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol therapy</br>d. Sputum cultures for acid-fast bacilli</br>e. No further management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Isoniazid therapy (INH × 9 months + pyridoxine)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 23-y-o woman, asymptomatic, normal CXR → no evidence of active disease.<br /></br>• 15-mm induration on a TST (PPD) → exceeds every diagnostic threshold (≥15 mm for everyone; ≥10 mm for recent immigrants from high-prevalence countries).<br /></br>• BCG vaccine in infancy → ignore when interpreting TST.<br /></br>• Nursing student → future health-care worker, so treating latent TB protects both her and her patients.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Decide whether the TST is positive based on size + risk category.<br /></br>2. If positive, rule out active TB clinically and with CXR.<br /></br>3. If CXR is normal → latent TB infection (LTBI) → give one drug (INH 6–9 mo or rifampin 4 mo).<br /></br>4. If CXR or symptoms suggest active TB → collect sputum and treat with 4-drug regimen.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why INH?<br /></br>• She has LTBI: positive TST, normal CXR, no symptoms.<br /></br>• LTBI carries an ~5–10 % lifetime risk of reactivation; risk rises with future immunosuppression.<br /></br>• INH for 9 months lowers reactivation risk by &gt;90 %.<br /></br>“Take-home message”: A positive TST with a normal CXR = treat latent TB with INH (plus pyridoxine).</p></br><p>What to document: TST size, date read, risk category, CXR result, absence of symptoms, counselling about INH + vitamin B6, baseline LFTs.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• PPD elicits a delayed (type IV) hypersensitivity reaction if memory T cells recognize mycobacterial proteins.<br /></br>• BCG may cause mild TST positivity, but effect wanes after ~5 yrs; guidelines state to interpret TST exactly the same as in non-vaccinated persons.<br /></br>• LTBI = contained organisms in granulomas (dormant) → potential future reactivation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>Latent TB regimens (choose one):<br /></br>1. INH daily × 9 mo (gold standard for most) ± pyridoxine 25–50 mg/d to prevent neuropathy.<br /></br>2. Rifampin daily × 4 mo.<br /></br>3. INH + rifapentine once weekly × 3 mo (DOT).  </p></br><p>Baseline labs: AST/ALT; repeat only if symptoms or risk factors for hepatotoxicity.</p></br><p>Active TB (NOT the case): 2-month RIPE (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) → 4-month continuation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“15-10-5 Rule”<br /></br>• ≥15 mm → everyone.<br /></br>• ≥10 mm → immigration &lt;5 yrs, healthcare work, chronic disease, kids &lt;4 y.<br /></br>• ≥5 mm → HIV, close contact, fibrotic CXR, transplant/immunosuppressed.</p></br><p>LATENT = “One drug, Long time.” (INH 9 mo).<br /></br>ACTIVE = “Four drugs, Short burst.” (RIPE 2 mo → dual 4 mo).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Repeat PPD in 2 weeks<br /></br>• Booster phenomenon used only when initial test is negative. Her test is already positive.</p></br><p>c. Rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol (multidrug)<br /></br>• Indicated for ACTIVE TB; she lacks symptoms and has a normal CXR.</p></br><p>d. Sputum cultures for acid-fast bacilli<br /></br>• Needed only if CXR or symptoms suggest pulmonary TB. Not necessary with a negative CXR and no symptoms.</p></br><p>e. No further management<br /></br>• Untreated LTBI carries risk of reactivation; CDC/USPSTF recommend chemoprophylaxis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Point:<br /></br>Positive TST meeting risk-based threshold + negative CXR = latent TB → give INH 6–9 months with pyridoxine.</p>
11. A 76-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-day history of severe, right-sided chest pain that increases with deep inspiration and coughing. She also has a 1-week history of intermittent fever, malaise, and loss of appetite. Ten days ago, she was diagnosed with pneumonia of the right lower lobe. Azithromycin therapy was begun at that time. She has no other history of serious illness and takes no other medications. On arrival, her temperature is 39.5°C (103.1°F), pulse is 92/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 94 percent. On pulmonary examination, there is egophony and dullness to percussion over the right lower lobe. Breath sounds are decreased on the right. A chest x-ray shows a right pleural effusion. Thoracentesis is performed; pleural fluid analysis shows a leukocyte count of 16,000/mm3, glucose concentration of 50 mg/dL, and pH of 7.12. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest</br>b. Intravenous furosemide therapy</br>c. Intubation and mechanical ventilation</br>d. Placement of a chest tube</br>e. Pleurodesis of the right pleural space</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Placement of a chest tube  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Timeline: Recent bacterial pneumonia → new pleuritic chest pain &amp; fever 10 days later → think “parapneumonic complication.”<br /></br>• Exam: Egophony, dullness, ↓ breath sounds = effusion over area of prior pneumonia.<br /></br>• Thoracentesis clues (most tested points):<br /></br>  – WBC 16,000/​mm³ (purulent)<br /></br>  – Glucose 50 mg/dL (low)<br /></br>  – pH 7.12 (acidic)<br /></br>These three findings together define a “complicated parapneumonic effusion” or early empyema.<br /></br>Key USMLE rule: pH &lt; 7.20 OR glucose &lt; 60 OR frank pus/positive culture = drain it.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar items<br /></br>1. Recognize post-pneumonia effusion.<br /></br>2. Recall Light’s criteria tell you exudate vs transudate; THEN look at pH &amp; glucose to decide if “complicated.”<br /></br>3. Acidic or hypoglycorrhachic pleural fluid → chest tube ± intrapleural fibrinolytics.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Pneumonia not improving + new effusion → obtain diagnostic thoracentesis.<br /></br>2. Fluid chemistry shows low pH / glucose → bacteria consume glucose &amp; make lactic acid → pleural space no longer sterilized by antibiotics.<br /></br>3. Antibiotics alone cannot penetrate thick fibrinous loculations; mechanical drainage is required.<br /></br>Take-home: Drain pleural space whenever biochemical markers or gross appearance indicate complicated effusion/empyema.<br /></br>Documentation: note thoracentesis results, decision for tube thoracostomy, inform patient/family of risks/benefits, start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (eg, pip-tazo + vancomycin) while awaiting culture.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Parapneumonic effusion spectrum:<br /></br>  – Uncomplicated: sterile exudate; pH &gt; 7.2; glucose &gt; 60 → antibiotics only.<br /></br>  – Complicated: bacterial invasion; pH &lt; 7.2 or glucose &lt; 60 → fibrin deposition → must drain.<br /></br>  – Empyema: gross pus or positive culture → chest tube or surgical decortication.  </p></br><p>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Immediate: Tube thoracostomy (large-bore chest tube) + appropriate IV antibiotics.<br /></br>2. If loculated or fails to resolve → intrapleural tPA/DNase or video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS).<br /></br>3. Imaging (ultrasound or CT) is useful for guidance but should not delay drainage once criteria are met.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“pH = ‘P’ for Pull the tube: Pleural pH &lt; 7.2? Pull out a chest tube.”<br /></br>“Low Sugar, Low pH → L ower rib space with a tube.”  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. CT scan of the chest – Helpful for delineating loculations, but management criteria already fulfilled; delaying drainage increases morbidity.<br /></br>b. Intravenous furosemide therapy – Treats transudative effusions from CHF, cirrhosis, etc. Exudative chemistry &amp; recent pneumonia make this inappropriate.<br /></br>c. Intubation and mechanical ventilation – No respiratory failure; oxygen sat 94% on room air. Doesn’t address the infected pleural space.<br /></br>e. Pleurodesis – Used for recurrent malignant effusions or spontaneous pneumothorax prevention, not for acute empyema.  </p></br><p>Key takeaway: Post-pneumonia effusion with pH &lt; 7.2 or glucose &lt; 60 = complicated parapneumonic effusion → chest tube drainage plus antibiotics.</p>
12. A 45-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of mild pain and swelling of her right hand; the pain radiates to her shoulder. During this time, she also has had episodes of numbness and tingling of her right thumb and index and ring fingers, especially after prolonged typing. She has not sustained any recent injury. She is right-hand dominant. Her medications are insulin and ibuprofen. Vital signs are within normal limits. The neck is nontender, and range of motion is full. Spurling test is negative. Muscle strength is 5/5 in the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and subscapularis. Radial pulses are 2+ bilaterally. Motor and sensory testing of the radial, median, and ulnar nerves shows no abnormalities. There is no thenar atrophy. Phalen test is positive on the right and negative on the left. Impingement testing is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. MRI of the right shoulder  </br>b. Oral hydrocodone therapy  </br>c. Physical therapy  </br>d. Triamcinolone injection therapy  </br>e. Use of a computer keyboard with a wrist pad</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Use of a computer keyboard with a wrist pad (wrist-splint/ergonomic modification)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy</br>1. Key phrases that point to carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS):<br /></br>   • “Episodes of numbness and tingling of her right thumb and index and ring fingers” → median nerve sensory distribution.<br /></br>   • “Especially after prolonged typing” → repetitive wrist flexion/extension, a classic precipitant.<br /></br>   • Positive Phalen maneuver (90-sec forced wrist flexion elicits symptoms) → highly suggestive of CTS.<br /></br>   • Absence of thenar atrophy or motor deficit → early/mild disease.</p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent negatives that rule out mimics:<br /></br>   • Full cervical ROM, negative Spurling → unlikely cervical radiculopathy.<br /></br>   • Normal shoulder exam → improbable rotator cuff pathology.<br /></br>   • Normal radial/ulnar nerve testing → localizes to median nerve only.<br /></br>   • Radial pulses 2+ → excludes vascular entrapment (thoracic outlet).</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step 2, once classic CTS findings are present, focus on disease severity.  Mild = nocturnal/positional paresthesias only → start with conservative splint-based therapy before injections or surgery.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Reasoning for the Correct Choice</br>• First-line treatment for mild, early CTS is nocturnal neutral-position wrist splinting and ergonomic activity modification (e.g., padded keyboard, frequent breaks).<br /></br>• Splinting decreases median nerve compression during sleep and repetitive tasks, often relieving symptoms within weeks.<br /></br>• No radiologic imaging or opioids are indicated at this stage; steroid injection is reserved for persistent or moderate-to-severe disease or when conservative measures fail.</p></br><p>Take-home message: CTS management is stepwise—start conservative (wrist splint/ergonomics ± NSAIDs), escalate to steroid injection if necessary, and reserve surgical decompression for refractory or advanced cases with weakness/atrophy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review</br>• Carpal tunnel = fibro-osseous canal under the transverse carpal ligament.<br /></br>• Median nerve compression leads to paresthesias in palmar thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger; motor fibers to thenar muscles are affected later (thenar wasting/weakness).<br /></br>• Risk factors: Repetitive wrist motion, pregnancy, diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, obesity.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Pearls</br>• Diagnosis is clinical; nerve-conduction studies confirm when diagnosis is unclear or before surgery.<br /></br>• Treatment ladder:<br /></br>  1. Wrist splinting (especially at night) + NSAIDs + ergonomic modification.<br /></br>  2. Corticosteroid injection into carpal tunnel (triamcinolone, methylprednisolone) if step 1 fails or symptoms moderate.<br /></br>  3. Surgical decompression (open or endoscopic) for refractory pain, motor deficit, or thenar atrophy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</br>• “Splint THEN Steroid THEN Surgery” (order of increasing invasiveness).<br /></br>• Phalen = Flexion (P and F look similar) → symptoms Positive → think “Pad” (wrist pad) first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</br>a. MRI of the right shoulder – Indicated for rotator cuff tear; shoulder exam and impingement testing are normal.<br /></br>b. Oral hydrocodone – Opioids are not first-line for neuropathic pain and do nothing for the underlying entrapment.<br /></br>c. Physical therapy – Helpful for shoulder or tendon pathology; does not directly address median nerve compression.<br /></br>d. Triamcinolone injection therapy – Effective for CTS but used after failure of splint/ergonomic measures or for moderate/severe symptoms. Early mild disease should receive conservative therapy first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Documentation Reminder (real practice)</br>• Document distribution of sensory symptoms, provocative test results (Phalen/Tinel), absence of motor weakness, and that patient was counseled on wrist-splint use and ergonomic adjustments.</p>
13. A 70-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 1-hour history of severe right-sided chest pain, shortness of breath, and dizziness. During this time, she also has had several coughing episodes, one of which produced blood-tinged sputum. Eighteen hours ago, she returned home from a cross-country airplane trip to see her daughter and grandchildren. The patient has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. She follows a vegetarian diet, is physically fit, and exercises daily. She appears anxious. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 45 kg (100 lb); BMI is 17 kg/m2. Her pulse is 98/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 106/84 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 88 percent. Examination shows jugular venous distention. Breath sounds are normal. On cardiac examination, S2 is increased; no murmurs are heard. Examination of the left calf shows tenderness to palpation and a positive Homan sign. Which of the following is the strongest predisposing risk factor for this patient's in-hospital mortality?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Age</br>b. Blood pressure</br>c. Hemoptysis</br>d. Lower extremity examination findings</br>e. Weight</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per answer key): A. Age</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Classic PE presentation: acute pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, dizziness/syncope‐like feeling, hemoptysis, hypoxemia (SpO₂ 88%), tachycardia, loud P₂, jugular venous distention, recent long-haul flight, and ipsilateral calf tenderness/Homan sign (DVT source).<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: recent immobility, hemoptysis, hypoxia, loud S2, DVT findings.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no pneumonia findings, no wheezing (asthma/COPD), no fever (infection).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: When a stem gives you obvious PE findings, the question usually shifts to prognosis or next step rather than diagnosing PE.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>The stem asks: “Which is the strongest predisposing risk factor for this patient’s in-hospital mortality?”<br /></br>For PE, validated prognostic tools are the Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index (PESI) and simplified PESI (sPESI).<br /></br>• Original PESI: Age (points = patient’s age in years) is the single largest weighted contributor.<br /></br>• sPESI gives 1 point if age &gt;80 y, but age still remains the dominant driver in most datasets.<br /></br>Key concept: Older patients have markedly higher short-term mortality after acute PE because of poorer cardiopulmonary reserve and greater likelihood of decompensation.</p></br><p>Among the answer choices, no other variable carries as much prognostic weight as age. Her BP is mildly low-normal (106/84)—hypotension &lt;90 systolic would be concerning, but she is above that threshold. Hemoptysis predicts PE presence, not mortality. Clinical DVT findings increase the likelihood of PE but do not worsen prognosis once embolism has occurred. Low weight/BMI is not consistently linked with higher early mortality.</p></br><p>Take-home message: When asked about PE mortality risk, think “Age first, then hemodynamic instability (SBP &lt;90), hypoxia, comorbid cancer/heart failure.”</p></br><p>Documentation note: In a real chart you’d note her PESI class, hemodynamic status, and need for ICU vs ward management.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>1. Venous stasis (18-hour flight) → DVT in calf.<br /></br>2. DVT embolizes to pulmonary arteries → ventilation-perfusion mismatch, ↑ pulmonary vascular resistance → acute right-heart strain (JVD, loud P₂).<br /></br>3. Mortality largely from right-ventricular failure or recurrent emboli; advanced age reduces physiologic reserve to compensate.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Management (for context)</br>• Immediate: Anticoagulation with IV unfractionated heparin or LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) unless contraindicated.<br /></br>• If hemodynamically unstable: consider thrombolysis (alteplase) or surgical embolectomy.<br /></br>• Risk stratification with PESI guides ICU vs floor and outpatient possibilities.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid</br>“PESI A ges you”: Age drives PESI—just add the patient’s Age to remember the biggest score.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</br>B. Blood pressure – Prognostic only if SBP &lt;90 mm Hg (massive PE). 106/84 is stable.<br /></br>C. Hemoptysis – Helps clinch PE diagnosis but has minimal effect on short-term mortality statistics.<br /></br>D. Lower extremity findings – DVT confirmation; presence or absence does not change inpatient PE mortality.<br /></br>E. Weight – Low BMI may correlate with bleeding risk on anticoagulation but is not an established mortality predictor in acute PE.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Key Takeaway</br>In acute pulmonary embolism, advanced age is the single strongest independent predictor of in-hospital mortality on validated scoring systems; always factor age first when stratifying PE patients on exams and in practice.</p>
14. A 3-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. He is at the 50th percentile for height, weight, and head circumference. His appetite is good. His pulse is 85/min. His blood pressure is 140/75 mm Hg and 140/78 mm Hg in the right and left upper extremities, respectively. His blood pressure is 144/76 mm Hg and 145/79 mm Hg in the right and left lower extremities, respectively. Funduscopic, cardiopulmonary, and abdominal examinations show no other abnormalities. Results of a complete blood count; serum electrolyte, urea nitrogen, and creatinine concentrations; and urinalysis are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Intravenous pyelography</br>b. Measurement of serum B-type natriuretic peptide</br>c. Polysomnography</br>d. Renal Doppler ultrasonography</br>e. Thyroid function tests</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Renal Doppler ultrasonography  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Abnormal vital sign: Systolic BP ≈140 mm Hg in all four extremities → well above the 95th-percentile for a 3-year-old (Stage 2 pediatric hypertension).  </li></br><li>No arm-to-leg gradient → argues against coarctation of the aorta.  </li></br><li>Completely normal history, growth curves, physical exam, UA, creatinine, and electrolytes → makes essential (primary) HTN unlikely in a 3-year-old and rules out most renal-parenchymal disease (would usually show hematuria, proteinuria, elevated creatinine, or abnormal electrolytes).  </li></br><li>In children &lt;6 yr, persistent HTN = secondary until proved otherwise; renovascular disease (renal artery stenosis, fibromuscular dysplasia) is a top culprit.  </li></br><li>First-line, non-invasive screen for renovascular disease = renal (duplex) Doppler US.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime a young child (&lt;6 yr) has new HTN, think “find the secondary cause” → start with the kidneys (parenchyma and vessels).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Confirm HTN (measure BPs in both arms &amp; a leg).<br /></br>Step 2: Look for coarctation gradient; none present.<br /></br>Step 3: Order basic labs/UA. All normal → parenchymal disease less likely.<br /></br>Step 4: Screen renal vasculature non-invasively → Renal Doppler US.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a preschool child with unexplained HTN and normal labs, the next diagnostic step is imaging of renal blood flow with Doppler ultrasonography.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls: note percentile of BP readings, absence of gradient, normal labs, and plan for renal vascular imaging.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Pediatric secondary HTN etiologies<br /></br>  – Renal parenchymal (GN, reflux nephropathy, PKD)<br /></br>  – Renovascular (renal artery stenosis, fibromuscular dysplasia, neurofibromatosis)<br /></br>  – Endocrine (CAH, hyperthyroidism, pheochromocytoma, Cushing)<br /></br>  – Misc (obstructive sleep apnea, coarctation, medications)  </p></br><p>• Renovascular HTN mechanism: ↓ renal perfusion → ↑ renin → RAAS activation → systemic vasoconstriction + volume expansion → HTN.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics hierarchy for suspected renovascular HTN in kids:<br /></br>1. Duplex renal Doppler US (screen)<br /></br>2. If equivocal → CT or MR angiography<br /></br>3. Definitive / therapeutic → catheter-based angiography with possible angioplasty  </p></br><p>Treatment options:<br /></br>• Percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (fibromuscular dysplasia)<br /></br>• Surgical revascularization (complex lesions)<br /></br>• Medical control (ACEi/ARB, CCB) while awaiting intervention  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“HTN less than FIVE, the problem’s the KIDNEY DRIVE”<br /></br>(&lt;5 yr old with HTN → look at kidneys first; drive = blood flow → Doppler)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Intravenous pyelography<br /></br>   • Evaluates collecting system/obstruction, not vascular supply; rarely used today.  </p></br><p>B. Serum B-type natriuretic peptide<br /></br>   • Screens for heart failure; child has normal exam and no HF symptoms.  </p></br><p>C. Polysomnography<br /></br>   • Obstructive sleep apnea can cause HTN, but usually in older, obese children with snoring—none of which is present.  </p></br><p>E. Thyroid function tests<br /></br>   • Hyperthyroidism causes systolic HTN + tachycardia, weight loss, tremor; none of these signs here, and it is an uncommon cause in toddlers.  </p></br><p>Key discriminator: Only renal Doppler directly evaluates the most likely etiology—renovascular disease—in a 3-year-old with isolated, unexplained hypertension and normal labs.</p>
15. A 57-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by paramedics 15 minutes after she was found unresponsive on a park bench. She was examined in the same emergency department 2 weeks ago because of a 2-month history of episodic unilateral throbbing headaches that typically occurred on the right side of the head but intermittently occurred on the left. Examination at that time showed no abnormalities. She was diagnosed with migraine, received an intramuscular injection of ketorolac, and discharged with instructions to schedule a follow-up examination with her primary care physician. Review of her medical records from that examination shows a 6-month history of chronic back pain, 15-year history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, and 40-year history of post-traumatic complex partial seizures. Her other medications are oxycodone, lisinopril, metformin, and carbamazepine. On arrival, she is obtunded. Her pulse is 78/min, respirations are 6/min, and blood pressure is 160/92 mm Hg. On examination, the pupils are 1 mm and minimally reactive to light. There are intermittent, symmetrical, semipuposeful movements of all extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are 1+. Babinski sign is absent bilaterally. On ice-water caloric testing, the eyes move toward the ear in which the water is placed. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's decreased level of consciousness?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Chronic subdural hematoma</br>b. Conversion disorder</br>c. Hypertensive encephalopathy</br>d. Hypoglycemia</br>e. Opioid intoxication</br>f. Ruptured saccular aneurysm</br>g. Status epilepticus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E.
