Opioid intoxication  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Scene/Presentation<br /></br>   • Found unresponsive in public → acute alteration in mental status.<br /></br>   • Current vitals: RR 6/min (profound bradypnea), pinpoint pupils 1 mm, obtunded.<br /></br>   • Intermittent semipurposeful limb movements → “agonal” or hypoxic myoclonus, not true seizures.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Medication history<br /></br>   • Prescription oxycodone for chronic back pain ⇒ access to a potent µ-opioid agonist.<br /></br>   • No other drugs in her list cause miosis plus respiratory depression.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Bedside neuro‐examination clues<br /></br>   • Deep tendon reflexes 1+, Babinski absent → globally depressed but not localizing to corticospinal tract.<br /></br>   • Ice-water caloric test: eyes deviate toward the irrigated ear (normal vestibulo-ocular reflex) → intact brain-stem function; therefore the cause is diffuse/metabolic/toxic, not a focal structural lesion.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Key takeaway pattern: “Triad” of opioid overdose = CNS depression + respiratory depression + miosis. Low respiratory rate is the single best bedside predictor.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any unresponsive patient with RR &lt; 8/min and pinpoint pupils = empiric naloxone.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (stepwise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Assess ABCs: airway is at risk given RR 6/min.  </li></br><li>Pattern recognition: pinpoint pupils + RR 6/min → think opioids, pontine hemorrhage, or organophosphates.  </li></br><li>Pontine lesion would abolish caloric eye deviation; here reflex is intact → rules out pontine bleed.  </li></br><li>Organophosphate exposure would add salivation, lacrimation, diaphoresis, bronchorrhea (“SLUDGE”)—absent.  </li></br><li>Only opioid intoxication cleanly fits all findings and her medication list → choice E.<br /></br>Documentation if real patient: mental status, vitals, pupils, respiratory rate, bedside neuro exam, medication list, naloxone response.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Core concept: Opioids suppress the medullary respiratory center (↑CO₂ threshold, ↓resp drive) and stimulate the Edinger-Westphal nucleus causing miosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Oxycodone binds µ-opioid receptors (Gi-protein-coupled) in brainstem and peripheral tissues.<br /></br>• Results:<br /></br>  – Inhibition of adenylate cyclase → ↓cAMP.<br /></br>  – Opens K⁺ channels, closes Ca²⁺ channels → neuronal hyperpolarization.<br /></br>  – Net effect: ↓respiratory center sensitivity to CO₂, producing hypoventilation and hypercapnia.<br /></br>• Miosis: µ-receptor activation in parasympathetic pathways of CN III → constricted pupils.<br /></br>• CNS depression: diffuse cortical suppression → obtundation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Immediate:<br /></br>1. Support airway, provide supplemental O₂, prepare to intubate if naloxone ineffective.<br /></br>2. Naloxone 0.04–0.4 mg IV push; repeat and titrate to RR ≥ 12/min.<br /></br>3. Monitor for acute withdrawal (hypertension, tachycardia, vomiting, pain).  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Point-of-care glucose (always in altered mental status).<br /></br>• ABG → hypercapnic respiratory acidosis likely.<br /></br>• Toxicology screen (qualitative confirmation).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Pinpoint pupils pick opioids.”<br /></br>“O-Pi-OiD triad = Out cold, Pinpoint, Inadequate breaths.”  </p></br><p>Visual memory palace: Imagine a small “pin” going through the pupil of a statue (pinpoint), the statue holding its breath (bradypnea), standing next to a giant prescription bottle labeled “OXY.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Chronic subdural hematoma<br /></br>• Presents with progressive headache, focal deficits, personality change weeks after mild trauma; pupils usually normal or asymmetric, respiration not markedly depressed.  </p></br><p>B. Conversion disorder<br /></br>• Psychiatric diagnosis of neurologic deficits incompatible with disease; pupillary reflexes and respiratory drive remain normal, caloric testing normal; requires evidence of psychological stressor, not present here.  </p></br><p>C. Hypertensive encephalopathy<br /></br>• Requires malignant BP (often &gt;180/120 with end-organ signs), presents with diffuse cerebral edema → headache, visual changes, seizures. Pupils normal, RR normal/high due to distress, brain-stem reflexes intact.  </p></br><p>D. Hypoglycemia<br /></br>• Can cause obtundation or seizures, but pupils normal, respirations not specifically depressed, and would not explain pinpoint pupils. Always check glucose, but triad favors opioids.  </p></br><p>F. Ruptured saccular aneurysm (subarachnoid hemorrhage)<br /></br>• “Worst headache of life,” sudden, often with vomiting, nuchal rigidity, photophobia; may have blown (dilated) pupil from CN III palsy, not pinpoint. Rapid loss of consciousness but typically RR not 6/min.  </p></br><p>G. Status epilepticus<br /></br>• Requires continuous seizure activity ≥5 min. Here movements are intermittent and semipurposeful; postictal state may cause altered consciousness but does not produce pinpoint pupils and profound respiratory depression.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Mu-opioid overdose classically produces pinpoint pupils and life-threatening respiratory depression with preserved brainstem reflexes. Recognize the triad, give naloxone, secure the airway.</p>
16. A 51-year-old man, hospitalized for management of a proximal jejunal enterocutaneous fistula, has a 2-day history of fever and malaise. Four weeks ago, he underwent laparotomy for a gunshot wound to the abdomen; the fistula was noted 1 week after the operation. Since undergoing the laparotomy, he has received total parenteral nutrition through a left subclavian central catheter. During the past 2 weeks, he has participated in physical therapy; until today, he was able to ambulate 300 ft and walk up two flights of stairs without shortness of breath. He has no other history of serious illness. His medications are somatostatin and morphine. His temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 105/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96 percent. Examination shows the fistula opening with draining enteric contents. An ostomy pouching system is placed, and the surrounding skin is well protected. Fistula output is 400 mL daily. Three days ago, his leukocyte count was 8000/mm3; today, it is 14,000/mm3. Serum studies today show an albumin concentration of 2.7 g/dL and prealbumin concentration of 19 mg/dL (N=19.5–35.8). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Blood cultures from the catheter site</br>b. Chest x-ray</br>c. CT scan of the abdomen</br>d. Exploratory laparotomy and resection of the fistula</br>e. Intravenous antifungal therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Blood cultures from the catheter site  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Timeline &amp; Setting<br /></br>   • Hospital day ≈ 28 after laparotomy; central venous catheter (CVC) has been in place the entire time for total parenteral nutrition (TPN).<br /></br>   • Fever (38.4 °C), tachycardia (105/min), leukocytosis (8 → 14 K) arose acutely over 48 h.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>High-yield risk clues<br /></br>   • “TPN through a left subclavian central catheter” for 4 wks.<br /></br>   • CVC + TPN is one of the highest-risk combinations for catheter-associated bloodstream infection (CRBSI), especially with gram-positive skin flora and Candida species.<br /></br>   • No abdominal exam abnormalities, fistula skin intact, fistula output stable—argues against new intra-abdominal sepsis.<br /></br>   • Normal SpO₂, no pulmonary complaints—pneumonia unlikely.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No cough, dyspnea, or hypoxia → lowers pre-test probability of pneumonia (CXR).<br /></br>   • Abdomen not tender or distended; fistula drainage unchanged → lowers probability of abscess requiring CT or laparotomy.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive &amp; metacognitive strategy:<br /></br>Whenever a hospitalized patient with a long-standing CVC + TPN develops unexplained fever/leukocytosis, think “catheter sepsis until proven otherwise.” First diagnostic step: obtain blood cultures (≥ 2 sets—one from catheter lumen, one peripherally).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Recognize fever in a patient with an indwelling device.<br /></br>Step 2: Identify the most common &amp; most easily sampled source (the CVC).<br /></br>Step 3: Before empiric therapy or imaging, obtain cultures to confirm source and guide therapy.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In suspected CRBSI, draw blood cultures (catheter + peripheral) promptly; then start empiric IV antibiotics ± catheter removal once cultures are sent.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• New fever/leukocytosis, suspected line infection.<br /></br>• Blood cultures ordered, plan for empiric vancomycin + gram-negative coverage after cultures drawn; discuss possible line removal pending results.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• CVC provides direct access for skin flora (Staph epidermidis, S. aureus) and, with TPN, for Candida species to colonize and seed the bloodstream.<br /></br>• Biofilm formation on catheter surfaces protects organisms from host immunity and antibiotics, leading to persistent bacteremia unless the catheter is removed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Highlights  </p></br><p>First-line diagnostic test<br /></br>• Simultaneous blood cultures: one from each catheter lumen + one peripheral.<br /></br>   – Differential time to positivity (&gt;2 h earlier in catheter sample) strongly suggests CRBSI.</p></br><p>Empiric treatment (after cultures drawn)<br /></br>• Vancomycin + cefepime or meropenem (institutional protocol) to cover MRSA &amp; gram-negatives.<br /></br>• Add an echinocandin (e.g., caspofungin) if TPN, neutropenia, or Candida on Gram stain—but only after cultures obtained.<br /></br>• Remove catheter if: severe sepsis, hemodynamic instability, S. aureus, Pseudomonas, Candida, or persistent bacteremia &gt;72 h.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Fever + Line = Draw before Drugs”<br /></br>• Any Unexplained Fever in pt with LINE → draw Blood cultures (catheter + peripheral) before antibiotics.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Chest x-ray<br /></br>   • Indicated for respiratory symptoms or abnormal oxygenation. Patient is oxygenating well, no cough, no chest findings.</p></br><p>C. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>   • Used to look for abscess or intra-abdominal source. Fistula site clean, abdominal exam benign, output stable; low pre-test probability.</p></br><p>D. Exploratory laparotomy and resection of the fistula<br /></br>   • Major surgery reserved for high-output (&gt;500 mL/d), refractory fistulas, or uncontrolled sepsis from abdominal source. No evidence surgical source here.</p></br><p>E. Intravenous antifungal therapy<br /></br>   • Candida is possible in TPN patients, but empiric antifungal is started only after blood cultures are drawn and initial Gram stain/culture suggest yeast or patient is severely septic. Cultures first.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a hospitalized patient with a long-standing TPN central line who develops fever/leukocytosis, the immediate next step is to obtain blood cultures from the catheter (and peripherally) to evaluate for catheter-related bloodstream infection.</p>
17. A study is conducted to investigate the attitude of patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus toward controlling their disease with lifestyle modifications, including diet and exercise. A total of 20 men and women aged 55 to 70 years are randomly selected from an outpatient diabetes clinic and are enrolled in the study. The male-to-female ratio among the patients is 1:1, and they are matched by age and BMI. Patients are assigned to groups led by a moderator who uses a standard set of scenarios to prompt discussion. The discussions are video recorded and reviewed to examine the patterns of the patients' attitudes toward lifestyle modification in order to improve control of their disease. Which of the following best describes this study design?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Case report</br>b. Case series</br>c. Matched case-control</br>d. Qualitative</br>e. Randomized trial</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Qualitative (focus-group study)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “attitude … toward controlling their disease,” “moderator,” “standard set of scenarios to prompt discussion,” “discussions are video-recorded and reviewed to examine patterns.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives:<br /></br>  – Small, purposely selected sample (20 pts)<br /></br>  – Data are words/ideas, not numbers (attitudes, patterns of discussion)<br /></br>  – Use of a moderator and recorded group discussion = classic focus-group methodology.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No intervention is being tested, no comparison of exposed vs unexposed, no outcome rates, no blinding, and no statistical end point—all rule out interventional or analytical quantitative designs.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, when you see “focus group,” “interview,” or “themes/attitudes,” immediately think “qualitative study.” If the primary data are quotations or perceptions rather than lab values or event rates, it is qualitative.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify unit of analysis → patient opinions, not physiologic measurements.<br /></br>2. Identify data collection method → moderated discussions.<br /></br>3. Determine intent → explore themes, not quantify risk or efficacy.<br /></br>4. Match to study design taxonomy → qualitative research (specifically, a focus-group study).</p></br><p>Take-home message: Qualitative studies explore “how” and “why” questions (attitudes, beliefs, decision-making) using interviews, focus groups, and thematic analysis.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real IRB protocol you would note the sampling strategy, informed-consent procedure for recording, and analytic approach (e.g., grounded theory, thematic coding).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) control benefits greatly from lifestyle modification, but adherence is limited by patient perceptions, beliefs, and social context—precisely what qualitative research is designed to elucidate.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review (not directly tested)<br /></br>• Lifestyle (diet, exercise, weight loss) is first-line therapy for T2DM and improves insulin sensitivity.<br /></br>• Understanding patient barriers (cultural diet, financial limitations, motivation) guides tailored interventions.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“FOCUS on Feelings” → Focus group = Qualitative study of Feelings &amp; attitudes.<br /></br>Quantitative counts; Qualitative quotes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Case report – describes a single patient with unusual presentation/outcome; no group discussion or moderator.<br /></br>B. Case series – descriptive report of several patients with similar condition; still no systematic discussion or analysis of attitudes.<br /></br>C. Matched case-control – starts with outcome (cases vs controls) and looks back for exposures; requires matching by variable(s) and statistical comparison—none of which is present.<br /></br>E. Randomized trial – participants randomly assigned to different interventions to measure outcomes; here, there is no intervention or outcome measure, only discussion.</p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only qualitative design gathers non-numeric, narrative data to understand perceptions, making answer D the best fit.</p>
18. A 42-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance 20 minutes after sustaining a stab wound to his right anterior chest. On arrival, the patient is uncooperative, refusing to lie flat on the gurney. Medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He says he drank six 12-oz beers and used cocaine today. Vital signs are pulse 130/min, respirations 28/min, and blood pressure 66/40 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. The patient appears anxious. Physical examination discloses a single stab wound in the medial right anterior chest, 3 cm below the right nipple. The trachea is midline. Breath sounds are present bilaterally. Cardiac examination discloses distant but normal S1 and S2. Abdominal examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. CT scan of the chest</br>c. Endotracheal intubation</br>d. Focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST)</br>e. Placement of a right chest tube</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases/clues:<br /></br>• “Stab wound … medial right anterior chest, 3 cm below the right nipple” – classic anatomic location overlying the right ventricle/pericardium.<br /></br>• Profound hypotension (66/40 mm Hg) and tachycardia (130/min) – patient is in hemorrhagic/obstructive shock.<br /></br>• Breath sounds present bilaterally, trachea midline, O2 sat 99 % – makes tension pneumothorax or massive hemothorax unlikely.<br /></br>• “Distant but normal S1 and S2” – muffled heart sounds; one element of Beck’s triad for cardiac tamponade (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, ↑ JVP—JVP hard to assess when patient upright).<br /></br>These findings should immediately raise concern for penetrating cardiac injury with pericardial tamponade.</p></br><p>Test-taking strategy:<br /></br>1. In any unstable trauma patient (SBP &lt; 90 mm Hg) ask: “Is there an immediately reversible cause of shock that can be found bedside?”<br /></br>2. Use the quickest, least invasive modality that can yield life-saving information without delaying definitive care. FAST fulfills this requirement.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Airway: Patient is speaking, SpO₂ 99 % → airway is patent; intubation is not urgently required and induction drugs may worsen hypotension.<br /></br>2. Breathing: Breath sounds bilaterally, no tracheal deviation → tension pneumo unlikely; no need for emergent chest tube.<br /></br>3. Circulation: Profound hypotension; likely obstructive (tamponade) vs hemorrhagic. Penetrating chest wound over heart + muffled sounds → tamponade most probable.<br /></br>4. Diagnostic confirmation in an unstable patient → Perform a FAST exam (specifically the pericardial window).<br /></br>5. If FAST shows pericardial fluid, go directly to emergent thoracotomy or pericardiocentesis.</p></br><p>Take-home point: In hemodynamically unstable trauma patients, a bedside FAST is the quickest way to diagnose pericardial tamponade or intra-abdominal hemorrhage and guides immediate operative intervention.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Record mechanism of injury, vitals, primary survey findings, indication for FAST, and results (e.g., “Pericardial strip of fluid positive → emergent OR for thoracotomy”).</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Penetrating cardiac tamponade<br /></br>• Etiology: laceration of coronary or myocardial vessel → blood accumulates in pericardial sac.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis: Rising pericardial pressure equals diastolic pressure → impaired ventricular filling → ↓ stroke volume &amp; BP.<br /></br>• Classic triad (Beck): Hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD (can be absent if patient is hypovolemic or sitting upright).<br /></br>• Electrical alternans on ECG and enlarged cardiac silhouette on CXR appear later—waiting for these can be fatal.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Bedside test: FAST (subxiphoid or parasternal window) – &gt;95 % sensitive for ≥100 mL pericardial blood.  </p></br><p>Definitive treatment (trauma algorithm):<br /></br>• Positive pericardial FAST + hemodynamic instability → emergent median sternotomy or ED thoracotomy.<br /></br>• If OR delayed, temporize with pericardiocentesis.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“FAST answers in FAST trauma”<br /></br>F – Fluid (pericardial or peritoneal)<br /></br>A – Assess<br /></br>S – Seconds (takes &lt;30 s per window)<br /></br>T – Tamponade (must not be missed)</p></br><p>Visual aid: Picture the right nipple (5th ICS MCL) sitting over the right ventricle—any knife tip there can hit the heart.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Chest X-ray<br /></br>• Too slow for a crashing patient; may miss tamponade; normal CXR does not rule it out.</p></br><p>b. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>• Requires hemodynamic stability and transport out of trauma bay; contraindicated with SBP 66 mm Hg.</p></br><p>c. Endotracheal intubation<br /></br>• Airway intact. Induction agents (propofol, etomidate ± positive-pressure ventilation) can worsen hypotension and obscure clinical signs; not first priority.</p></br><p>e. Placement of a right chest tube<br /></br>• Indicated for hemothorax or pneumothorax. Bilateral breath sounds and midline trachea argue against these. Unnecessary tubes waste precious minutes and do not address tamponade.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In unstable patients with penetrating chest trauma and signs of tamponade, the immediate bedside test of choice is a FAST examination; a positive result mandates emergent surgical drainage.</p>
19. A 37-year-old man comes to the clinic at the urging of his dentist 1 day after he was noted to have a blood pressure of 175/97 mm Hg following a dental extraction. The patient's blood pressure was normal prior to the procedure. The patient reports pain at the site of extraction but does not have headache, change in vision, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. His father has hypertension and sustained a myocardial infarction at age 45 years. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years. He drinks alcoholic beverages socially but does not use illicit drugs. He is 185 cm (6 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 79 kg (175 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Blood pressure now is 133/74 mm Hg; remaining vital signs are normal. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Determination of hemoglobin A1c</br>b. Exercise stress testing</br>c. Measurement of urine microalbumin concentration</br>d. Serum lipid studies</br>e. No further evaluation is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Serum lipid studies  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key clues:<br /></br>• 37-year-old man, asymptomatic at the visit → routine preventive care question, not an acute problem.<br /></br>• One isolated BP of 175/97 mm Hg after dental extraction, but repeat office BP = 133/74 mm Hg → not true hypertension.<br /></br>• Major cardiovascular (CV) risk factors present: father had an MI at 45 yr (premature coronary artery disease), patient is a current smoker (15-pack-year history).<br /></br>• Normal BMI, no diabetes history.<br /></br>The stem is therefore asking: “Given this patient’s profile, what screening test should be done now?”  </p></br><p>Test-taking strategy: when you see a healthy adult with CV risk factors, always think of lipid panel first unless something in the vignette explicitly directs you elsewhere (eg, diabetes symptoms for A1c, albuminuria for diabetes management, exertional chest pain for stress test).  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Confirm he does not need further BP work-up → repeated reading is normal; guidelines require ≥2 elevated readings on ≥2 different occasions.<br /></br>2. Identify modifiable CVD risk factors → positive FH of premature CAD + smoking.<br /></br>3. Age 20–39 with risk factors warrants fasting lipid panel (ACC/AHA &amp; USPSTF: “screen every 4–6 y starting at age 20; more frequently if high-risk”).<br /></br>Take-home message: In any adult ≥20 y, especially with ≥1 major CV risk factor, obtain baseline fasting lipid profile to calculate 10-year ASCVD risk and guide statin counseling.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Elevated LDL cholesterol accelerates atherosclerotic plaque formation. Smoking and a positive family history markedly raise lifetime ASCVD risk, so early identification and treatment of dyslipidemia (lifestyle ± statin) is the single most effective preventive strategy.  </p></br><p>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>• Test: Fasting (or non-fasting) total cholesterol, HDL-C, calculated LDL-C, ± triglycerides.<br /></br>• Management based on results:<br /></br>  – LDL ≥190 mg/dL → high-intensity statin.<br /></br>  – LDL 70–189 mg/dL + elevated 10-year ASCVD risk (≥7.5 %) → mod- to high-intensity statin.<br /></br>  – Counsel on smoking cessation, diet (Mediterranean/DASH), exercise.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“SCREEN at TWENTY”: Starting age 20, every 4-6 years, sooner if “SINS” present – Smoking, Immediate family MI &lt;55 men/&lt;65 women, New diabetes, Systolic BP ↑.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Hemoglobin A1c – USPSTF screens adults 40–70 y who are overweight/obese, or any age with symptoms of hyperglycemia. This patient is 37 y with normal BMI and no symptoms; not first-line.<br /></br>B. Exercise stress testing – Reserved for typical/atypical angina, abnormal resting ECG, or high ASCVD risk ≥40 y. Asymptomatic 37-y-old does not meet criteria; test has low positive predictive value here.<br /></br>C. Urine microalbumin – Used to detect early diabetic nephropathy or nephropathy in long-standing hypertension; neither applies.<br /></br>E. No further evaluation – Misses guideline-recommended lipid screening in a high-risk young adult.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: Young adults with any significant cardiovascular risk factors should receive serum lipid studies to guide primary prevention early—before clinical disease develops.</p>
20. A 3-day-old male newborn is evaluated in the hospital nursery because of a 2-hour history of episodes of vomiting green material. He was well prior to the onset of these episodes. He was born at 41 weeks' gestation following an uncomplicated pregnancy and cesarean delivery because of failure to progress. He appears listless. He is 50 cm (19.7 in; 50th percentile) long and weighs 3628 g (8 lb; 50th percentile). His temperature is 36.7°C (98.0°F), pulse is 170/min, respirations are 50/min, and blood pressure is 70/40 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds. The abdomen is nondistended, and the patient cries when it is palpated. The remainder of the examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is most likely to establish the diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Barium enema</br>b. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br>c. MRI of the abdomen</br>d. Upper gastrointestinal series</br>e. X-ray of the abdomen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Upper gastrointestinal (UGI) series  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that should immediately raise concern:<br /></br>   • “3-day-old” + “vomiting green material” → bilious emesis in a neonate = <strong>surgical emergency until proven otherwise</strong>.<br /></br>   • Previously “well” and term infant (41 wk) → rules out prematurity-related NEC; sudden onset favors volvulus rather than congenital atresia (which usually presents in the first few hours).<br /></br>   • Abdomen “nondistended” yet painful (cries on palpation) → early obstruction before bowel dilates; also typical for volvulus where obstruction is proximal.<br /></br>   • Normal oxygen saturation, acceptable perfusion (CR &lt;2 s) → no advanced shock yet but can deteriorate quickly.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>   • Step 1: Bilious vomit in neonate ➜ think malrotation ± midgut volvulus first.<br /></br>   • Step 2: Ask, “What imaging definitively shows the position of the duodenojejunal (DJ) junction?” ➜ UGI series.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Act fast—time to bowel necrosis can be hours.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Core concept: Malrotation with (or prior to) volvulus is the most common cause of sudden bilious vomiting in a healthy term neonate.<br /></br>• Definitive diagnosis hinges on locating the DJ flexure; if it sits to the right of the spine or the duodenum takes a “corkscrew” path, malrotation/volvulus is confirmed. Only a contrast UGI series reliably demonstrates this.<br /></br>• Documentation note (real patient): “Bilious emesis ×2 h, suspect malrotation/volvulus. Ordered STAT UGI contrast study; pediatric surgery paged emergently.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Normal embryology: Midgut rotates 270° counter-clockwise around the superior mesenteric artery (SMA).<br /></br>• Malrotation: Incomplete rotation → narrow mesenteric base, abnormal peritoneal (Ladd) bands across the duodenum.<br /></br>• Volvulus: Midgut twists around the SMA → acute obstruction + vascular compromise → ischemia, necrosis, perforation.<br /></br>• Presentation: Sudden bilious vomiting, abdominal pain/irritability, possible hematemesis or blood-tinged stool if ischemic.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostic hierarchy in bilious vomiting neonate:<br /></br>1. Initial plain abdominal X-ray (look for free air, double bubble, pneumatosis).<br /></br>2. If no perforation, <strong>urgent UGI series</strong> (water-soluble contrast) is the gold standard.<br /></br>   • Classic findings: DJ junction right of midline; “corkscrew” or “beak” sign.<br /></br>3. Once diagnosed ➜ emergent Ladd procedure + appendectomy; IV fluids, NG decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics pre-op.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Green vomit at THREE days, think THREE-quarter turn gone wrong (270°) — get the UGI!”  </p></br><p>OR  </p></br><p>“Bilious = ‘Bili-us’ … ‘Beeline’ to UGI.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Barium enema<br /></br>   • Detects Hirschsprung disease (transition zone) or meconium ileus (microcolon). Would not reliably visualize the DJ junction or volvulus.  </p></br><p>b. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)<br /></br>   • Visualizes mucosa; cannot assess bowel rotation or distal obstruction and carries perforation risk in neonates.  </p></br><p>c. MRI of the abdomen<br /></br>   • Excellent anatomy but impractical emergently (time-consuming, sedation) and not first-line.  </p></br><p>e. Abdominal X-ray<br /></br>   • Good initial screen (rules out perforation, may show double bubble) but findings in malrotation can be completely normal or nonspecific; therefore NOT the test that “establishes” the diagnosis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In a neonate with new-onset bilious vomiting, assume malrotation ± volvulus until proven otherwise. The most direct, rapid, and accurate way to establish the diagnosis is an urgent upper GI contrast series.</p>
21. A 66-year-old man comes to the office because of a 2-month history of right knee pain. He reports that the pain occurs for 5 to 10 minutes when he gets up in the morning, or after he walks for several blocks. He says his knee has not "given way" or "locked up." Acetaminophen has not provided relief of the pain. He reports no recent trauma to the knee but fell on it after tripping over a step 1 year ago. Medical history also is remarkable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are lisinopril and atorvastatin. He is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Blood pressure is 136/82 mm Hg. Examination of the lower extremities discloses normal flexion in both knees but decreased hyperextension in the right knee compared with the left knee. There is moderate medial joint line tenderness and a mild effusion of the right knee. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection to the right knee</br>b. Muscle-strengthening exercises targeting the right quadriceps</br>c. Replacement of acetaminophen with hydrocodone-acetaminophen</br>d. Use of orthotic shoe inserts</br>e. Weight loss</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>b. Muscle-strengthening exercises targeting the right quadriceps</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Key clues</br>   • Age 66 years – classic demographic for osteoarthritis (OA).<br /></br>   • Pain after activity (“after he walks several blocks”) and very brief morning stiffness (5-10 min) – fits OA, not inflammatory arthritis (&gt;30 min stiffness).<br /></br>   • No locking or giving-way – argues against meniscal tear or ligamentous injury.<br /></br>   • Exam: mild effusion, medial joint-line tenderness, loss of terminal extension → typical medial-compartment OA.<br /></br>   • BMI 21 kg/m² – normal; weight loss not a therapeutic target.</p></br><ol></br><li>Cognitive approach</br>   • Step 1: Identify degenerative rather than inflammatory pathology (short stiffness, activity-related pain).<br /></br>   • Step 2: Recall OA treatment ladder: (a) non-pharmacologic (exercise/strengthen, weight control) → (b) topical/oral NSAIDs or acetaminophen → (c) intra-articular steroid, viscosupplement, orthotics → (d) surgery.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Patient has attempted only acetaminophen; next guideline-directed move is therapeutic exercise.</li></br></ol></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Quadriceps weakness allows greater medial-compartment load and worsens knee OA pain. Targeted strengthening (straight-leg raises, seated knee extensions, mini-squats, cycling, aquatic therapy) consistently decreases pain and improves function, and society guidelines (AAOS, ACR, ACP) list it as first-line therapy.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>Osteoarthritis = progressive articular cartilage degeneration → subchondral bone remodeling and osteophytes. Medial compartment commonly bears highest load, so malalignment or weak quadriceps ↑ medial stress → pain, brief stiffness, crepitus, small effusions.</p></br><p>Treatments and Diagnostics</br>First-line</br>• Exercise: quadriceps and hip abductor strengthening, low-impact aerobic activity.<br /></br>• Weight loss if BMI ≥25 kg/m².<br /></br>• Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) or oral NSAIDs if no contraindication.<br /></br>Second-line</br>• Intra-articular corticosteroid (triamcinolone) for flares or persistent symptoms.<br /></br>• Orthotics/unloader braces if varus/valgus alignment.<br /></br>Ultimate</br>• Total knee arthroplasty for end-stage disease.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“OA gets better when the QUAD is on the JOB.”<br /></br>Visualize the quadriceps muscle acting like a shock-absorber protecting medial cartilage.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection – Effective for flare pain but reserved after failure of non-pharmacologic ± NSAIDs; unnecessary early step here.</p></br><p>c. Hydrocodone-acetaminophen – Opioids are last-ditch options for severe, refractory OA or surgical non-candidates; risk &gt; benefit this early.</p></br><p>d. Orthotic shoe inserts – Can off-load medial compartment in varus knees, but evidence weaker than exercise; used as adjunct, not sole initial therapy.</p></br><p>e. Weight loss – Crucial when BMI ≥25 kg/m²; patient is normal weight (BMI 21), so no meaningful benefit.</p></br><p>Take-Home Message</br>In knee osteoarthritis, always start with non-pharmacologic therapy—especially quadriceps-strengthening exercises—before escalating to injections, opioids, or surgery.</p>
22. A 23-year-old man who is a US Army veteran comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of depressed mood; difficulty concentrating; decreased appetite; and decreased interest in his usual activities, such as exercising and spending time with friends. He also has had difficulty sleeping during this period, including being unable to fall asleep for 2 hours and then waking up "for no reason" in the middle of the night; he does not feel rested in the morning. He says that he sometimes thinks he would be better off dead, but he does not have any plans to harm himself. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. He asks for treatment with a medication that will not interfere with his sexual function. Which of the following medications is most appropriate for this patient at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Fluoxetine</br>b. Mirtazapine</br>c. Nortriptyline</br>d. Paroxetine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Mirtazapine  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• 2-month history of ≥5 SIGECAPS symptoms: depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia (initial + middle), impaired concentration, ↓ appetite, passive suicidal ideation → meets DSM-5 criteria for major depressive disorder (MDD).<br /></br>• Patient specifically requests “a medication that will not interfere with his sexual function.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives for choosing mirtazapine: insomnia and poor appetite/weight loss (mirtazapine is sedating and appetite-stimulating).<br /></br>• Pertinent negative: no cardiac history (important when considering TCAs).  </p></br><p>Test-taking strategy<br /></br>1. Identify disorder (MDD).<br /></br>2. Note patient-directed constraint (avoid sexual dysfunction).<br /></br>3. Match pharmacology side-effect profiles to patient’s needs.<br /></br>4. Eliminate distractors that worsen the patient’s concerns.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why mirtazapine?<br /></br>• Mechanism: central α2-autoreceptor antagonist → ↑ norepinephrine &amp; serotonin; 5-HT2/5-HT3 and H1 blockade.<br /></br>• Sexual function: minimal 5-HT2 stimulation → very low rate of sexual dysfunction.<br /></br>• Helpful side effects: H1 blockade → sedation (good for sleep-initiation and maintenance insomnia); 5-HT2C blockade → ↑ appetite/weight gain (offsets his decreased appetite).<br /></br>• Monitoring: weight/BMI, fasting lipids; generally no EKG needed in a healthy young adult.  </p></br><p>Document in the chart: MDD diagnosis, passive SI without plan (no emergent safety issue), rationale for choosing mirtazapine, counseling on sedation/weight gain, follow-up plan in 2–4 weeks, hotline info.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: When treating MDD in a patient who must avoid SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction—especially when insomnia or weight loss are present—mirtazapine is first-line.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>MDD<br /></br>• Etiology: dysregulation of monoamines (serotonin, NE, DA) ± HPA-axis hyperactivity.<br /></br>• SIGECAPS mnemonic: Sleep change, Interest ↓, Guilt, Energy ↓, Concentration ↓, Appetite/weight change, Psychomotor change, Suicidal thoughts (≥5 for ≥2 wk).<br /></br>• Sexual dysfunction with SSRIs/SNRIs results from excessive serotonin at 5-HT2A receptors decreasing dopaminergic tone in mesolimbic pathways.  </p></br><p>Mirtazapine<br /></br>• α2-antagonism → disinhibits NE &amp; 5-HT release.<br /></br>• 5-HT2/3 blockade → ↓ anxiety, nausea, sexual dysfunction.<br /></br>• H1 blockade → sedation, weight gain.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>First-line pharmacotherapy for MDD: SSRI, SNRI, bupropion, mirtazapine.<br /></br>• If insomnia, weight loss, or sexual side-effect concern → mirtazapine.<br /></br>• If hypersomnia or weight gain → avoid mirtazapine; consider bupropion (but bupropion can worsen anxiety/insomnia).<br /></br>• Always screen for suicidality; passive SI without plan allows outpatient treatment with close follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MIRTA-zzz-apine”:<br /></br>• “zzz” = sedation<br /></br>• “a-pine” = you’ll pine for more food (weight gain)<br /></br>• “zzz &amp; pine but keep the shine” = helps sleep/appetite while sexual function still “shines.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Fluoxetine (SSRI)<br /></br>• Commonly causes delayed ejaculation, anorgasmia, ↓ libido.<br /></br>• Activating—may worsen his insomnia.  </p></br><p>c. Nortriptyline (TCA)<br /></br>• Anticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention), antihistamine (sedation, weight gain), α-blockade (orthostasis).<br /></br>• Sexual dysfunction less than SSRIs but cardiotoxic (QT prolongation, lethal in overdose)—avoid in young healthy patient when safer options exist.  </p></br><p>d. Paroxetine (SSRI)<br /></br>• Highest sexual-side-effect burden among SSRIs; also anticholinergic and causes weight gain.<br /></br>• Short half-life → bad discontinuation syndrome.  </p></br><p>These options do not meet the patient’s priority (preserve sexual function) or have unfavorable risk profiles compared with mirtazapine.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>In summary, mirtazapine aligns with the patient’s clinical picture (MDD with insomnia and ↓ appetite) and his explicit request to avoid sexual side effects, making it the most appropriate first-line agent.</p>
23. A 23-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 1-week history of moderate abdominal cramps and bloody diarrhea. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. There is no family history of gastrointestinal disease. The patient has not traveled recently or had sick contacts. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F). While lying flat, pulse is 110/min and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg; while standing, pulse is 120/min and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. The abdomen is mildly distended and soft; there is mild, diffuse tenderness to palpation. Hematocrit is 36 percent, and leukocyte count is 13,000/mm3. Intravenous fluid therapy is begun. Stool culture results are negative. Colonoscopy shows inflamed mucosa and erythematous and raised mucosa over the rectum and sigmoid. The remainder of the colon appears normal. Colonic biopsy specimen shows crypt branching and abscesses. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Hydrocortisone therapy</br>b. Infliximab therapy</br>c. Mercaptopurine therapy</br>d. Methotrexate therapy</br>e. No pharmacotherapy is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>a. Hydrocortisone therapy (topical or systemic corticosteroid for an acute moderate flare of ulcerative colitis)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues</br>   • “Bloody diarrhea” + “abdominal cramps” → inflammatory colitis rather than IBS or infection<br /></br>   • Colonoscopy: rectum &amp; sigmoid inflamed, rest normal → continuous, distal disease = classic for ulcerative colitis (UC)<br /></br>   • Biopsy: “crypt branching and crypt abscesses” → pathognomonic histology for UC<br /></br>   • 1-week history, orthostatic vitals, leukocytosis → active flare of at least moderate severity<br /></br>   • Stool cultures negative → rules out infectious colitis</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives</br>   • Rectal involvement (always present in UC)<br /></br>   • Continuous pattern limited to colon<br /></br>   • Systemic signs (tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension)</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives</br>   • No travel, no sick contacts, temp only 37.2 °C → decreases likelihood of infectious colitis<br /></br>   • No family history ≠ protective; UC still possible</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step 2 questions love the “scope shows rectal involvement + crypt abscesses” → immediately think ulcerative colitis. Next, categorize severity (mild, moderate, severe, fulminant) and location (proctitis, left-sided, pancolitis) to choose therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step-wise approach to UC flare:</br>1. Confirm UC diagnosis (scope + biopsy).<br /></br>2. Assess severity:<br /></br>   • Mild–mod: &lt;6 stools/day, minimal systemic signs<br /></br>   • Moderate: ≥6 stools/day, mild anemia, low-grade fever, tachycardia (this patient)<br /></br>   • Severe: &gt;10 stools/day, fever ≥39 °C, anemia, marked tachycardia<br /></br>3. Choose induction (flare) therapy:<br /></br>   – Mild distal disease → topical 5-ASA (mesalamine) ± oral 5-ASA<br /></br>   – Moderate disease or failure of 5-ASA → topical or systemic corticosteroid<br /></br>   – Severe or steroid-refractory disease → biologic (infliximab) or cyclosporine, then surgery if needed  </p></br><p>Because the patient is already demonstrating systemic signs and is being treated in the office/ED setting, the next step is to start corticosteroid therapy (hydrocortisone or prednisone). Hence answer A.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): disease activity, stool frequency, vitals, labs, colonoscopy findings, treatment plan, counseling about steroid side effects.</p></br><p>Take-home concept:<br /></br>Corticosteroids are the first-line drugs for inducing remission in a moderate UC flare.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)</p></br><p>• UC = chronic relapsing idiopathic inflammatory disease limited to the mucosa/submucosa of colon, always involves rectum, spreads proximally in a continuous fashion.<br /></br>• Crypt abscess = accumulation of neutrophils in glandular lumen ⇒ hallmark of UC.<br /></br>• Flare triggers: infections, NSAIDs, stress, smoking cessation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review</p></br><p>Induction (flare)<br /></br>1. Distal mild → topical mesalamine suppository/enema.<br /></br>2. Moderate → glucocorticoid (topical hydrocortisone enema for distal disease or oral prednisone 40-60 mg).<br /></br>3. Severe or steroid-refractory → biologic (infliximab, adalimumab) or IV cyclosporine.  </p></br><p>Maintenance (prevent relapse)<br /></br>• 5-ASA (mesalamine) is first-line.<br /></br>• Azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) for steroid-dependent pts.<br /></br>• Methotrexate more useful in Crohn disease than UC.<br /></br>• Biologics for patients who fail immunomodulators.</p></br><p>Diagnostic tools if severity uncertain<br /></br>• CBC, ESR/CRP, stool cultures, CT Abd/Pelvis if megacolon suspected.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“UC = U Continuous Colon”<br /></br>Flare treatment ladder (think “S-B-I-S”):<br /></br>• Step 1: Suppository (mesalamine)<br /></br>• Step 2: Budesonide/hydrocortisone (steroid)<br /></br>• Step 3: Infliximab (biologic)<br /></br>• Step 4: Surgery (colectomy)</p></br><p>Visual: imagine a “flare ladder” rising from mesalamine at the bottom up to surgery on top.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</p></br><p>b. Infliximab therapy<br /></br>   • Anti-TNF biologic reserved for severe disease or steroid-refractory moderate disease. Starting here skips the appropriate step and exposes to unnecessary risks (infusion reactions, reactivation of TB).</p></br><p>c. Mercaptopurine therapy (6-MP)<br /></br>   • Thiopurine immunomodulator used for MAINTENANCE, not for acute induction because onset is slow (8-12 weeks). Would not control a current flare.</p></br><p>d. Methotrexate therapy<br /></br>   • Used mostly for Crohn disease maintenance or steroid-refractory Crohn; very limited role in UC. Also slow onset.</p></br><p>e. No pharmacotherapy is indicated<br /></br>   • Incorrect because the patient is symptomatic with systemic signs and evidence of mucosal inflammation—requires treatment to induce remission and prevent complications (e.g., toxic megacolon, anemia).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line: A moderate ulcerative colitis flare limited to the rectosigmoid colon should be treated with corticosteroids (e.g., hydrocortisone enema or systemic prednisone) to induce remission.</p>
24. A 21-year-old woman comes to student health services because of a 36-hour history of fever, sore throat, and headache. Two weeks ago, she was treated with a 5-day course of ibuprofen for muscle pain, fatigue, and malaise thought to be associated with a virus. She has no history of serious illness. Her only current medication is an oral contraceptive. Temperature is 38.3°C (101.0°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg. Examination shows edema of the pharynx and tonsils. Multiple firm, mobile, tender lymph nodes are palpated in the cervical chain bilaterally. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. There is no abdominal tenderness. The spleen tip is palpated 5 cm below the left costal margin. Her leukocyte count is 14,000/mm3 (40 percent segmented neutrophils, 3 percent eosinophils, 55 percent lymphocytes, and 2 percent monocytes). Monospot test is positive. The patient lives in a college dormitory. Use of which of the following measures by the patient is most likely to prevent transmission of her illness?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Frequent hand washing until symptoms resolve</br>b. Isolation until a second Monospot test is negative</br>c. Isolation until symptoms resolve</br>d. No specific measures are indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. No specific measures are indicated</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 21-year-old college student with fever, sore throat, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly<br /></br>   • Atypical lymphocytosis (55 % lymphocytes)<br /></br>   • Positive Monospot (heterophile antibody)<br /></br>   → Classic picture of acute Epstein–Barr virus (EBV) infectious mononucleosis.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives: pharyngitis + tender posterior cervical nodes + splenomegaly + atypical lymphocytes + positive heterophile test.  </p></br></li></br><li>Pertinent negatives: no purulent exudate or severe neutrophilia typical of bacterial pharyngitis; no dyspnea or stridor that would suggest epiglottitis.  </li></br><li>Cognitive strategy: once EBV mono is recognized, recall that recommendations for preventing spread are very different from infections transmitted by respiratory droplets (e.g., group A strep).  </li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Transmission: EBV is spread through intimate contact with saliva (“kissing disease”), not casual respiratory droplets.<br /></br>• Viral shedding: patients continue to shed virus intermittently in saliva for ≥6 months (often &gt;12 months) after symptoms resolve, and many healthy people chronically shed the virus.<br /></br>• Public-health implication: isolating symptomatic patients or advising special hand-hygiene routines does little to influence transmission because:<br /></br>  – They are infectious long after symptoms disappear.<br /></br>  – Many asymptomatic carriers exist in the community.<br /></br>• Therefore, standard public-health recommendations for mono in otherwise healthy adults = no special restrictions beyond routine hygiene.<br /></br>• The only lifestyle advice generally given is to avoid contact sports for ≥3 weeks to reduce risk of splenic rupture, but that is for the patient’s safety, not for infection control.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: For EBV infectious mononucleosis, there are no evidence-based isolation or prophylactic measures required to protect others.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real-life encounter): record diagnosis, education provided (self-limited illness, avoid contact sports), and that no isolation is required.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• EBV infects B lymphocytes via CD21.<br /></br>• Host immune response → atypical reactive T-cells seen on smear &amp; produces heterophile antibodies detected by Monospot.<br /></br>• Latent infection persists in B-cells; intermittent salivary shedding is common.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>• Diagnosis: clinical triad + heterophile antibody test (Monospot).<br /></br>• Management: supportive (NSAIDs/acetaminophen for fever, rest, hydration).<br /></br>• Avoid ampicillin/amoxicillin (can trigger rash).<br /></br>• No role for antivirals or antibiotics in uncomplicated cases.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Mono = Months Of salivary shedding → No isolation.”<br /></br>or visualize a student blowing soap bubbles labeled “EBV” for months—impossible to catch them all, so no isolation rules.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Frequent hand washing until symptoms resolve<br /></br>   – Hand hygiene is always good practice but does not specifically prevent EBV spread because transmission requires direct saliva exchange, not surface contamination.  </p></br><p>B. Isolation until a second Monospot test is negative<br /></br>   – Heterophile antibodies may remain positive for ≥1 year; isolation would be impractical and unnecessary.  </p></br><p>C. Isolation until symptoms resolve<br /></br>   – Patients remain infectious long after clinical recovery; isolation for the symptomatic period alone does not prevent transmission and is not recommended.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: EBV mono is ubiquitous, spread by intimate salivary contact, and patients shed virus long-term; therefore, no special public-health measures are recommended to prevent transmission.</p>
25. During a routine annual examination, an asymptomatic 46-year-old man is found to have a 1-cm prostatic nodule. Physical findings are otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Reassurance and evaluation in 6 months</br>b. CT scan of the abdomen</br>c. Intravenous pyelography</br>d. Retrograde cystography</br>e. Prostatic biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Prostatic biopsy (trans-rectal ultrasound-guided needle core biopsy)</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “46-year-old man” – age range in which prostate cancer begins to appear (risk rises &gt;40 y).<br /></br>• “Routine annual examination” &amp; “asymptomatic” – prostate cancer is often clinically silent early.<br /></br>• “1-cm prostatic nodule” on DRE – focal, firm nodule = classic worrisome finding for malignancy.<br /></br>• No urinary symptoms, no systemic signs – rules out prostatitis (painful) and makes BPH (diffuse, rubbery enlargement) less likely.  </p></br><p>Approach to similar questions<br /></br>1. Any focal nodule or induration on digital rectal exam (DRE) = malignancy until proven otherwise.<br /></br>2. Cancer diagnosis ALWAYS requires tissue. Imaging alone cannot establish histology.<br /></br>3. Therefore, next step after an abnormal DRE (or markedly elevated PSA) is trans-rectal ultrasound (TRUS)-guided core biopsy.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Abnormal screening finding (palpable nodule).<br /></br>2. Differential: prostate cancer &gt; granulomatous prostatitis, focal BPH nodule, calcification.<br /></br>3. Malignancy must be excluded because prognosis changes dramatically with early detection.<br /></br>4. Definitive diagnosis = histology → order TRUS-guided prostate biopsy.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>• Palpable prostate nodule = biopsy.<br /></br>• PSA can be ordered concurrently, but it does NOT replace biopsy when a nodule is already felt.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• DRE characteristics (size, location, consistency).<br /></br>• Shared decision-making discussion about biopsy risks/benefits.<br /></br>• PSA value ordered same day.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Prostate adenocarcinoma<br /></br>• Arises most commonly in the peripheral (posterior) zone → palpable as firm nodule.<br /></br>• Androgen-dependent; risk factors: age, African ancestry, family history (BRCA2, HOXB13), high-fat diet.<br /></br>• Early disease asymptomatic → detected by PSA or DRE.<br /></br>• Gleason grade &amp; stage dictate prognosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Overview  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. TRUS-guided core biopsies (generally ≥12 cores).<br /></br>2. After histologic confirmation: PSA, MRI pelvis ± CT abdomen/pelvis &amp; bone scan are used for staging, NOT for initial diagnosis.</p></br><p>Treatment snapshot<br /></br>• Localized low-risk: active surveillance, radical prostatectomy, or radiotherapy.<br /></br>• Higher-risk or metastatic: androgen-deprivation therapy (leuprolide, degarelix) ± anti-androgens (bicalutamide, enzalutamide), chemo (docetaxel), or radiopharmaceuticals.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Feel a lump? Grab a chunk.”<br /></br>• Lump (nodule) on prostate → Chunk (core biopsy).  </p></br><p>DAP mnemonic for prostate cancer work-up:<br /></br>D = DRE abnormal →<br /></br>A = Assess PSA same visit →<br /></br>P = Pathology with (P)rostate biopsy comes next.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Reassurance and 6-month follow-up<br /></br>• Delay risks missing early curable cancer; unacceptable with a palpable nodule.</p></br><p>b. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>• Imaging useful for staging AFTER diagnosis. Cannot provide histology; unnecessary as first step.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous pyelography (IVP)<br /></br>• Evaluates kidneys/ureters for obstruction or stones; unrelated to prostate evaluation.</p></br><p>d. Retrograde cystography<br /></br>• Used for bladder trauma or vesicoureteral reflux assessment, not for prostate lesions.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Summary<br /></br>A firm, focal prostate nodule on DRE is presumed malignant until proven otherwise. The immediate next step is a trans-rectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy to obtain tissue for histologic confirmation.</p>
26. A previously healthy 4-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-day history of fever and episodes of vomiting and crying. His temperature is 39.1°C (102.4°F), pulse is 142/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 88/54 mm Hg. He is fussy but consolable. Examination shows an uncircumcised penis. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit          35 percent</br>Leukocyte count     19,500/mm3</br>Platelet count      250,000/mm3</br>Urine</br>RBC                 10/hpf</br>WBC                 30/hpf</br>Urine culture grows greater than 100,000 colonies/mL of Escherichia coli. Blood culture is negative. Renal ultrasonography shows no abnormalities. Voiding cystourethrography shows a backflow of urine from the bladder to the renal pelvis. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention to prevent future morbidity from this condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Low-sodium diet</br>b. Daily antibiotic therapy</br>c. Daily furosemide therapy</br>d. Circumcision</br>e. Vesicostomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Daily antibiotic therapy (continuous low-dose antimicrobial prophylaxis)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Cognitive Approach  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 4-month-old with fever + vomiting → think “febrile UTI/pyelonephritis” in an infant.<br /></br>   • UA: &gt;30 WBC/hpf, 10 RBC/hpf; culture &gt;100 000 E coli → confirms UTI.<br /></br>   • Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG): “back-flow of urine from bladder to renal pelvis” = vesicoureteral reflux (VUR).<br /></br>   • Normal renal US → no congenital dysplasia/obstruction.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>What the question is really asking<br /></br>   “Which intervention prevents future morbidity?” → They are testing proper long-term management of VUR to avoid renal scarring, hypertension, and CKD.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>   Anytime you see: febrile UTI in an infant + VCUG shows VUR ⇒ think “chronic suppressive antibiotics until reflux resolves or surgery is needed.”</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>a) Infant with febrile UTI → do VCUG (guideline).<br /></br>b) VCUG positive for reflux → risk of recurrent pyelonephritis → renal scarring.<br /></br>c) First-line prevention = continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., nightly trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, nitrofurantoin, or amoxicillin in very young infants).<br /></br>d) Surgery (ureteral reimplantation) is reserved for breakthrough infections or very high-grade reflux (IV-V) despite prophylaxis.</p></br><p>Take-home: Early, low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis is the evidence-based method to reduce recurrent UTIs and long-term renal injury in children with VUR.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): grade of reflux, discussion with parents regarding prophylaxis plan, antibiotic chosen, follow-up imaging schedule.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• VUR = incompetence of vesicoureteral junction → retrograde urine flow.<br /></br>• Repeated bladder infections ascend to kidneys → inflammation → interstitial fibrosis → cortical scarring (reflux nephropathy).<br /></br>• Long-term sequelae: proteinuria, hypertension, CKD.<br /></br>• Low-grade (I–II) often resolves spontaneously by age 4–5; higher grades less likely.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Continuous antimicrobial prophylaxis<br /></br>   • &lt;2 mos: amoxicillin.<br /></br>   • ≥2 mos: TMP-SMX or nitrofurantoin at ¼ of treatment dose once nightly.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Monitoring<br /></br>   • Urine culture for febrile illnesses.<br /></br>   • Repeat VCUG or nuclear renal scan after 1–2 y or if breakthrough UTI.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Escalation<br /></br>   • Breakthrough febrile UTIs or grade IV–V → surgical ureteral reimplantation or endoscopic injection of dextranomer/hyaluronic acid.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“Reflux? REF–LUX keeps BUGS up.”<br /></br>REF = Reflux<br /></br>LUX = Low-dose Urinary X-biotics (antibiotics)<br /></br>Keeps BUGS from going UP to kidneys.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Low-sodium diet<br /></br>   • Used for hypertension/heart failure, not for preventing renal scarring from VUR in an infant.</p></br><p>C. Daily furosemide therapy<br /></br>   • Loop diuretic treats fluid overload; does not address infection risk and would worsen dehydration in infants.</p></br><p>D. Circumcision<br /></br>   • Does lower UTI risk in uncircumcised boys but does NOT prevent reflux-related renal damage once VUR exists; not a substitute for prophylaxis.</p></br><p>E. Vesicostomy<br /></br>   • Surgical diversion used for severe bladder outlet obstruction (e.g., posterior urethral valves) or neurogenic bladder; unnecessary for uncomplicated VUR.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Infants with vesicoureteral reflux are placed on continuous low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent recurrent pyelonephritis and subsequent renal scarring.</p>
27. A 14-year-old girl is brought to the physician because of a generalized itchy rash for 4 days. During this period, she has also had abdominal cramps, joint pain, and fever; she has not had double vision. She received an intramuscular injection of botulism antitoxin 10 days ago because of an outbreak of botulism at a restaurant where she had eaten. Immunizations are up-to-date. She appears mildly ill. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F). Examination shows mild facial edema. There are scattered, edematous, soft nodules over the trunk and extremities. Abdominal examination shows tenderness to palpation without rebound. Active and passive range of motion of the wrists, knees, and ankles is limited by pain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Erythema nodosum</br>b. Lyme disease</br>c. Mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome (Kawasaki disease)</br>d. Rheumatic fever</br>e. Salmonella enteritidis infection</br>f. Serum sickness</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Serum sickness  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Timeline<br /></br>   • Injection with botulism antitoxin (equine-derived immunoglobulin) 10 days ago → classic 7–14-day window for a type III (immune-complex) reaction.  </li></br><li>Symptoms<br /></br>   • Generalized pruritic rash, fever, arthralgias, abdominal cramps.<br /></br>   • Limited active &amp; passive ROM because of joint pain (arthralgia &gt; frank arthritis).  </li></br><li>Skin findings<br /></br>   • “Scattered, edematous, soft nodules” = urticaria-like, not erythema nodosum or target lesions.  </li></br><li>Other hints<br /></br>   • Mild facial edema (angioedema-type swelling often accompanies urticaria).<br /></br>   • No diplopia (rules out recurrent botulism).<br /></br>Taken together, the vignette screams immune-complex serum sickness after exposure to heterologous antiserum.</li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>• Whenever you see heterologous antibodies/antitoxin, monoclonal antibodies, or certain drugs (e.g., penicillin, cefaclor, TMP-SMX) → set a mental “1–2-week” timer → look for triad of fever + urticarial rash + arthralgia ± lymphadenopathy/edema.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why choice F is right)</p></br><p>Step-wise linking of clues:<br /></br>1. Exposure: equine botulism antitoxin.<br /></br>2. Latency: symptom onset ≈10 days later.<br /></br>3. Clinical triad: pruritic urticaria-like rash, fever, polyarthralgias.<br /></br>4. Systemic clues: abdominal pain (immune-complex vasculitis can involve gut), facial edema (complement-mediated vascular permeability).<br /></br>Conclusion → Serum sickness.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Serum sickness = type III immune-complex disease occurring 1–2 weeks after exposure to foreign proteins or certain drugs, producing fever + urticaria + arthralgia ± proteinuria/edema.</p></br><p>Documentation in a real encounter would include: recent exposure to heterologous protein, description of rash (size, distribution, blanchability), joint exam, abdominal exam, vitals, and plan to stop offending agent, provide antihistamines ± corticosteroids, and order complement levels/UA if needed.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Etiology: Circulating antigen–antibody complexes (IgG or IgM vs foreign protein) deposit in small vessels → complement activation → tissue inflammation.<br /></br>• Labs (if obtained): ↓C3, ↑ESR/CRP, sometimes proteinuria/hematuria.<br /></br>• Resolution: self-limited over 1–2 weeks once antigen is cleared.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</p></br><ol></br><li>Discontinue/avoid further exposure to the antigen.  </li></br><li>Symptomatic therapy:<br /></br>   • Oral second-generation antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine) for pruritus.<br /></br>   • NSAIDs for arthralgia (unless contraindicated).<br /></br>   • Short prednisone taper for severe disease.  </li></br><li>For future toxin exposure: use human-derived botulism immune globulin or desensitize if absolutely necessary.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“Serum makes you SICK 1-2-3”:<br /></br>1 week latency<br /></br>2 main systems (Skin + Joints)<br /></br>3 findings: Fever, Pruritic urticaria, Arthralgia</p></br><p>Visual aid: Picture a “serum” bottle spilling complexes that stick to vessels, causing red wheals on the skin and rusty gears in the joints.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</p></br><p>a. Erythema nodosum<br /></br>   • Painful erythematous subcutaneous nodules, usually over shins, not pruritic wheals; often associated with sarcoidosis, IBD, OCPs. No link to antitoxin.</p></br><p>b. Lyme disease<br /></br>   • Requires Ixodes tick exposure; rash is erythema migrans (annular), not urticarial; latency weeks to months; would have migratory arthritis, not widespread urticaria.</p></br><p>c. Kawasaki disease (mucocutaneous lymph node syndrome)<br /></br>   • Age &lt;5 yrs, prolonged high fever, conjunctivitis, oral mucous membrane changes, cervical LAD, edema/desquamation of hands/feet. Patient is 14 yrs, pruritic rash predominates, no mucosal findings.</p></br><p>d. Rheumatic fever<br /></br>   • Occurs 2–4 wks after β-hemolytic strep pharyngitis; migratory polyarthritis, carditis, chorea, erythema marginatum (non-pruritic serpiginous rash), subcutaneous nodules. No strep history, wrong rash type.</p></br><p>e. Salmonella enteritidis infection<br /></br>   • Causes gastroenteritis with diarrhea ± fever; no urticarial rash or arthralgia; incubation ≤72 hrs, not 10 days.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>A 1–2-week delayed triad of fever, pruritic urticaria-like rash, and polyarthralgias after exposure to foreign serum or certain drugs is classic for serum sickness (type III hypersensitivity).</p>
28. An 11-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 40 minutes after he was found slumped over in an alley. He appears somewhat disheveled and dirty. He is groggy and somewhat confused but smiling. He is unable to give coherent answers to most questions, and his speech is slurred. His pulse is 124/min, respirations are 10/min, and blood pressure is 100/62 mm Hg while supine and 82/48 mm Hg while sitting. Pupils are 5 mm and reactive to light. There is occasional nystagmus on lateral gaze. He falls asleep in the examining room. Intoxication with which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Amphetamines</br>b. Heroin</br>c. Inhalants</br>d. LSD</br>e. Marijuana</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Inhalants  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age &amp; Setting<br /></br>   • 11-year-old boy, found in an alley → typical demographic and location for “huffing / sniffing” glue, paint, aerosol propellants, etc.<br /></br>2. Mental status &amp; speech<br /></br>   • Groggy, confused, intermittently euphoric (“smiling”), slurred speech → CNS depressant with brief initial euphoria.<br /></br>3. Ocular findings<br /></br>   • Pupils 5 mm, reactive (≈normal) + horizontal nystagmus → classic with volatile solvents; opioids cause pinpoint pupils, PCP often gives vertical nystagmus and agitation.<br /></br>4. Vital signs<br /></br>   • Tachycardia (124) + marked orthostatic hypotension → vasodilation from volatile hydrocarbons; stimulants would give hypertension, hallucinogens variable, opioids bradycardia.<br /></br>5. Other clues<br /></br>   • Dirty clothing / chemical odor often described (not given but implied by “disheveled and dirty”).<br /></br>Key pattern to recognize: Pre-teen/early-teen, brief euphoria followed by CNS depression, slurred speech, ataxia/nystagmus, normal pupils → think inhalant intoxication.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see a very young patient with CNS depression but NORMAL-sized pupils and nystagmus, inhalants top the list. Pupillary size is the quickest sorting tool (tiny = opioid, large = stimulant/hallucinogen, normal ± nystagmus = inhalant).</p></br><hr /></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify drug class likely used by 10–14 y/o → inhalants common because inexpensive &amp; accessible.<br /></br>2. Match physiologic effects<br /></br>   • Early euphoria → “smiling”<br /></br>   • CNS depression → somnolence, slurred speech, ↓RR 10/min<br /></br>   • Eye findings → nystagmus without significant miosis/mydriasis<br /></br>   • Cardiovascular → tachycardia + vasodilation → orthostatic hypotension<br /></br>3. Rule out other choices by mismatched cardinal features (see Incorrect Answer section).<br /></br>Take-home: Volatile solvent inhalation produces brief stimulant-like high followed by alcohol-like intoxication and nystagmus.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record level of consciousness, vitals, neuro exam (including eye findings), odor on breath/clothes, and consider serum chemistries and tox screen (standard urine tox won’t detect most inhalants).</p></br><hr /></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Agents: toluene, butane, propane, spray paint propellants, glue, lighter fluid.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Lipid-soluble hydrocarbons rapidly absorbed via pulmonary capillaries → diffuse into CNS → transient dopamine surge (euphoria) then GABA-mediated CNS depression.<br /></br>• Systemic effects<br /></br>  – Cardiac sensitization to catecholamines → fatal arrhythmias (“sudden sniffing death”).<br /></br>  – Vasodilation → hypotension, syncope.<br /></br>  – Cerebellar &amp; vestibular suppression → ataxia, nystagmus.<br /></br>  – Chronic use → polyneuropathy, cerebellar degeneration, renal tubular acidosis (toluene).  </p></br><hr /></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes<br /></br>1. Airway, breathing, circulation (ABCs) – high-flow O₂; monitor for dysrhythmias.<br /></br>2. No specific antidote.<br /></br>3. Evaluate electrolytes, creatinine, UA if chronic use suspected.<br /></br>4. Counsel on substance abuse; involve social services.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Glue must be NICE”:<br /></br>Nystagmus<br /></br>Intoxication like alcohol (slurred speech, ataxia)<br /></br>Children/teens<br /></br>Euphoria → CNS depression  </p></br><p>Or think “InHALants = HAL (He’s A Little kid).”</p></br><hr /></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amphetamines<br /></br>   • Findings: agitation, insomnia, diaphoresis, mydriasis, hypertension, tachypnea.<br /></br>   • Vignette mismatch: patient is sedated with bradypnea and hypotension, not agitated or hypertensive; pupils are not markedly dilated.</p></br><p>b. Heroin (opioid)<br /></br>   • Classic triad: miosis (pinpoint pupils), respiratory depression, CNS depression.<br /></br>   • Vignette mismatch: pupils are normal-large (5 mm), not pinpoint; nystagmus uncommon.</p></br><p>d. LSD (lysergic acid diethylamide)<br /></br>   • Hallucinations, visual distortions, panic, mydriasis, tachycardia, hypertension; patients are usually alert or anxious, not sedated.<br /></br>   • Vignette mismatch: no hallucinations reported; patient is somnolent with hypotension.</p></br><p>e. Marijuana<br /></br>   • Mild euphoria, conjunctival injection, ↑appetite, dry mouth, mild tachycardia. Nystagmus and significant speech slurring are rare; severe hypotension unusual.<br /></br>   • Vignette mismatch: pronounced CNS depression, orthostatic hypotension, nystagmus.</p></br><hr /></br><p>Core Takeaway<br /></br>In a pre-teen found intoxicated with slurred speech, nystagmus, normal-sized pupils, tachycardia, and orthostatic hypotension, always think of inhalant (volatile solvent) abuse. Rapid onset/offset euphoria followed by alcohol-like CNS depression is pathognomonic.</p>
29. A 25-year-old man comes to the physician for an examination prior to starting an exercise program consisting of swimming four times weekly. He feels well but wants to change his sedentary lifestyle. He has smoked one-half pack of cigarettes daily for 9 years. His father had a myocardial infarction at the age of 52 years. The patient is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall and weighs 109 kg (240 lb); BMI is 34 kg/m2. His pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 138/76 mm Hg. The remainder of the examination and an ECG show no other abnormalities. In addition to obtaining serum lipid studies and recommending that the patient stop smoking, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Approval of the exercise program</br>b. Exercise stress test</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring</br>e. Cardiac catheterization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a.  Approval of the exercise program  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1.
