Patient factors<br /></br>   • Age: 25 y (young)<br /></br>   • Symptoms: NONE (no chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, palpitations).<br /></br>   • Known disease: NONE (no CAD, structural heart disease, pulmonary or metabolic disease).<br /></br>   • Risk factors present: cigarette smoking, obesity (BMI 34), first-degree relative with premature MI.  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Exercise plan<br /></br>   • Swimming 4×/week → generally a moderate-intensity aerobic program.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key guideline clue<br /></br>   • Current ACC/AHA/ACSM pre-participation screening:<br /></br>     – “Asymptomatic individuals &lt;40 y with no known cardiovascular, pulmonary, or metabolic disease may begin a moderate-intensity program without additional testing.”<br /></br>   • AHA/USPSTF: Routine resting ECG, stress testing, or imaging in asymptomatic low-to-moderate risk adults offers no mortality benefit and increases false-positive findings and downstream invasive tests.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive take-away: On test day, ask three questions:<br /></br>   1. Does the patient have symptoms?<br /></br>   2. Do they have diagnosed cardiopulmonary or metabolic disease?<br /></br>   3. Is the planned activity vigorous (&gt;6 METs)?<br /></br>If all are “No,” approve exercise; do not order tests.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• The patient is “moderate risk” by older ACSM stratification (≤40 y, ≥2 risk factors) but guidelines only advise a stress test if he plans VIGOROUS (&gt;60% VO₂ max/ &gt;6 MET) exercise. Recreational swimming four times weekly is usually MODERATE; clearance alone is adequate.<br /></br>• Additional testing in asymptomatic young adults generates more harm (false positives, radiation, anxiety) than benefit.<br /></br>Core concept: Screening cardiac testing is reserved for (1) symptoms or (2) high-risk/vigorous-exercise candidates, not simply for risk factors.</p></br><p>Documentation tip (real life): “Reviewed risk factors; patient asymptomatic; no history of heart, lung, or metabolic disease. Approved to start moderate swimming program; advised smoking cessation, weight reduction, fasting lipid panel ordered.”</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Why we care)<br /></br>• Coronary atherosclerosis develops over decades; at 25 y the absolute event risk remains low despite risk factors.<br /></br>• Exercise itself improves lipid profile, insulin sensitivity, endothelial function, and smoking cessation rates—benefits outweigh the small acute exercise risk in low-to-moderate risk populations.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line today:<br /></br>• Approve moderate exercise.<br /></br>• Counsel: gradual progression, warm-up/cool-down, stop if chest pain/dyspnea.<br /></br>• Risk-factor management: smoking cessation aids (varenicline, bupropion, NRT), diet, weight loss, fasting lipids (already planned).</p></br><p>When WOULD you test?<br /></br>• Symptoms suggestive of ischemia or arrhythmia.<br /></br>• Known heart disease.<br /></br>• Men &gt;40 y or women &gt;50 y doing competitive/vigorous sports.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid<br /></br>“PAR-Q3”: Before stress testing ask 3 Q’s—Pain? (symptoms) Ailment? (known disease) Rate-intense? (vigorous plan). If all “NO,” give the Par-Q (Participation-Readiness Questionnaire) stamp of approval.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Exercise stress test<br /></br>   – Appropriate for symptomatic patients or asymptomatic patients ≥40 y (men) or ≥50 y (women) starting vigorous activity. Would yield low pre-test probability here → high false-positive rate.  </p></br><p>c. Echocardiography<br /></br>   – Screens structural heart disease or valvular pathology; indicated only if murmur, heart failure signs, or congenital disease suspicion. Normal exam and ECG.  </p></br><p>d. 24-hr ambulatory ECG (Holter)<br /></br>   – Used to evaluate suspected arrhythmias causing syncope, palpitations, or rate-related symptoms. Patient is asymptomatic.  </p></br><p>e. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   – Invasive diagnostic/therapeutic procedure reserved for confirmed ischemia, high-risk stress-test findings, or acute coronary syndromes. Not a screening tool.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In an asymptomatic 25-year-old with risk factors but no known cardiac disease who wishes to start a moderate exercise regimen, simply approve the program and address modifiable risks; further cardiac testing is unnecessary and potentially harmful.</p>
30. A 32-year-old woman comes to an emergency department in New England because of a 5-day history of fever. She has not had any other symptoms. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She has been sexually active with one male partner for 5 years, and they use condoms consistently. She has a healthy 2-year-old son. She immigrated to the USA from Kenya 6 weeks ago. She appears ill. Her temperature is 38.9°C (102°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Her hematocrit is 29 percent, leukocyte count is 6000/mm3 (88 percent segmented neutrophils, 10 percent lymphocytes, and 2 percent monocytes), and serum lactate dehydrogenase activity is 450 U/L. Which of the following is the most likely causal organism	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Borrelia burgdorferi</br>b. Hepatitis B virus</br>c. HIV</br>d. Plasmodium falciparum</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Plasmodium falciparum  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 5-day history of fever with NO localizing symptoms<br /></br>   • Recent arrival (6 weeks ago) from Kenya → highly endemic for malaria<br /></br>   • Anemia (hematocrit 29 %) and ↑LDH (450 U/L) → laboratory evidence of hemolytic process<br /></br>   • Normal total WBC count (6000/mm³) argues against typical acute bacterial sepsis.<br /></br>2. Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No rash, arthralgias, meningismus, sore throat, jaundice, or focal infection.<br /></br>   • No leukocytosis or transaminase elevation.<br /></br>3. Cognitive road map<br /></br>   a. Fever in a recent traveler from sub-Saharan Africa → think malaria first.<br /></br>   b. Anemia + elevated LDH = intravascular hemolysis, classic for malaria (especially falciparum).<br /></br>   c. Incubation for P. falciparum is usually 7-30 days; presentation 6 weeks after leaving Africa fits perfectly.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Plasmodium falciparum infects RBCs of all ages → high parasitemia → brisk hemolysis → anemia &amp; ↑LDH.<br /></br>• Falciparum often produces irregular (not clock-like) fevers, so the absence of every-48-h or 72-h periodicity does NOT rule it out.<br /></br>Take-home message: In ANY febrile patient who has been in an endemic area within the past 1–2 months, malaria must be the first diagnosis considered—lab clues are hemolytic anemia and elevated LDH.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Pearls<br /></br>• Mosquito (Anopheles) injects sporozoites → liver stage (schizonts) → blood stage (merozoites invade RBCs).<br /></br>• Falciparum lacks dormant liver hypnozoites (unlike P. vivax/ovale) but causes the highest parasitemias and most severe disease (cerebral malaria, renal failure).<br /></br>• Hemolysis releases LDH and free heme → anemia, jaundice, dark urine.  </p></br><p>Diagnostics &amp; Management (for real-life patients)<br /></br>1. Thick &amp; thin peripheral blood smears (gold standard) or rapid antigen test.<br /></br>2. First-line therapy depends on resistance profile:<br /></br>   • Uncomplicated: atovaquone-proguanil, artemether-lumefantrine, or oral quinine + doxy/clinda.<br /></br>   • Severe: IV artesunate (or IV quinidine in places where artesunate is not available).<br /></br>3. Always assess for complications (hypoglycemia, acute renal failure, cerebral malaria).  </p></br><p>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“Falciparum is your PAL from Africa who BREAKS your red cells”:<br /></br>P – Plasmodium<br /></br>A – All ages of RBCs infected<br /></br>L – LDH rises from hemolysis<br /></br>Africa – endemic region  </p></br><p>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme disease)<br /></br>   • Endemic to New England, but requires tick exposure and usually presents with erythema migrans or later neurologic/arthritic findings, not hemolytic anemia.  </p></br><p>b. Hepatitis B virus<br /></br>   • Incubation 1–4 months; would show marked transaminase elevation ± jaundice, not isolated fever with anemia.  </p></br><p>c. HIV<br /></br>   • Acute retroviral syndrome includes fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, mucocutaneous rash. Anemia and high LDH from hemolysis are not typical in the acute stage.  </p></br><p>e. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>   • Would usually produce a focal infection or sepsis with high fever and pronounced leukocytosis or neutrophilia, not normal WBC count with hemolysis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tip<br /></br>Any “F”everish traveler From “F”oreign Africa with anemia → think “F”alciparum.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>When documenting (real patient)<br /></br>• Travel history with dates/locations<br /></br>• Malaria prophylaxis adherence<br /></br>• Vital signs &amp; complete ROS<br /></br>• CBC, CMP, peripheral smear ordered<br /></br>• Plan: empiric antimalarial therapy if smear pending but suspicion high.</p>
31. A 42-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the physician because of a 10-month history of severe menstrual cramps and increased menstrual flow. Menses occur at regular 30- to 32-day intervals and last 4 to 6 days. She has severe cramps and heavy flow for the first 4 days of menses. She previously had heavy flow only for the first 2 days of menses. She has not had passage of clots or breakthrough bleeding. She has no history of serious illness. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Pelvic examination shows a smooth, globular-shaped, mobile uterus consistent in size with a 10-week gestation; there is mild tenderness to palpation with no contour abnormalities. Examination shows no adnexal tenderness or masses. Pelvic ultrasonography, including sonohysterography, shows diffuse uterine enlargement and a normal endometrial cavity. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's menstrual symptoms?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Adenomyosis</br>b. Endometrial carcinoma</br>c. Endometrial polyp</br>d. Leiomyomata uteri</br>e. Polycystic ovarian syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Adenomyosis  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key “buzz” phrases that steer you to adenomyosis<br /></br>• Woman ≥40 yr, multiparous (G2P2)<br /></br>• New-onset, progressively worsening dysmenorrhea + heavy, REGULAR menses<br /></br>• Bimanual exam: smooth, symmetrically globular uterus ≈10-wk size, mildly tender<br /></br>• Pelvic US/sonohysterogram: diffuse myometrial enlargement; endometrial cavity normal (no intracavitary mass, no external nodularity).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives that rule out competitors<br /></br>• No post-menopausal age, no irregular spotting → argues against endometrial carcinoma or polyps.<br /></br>• No irregular, “lumpy-bumpy” contour on exam or US → argues against leiomyomata.<br /></br>• Cycles are regular (30-32 days) → argues against PCOS (typically oligomenorrhea/amenorrhea).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When the stem pairs 1) heavy, painful REGULAR menses with 2) a symmetrically enlarged, tender uterus in a woman &gt;40 yr, think “endometrial tissue IN the myometrium → adenomyosis.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify abnormal uterine bleeding pattern → heavy, prolonged flow limited to menses (menorrhagia).  </li></br><li>Ask “painful or painless?” → pronounced dysmenorrhea.  </li></br><li>Pelvic exam finding → uniformly enlarged, tender uterus without nodules.  </li></br><li>Imaging confirms diffuse thickening, normal cavity.  </li></br><li>Best single diagnosis that fits all: adenomyosis.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-Home Message: Adenomyosis = ectopic endometrial glands + stroma within myometrium → myometrial hypertrophy → symmetric enlargement, cyclic pain, and heavy regular bleeding.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient) would include: duration/severity of bleeding, impact on quality of life, exam size estimate (“10-wk-size uterus, symmetrical, mildly tender”), imaging results, and counseling re: medical vs. surgical options.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Invagination of basalis layer of endometrium into myometrium (exact mechanism unclear; parity and prior uterine surgery are risk factors).<br /></br>• Cyclic hormonal response within myometrium → swelling, inflammation → dysmenorrhea; increased surface area &amp; local prostaglandins → heavy flow.<br /></br>• Uterus becomes boggy, globular, symmetrically enlarged.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Confirmatory test: MRI is most sensitive; definitive diagnosis often after hysterectomy specimen.  </p></br><p>Management ladder<br /></br>1. NSAIDs + combined OCPs or progestin-only therapies (levonorgestrel IUD) to ↓ bleeding/pain.<br /></br>2. GnRH agonists (e.g., leuprolide) for refractory symptoms.<br /></br>3. Definitive: hysterectomy (curative, chosen if childbearing complete and medical therapy fails).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “A-DENO-Myosis = A Dense Endometrium IN Myometrium” → dense, uniformly enlarged uterus.<br /></br>Visual: Picture chocolate chips (endometrial glands) diffused through a cake (myometrium); whole cake gets bigger, not lumpy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Endometrial carcinoma<br /></br>• Classic presentation = postmenopausal bleeding or irregular intermenstrual bleeding, not heavy-regular menses.<br /></br>• Uterus usually normal size early; risk factors (obesity, unopposed estrogen) not mentioned.<br /></br>• Ultrasound often shows thickened endometrial stripe, not “normal cavity.”  </p></br><p>C. Endometrial polyp<br /></br>• Causes painless intermenstrual spotting; uterus size/shape normal.<br /></br>• Sonohysterography would reveal a focal intracavitary mass. Absent here.  </p></br><p>D. Leiomyomata uteri (fibroids)<br /></br>• Can cause heavy bleeding but uterus classically IRREGULAR, firm, nodular; exam and US would show contour abnormalities or discrete masses.<br /></br>• Submucosal fibroids distort cavity; none seen.  </p></br><p>E. Polycystic ovarian syndrome<br /></br>• Presents with oligomenorrhea/amenorrhea, infertility, hirsutism, obesity; cycles irregular, not heavier.<br /></br>• Uterus size normal; ovarian cysts on US (not described).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Heavy, painful, regular menses + symmetric tender uterine enlargement in a multiparous woman &gt;40 yr = adenomyosis.</p>
32. A study is conducted to assess the efficacy of a new medication to relieve pain. A total of 1000 patients with pain are enrolled and randomly assigned to receive either the new drug or the standard care. Results show that the new drug is more effective than the standard care, but 2.4 percent of patients receiving the new drug developed new-onset hypertension, compared with 1.6 percent of patients receiving the standard care. Based on these results, which of the following is the number needed to harm with the new drug to cause new-onset hypertension in one patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. 50</br>b. 75</br>c. 100</br>d. 125</br>e. 150</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. 125  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key data points<br /></br>• Randomized comparison: New drug vs standard care → allows direct calculation of risk difference (attributable risk).<br /></br>• Adverse outcome of interest: new-onset hypertension.<br /></br>• Incidence in treatment group = 2.4 % (0.024).<br /></br>• Incidence in control group = 1.6 % (0.016).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positive: A higher incidence of hypertension in the drug arm (harm signal).<br /></br>Pertinent negative: No other competing numbers given, so focus strictly on hypertension frequencies.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify that the question is asking for “number needed to harm” (NNH).<br /></br>2. Recall formula: NNH = 1 / (RiskT − RiskC).<br /></br>3. Convert percentages to decimals to avoid arithmetic errors.<br /></br>4. Perform quick estimation: difference ≈ 0.8 % → 1 / 0.008 ≈ 125.<br /></br>Tip: On exams, keep “1/0.01 = 100” in mind; if difference is slightly smaller (0.008), answer will be slightly larger (&gt;100).  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise calculation<br /></br>1. Risk in treatment (R_t) = 2.4 % = 0.024<br /></br>2. Risk in control (R_c) = 1.6 % = 0.016<br /></br>3. Attributable risk (AR) = R_t − R_c = 0.024 − 0.016 = 0.008<br /></br>4. NNH = 1 / AR = 1 / 0.008 = 125  </p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>• NNH quantifies how many patients must receive a therapy before ONE additional adverse event occurs compared with control. It is the inverse of the absolute risk increase (ARI).  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient encounter): If prescribing this drug, you would document discussion of potential 0.8 % absolute increase in hypertension risk and inform the patient of the number needed to harm (~1 in 125).  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)  </p></br><p>Hypertension development can result from drug-induced mechanisms (e.g., fluid retention, sympathetic activation). Quantifying that risk through measures like ARI and NNH informs risk-benefit discussions.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Treatments / Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Not directly tested here. However, when an adverse effect such as hypertension is identified, standard management includes:<br /></br>• Baseline and interval blood pressure monitoring.<br /></br>• Lifestyle advice, medication adjustment or antihypertensives if persistent.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “HARMed by ONE”<br /></br>H = Harm<br /></br>A = Absolute risk increase<br /></br>R = Remember to subtract<br /></br>M = Make it a decimal<br /></br>ONE = Invert to get number (NNH or NNT).  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture 1000 people in an auditorium. Eight extra cases (0.8 %) develop hypertension because of the drug. Divide 1000 / 8 ≈ 125 seats per case → each block of 125 people leads to 1 extra harmed.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 50<br /></br>• Would correspond to an absolute risk increase of 2 % (0.02). That would require treatment risk ~3.6 % given control 1.6 %, which is not the case.  </p></br><p>b. 75<br /></br>• Implies ARI ≈ 1.33 % (0.013). Difference given is smaller (0.8 %), so 75 is too low.  </p></br><p>c. 100<br /></br>• Implies ARI = 1 % (0.01). Again, actual ARI is 0.8 %.  </p></br><p>e. 150<br /></br>• Implies ARI ≈ 0.67 % (0.0067). Difference here is larger (0.8 %), so 150 is too high.  </p></br><p>Common trap: Students often confuse NNH/NNT with relative risk or forget to convert percentages to decimals, leading to off-by-factor-of-10 errors.  </p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>NNH = 1 ÷ (2.4 % − 1.6 %) = 1 ÷ 0.8 % = 125. Roughly one extra patient will develop hypertension for every 125 individuals treated with the new medication instead of standard care.</p>
33. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 4-week history of progressive generalized weakness and urinary frequency. She says that she needs to stop and rest halfway up the stairs in her home and that she also has difficulty carrying grocery bags. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her younger sister and brother have hypertension. Her pulse is 88/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 174/102 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination shows mild arteriovenous nicking. Muscle strength is 5/5 throughout, and reflexes are normal. Serum studies show:</br>Na+ 143 mEq/L  </br>K+ 3.0 mEq/L  </br>Cl− 102 mEq/L  </br>HCO3− 30 mEq/L  </br>Which of the following additional serum findings are most likely in this patient?  </br>Aldosterone Concentration    Renin Activity	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Decreased    decreased  </br>b. Decreased    increased  </br>c. Decreased    normal  </br>d. Increased    decreased  </br>e. Increased    increased  </br>f. Normal    decreased  </br>g. Normal    increased</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Increased aldosterone ‒ decreased renin  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential:<br /></br>1. New-onset, difficult-to-control hypertension in a relatively young patient (42 y).<br /></br>2. Unexplained hypokalemia (K⁺ = 3.0 mEq/L).<br /></br>3. Metabolic alkalosis (HCO₃⁻ = 30 mEq/L).<br /></br>4. No diuretic or medication history to account for the electrolyte pattern.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: HTN + hypo-K⁺ + metabolic alkalosis → think excess mineralocorticoid activity.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: Normal neuromuscular exam (rules out severe periodic paralysis), no edema (points away from kidney disease with salt/water retention).  </p></br><p>Cognitive road-map:<br /></br>1. “Hypertension + low K⁺” → ask “What is aldo doing? What is renin doing?”<br /></br>2. Decide whether the driver is the adrenal gland itself (primary) or the kidney sensing low perfusion (secondary).  </p></br><p>Here, the blood pressure is high, so renal perfusion pressure is high → Juxtaglomerular apparatus should shut off renin secretion → low renin.<br /></br>If renin is low yet mineralocorticoid effect is strong, aldosterone must be autonomously high → primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome).  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Hyperaldosterone state gives:</br>   • ↑ Na⁺ reabsorption → hypertension (but only mild ↑Na⁺ concentration because of obligate water reabsorption).<br /></br>   • ↑ K⁺ and H⁺ secretion → hypokalemia + metabolic alkalosis.<br /></br>2. Because the hypertension is generated at the adrenal level, renal baroreceptors see high pressure → suppress renin.<br /></br>3. Result: ↑ aldosterone, ↓ renin.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: A markedly elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio (&gt;20–30) is the screening hallmark of primary hyperaldosteronism.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): record BP readings, serum electrolytes, medications, family history, and order plasma aldosterone concentration (PAC), plasma renin activity (PRA), and PAC/PRA ratio.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Primary hyperaldosteronism<br /></br>• Etiology: unilateral adrenal adenoma (Conn), bilateral adrenal hyperplasia (most common overall), rarely adrenal carcinoma.<br /></br>• Mechanism: autonomous secretion of aldosterone independent of RAAS.<br /></br>• Clinical: HTN (often resistant), muscle weakness, polyuria/polydipsia from K⁺ wasting, NO edema because of “aldosterone escape” (pressure natriuresis &amp; ANP).  </p></br><p>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Confirmatory testing after screening: oral salt load test, saline infusion test, fludrocortisone suppression test.<br /></br>2. Localization: adrenal CT ± adrenal vein sampling.<br /></br>3. Therapy:<br /></br>   • Unilateral disease → laparoscopic adrenalectomy.<br /></br>   • Bilateral hyperplasia → mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone or eplerenone).<br /></br>   • Treat associated HTN and monitor K⁺.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Primary problem, Primary aldosterone.”<br /></br>“Renin is Repressed.” → PRiMary = Primary Aldo ↑, Renin ↓<br /></br>Mnemonic: “CONN commits to one: Aldo high, Renin none.”  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. ↓ Aldosterone / ↓ Renin – Seen with exogenous mineralocorticoid (e.g., fludrocortisone), Cushing syndrome (excess cortisol weakly stimulates mineralocorticoid receptors), congenital adrenal enzyme excess producing DOC. Vignette lacks exogenous meds, cortisol signs.  </p></br><p>b. ↓ Aldosterone / ↑ Renin – Would occur with ACE-I or ARB therapy, aldosterone synthase deficiency, or hyporeninemic states corrected by diuretics; patient is on no drugs and has HTN—not compatible.  </p></br><p>c. ↓ Aldosterone / Normal Renin – Addison disease (primary adrenal insufficiency): presents with hypotension, hyper-K⁺, metabolic acidosis, hyperpigmentation—opposite of this case.  </p></br><p>e. ↑ Aldosterone / ↑ Renin – Secondary hyperaldosteronism (renal artery stenosis, malignant HTN, renin-secreting tumor, diuretic use). Those patients have renal hypoperfusion; renin would not be suppressed.  </p></br><p>f. Normal Aldosterone / ↓ Renin – Liddle syndrome or gain-of-function ENaC mutation: severe early-onset HTN, low renin/aldosterone, hypokalemia. Aldosterone would be normal/low, not high.  </p></br><p>g. Normal Aldosterone / ↑ Renin – Seen with diuretic therapy, renin-secreting tumor when aldosterone synthase blocked. Doesn’t fit labs or history.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: The only pattern that matches HTN + hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with suppressed renin is elevated aldosterone from a primary adrenal source → answer choice d.</p>
34. A previously healthy 16-year-old girl is admitted to the hospital because of a 4-day history of fever, malaise, headache, and abdominal pain. She was present at the site of a suspected bioterrorism attack 14 days ago. Her temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 96/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. After 1 day of supportive therapy, her fever subsides, but there are vesicular lesions at the same stage of development primarily over the mouth, face, and extremities with limited distribution across the trunk. Immunization of the patient's contacts with which of the following is most likely to prevent the spread of this illness?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Anthrax</br>b. Plague</br>c. Rubella</br>d. Vaccinia</br>e. Varicella</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Vaccinia (live‐attenuated smallpox vaccine)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key time line: 14-day incubation after a “suspected bioterrorism attack.” Smallpox (variola major) classically incubates 7-17 days.<br /></br>• Prodrome: 4 days of high fever (40 °C), malaise, headache, abdominal pain → typical pre-eruptive phase of smallpox.<br /></br>• Rash description: “Vesicular lesions at the same stage of development” concentrated on the face, mouth, and extremities with sparse trunk involvement. Smallpox lesions are:</br>  – Synchronous (all lesions in any one area look identical)<br /></br>  – Centrifugal (more on face &amp; limbs than trunk)<br /></br>  – Begin as macules → papules → firm deep-seated vesicles/pustules.<br /></br>• Fever “breaks” then rash appears (“fever then rash” pattern).<br /></br>These features sharply contrast with varicella, which has asynchronous lesions and a centripetal (trunk-predominant) distribution.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: In any bioterror stem, immediately think of Category A agents (anthrax, plague, smallpox, tularemia, botulinum toxin, viral hemorrhagic fevers). Scan for pathognomonic rash or pulmonary findings to narrow further.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Goal in real life and on exams: stop secondary transmission of smallpox. The only proven post-exposure prophylaxis is immediate vaccination with the live vaccinia virus (smallpox vaccine). Given within 3 days of exposure it prevents disease; by day 7 it markedly attenuates severity. Therefore, immunizing contacts with vaccinia is the correct strategy.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Smallpox = centrifugal, synchronous vesicles/pustules after a febrile prodrome; control = ring vaccination with vaccinia.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): type of exposure, date/time, vaccination status, counseling provided, reporting to public health authorities.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Variola virus, a DNA orthopoxvirus.<br /></br>• Humans are the only reservoir; transmission via respiratory droplets or direct contact with lesion fluid or fomites.<br /></br>• Virus replicates in respiratory mucosa → lymphatics → viremia → dermal blood vessels → characteristic rash.<br /></br>• High mortality (≈30% for variola major).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes<br /></br>• No proven curative therapy; tecovirimat (TPOXX) and cidofovir can be used investigationally.<br /></br>• Diagnosis: PCR from lesion fluid; handled in BSL-4 labs.<br /></br>• Infection control: airborne &amp; contact isolation; ring vaccination of contacts with vaccinia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“SmallPOX = Same-stage Pustules that are Outer (extremities) &amp; X-treme fever.”<br /></br>Remember “Vac-cine-ia for Vari-ola” (the vaccine is a different orthopoxvirus).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Anthrax vaccine: Adsorbed protective antigen vaccine used for military/high-risk labs; ineffective against smallpox; anthrax presents with painless eschar or hemorrhagic mediastinitis, not vesicular rash.<br /></br>b. Plague vaccine: Experimental; plague presents with bubonic nodes or pneumonia, not vesicular rash.<br /></br>c. Rubella (MMR) vaccine: Prevents congenital rubella; rubella rash is fine, maculopapular, starts on face then spreads, and lesions are not vesicular.<br /></br>e. Varicella vaccine: Live attenuated VZV; protects against chickenpox, whose rash is asynchronous and trunk-heavy; gives no cross-protection against variola.</p></br><p>Focus point: Only vaccinia confers immunity to smallpox, making option d the sole prophylactic choice for exposed contacts.</p>
35. A 47-year-old man comes to the emergency department 12 hours after the onset of severe shortness of breath, dizziness, and generalized weakness. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and insulin. He has had 19 visits to the emergency department during the past 3 years because of poor blood glucose control. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years. His temperature is 38.6°C (101.4°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 34/min, and blood pressure is 90/70 mm Hg. A photograph of the plantar aspect of the right foot is shown. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL</br>Hematocrit 35 percent</br>Leukocyte count 26,000/mm3</br>Serum</br>Na+ 132 mEq/L</br>K+ 5.4 mEq/L</br>Cl- 95 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 13 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 29 mg/dL</br>Glucose 733 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL</br></br>Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>pH 7.22</br>Pco2 21 mm Hg</br>Po2 98 mm Hg</br></br>Intravenous fluids, antibiotics, and insulin are administered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of hemoglobin A1c</br>b. Measurement of serum osmolality</br>c. Measurement of urinary glucose concentration</br>d. X-rays of the foot</br>e. Ultrasonography of the foot</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>D. Plain radiographs (x-rays) of the foot</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Long-standing, poorly controlled diabetes with multiple ED visits → very high risk for diabetic foot infections and osteomyelitis.<br /></br>  – Fever, tachycardia, hypotension, leukocytosis → systemic infection/sepsis.<br /></br>  – “Photograph of the plantar aspect of the right foot” (not shown here) – NBME almost always shows an ulcer with surrounding erythema or black necrotic tissue.<br /></br>  – Severe hyperglycemia (733 mg/dL) and anion-gap metabolic acidosis (Na-[Cl+HCO₃] = 24) → diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives/positives<br /></br>  – No mention of trauma or travel; focus is local diabetic foot pathology.<br /></br>  – Acute management of DKA (fluids + insulin) and broad-spectrum antibiotics has already been started, so next step must address infection source.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>When a diabetic patient presents with systemic infection AND a limb ulcer, always ask “Have we proven whether bone or deep soft tissue is involved?” The fastest, cheapest first test is a plain x-ray to look for:<br /></br>  1. Subcutaneous gas (necrotizing infection).<br /></br>  2. Periosteal reaction, cortical destruction (osteomyelitis).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify life-threatening metabolic derangement → Treat DKA (done).<br /></br>2. Identify and control potential source of sepsis → diabetic foot ulcer.<br /></br>3. To guide surgical vs. medical therapy, determine depth: cellulitis only vs. necrotizing fasciitis vs. osteomyelitis.<br /></br>4. First diagnostic study: plain radiograph. It is quick, inexpensive, shows both gas in soft tissues and bony destruction. If positive for gas or bone involvement, immediate surgical consultation for debridement/amputation is required.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>In any diabetic with a foot ulcer and systemic signs of infection, obtain a plain radiograph early to evaluate for gas gangrene and/or osteomyelitis.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Location, size, depth of ulcer; presence of exposed bone or probe-to-bone test.<br /></br>• Neurovascular status.<br /></br>• Plan: “Plain radiograph of right foot to evaluate for gas/osteomyelitis; if positive, emergent ortho/podiatry consult.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Diabetes impairs neutrophil chemotaxis, microvascular perfusion, and sensation → minor trauma progresses to ulcer.<br /></br>• Infection can track along tendon sheaths/fascia → necrotizing infection with gas-producing organisms (e.g., Clostridium, anaerobes, facultative Gram-negatives).<br /></br>• Osteomyelitis arises by contiguous spread from ulcer; bone exposure or ulcer &gt;2 cm² and &gt;3 mm deep has high predictive value.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Overview</br>1. Imaging<br /></br>   • First: plain x-ray (detects gas, late bony changes).<br /></br>   • If x-ray equivocal but suspicion high: MRI (most sensitive for early osteomyelitis).<br /></br>2. Labs already obtained (CBC, BMP, ABG). ESR/CRP often elevated but nonspecific.<br /></br>3. Management<br /></br>   • Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam + vancomycin).<br /></br>   • Tight glycemic control with insulin drip.<br /></br>   • Surgical debridement if necrotizing infection or osteomyelitis not responsive to prolonged IV antibiotics.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“BAD SOLES” for diabetic foot evaluation:<br /></br>B – Bone probe test<br /></br>A – Arterial supply (ABI)<br /></br>D – Debridement need?<br /></br>S – Swelling/Sepsis signs<br /></br>O – Osteomyelitis? → ORDER X-RAY FIRST<br /></br>L – Location (plantar, dorsal, toe)<br /></br>E – Edges/Exudate<br /></br>S – Sensation (monofilament)</p></br><p>Think “X-ray before X-it” (exit) – get the film before the patient exits to the OR or ward.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</br>A. Hemoglobin A1c<br /></br>   • Reflects average glycemia over 3 months; useful for chronic outpatient management, not acute source control or sepsis work-up.</p></br><p>B. Serum osmolality<br /></br>   • Would help differentiate hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) from DKA; clinical/lab picture (anion-gap acidosis, ketones implied) already establishes DKA, and treatment has begun. Osmolality will not change immediate management of infection.</p></br><p>C. Urinary glucose concentration<br /></br>   • Adds no new information (serum glucose already 733 mg/dL); does not assess depth of infection.</p></br><p>E. Ultrasonography of the foot<br /></br>   • Good for detecting fluid collections/abscesses but poor for gas and cortical bone changes; operator dependent; not first-line for osteomyelitis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a septic diabetic with a foot ulcer, the next diagnostic priority after stabilizing DKA is to rule out gas gangrene or osteomyelitis. Obtain plain radiographs of the foot immediately.</p>
36. A 77-year-old woman, gravida 4, para 4, is admitted to the hospital for treatment of pneumonia. On hospital day 4, she has an episode of severe coughing, after which she states that she has had involuntary loss of urine. She states that she feels like “something is bulging” from her vagina. Her four pregnancies were uncomplicated and ended in spontaneous vaginal deliveries. Menopause occurred 25 years ago. She has not had postmenopausal bleeding and has never received hormone therapy. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 75/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. The external genitalia are atrophic but otherwise normal. The anterior vaginal segment is descended halfway past the hymenal ring. Speculum examination shows a normal cervix. The uterus is small, nontender, and anteflexed, and there are no adnexal masses. Rectal examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Cystocele</br>b. Enterocele</br>c. Rectocele</br>d. Uterine inversion</br>e. Uterine prolapse</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Cystocele  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key phrases that immediately narrow the differential:<br /></br>   • “Episode of severe coughing … involuntary loss of urine” = stress urinary incontinence → look for anterior compartment defect.<br /></br>   • “Feels like ‘something is bulging’ from her vagina.”<br /></br>   • “Anterior vaginal segment is descended halfway past the hymenal ring.”<br /></br>   • 77 y/o, multiparous, post-menopausal → classic risk factors for pelvic floor laxity.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pertinent negatives:<br /></br>   • Cervix and uterus are in normal position (“normal cervix,” “small, anteflexed uterus”) → rules out uterine prolapse or inversion.<br /></br>   • No posterior vaginal wall bulge on bimanual or speculum exam → argues against rectocele or enterocele.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Approach for exam day:<br /></br>   • Localize the compartment (anterior vs posterior vs apical).<br /></br>   • Pair the compartment with the most common organ that herniates:<br /></br>        Anterior → bladder (cystocele)<br /></br>        Posterior → rectum (rectocele)<br /></br>        Apical/upper posterior → small bowel (enterocele)<br /></br>        Cervix/uterus through introitus → uterine prolapse  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why cystocele fits best)<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Chronic risk factors (multiparity, age-related estrogen loss) weaken the endopelvic fascia and levator ani.<br /></br>2. Acute rise in intra-abdominal pressure (coughing spell) pushes the bladder through the attenuated pubocervical fascia into the vaginal canal.<br /></br>3. Bladder descent + urethral hypermobility → stress incontinence (urine loss with cough).<br /></br>4. On exam an ANTERIOR vaginal wall bulge that protrudes beyond the hymen = classic cystocele (POP-Q stage II).  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>“Anterior wall bulge + stress leakage in an older multiparous woman = cystocele.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: note stage/degree of prolapse, presence of incontinence, and impact on quality of life for future urogynecologic referral.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• The pubocervical fascia supports the bladder and urethra.<br /></br>• Estrogen deficiency and obstetric trauma weaken this fascia.<br /></br>• ↑Intra-abdominal pressure (coughing, lifting, obesity, COPD) forces the bladder to herniate into the vagina.<br /></br>• Urethral kinking is lost → stress urinary incontinence.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot (FYI)<br /></br>First-line: pelvic floor muscle training (Kegels), weight loss, pessary.<br /></br>Persistent/severe: anterior colporrhaphy ± mid-urethral sling (e.g., tension-free vaginal tape).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Cystocele = Cyst (bladder) Comes through the Curtain (anterior wall).”<br /></br>Visualize bladder as a water balloon pushing the front (anterior) tent flap.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Enterocele – Herniation of peritoneum + small bowel into the UPPER POSTERIOR vaginal wall; often after hysterectomy; may hear bowel sounds in the sac. Not an anterior defect and not associated with stress incontinence.<br /></br>c. Rectocele – Posterior vaginal wall bulge produced by rectum; symptoms include difficulty with defecation, need to splint posterior wall. Exam would describe a posterior defect.<br /></br>d. Uterine inversion – Acute obstetric emergency immediately after delivery; uterus not palpable abdominally and a mass protrudes through cervix. This patient delivered 25 years ago.<br /></br>e. Uterine prolapse – Descent of cervix and uterus through vaginal canal; exam would say cervix visible at or beyond introitus. Here the cervix and uterus are normal.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: equating “bulge” with uterine prolapse without localizing which vaginal wall is involved. Always read the anatomic descriptors carefully.</p>
37. A 1-day-old female newborn is examined in the nursery. She was born at term to a 31-year-old primigravid woman following an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery. The mother has systemic lupus erythematosus; she was asymptomatic during pregnancy, and her only medication was a prenatal vitamin. The patient’s Apgar scores were 9 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 70/min, respirations are 50/min, and blood pressure is 75/45 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cardiac catheterization</br>b. Chemical cardioversion</br>c. Insertion of a pacemaker</br>d. Intravenous administration of adenosine</br>e. Oral administration of digoxin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Insertion of a pacemaker  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key buzz-phrases &amp; data<br /></br>   • “Mother has systemic lupus erythematosus” → think maternal anti-Ro/SSA antibodies crossing the placenta.<br /></br>   • “1-day-old” + HR 70/min (newborn normals ≈120-160/min) → marked neonatal bradycardia.<br /></br>   • Normal physical exam yet abnormal ECG provided (classic picture on NBME = dissociation of P waves &amp; QRS complexes) → complete (third-degree) AV block.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No structural cardiac abnormality or perinatal distress (Apgars 9/9).<br /></br>   • No mention of drug exposure causing AV nodal depression (e.g., β-blockers, MgSO₄).<br /></br>   • Therefore isolated conduction problem = congenital heart block due to neonatal lupus.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Whenever you see maternal SLE/anti-Ro in a neonate + bradycardia, immediately place congenital heart block high on your differential and anticipate the need for pacing.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>A. Maternal SLE → transplacental anti-Ro/SSA IgG → fetal inflammatory fibrosis of AV node.<br /></br>B. Resulting pathology = congenital complete AV block.<br /></br>C. Neonatal bradycardia &lt;80/min greatly reduces stroke volume &amp; cardiac output (newborns are rate-dependent).<br /></br>D. Medical therapies (adenosine, digoxin, “chemical cardioversion”) either slow conduction or are ineffective because the problem is lack of AV conduction, not a tachy-arrhythmia.<br /></br>E. Definitive therapy = permanent pacing; immediate temporary pacing if unstable.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Neonatal complete heart block from anti-Ro requires pacemaker placement, even if initially asymptomatic, to prevent low-output heart failure and sudden death.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record fetal/neonatal heart rate trend, ECG evidence of AV dissociation, maternal antibody status, and informed-consent discussion for pacemaker insertion.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Maternal anti-Ro/SSA (± anti-La/SSB) antibodies (Type II hypersensitivity) → bind fetal cardiac conduction tissue → complement activation → fibrosis &amp; calcification of AV node.<br /></br>• Incidence: ~2% of SLE pregnancies; recurrence risk higher if prior affected child.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Persistent fetal/neonatal HR 50-80/min, possible hydrops fetalis in utero, congenital heart block after birth.<br /></br>• Natural history: Many remain bradycardic; ~1/3 develop cardiomyopathy; mortality up to 20% without pacing.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><ol></br><li>Acute/initial<br /></br>   • ECG to confirm complete AV block (independent atrial &amp; ventricular rates).<br /></br>   • Echocardiography to exclude structural lesions and evaluate ventricular function.  </li></br><li>Definitive<br /></br>   • Permanent pacemaker implantation (epicardial leads in neonates).  </li></br><li>Supportive</br>   • Manage heart failure if present (diuretics, afterload reduction).<br /></br>   • Maternal counseling for future pregnancies (hydroxychloroquine lowers recurrence).  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“RO-block baby goes SLOW—needs a GLOW (pacemaker).”<br /></br>• RO (anti-Ro) → block (heart block) → SLOW heart rate → give the heart a “GLOW” (electric spark = pacemaker).  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a red “RO” stop sign sitting on the AV junction, stopping traffic—only a permanent traffic light (pacemaker) can re-establish flow.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   • Used for diagnostic hemodynamic evaluation or interventional closure procedures, not for isolated conduction defects. Would not correct AV block.  </p></br><p>b. Chemical cardioversion<br /></br>   • (e.g., procainamide, amiodarone) indicated for tachy-arrhythmias like SVT or atrial flutter. Here rhythm is slow, not fast.  </p></br><p>d. Intravenous adenosine<br /></br>   • Temporarily blocks AV node—used to terminate SVT. Would worsen or reveal block, not treat it.  </p></br><p>e. Oral digoxin<br /></br>   • Positive inotrope/AV nodal suppressant used for heart failure or SVT rate control. It cannot re-establish conduction across a fibrosed AV node and could paradoxically slow the ventricular rate further.  </p></br><p>Common trap: Students reflexively reach for “adenosine” in any arrhythmia question; always check whether rhythm is tachy- or brady-cardic and whether the problem is conduction failure versus re-entry.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Neonatal bradycardia + maternal SLE = congenital complete AV block from anti-Ro antibodies. The only effective therapy is permanent pacemaker implantation.</p>
38. A 6-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of dry scaly skin for 2 weeks. Over the past 3 months, he has had recurrent episodes of otitis media treated with amoxicillin. There are scattered petechiae over the entire body and mild generalized eczema. Examination shows cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly. His platelet count is 25,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Adenosine deaminase deficiency</br>b. Ataxia-telangiectasia</br>c. Cellular immunodeficiency with immunoglobulins</br>d. Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis</br>e. Combined immunodeficiency disease</br>f. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia (DiGeorge syndrome)</br>g. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>g. Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age &amp; timing – 6-month-old boy. Maternal IgG has fallen, so congenital immunodeficiencies start to declare themselves.<br /></br>2. Recurrent infections – multiple otitis media despite appropriate antibiotics ⇒ humoral ± cellular immune defect, classically with encapsulated organisms.<br /></br>3. Skin findings – “dry scaly skin” + “mild generalized eczema.”<br /></br>4. Bleeding tendency – scattered petechiae + platelet count 25,000/mm³ (severe thrombocytopenia).<br /></br>5. Additional PE – cervical LAD and hepatosplenomegaly (spleen clears abnormal platelets; chronic immune activation).  </p></br><p>Triad to recognize:<br /></br>Eczema + Recurrent infections + Thrombocytopenia = Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (X-linked).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime a pediatric stem gives bleeding/petechiae AND eczema, immediately scan mental list for Wiskott-Aldrich before considering anything else.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify bleeding diathesis – petechiae + platelets 25 K.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for co-existing immunologic/dermatologic findings – frequent otitis media + eczema.<br /></br>Step 3: Match to classic triads; W-A is the only congenital immunodeficiency whose “must know” triad includes thrombocytopenia.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Wiskott-Aldrich = “WATER”<br /></br>• W = Wiskott<br /></br>• A = Aldrich<br /></br>• T = Thrombocytopenia (small platelets)<br /></br>• E = Eczema<br /></br>• R = Recurrent infections  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): document bleeding history, infection frequency, platelet size on smear, immunoglobulin panel, family history of X-linked disease, plan for hematology/ID referral and HSCT evaluation.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gene: WAS on X-chromosome → defective WASp protein.<br /></br>• Mechanism: impaired actin cytoskeleton rearrangement in T cells and platelets →<br /></br>  – Ineffective T-cell signaling ⇒ ↓ T-dependent B-cell responses, ↓ IgM, ↑/normal IgA &amp; IgE.<br /></br>  – Platelets produced small and rapidly cleared ⇒ thrombocytopenia &amp; bleeding.<br /></br>• Course: Infections (otitis, sinusitis, pneumonia, meningitis), bleeding, autoimmune disease, ↑ risk lymphoma/leukemia.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Management (for exam)<br /></br>Definitive: Allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplant (HSCT).<br /></br>Supportive:<br /></br>• IVIG &amp; prophylactic antibiotics (eg, TMP-SMX, azithro)<br /></br>• Platelet transfusions or eltrombopag when needed<br /></br>• Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin, live vaccines<br /></br>• Eczema care: emollients, topical steroids.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>1. Mnemonic “WATER.”<br /></br>2. Visual: Think of a water (W-A-T-E-R) gun squirting on skin (eczema) while platelets drain out (bleeding) and bugs crawl around (infections).<br /></br>3. Platelets are ‘tiny like water droplets’—helps recall “small platelets” on smear.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency – a form of severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID). Presents in first months with chronic diarrhea, thrush, failure to thrive, absence of lymphoid tissue. Bleeding/eczema NOT classic. Platelets normal.<br /></br>b. Ataxia-telangiectasia – Progressive cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, sinopulmonary infections, ↑ AFP. Presents later in infancy/early childhood; no thrombocytopenia/eczema.<br /></br>c. Cellular immunodeficiency with immunoglobulins – vague; would resemble SCID (recurrent severe viral/fungal infections, lymphopenia) without thrombocytopenia.<br /></br>d. Chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis – isolated T-cell dysfunction causing Candida infections of skin/mucosa, but platelets and eczema normal.<br /></br>e. Combined immunodeficiency disease (e.g., IL-2Rγ chain SCID) – severe viral, bacterial, fungal infections early; absent thymic shadow, lymphopenia, chronic diarrhea. No petechiae/low platelets.<br /></br>f. Thymic-parathyroid dysplasia (DiGeorge) – CATCH-22 (Cardiac, Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia, Cleft, Hypocalcemia). Presents with tetany, viral/fungal infections; platelets normal, eczema absent.  </p></br><p>Key distinguishing feature for Wiskott-Aldrich: bleeding from true thrombocytopenia + eczema; none of the other choices combine these findings.</p>
39. A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents 1 day after they noticed that he was not using his left arm. There is no known injury to the arm. He appears comfortable but resists any attempt to move the affected arm because of pain. He holds the left upper extremity with the elbow flexed and the forearm in pronation. There is no evidence of injury. Radial pulses are normal. Sensation is full over the affected extremity. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Notification of child protective services</br>b. Supination of the forearm with the elbow in slight flexion</br>c. X-ray of the left elbow and forearm and skeletal survey</br>d. X-ray of the left elbow and forearm only</br>e. Aspiration of the elbow joint for cytology</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Supination of the forearm with the elbow in slight flexion (classic reduction maneuver for radial head subluxation, “nurse-maid’s elbow”)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age 4 yrs: peak incidence of nurse-maid’s elbow is 1–4 yrs because the radial head is small and the annular ligament is lax.<br /></br>• “Was not using his left arm … no known injury … holds the arm flexed and pronated”: pathognomonic resting posture after radial head subluxation. Kids keep the elbow at 90° and the forearm pronated because supination is painful.<br /></br>• “Appears comfortable, resists movement because of pain” but NO swelling, erythema, warmth, bruising, deformity, or neurovascular compromise → rules out fracture, septic arthritis, compartment syndrome.<br /></br>Take-home pattern: Toddler + sudden refusal to use arm + flexed/pronated posture + normal inspection = radial head subluxation until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tips<br /></br>1. On exam day, the forearm-pronated toddler = “Think nurse-maid’s, skip the X-ray, do the reduction.”<br /></br>2. Radiographs are reserved only if: (a) unclear history, (b) focal bony tenderness/deformity, or (c) failed reduction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why B is correct)<br /></br>Step 1: Identify classic presentation of radial head subluxation.<br /></br>Step 2: Understand that diagnosis is clinical; imaging is unnecessary if the story is textbook.<br /></br>Step 3: Immediate bedside reduction (supination-flexion OR hyper-pronation) relieves pain in seconds and returns full use.<br /></br>Documentation in real life: history, physical findings, neurovascular status pre- and post-reduction, reduction maneuver used, and return of function.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: For a child 1–4 yrs with a painless-looking arm held flexed/pronated after traction, go straight to reduction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Mechanism: Sudden longitudinal traction on a pronated forearm (caregiver pulling child by hand, swinging by arms, picking child up).<br /></br>• Radial head slips distally through the annular ligament, becoming trapped; the ligament interposes between radial head &amp; capitellum → pain on supination.<br /></br>• No swelling or ecchymosis because there is no true ligament rupture or fracture.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Maneuvers:<br /></br>  1. Supination while flexing the elbow (answer B).<br /></br>  2. Hyper-pronation with slight flexion (higher first-attempt success in some studies).<br /></br>• A palpable/ audible “click” often signals success; child begins using arm within minutes.<br /></br>• Immobilization is NOT required after successful reduction.<br /></br>• Imaging: obtain X-ray only if atypical features or failed reduction attempt.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“4-year-old + flexed/pronated arm = four letters: SNAP”<br /></br>S – Subluxation<br /></br>N – Nurse-maid’s elbow<br /></br>A – Annular ligament<br /></br>P – Pronation posture<br /></br>Management: “SNAP it back” (Supinate &amp; flex).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Notify child protective services<br /></br>   • Radial head subluxation is most often accidental (sudden pull). No bruising, inconsistent history, or other injury to suggest abuse.</p></br><p>c. X-ray left elbow/forearm AND full skeletal survey<br /></br>   • Skeletal survey screens for occult fractures in suspected abuse; not indicated without red flags for abuse. Adds radiation, delays definitive therapy.</p></br><p>d. X-ray left elbow/forearm only<br /></br>   • Imaging unnecessary with classic presentation; negative films would not change management and expose child to radiation.</p></br><p>e. Aspiration of the elbow for cytology<br /></br>   • Used when septic arthritis is suspected (fever, erythema, swelling, limited passive ROM). None of those findings are present.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a toddler with the typical “won’t use arm + flexed/pronated posture” and normal exam, immediately perform a supination-flexion (or hyper-pronation) maneuver to reduce nurse-maid’s elbow—no imaging first.</p>
40. A 22-year-old primigravid woman at 15 weeks’ gestation comes to the physician for her first prenatal visit. She has no history of serious illness, and pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 20-week gestation. Ultrasonography shows twins, both male, with a single placenta and no membrane between the fetuses. This patient should be advised that there is an increased risk for which of the following?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Conjoined twins</br>b. Polyhydramnios and oligohydramnios</br>c. Postmaturity syndrome</br>d. Umbilical cord entanglement</br>e. Uterine rupture</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Umbilical cord entanglement  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Single placenta” → monochorionic (both twins share a chorion).<br /></br>• “No membrane between the fetuses” → monoamniotic (they also share the same amniotic sac).<br /></br>• Monochorionic-monoamniotic (“Mo-Mo”) twins account for &lt;1% of twin gestations but carry the highest perinatal mortality.<br /></br>• Key pattern to recognize on the exam: Mo-Mo = highest risk of umbilical cords wrapping around or knotting with each other, leading to acute fetal hypoxia or demise.</p></br><p>Test-taking approach:<br /></br>1. Identify chorionicity (placenta number) and amnionicity (membrane).<br /></br>2. Recall the unique complications tied to each twin type.<br /></br>   • Di-Di → prematurity, growth discordance.<br /></br>   • Mono-Di → twin-to-twin transfusion (poly-oli sequence).<br /></br>   • Mono-Mono → umbilical cord entanglement ± twin demise.<br /></br>3. Match the risk with the answer list.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Why cord entanglement?<br /></br>• With no separating membrane, the two umbilical cords float freely in a single sac.<br /></br>• Fetal movements allow cords to twist, knot, or compress each other.<br /></br>• This can intermittently or acutely cut off blood flow → non-reassuring FHTs, IUFD.  </p></br><p>Documentation essentials (real life):<br /></br>• Ultrasound report confirming Mo-Mo.<br /></br>• Counseling given: ↑ risk of cord accident, inpatient monitoring from ~24–26 wks, planned C-section at 32–34 wks.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept:<br /></br>Monochorionic-monoamniotic = “Mo-Mo makes cords Mo-re likely to knot.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Twins arise from cleavage of a single fertilized ovum at different times:<br /></br>  – Day 0–3 → Di-Di<br /></br>  – Day 4–8 → Mono-Di<br /></br>  – Day 8-13 → Mono-Mo<br /></br>  – ≥Day 13 → Conjoined<br /></br>• Lack of dividing membrane after day 8 → shared sac → cord accidents.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Pearls<br /></br>• Serial growth scans and non-stress testing starting early third trimester.<br /></br>• Elective delivery by C-section at 32–34 weeks before labor ensues.<br /></br>• No proven preventive therapy for entanglement—only vigilant surveillance.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>• “Mo-Mo = NO-NO membrane → Cords go GO-GO around each other.”<br /></br>• Picture two earbuds in one pocket— inevitable tangling.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Conjoined twins<br /></br>   • Require even later embryonic division (≥day 13).<br /></br>   • Ultrasound would show fused anatomy; far less common than cord entanglement.</p></br><p>b. Polyhydramnios and oligohydramnios (twin-to-twin transfusion)<br /></br>   • Occurs in monochorionic-DIAMNIOTIC twins because each fetus has its own sac; cannot occur when both share one sac.</p></br><p>c. Postmaturity syndrome<br /></br>   • Multiple gestations generally deliver preterm, not post-term; risk is lower, not higher.</p></br><p>e. Uterine rupture<br /></br>   • Risk related to prior uterine surgery or obstructed labor, not specifically increased by Mo-Mo status.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a monochorionic-monoamniotic twin pregnancy, the absence of an intertwin membrane places the fetuses at the greatest risk for umbilical cord entanglement.</p>
41. A 37-year-old primigravid woman at 9 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital because of progressive nausea and vomiting. Her symptoms began at 6 weeks’ gestation but have worsened so that she can no longer keep food or fluids down. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 105/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 9-week gestation. Her serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration is 0.4 µU/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Measurement of serum prolactin concentration  </br>b. Ultrasonography of the thyroid glad  </br>c. CT scan of the head  </br>d. Intravenous administration of fluids  </br>e. Propylthiouracil therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Intravenous administration of fluids  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key clues<br /></br>   • 9-week pregnancy with intractable nausea/vomiting starting at 6 weeks → classic time-frame for hyperemesis gravidarum (HG).<br /></br>   • “Can no longer keep food or fluids down,” pulse 105/min → volume depletion.<br /></br>   • Normal uterine size → rules out molar pregnancy (would expect uterus &gt; dates).<br /></br>   • TSH 0.4 µU/mL (slightly low) → likely hCG-mediated, physiologic in early pregnancy.  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Afebrile, no abdominal pain or neuro deficits → infection, intracranial pathology unlikely.<br /></br>   • No goiter, thyroid bruit, ophthalmopathy → overt hyperthyroidism improbable.  </li></br><li>Cognitive road map<br /></br>   a. Identify the syndrome (hyperemesis gravidarum).<br /></br>   b. Recognize immediate problem (volume depletion, risk of ketosis/electrolyte derangements).<br /></br>   c. Stabilize the patient (IV fluids ± thiamine) before ancillary tests or specific antiemetics.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home pattern: any pregnant patient with severe N/V → think HG, check vitals/volume status, and start IV fluids first.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Diagnose HG: persistent vomiting before 20 weeks, weight loss, ketonuria, volume depletion.  </li></br><li>Initial management priorities:<br /></br>   • Restore intravascular volume (normal saline or lactated Ringer).<br /></br>   • Give thiamine BEFORE dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.<br /></br>   • Correct electrolytes, then start antiemetics (pyridoxine-doxylamine, ondansetron, etc.).  </li></br><li>Why not “treat the TSH”? hCG shares an α-subunit with TSH → mild, transient TSH suppression is normal; treatment unnecessary unless overt hyperthyroid symptoms + elevated free T4.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearls: note orthostatic vitals, mucous membrane dryness, intake/output, ketonuria, weight change, and IV fluids + thiamine given.  </p></br><p>Core concept: In any potentially volume-depleted pregnant patient, stabilization with IV fluids precedes diagnostic work-up or disease-specific therapy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Hyperemesis gravidarum affects ~0.3–2% of pregnancies.<br /></br>• Driven by rapidly rising β-hCG and estrogen levels → triggers central vomiting center.<br /></br>• Complications: dehydration, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, hypokalemia, ketosis, Wernicke encephalopathy (thiamine depletion).<br /></br>• β-hCG stimulates TSH receptors → transient ↑free T4 and ↓TSH; usually subclinical.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line acute management<br /></br>1. IV isotonic crystalloid ± dextrose after thiamine.<br /></br>2. Antiemetics:<br /></br>   • Pyridoxine (vit B6) ± doxylamine (1st line oral).<br /></br>   • Ondansetron, metoclopramide, promethazine if refractory.<br /></br>3. Nutritional support: small frequent meals → enteral tube feeds → TPN if severe.<br /></br>4. Hospitalization criteria: inability to tolerate PO, &gt;5% pre-pregnancy wt loss, ketonuria, electrolyte imbalance.  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (after stabilization)<br /></br>• Urinalysis (ketones).<br /></br>• CMP for electrolytes/LFTs.<br /></br>• Thyroid studies only to rule out true thyrotoxicosis if clinical suspicion.<br /></br>• Pelvic US if uterine size &gt; dates to exclude molar pregnancy or twins (higher hCG).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“H”yperemesis → think “H2O first.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “Give THEM fluids”<br /></br>T – Thiamine<br /></br>H – Hydration (NS/LR)<br /></br>E – Electrolytes<br /></br>M – Metoclopramide/anti-emetics  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Measure serum prolactin<br /></br>• Elevated prolactin can cause nausea but not severe intractable vomiting nor volume depletion; no galactorrhea or amenorrhea history.  </p></br><p>B. Thyroid ultrasound<br /></br>• TSH suppression here is physiologic; no goiter or thyroid tenderness. US would not change immediate management.  </p></br><p>C. CT scan of the head<br /></br>• Indicated for intracranial pathology (tumor, hemorrhage) causing vomiting; patient lacks neurological signs, and radiation risk in pregnancy.  </p></br><p>E. Propylthiouracil therapy<br /></br>• Used for overt hyperthyroidism in 1st trimester (clinical thyrotoxicosis + elevated free T4). Mild TSH suppression alone without symptoms does not warrant PTU—risk of hepatotoxicity and fetal hypothyroidism.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Hyperemesis gravidarum = early pregnancy, severe N/V, dehydration. First priority is resuscitation with IV fluids (plus thiamine), then antiemetics and supportive care. Other investigations or therapies can wait until the patient is stabilized.</p>
42. A 2-month-old boy is brought to the physician for a well-child examination. He was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy, labor, and delivery. He has been breast-feeding six to eight times daily since birth. He smiles and lifts his head. He is at the 50th percentile for length, 75th percentile for weight, and 25th percentile for head circumference. Examination shows no abnormalities. His mother asks for nutritional recommendations. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation for the infant?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Add cow milk-based formula</br>b. Add pureed fruit</br>c. Add rice cereal</br>d. Switch to soy-based formula</br>e. Begin vitamin C supplementation</br>f. Begin vitamin D supplementation</br>g. Begin vitamin K supplementation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>F. Begin vitamin D supplementation</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key words: “2-month-old,” “exclusively breast-feeding,” “well-child exam,” “no abnormalities.”<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: Only breast milk for nutrition; the baby is growing appropriately.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No formula intake, no solid foods yet, no sun-exposure information.<br /></br>• Board pattern: AAP and USPSTF recommend 400 IU/day of vitamin D for all exclusively (or mostly) breast-fed infants starting within the first few days of life. Formula-fed infants consume fortified vitamin D and usually do not need an extra supplement.<br /></br>Test-taking tip: Any healthy term infant who is breast-feeding ≥ 50 % of feeds needs written vitamin D supplementation in the plan. Do not be distracted by “vitamin K” (already given IM at birth) or “start solids” (not until 4-6 mo).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1 – Identify nutritional gap: Breast milk has very low vitamin D (≈ 20 IU/L).<br /></br>Step 2 – Assess risk: Without supplementation the infant can develop hypocalcemia → rickets, delayed fontanelle closure, bowing of legs.<br /></br>Step 3 – Intervention: Oral vitamin D drops 400 IU daily until the baby is consuming ≥ 1 L (≈ 32 oz) of vitamin D-fortified formula or whole milk after 12 mo.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Breast milk is best overall but “lacks the D.” Always add vitamin D drops.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Exclusively breast-fed. Will start vit D 400 IU PO daily. Parents counseled.”</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Vitamin D (cholecalciferol) is necessary for intestinal Ca²⁺ &amp; PO₄³⁻ absorption and bone mineralization.<br /></br>• Sources: sunlight-driven skin synthesis and fortified foods/formula. Modern lifestyle (indoor living, sunscreen) + exclusive breastfeeding → deficiency risk.<br /></br>• Deficiency in infants: Rickets (rachitic rosary, genu varum), seizures from hypocalcemic tetany.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Notes</br>No lab work needed in an asymptomatic well child.<br /></br>Therapy: 400 IU vitamin D₃ (cholecalciferol) oral drops daily. Continue until adequate dietary intake or age 1 yr.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>• “Breast is best, but it’s missing the ‘D-light.’” → Give the D.<br /></br>• K = one-time shot (K for “Kick-start clotting” at birth).<br /></br>• D = Daily Drops.<br /></br>• Solids start at “4 to 6” (remember the rhyme “sit at 6, eats at 6”).</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Add cow-milk formula: Would provide vitamin D, but unnecessary since exclusive breast-feeding is recommended for the first 6 mo; supplement, don’t substitute.<br /></br>b. Add pureed fruit: Solids are not introduced until 4–6 mo; fruit does not address vitamin D deficiency.<br /></br>c. Add rice cereal: Same timing issue as above.<br /></br>d. Switch to soy-based formula: Only indicated in galactosemia or cow-milk protein allergy.<br /></br>e. Begin vitamin C supplementation: Breast milk already meets vitamin C needs; scurvy is rare in term breast-fed infants.<br /></br>g. Begin vitamin K supplementation: Term newborns receive a single IM dose at birth that provides adequate stores until solid foods; daily oral K is not recommended in the U.S.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>All exclusively (or &gt; 50 %) breast-fed infants need 400 IU oral vitamin D each day starting in the first few days of life to prevent rickets and hypocalcemia.</p>
43. A 67-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of aching in his thighs and buttocks after he walks 200 feet.  </br>His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. Femoral and distal pulses are absent bilaterally. Serum studies show a urea nitrogen concentration of 30 mg/dL and creatinine concentration of 1.5 mg/dL. He undergoes aortic arteriography. Three days later, his serum creatinine concentration is 2.8 mg/dL. Administration of which of the following prior to the arteriography is most likely to have prevented progression of this patient’s renal insufficiency?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Dopamine  </br>b. Mannitol  </br>c. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs  </br>d. Infusion of 0.9 percent saline  </br>e. No intervention is likely to have prevented progression of this patient's renal insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Infusion of 0.9 percent saline</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases/clues<br /></br>• 67-year-old man with peripheral vascular disease → needs diagnostic arteriography (large iodinated contrast load).<br /></br>• Baseline CKD: BUN 30 mg/dL, Cr 1.5 mg/dL.<br /></br>• 3 days post-procedure Cr rises to 2.8 mg/dL → classic timing and pattern for contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN).<br /></br>• Question asks for an intervention given <em>before</em> contrast that “is most likely to have prevented progression.”<br /></br>High-yield pattern<br /></br>Older patient + pre-existing renal impairment + iodinated contrast → think CIN. Main proven prophylaxis = isotonic IV volume expansion (0.9 % saline).  </p></br><p>Test-taking tip<br /></br>Whenever a question mentions contrast exposure, first check baseline renal function and hydration status. If creatinine is already elevated or the patient is volume-depleted, choose isotonic saline hydration (or N-acetylcysteine if offered) as prophylaxis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify risk: CKD + contrast → high CIN risk.  </li></br><li>Pathogenesis of CIN: intrarenal vasoconstriction + medullary ischemia + direct tubular toxicity. Both worsened by <em>volume depletion</em>.  </li></br><li>Evidence-based prevention: isotonic saline 1–3 mL/kg/h starting 6–12 h before and continuing 6–12 h after the study. Hydration dilutes contrast, blunts vasoconstrictive rise in tubuloglomerular feedback, and maintains GFR.<br /></br>Core takeaway: The single most effective, widely proven, inexpensive preventive step for CIN is aggressive isotonic IV hydration.</li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation (real patient)<br /></br>• Baseline eGFR, BUN/Cr<br /></br>• Informed consent re: CIN risk<br /></br>• IV 0.9 % saline ordered: rate, start/stop times<br /></br>• Contrast type &amp; volume used</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Contrast media →<br /></br>• ↑ osmolar load → endothelial dysfunction → adenosine/endothelin-mediated vasoconstriction → ↓ renal medullary blood flow.<br /></br>• Direct tubular epithelial cell toxicity → reactive oxygen species.<br /></br>• Result = acute tubular necrosis pattern peaking 3–5 days; usually non-oliguric; muddy brown casts may appear.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: CKD (Cr &gt;1.2 mg/dL or eGFR &lt;60), diabetes, age &gt;70, heart failure, hypotension, high contrast volume, concurrent nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Pre-procedure<br /></br>• Isotonic saline 0.9 % (best evidence)<br /></br>• Low or iso-osmolar contrast agent<br /></br>• Hold NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, diuretics if possible<br /></br>• Consider N-acetylcysteine (mixed evidence but low risk)  </p></br><p>Post-procedure<br /></br>• Continue hydration<br /></br>• Monitor Cr at 24–48 h  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Wet before you dye.”<br /></br>Visualize a firefighter (IV saline) spraying down kidneys <em>before</em> the paint (contrast) gets on them.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Dopamine (low-dose “renal dose”): Historically thought to ↑ renal flow, but RCTs show no prevention of CIN; may cause tachyarrhythmias.  </p></br><p>b. Mannitol: Osmotic diuretic; can worsen intravascular volume depletion and precipitate CIN. No proven benefit.  </p></br><p>c. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs: Inhibit prostaglandin-mediated afferent dilation → <em>decrease</em> renal perfusion; increase CIN risk.  </p></br><p>e. No intervention: Contradicted by robust evidence that isotonic hydration lowers incidence/severity of CIN.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Contrast-induced nephropathy is largely preventable. The single, evidence-based prophylactic measure is adequate isotonic saline hydration beginning before and continuing after iodinated contrast exposure.</p>
44. A 57-year-old man comes to the physician because of an 8-month history of a nonproductive cough. He has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight gain during the past 6 months despite no change in appetite.
