He has hypertension treated with atenolol. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 39 years. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 98 kg (216 lb); BMI is 32 kg/m2. His temperature is 37.1 °C (98.8 °F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 152/98 mm Hg; one year ago, his blood pressure was 132/78 mm Hg. Examination shows purple striae over the abdomen. His serum potassium concentration is 3.4 mEq/L, and fasting serum glucose concentration is 119 mg/dL. An X-ray of the chest shows a right upper lobe nodule. In addition to a biopsy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. 24-Hour urine collection for measurement of cortisol concentration</br>b. Measurement of plasma metanephrine concentration</br>c. Measurement of serum aldosterone:renin ratio</br>d. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>e. Renal artery duplex scan</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. 24-hour urine collection for measurement of cortisol concentration  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to Cushing syndrome:<br /></br>• Progressive weight gain despite unchanged appetite<br /></br>• New or worsening hypertension (132/78 → 152/98)<br /></br>• Purple abdominal striae (wider, violaceous stretch marks are classic)<br /></br>• Hypokalemia (K⁺ 3.4 mEq/L) and impaired fasting glucose (119 mg/dL)<br /></br>• 39-pack-year smoker with a new right-upper-lobe pulmonary nodule → raises suspicion for ectopic ACTH–producing small-cell lung carcinoma or bronchial carcinoid<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no episodic palpitations/headaches (pheochromocytoma), no muscle cramps/polyuria (primary hyperaldosteronism), no thyroid symptoms.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Recognize stigmata of cortisol excess.<br /></br>2. Remember that work-up of Cushing syndrome is a two-step process:<br /></br>   a. Confirm hypercortisolism (screening).<br /></br>   b. If positive, determine the ACTH source.<br /></br>3. Choose one of the three accepted first-line screening tests (low-dose dexamethasone suppression, late-night salivary cortisol, 24-hour urinary free cortisol). Pick whichever appears in the answer set.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why 24-hour urinary free cortisol?<br /></br>• It is an evidence-based, first-line screening study endorsed by Endocrine Society guidelines to confirm pathologic hypercortisolism.<br /></br>• Before measuring ACTH or imaging the pituitary/adrenals, you must prove that cortisol is actually elevated.<br /></br>• In this vignette, the chest nodule hints at an ectopic ACTH source, but confirmation of excess cortisol is obligatory before localization.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Always confirm excess cortisol first; 24-hour urinary free cortisol is one of the three accepted screening tools.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Pearl  </p></br><p>Ectopic ACTH production → bilateral adrenal hyperplasia → elevated cortisol:<br /></br>• Cortisol increases α₁-receptor sensitivity and up-regulates hepatic gluconeogenesis → hypertension &amp; hyperglycemia.<br /></br>• High cortisol overwhelms 11β-HSD2 in the kidney → cortisol binds mineralocorticoid receptors → hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis.<br /></br>• Protein catabolism &amp; dermal collagen breakdown → wide, purple striae.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Further Work-up  </p></br><p>If screening confirms hypercortisolism:<br /></br>1. Measure plasma ACTH.<br /></br>   • Low ACTH → adrenal source.<br /></br>   • High/normal ACTH → pituitary vs ectopic.<br /></br>2. High-dose dexamethasone suppression or CRH stimulation test ± imaging (MRI pituitary, CT chest/abdomen).<br /></br>Definitive therapy for ectopic ACTH (e.g., small-cell lung cancer) usually involves surgical resection and/or chemotherapy plus medical blockade of steroidogenesis (ketoconazole, metyrapone) if needed.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“DUL” screens for Cushing:<br /></br>D – low-Dose dexamethasone suppression test<br /></br>U – 24-hour Urinary free cortisol<br /></br>L – Late-night salivary cortisol  </p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture a “DUeL” between a moon-faced cowboy (Cushing features) and three competitors labeled D, U, and L.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Plasma metanephrines – screens for pheochromocytoma (paroxysmal headaches, sweating, tachycardia). Patient’s HTN is chronic, and he has classic Cushing stigmata instead.  </p></br><p>C. Aldosterone:renin ratio – evaluates primary hyperaldosteronism (resistant HTN, hypokalemia without striae or glucose intolerance). Would not explain purple striae or pulmonary nodule.  </p></br><p>D. Serum TSH – thyroid disease causes weight change and BP variation but not striae, hypokalemia, or lung nodule.  </p></br><p>E. Renal artery duplex scan – used for renovascular HTN; does not explain striae, hyperglycemia, or hypokalemia.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Purple striae + weight gain + worsened HTN + hypokalemia + smoker with lung nodule = suspect ectopic ACTH → Cushing syndrome.<br /></br>First step: prove cortisol excess with a 24-hour urinary free cortisol measurement.</p>
45. A 12-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 1-year history of recurrent episodes of increased urinary urgency and frequency, increased thirst, and bed wetting. The episodes occur every 3 to 4 weeks and last 2 to 3 days; he does not wet the bed between episodes. He has not had abdominal pain or a burning sensation on urination. He was toilet trained at the age of 3 years and had not wet the bed since the age of 4 years. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His parents divorced 1 year ago; his mother is the custodial parent, and he visits his father on occasional weekends. He is at the 50th percentile for height, at the 95th percentile for weight, and above the 95th percentile for BMI. Genital and pubic hair development are Tanner stage 1. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum glucose    85 mg/dL</br>Urine (first morning void)</br>Specific gravity 1.015</br>Blood            negative</br>Glucose          negative</br>Protein          negative</br></br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Diabetes insipidus</br>b. Primary enuresis</br>c. Psychogenic polydipsia</br>d. Type 1 diabetes mellitus</br>e. Type 2 diabetes mellitus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Psychogenic (primary) polydipsia  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Symptom pattern<br /></br>   • Recurrent bursts of polyuria / polydipsia every 3–4 weeks, lasting only 2–3 days.<br /></br>   • Completely dry and asymptomatic between episodes → argues against a fixed renal or endocrine defect.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Objective data<br /></br>   • First-morning urine specific gravity = 1.015 (normal ability to concentrate).<br /></br>   • Serum glucose 85 mg/dL; urinalysis negative for glucose, blood, protein.<br /></br>   • Normal concentrating ability + normal glucose = rules out diabetes insipidus and both types of diabetes mellitus.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Psychosocial clue<br /></br>   • Parents divorced 1 year ago → common precipitant for stress-related behaviors (excess water-drinking, attention seeking).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Growth / puberty<br /></br>   • Overweight (BMI &gt; 95th %) but pre-pubertal; obesity alone does not create episodic symptoms; helps weigh T2DM but labs are normal.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In any polyuria question, first check serum/urine glucose (DM); next, urine specific gravity (DI vs normal); finally, look for situational triggers (psychogenic).  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why C is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach<br /></br>1. Polyuria differential = Diabetes mellitus, Diabetes insipidus, primary polydipsia, UTI, structural GU issues.<br /></br>2. Normal glucose and no glucosuria exclude DM.<br /></br>3. Ability to concentrate urine (SG &gt; 1.010 on a first-morning sample) excludes central or nephrogenic DI (would have SG &lt; 1.006 despite overnight water deprivation).<br /></br>4. Intermittent episodes tied to possible stressor + normal labs → primary (psychogenic) polydipsia.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: A normal first-morning urine specific gravity essentially rules out diabetes insipidus; episodic symptoms point toward excess voluntary water intake.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Detailed fluid-intake history (timing, quantity, triggers)<br /></br>• Psychosocial assessment (stressors, mood, attention-seeking behaviors)<br /></br>• Education given to family about limiting free water and scheduling voids  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Primary polydipsia: Excessive voluntary water intake → transient dilutional polyuria; kidneys &amp; ADH axis remain intact, so when intake normalizes, concentrating ability returns to normal. Often associated with psychiatric illness or situational stress in children.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Management  </p></br><p>Diagnosis is clinical after exclusion of DM and DI.<br /></br>Optional: Water-deprivation test shows ability to concentrate urine (&gt;600 mOsm/kg) before desmopressin, confirming intact ADH axis.  </p></br><p>Management<br /></br>• Identify/modify psychosocial triggers.<br /></br>• Behavioral strategies: scheduled fluid intake, positive reinforcement, limit evening fluids.<br /></br>• Psychiatric/psychology referral if underlying anxiety or adjustment disorder.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3 Ds for Polyuria” mnemonic:<br /></br>1. Diabetes Mellitus → high glucose, glucosuria<br /></br>2. Diabetes Insipidus → Dilute urine even at Dawn (SG &lt; 1.006)<br /></br>3. Drink (primary polydipsia) → Dilute only when Drinking  </p></br><p>Remember: DI = “Insipid = always insipid urine.” If first-morning SG &gt; 1.010, DI is out.  </p></br><p>───────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Diabetes insipidus<br /></br>   • Would have chronic—not episodic—polyuria/polydipsia.<br /></br>   • First-morning SG &lt; 1.006 regardless of intake.<br /></br>   • Often associated with hypernatremia if water access limited.  </p></br><p>B. Primary enuresis<br /></br>   • Bed-wetting ≥2 times/week for ≥3 months in a child ≥5 y who has never been consistently dry.<br /></br>   • This patient was completely dry from age 4 to 11; his wetting is secondary and episodic.  </p></br><p>D. Type 1 diabetes mellitus<br /></br>   • Polyuria/polydipsia continuous.<br /></br>   • Weight loss, often ketonuria.<br /></br>   • Serum glucose elevated with glucosuria.  </p></br><p>E. Type 2 diabetes mellitus<br /></br>   • Obesity makes it tempting, but glucose would be ≥126 mg/dL fasting or random ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms; glucosuria common.<br /></br>   • Symptoms would be persistent, not episodic.  </p></br><p>Key difference: Only psychogenic polydipsia fits normal labs + intermittent episodes triggered by behavior/stress.</p>
46. A 17-month-old infant with sickle cell disease has a 1-day history of rectal temperatures to 40.6°C (105°F). He has had rhinorrhea and a cough for 3 days. He receives daily penicillin prophylaxis and has received all appropriate immunizations. He is slightly irritable and has clear rhinorrhea. His spleen is palpable 2 cm below the left costal margin. The remainder of the examination is normal. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL</br>Leukocytes 18,700/mm3</br>Segmented neutrophils 65 percent</br>Lymphocytes 30 percent</br>Bands 3 percent</br>Monocytes 2 percent</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy for this patient?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Benzathine penicillin, intramuscularly</br>b. Ceftriaxone, parenterally</br>c. Ciprofloxacin, orally</br>d. A decongestant, orally</br>e. Erythromycin, orally</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Ceftriaxone, parenterally  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words/phrases<br /></br>  – “17-month-old infant with sickle cell disease (SCD)” → functional asplenia by early childhood.<br /></br>  – “Rectal temp 40.6 °C (105 °F) × 1 day” → any fever ≥38.5 °C in SCD = possible sepsis.<br /></br>  – “Rhinorrhea &amp; cough” = viral prodrome possible, but cannot reassure.<br /></br>  – WBC 18,700 with neutrophilia → inflammatory / infectious process.<br /></br>  – “Receives daily penicillin prophylaxis &amp; all immunizations” → ↓ but not elimination of risk; pen-resistant encapsulated organisms still possible.<br /></br>  – “Spleen palpable 2 cm” → still some splenic tissue, but opsonization already markedly impaired.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for exam: In any child with SCD + fever, jump immediately to “treat for invasive encapsulated bacterial infection with IV/IM 3rd-generation cephalosporin.” Do not be distracted by mild URI findings.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify special population – SCD = functional asplenia → high risk bacteremia/sepsis by Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseria meningitidis, Salmonella, etc.<br /></br>Step 2: Recognize red-flag finding – high fever. Guideline: ≥38.5 °C warrants blood cultures and empiric parenteral antibiotics.<br /></br>Step 3: Choose drug – 3rd-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) because:<br /></br>  • Excellent bactericidal activity vs penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae &amp; H. influenzae.<br /></br>  • Single daily dose possible (IM or IV) while awaiting cultures.<br /></br>  • Covers most Gram-negatives that threaten SCD patients (e.g., Salmonella).  </p></br><p>Take-home: Fever in SCD = emergency → give parenteral ceftriaxone immediately after cultures. Document discussion with parents about sepsis risk, cultures drawn, antibiotic given, vitals monitored.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• SCD → repeated splenic vaso-occlusion → autoinfarction by age &lt;5 yr → loss of IgM-mediated opsonization.<br /></br>• Encapsulated bacteria evade phagocytosis unless opsonized → ↑ risk overwhelming sepsis.<br /></br>• Daily oral penicillin &amp; conjugate vaccines greatly lower but do not abolish risk; resistant serotypes still occur.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Immediate ED/clinic protocol for febrile SCD child:<br /></br>1. Blood culture, CBC, reticulocyte count.<br /></br>2. Parenteral 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 50–75 mg/kg IM/IV).<br /></br>3. Observe ± admit; continue antibiotics until cultures negative ≥24–48 h and child clinically well.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Fever + Sickle = Stab with Cef-TRI-axe-one.”<br /></br>(Fever, Sickle → Treat Rapidly IM/IV with Ceftriaxone).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Benzathine penicillin IM<br /></br>• Long-acting prophylaxis for rheumatic fever or secondary prophylaxis in SCD if oral doses missed.<br /></br>• Not adequate for acute bacteremia and does not cover penicillin-resistant pneumococci.  </p></br><p>c. Ciprofloxacin orally<br /></br>• Fluoroquinolone active against Gram-neg rods but poor activity vs S. pneumoniae; oral route slower. Not first-line empiric therapy for febrile SCD child.  </p></br><p>d. Decongestant orally<br /></br>• Only symptomatic relief for URI; ignores life-threatening risk of sepsis.  </p></br><p>e. Erythromycin orally<br /></br>• Covers atypicals and some Gram-positives but unreliable vs H. influenzae and resistant pneumococci; oral therapy inadequate.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Attributing fever to mild viral URI in SCD patient—always over-treat rather than under-treat.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>A child with sickle cell disease and any significant fever must receive immediate parenteral broad-spectrum antibiotics—ceftriaxone is the drug of choice—because functional asplenia places them at extreme risk for rapid, fatal sepsis from encapsulated organisms.</p>
47. A 32-year-old primigravid woman at 27 weeks’ gestation is brought to the emergency department because of painless vaginal bleeding for 6 hours. She has to change sanitary pads every 2 hours. She has had no prenatal care. Ultrasonography at 16 weeks’ gestation showed a normal fetus and a posterior fundal placenta. Her pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pelvic examination shows normal external genitalia. The uterus measures 27 cm and is nontender. Speculum examination shows a 2-cm friable ulcer on the cervix. The cervix is long, the external os is 1 cm dilated, and the internal os is closed. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the bleeding?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Cervical cancer</br>b. Cervical condylomata acuminata</br>c. Endocervical polyp</br>d. Hydatidiform mole</br>e. Placenta previa</br>f. Premature labor</br>g. Spontaneous abortion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Cervical cancer  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues to circle on test day<br /></br>• 27-week pregnancy with PAINLESS vaginal bleeding ⇒ always think “placenta previa … or something at the cervix.”<br /></br>• 16-week ultrasound: placenta was posterior fundal (therefore NOT low-lying; essentially rules out placenta previa).<br /></br>• Speculum exam (the safest way to look when previa is possible) shows a 2-cm FRIABLE ULCER on the cervix. “Friable” = bleeds easily when touched → classic for invasive cervical carcinoma.<br /></br>• Uterus nontender, normal size for gestational age, no contractions → argues against placental abruption or preterm labor.<br /></br>• Internal os closed, external os only 1 cm → not an actively laboring cervix.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any question on third-trimester bleeding demands you first rule out placenta previa/abruption. If facts don’t fit (normal placenta, no pain/tenderness), redirect your search to the cervix or vagina.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why option A is correct)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Location of the placenta is normal → bleeding source must be lower genital tract.  </li></br><li>Friable ulcerated lesion on cervix = pathognomonic description of invasive cervical cancer.  </li></br><li>Pregnancy increases cervical vascularity, making cancer lesions bleed more easily with minimal trauma (e.g., walking, intercourse), producing painless bleeding.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Painless bleeding late in pregnancy + friable cervical lesion = think cervical cancer, especially when previa is excluded by ultrasound.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real-life): “Visualized a 2-cm friable ulcer on ectocervix at 6 o’clock that bled on contact—concern for invasive carcinoma. No contractions, fetal heart rate reassuring.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Most cases are HPV-16/18-related squamous cell carcinoma arising at the transformation zone.<br /></br>• Pregnancy does not accelerate carcinogenesis but makes existing lesions more vascular and symptomatic.<br /></br>• Risk factors: lack of screening, early sexual activity, multiple partners, smoking, immunosuppression.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot (high yield)  </p></br><p>Immediate:<br /></br>• Avoid digital cervical exam (bleeding).<br /></br>• Fetal monitoring, maternal stabilization.<br /></br>Definitive diagnosis:<br /></br>• Directed punch biopsy of the lesion (safe in pregnancy).<br /></br>Staging &amp; treatment depend on gestational age and stage:<br /></br>• Early stage (<IA2): conization or simple trachelectomy; delivery at term.<br />• Advanced disease or >24 wk: delay definitive therapy until postpartum if feasible; otherwise consider chemo-radiation with maternal counseling.</p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Friable = Fatal” → a friable cervical lesion in pregnancy signals cancer.<br /></br>“Placenta OK? Look at the cervix today!” – if ultrasound says placenta is normal, shift focus to cervical pathology.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Cervical condylomata acuminata<br /></br>• HPV 6/11 warts are soft, exophytic, cauliflower-like, not ulcerative/friable ulcers. Typically cause minimal bleeding.  </p></br><p>C. Endocervical polyp<br /></br>• Appears as smooth, cherry-red protrusion from os; rarely exceeds a few millimeters and is not ulcerated/friable.  </p></br><p>D. Hydatidiform mole<br /></br>• Presents in 1st trimester with excessive nausea, very high β-hCG, uterine size &gt;&gt; dates, “snow-storm” ultrasound. Not compatible with a normal 27-week fetus.  </p></br><p>E. Placenta previa<br /></br>• Classic painless 3rd-trimester bleeding BUT ultrasound must show placenta covering/near internal os. Prior scan showed posterior fundal placenta → rules out previa.  </p></br><p>F. Premature labor<br /></br>• Would have regular painful contractions, progressive cervical dilation, and often a tender uterus. Here cervix long, minimal dilation, no pain.  </p></br><p>G. Spontaneous abortion<br /></br>• Defined as pregnancy loss &lt;20 weeks (some sources &lt;22-24 wk). At 27 weeks this is preterm labor, not abortion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>When third-trimester bleeding is painless and placenta previa is excluded by imaging, a friable cervical lesion virtually clinches the diagnosis of cervical cancer. Always perform a speculum exam before any digital exam in bleeding pregnancies to avoid missing cervical pathology (and to avoid provoking hemorrhage if a previa is present).</p>
48. A 1-week-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 2-day history of fever and irritability. His temperature is 39°C (102.2°F). Examination shows decreased motion of the left hip with tenderness. An x-ray of the hip shows increased joint space. Aspiration of the hip joint yields 3 mL of purulent fluid. A culture of the fluid is most likely to grow which of the following organisms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Haemophilus influenzae type b</br>b. Listeria monocytogenes</br>c. Neisseria gonorrhoeae</br>d. Shigella boydii</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• 1-week-old infant + fever (39 °C) + irritability = red flag for serious bacterial infection.</br>• “Decreased motion of the left hip with tenderness” → classic presentation of septic arthritis; neonates show pseudoparalysis because moving the joint hurts.</br>• Hip x-ray: “increased joint space” suggests effusion.</br>• “Aspiration … purulent fluid” clinches septic arthritis (must be treated as an emergency to prevent joint destruction and growth disturbance).</br>Key high-yield cue: Most common organisms in neonatal septic arthritis = Staphylococcus aureus, group B Streptococcus, and gram-negative rods (e.g., E. coli). Among the answer choices, only Staph aureus fits.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step questions, once you identify septic arthritis, first ask “What age group is this?” then recall the bugs most common for that age.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Recognize the syndrome: acute mono-articular joint pain, limited ROM, systemic signs → septic arthritis.</br>2. Age &lt;3 months → think Staph aureus ➝ GBS ➝ gram-negatives.</br>3. Choose the organism present in the options that matches “most common” or “typical” for that age group—in this stem, Staph aureus.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Staph aureus is the single most common cause of septic arthritis in ALL age groups; in neonates it remains at the top of the list.</p></br><p>What to document (real life): joint appearance, ROM, neurovascular status, vitals, aspiration results, empiric antibiotic choice, plan for surgical drainage.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Spread: almost always hematogenous seeding of the synovium. Neonatal circulation to the metaphysis allows easy bacterial entry into the joint capsule.</br>• Staph aureus virulence: Protein A (evades opsonization), adhesins (cling to cartilage), cytotoxins → rapid cartilage destruction within hours.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment</br>• Emergent arthrotomy or needle drainage + IV empiric therapy:</br>  – Vancomycin (cover MRSA) PLUS third-gen cephalosporin (e.g., cefotaxime) for gram-negatives until cultures return.</br>• Narrow therapy once culture/ sensitivities result (e.g., nafcillin/oxacillin for MSSA; vancomycin or linezolid for MRSA).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“SEPtic joints in babies love the ‘G-NEG STAFF’ ”</br>S – Staph aureus (most common)</br>E – E. coli / other gram-negative rods</br>P – (Group B) Strep (think “Pregnancy” transmission)</p></br><p>Visual: Picture a STAFF of golden staph bacteria poking inside the ball-and-socket hip joint of a newborn.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Haemophilus influenzae type b</br>• Historically common in 2–5 y/o before Hib vaccine; rare in neonates today.</br>• Would expect an undervaccinated toddler, not a 1-week-old.</p></br><p>b. Listeria monocytogenes</br>• Causes neonatal sepsis and meningitis from vertical transmission, not primary septic arthritis.</p></br><p>c. Neisseria gonorrhoeae</br>• Causes septic arthritis in sexually active adolescents/adults; in neonates mainly causes conjunctivitis (ophthalmia neonatorum).</p></br><p>d. Shigella boydii</br>• Enteric pathogen → dysentery; may trigger REACTIVE arthritis weeks later, which is sterile and occurs in HLA-B27 individuals, not acute purulent arthritis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In any age, but especially in neonates, think Staphylococcus aureus first for purulent septic arthritis of a single joint.</p>
49. A 27-year-old man with schizophrenia is brought to the emergency department by his mother because he says he has been unable to see anything for the past 3 hours. They have been on a car trip across the country for the past 6 days. Last night, he reported hearing voices, so they stopped in an emergency department by the highway; he was administered haloperidol and was released 1 hour later. He has no other history of serious illness. His only regularly scheduled medication is intramuscular fluphenazine every 3 weeks. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 142/78 mm Hg. On examination, he is lying on a stretcher, moaning, with his upper extremities held rigidly to his side. The eyes are rotated superiorly. The neck is rigid. On mental status examination, he is alert but too distressed to answer questions. His leukocyte count is 9800/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current visual problems?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Adverse effect of haloperidol</br>b. Factitious disorder</br>c. Hallucination related to his schizophrenia</br>d. Meningitis</br>e. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Adverse effect of haloperidol (acute dystonic reaction)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key time cue: “unable to see anything for the past 3 hours” after receiving haloperidol “last night” → onset within hours of a dopamine-blocking antipsychotic = textbook for acute dystonia.<br /></br>• Classic physical findings: </br>  – “Eyes are rotated superiorly” → oculogyric crisis.<br /></br>  – “Upper extremities held rigidly to his side” and “neck is rigid” → sustained muscle contraction.<br /></br>• Temp 37.1 °C, normal WBC → argues against infection or neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), which classically presents with fever and leukocytosis.<br /></br>• Cognitive approach: Anytime a question mentions sudden muscle rigidity, abnormal eye deviation, and recent high-potency antipsychotic, think acute dystonia.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Use the “ADAPT” mnemonic for extrapyramidal symptoms after antipsychotics:</br>A – Acute Dystonia (hours–days)</br>D – akathisia (Days)</br>A – parkinsonism (weeks)</br>T – Tardive dyskinesia (months–years)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Identify recent exposure to a high-potency D2 blocker (haloperidol).<br /></br>2. Note timeline (hours) and focal muscle contractions → acute dystonia.<br /></br>3. Oculogyric crisis makes the patient “unable to see” because his eyes are forced upward.<br /></br>4. Therefore the visual disturbance is not true blindness; it is an antipsychotic adverse effect.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Acute dystonia is an extrapyramidal side effect caused by dopamine blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway and is treated emergently with an anticholinergic (benztropine) or antihistamine with anticholinergic properties (diphenhydramine).</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record drug given, time of onset of symptoms, physical findings (oculogyric crisis, limb/neck rigidity), vitals (lack of fever), and treatment (benztropine/diphenhydramine) with response.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• High-potency antipsychotics (haloperidol, fluphenazine) strongly block D2 receptors → acute imbalance: ↓ dopamine vs. unchanged acetylcholine in basal ganglia → excessive cholinergic tone → sustained involuntary muscle contraction (dystonia).<br /></br>• Oculogyric crisis is a focal dystonia of extra-ocular muscles forcing the gaze upward.<br /></br>• Occurs within hours to a few days after dose initiation or increase.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes</br>1. Immediate: IM benztropine or diphenhydramine. Symptoms often resolve within minutes.<br /></br>2. Prevent recurrence: lower dose, switch to lower-potency or atypical antipsychotic, or add scheduled anticholinergic if antipsychotic must be continued.<br /></br>(No imaging or labs needed unless diagnosis uncertain.)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</br>• “Haloperidol makes eyes Roll-o-peridol” – reminds you of oculogyric crisis from haloperidol.<br /></br>• “Benztropine for the Benzed-up muscles” – use benztropine to calm the rigid muscles.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Factitious disorder<br /></br>   – Would require intentional feigning of blindness without physical signs (no eye deviation). Here the abnormal exam (locked upward gaze, neck rigidity) is objective, not feigned.</p></br><p>C. Hallucination related to schizophrenia<br /></br>   – Visual hallucinations are uncommon in primary schizophrenia (auditory &gt; visual) and would not produce fixed upward eye deviation or neck rigidity.</p></br><p>D. Meningitis<br /></br>   – Typically presents with fever, severe headache, photophobia, nuchal rigidity, and abnormal CSF. This patient is afebrile, has normal WBC, and just received haloperidol.</p></br><p>E. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome<br /></br>   – Requires hyperthermia (&gt;38 °C), severe “lead-pipe” rigidity, autonomic instability, elevated CK, and leukocytosis. Only neck rigidity is present; no fever or autonomic findings. Timeline for NMS is days–weeks after starting drug, not hours.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Within hours of a high-potency antipsychotic, sudden eye deviation and muscle rigidity = acute dystonia. Treat with benztropine or diphenhydramine.</p>
50. A 32-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of changes in his behavior during the past 2 weeks. Four weeks ago, he witnessed a close friend die in a motor vehicle collision. On the evening of the collision, the patient had offered to drive his friend home after they had a few beers, but the friend declined. As the friend walked away from the patient's car, he was struck by an oncoming vehicle. The patient states that he does not remember much about the incident. During the past 2 weeks, his wife reports that he has been awakening nightly in a sweat and shouting "Watch out!" She states that he has been more emotionally distant and that he has not wanted to socialize with friends. He has not driven his car for 10 days. The patient reports difficulty sleeping and concentrating and says that "things just don't seem real at times." He says he used to drink alcohol only rarely but has been drinking two beers nightly for 3 weeks to help him calm his mind and sleep. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 145/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he has a flat affect but is irritable. He says that he has not been feeling much of anything lately. He has thought about death frequently during the past 2 weeks but says he does not want to commit suicide. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Adjustment disorder</br>c. Bereavement</br>d. Dissociative identity disorder</br>e. Major depressive disorder</br>f. Panic disorder</br>g. Substance-induced mood disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Acute stress disorder (ASD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key trauma cue: “witnessed a close friend die in a motor-vehicle collision.” Exposure to threatened death is a DSM-5 trauma criterion.</br>• Timing: Trauma occurred 4 weeks (≈28 days) ago. ASD must begin within 3 days and resolve within 1 month of the event—he is still inside that window.</br>• Intrusion symptoms: nightly nightmares (“Watch out!”).</br>• Avoidance: stopped driving his car; socially withdrawn.</br>• Negative mood/dissociation: feels emotionally numb, “things just don’t seem real” (derealization).</br>• Hyperarousal: sleep difficulty, irritability, concentration problems, nocturnal sweating.</br>• Functional impairment: marital concern, social isolation, increased alcohol use.</br>These features fulfill DSM-5 criteria for ASD and nothing in the stem exceeds the 1-month duration required for PTSD.</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Tip:<br /></br>When a trauma vignette appears, immediately anchor on two axes:<br /></br>1. Type of stressor (life-threatening vs non-life-threatening)<br /></br>2. Duration since event (&lt;1 mo = ASD, &gt;1 mo = PTSD, &lt;3 mo non-traumatic = Adjustment)<br /></br>This simple timeline filter prevents most answer-choice traps.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach:</br>1. Confirm exposure to traumatic event → yes.</br>2. Identify core clusters (intrusion, avoidance, negative mood/dissociation, hyperarousal) → present.</br>3. Verify duration 3 days – 1 month → trauma 4 weeks ago, symptoms ongoing but &lt;1 month ⇒ ASD.</br>4. Exclude alternative diagnoses → see “Incorrect Answer Analysis” below.</p></br><p>Take-home Message:<br /></br>Acute stress disorder = PTSD-like symptoms that occur and resolve within the first month after a traumatic event.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient):<br /></br>• Nature/date of trauma exposure<br /></br>• Symptom clusters &amp; onset<br /></br>• Functional impairment<br /></br>• Safety assessment (suicidality)<br /></br>• Substance use<br /></br>• Plan: psychoeducation, trauma-focused CBT referral, consider short-term pharmacologic sleep aid if needed.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Trauma triggers excessive amygdala activation, impaired prefrontal inhibition, and dysregulated hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis.<br /></br>• ASD represents an acute maladaptive stress response; roughly 50% transition to PTSD if symptoms persist beyond 1 month.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>First-line:</br>1. Trauma-focused cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), especially exposure-based techniques and cognitive restructuring.<br /></br>2. Short-term pharmacologic support (if needed): low-dose non-benzodiazepine sleep aids (e.g., trazodone) or prazosin for severe nightmares; SSRIs usually reserved for persistent (&gt;1 mo) symptoms.</p></br><p>Diagnostics:</br>No labs/imaging required for diagnosis, but screen for comorbid substance use and depression; monitor suicidality.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“3-3-1 Rule” for Trauma Disorders<br /></br>• ≥1 intrusive thought/nightmare<br /></br>• ≥3 days duration<br /></br>• ≤1 month after event → Acute Stress Disorder<br /></br>If &gt;1 month, switch the “A” to “P” (Post-Traumatic).</p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a calendar with the trauma date circled; shade only the first page (&lt;1 month) in red = ASD, turn the page (&gt;1 month) = PTSD.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Adjustment disorder<br /></br>• Trigger is a non-life-threatening stressor (e.g., job loss, breakup).<br /></br>• Lacks trauma-specific intrusion or hyperarousal.  </p></br><p>C. Bereavement (normal or complicated)<br /></br>• Grief waves but not flashbacks, derealization, or avoidance of trauma reminders.<br /></br>• Emotional pain is sadness rather than fear/helplessness; sleep/concentration impairment milder.</p></br><p>D. Dissociative identity disorder<br /></br>• Requires ≥2 distinct personality states with amnesia for everyday events. Not described here.</p></br><p>E. Major depressive disorder<br /></br>• Needs ≥2 weeks depressed mood or anhedonia plus ≥5 SIGECAPS criteria.<br /></br>• Patient’s core problem is trauma re-experiencing, not pervasive depression; affect is “flat” but not persistently sad or anhedonic.</p></br><p>F. Panic disorder<br /></br>• Recurrent unexpected panic attacks with ≥1 month worry/behavioral change. No episodes of acute sympathetic surge (palpitations, chest pain, etc.) described.</p></br><p>G. Substance-induced mood disorder<br /></br>• Symptoms must be temporally related to intoxication/withdrawal and exceed simple coping use. Two nightly beers began after symptoms, not before.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Because the patient exhibits multiple PTSD-type symptoms beginning within one month of a traumatic event, the most likely diagnosis is Acute Stress Disorder.</p>
51. A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-week history of moderate pain in his legs when walking. He says the pain begins after he walks approximately 50 feet; it improves if he stops walking but returns when he tries to move again. He says the pain is worse in his right leg. He has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. His medications are lisinopril, amlodipine, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 40 years. He appears in no acute distress. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 107 kg (235 lb); BMI is 33 kg/m2. His blood pressure is 150/86 mm Hg. Examination shows sparse hair from 6 cm above the foot to the dorsum of the foot bilaterally. Lower extremity pulses are 1+. At this time, it is most appropriate to evaluate this patient for which of the following conditions?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Abdominal aortic aneurysm</br>b. Carotid artery stenosis</br>c. Deep venous thrombosis</br>d. Peripheral neuropathy</br>e. Venous insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA)  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: 67-year-old man, 40-pack-year smoker, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, calf pain after 50 ft that stops with rest, diminished distal pulses, loss of distal hair.<br /></br>• Classic presentation of intermittent claudication → peripheral arterial disease (PAD).<br /></br>• Question stem: “At this time, it is most appropriate to evaluate this patient for which condition?” = What condition is epidemiologically linked to newly diagnosed PAD and for which evidence-based screening is recommended?</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a vignette establishes PAD, immediately think: 1) associated atherosclerotic beds (coronary, carotid) and 2) screenable complications (AAA). Only AAA has a strong, guideline-supported one-time screening test in asymptomatic people fitting this patient’s profile.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Diagnose PAD from exertional leg pain relieved by rest + exam findings.<br /></br>2. Recognize he is a 65–75 y male who has EVER smoked.<br /></br>3. USPSTF &amp; SVS guidelines: one-time abdominal duplex ultrasonography to screen for AAA in this exact demographic; risk is even higher when another atherosclerotic disease (PAD) is present.<br /></br>Core takeaway: New PAD in an older male smoker → must rule out silent AAA.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Discussed USPSTF recommendation for one-time abdominal ultrasound screening for AAA given age 67, 80-pack-year history, and newly diagnosed PAD. Patient consented.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• PAD, carotid disease, CAD, and AAA all share the same underlying pathology: systemic atherosclerosis.<br /></br>• Smoking accelerates elastin degradation and transmural inflammation in the abdominal aorta → loss of medial elastic fibers → aneurysm.<br /></br>• Prevalence of AAA in men &gt;65 who smoke ≈ 5–10%; concomitant PAD increases odds ratio ≈ 2-3×.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Notes<br /></br>AAA screening:<br /></br>• Modality: abdominal duplex ultrasound (cheap, non-invasive, 95% sensitive).<br /></br>• Frequency: one-time if normal (&lt;3 cm).  </p></br><p>PAD management (not directly asked but high-yield):<br /></br>1. Smoking cessation (most effective).<br /></br>2. Structured exercise program.<br /></br>3. High-intensity statin (already on atorvastatin, verify dose).<br /></br>4. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel).<br /></br>5. Cilostazol or revascularization for lifestyle-limiting claudication after conservative therapy.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PAD = ‘PADS’ remind you to screen for A-A-A”<br /></br>P  – Peripheral<br /></br>A  – Arterial<br /></br>D  – Disease<br /></br>S  – Screen for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm  </p></br><p>Visual: Imagine a road sign “65-75, SMOKER, PAD → NEXT EXIT: AAA ULTRASOUND.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Carotid artery stenosis – Asymptomatic screening is NOT recommended by USPSTF (grade D). Only evaluated pre-op for CABG/CEA or with neurologic symptoms.<br /></br>C. Deep venous thrombosis – Causes acute unilateral swelling &amp; pain, not exercise-induced claudication. No indication to screen an asymptomatic patient.<br /></br>D. Peripheral neuropathy – Presents with burning/tingling at rest, loss of sensation, ulcers; pain not classically exertional/relieved by rest. Also, neuropathy screening is tied to diabetes, not PAD.<br /></br>E. Venous insufficiency – Produces chronic edema, hyperpigmentation, stasis dermatitis, ulceration improved by leg elevation, not exertional calf pain.</p></br><p>Key difference: Only AAA carries a strong, evidence-based screening mandate in this specific high-risk population.</p>
52. A 52-year-old man comes to the physician 2 weeks after he noted yellowing of his skin, which has since resolved spontaneously. He has hypertension and osteoarthritis. His medications are hydrochlorothiazide and tramadol. He uses intravenous heroin weekly; his last injection was 2 days ago. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 79 kg (175 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show:</br>Bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL</br>Alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L</br>AST 250 U/L</br>ALT 300 U/L</br>Hepatitis A antibody negative</br>HBsAg negative</br>HBsAb negative</br>Anti-HCV negative</br>Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Anticentromere antibody testing</br>b. Anti-HBc IgM testing</br>c. Anti-smooth muscle antibody test</br>d. Determination of serum transferrin saturation</br>e. Serum antinuclear antibody assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Anti-HBc IgM testing  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Acute, self-limited jaundice that resolved in ~2 weeks → suggests a transient hepatocellular injury rather than chronic liver disease.<br /></br>• Hepatocellular enzyme pattern: AST 250 &amp; ALT 300 U/L (≈5–6× upper limit); alkaline phosphatase only mildly ↑ → favors viral or toxic hepatitis.<br /></br>• High-risk exposure: weekly IV heroin (needle sharing) → classic risk factor for hepatitis B virus (HBV) transmission.<br /></br>• Serologies at presentation:<br /></br>  – HBsAg negative<br /></br>  – HBsAb (anti-HBs) negative<br /></br>  – Anti-HCV negative<br /></br>  – HAV IgG/IgM negative<br /></br>This combination makes students think “no hepatitis,” but it actually fits the classic “window period” of acute HBV infection, when:<br /></br> HBsAg has disappeared, anti-HBs has not yet appeared, and the ONLY positive marker is anti-HBc IgM.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any patient with recent jaundice + risk factors + negative HBsAg &amp; anti-HBs should trigger the thought, “Check anti-HBc IgM for the HBV window period.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify pattern: Brief jaundice + hepatocellular enzymes = acute hepatitis.  </li></br><li>Assess risk factors: IV drug use → HBV/HCV risk.  </li></br><li>Interpret serology: Both HBsAg &amp; anti-HBs negative = suspicious for HBV window period.  </li></br><li>Next diagnostic step: Order anti-HBc IgM to confirm acute HBV.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Anti-HBc IgM is the sole serologic marker of HBV during the window period and therefore confirms acute HBV infection when other HBV markers are negative.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• History of IV drug use and timing of last injection<br /></br>• Onset, duration, and resolution of jaundice<br /></br>• Full HBV serology panel ordered, including anti-HBc IgM<br /></br>• Counseling on transmission risk and vaccination of contacts  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• HBV serologic timeline:<br /></br>  – HBsAg appears first, indicates infectivity.<br /></br>  – Anti-HBc IgM appears shortly after symptoms begin and persists for 4-6 mo.<br /></br>  – HBsAg disappears → “window.”<br /></br>  – Anti-HBs (protective) forms later, marking recovery or immunity.<br /></br>• During the window period, anti-HBc IgM is the ONLY positive marker.<br /></br>• Failure to recognize this period is a common exam pitfall.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Work-up Review  </p></br><p>Acute HBV (most immunocompetent adults):<br /></br>• Supportive care; &gt;95 % clear the virus.<br /></br>• Antivirals (tenofovir, entecavir) reserved for fulminant hepatitis or prolonged severe disease.<br /></br>• Counsel on alcohol abstinence, needle hygiene, and vaccination of close contacts.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic panel to order: HBsAg, anti-HBc IgM, anti-HBs, HBV DNA (viral load), ± liver function tests.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Window = Core”:<br /></br>When the window shades are down (HBsAg gone) and the sunlight (anti-HBs) hasn’t come up, the CORE (anti-HBc IgM) is still in the room.  </p></br><p>HBV Serology Ladder:<br /></br>S (surface) – C (core) – S (surface antibody)<br /></br>Positive in that order; only C remains in the middle.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Anticentromere antibody → Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST). No jaundice or transaminase rise; irrelevant here.  </p></br><p>c. Anti-smooth muscle antibody → Autoimmune hepatitis; typically in young women, ANA often positive, no IV drug risk, often markedly ↑ ALT (&gt;1000 U/L).  </p></br><p>d. Serum transferrin saturation → Hemochromatosis; chronic iron overload causes cirrhosis, diabetes, bronze skin; not a brief self-limited jaundice.  </p></br><p>e. ANA assay → Broad marker for autoimmune diseases (lupus, autoimmune hepatitis types 1); again pattern and demographics do not fit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A patient with recent jaundice, elevated AST/ALT, IV drug use, and negative HBsAg &amp; anti-HBs is in the HBV window period. Anti-HBc IgM is the single best test to confirm acute hepatitis B infection.</p>
53. A 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of increasing difficulty with swallowing. He first noted difficulty swallowing solids; during the past 3 weeks, he has had difficulty swallowing fluids. He has had an 18-kg (40-lb) weight loss during this time. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He has smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 34 years and drinks 16 oz of vodka three times weekly. The patient is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m^2. He appears cachectic. Examination shows no other abnormalities. A percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube is placed, and enteral nutrition is begun. During the next 36 hours, this patient is at increased risk for which of the following laboratory abnormalities?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Hyperkalemia</br>b. Hypernatremia</br>c. Hyperphosphatemia</br>d. Hyponatremia</br>e. Hypophosphatemia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Hypophosphatemia</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>• “3-month history of difficulty swallowing” + 18-kg weight loss → severe protein-calorie malnutrition (classic esophageal carcinoma presentation).<br /></br>• “PEG tube is placed, and enteral nutrition is begun” → rapid nutritional repletion after a prolonged fasting/low-intake state.<br /></br>• Time frame: “during the next 36 hours” → early phase after restarting calories when metabolic shifts are maximal.<br /></br>Key testing pattern: malnourished + new calories → think refeeding syndrome → look for electrolyte that predictably falls (PO4³⁻).</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any USMLE vignette combining (1) cachexia/malnutrition, (2) sudden carbohydrate load (TPN, tube feeds, IV dextrose), and (3) ask for a lab change → default to hypophosphatemia unless explicitly ruled out.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Chronic starvation → ↓ insulin, ↑ glucagon/cortisol → body catabolizes fat &amp; protein, total body stores of PO4, K, Mg already depleted although serum levels may appear “normal.”<br /></br>2. Refeeding (especially carbohydrates) → surge in insulin.<br /></br>3. Insulin drives glucose + PO4³⁻ + K⁺ + Mg²⁺ into cells for glycolysis/ATP synthesis.<br /></br>4. Serum phosphate plummets first and to the greatest degree → hallmark of refeeding syndrome.<br /></br>5. Consequences: muscle weakness, respiratory failure (↓ diaphragmatic ATP), hemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, arrhythmias.</p></br><p>Documentation point: Before initiating feeds in a severely malnourished patient, plan for “refeeding labs (BMP, Mg, PO4) q6–8 h, thiamine 100 mg IV, start feeds at 10 kcal/kg/day.”</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Refeeding syndrome = rapid intracellular shift of electrolytes after insulin spike.<br /></br>• Risk factors: BMI &lt;18.5, &gt;10% weight loss in 2 mo, little/no intake for &gt;7 days, alcoholism, cancer cachexia.<br /></br>• Phosphate is the rate-limiting substrate for ATP, 2,3-BPG, and leukocyte function; depletion → organ dysfunction.</p></br><p>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review</br>1. Check baseline BMP, Mg, PO4 before feeding.<br /></br>2. Give thiamine prior to first carbohydrate load (prevents Wernicke encephalopathy).<br /></br>3. Start feeds slowly (≤10 kcal/kg/day) and advance over 4–7 days.<br /></br>4. Replace PO4, K, Mg aggressively as needed.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“Re-PHED-ing syndrome”<br /></br>R = Restart nutrition<br /></br>P = PO4 drops<br /></br>H = Hypo-K, Hypo-Mg (but focus on phosphate)<br /></br>E = Electrolytes shift<br /></br>D = Deadly if not recognized</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Hyperkalemia – Insulin causes K⁺ to move INTO cells → hypokalemia, not hyper.<br /></br>b. Hypernatremia – Sodium balance is largely unaffected by insulin-driven shifts; disturbances are usually fluid related, not classic for refeeding.<br /></br>c. Hyperphosphatemia – The opposite of the hallmark finding; occurs in tumor lysis or renal failure, not refeeding.<br /></br>d. Hyponatremia – Can occur if hypotonic fluids are given, but not the classic early abnormality asked on exams.<br /></br>e. Hypophosphatemia – Correct, see above.</p></br><p>Take-Home Message</br>In any malnourished patient who is suddenly refed, the first and most dangerous electrolyte abnormality is hypophosphatemia due to an insulin-mediated intracellular shift. Monitor and replace phosphate, potassium, and magnesium while starting feeds slowly.</p>
54. A 19-year-old woman with celiac disease comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of daily diffuse aching in her arms and legs. She has not had weakness or numbness. She continues to have diarrhea approximately 5 days weekly. She tries to avoid eating gluten but has found it difficult to maintain an entirely gluten-free diet. Her medications are ferrous sulfate and an oral contraceptive. Her last examination was 2 years ago. She appears tired. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. Her pulse is 67/min, and blood pressure is 105/72 mm Hg. The abdomen is soft, mildly tender, and nondistended; there is no rebound, guarding, or masses. There is mild weakness of the hip flexors and extensors. Serum studies show:</br>Na+ 137 mEq/L</br>K+ 3.9 mEq/L</br>Cl– 105 mEq/L</br>HCO3– 22 mEq/L</br>Ca2+ 8.4 mg/dL</br>Urea nitrogen 20 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL</br>Phosphorus 2.7 mg/dL</br>Alkaline phosphatase 175 U/L</br>Parathyroid hormone, intact 870 pg/mL</br>25-Hydroxyvitamin D 7 ng/mL (N>20 ng/mL)</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Hereditary hypophosphatemic rickets</br>b. 1α-Hydroxylase deficiency</br>c. Osteodystrophy</br>d. Osteomalacia</br>e. Primary hyperparathyroidism</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Osteomalacia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>Key clues that immediately narrow the differential:<br /></br>• Known celiac disease + ongoing diarrhea → fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption<br /></br>• Low 25-OH vitamin D (7 ng/mL; normal &gt; 20)<br /></br>• Hypocalcemia (Ca 8.4 mg/dL) and hypophosphatemia (P 2.7 mg/dL)<br /></br>• Very high PTH (870 pg/mL) and elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP 175 U/L)<br /></br>• Diffuse bone pain + proximal muscle weakness (hip flexors/extensors)  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Low D, low Ca, low PO4, high PTH, high ALP + bone pain” = defective mineralization of osteoid in an adult → osteomalacia.  </p></br><p>Test-taking approach: 1) Look for malabsorption or low sun exposure, 2) check 25-OH vitamin D first (storage form), 3) match the classic lab pattern above. Anything outside that pattern should push you to other answer choices.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING (Why the given answer is correct)  </p></br><p>Step 1 – Etiology: Celiac disease → fat-soluble vitamin loss → severe vitamin D deficiency (shown by low 25-OH level).  </p></br><p>Step 2 – Pathophysiology cascade:<br /></br>• ↓ Vitamin D → ↓ intestinal Ca/PO4 absorption → mild hypocalcemia &amp; hypophosphatemia.<br /></br>• ↓ Ca triggers secondary hyperparathyroidism (PTH 870 pg/mL).<br /></br>• PTH further wastes PO4 in urine and stimulates osteoblast activity → ↑ ALP.<br /></br>• Result = inadequate mineralization of newly formed osteoid in adults = osteomalacia (childhood equivalent = rickets).  </p></br><p>Step 3 – Clinical presentation: bone pain, diffuse aching, waddling gait, proximal muscle weakness; fractures if severe. Everything fits the vignette.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In adults with malabsorption, chronic kidney/liver disease, or little sun exposure, think osteomalacia and memorize the classic lab quartet (↓Ca, ↓PO4, ↑ALP, ↑PTH) plus low 25-OH vitamin D.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls for real-life care: Record vitamin D levels, Ca/PO4 results, PTH, DEXA or x-ray findings (Looser zones), and start vitamin D (cholecalciferol or ergocalciferol) and calcium supplementation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Vitamin D (cholecalciferol/ergocalciferol) is hydroxylated in liver to 25-OH and in kidney to 1,25-OH (calcitriol). 25-OH level best reflects body stores.<br /></br>• Deficiency → failure to mineralize osteoid matrix → soft, weak bones.<br /></br>• In adults = osteomalacia; in children (open growth plates) = rickets (bowing, rachitic rosary).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: malabsorption (celiac, CF, cholestasis), vegan diet without supplementation, no sun, chronic anticonvulsant use (↑CYP-450), renal disease (can’t make 1,25-OH).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC PEARLS  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm low 25-OH vitamin D.  </li></br><li>Replace vitamin D: 50,000 IU oral ergocalciferol weekly x 8–12 weeks, then maintenance 1,000–2,000 IU daily; or high-dose cholecalciferol.  </li></br><li>Give elemental calcium 1,000–1,500 mg/day; treat underlying malabsorption (strict gluten-free diet).  </li></br><li>Monitor Ca, PO4, ALP, 25-OH D, and PTH every 3-6 months.  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS  </p></br><p>“ALP-D CAP” mnemonic for osteomalacia labs:<br /></br>A = ↑ Alkaline phosphatase<br /></br>L = Low vitamin D (25-OH)<br /></br>P = ↑ Parathyroid hormone<br /></br>D = Diffuse bone pain<br /></br>C = ↓ Calcium<br /></br>A = Aching in muscles<br /></br>P = ↓ Phosphate  </p></br><p>Visual cue: Picture “soft (mal) bones in an adult house (Asia-‘malacia’) because the cement (Ca/PO4) is missing.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER ANALYSIS  </p></br><p>a. Hereditary hypophosphatemic rickets<br /></br>• Genetic (usually X-linked dominant) renal phosphate wasting.<br /></br>• PO4 low, ALP high, BUT vitamin D levels normal, PTH normal, Ca normal.<br /></br>• Usually presents in childhood with limb deformities. Not consistent with low 25-OH vitamin D or adult onset.  </p></br><p>b. 1α-Hydroxylase deficiency<br /></br>• Inability of kidney to convert 25-OH to active 1,25-OH vitamin D.<br /></br>• 25-OH level would be normal or elevated (not low). Typically presents in infancy with rickets, not adult with malabsorption.  </p></br><p>c. Osteodystrophy (renal osteodystrophy)<br /></br>• Due to chronic kidney disease (CKD). Should see elevated BUN/Cr and often low 1,25-OH vitamin D with normal 25-OH. Creatinine here is normal (0.8 mg/dL), ruling this out.  </p></br><p>e. Primary hyperparathyroidism<br /></br>• Autonomous PTH secretion → ↑Ca, ↓PO4, ↑ALP; vitamin D usually normal.<br /></br>• Vignette shows low Ca, not high.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line: Severe vitamin D deficiency from celiac malabsorption causes osteomalacia, producing bone pain, proximal muscle weakness, and the classic lab pattern of ↓Ca, ↓PO4, ↑PTH, ↑ALP, and low 25-OH vitamin D.</p>
55. A 57-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 6-month history of productive cough that has worsened during the past week. During the past 6 weeks, she also has had progressive shortness of breath, fatigue, and sputum production. She has had four episodes of pneumonia during the past 3 years. She takes no medications. She appears ill. Her temperature is 38.4°C (101°F), pulse is 96/min, and respirations are 22/min. Diffuse crackles and rhonchi are heard bilaterally. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A chest x-ray shows linear atelectasis and dilated and thickened airways. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Chest physical therapy</br>b. High-frequency chest wall compression</br>c. Inhaled administration of antibiotics</br>d. Inhaled administration of dornase alfa</br>e. Intravenous administration of antibiotics</br>f. Intravenous administration of corticosteroids</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Intravenous administration of antibiotics  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 6-month productive cough, multiple pneumonias → chronic airway disease.<br /></br>• Chest x-ray: “dilated and thickened airways” + “linear atelectasis” → classic for bronchiectasis (permanent, irreversible bronchial dilation).<br /></br>• Acute change: past week of ↑ cough, fever (38.4 °C), tachypnea, systemic toxicity → infectious exacerbation.<br /></br>• Appears ill enough for ED evaluation; likely inpatient care is required.</p></br><p>Pertinent positives: purulent sputum, fever, diffuse crackles/rhonchi, imaging findings.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no wheezing or obstructive pattern described that would suggest COPD/asthma exacerbation responsive to steroids.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize chronic process (bronchiectasis) by buzzwords (recurrent pneumonia, “tram-track”/dilated bronchi on imaging).<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is this a stable patient needing routine airway clearance, or an acute infectious exacerbation requiring antibiotics?”<br /></br>3. Note systemic signs → choose systemic therapy (IV antibiotics) rather than maintenance measures.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Bronchiectasis often colonized by bacteria (H. influenzae, Pseudomonas, S. aureus).<br /></br>2. Exacerbation defined by ≥2 of: ↑ volume or purulence of sputum, ↑ dyspnea, cough, or systemic features (fever). She meets all.<br /></br>3. Hospital admission &amp; IV antibiotics are indicated when patients have:<br /></br>   • Marked systemic illness (fever, tachypnea)<br /></br>   • Significant hypoxemia or comorbidities<br /></br>   • Inability to tolerate oral agents<br /></br>4. Therefore, empiric IV coverage (eg, piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime + azithromycin until culture results) is the most appropriate next step.</p></br><p>Take-home: Acute exacerbation of bronchiectasis → start empiric systemic antibiotics, then airway clearance adjuncts.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: Record vitals, sputum character, lung exam, imaging findings, and plan for blood/sputum cultures and IV antibiotics.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Repeated infection or obstruction → neutrophilic inflammation → destruction of elastic/muscular bronchial wall → permanent dilation.<br /></br>• Poor mucociliary clearance traps organisms → chronic colonization and recurrent infections.<br /></br>• Exacerbations typically bacterial; Pseudomonas especially in severe disease.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Acute exacerbation:<br /></br>1. Obtain sputum culture &amp; gram stain.<br /></br>2. Start empiric IV antibiotics targeting common organisms; tailor to culture.<br /></br>3. Supportive: oxygen, hydration, bronchodilators if airflow limitation, airway clearance (chest PT) once stabilized.</p></br><p>Maintenance (between exacerbations):<br /></br>• Daily chest physiotherapy or HF chest wall oscillation.<br /></br>• Inhaled bronchodilators if concomitant obstruction.<br /></br>• Macrolide prophylaxis or inhaled antibiotics for patients with frequent exacerbations or chronic Pseudomonas colonization.<br /></br>• Vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcus).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BEAT the bugs in Bronchiectasis flare”:<br /></br>B – Broad IV antibiotics first<br /></br>E – Evaluate sputum culture<br /></br>A – Airway clearance after stabilization<br /></br>T – Titrate therapy based on cultures</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chest physical therapy – Essential long-term mucus clearance but does not treat the acute bacterial infection causing current deterioration.</p></br><p>b. High-frequency chest wall compression – Same reasoning as (a); useful chronic adjunct, not first step in a febrile exacerbation requiring hospitalization.</p></br><p>c. Inhaled antibiotics – Indicated for chronic Pseudomonas colonization to reduce future exacerbations; onset too slow and delivery inadequate for acute severe infection.</p></br><p>d. Inhaled dornase alfa – Mucolytic used in cystic fibrosis, not beneficial in non-CF bronchiectasis.</p></br><p>f. Intravenous corticosteroids – Helpful in asthma/COPD exacerbations driven by airway inflammation, not indicated for bronchiectasis infection and could worsen infection.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Febrile, systemically ill patient with bronchiectasis flare = bacterial exacerbation → start empiric intravenous antibiotics immediately.</p>
56. A 77-year-old man with hyperlipidemia comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of “turning yellow.” During the past 4 weeks, he also has had a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss despite no change in appetite. He says he feels well. He has not had abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel movements. His medications are atorvastatin and daily aspirin. He is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 79 kg (175 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min and regular, and blood pressure is 130/75 mm Hg. Examination shows jaundice and scleral icterus. There is a palpable, nontender gallbladder; there is no hepatosplenomegaly, and no abdominal masses are noted. Rectal examination shows no masses. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cholelithiasis</br>b. Hepatitis A</br>c. Liver cancer</br>d. Medication adverse effect</br>e. Pancreatic cancer</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Pancreatic cancer (most commonly an adenocarcinoma in the head of the pancreas)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key words &amp; clues:<br /></br>• 77-year-old man – malignancy risk rises with age.<br /></br>• 3-day history of “turning yellow” + scleral icterus → clinical jaundice.<br /></br>• 4-week 2.3-kg (5-lb) unintentional weight loss → red flag for cancer.<br /></br>• No abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or ∆ in bowel habits → “painless jaundice.”<br /></br>• Palpable, nontender gallbladder on exam (Courvoisier sign).<br /></br>• No hepatosplenomegaly; stool guaiac negative.</p></br><p>How these details narrow the differential:<br /></br>Painless obstructive jaundice + Courvoisier sign = malignant obstruction of extra-hepatic bile duct, classically by a carcinoma in the pancreatic head (can also be cholangiocarcinoma or ampullary cancer, but pancreatic ca is by far most common).<br /></br>Pertinent negatives (no pain, no gallstone colic, no viral prodrome, no diffuse liver mass) help rule out cholelithiasis, hepatitis, or primary liver cancer.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see “painless jaundice” immediately scan the stem for “palpable gallbladder”; if present, jump to pancreatic head cancer unless another malignant obstructer is explicitly described.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thinking:<br /></br>1. Identify jaundice → determine if hepatocellular vs obstructive.<br /></br>2. Presence of painless jaundice + palpable GB → extra-hepatic obstruction.<br /></br>3. In an elderly patient with weight loss → think malignancy, most often pancreatic adenocarcinoma in the head compressing the common bile duct.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Courvoisier sign (nontender, distended gallbladder + painless jaundice) = malignant obstruction until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): onset of jaundice, weight change, absence of pain, physical finding of enlarged, nontender GB, plan for abdominal imaging (ultrasound → contrast CT) and oncology referral.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Pancreatic head tumor encircles/distorts the distal common bile duct → intra- and extra-hepatic biliary dilation → conjugated hyperbilirubinemia → jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, pruritus.<br /></br>• Tumor usually adenocarcinoma arising from ductal epithelium; risk factors: smoking, chronic pancreatitis, DM, obesity, age &gt;55, family history.<br /></br>• Weight loss results from cancer-associated catabolism and anorexia.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Initial imaging: RUQ ultrasound (look for biliary dilation); if positive, contrast CT or MRCP to define mass, stage disease.  </li></br><li>Labs: ↑ direct bilirubin, ↑ ALP, mild or no transaminase elevation; CA 19-9 often elevated (not screening).  </li></br><li>Management:<br /></br>   • Resectable (no major vessel invasion, no distant mets) → Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) ± adjuvant chemo.<br /></br>   • Borderline/unresectable → neoadjuvant chemoradiation.<br /></br>   • Metastatic → palliative stenting of bile duct for pruritus/itching, systemic chemotherapy (e.g., FOLFIRINOX).</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Courvoisier = Cancer”:<br /></br>Courvoisier sign (COOL gallbladder – not hot) = think Carcinoma Obstructing at the head of pancreas.</p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture a yellow (jaundiced) painless banana sitting atop a cool (nontender) green balloon (distended GB) blocking a river (bile duct) leading from the pancreas.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Cholelithiasis<br /></br>   • Stone obstruction usually causes biliary colic or cholecystitis → right upper quadrant pain, tender gallbladder, fever. A chronically stone-filled GB is often shrunken, not enlarged. Painless jaundice with palpable GB argues strongly against stones.  </p></br><p>b. Hepatitis A<br /></br>   • Presents with prodromal fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea, RUQ tenderness, markedly ↑ AST/ALT. Gallbladder is not enlarged. Transmission history (travel, contaminated food) often present.  </p></br><p>c. Liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma)<br /></br>   • Frequently occurs in cirrhotic liver; exam shows hepatomegaly, sometimes abdominal pain; obstruction of extra-hepatic bile duct uncommon, so palpable GB is not expected. Jaundice tends to be late and associated with tender liver, not painless.  </p></br><p>d. Medication adverse effect<br /></br>   • Atorvastatin can cause mild hepatocellular injury, but would produce AST/ALT elevation rather than obstructive pattern; GB remains small, no Courvoisier sign. Aspirin does not cause jaundice in therapeutic doses.  </p></br><p>e. Pancreatic cancer (Correct)<br /></br>   • Only choice that explains painless obstructive jaundice and Courvoisier sign in an elderly patient with weight loss.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Elderly patient + painless jaundice + palpable, nontender gallbladder = malignant obstruction, overwhelmingly pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. Recognizing Courvoisier sign is a must-know diagnostic shortcut for Shelf and Step 2 CK exams.</p>
57. A 17-year-old boy comes to the physician for an examination prior to participation in school sports. He feels well. He has no history of concussions or musculoskeletal injuries. He has never fainted with exercise. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. There is no family history of unexplained or cardiac death in adults younger than 50 years. The patient appears well. He is 188 cm (6 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 65 kg (143 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. His arm span is 200 cm (6 ft 6 in). Vital signs are within normal limits. The upper-to-lower body ratio is decreased. Examination shows a long, thin face and large ears. The sclerae are blue, but the retina can be visualized without difficulty. The upper extremities are thin. Examination of the chest shows pectus carinatum. No murmurs are heard on cardiac examination; S1 and S2 are normal. Peripheral pulses are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation prior to providing clearance for this patient to participate in sports?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cardiac MR angiography</br>b. Echocardiography</br>c. Exercise stress test</br>d. 24-Hour ambulatory ECG monitoring</br>e. Slit-lamp examination</br>f. Visual field testing</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Echocardiography  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Pattern-recognition clues for Marfan syndrome<br /></br>   • Tall, thin adolescent (188 cm/65 kg; BMI 18).<br /></br>   • Arm-span &gt; height (200 cm vs 188 cm).<br /></br>   • Decreased upper-to-lower body ratio, pectus carinatum, long thin facies/large ears.<br /></br>   • Normal intellect, no family history yet—remember 25 % of Marfan cases are de-novo.<br /></br>2. Why the “blue sclerae”? – uncommon but can be seen with thin sclera in Marfan; the key is that the retina is easily visualized → NOT lens dislocation (would obstruct view).<br /></br>3. Sports clearance issue – The biggest life-threatening complication in Marfan is progressive dilation of the aortic root → risk of dissection/rupture during strenuous activity.<br /></br>   • Aortic root diameter determines restriction from most competitive sports.<br /></br>   • Guidelines (AHA/ACC, Bethesda) recommend echocardiographic screening at diagnosis and every 6–12 mo during adolescence.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On sports-physical questions, whenever you see marfanoid habitus, immediately ask “has the aorta been imaged?” If not, order an echo before signing the clearance form.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-by-step)<br /></br>1. Suspect Marfan → must evaluate cardiovascular manifestations.<br /></br>2. Transthoracic echocardiography is the first-line, non-invasive, widely available modality to measure:<br /></br>   • Aortic root &amp; ascending aorta diameter.<br /></br>   • Mitral valve prolapse, aortic regurgitation.<br /></br>3. Result guides:<br /></br>   • Normal root (&lt;2.1 cm/m² BSA): may allow low-static sports.<br /></br>   • Dilated root: restrict strenuous sports, start β-blocker or losartan, refer to cardio-thoracic surgery if ≥5 cm or rapid growth.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In any marfanoid patient, echo precedes sports clearance.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real clinic): note marfanoid features, vital signs, family history, prior imaging, and echocardiogram results with specific diameters.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Marfan = AD mutation in FBN1 → defective fibrillin-1 → weakened elastic tissue in aorta, ligaments, ciliary zonules.<br /></br>• Cardiovascular: cystic medial necrosis → progressive proximal aortic dilation, aneurysm, dissection.<br /></br>• Skeletal &amp; ocular signs aid recognition but do NOT determine exercise risk; the aorta does.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>First-line diagnostic: Transthoracic echocardiogram.<br /></br>Repeat: annually or sooner if dilation.<br /></br>Medical: β-blockers, ARBs (e.g., losartan) slow dilation.<br /></br>Surgical: Elective aortic root replacement if ≥5 cm or rapid growth.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Mnemonic<br /></br>“MAFAN”<br /></br>M – Measure<br /></br>A – Aorta<br /></br>F – First (before)<br /></br>A – Athletic<br /></br>N – Nod of clearance  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Cardiac MR angiography – Excellent anatomic detail but unnecessary as initial screen; reserved if echo suboptimal or for pre-operative planning.<br /></br>C. Exercise stress test – Screens for ischemia/arrhythmia; Marfan deaths are from aortic dissection, not occult CAD in a healthy 17-year-old.<br /></br>D. 24-h ambulatory ECG – Useful for symptomatic palpitations or suspected arrhythmogenic syndromes; patient is asymptomatic.<br /></br>E. Slit-lamp examination – Detects ectopia lentis (up to 60 % of Marfan pts) but lens dislocation does not determine sports eligibility. Eye exam can be done later by ophthalmology.<br /></br>F. Visual field testing – Evaluates glaucoma/optic nerve; unrelated to acute sports risk.</p></br><p>Common pitfalls: picking slit-lamp because ocular issues are classic; remember the exam asks what you MUST do before clearing for sports—the life-threatening issue is the aorta.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Marfanoid + sports physical → always obtain a baseline echocardiogram to rule out aortic root dilation before granting clearance.</p>
58. A previously healthy 1-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his mother because of a 3-day history of fever, fussiness, and poor feeding. He was born at 38 weeks’ gestation and delivered by a midwife at home. His mother received no prenatal care. Immunizations are up-to-date. On arrival, the patient is irritable but consolable and does not appear toxic. His temperature is 40°C (104°F), pulse is 140/min, and respirations are 40/min. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds.
