The anterior fontanel is open, soft, and flat. The mucous membranes are moist. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Urinalysis shows:</br>pH 6.0</br>Specific gravity 1.005</br>Blood 1+</br>Protein 1+</br>RBC 10/hpf</br>WBC >100/hpf</br>Nitrites negative</br>Leukocyte esterase positive</br>Results of a complete blood count, serum studies, and blood and urine cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen</br>b. Dimercaptosuccinic acid renal scan</br>c. Intravenous pyelography</br>d. Renal ultrasonography</br>e. X-ray of the abdomen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Renal ultrasonography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words/phrases: 1-year-old boy, 3-day fever (104 °F), irritability, poor feeding, UA with &gt;100 WBC/hpf, +leukocyte esterase, microscopic hematuria, nitrite-negative.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: Fever in a non-toxic infant, marked pyuria → febrile urinary-tract infection (UTI) / acute pyelonephritis until proven otherwise.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: Soft fontanel, moist mucosa, normal cap refill → not septic/shocked; no abdominal mass or tenderness.<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: First febrile UTI in a male &lt;2 yr automatically triggers an anatomic evaluation of the urinary tract.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Any NBME vignette with “child &lt;2 yr + fever + pyuria” → immediately think “first febrile UTI → renal &amp; bladder ultrasound (RBUS).” If the stem asks for “next diagnostic step,” do NOT jump to voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) or CT; start with the low-risk screen (RBUS).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1  Identify the illness: UA confirms pyelonephritis (pyuria; WBC &gt;100/hpf; leukocyte esterase).<br /></br>Step 2  Apply guideline: AAP &amp; AUA recommend RBUS for ALL infants &amp; toddlers (≤24 mo) after their first febrile UTI, irrespective of organism or nitrite status.<br /></br>Step 3  Goal of imaging: Detect correctable structural anomalies—e.g., hydronephrosis, duplex systems, posterior urethral valves—that predispose to recurrent infection and renal scarring.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: “First hot UTI in kids under two? Look with ultrasound first.”  </p></br><p>Documentation if real patient: “Febrile UTI; plan: start IV ceftriaxone, obtain renal/bladder ultrasound per AAP guidelines.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)<br /></br>• Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) or obstructive lesions (posterior urethral valve, UPJ obstruction) allow infected urine to reach the kidney → pyelonephritis → potential scarring, hypertension, CKD.<br /></br>• Ultrasound is radiation-free, detects hydronephrosis, scarring, ureteral dilation, and bladder wall thickening.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Renal &amp; bladder ultrasound now.<br /></br>2. VCUG only if ultrasound abnormal or with recurrent febrile UTIs.<br /></br>3. DMSA scan 4–6 mo later if concern for renal scarring.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>• Empiric IV third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone) or aminoglycoside pending cultures.<br /></br>• 7–14 d total antibiotic course.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“FIRST FEVER, FIRST SCAN” → First febrile UTI = First scan (RBUS).<br /></br>“RBUS Before Reflux” → Ultrasound precedes VCUG for reflux work-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>• High radiation; used for complicated pyelonephritis (abscess, emphysematous changes) or trauma, not for first uncomplicated febrile UTI in infants.  </p></br><p>B. Dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) renal scan<br /></br>• Nuclear study for cortical scarring; performed months later, not acutely. Doesn’t identify obstructive anatomy.  </p></br><p>C. Intravenous pyelography (IVP)<br /></br>• Outdated, high radiation/contrast load; largely replaced by ultrasound + VCUG. Not guideline-recommended.  </p></br><p>E. Plain abdominal X-ray<br /></br>• Cannot visualize kidneys/ureters adequately; minimal use in UTI evaluation unless suspecting fecalith/obstruction, which the case does not suggest.  </p></br><p>Common trap: jumping to VCUG or CT. Remember the step-wise, least-invasive approach.</p>
59. A 56-year-old man comes to the physician with his wife because of a 2-year history of increasing restlessness while he is asleep. His wife reports that he kicks his legs and swings his arms several times during the night. He appears to be asleep during these episodes, and he typically says that he was dreaming if awakened during an episode. His wife now sleeps in a separate bed after he hit her during one of the episodes 1 year ago. He has a 3-year history of mild depression treated with weekly psychotherapy. He takes no medications. He does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Examination shows an occasional mild, rhythmic, involuntary shaking of the right hand that is most noticeable when the hand is in his lap. This patient is at greatest risk for developing which of the following conditions?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Alzheimer disease</br>b. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis</br>c. Corticobasal degeneration</br>d. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease</br>e. Parkinson disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Parkinson disease  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “kicks his legs and swings his arms several times during the night,” “appears to be asleep during these episodes,” “says that he was dreaming if awakened,” and wife was struck → classic dream-enactment behavior.<br /></br>• Dream enactment during sleep = REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD). Normally REM sleep is accompanied by skeletal-muscle atonia; in RBD that atonia is lost.<br /></br>• Physical exam clue: “mild, rhythmic, involuntary shaking of the right hand… most noticeable when the hand is in his lap” → a resting tremor, the cardinal early sign of Parkinson disease (PD).<br /></br>• Cognitive approach: Whenever you see violent movements during sleep + recall of vivid dreams, instantly think “RBD → synucleinopathy risk.” A resting tremor seals the deal for PD risk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify the sleep disorder → REM Sleep Behavior Disorder.<br /></br>2. Recall epidemiology: &gt;80% of idiopathic RBD patients eventually develop an α-synuclein neurodegenerative disease (Parkinson disease, Dementia with Lewy bodies, or Multiple System Atrophy).<br /></br>3. Look for prodromal PD signs (resting tremor, constipation, hyposmia) – he already shows resting tremor.<br /></br>4. Therefore the future condition he is most at risk for is Parkinson disease.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Idiopathic RBD is often the earliest clinically recognizable manifestation of Parkinsonian synucleinopathy, preceding motor symptoms by years.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient<br /></br>• Sleep history consistent with RBD (dream enactment, injury to bed partner).<br /></br>• Neurologic exam: unilateral resting tremor.<br /></br>• Plan: polysomnography to confirm RBD, safety counseling, consider melatonin or low-dose clonazepam, and schedule neurologic follow-up for prodromal PD monitoring.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• REM atonia is generated by the sublaterodorsal (SLD) nucleus in the pons hyperpolarizing spinal α-motor neurons.<br /></br>• α-Synuclein deposition in pontine tegmentum disrupts this circuitry → loss of atonia → RBD.<br /></br>• The same misfolded protein later accumulates in substantia nigra pars compacta → dopaminergic neuron loss → Parkinson motor features (resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield bullets)<br /></br>• Confirm RBD with nocturnal polysomnography showing REM sleep without atonia.<br /></br>• First-line symptomatic therapy:<br /></br>  – Melatonin 3–12 mg nightly (fewer side effects)<br /></br>  – Low-dose clonazepam 0.25–1 mg if melatonin ineffective.<br /></br>• PD once diagnosed: carbidopa/levodopa, dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole), MAO-B inhibitors (rasagiline), etc.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“REMember to Punch = REM Punch → α-Syn Punches the Nigra.”<br /></br>Visualize a boxer (dreamer) punching through a fence labeled “REM Atonia,” wearing a glove marked α-syn → behind him stands a statue of Parkinson (shaking).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Alzheimer disease – Associated with insomnia, sundowning, and fragmented sleep, not violent dream enactment. No early resting tremor.<br /></br>b. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis – Progressive upper &amp; lower motor neuron weakness, but sleep behavior is not a hallmark and tremor is uncommon.<br /></br>c. Corticobasal degeneration – An atypical parkinsonism with asymmetric rigidity/apraxia and cortical signs (alien limb, myoclonus); RBD is far less common than in synucleinopathies, and a classic resting tremor is unusual early.<br /></br>d. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease – Rapidly progressive dementia, ataxia, myoclonus; sleep disturbance is not characterized by chronic dream enactment.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: conflating “movement during sleep” with periodic limb movement disorder or sleepwalking. Those occur in NREM sleep and patients do NOT recall vivid dreams. REM dream enactment + synucleinopathy association is the testable distinction.</p>
60. A 16-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 15 minutes after involvement in a motor vehicle collision. He was the restrained driver. His car crashed into the car in front of him, then was hit from behind by a pickup truck. The patient's car was destroyed, but he was able to walk away from the scene of the collision. On arrival, he is upset and reports moderate chest pain. His pulse is 100/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Examination shows erythema over the chest in the area where his seat belt had been fastened. No other abnormalities are noted. Serum studies show:</br>Creatine kinase, total    221 U/L</br>MB fraction               20 percent (N<5 percent total CK)</br>Troponin I                1 ng/mL (N<0.1)</br>Troponin T                0.3 ng/mL (N<0.1)</br>Chest x-rays show no abnormalities. An ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm and nonspecific T-wave inversion in leads V3 through V6. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Hyperoxygenation</br>b. Intravenous administration of a β-adrenergic blocking agent</br>c. Intravenous administration of 0.45 percent saline</br>d. Serial ECG monitoring</br>e. Thrombolysis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selected by Exam Key<br /></br>d. Serial ECG monitoring</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Mechanism of injury: high-speed, front-and-rear impact, seat-belt sign → classic setup for blunt cardiac injury (myocardial contusion).<br /></br>2. Symptoms/Signs: immediate chest pain, erythematous “seat-belt” mark, otherwise stable vitals.<br /></br>3. Cardiac biomarkers:<br /></br>   • CK-MB 20 % of total (≫ 5 %)<br /></br>   • Troponin I 1.0 ng/mL, Troponin T 0.3 ng/mL (both ↑)<br /></br>   These indicate myocardial cell injury but do not specify coronary occlusion.<br /></br>4. ECG: sinus rhythm + nonspecific T-wave inversions (no ST-segment elevation).<br /></br>5. Chest x-ray normal → no aortic injury/pneumothorax.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map:<br /></br>Step 1 – Ask “Could this be an acute coronary occlusion (STEMI/NSTEMI)?”  → Unlikely in a healthy 16-year-old; mechanism is blunt trauma.<br /></br>Step 2 – Recall guideline: After blunt chest trauma, obtain an ECG ± troponin. If either is abnormal → admit/observe on continuous telemetry with serial ECGs &amp; enzymes for 24 h to detect arrhythmias or evolving myocardial damage.<br /></br>Step 3 – Choose the option that matches: serial ECG monitoring.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Blunt myocardial contusion can produce patchy myocardial necrosis → enzyme leak and, most importantly, conduction abnormalities (PACs, PVCs, AV block, VT/VF) that often declare themselves within the first 24 h.<br /></br>• Treatment is largely supportive; the key is early detection and management of dysrhythmias → continuous cardiac monitoring and repeat ECGs.<br /></br>Take-home rule: “ECG or troponin abnormal after chest trauma? Telemetry for 24–48 h.”</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): mechanism of injury, vitals, seat-belt sign, labs, initial ECG, plan for admission to monitored bed with serial ECG/troponin q6–8 h.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Deceleration/compression forces bruise the myocardium (most commonly the right ventricle against sternum). Local hemorrhage and edema disrupt myocytes and conduction pathways → enzyme elevation and arrhythmia risk. Unlike ischemic MI, there is no primary coronary thrombosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls</br>• Initial screen: ECG ± troponin I/T.<br /></br>• Both normal → observe 4–6 h, then discharge if stable.<br /></br>• Any abnormality → continuous telemetry, serial ECGs/enzymes, echocardiography if hemodynamic instability, wall-motion abnormality, or new murmur.<br /></br>• Treat arrhythmias per ACLS; otherwise no specific drug therapy is mandated.<br /></br>• Anticoagulation/thrombolysis are contraindicated in multi-trauma patients.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Memorization Tip</br>Mnemonic “BAT rule” for Blunt chest trauma:<br /></br>B – Biomarker abnormal OR<br /></br>A – Abnormal ECG →<br /></br>T – Telemetry (serial ECGs/enzyme Trend) for 24 h.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Hyperoxygenation – Useful for CO poisoning or hypoxemia; patient is normoxic and stable. Does not address arrhythmia risk.<br /></br>b. IV β-blocker – Standard early MI therapy to reduce demand, but contusion is not supply–demand ischemia. β-blockers may worsen trauma-related hypotension/bradyarrhythmia.<br /></br>c. IV 0.45 % saline – Hypotonic saline treats hypernatremia/dehydration; patient is hemodynamically stable, euvolemic.<br /></br>e. Thrombolysis – Indicated in STEMI due to thrombotic occlusion; contraindicated in recent major trauma (high bleeding risk) and unnecessary in contusion.</p></br><p>────────────────────</br>Core Takeaway</br>After blunt chest trauma with ANY ECG or troponin abnormality, the safest and guideline-endorsed management is admission for continuous cardiac monitoring with serial ECGs to catch life-threatening arrhythmias early.</p>
61. A state health department is alerted to a potential outbreak of hepatitis A during the past 2 weeks involving a restaurant in a city with a population of 350,000.  </br>After a preliminary descriptive analysis, including case identification, the health department matches cases with control subjects and examines foods that are potential sources of infection.  </br>Which of the following is the most appropriate statistical analysis for the investigators to determine the source of this outbreak?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Absolute risk  </br>b. Attributable risk  </br>c. Odds ratio  </br>d. Relative risk  </br>e. Risk reduction</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. Odds ratio  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; why they matter<br /></br>• “Potential outbreak of hepatitis A” – rare, acute outcome → incidence in whole city is very low.<br /></br>• “Matches cases with control subjects” – classic wording for a case-control study (start with diseased and non-diseased, look back for exposure).<br /></br>• “Examines foods that are potential sources of infection” – looking retrospectively for exposures.  </p></br><p>Approach to similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the study design (cohort = start with exposure, follow for disease; case-control = start with disease status).<br /></br>2. Link design to the correct measure of association (case-control → odds ratio; cohort → relative risk or risk ratio).<br /></br>3. Eliminate measures that require incidence data (absolute risk, relative risk, risk reduction, attributable risk).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why odds ratio?<br /></br>• In a case-control study investigators cannot calculate incidence because the number of exposed vs. unexposed people in the source population is unknown—they purposely select the number of cases and controls.<br /></br>• The odds of exposure among cases is compared with the odds of exposure among controls.<br /></br>• Mathematically: OR = (ad)/(bc) from a 2×2 table.<br /></br>Core takeaway: “Case-control → Odds Ratio.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>If this were a real outbreak investigation, the record would note the study design (matched case-control), matching variables (e.g., age, sex), exposure assessment method (food histories), and statistical measure used (OR with 95 % CI).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Brief Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Hepatitis A virus<br /></br>• Fecal-oral transmission, classically via contaminated food or water.<br /></br>• Incubation ≈ 28 days; patients present with abrupt onset of fever, malaise, jaundice.<br /></br>• No chronic infection; post-exposure prophylaxis = HAV vaccine or immune globulin within 2 weeks.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests (context)  </p></br><p>Outbreak control steps<br /></br>1. Identify source (statistic = odds ratio of specific foods).<br /></br>2. Stop exposure (close restaurant or specific food handling).<br /></br>3. Post-exposure prophylaxis to patrons and staff within 14 days.<br /></br>4. Report and educate on hand hygiene and vaccination.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Odds of exposure in Disease” → ODDS ratio → “ODDS starts with D = Disease first.”<br /></br>“RR looks Forward” – Relative Risk follows people forward from exposure.  </p></br><p>Quick table:<br /></br>Case-control → OR<br /></br>Cohort → RR/AR<br /></br>Randomized trial → RR, ARR, RRR, NNT  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Absolute risk<br /></br>• Definition: Incidence of disease in an exposed group.<br /></br>• Requires knowing total number of exposed people in population → impossible in a case-control design.  </p></br><p>b. Attributable risk (risk difference)<br /></br>• Incidence in exposed – incidence in unexposed.<br /></br>• Also needs incidence data → suited for cohort studies.  </p></br><p>d. Relative risk (risk ratio)<br /></br>• Incidence in exposed ÷ incidence in unexposed.<br /></br>• Cannot be obtained when investigators fix the number of diseased vs. non-diseased subjects.  </p></br><p>e. Risk reduction (relative or absolute)<br /></br>• Derived from randomized controlled trials or prospective cohorts comparing interventions; again relies on incidence of outcomes.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Seeing “outbreak” and thinking “relative risk.” Always key on the phrase “cases matched with controls.” That signals a case-control study and therefore an odds ratio.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A matched case-control investigation of a food-borne hepatitis A outbreak must use an odds ratio to quantify the association between each food item (exposure) and hepatitis A infection (outcome).</p>
62. A 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 6-hour history of thirst and fatigue and a 2-hour history of light-headedness. He has been working outside during the past 2 days in temperatures reaching 90.0°F. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. On arrival, he is lethargic. He is oriented to person but not to place or time. His temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 74/min and irregular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 95/64 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. No bruits are heard. No murmurs are heard on cardiac examination. The abdomen is nontender. ECG is shown. In addition to intravenous administration of fluids, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Adenosine therapy</br>b. Atropine therapy</br>c. Lidocaine therapy</br>d. Placement of a pacemaker</br>e. Observation only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>e. Observation only  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• New-onset thirst, fatigue, light-headedness after working outdoors in 90 °F heat → suggests dehydration with mild volume depletion, not a primary cardiac disorder.<br /></br>• Vital signs: T 37.2 °C, BP 95/64 (mm Hg) (soft but perfusing), HR 74/min and “irregular” (not fast, not slow).<br /></br>• Patient is lethargic but arousable; lungs clear; no murmurs/bruits → no evidence of acute MI, CHF, or valvular disease.<br /></br>• ECG (image not shown on exam screen, but by answer key) demonstrates isolated premature ventricular complexes (PVCs) in an otherwise normal sinus rhythm. PVCs classically produce an “irregular” pulse when the average rate is still in the 60–90 bpm range.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test day<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is this rhythm immediately life-threatening?” HR is neither tachy &gt;150 nor brady &lt;40, BP is low-normal, mentation improves with fluids → No.<br /></br>2. Identify specific rhythm. Single PVCs are recognized by a wide QRS with compensatory pause; they often occur in the setting of stress, stimulant use, or electrolyte/volume disturbance.<br /></br>3. Decide if the PVCs themselves need treatment. In a hemodynamically stable patient without structural heart disease, they do not. Treat precipitating factors (fluids/electrolytes); otherwise observe.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why “Observation only” is correct<br /></br>• PVCs in a stable patient are almost always benign.<br /></br>• Guidelines (ACC/AHA/HRS) state that therapy (β-blocker, amiodarone, ablation) is reserved for:<br /></br>  – Symptomatic PVCs that impair quality of life, or<br /></br>  – PVC-induced cardiomyopathy, or<br /></br>  – A short-coupled PVC precipitating VT/VF.<br /></br>• Here, symptoms correlate with dehydration (thirst, orthostasis). The first therapeutic step is volume resuscitation; no anti-arrhythmic drug or device is indicated.<br /></br>Take-home: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic PVCs = reassure/observe + correct underlying triggers (electrolytes, hypoxia, ischemia, stimulants).  </p></br><p>What you would document<br /></br>“ECG: NSR with isolated unifocal PVCs. No sustained VT, no pauses &gt;3 s. Patient hemodynamically stable. Plan: IV isotonic saline; replete electrolytes; monitor telemetry.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>PVCs<br /></br>• Ectopic ventricular focus fires earlier than next expected sinus beat → wide QRS, usually &gt;120 ms, followed by full compensatory pause.<br /></br>• Common precipitants: catecholamine surge, fever, dehydration, hypokalemia/magnesemia, hypoxia, stimulants (caffeine), and structural heart disease.<br /></br>• Usually benign in structurally normal hearts; frequency often falls once trigger corrected.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Immediate management<br /></br>1. Assess hemodynamic stability (ABC’s, vitals).<br /></br>2. Address precipitants: give IV normal saline, check BMP/Mg, consider troponin if concerned for ischemia.<br /></br>3. Cardiac monitoring for escalation to sustained VT (rare in this scenario).  </p></br><p>No anti-arrhythmic drug, pacemaker, or AV-node agent indicated.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>PVC “RASH”:<br /></br>R – Reassure/observe if stable<br /></br>A – Address triggers (electrolytes, ischemia, stimulants, Hypovolemia)<br /></br>S – Structural heart disease? (Echo if frequent)<br /></br>H – Hemodynamic compromise → treat (rare)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Adenosine therapy<br /></br>• Used for narrow-complex paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (re-entry), not for isolated PVCs. Rhythm here is not SVT and QRS is wide.  </p></br><p>b. Atropine therapy<br /></br>• Indicated for symptomatic bradycardia or high-grade AV block. HR here is 74/min, not bradycardic; problem is ectopic beats, not AV nodal block.  </p></br><p>c. Lidocaine therapy<br /></br>• IV lidocaine is for unstable or ischemia-induced ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation. Single PVCs without ischemia do not warrant lidocaine; giving it could provoke hypotension or CNS toxicity.  </p></br><p>d. Placement of a pacemaker<br /></br>• Pacemakers treat chronic symptomatic bradyarrhythmias or high-grade AV block. Patient’s intrinsic sinus node is functioning; pacing does not suppress PVCs.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Isolated PVCs in a hemodynamically stable, otherwise healthy adult require no acute pharmacologic or device therapy. Observe and correct reversible precipitants—most resolve with hydration and electrolyte normalization.</p>
63. A previously healthy 19-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of an increasingly severe bilateral headache and a 1-day history of fever and diarrhea. He says he feels sore all over. Use of acetaminophen has not relieved his headache. He is a college student and lives in a dormitory. He is in moderate distress and closes his eyes when lights are turned on. His temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes and nuchal rigidity. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Neurologic examination, including mental status examination, shows no abnormalities. His serum glucose concentration is 90 mg/dL. A CT scan of the head without contrast shows no abnormalities. A lumbar puncture is performed. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis shows a glucose concentration of 70 mg/dL, a protein concentration of 35 mg/dL, and a leukocyte count of 50/mm3 (100 percent monocytes). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Aseptic meningitis</br>b. Bacterial meningitis</br>c. Fungal meningitis</br>d. Parasitic meningoencephalitis</br>e. Viral encephalitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Aseptic (viral) meningitis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Red-flag words that shout “meningitis”:<br /></br>   • Bilateral pounding headache, photophobia (closes eyes with lights), fever, nuchal rigidity.  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives that rule out encephalitis or severe bacterial disease:<br /></br>   • Normal mental status, no focal neurologic deficits, normal fundus (no papilledema).  </li></br><li>CSF pattern is the clincher:<br /></br>   • Opening CT is normal ➔ safe to tap.<br /></br>   • Glucose 70 mg/dL while serum ≈ 90 mg/dL  ➔ essentially normal (viral).<br /></br>   • Protein 35 mg/dL ➔ mild elevation (viral).<br /></br>   • WBC 50/mm³, 100 % mononuclear (lymphocytes/monocytes) ➔ viral.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Step-wise cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>   a. Does the vignette meet a meningeal syndrome pattern? (YES)<br /></br>   b. Examine mental status for encephalopathy (NO → favors meningitis over encephalitis).<br /></br>   c. Go straight to the CSF cell differential → lymphocytes vs neutrophils vs eosinophils.<br /></br>   d. Compare glucose &amp; protein to decide: viral (normal glucose, mild protein), bacterial (low glucose, high protein), fungal/TB (very low glucose, very high protein, lymphocytes).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• “Aseptic meningitis” is a classic USMLE code word for viral meningitis (most commonly enteroviruses such as Coxsackie and echovirus, especially in young adults in close quarters like dormitories).<br /></br>• Normal mentation distinguishes meningitis from encephalitis, where viral infection (e.g., HSV-1) actually involves brain parenchyma and produces confusion, seizures, focal deficits.<br /></br>Core takeaway: CSF with normal glucose + lymphocytic pleocytosis + mild protein elevation = viral/aseptic meningitis.  </p></br><p>Documentation items (real life)<br /></br>   • Appearance, vitals, meningeal signs<br /></br>   • Neurologic exam (normal cognition)<br /></br>   • CSF opening pressure, glucose, protein, cell count/differential<br /></br>   • Empiric antibiotics initiated and stopped when cultures negative, etc.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Enteroviruses (picornavirus family) spread via fecal-oral route, invade meninges hematogenously.<br /></br>• Viral infection triggers lymphocytic infiltration without significant consumption of CSF glucose; protein leaks mildly from inflamed meninges.<br /></br>• Prognosis excellent; symptoms usually resolve in 7-10 days.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnosis &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>   • CSF PCR for enteroviruses, HSV, VZV if needed.<br /></br>   • Blood cultures taken before empiric therapy.  </p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>   • Supportive: IV or oral fluids, analgesics, anti-emetics.<br /></br>   • Empiric acyclovir if HSV cannot be excluded immediately.<br /></br>   • Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics are often started until bacterial meningitis is ruled out by culture.<br /></br>   • Isolation precautions if enterovirus or mumps suspected.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Viral = Very normal”<br /></br>   V – Viral<br /></br>   E – Everything (glucose)<br /></br>   R – Remains<br /></br>   Y – (near) normal  </p></br><p>Quick CSF table mnemonic:<br /></br>   Nerdy Bugs Feel Terrible<br /></br>   • Neutrophils → Bacterial (low glu, high protein)<br /></br>   • Lymphocytes → Viral (normal glu, mild protein)<br /></br>   • Lymphocytes + Low Glu + Very High protein → Fungal/TB  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Bacterial meningitis<br /></br>   • Would show WBC &gt;1000/mm³, neutrophilic predominance, glucose &lt;40 mg/dL (&lt;40% serum), protein &gt;100 mg/dL. Patient’s CSF is nowhere near this pattern.  </p></br><p>C. Fungal meningitis (e.g., Cryptococcus)<br /></br>   • Typically in immunocompromised. CSF: very low glucose, markedly elevated protein, lymphocytes. Not present here; patient is immunocompetent with normal glucose.  </p></br><p>D. Parasitic meningoencephalitis (Naegleria, Angiostrongylus, etc.)<br /></br>   • Often fulminant encephalopathy, high mortality, neutrophils or eosinophils in CSF, exposure to warm freshwater. None of those clues are provided.  </p></br><p>E. Viral encephalitis<br /></br>   • Same CSF profile as viral meningitis but MUST have altered mental status, seizures, or focal neurologic deficits (e.g., HSV temporal lobe signs). This patient’s neuro exam is completely normal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>Normal mentation + CSF lymphocytes with normal glucose/mild protein = aseptic (viral) meningitis.</p>
64. An 82-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 2-hour history of progressive shortness of breath. He has not had chest pain, fever, chills, or cough. He has coronary artery disease, essential hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia. Three years ago, he underwent uncomplicated coronary artery bypass grafting. His medications are clopidogrel, enalapril, rosuvastatin, and daily aspirin. He smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 59 years but quit 3 years ago. He drinks two glasses of wine daily. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 90/min and regular, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 118/78 mm Hg. Examination shows jugular venous distention. Crackles are heard in the lung bases; scattered expiratory wheezes are heard in all lung fields. On cardiac examination, an S3 gallop is heard. There is ≥2+ pretibial and 3+ pedal edema bilaterally. A chest x-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, prominent hilar vascularity, and cardiomegaly. Which of the following is most likely to be decreased in this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Central venous pressure</br>b. Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure</br>c. Shunt fraction (Qs/Qt)</br>d. Stroke volume index</br>e. Systemic vascular resistance</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Stroke volume index  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: “progressive shortness of breath,” JVD, S3 gallop, crackles, bilateral edema, cardiomegaly, pulmonary vascular congestion on CXR → classic acute decompensated congestive heart failure (CHF) with volume overload.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: age, ischemic heart disease history, S3 (dilated ventricle), pulmonary rales, peripheral edema.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: afebrile, no chest pain → argues against pneumonia, PE, acute MI as primary problem.<br /></br>Approach tip: When a stem asks for hemodynamic variables in a CHF picture, immediately recall the “CHF hemodynamic profile”: ↑ filling pressures (CVP &amp; PCWP), ↑ SVR, ↓ stroke volume / cardiac output, and often ↑ shunt (V/Q mismatch).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Failing LV has ↓ contractility → ↓ stroke volume (SV).<br /></br>2. To maintain arterial pressure, the body activates sympathetic &amp; RAAS pathways → vasoconstriction → ↑ systemic vascular resistance (SVR).<br /></br>3. Back-pressure:<br /></br>   • Left-sided → ↑ pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (PCWP) → pulmonary edema.<br /></br>   • Right-sided or overall volume overload → ↑ central venous pressure (CVP) → JVD &amp; peripheral edema.<br /></br>4. Fluid-filled alveoli create physiologic intrapulmonary shunt → ↑ Qs/Qt.<br /></br>Thus, among the listed variables, the one that falls is stroke volume index (SV corrected for body surface area).  </p></br><p>Take-home: In systolic CHF, pressures &amp; resistance go up; forward flow (SV/CO) goes down.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): volume status exam findings, heart sounds (S3), CXR findings, initial diuretic dose, oxygen saturation, response to therapy.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Etiology: Ischemic cardiomyopathy (prior CABG) → chronic LV systolic dysfunction.<br /></br>Mechanism: Impaired contractility → ↓ ejection → blood remains in ventricle → ↑ LV end-diastolic pressure transmitted to LA &amp; pulmonary veins → pulmonary congestion. Neurohormonal activation → vasoconstriction &amp; salt/water retention.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Initial work-up: BNP, troponin, ECG, bedside echo, basic chem panel.<br /></br>First-line acute therapy:<br /></br>• Loop diuretic (e.g., IV furosemide) to off-load preload.<br /></br>• Oxygen, ± nitrates, ± non-invasive ventilation.<br /></br>• Address precipitating factors (ischemia, arrhythmia, dietary indiscretion, infection).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“CHF = PPR ↓CO”<br /></br>P = Pressures up (CVP, PCWP)<br /></br>P = Peripheral &amp; Pulmonary edema<br /></br>R = Resistance up (SVR)<br /></br>↓CO = ↓ Cardiac Output → ↓ Stroke Volume  </p></br><p>“Failing pump can’t push.” (Stroke volume falls.)  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Central venous pressure – Should be elevated (JVD, peripheral edema).<br /></br>b. Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure – Elevated (pulmonary edema on CXR, S3).<br /></br>c. Shunt fraction (Qs/Qt) – Increased due to fluid-filled alveoli causing low V/Q units.<br /></br>e. Systemic vascular resistance – Usually increased because of sympathetic/RAAS-mediated vasoconstriction in low-output states.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In acute decompensated systolic heart failure, the heart’s forward pumping ability (stroke volume) decreases, while filling pressures and systemic resistance rise. Stroke volume index is therefore the major hemodynamic variable that is decreased.</p>
65. A 67-year-old woman comes to the office for evaluation 2 weeks before a scheduled right total hip replacement under general anesthesia. She has a 20-year history of rheumatoid arthritis. Current medications are methotrexate, prednisone, and leflunomide. On examination, any movement of the right hip causes pain. Results of laboratory studies are within the reference ranges. In addition to a chest x-ray and posteroanterior and lateral x-rays of the right hip, which of the following is the most appropriate imaging study before the operation?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Chest x-rays on inspiration and expiration</br>b. CT scan of the abdomen</br>c. Lateral x-ray of the cervical spine on flexion and extension</br>d. MRI of the right hip</br>e. Odontoid x-ray of the open mouth</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Lateral x-ray of the cervical spine on flexion and extension  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• Long-standing (20-year) rheumatoid arthritis (RA)<br /></br>• Elective surgery that requires general anesthesia and endotracheal intubation<br /></br>• No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities on routine pre-op labs or plain films  </p></br><p>How these clues narrow the differential<br /></br>• RA commonly involves the cervical spine, especially the atlantoaxial (C1–C2) joint.<br /></br>• Chronic synovitis and pannus formation can erode the transverse ligament → atlantoaxial instability (AAI) and possible odontoid subluxation.<br /></br>• During laryngoscopy the neck is flexed and extended; unrecognized AAI can precipitate spinal cord compression, acute quadriplegia, or even apnea.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Whenever you see “long-standing RA” + “upcoming surgery with intubation,” immediately think “screen for cervical spine instability with flexion-extension lateral films.” The pattern is high-yield on NBME/Step exams.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thinking<br /></br>1. Identify anesthetic risk unique to RA: AAI.<br /></br>2. Determine the study that best detects dynamic instability: lateral C-spine x-rays taken in both flexion and extension positions.<br /></br>3. Order this test pre-operatively to guide anesthetic technique and protect the spinal cord.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Patients with long-standing RA need dynamic lateral cervical spine x-rays before any procedure that involves neck manipulation.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life)<br /></br>• History of RA with duration, neurologic symptoms (paresthesias, weakness), examination findings.<br /></br>• Results of flexion-extension C-spine films and anesthesiology notification.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Etiology: RA synovitis erodes the dens and transverse ligament → laxity at C1–C2.<br /></br>Risk factors: Disease &gt;10 yrs, high RF/anti-CCP titers, corticosteroid use, previous cervical symptoms.<br /></br>Clinical manifestations: Occipital headaches, myelopathic signs, but may be asymptomatic until neck is manipulated.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnostic hierarchy<br /></br>1. Screening: Dynamic (flex-ext) lateral radiographs.<br /></br>2. If abnormal or equivocal: CT or MRI for surgical planning.  </p></br><p>Management if instability present<br /></br>• Modify airway technique (awake fiber-optic intubation, cervical collar).<br /></br>• Consider neurosurgical stabilization if severe.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Rheum at the top, check before you drop.”<br /></br>Visualize a red (rheum) traffic light at the atlanto-axial junction; you must “look both ways” (flex &amp; extend) before proceeding with intubation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chest x-rays on inspiration &amp; expiration<br /></br>• Used to detect small pneumothorax or diaphragmatic paralysis, not relevant here.<br /></br>• Does not address the unique RA complication during intubation.  </p></br><p>b. CT scan of the abdomen<br /></br>• No abdominal pathology is suggested; adds unnecessary radiation.  </p></br><p>d. MRI of the right hip<br /></br>• Already have PA &amp; lateral hip films for arthroplasty planning; MRI won’t change surgical approach.  </p></br><p>e. Odontoid (open-mouth) x-ray<br /></br>• Visualizes the dens but is static; misses dynamic subluxation that only appears on flexion/extension views. Flex-ext lateral films are the gold standard screening test.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In long-standing RA, always obtain flexion-extension lateral cervical spine x-rays before procedures requiring neck manipulation to rule out atlantoaxial instability and prevent catastrophic spinal cord injury.</p>
66. A 30-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after a 20-second generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He has a 3-week history of early morning projectile vomiting, headache, and blurred vision. On arrival, he is mildly lethargic. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. His father died of brain cancer at the age of 45 years. The patient has no known sick contacts. He does not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 138/98 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Finger-nose testing shows dysmetria. There is decreased rapid alternating movement of the hands. His gait is unsteady. An MRI of the brain shows a vascular tumor in the posterior fossa and ventriculomegaly of the third and lateral ventricles. Which of the following tests is most likely to reveal a second tumor with the same genetic abnormality?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Bone marrow biopsy</br>b. Colonoscopy</br>c. CT scan of abdomen</br>d. Mammography</br>e. Serum carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (given by user):<br /></br>C. CT scan of abdomen  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>   • “30-year-old man … 20-second generalized tonic-clonic seizure” – new-onset seizure → structural brain lesion until proven otherwise.<br /></br>   • “3-week history of early-morning projectile vomiting, headache, blurred vision” – classic for ↑ intracranial pressure (ICP).<br /></br>   • MRI: “vascular tumor in the posterior fossa” + obstructive “ventriculomegaly” – strongly suggests a cerebellar hemangioblastoma (highly vascular, cystic mass).<br /></br>   • “Father died of brain cancer at 45” – red flag for an autosomal-dominant cancer syndrome.<br /></br>   • Cerebellar signs: dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia, ataxic gait – localize to cerebellum.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   Cerebellar hemangioblastoma + family history in a young adult → think Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see a highly vascular cerebellar or retinal tumor in a young patient, immediately scan your memory for VHL and ask, “What other tumors travel with VHL?” Then choose the test that looks for the most common visceral companion—renal cell carcinoma (RCC).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise linkage:<br /></br>1. Posterior-fossa vascular tumor → hemangioblastoma.<br /></br>2. Hemangioblastoma in a young patient with positive family history → suspect VHL (AD mutation of VHL tumor-suppressor gene on chromosome 3p).<br /></br>3. VHL manifests with multiple tumors derived from the same germline mutation; the most frequent visceral neoplasm is clear-cell renal cell carcinoma (up to 70% lifetime risk).<br /></br>4. Best screening study for RCC and other abdominal VHL lesions (pheochromocytoma, pancreatic cysts/neuroendocrine tumors) is a cross-sectional abdominal imaging study—CT of the abdomen.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In a patient with a cerebellar hemangioblastoma, always evaluate the kidneys, adrenals, and pancreas for additional VHL-related tumors; abdominal CT is the single most useful additional study.  </p></br><p>Documentation point (real life): “Given suspicion for VHL syndrome, will obtain CT abdomen/pelvis to screen for RCC, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic lesions; genetic counseling recommended.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Gene: VHL (chromosome 3p) encodes a ubiquitin-ligase complex that degrades hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF).<br /></br>• Loss of VHL → ↑ HIF → ↑ VEGF, PDGF → highly vascular tumors.<br /></br>• Tumor spectrum:<br /></br>  – Hemangioblastomas (cerebellum, retina, brainstem, spinal cord)<br /></br>  – Clear-cell RCC (bilateral/multifocal)<br /></br>  – Pheochromocytoma (10–20%)<br /></br>  – Pancreatic serous or mucinous cysts, neuroendocrine tumors<br /></br>  – Endolymphatic sac tumors (inner ear)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• CNS hemangioblastoma: surgical resection ± stereotactic radiosurgery.<br /></br>• VHL surveillance: annual/biannual retinal exam, MRI brain/spine, abdominal CT or MRI (kidneys, adrenals, pancreas), plasma metanephrines.<br /></br>• RCC: partial or radical nephrectomy; small lesions may be ablated.<br /></br>• Pheochromocytoma: α-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) → β-blockade → adrenalectomy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“VHL = 3 letters → Chromosome 3 → 3 big organs”<br /></br>1. Brain (hemangioblastoma)<br /></br>2. Kidneys (RCC)<br /></br>3. Adrenals (pheochromocytoma)  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: HIPPEL<br /></br>H – Hemangioblastoma (CNS, retina)<br /></br>I – Increased HIF (pathway)<br /></br>P – Pheochromocytoma / Pancreatic tumors<br /></br>P – Partial nephrectomy for RCC<br /></br>E – Ear (endolymphatic sac tumor)<br /></br>L – Liver cysts (less common)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Bone marrow biopsy – would be appropriate if suspecting hematologic malignancy (e.g., leukemias) or metastatic infiltration; not classically linked to VHL.<br /></br>B. Colonoscopy – screens for colorectal neoplasia (e.g., FAP, Lynch). VHL has no correlation with colon polyps or carcinoma.<br /></br>D. Mammography – for breast cancer surveillance; VHL does not increase breast cancer risk.<br /></br>E. Serum CEA assay – tumor marker for colorectal, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, and some lung/breast cancers; not useful for hemangioblastoma/RCC and not a screening tool in VHL.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Students may jump to colonoscopy because “family history of cancer” or to CEA because “tumor marker” sounds broad. Always anchor on the specific syndrome suggested by the stem.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Cerebellar hemangioblastoma in a young patient + family history → think Von Hippel-Lindau. The most common visceral tumor in VHL is clear-cell renal cell carcinoma, best detected with an abdominal CT scan.</p>
67. A 19-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital because of a 6-day history of severe abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. She has no history of serious illness. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 75/min, respirations are 26/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Abdominal examination shows no distention; there is mild tenderness to palpation. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin              9.2 g/dL</br>Reticulocyte count      5.4 percent</br>Platelet count          17,000/mm3</br>Serum</br>Total bilirubin         2.5 mg/dL</br>Haptoglobin             8.2 mg/dL (N=40–265)</br>Lactate dehydrogenase   4285 U/L</br>Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Escherichia coli</br>b. Giardia lamblia</br>c. Salmonella typhi</br>d. Shigella sonnei</br>e. Staphylococcus aureus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Escherichia coli (enterohemorrhagic strain, classically O157:H7)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Bloody diarrhea for ≈1 week after an apparent food-borne illness → points to an invasive or toxin-producing enteric pathogen (EHEC, Shigella, Campylobacter, etc.).  </li></br><li>Triad suggestive of hemolytic-uremic syndrome (HUS):<br /></br>   • Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia → ↓haptoglobin, ↑LDH, ↑indirect bilirubin, schistocytes, and compensatory ↑reticulocytes.<br /></br>   • Thrombocytopenia → platelets 17 000/mm³.<br /></br>   • Renal injury is often present; creatinine isn’t given but HUS can precede azotemia by a day or two in some patients.  </li></br><li>Low-grade fever; abdomen only mildly tender (classic for EHEC—minimal fever relative to inflammatory bowel infections).  </li></br><li>Age (children/young adults) further supports typical HUS epidemiology.</li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach for exams:<br /></br>• Whenever you see bloody diarrhea + anemia + thrombocytopenia → immediately think “HUS → EHEC until proven otherwise.”<br /></br>• Scan labs for the hemolysis pattern (↑LDH, ↑bilirubin, ↓haptoglobin, ↑retics).<br /></br>• Ask: “Which bug produces Shiga or Shiga-like toxin and is common in the U.S.?” → EHEC O157:H7.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify bloody diarrhea → invasive-/toxin-mediated pathogen.<br /></br>Step 2: Note evidence of MAHA (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia) and marked thrombocytopenia → HUS/TTP spectrum.<br /></br>Step 3: Associate HUS with Shiga-like toxin → EHEC most common in North America.<br /></br>Core takeaway: HUS following bloody diarrhea is almost always due to enterohemorrhagic E. coli.</p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient: stool output/appearance, possible exposure (undercooked beef, unpasteurized juice, petting zoos), baseline renal function, hemolysis labs, daily CBC, strict I&amp;O, neuro checks.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• EHEC produces Shiga-like toxin (Stx) that inactivates 60S ribosomes in intestinal and endothelial cells.<br /></br>• Endothelial injury in renal glomeruli → platelet microthrombi → mechanical shearing of RBCs (schistocytes) + platelet consumption → anemia + thrombocytopenia.<br /></br>• Microthrombi obstruct renal microcirculation → acute kidney injury (creatinine usually rises).<br /></br>• Typically follows ingestion of undercooked ground beef, raw leafy vegetables, or unpasteurized cider; incubation 3–4 days.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Supportive care: IV fluids, meticulous electrolytes, early nephrology involvement (dialysis if needed).  </li></br><li>Do NOT give antibiotics or antimotility agents; they increase toxin release and worsen HUS.  </li></br><li>Confirm with stool culture on sorbitol-MacConkey agar or PCR for Stx genes, but treatment is supportive regardless.  </li></br><li>Monitor CBC, creatinine, electrolytes daily.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“HUS = Hemolysis, Uremia, Shiga toxin.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “Hamburger → Hemolysis” (undercooked hamburger = EHEC).<br /></br>Visual: Picture a cow (EHEC source) kicking red and white blood cells causing them to fragment and fall onto kidneys.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Giardia lamblia<br /></br>• Causes greasy, foul-smelling, non-bloody malabsorptive diarrhea.<br /></br>• No hemolysis, no thrombocytopenia.</p></br><p>c. Salmonella typhi<br /></br>• Typhoid fever: stepwise fever, relative bradycardia, rose spots, possible constipation → “pea-soup” diarrhea later.<br /></br>• Hemolytic anemia/thrombocytopenia uncommon.</p></br><p>d. Shigella sonnei<br /></br>• Causes dysentery, but classic HUS is associated with Shigella dysenteriae type 1 (not S. sonnei) and is far less common in the U.S. than EHEC-related HUS.</p></br><p>e. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>• Pre-formed enterotoxin → rapid‐onset vomiting and watery diarrhea within 6 hours (“potato-salad picnic bug”).<br /></br>• Self-limited, afebrile, no bloody stool, no hemolysis.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Choosing Shigella because of “Shiga toxin”—remember U.S. boards emphasize EHEC O157:H7 for post-diarrheal HUS.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Bloody diarrhea followed by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia = hemolytic-uremic syndrome. In North America, think enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (O157:H7). Avoid antibiotics; manage supportively.</p>
68. A 26-year-old nulligravid woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-week history of a mildly painful lump in her right breast. She has no history of serious illness or operative procedures. She takes no medications. Her paternal aunt was diagnosed with invasive breast cancer at the age of 65 years and underwent successful treatment with mastectomy and chemotherapy. A well-circumscribed, mildly tender, 1.5-cm mass is palpated in the upper outer quadrant of the patient's right breast, approximately 4 cm from the nipple. No other abnormalities are noted. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Excisional biopsy of the mass and axillary node dissection</br>b. Follow-up examination in 1 month</br>c. Mammography of the right breast</br>d. Stereotactic biopsy of the mass</br>e. Ultrasonography of the right breast</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Ultrasonography of the right breast  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; parity: 26-year-old, nulligravid – very dense breasts; mammography has low sensitivity.<br /></br>• Mass description: 1.5-cm, well-circumscribed, mildly tender → classic for a benign cyst or fibroadenoma.<br /></br>• Location: upper-outer quadrant (common but not diagnostic).<br /></br>• Family history: only one second-degree relative (paternal aunt) with cancer at 65 → not high-risk criteria (e.g., mother/sister, &lt;50 yr, multiple relatives, BRCA).<br /></br>• No “red-flag” findings: no skin changes, nipple retraction, pathologic discharge, weight loss, systemic symptoms.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Take-away<br /></br>Step 1: Ask, “How old is she?”<br /></br>Step 2: Decide image modality.<br /></br>• &lt;30 → Ultrasound first.<br /></br>• ≥30 or highly suspicious → Diagnostic mammogram (±US).<br /></br>Step 3: If ultrasound shows simple cyst → aspirate or reassure; solid or complex → core needle (stereotactic) biopsy.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Ultrasound differentiates:<br /></br>1. Simple cyst (anechoic, posterior enhancement) → benign.<br /></br>2. Complex cyst or solid mass → proceed to percutaneous core needle biopsy.<br /></br>It is fast, inexpensive, and spares radiation exposure. This satisfies the “triple-test” paradigm (exam + imaging + tissue when needed).  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Fibroadenoma: benign proliferation of stromal and epithelial elements; classically painless, firm, rubbery, mobile. Can be slightly tender pre-menses.<br /></br>• Breast cyst: hormonally driven duct obstruction; cyclic pain, well-circumscribed, fluctuant.<br /></br>Both peak in women 15-35 yr.  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Overview<br /></br>1. Ultrasound (diagnostic).<br /></br>2. If simple cyst and asymptomatic → reassure or aspirate.<br /></br>3. If solid or complex → ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy.<br /></br>4. Only if malignancy confirmed → lumpectomy ± sentinel node or mastectomy.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“U-29” mnemonic: Ultrasound is first choice for women Under 30 with a palpable breast mass.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Excisional biopsy + axillary node dissection<br /></br>• Indicated only after malignancy is confirmed and staging warrants nodal evaluation (usually sentinel node first). Too aggressive for initial work-up of a young woman with likely benign mass.  </p></br><p>B. Follow-up exam in 1 month<br /></br>• May miss an early cancer; current guidelines recommend imaging for any new discrete mass regardless of cyclic tenderness. Observation alone is insufficient.  </p></br><p>C. Mammography of the right breast<br /></br>• Breast tissue is very dense in women &lt;30; sensitivity is poor and exposes patient to unnecessary radiation. Mammogram is indicated ≥30 yr or for suspicious ultrasound findings.  </p></br><p>D. Stereotactic biopsy of the mass<br /></br>• Correct if imaging shows a suspicious solid lesion or microcalcifications; but you need imaging first to guide the biopsy. Skipping the ultrasound is premature.  </p></br><p>Core Takeaway<br /></br>For any palpable breast mass in a woman younger than 30 years, first obtain a targeted breast ultrasound.</p>
69. A 32-year-old man who is in the US Marine Corps is being discharged from the hospital after sustaining injuries 2 weeks ago in Southwest Asia when a rocket exploded a few yards away from him. He sustained fragmentation wounds to the right upper extremity, abdomen, and lower extremities bilaterally. At the time of injury, he received a dose of tetanus toxoid and underwent debridement and irrigation of his soft tissue wounds. Exploratory laparotomy showed a hepatic laceration and splenic laceration requiring splenectomy. He has no other history of major medical illness and takes no medications. He does not smoke. He drinks four 12-oz beers every weekend. Today, he reports no concerns. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination shows a well-healing midline abdominal incision and well-healing wounds over the right upper and lower extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate vaccination for this patient at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Hepatitis B</br>b. Meningococcal conjugate</br>c. Tetanus immune globulin</br>d. Tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis (Tdap)</br>e. Trivalent influenza</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (per key): B. Meningococcal conjugate vaccine  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• 32-y-o man had a splenectomy 2 weeks ago for trauma.<br /></br>• Healing well; no acute wound issues.<br /></br>• Question: “Which vaccine is most appropriate at this time?”<br /></br>Key “buzz phrase”: anatomic asplenia → susceptible to encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae type b). Immunization against these organisms is time-sensitive (≥14 days before or after splenectomy).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step 2, once you see “splenectomy,” immediately screen answer choices for vaccines against encapsulated bacteria; next decide which one is due first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify risk: Functional/anatomic asplenia → impaired IgM-mediated opsonization and rapid clearance of encapsulated bacteria.<br /></br>2. Evidence-based guidance:<br /></br>   a. Meningococcal conjugate (MenACWY): Two-dose primary series 8 wk apart; give first dose ≥14 days after splenectomy if not given pre-op.<br /></br>   b. Pneumococcal: PCV13 now, PPSV23 ≥8 wk later.<br /></br>   c. Hib: one-time dose if not received in childhood.<br /></br>3. Which of these appears in answer options? Only meningococcal conjugate is listed, so it is the indicated vaccine right now.</p></br><p>Take-home: Post-splenectomy = urgent administration of MenACWY (plus PCV13 and Hib when available).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• Spleen removes opsonized bacteria and is a major source of IgM-producing memory B-cells.<br /></br>• Without a spleen, patients can develop overwhelming postsplenectomy sepsis (OPSS), classically from Neisseria meningitidis, Strep pneumo, or Hib. Risk highest in first 1–2 yr but persists lifelong.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>TREATMENT / VACCINATION SCHEDULE PEARLS<br /></br>(“What would you actually do?”)<br /></br>1. MenACWY: First dose now (≥14 d post-op) → 2nd dose in 8 wk → booster every 5 yr.<br /></br>2. PCV13: Give now as well (not in options).<br /></br>   → PPSV23 ≥8 wk later, and booster at 5 yr &amp; age 65.<br /></br>3. Hib: single adult dose (if not received) at next visit.<br /></br>4. Annual inactivated influenza each fall.<br /></br>5. Daily oral penicillin prophylaxis is considered in children ≤5 yr but not routine in adults.</p></br><p>Mnemonic: “SHiN the spleen” (Strep pneumo, Hib, Neisseria) = vaccinate ASAP.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>INCORRECT ANSWER QUICK REVIEW<br /></br>A. Hepatitis B – Indicated for chronic liver disease, IV drug use, MSM, healthcare exposure, etc. Not specifically for splenectomy; no urgency.<br /></br>C. Tetanus immune globulin – Reserved for dirty wounds with uncertain or &lt;3-dose tetanus history. He already got tetanus toxoid and presumably completed childhood series.<br /></br>D. Tdap – Adult booster every 10 yr or once if never received; he just received tetanus toxoid 2 wks ago, so not needed now.<br /></br>E. Trivalent influenza – Excellent idea but given annually in the fall; MenACWY has greater immediate impact on mortality risk in new asplenic patient.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>• “No Spleen? Give SHiN vaccines within 2 weeks.”<br /></br>   S – Strep pneumo (PCV13/PPSV23)<br /></br>   Hi – Haemophilus influenzae type b<br /></br>   N – Neisseria meningitidis (MenACWY ± MenB)  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture the spleen as a SHiN-y shield; when it’s gone, you must replace the shield with vaccines.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Documentation (real world)<br /></br>• “Patient is 2 weeks post-splenectomy; received first dose MenACWY today. Will administer PCV13 and Hib vaccine before discharge, arrange PPSV23 and MenACWY booster per CDC schedule. Provided vaccine information sheet, discussed OPSI precautions.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway: After splenectomy, the single most urgent vaccination is against Neisseria meningitidis with the conjugate vaccine; administer as soon as the 14-day perioperative window allows.</p>
70. A 55-year-old woman comes to the office because of a 6-month history of cough productive of yellow sputum and a 1-week history of intermittent bloody streaks in her sputum. She also reports occasional night sweats during the past 3 months and an unintentional weight loss of 4.5 kg (10 lb) during the past year. She has not had fever, chills, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Medical history is notable for scoliosis and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Her only medication is omeprazole. She does not smoke cigarettes. She is 155 cm (5 ft 1 in) tall and weighs 40 kg (88 lb); BMI is 17 kg/m2. Temperature is 36.5 °C (97.7 °F), pulse is 102/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/61 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Auscultation of the lungs shows inspiratory crackles in the anterior right mid lung field. Musculoskeletal examination shows marked scoliosis. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. X-ray of the chest shows multiple nodules in the left lower lobe. CT scan of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bronchial artery embolization</br>b. CT-guided needle biopsy of a lung nodule</br>c. Levofloxacin therapy</br>d. Sputum stain for acid-fast bacilli</br>e. Thoracoscopic lobectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Sputum stain for acid-fast bacilli (AFB)</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately narrow the differential:<br /></br>• Subacute-to-chronic course (6 months of productive cough, 3 months of night sweats, 10-lb weight loss) → think mycobacterial or fungal infection, not typical CAP.<br /></br>• Intermittent hemoptysis, yellow sputum, inspiratory crackles → endobronchial or cavitary process.<br /></br>• Thin, middle-aged woman with scoliosis/BMI 17 → classic “Lady Windermere phenotype” (elderly, thin women with skeletal abnormalities) seen with nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) such as Mycobacterium avium complex or M kansasii.<br /></br>• Chest x-ray: multiple nodules LLL; CT (not shown on exam day you would see) typically demonstrates nodular/“tree-in-bud” opacities ± bronchiectasis in the right middle lobe &amp; lingula—hallmark of NTM lung disease.<br /></br>• Vitals essentially normal, O2 sat 98% → chronic indolent infection rather than acute pneumonia.</p></br><p>Big test-taking pattern: chronic constitutional symptoms + hemoptysis + nodular/bronchiectatic changes = first diagnostic step is to obtain sputum for AFB smear &amp; culture.</p></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: Any subacute pulmonary picture with weight loss and hemoptysis must rule out mycobacterial disease before giving fluoroquinolone or steroids (which could mask TB and delay diagnosis).</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why the correct answer is right)</p></br><p>Step 1: Recognize that the presentation is chronic, not acute.<br /></br>Step 2: Identify risk factors and CT pattern suggestive of atypical mycobacterial infection.<br /></br>Step 3: Initial, least-invasive, high-yield diagnostic test for suspected mycobacteria = expectorated (or induced) sputum for AFB smear, culture, and mycobacterial PCR.<br /></br>• Requires three early-morning samples on separate days.<br /></br>• Guides both diagnosis and speciation (MTB vs MAC) and susceptibility testing.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Always obtain AFB sputum studies first in a patient with chronic cough/weight loss/nodular lung findings before proceeding to invasive procedures or empiric antibiotics.</p></br><p>Documentation note: “Collected first of three early-morning sputum samples for AFB smear/culture; patient instructed to return for additional specimens.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Nontuberculous mycobacteria (especially MAC) colonize airways; in predisposed thin, elderly women with chest wall abnormalities they cause nodular bronchiectatic disease. Chronic airway inflammation → cough, sputum, hemoptysis, systemic symptoms. CT: cylindrical bronchiectasis + small nodules in right middle lobe &amp; lingula (“Lady Windermere syndrome”). Scoliosis and pectus excavatum are common comorbidities.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Three sputum AFB smears &amp; cultures ± PCR.<br /></br>2. High-resolution CT (already done) to show distribution and severity.<br /></br>3. Rule out cystic fibrosis or α-1-antitrypsin deficiency if presentation atypical.</p></br><p>Treatment (only after confirmed disease + symptoms/progression)<br /></br>• MAC: Azithromycin + Ethambutol + Rifampin for ≥12 months AND radiographic clearance.<br /></br>• TB (if MTB identified): RIPE therapy per susceptibility.<br /></br>Always obtain susceptibilities because macrolide resistance changes regimen.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Lady Windermere crosses her AFB-stained FAN”<br /></br>• Lady Windermere (thin older woman)<br /></br>• AFB = Acid-Fast Bacilli smear<br /></br>• FAN = Fatigue, Anorexia (weight loss), Night sweats  </p></br><p>Or visualize a thin Victorian lady (Windermere) coughing into a lace handkerchief with “AFB” embroidered on it—remind yourself “Send sputum for AFB.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Bronchial artery embolization<br /></br>• Used for massive or life-threatening hemoptysis (&gt;200 mL/24 h). Patient has only streaky hemoptysis.</p></br><p>b. CT-guided needle biopsy of a lung nodule<br /></br>• Indicated for solitary peripheral nodules suspected to be malignant (&gt;8 mm, smoker history, etc.). Here multiple nodules and bronchiectasis suggest infection; biopsy is more invasive than necessary and does not rule out MTB/NTM.</p></br><p>c. Levofloxacin therapy<br /></br>• Empiric outpatient CAP Rx. Fluoroquinolones have activity against MTB and can transiently improve symptoms, delaying TB diagnosis (a well-known USMLE trap).</p></br><p>e. Thoracoscopic lobectomy<br /></br>• Surgical resection is considered only for localized, drug-resistant NTM disease or malignancy after medical therapy fails. Not first step.</p></br><p>───────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Indolent pulmonary symptoms, weight loss, hemoptysis, and nodular/bronchiectatic CT findings in a thin woman should prompt immediate sputum staining and culture for acid-fast bacilli before any antibiotics or invasive procedures.</p>
71. A 25-year-old man is in the intensive care unit 2 weeks after he sustained third-degree burns over 55 percent of his total body surface area. He is sedated with fentanyl and midazolam, orotracheally intubated, and mechanically ventilated. He has undergone five skin grafting procedures since admission. His burns are 45 percent covered. He has not had any infection. Pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 105/62 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities of the head or neck. There are clean, dry bandages over the chest, abdomen, and lower extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management of this patient's airway?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Change the orotracheal intubation tube every 3 days</br>b. Switch to a laryngeal mask airway</br>c. Switch to nasotracheal intubation</br>d. Switch to a tracheostomy tube</br>e. No change in management is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: D ‒ Switch to a tracheostomy tube</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Time on the endotracheal tube: 2 weeks of continuous mechanical ventilation → classic “prolonged intubation” timeframe.<br /></br>• Future airway needs: Severe (55 % TBSA) third-degree burns + only 45 % graft coverage = many more operations, rehabilitation, and ventilatory support ahead.<br /></br>• Current cardiorespiratory status: Stable vitals, no active infection → can safely tolerate a tracheostomy.<br /></br>Cognitive road-map for test day: As soon as you see &gt;7–10 days of anticipated ventilation, your reflex should be “consider trach.” The stem often reassures you there is no airway edema or contraindication.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Ask, “Will this patient likely need the tube &gt;7-10 days?”—Yes.<br /></br>2. If yes, tracheostomy lowers ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), decreases airway resistance, allows better pulmonary hygiene, permits lighter sedation, and prevents subglottic stenosis.<br /></br>3. Therefore, convert to a trach now rather than waiting longer.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology/Practical Pearls<br /></br>• Prolonged translaryngeal intubation irritates the vocal cords and cricoid/tracheal mucosa → pressure necrosis → granulation → subglottic/tracheal stenosis.<br /></br>• Tracheostomy tube sits below the cords, has larger diameter, and is easier to clean/replace.<br /></br>• Burn patients often require multiple trips to the OR; a trach simplifies airway management and decreases aspiration risk.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Considerations<br /></br>Preferred timing: 7–10 days if ongoing ventilatory support is anticipated.<br /></br>Procedure: Percutaneous or surgical tracheostomy at bedside or in OR; document informed consent, indication (prolonged ventilation), and absence of contraindications (coagulopathy, neck infection, etc.).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Ten-day Tube Test” → If &gt;10 days on ETT or foreseeable need, trade the Tube for a Trach.<br /></br>Visualize the number 10 morphing into a “T” for Trach.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Change OETT every 3 days – Not evidence-based; risk of repeated laryngoscopy trauma; does not address long-term complications.<br /></br>B. Laryngeal mask airway – Designed for short procedures; no cuffed seal for positive-pressure ventilation &gt;1–2 hours; high aspiration risk.<br /></br>C. Nasotracheal intubation – Still translaryngeal; higher sinusitis and VAP rates; offers no advantage over oral ETT for long duration.<br /></br>E. No change – Misses opportunity to reduce complications now that prolonged ventilation is certain.</p></br><p>Key takeaway: In any patient who has already required (or clearly will require) &gt;7–10 days of mechanical ventilation, especially those with major burns or anticipated repeated surgeries, plan for an early tracheostomy.</p>
72. A 42-year-old woman comes to the office for follow-up of a renal calculus that was diagnosed in the emergency department 2 weeks ago. She passed the calculus in the emergency department after several hours of treatment with intravenous saline and pain medication. Today, she says she has not had any pain since that time. Her only medications are occasional acetaminophen and ibuprofen. She exercises three to four times weekly, follows a low-sodium diet, does not smoke cigarettes, and drinks two glasses of red wine nightly. She works as a physician. She is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities.  </br>Results of serum studies are shown:  </br>Calcium 11.0 mg/dL  </br>Urea nitrogen 15 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL  </br>Phosphorus 3.8 mg/dL  </br></br>Results of the remainder of a basic metabolic panel and complete blood count are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is most appropriate to obtain to evaluate this patient's increased serum calcium concentration?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. DEXA scan  </br>b. Ionized serum calcium concentration  </br>c. Mammography  </br>d. Radionuclide bone scan  </br>e. Serum parathyroid hormone concentration  </br>f. Urinary calcium excretion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Serum parathyroid hormone (PTH) concentration  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key finding: Persistently elevated total serum calcium (11.0 mg/dL) discovered at an outpatient follow-up visit in an otherwise healthy 42-year-old woman.<br /></br>• Associated clue: Recent calcium-containing renal stone (“stones”)—a classic manifestation of chronic hypercalcemia.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No constitutional symptoms, normal creatinine, normal phosphorus (argues against advanced malignancy or severe hyperparathyroidism with phosphaturia).<br /></br>• Differential for hypercalcemia:<br /></br>  – PTH-dependent (↑ or inappropriately normal PTH): primary hyperparathyroidism, lithium use, familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia (FHH).<br /></br>  – PTH-independent (suppressed PTH): malignancy (PTHrP, bone mets), vitamin D intoxication, granulomatous disease, thyrotoxicosis, medications (thiazides), prolonged immobilization.<br /></br>Metacognitive approach: When you see an asymptomatic outpatient hypercalcemia, FIRST split the differential by checking the PTH level. Everything downstream (imaging, urine calcium, etc.) hinges on knowing if the hypercalcemia is PTH-driven.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Confirm true hypercalcemia (already done on basic metabolic panel).<br /></br>Step 2: Determine whether the hypercalcemia is PTH-dependent or PTH-independent → order a serum PTH level.<br /></br>Core takeaway: “PTH first” is the algorithmic next step whenever unexplained hypercalcemia is found.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls if this were a real encounter: “Repeat serum calcium confirmed hypercalcemia (11 mg/dL). Plan to obtain intact PTH to differentiate PTH-mediated vs non–PTH-mediated etiologies and guide further work-up.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT)<br /></br>• Etiology: parathyroid adenoma (~85%), hyperplasia, rarely carcinoma.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Autonomous PTH secretion → ↑ bone resorption, ↑ renal Ca²⁺ reabsorption, ↑ renal 1-α-hydroxylase activity → ↑ 1,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D → ↑ intestinal Ca²⁺ absorption.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: “Stones, bones, abdominal groans, psychic overtones.” Nephrolithiasis is often the first clue.<br /></br>• Labs: ↑ Ca²⁺, ↑/inappropriately normal PTH, ↓ or normal phosphorus, ↑ urinary Ca²⁺.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot<br /></br>1. Intact PTH level.<br /></br>2. If PTH high/normal → evaluate 24-hr urine Ca²⁺ (differentiate PHPT from FHH), neck US/99mTc-sestamibi scan for localization if surgery planned.<br /></br>3. Definitive Tx: parathyroidectomy if symptomatic, nephrolithiasis, osteoporosis, age &lt;50, or Ca²⁺ ≥1 mg/dL above upper limit.
