Otherwise consider cinacalcet or monitoring.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“High Ca²⁺? Play ‘PTH or Not PTH’ first.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: “HIP-VOMIT” for PTH-independent causes<br /></br>• H yperthyroidism<br /></br>• I mmobilization<br /></br>• P THrP (malignancy)<br /></br>• V itamin D excess<br /></br>• O verdose of vitamin A<br /></br>• M ilk-alkali / Meds (thiazides)<br /></br>• I nflammatory granulomas (sarcoid)<br /></br>• T umor bone mets  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. DEXA scan – Assesses bone density, appropriate only AFTER confirming PTH-mediated disease or other risk factors. It does not determine the cause of hypercalcemia.<br /></br>b. Ionized serum calcium – Useful if you suspect pseudohypercalcemia (e.g., very high albumin). Total Ca²⁺ is already elevated and there’s no albumin value suggesting artifact; you still need the PTH to direct the work-up.<br /></br>c. Mammography – Age-appropriate cancer screening but unrelated to the immediate evaluation of hypercalcemia.<br /></br>d. Radionuclide bone scan – Detects metastatic bone lesions; malignancy is possible but you must first do the inexpensive, guideline-directed PTH test.<br /></br>f. Urinary calcium excretion – Ordered after PTH results to distinguish PHPT (↑ urine Ca²⁺) from FHH (↓ urine Ca²⁺). It is not the first step.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>The algorithmic first test in any newly confirmed hypercalcemia is an intact serum parathyroid hormone level to decide whether the process is PTH-dependent or not.</p>
73. An asymptomatic 57-year-old woman comes to the physician to request a new blood test for the early detection of pancreatic cancer that she read about in a newspaper article. She is concerned about her risk because her stepmother died of pancreatic cancer at the age of 61 years. The patient has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Examination shows no abnormalities. The physician agrees to perform the test, and the results are positive. The prevalence of pancreatic cancer in the community is 1 percent. The test has a sensitivity of 85 percent and a specificity of 90 percent. In a discussion of the test results with the patient, which of the following is the most appropriate explanation for her risk for pancreatic cancer?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. She is at high risk, because of her family history</br>b. She is at high risk, because of the high sensitivity of the test</br>c. She is at high risk, because of the high specificity of the test</br>d. She is not at high risk, because of the low pretest probability</br>e. She is not at high risk, because the sensitivity and specificity are similar</br>f. She is not at high risk, because she has no symptoms</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. “She is not at high risk, because of the low pretest probability.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Asymptomatic, average-risk 57-year-old woman.<br /></br>• Family history is in a step-mother → NOT a biologic (first-degree) relative.<br /></br>• Community prevalence of pancreatic cancer = 1 %. (This is her pre-test probability.)<br /></br>• Test characteristics: sensitivity 85 %, specificity 90 %.<br /></br>Task: interpret a POSITIVE result.</p></br><p>How to approach similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify the patient’s true baseline risk (prevalence or relevant risk factors).<br /></br>2. Recall that what the patient really wants to know after a positive result is the Positive Predictive Value (PPV).<br /></br>3. Recognize that PPV is heavily driven by disease prevalence. Low prevalence → low PPV, even for “good” tests.<br /></br>4. If prevalence is given (1 %), crunch the 2×2 table or use the PPV formula.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Pre-test probability (prevalence) = 1 % (0.01).<br /></br>2. Plug into PPV:<br /></br>   PPV = (Sensitivity × Prevalence) ÷ [(Sensitivity × Prevalence) + (1 – Specificity) × (1 – Prevalence)]<br /></br>   = (0.85 × 0.01) ÷ [(0.85 × 0.01) + (0.10 × 0.99)]<br /></br>   = 0.0085 ÷ (0.0085 + 0.099) ≈ 0.0085 ÷ 0.1075 ≈ 0.079, or ~8 %.<br /></br>3. Interpretation: Out of 100 identical patients with a positive test, only ~8 truly have pancreatic cancer → patient is NOT at “high” risk despite the positive result.</p></br><p>Take-home concept<br /></br>PPV “parallels prevalence.” In diseases with very low prevalence, even good tests yield many false positives, so a single positive result does not confer a high post-test probability.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl if this were a real patient<br /></br>• Note test result, numerical PPV discussion, counsel on uncertainty, plan for confirmatory imaging (eg, contrast CT) before labeling diagnosis.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Pancreatic adenocarcinoma risk factors<br /></br>• Age &gt;55, smoking, chronic pancreatitis, DM, hereditary syndromes (BRCA2, Peutz-Jeghers, FAMMM).<br /></br>• First-degree relative doubles risk; a step-parent does NOT.<br /></br>Thus her baseline prevalence remains that of the general population (~1 %).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>A positive screening assay must be followed by:<br /></br>• High-quality pancreatic protocol CT or MRCP ± EUS-guided biopsy to confirm disease before any intervention.<br /></br>• Emphasize to students: Never treat off a screening test alone.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“3 P’s” mnemonic:<br /></br>PPV Parallels Prevalence. NPV (Negative Predictive Value) is inverse (↑ when prevalence ↓).</p></br><p>SeNsitive → rule OUT (few false negatives).<br /></br>SPecific → rule IN (few false positives).<br /></br>But neither guarantees high PPV when prevalence is low.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. “High risk due to family history.”<br /></br>• Step-mother ≠ biologic relative → no genetic link; no meaningful risk elevation.</p></br><p>B. “High risk due to high sensitivity.”<br /></br>• Sensitivity governs false-negative rate, not probability that a positive result is true. High sensitivity alone cannot overcome low prevalence.</p></br><p>C. “High risk due to high specificity.”<br /></br>• Specificity influences false-positive rate but PPV still depends heavily on prevalence. 90 % specificity with 1 % prevalence still yields many false positives.</p></br><p>E. “Not at high risk because sensitivity &amp; specificity are similar.”<br /></br>• Similarity of Se &amp; Sp is irrelevant. Risk hinges on post-test probability (PPV).</p></br><p>F. “Not at high risk because she has no symptoms.”<br /></br>• Lack of symptoms doesn’t negate risk; many cancers are asymptomatic early. The correct rationale is low pre-test probability, not absence of symptoms.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In low-prevalence settings, even a positive result on a reasonably sensitive/specific test rarely confers a high likelihood of disease. Always translate test characteristics + prevalence into PPV before labeling a patient “high risk.”</p>
74. A 37-year-old woman comes to the physician with her husband because she has been unable to conceive for 13 months. They each have one child from previous marriages. The patient reports that menses have been occurring at irregular 4-month intervals during the past year. Her last menstrual period was 3 months ago. She has no history of gynecologic illness or abnormal Pap smears. She takes no medications. She exercises regularly but says that she has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight gain during the past 2 years. She appears well nourished. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (154 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no hirsutism or galactorrhea. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. A urine pregnancy test is negative. Basal body temperature charts show a monophasic pattern. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of serum estradiol concentration</br>b. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>c. Semen analysis</br>d. Hysterosalpingography</br>e. Laparoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) concentration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key positives<br /></br>  – 13 months of infertility (meets definition &gt;12 mo)<br /></br>  – Oligo-/amenorrhea (menses every 4 mo, last period 3 mo ago)<br /></br>  – Basal body temperature (BBT) chart is monophasic → anovulation<br /></br>  – No hyperandrogenism (no hirsutism), no galactorrhea, normal pelvic exam<br /></br>  – Prior fertility in both partners → decreased concern for primary anatomic or male factor initially<br /></br>• Key negatives<br /></br>  – Pregnancy ruled out (negative urine test)<br /></br>  – BMI 25 (not underweight), no heavy exercise or eating disorder to suggest hypothalamic amenorrhea  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Infertility + anovulation → search for endocrine causes.<br /></br>2. After excluding pregnancy, first-line screening labs = TSH and prolactin (both inexpensive, non-invasive, common causes).<br /></br>3. Proceed to more invasive or expensive tests only if initial screen is normal.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Hypothyroidism elevates TRH, which raises prolactin, inhibiting pulsatile GnRH → anovulation and infertility.<br /></br>• Because hypothyroidism is common, easily measured, and readily treated, TSH is the next diagnostic step.<br /></br>• Documentation: “Patient with secondary infertility and chronic anovulation. Plan: order serum TSH (and prolactin) to evaluate for thyroid or hyperprolactinemia-mediated ovulatory dysfunction.”  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In any woman with infertility and menstrual irregularity, always rule out pregnancy, then check TSH ± prolactin before pursuing male factor or invasive female investigations.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Normal fertility requires ovulation → mid-cycle LH surge → post-ovulatory ↑progesterone, producing a biphasic BBT chart.<br /></br>• Hypothyroidism → ↑TRH → ↑prolactin → ↓GnRH pulsatility → ↓FSH/LH → anovulation → monophasic BBT.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• If TSH elevated → start levothyroxine; ovulatory cycles often resume, improving fertility.<br /></br>• If TSH normal, order serum prolactin next; if prolactin normal → move to male factor (semen analysis) and tubal factors (hysterosalpingography, laparoscopy) as indicated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Preg-TSH-Pro” sequence for anovulation work-up:<br /></br>P – Pregnancy test (β-hCG)<br /></br>T – TSH<br /></br>S – Serum prolactin (Hyper-Prolactinemia)  </p></br><p>Visual: stop-light. Green = pregnancy, Yellow = thyroid, Red = prolactin; clear these before going down the fertility road.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Estradiol level<br /></br>   • Low estradiol reflects ovarian failure but is downstream. TSH is more common, cheaper, and checked first.  </p></br><p>c. Semen analysis<br /></br>   • Always part of infertility work-up, but only after obvious female anovulation causes are excluded. Her BBT proves anovulation, so female endocrine evaluation precedes male testing.  </p></br><p>d. Hysterosalpingography<br /></br>   • Evaluates uterine cavity &amp; tubal patency—important if cycles are ovulatory or endocrine labs normal. Not first step when anovulation is evident.  </p></br><p>e. Laparoscopy<br /></br>   • Invasive; reserved for suspected endometriosis or adhesions after simpler tests negative. Overkill at this stage.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a woman with infertility and clear evidence of anovulation, pregnancy test first, then TSH (and prolactin). Early identification and treatment of hypothyroidism can restore fertility, making TSH measurement the most appropriate next diagnostic step.</p>
75. A 42-year-old man with anemia comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 28 percent</br>Mean corpuscular volume 62 µm³</br>Leukocyte count 8000/mm³</br>Reticulocyte count 5 percent</br>Platelet count 150,000/mm³</br>Serum bilirubin 2.7 mg/dL</br></br>Hemoglobin electrophoresis shows:</br>Hemoglobin A 35 percent (N=95 percent–98 percent)</br>Hemoglobin A2 6 percent (N=2 percent–4 percent)</br>Hemoglobin F 59 percent (N=0 percent–2 percent)</br></br>A blood smear shows numerous nucleated erythrocytes with marked anisopoikilocytosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency</br>b. Hereditary spherocytosis</br>c. Iron deficiency anemia</br>d. Sickle cell disease</br>e. Thalassemia intermedia</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (given): e. Thalassemia intermedia  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>Key diagnostic clues<br /></br>• Microcytic indices – MCV 62 µm³ (think iron deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, lead, thalassemia, sideroblastic; “T.I.L.A.S.”)<br /></br>• Hemolysis markers – reticulocytes 5 % (↑), bilirubin 2.7 mg/dL (indirect ↑)<br /></br>• Electrophoresis – HbA 35 % (↓), HbA₂ 6 % (mild ↑), HbF 59 % (massive ↑)<br /></br>• Peripheral smear – nucleated RBCs + marked anisopoikilocytosis (classic for ineffective erythropoiesis in thalassemia)  </p></br><p>How these clues narrow the differential<br /></br>1. Microcytosis with elevated retic count ⇒ microcytic hemolytic process (rules out pure iron deficiency, which has ↓ retic).<br /></br>2. Electrophoresis pattern is the clincher: high HbF ± high HbA₂ with low HbA is diagnostic of β-thalassemia phenotype.<br /></br>3. Patient is 42 y/o and not transfusion-dependent ⇒ milder form → “β-thalassemia intermedia”. (β-major presents in infancy; β-minor has normal HbF).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exam questions, any microcytic anemia + very high HbF on electrophoresis → jump to β-thalassemia (intermedia or major depending on severity).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify anemia type → low MCV.<br /></br>2. Decide if production vs. destruction → high retic &amp; bili = hemolysis/ineffective erythropoiesis.<br /></br>3. Order of tests on USMLE logic → “after CBC, do iron studies or electrophoresis.” Here electrophoresis provided and diagnostic.<br /></br>4. Interpret electrophoresis → HbA massively reduced, HbF dominant. β-chain problem.<br /></br>5. Determine severity by age, transfusion need → survive to adulthood → intermedia.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: β-thalassemia intermedia = decreased/absent β-chain synthesis causing chronically elevated HbF ± HbA₂ and microcytic hemolytic anemia that is less severe than β-major.  </p></br><p>Documentation bullets (real patient)<br /></br>• Chronic hemolytic microcytic anemia with electrophoretic pattern consistent with β-thalassemia intermedia.<br /></br>• Counsel regarding genetic nature, possible need for intermittent transfusion, folate supplementation, iron chelation if transfused, and family screening.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>• β-thalassemia: point mutations in β-globin gene → ↓ or absent β-chain production.<br /></br>• Unpaired α-chains precipitate → ineffective erythropoiesis + hemolysis (marrow &amp; spleen) → anemia, high retic, indirect hyperbilirubinemia.<br /></br>• To compensate, γ-chain production persists → ↑ HbF. δ-chain may modestly rise → ↑ HbA₂.<br /></br>• Intermedia genotype often β⁺/β⁺ or β⁰/β⁺ (some β-chain made).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>TREATMENT / DIAGNOSTICS PEARLS<br /></br>• Baseline: folic acid, avoid excess iron.<br /></br>• Intermittent transfusions during stress (infection, pregnancy).<br /></br>• Monitor ferritin &amp; liver iron; chelation (deferoxamine, deferasirox) if cumulative transfusions.<br /></br>• Consider splenectomy if hypersplenism; hematopoietic stem-cell transplant is curative but reserved for severe phenotypes.<br /></br>• Genetic counseling—autosomal recessive.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“β-Tal = Big F rise”: In β-thalassemia, F (fetal hemoglobin) is the predominant fraction.<br /></br>MCV in the 60s + HbF &gt;50 % → think “β-thalassemia intermedia/major.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>WHY THE OTHER CHOICES ARE WRONG  </p></br><p>a. Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency<br /></br>• Normocytic, episodic hemolysis after oxidative stress, bite cells/Heinz bodies.<br /></br>• Electrophoresis normal (HbA majority).  </p></br><p>b. Hereditary spherocytosis<br /></br>• Normocytic or mild microcytosis, ↑ MCHC, spherocytes on smear, positive osmotic fragility.<br /></br>• Normal electrophoresis (HbA 95-98 %).  </p></br><p>c. Iron deficiency anemia<br /></br>• Microcytic but ↓ retic count, normal electrophoresis, low ferritin, ↑ TIBC.<br /></br>• No hemolytic markers (bilirubin normal).  </p></br><p>d. Sickle cell disease (HbSS)<br /></br>• Electrophoresis: HbS ~85-95 %, HbF 5-15 %, HbA 0 %.<br /></br>• Would not have high HbA₂; HbS band would be detected.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom line: A microcytic hemolytic anemia with markedly elevated HbF and reduced HbA in an adult who is not transfusion-dependent = β-thalassemia intermedia.</p>
76. A 24-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-week history of worsening cough productive of scant yellowish sputum. He smokes marijuana daily but does not use tobacco or alcohol. He had asthma in childhood but has not required any medication for more than 10 years. He has sickle cell trait. His temperature is 38°C (100.5°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 96 percent. Examination shows a persistent cough and moist oral mucosa. Crackles are heard in the right middle lung field. Cardiac and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. His hemoglobin concentration is 14 g/dL, leukocyte count is 12,000/mm3, and platelet count is 580,000/mm3. An x-ray of the chest is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Oral azithromycin</br>b. Oral cephalexin</br>c. Intravenous ampicillin</br>d. Intravenous ampicillin-sulbactam</br>e. Intravenous ceftazidime</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Oral azithromycin</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key findings<br /></br>   • Age 24, otherwise healthy (remote childhood asthma, sickle-cell trait only)<br /></br>   • Acute 1-week cough, scant yellow sputum, low-grade fever (38 °C)<br /></br>   • Mild leukocytosis (12 K) and reactive thrombocytosis (580 K)<br /></br>   • Crackles localized to right mid-lung; CXR (image) shows a single lobar infiltrate without effusion<br /></br>   • Normal vital signs except low-grade fever; SaO₂ 96 % on room air</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>What these clues mean<br /></br>   • Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is the presumptive diagnosis.<br /></br>   • No “CURB-65” severity markers (Confusion, Uremia, Respiratory rate ≥30, BP &lt;90/60, age ≥65) → safe for outpatient treatment.<br /></br>   • No significant cardiopulmonary, renal, hepatic, or immunocompromising comorbidities that would broaden coverage.<br /></br>   • Therefore choose standard outpatient CAP therapy per IDSA/ATS guidelines.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive/Métacognitive take-away<br /></br>   Approach every CAP vignette with two rapid questions:<br /></br>   a. Where will you treat (out- vs in- patient)? – Use CURB-65.<br /></br>   b. Does the patient have comorbidities or antibiotic risks? – Decides narrow vs broad regimen.<br /></br>   For a young, healthy outpatient → macrolide (or doxycycline) monotherapy.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Diagnose CAP from history, exam, and CXR.<br /></br>Step 2: Assess severity (CURB-65 = 0). → Outpatient.<br /></br>Step 3: Pick regimen:<br /></br>   • Outpatient, no comorbidities → macrolide (azithro/clarithro) or doxycycline.<br /></br>   • Outpatient with comorbidities → β-lactam + macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone.<br /></br>   • Inpatient (non-ICU) → IV β-lactam + macrolide or resp. FQ.<br /></br>   • ICU → broader β-lactam + azithro or FQ ± MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: “Azithro alone is enough for a healthy outpatient with uncomplicated CAP.”</p></br><p>Documentation note: diagnosis, CURB-65 = 0, plan for oral azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg days 2-5, return precautions, and follow-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>CAP arises from inhalation/aspiration of pathogens (Strep pneumo, H. influenzae, Moraxella, atypicals). In young immunocompetent adults, S. pneumoniae or atypicals (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia) dominate. Macrolides inhibit the 50S ribosomal subunit, covering both typical (S. pneumo) and atypical organisms.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line outpatient: azithromycin (500 mg PO day 1, then 250 mg PO daily ×4) or doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×5-7 d.<br /></br>• No need for blood cultures or sputum cultures in low-severity outpatient CAP.<br /></br>• CXR follow-up only if symptoms persist &gt;7-10 weeks.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“CAP CAP” mnemonic:<br /></br>   C – Check CURB-65<br /></br>   A – Assess comorbidities<br /></br>   P – Place of treatment (out/inpatient)<br /></br>   C – Choose macrolide (if healthy outpatient)<br /></br>   A – Add β-lactam if comorbid<br /></br>   P – Pseudomonal/MRSA coverage if severe/hospitalized</p></br><p>Or think “Azithro Alone for Able-bodied Adults.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Oral cephalexin – 1st-gen cephalosporin good for skin/soft-tissue MSSA/Strep; no atypical coverage and suboptimal S. pneumo activity in lung tissue.<br /></br>C. IV ampicillin – IV route unnecessary; poor atypical coverage; used for neonatal GBS, Listeria, enterococcus, not routine outpatient CAP.<br /></br>D. IV ampicillin-sulbactam – Inpatient aspiration or polymicrobial, broader than needed, IV only.<br /></br>E. IV ceftazidime – Anti-Pseudomonal 3rd-gen cephalosporin for hospital-acquired/ICU pneumonia; unnecessary and IV.</p></br><p>Each incorrect option either lacks atypical coverage, is overly broad, or requires hospitalization, none of which fit this patient’s presentation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A young, otherwise healthy patient with mild community-acquired pneumonia should receive outpatient macrolide monotherapy—oral azithromycin.</p>
77. A 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician because his parents are concerned that he is much shorter than his peers. He feels well. On questioning, he reports that he has not had constipation, dry skin, or urinary frequency. He has no history of serious illness. There is no family history of growth abnormalities. His mother is 160 cm (5 ft 3 in) tall, and his father is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall. The patient is just below the 3rd percentile for height and weight and at the 50th percentile for BMI. Review of his growth chart shows he has been growing parallel to but below the 3rd percentile since the age of 2 years. On examination, the thyroid gland is barely palpable and appears normal. Genital and pubic hair development are Tanner stage 1. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Constitutional growth delay</br>b. Familial short stature</br>c. Growth hormone deficiency</br>d. Hypothyroidism</br>e. Type 1 diabetes mellitus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Constitutional growth delay (CGD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age/sex: 14-year-old boy.<br /></br>• Height/weight percentiles: just below 3rd % for both height and weight, BMI 50th % → proportionate short stature.<br /></br>• Growth-chart pattern: has tracked parallel to (not crossing) the 3rd % since age 2 → normal growth velocity, no downward inflection.<br /></br>• Puberty: Tanner stage 1 for both genitalia and pubic hair at age 14 → markedly delayed puberty.<br /></br>• ROS: no systemic symptoms (no constipation, dry skin, polyuria, etc.).<br /></br>• Family: parents of average height; no family history of short stature.<br /></br>Key pattern: Normal growth velocity + delayed skeletal/sexual maturation + otherwise healthy = CGD.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a stem shows (1) proportionate short stature, (2) normal growth velocity, and (3) delayed puberty, immediately think CGD. Ask yourself, “Is this a late bloomer with normal-velocity growth?”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Determine if growth velocity is normal: yes (parallel curve).<br /></br>2. Decide if short stature is proportionate or disproportionate: proportionate (weight low in same range).<br /></br>3. Look for systemic disease clues: none.<br /></br>4. Evaluate pubertal status: clearly delayed.<br /></br>5. Synthesize → CGD: the classic benign “late bloomer.”<br /></br>Core takeaway: CGD = normal growth rate but delayed bone age and puberty; projected adult height is normal.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic): record percentile trends, Tanner staging, review of systems for chronic disease, family heights, and plan for bone-age radiograph + follow-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>CGD is a variant of normal development.<br /></br>• Etiology: temporary delay in hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis activation.<br /></br>• Bone age: delayed (≈ chronological age – 2 y).<br /></br>• Final adult height: normal, because growth continues when peers have stopped.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Management Quick Review<br /></br>• Bone-age x-ray (left hand/wrist): bone age &lt; chronological age.<br /></br>• Reassurance + yearly monitoring is usually all that’s required.<br /></br>• Short course of low-dose testosterone can be considered if major psychosocial distress.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Late Bloomer 3-D Rule”<br /></br>1. Delayed bone age<br /></br>2. Delayed puberty<br /></br>3. Doesn’t drop off the curve (velocity normal)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>B. Familial short stature<br /></br>• Parents are short or mid-parental height predicts a short adult.<br /></br>• Bone age ≈ chronological age; puberty onset normal.<br /></br>• Our patient’s puberty is delayed and parents are average height.</p></br><p>C. Growth hormone (GH) deficiency<br /></br>• Growth velocity slows; child falls off their curve after infancy.<br /></br>• Often ↑ weight-for-height (“cherubic” facies), possible hypoglycemia, midline defects.<br /></br>• This boy’s velocity is normal and parallel.</p></br><p>D. Hypothyroidism<br /></br>• Linear growth failure with weight gain, constipation, dry skin, sluggish reflexes ± goiter; velocity slows (drops off curve).<br /></br>• None of those symptoms present; velocity normal.</p></br><p>E. Type 1 diabetes mellitus<br /></br>• Presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, possible DKA—not isolated short stature.<br /></br>• Growth usually normal until metabolic decompensation; vignette lacks classic symptoms.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A healthy adolescent with proportionate short stature, normal growth velocity, and markedly delayed puberty = Constitutional growth delay. Expect a normal adult height after late pubertal catch-up.</p>
78. A 25-year-old primigravid woman at 20 weeks’ gestation comes to the physician because of a 3-week history of feeling flushed and shaking of her hands. She has been crying frequently, and her husband has told her that she seems more irritable. Her pregnancy had been uncomplicated. She has had a 4.5-kg (10-lb) weight loss since her last examination 4 weeks ago. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 115/min, and blood pressure is 145/90 mm Hg. Her skin is smooth, moist, and velvety to the touch. Examination shows a small, diffuse, smooth, soft goiter. There is a fine continuous tremor bilaterally. Her serum free thyroxine concentration is 14.2 ng/dL, and serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration is less than 0.002 μU/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Administration of antithyroid medications</br>b. Administration of oral saturated solution of potassium iodide</br>c. Initiation of 131I therapy</br>d. External-beam radiation therapy to the thyroid</br>e. Thyroidectomy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>a. Administration of antithyroid medications  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that scream hyperthyroidism/Graves disease:<br /></br>• 20-week pregnant woman (physiologic thyroid changes in pregnancy are mild; this is pathologic).<br /></br>• 3-week history of heat intolerance (“feeling flushed”), weight loss, fine tremor, irritability, moist/velvety skin, tachycardia, and a diffuse non-nodular goiter.<br /></br>• Labs: very high free T4 (14.2 ng/dL; nl ≈ 0.8–2.7) and undetectable TSH (&lt;0.002 µU/mL).  </p></br><p>Pertinent negatives: no thyroid eye findings or pretibial myxedema are mentioned, ruling out severe orbitopathy that would change management.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize overt hyperthyroidism.<br /></br>2. Ask, “Is the patient pregnant?” → Yes → Radioiodine is automatically excluded.<br /></br>3. First-line therapy in pregnancy = antithyroid drugs (ATDs). Decide PTU vs methimazole based on trimester (see below).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Confirm diagnosis: suppressed TSH + elevated free T4 → overt hyperthyroidism.<br /></br>2. Determine etiology likely Graves (diffuse, soft goiter, young woman).<br /></br>3. Apply pregnancy algorithm:<br /></br>   • 1st trimester: propylthiouracil (PTU) is preferred.<br /></br>   • 2nd &amp; 3rd trimester: switch to methimazole to avoid PTU hepatotoxicity.<br /></br>   At 20 weeks (early 2nd trimester) you still initiate an ATD; many clinicians continue PTU until week 16–20 then transition to methimazole. The NBME simply wants “antithyroid medications” as the initial step.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In pregnant patients with Graves disease, start an antithyroid drug; radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated and surgery is reserved for refractory cases or drug intolerance.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record maternal symptoms, thyroid exam, free T4 &amp; TSH values, fetal gestational age, and treatment plan including drug choice and monitoring schedule.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Graves disease = IgG thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins bind the TSH receptor → unregulated thyroid hormone synthesis. Pregnancy can worsen Graves due to immune modulation and hCG’s weak TSH-like effects. Maternal thyrotoxicosis increases risk of miscarriage, pre-eclampsia, preterm delivery, and fetal/neonatal thyrotoxicosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Monitoring  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Antithyroid drugs<br /></br>   • Propylthiouracil (PTU): inhibits thyroid peroxidase AND 5’-deiodinase. Drug of choice in 1st trimester.<br /></br>   • Methimazole: inhibits thyroid peroxidase; fewer liver side-effects; preferred after 1st trimester.<br /></br>   Goal: keep free T4 in high-normal range to prevent fetal hypothyroidism; measure free T4 every 4–6 weeks and adjust dose.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Symptomatic control<br /></br>   • β-blocker (propranolol) can be used short-term for tremor/tachycardia but was not listed in the options.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Alternatives when ATDs fail or are contraindicated<br /></br>   • Thyroidectomy (2nd trimester ideal) after 6–8 weeks of ATDs ± potassium iodide pre-op.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Contraindicated<br /></br>   • Radioactive iodine (¹³¹I) crosses placenta and destroys fetal thyroid.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “PTU for Pregnancy’s First Trimester Use; then Move to Methimazole.”<br /></br>Radioiodine: “Radio-I races through placenta and ruins baby’s thyroid.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Oral saturated potassium iodide (SSKI)<br /></br>   • Blocks thyroid hormone release and organification acutely (Wolff-Chaikoff) – used pre-op or in thyroid storm. Can cause fetal goiter/hypothyroidism; not first-line chronic therapy.  </p></br><p>c. ¹³¹I therapy<br /></br>   • Definitive for Graves in non-pregnant adults, but absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal thyroid ablation and radiation exposure.  </p></br><p>d. External-beam radiation to the thyroid<br /></br>   • Not a treatment for Graves; used rarely for thyroid malignancies or orbital decompression, not diffuse hyperthyroidism.  </p></br><p>e. Thyroidectomy<br /></br>   • Acceptable in 2nd trimester if ATDs are contraindicated (allergy, agranulocytosis) or very large goiter/obstructive symptoms. More invasive than necessary for initial management.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Pregnant woman with overt hyperthyroidism → start an antithyroid drug (PTU early, methimazole later) and adjust to keep free T4 in the high-normal range.</p>
79. A 47-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the physician because of a 1-month history of indigestion after large meals and a 2-week history of poor exercise tolerance. She can no longer walk up one flight of stairs without becoming short of breath. She also has hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis that affects both knees. Her mother had a myocardial infarction at the age of 60 years. The patient’s medications are long-acting insulin, atorvastatin, enalapril, and 81-mg aspirin daily. She has never smoked cigarettes. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 82 kg (180 lb); BMI is 29 kg/m2. Examination shows no abnormalities. A resting ECG shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Measurement of serum cardiac enzyme activities</br>b. Exercise stress test</br>c. Adenosine nuclear stress test</br>d. Echocardiography</br>e. Cardiac catheterization</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>c. Adenosine nuclear stress test (pharmacologic myocardial perfusion imaging)</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – 47-year-old woman with diabetes, HTN, HLD and +FH → high-risk CAD profile<br /></br>  – “Indigestion” after meals + new exertional dyspnea = classic anginal equivalent in diabetics<br /></br>  – Can no longer climb one flight of stairs → markedly limited functional capacity<br /></br>  – Normal resting physical exam and ECG  </p></br><p>• Step-wise reasoning<br /></br>  1. She has stable, non–acute symptoms → do not check serial troponins.<br /></br>  2. Need to evaluate for obstructive coronary disease.<br /></br>  3. First choice is a stress test.<br /></br>  4. Exercise stress ECG requires the patient to reach 85% of age-predicted max HR; osteoarthritis and current dyspnea make that unlikely.<br /></br>  5. Therefore choose a pharmacologic stress modality (vasodilator or dobutamine).<br /></br>  6. Because baseline ECG is normal, either stress echo or nuclear perfusion imaging is acceptable; the stem offers the nuclear option.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Always ask two questions with chest-pain vignettes:<br /></br>A) Acute coronary syndrome? (if yes → enzymes/angiography)<br /></br>B) If stable, can the patient exercise adequately? If no → pick pharmacologic stress.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Who gets which stress test?</br>  – Low pretest probability → no test<br /></br>  – Intermediate probability &amp; can exercise → exercise ECG<br /></br>  – Intermediate probability &amp; cannot exercise or has baseline ECG abnormalities → pharmacologic stress with imaging (nuclear perfusion or stress echo)<br /></br>  – Very high probability or strongly positive stress → go straight to coronary angiography</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Limited ability to exercise + intermediate CAD probability → pharmacologic nuclear stress test.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic)<br /></br>• Characterize symptoms, functional class, risk factors, contraindications to vasodilator (COPD, AV block, caffeine), consent for test.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)</br>• Diabetes masks classic chest pain; autonomic neuropathy produces “silent” ischemia presenting as dyspnea, fatigue, or epigastric discomfort.<br /></br>• Adenosine causes coronary vasodilation; diseased vessels cannot dilate further → relative perfusion defect on radionuclide imaging.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot</br>• Positive perfusion defect → start high-intensity statin (already), optimize DM/HTN, consider β-blocker, refer for coronary angiography ± revascularization.<br /></br>• Negative test → continue risk-factor modification.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“DARE” for choosing stress modality<br /></br>D = Diabetes/Disabled →<br /></br>A = Adenosine (or other pharmacologic)<br /></br>R = Resting ECG normal<br /></br>E = Evaluate ischemia with nuclear or echo imaging</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Serum cardiac enzymes<br /></br>   – Rules out acute MI; her symptoms are chronic and stable, ECG is normal, no acute chest pain.<br /></br>b. Exercise stress test<br /></br>   – Requires adequate exercise capacity; she cannot even climb one flight of stairs (METS &lt; 4). Likely nondiagnostic.<br /></br>d. Echocardiography (resting)<br /></br>   – Good for structure/valves and EF, but not for detecting inducible ischemia unless combined with stress (stress echo).<br /></br>e. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   – Invasive and reserved when noninvasive test is positive or patient presents with high-risk/ACS features. She is intermediate risk with stable symptoms.</p></br><p>───────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Intermediate-risk, poor exercise tolerance, stable symptoms → choose a pharmacologic nuclear (adenosine) stress test to detect inducible myocardial ischemia.</p>
80. Seven days after he had a cerebral infarction, a 72-year-old man remains in the hospital and cannot be weaned from the ventilator. He is unable to eat or drink. He has a signed advance directive that requests withdrawal of all life support if he is unable to survive without it. Examination shows cortical blindness. The gag reflex is poor. There is expressive aphasia. The patient is able to respond to questions using nonverbal techniques. He can correctly identify the voices of family members. The physician asks the patient if he would like a tracheostomy and gastrostomy feeding tube, and the patient indicates “no” by squeezing the physician’s hand twice. The patient’s wife and son support his decision and request intravenous morphine to allow him “to die quickly.” Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Ask the patient to confirm his wishes to have the ventilator support removed</br>b. Continue ventilator support and obtain a court order to determine competency</br>c. Continue ventilator support until cardiac function ceases</br>d. Discontinue ventilator support and abide by the family’s wishes</br>e. Discontinue ventilator support and reconnect if adequate cardiac function continues beyond 1 hour</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Ask the patient to confirm his wishes to have the ventilator support removed  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key facts pointing to decision-making capacity<br /></br>   • Awake, follows commands (squeezes hand twice appropriately).<br /></br>   • Recognizes voices, understands questions, communicates reliably with agreed-on signals.<br /></br>   • No evidence of delirium or severe cognitive impairment.<br /></br>   ⇒ He retains decision-making capacity despite aphasia and blindness.<br /></br>2. Existing advance directive<br /></br>   • Explicitly requests withdrawal of all life-support if survival depends on it.<br /></br>   • Still need to verify patient’s CURRENT wishes because a competent patient always supersedes a prior directive.<br /></br>3. Family request for “IV morphine to allow him to die quickly”<br /></br>   • Indicates intent to hasten death → active euthanasia (illegal in almost all U.S. jurisdictions).<br /></br>   • Comfort-focused opioid dosing (palliative) is ethical, but using morphine to deliberately cause death is not.<br /></br>Cognitive approach for future questions:<br /></br>• Step 1: Is the patient competent?<br /></br>• Step 2: If yes → speak to patient first.<br /></br>• Step 3: If no → follow advance directive or surrogate.<br /></br>• Step 4: Distinguish palliative care (symptom relief) from euthanasia (intent to end life).  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Because he is competent, you must:<br /></br>1. Confirm he truly wishes to stop ventilation now.<br /></br>2. Explain consequences (likely rapid death).<br /></br>3. Provide standard palliative measures after withdrawal (e.g., morphine for dyspnea in proportionate doses).<br /></br>4. Document the discussion, patient’s signal system, and confirmation of wishes.<br /></br>Core takeaway: A competent patient’s autonomous, contemporaneous decision is paramount. Advance directives and family wishes are secondary.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>• Large cerebral infarct → cortical blindness, expressive aphasia, impaired swallow/gag → ventilator dependence.<br /></br>• No pathophysiology affects capacity; decisional capacity is a cognitive function independent of motor/sensory loss.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>• Withdrawal of mechanical ventilation in a competent patient = ethically and legally equivalent to withholding it.<br /></br>• Palliative care: low-dose IV morphine titrated to relieve air hunger; intent is symptom control, not death.<br /></br>• No court order needed if patient is competent.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“C-A-P” for end-of-life questions:<br /></br>C – Capacity?<br /></br>A – Advance directive?<br /></br>P – Present wishes supersede past documents/surrogates.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Court order for competency: Wrong—he is already shown competent.<br /></br>c. Continue ventilation until cardiac arrest: Ignores patient autonomy.<br /></br>d. Discontinue ventilation AND give morphine “to die quickly”: The withdrawal is ethical, but intentionally hastening death with lethal morphine is active euthanasia.<br /></br>e. Disconnect, reconnect if alive at 1 h: No ethical/legal basis; violates patient autonomy and is unnecessary.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Always verify a conscious patient’s current wishes before withdrawing life support. Administer comfort care proportionately, but do not provide medication with the primary intent to hasten death.</p>
81. A previously healthy 14-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 1-week history of a rash over his neck, back, and chest. It is not itchy or painful. He has had no other symptoms. A photograph of the lesions is shown. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Topical benzoyl peroxide</br>b. Topical clindamycin</br>c. Topical hydrocortisone</br>d. Topical mupirocin</br>e. Topical permethrin</br>f. Topical selenium sulfide</br>g. Oral griseofulvin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Topical selenium sulfide</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 14-year-old (adolescent → oily skin, sebaceous activity).<br /></br>  – “Rash over his neck, back, and chest” → classic truncal &amp; seborrheic distribution.<br /></br>  – “Not itchy or painful” → rules out scabies, eczema, allergic contact, etc.<br /></br>  – Photograph (NBME usually shows multiple hypo-/hyperpigmented macules with subtle scale).  </p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>  + Multiple macules/patches, fine scale, no inflammation → pityriasis (tinea) versicolor.<br /></br>  – No pruritus, no pustules → not acne, folliculitis, scabies, or dermatitis.<br /></br>  – No honey-colored crusts → not impetigo.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exams, a non-pruritic, variably pigmented rash on the upper trunk in an adolescent should instantly cue “tinea versicolor → selenium sulfide or topical azole.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Pattern recognition: chest/neck/back macules of different shades + fine scale → pityriasis versicolor.<br /></br>2. Etiology: Malassezia furfur, a lipophilic yeast on sebaceous skin.<br /></br>3. First-line therapy: topical selenium sulfide 2.5 % lotion/shampoo applied overnight x1 wk OR topical azole (ketoconazole, clotrimazole).<br /></br>4. Therefore, choose topical selenium sulfide.</p></br><p>Take-home: Pityriasis versicolor = Malassezia; treat with topical selenium sulfide.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record distribution, color change, KOH exam (“spaghetti &amp; meatballs”), treatment given, and patient education about recurrence.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Malassezia metabolizes skin lipids → produces acids that inhibit melanocyte function → hypo- or hyperpigmented spots.<br /></br>• Warm, humid environments, sweating, and adolescents’ increased sebum promote overgrowth.<br /></br>• KOH prep shows hyphae + budding yeast (“spaghetti and meatballs”).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• First-line:<br /></br>  – Topical selenium sulfide 2.5 % shampoo/lotion (leave on 10-15 min daily x 5–7 days).<br /></br>  – Alternative: topical ketoconazole, clotrimazole, terbinafine.<br /></br>• Refractory or widespread: single-dose oral fluconazole or itraconazole.<br /></br>• Labs rarely needed; KOH can confirm if diagnosis uncertain.<br /></br>• Recurrence common; monthly prophylactic selenium wash may be advised.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “Versicolor has VARI-colors; SELena (selenium) fixes the shades.”<br /></br>• Picture a bottle of SElENium shampoo pouring over a multi-colored T-shirt (trunk).<br /></br>• PPPP: Pityriasis (nonPruritic, Patchy Pigment) → Put on (selenium) Potion.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Topical benzoyl peroxide – Used for acne vulgaris; targets Cutibacterium; would show comedones/pustules.<br /></br>b. Topical clindamycin – Also acne/folliculitis; requires inflammatory papules/pustules.<br /></br>c. Topical hydrocortisone – For eczematous, pruritic, erythematous lesions; steroids worsen fungal infections.<br /></br>d. Topical mupirocin – Treats impetigo or MRSA colonization; would show honey-colored crusts.<br /></br>e. Topical permethrin – For scabies/lice; scabies is intensely pruritic and favors finger webs, wrists, belt line.<br /></br>g. Oral griseofulvin – Systemic therapy for dermatophyte infections (tinea capitis, onychomycosis); Malassezia is a yeast, and topical therapy suffices.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Core “Shelf” Takeaway<br /></br>Non-pruritic, variably pigmented, fine-scaling macules on the upper trunk in an adolescent = tinea versicolor → treat with topical selenium sulfide (or topical azole).</p>
82. A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 1 hour after he sustained a stab wound to his abdomen. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is alert and communicative and reports severe abdominal pain. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 130/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a 2-cm, jagged laceration over the anterior abdominal wall in the left lower quadrant. Wound exploration shows penetration of the underlying fascia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. CT scan of the abdomen with contrast</br>b. Gastrografin contrast study</br>c. Laparotomy</br>d. X-rays of the chest and abdomen</br>e. Observation only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Laparotomy  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Stab wound to his abdomen … 2-cm jagged laceration … exploration shows penetration of the underlying fascia.”<br /></br>  – Penetration through the anterior rectus fascia = definite violation of the peritoneal cavity is very likely.<br /></br>• Vital signs: pulse 130/min (tachycardia = early hypovolemia/hemorrhage), BP 110/70 (borderline), T 37.8 °C.<br /></br>• Symptom: “severe abdominal pain.”  In penetrating trauma, pain + fascial violation is treated as peritonitis until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For penetrating anterior abdominal trauma, ask 2 questions first:<br /></br>1. Did the injury clearly penetrate the fascia/peritoneum?<br /></br>2. Is there any evidence of peritonitis or hemodynamic instability?<br /></br>If the answer to either is “yes,” the NBME/USMLE answer is almost always “go to the OR (exploratory laparotomy).” Advanced imaging is reserved only when BOTH answers are “no.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. The knife reached the peritoneal cavity (penetration of fascia).<br /></br>2. Tachycardia suggests ongoing bleeding or visceral injury.<br /></br>3. Immediate life-threatening intra-abdominal injuries (e.g., bowel perforation, mesenteric or solid-organ vascular injury) must be ruled out and treated — best accomplished by emergent laparotomy.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Any penetrating abdominal injury with (a) fascial penetration plus either (b) peritonitis or (c) hemodynamic changes → mandatory exploratory laparotomy.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient: mechanism of injury, time of injury, vitals, focused abdominal exam (tenderness, guarding, rigidity), wound description, bedside FAST if done, and informed consent for emergent laparotomy.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Stab wounds can injure hollow viscera (small bowel, colon, stomach) → spillage of GI contents → chemical then bacterial peritonitis.<br /></br>• Solid-organ or mesenteric vessel laceration → hemorrhagic shock (tachycardia is an early sign).<br /></br>• Prompt surgical exploration allows direct control of hemorrhage, repair of visceral injuries, lavage, and placement of drains. Delay markedly increases risk of sepsis and death.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (Why NOT the others first?)<br /></br>• CT abdomen with IV contrast: Useful only if patient is hemodynamically stable AND there is no clear peritoneal violation or peritonitis. Here, fascial violation + tachycardia = go directly to OR.<br /></br>• Gastrografin study: Reserved for suspected esophageal perforation. Not applicable.<br /></br>• Chest/abdominal x-rays: May show free air, but waiting adds no therapeutic value when the indication for laparotomy is already present.<br /></br>• Observation only: Unsafe; risk of missed hollow-viscus injury and delayed sepsis/bleeding.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“PIP rule” for penetrating abdominal injuries:<br /></br>P – Peritoneal violation?<br /></br>I – Instability (hemodynamic) or<br /></br>P – Peritonitis present?<br /></br>If either “I” or second “P” = YES → OR (laparotomy).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. CT scan with contrast: Correct only if the patient were completely stable, no peritonitis, and unclear fascial penetration. Here, fascia is breached and vitals show tachycardia.<br /></br>b. Gastrografin contrast study: Evaluates esophagus, sometimes bowel leaks post-op. Irrelevant here.<br /></br>d. X-rays of chest/abdomen: May demonstrate pneumoperitoneum, but finding free air would still mandate laparotomy; waiting wastes time.<br /></br>e. Observation only: Appropriate for superficial abdominal wall injuries that do NOT penetrate fascia and in fully stable patients—criteria not met.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Penetrating abdominal trauma + fascial penetration + signs of instability/peritonitis = immediate exploratory laparotomy; imaging is for the truly stable, equivocal cases only.</p>
83. A 16-month-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because of a 2-day history of rash. He has not been scratching it. He has not had runny nose, watery eyes, cough, or change in appetite. There are no current known cases of measles in the community, and his family has not traveled recently. He was born at 39 weeks’ gestation following an uncomplicated pregnancy and delivery. He was breast-fed for the first 6 months. He received all recommended vaccines for the first 6 months of life. His mother declined to have him receive the varicella vaccine. He received the measles-mumps-rubella vaccine 10 days ago. Growth and development are appropriate for age. On examination, he is playful and alert. His temperature is 37.7 °C (99.9 °F). A photograph of the rash is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's rash?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Chickenpox</br>b. Erythema infectiosum (fifth disease)</br>c. Replication of a live vaccine virus strain</br>d. Roseola (exanthema subitum)</br>e. Scarlet fever</br>f. Syphilis</br>g. Wild-type measles virus infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Replication of a live vaccine virus strain  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>• Timing: Rash began 10 days after the measles-mumps-rubella (MMR) vaccination. Live-attenuated vaccines typically cause mild “mini-infection” reactions 7–14 days after administration.<br /></br>• Symptoms: Low-grade fever (≤100 °F), no coryza, cough, conjunctivitis, or Koplik spots. Child is playful and non-toxic. Those benign findings point away from true measles and toward a vaccine reaction.<br /></br>• Rash description (photo): Non-pruritic, faint, blanching, maculopapular lesions—characteristic of a post-MMR rash.<br /></br>Key metacognitive cue: Always check the immunization history and look for the classic 1–2-week window after live vaccines (MMR, varicella, intranasal influenza, yellow fever).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify recent live vaccine.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Is the presentation milder than the wild-type disease and occurring in the right time window?”<br /></br>3. If yes and there are no systemic ‘3 C’s’ of measles (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis), choose “vaccine virus replication.”<br /></br>Take-home message: Up to 5–15 % of children develop a mild maculopapular rash and low-grade fever 1–2 weeks after MMR. No treatment is necessary; the child is not contagious.  </p></br><p>Documentation note if real patient: “Benign, self-limited post-MMR vaccine rash; parents reassured; return precautions given.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• The live-attenuated measles strain replicates locally → transient viremia → T-cell–mediated immune response → mild morbilliform rash.<br /></br>• Because the virus is attenuated, systemic illness is minimal, and secondary transmission does not occur.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• No lab tests or antivirals needed. Reassurance and symptomatic care (e.g., acetaminophen).<br /></br>• Avoid unnecessary antibiotics or further isolation.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Quick Memory Aids<br /></br>“MMR → ‘Mini Measles Rash’ at 1–2 weeks.”<br /></br>Live vaccines Live in your body for “1–2 weeks,” often giving a “light” version of the disease.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Chickenpox: Vesicular, intensely pruritic lesions in multiple stages of healing; not a flat maculopapular rash.<br /></br>B. Erythema infectiosum (Parvovirus B19): “Slapped-cheek” facial erythema followed by lacy reticular body rash; absent here.<br /></br>D. Roseola (HHV-6): High fever for 3–5 days THEN rash as fever defervesces; this child never had high fever first.<br /></br>E. Scarlet fever: Sandpaper-like rash + pharyngitis, strawberry tongue, circumoral pallor; none present.<br /></br>F. Syphilis: Secondary syphilis causes diffuse maculopapular rash including palms/soles; extremely rare in toddlers and would require other risk factors.<br /></br>G. Wild-type measles virus infection: High fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots, and often community exposure—absent in this case.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A mild, self-limited morbilliform rash 7–14 days after MMR is due to replication of the live-attenuated vaccine strain, not to wild-type infection or another exanthem.</p>
84. A 12-year-old girl is brought to the office by her mother because of a 3-month history of painless lumps around her knees and ankles. They have not affected her activity. She has not had nausea, vomiting, fever, chills, or malaise. She has no history of serious illness. Immunizations are up-to-date. Growth and development are appropriate for age. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows aligned extremities. There are 10-mm and 5-mm masses palpable over the anterolateral aspect of the knees and ankles, respectively. Range of motion of the knees and ankles is full and painless. Neurovascular examination shows no abnormalities. X-rays of the knees and ankles show bony extensions corresponding to each palpated mass, appearing to originate at or near the physes. The bony extensions are well corticated with no sclerotic or lytic properties. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chondroblastoma</br>b. Ewing sarcoma</br>c. Langerhans cell histiocytosis</br>d. Osteochondroma</br>e. Osteoid osteoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Osteochondroma  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Age 12 yr → skeletal immaturity (open physes).<br /></br>• “Painless lumps … knees and ankles” present for months; activity &amp; ROM unaffected → benign, slow-growing process.<br /></br>• Masses “palpable over the anterolateral aspect” of long-bone metaphyses.<br /></br>• Plain films: “bony extensions … originate at or near the physes,” “well-corticated,” “no sclerotic or lytic properties.”<br /></br>• Multiplicity (knees + ankles) favors a developmental, not neoplastic or infectious, process.  </p></br><p>Cognitive pathway for exams<br /></br>1. Painless bone bump in a child → think osteochondroma first.<br /></br>2. Look at location: metaphysis near growth plate.<br /></br>3. Imaging buzzwords: pedunculated or sessile, cortex &amp; medulla continuous with parent bone, cortical “cap” described as well corticated.  </p></br><p>Pitfall to avoid: malignant bone tumors (Ewing, osteosarcoma) almost always hurt and show aggressive x-ray findings (periosteal reaction, lytic or sclerotic changes).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach<br /></br>1. Benign vs malignant: no pain, no systemic signs, benign-appearing cortex → benign.<br /></br>2. Benign metaphyseal lesion projecting away from epiphysis = osteochondroma.<br /></br>3. Multiple lesions? Could be hereditary multiple exostoses, but single-lesion or multiple both satisfy diagnosis osteochondroma/exostosis.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Osteochondroma = most common benign bone tumor in children; painless, pedunculated/sessile metaphyseal outgrowth continuous with cortex &amp; medulla; stops growing once physes close.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real patient)<br /></br>• Benign exam and imaging findings explained to family.<br /></br>• Education: monitor for growth, pain, or neurologic/vascular compromise; routine follow-up until skeletal maturity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: cartilage-capped exostosis arising from aberrant lateral growth of the physis.<br /></br>• Risk factors: sporadic or autosomal dominant EXT1/EXT2 mutation (multiple hereditary exostoses).<br /></br>• Natural history: enlarges with growth, usually asymptomatic; rare malignant degeneration to chondrosarcoma in adulthood (&lt;1%).<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: palpable hard mass near metaphysis, possible mechanical irritation or nerve/vascular impingement if large.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>• Diagnosis: plain radiograph usually diagnostic – shows stalk or broad base with cortical &amp; medullary continuity.<br /></br>• Treatment: none if asymptomatic. Excision indicated for:<br /></br>  – Pain, mechanical symptoms, deformity<br /></br>  – Lesion growth after skeletal maturity (concern for malignant transformation).<br /></br>• No routine labs needed; MRI only if neurovascular symptoms or unclear cartilage-cap thickness.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“Osteo-CHON-droma = Cartilage-capped HONking bump”:<br /></br>C – Cartilage cap<br /></br>H – Happens near pHysis (growth plate)<br /></br>O – Outgrowth continuous with cortex<br /></br>N – No pain / No systemic signs  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for aggressive bone lesions to rule out (“PLEaSe” = Painful, Lytic, Elevated periosteum, Systemic signs). Osteochondroma lacks these.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Chondroblastoma<br /></br>• Epiphyseal (not metaphyseal) lesion in adolescents, usually painful; x-ray shows lytic lesion with stippled calcifications, not a pedunculated cortical outgrowth.  </p></br><p>b. Ewing sarcoma<br /></br>• Malignant diaphyseal tumor, severe pain, fever, elevated ESR; x-ray with permeative lytic lesion &amp; onion-skin periosteal reaction—none seen here.  </p></br><p>c. Langerhans cell histiocytosis<br /></br>• Can cause multiple lytic “punched-out” lesions, often skull/ribs; may have skin rash, diabetes insipidus; x-ray lytic, not corticated exostosis.  </p></br><p>e. Osteoid osteoma<br /></br>• Small (&lt;1.5 cm) nidus in cortical diaphysis, causes nocturnal pain relieved by NSAIDs; radiolucent nidus with surrounding sclerosis, not a large metaphyseal exostosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A painless, well-corticated, metaphyseal outgrowth continuous with parent bone in a skeletally immature patient is classic for osteochondroma.</p>
85. A 32-year-old man with major depressive disorder has had the sensation of insects crawling under his skin for 2 weeks. He was initially treated for scabies in the emergency department. When he went to the dermatology clinic for a follow-up examination, he was told he had herpes zoster. He has continued to use acyclovir and the topical scabies preparation prescribed, but his symptoms have worsened, and he now sees tiny worms crawling out from his skin. He appears anxious but healthy. He has scattered excoriations over his face, head, abdomen, and upper and lower extremities. The lesions are punched-out sores on a clean base.</br>Toxicology screening is most likely to show which of the following?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acetaminophen</br>b. Methamphetamine</br>c. Opiates</br>d. Organophosphates</br>e. Paraquat</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Methamphetamine (an amphetamine-type stimulant)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Sensation of insects crawling under his skin … now sees tiny worms” → tactile hallucinations / formication.<br /></br>   • “Scattered excoriations … punched-out sores on a clean base” → skin-picking/‘meth mites.’<br /></br>   • Symptoms waxing and worsening despite appropriate dermatologic therapy → non-dermatologic etiology.<br /></br>   • Age 32, history of major depressive disorder (common comorbidity with substance use).<br /></br>   • Appears anxious but otherwise medically well.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Tactile hallucinations and excoriations.<br /></br>   – No cholinergic crisis, miosis, respiratory depression, liver failure, or pulmonary injury.<br /></br>   – No obvious exposure history to pesticides or herbicides.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Any “bugs crawling under the skin” on Shelf/Step is a flashing neon sign for stimulant (cocaine/amphetamines) intoxication.<br /></br>   • Always reconcile physical lesions—excoriations without primary rash— with self-induced scratching seen in stimulant users.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Recognize the classic symptom cluster (formication + excoriations).<br /></br>Step 2: Recall high-yield associations: cocaine and amphetamine intoxication. (Cocaine’s serum half-life is short; meth produces more prolonged symptoms matching a 2-week course.)<br /></br>Step 3: Choose the drug that will be detected on a routine urine toxicology screen → methamphetamine.  </p></br><p>Take-home: Stimulant-induced psychosis can mimic primary dermatologic or psychiatric disease; look for tactile hallucinations, insomnia, weight loss, bruxism, and skin-picking.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): Record substance-use history, psychiatric evaluation, and offer referral for addiction treatment.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Amphetamines increase synaptic dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin by promoting presynaptic release and blocking reuptake.<br /></br>• Dopamine excess in mesolimbic pathways → psychosis, hallucinations.<br /></br>• Sympathomimetic surge → anxiety, insomnia, tachycardia, hypertension.<br /></br>• Chronic use → neurotoxicity, severe dental caries (“meth mouth”), repetitive behaviors, skin-picking leading to excoriations with clean bases.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Management (high-yield recap)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Urine tox screen: positive for amphetamines for 1–3 days (longer with heavy use).  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Acute:<br /></br>   – Benzodiazepines for agitation.<br /></br>   – Haloperidol if severe psychosis.<br /></br>2. Chronic:<br /></br>   – Cognitive-behavioral therapy, contingency management.<br /></br>   – Treat comorbid psychiatric disorders; consider bupropion or naltrexone (investigational for stimulant use).<br /></br>   – Skin care, wound infection prophylaxis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“METH BUGS” mnemonic:<br /></br>M – Methamphetamine<br /></br>E – Excoriations<br /></br>T – Tactile hallucinations (formication)<br /></br>H – Hypervigilance / Hypertension<br /></br>B – Bruxism<br /></br>U – Underweight (weight loss)<br /></br>G – Grandiose / psychotic thoughts<br /></br>S – Sympathomimetic signs  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Acetaminophen – Overdose causes hepatic necrosis (↑AST/ALT, coagulopathy), not hallucinations; tox screen is serum APAP level, not routine urine screen.  </p></br><p>C. Opiates – Intoxication presents with miosis, respiratory depression; skin findings limited to track marks or pruritus, not formication.  </p></br><p>D. Organophosphates – Cholinesterase inhibitors → DUMBBELS (Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm, Bradycardia, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation). No tactile hallucinations.  </p></br><p>E. Paraquat – Herbicide; ingestion → pulmonary fibrosis and renal failure, not dermatologic hallucinosis.  </p></br><p>Key discriminators: only stimulants (meth/cocaine) produce the classic “bugs crawling” + self-induced skin lesions on exams.</p>
86. A 37-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 3, comes for a routine health maintenance examination. Physical examination shows a 3-cm, nontender, fluctuant mass involving the left posterior vulva underlying the mucosa of the vestibule and external to the hymenal ring. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Bartholin duct cyst</br>b. Episiotomy inclusion cyst</br>c. Mesonephric (wolffian) duct cyst</br>d. Sebaceous cyst</br>e. Vestibular gland cyst</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Bartholin duct cyst  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Location clues: “posterior vulva … underlying the mucosa of the vestibule and external to the hymenal ring” = the classic site of the Bartholin glands (4- and 8-o’clock positions of the vestibule).<br /></br>• Consistency: “nontender, fluctuant mass” → cyst (fluid-filled, soft).<br /></br>• Size: 3 cm—typical for an obstructed Bartholin duct; small inclusion/vestibular cysts are usually &lt;1 cm.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no pain, erythema, or fever, so it is a cyst, not an abscess.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>1. Identify anatomic landmark (vestibule vs labia majora vs lateral vagina).<br /></br>2. Decide solid vs cystic and tender vs nontender (cyst vs abscess).<br /></br>3. Match location to named structures (Bartholin posterior vestibule; Skene/paraurethral anterior vestibule; sebaceous on hair-bearing skin).  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Obstruction of a Bartholin duct → retained mucous → painless “bulge” in the posterior vestibule. A word catheter or marsupialization is offered if symptomatic or recurrent. Documentation would note location (4 or 8 o’clock vestibule), size, tenderness, discharge, and counsel on treatment options.<br /></br>Take-home: Any fluctuant mass at 4/8 o’clock of the vestibule in a reproductive-age woman is a Bartholin cyst until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Bartholin glands (greater vestibular glands) secrete mucous for vaginal lubrication.<br /></br>• Etiology: ductal occlusion from inflammation, trauma, or infection → mucous retention cyst.<br /></br>• If bacteria (E. coli, S. aureus, N. gonorrhoeae) seed the cyst, it becomes a painful abscess.  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Asymptomatic: no therapy or warm soaks.<br /></br>Symptomatic/recurrent:<br /></br>1. Word catheter placement (first-line).<br /></br>2. Marsupialization if recurrence.<br /></br>3. Culture &amp; antibiotics only if abscess or high STI risk.<br /></br>Imaging (pelvic MRI/CT) reserved for women &gt;40 y to exclude malignancy if mass is atypical.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Bartholin = Back Bottom of the vestibule (4 &amp; 8 o’clock).”<br /></br>“Word for the Bartholin word catheter.”  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Episiotomy inclusion cyst – Occurs along a prior episiotomy scar at the posterior fourchette/perineal body, not underneath vestibular mucosa lateral to hymen. Often firm, not fluctuant.<br /></br>c. Mesonephric (Wolffian) duct cyst – Found in the lateral vaginal wall (inside the vaginal canal), not external vestibule.<br /></br>d. Sebaceous cyst – Arises from hair-bearing skin (labia majora, mons pubis); covered by skin, not mucosa, and usually more anterior/lateral.<br /></br>e. Vestibular (minor mucous) gland cyst – Scattered small (&lt;1 cm) cysts anywhere in the vestibule, predominately anterior; rarely reach 3 cm.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only Bartholin glands produce a large, posterior, mucosa-covered, fluctuant mass.</p>
87. An asymptomatic 47-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She has no history of rheumatic fever. She takes no medications. Her pulse is 70/min, and blood pressure is 150/60 mm Hg. A grade 2/6 decrescendo murmur that begins after S2 is heard at the left sternal border. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Aortic valve insufficiency</br>b. Aortic valve stenosis</br>c. Mitral valve regurgitation</br>d. Mitral valve stenosis</br>e. Tricuspid valve regurgitation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Aortic valve insufficiency (aortic regurgitation)</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues and why they matter:<br /></br>1. “Grade 2/6 decrescendo murmur that begins after S2”<br /></br>   • A diastolic murmur (because it starts right after S2).<br /></br>   • “Decrescendo” is classic language for the blowing, early‐diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation (AR).  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>“Heard at the left sternal border”<br /></br>   • AR is best auscultated in the 3rd–4th left intercostal space with the patient sitting up, leaning forward, and at end-expiration.<br /></br>   • Aortic stenosis tends to be at the right upper sternal border; mitral lesions are loudest at the apex.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Blood pressure 150/60 mm Hg (wide pulse pressure)<br /></br>   • AR causes regurgitant flow back into the LV → ↑ stroke volume (↑ SBP) and rapid runoff into the LV during diastole (↓ DBP).<br /></br>   • This physiologic “water-hammer” finding is extremely high-yield for AR questions.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pertinent negatives helping rule out others:<br /></br>• No opening snap, rumbling quality, or rheumatic fever history → argues against mitral stenosis.<br /></br>• Murmur is diastolic, not holosystolic (mitral/tricuspid regurg) or systolic (aortic stenosis).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>For any diastolic murmur, immediately ask: Is it AR (early-diastolic decrescendo, LSB) or MS (mid-diastolic rumble with opening snap, apex)? Systolic vs diastolic is often enough to whittle choices to one or two answers.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Categorize murmur timing → diastolic.  </li></br><li>Localize best auscultation point → LSB.  </li></br><li>Characterize shape → early, high-pitched decrescendo “blow.”  </li></br><li>Correlate with hemodynamics → wide pulse pressure.<br /></br>Therefore: chronic aortic regurgitation.<br /></br>Key takeaway: The early-diastolic decrescendo murmur + wide pulse pressure = AR until proven otherwise.</li></br></ol></br><p>In real practice you would document: quality, timing, location, radiation, maneuvers, vitals (especially pulse pressure), and plan for transthoracic echocardiography.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Causes: congenital bicuspid valve, rheumatic disease, endocarditis, aortic root dilation (e.g., hypertension, Marfan, syphilitic aortitis).<br /></br>• Regurgitation during diastole → LV volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → widened pulse pressure, bounding pulses, head-bob (de Musset), nail bed pulsations (Quincke).<br /></br>• Eventually leads to LV dysfunction and HF if uncorrected.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Transthoracic echo (TTE): assess regurgitant volume, LV size/function, aortic root.<br /></br>• ECG and CXR may show LV enlargement but are not definitive.</p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>1. Medical (for chronic, asymptomatic, preserved EF):<br /></br>   • Afterload reduction → ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), ARB (losartan), or dihydropyridine CCB (nifedipine).<br /></br>   • Diuretics if volume overloaded.<br /></br>2. Surgical:<br /></br>   • Aortic valve replacement when symptomatic, LVEF ≤55%, or LV end-systolic dimension &gt;50 mm.<br /></br>   • Timing is critical—replace before irreversible LV dysfunction.</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“AR you OK?” mnemonic:<br /></br>A – Early diAstolic<br /></br>R – Regurgitant, decRescendo<br /></br>U – Wide pUlse pressure<br /></br>OK – best heard with patient leAning fOrward at end-expiration, Left sternal border.</p></br><p>“Diastolic = BAD” memory hook for test day: any diastolic murmur (AR, MS) usually indicates structural disease needing an echo and possible surgery (vs many innocent systolic murmurs).</p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Aortic valve stenosis<br /></br>   • Systolic, crescendo-decrescendo, right 2nd ICS, radiates to carotids; narrow (not wide) pulse pressure.  </p></br><p>C. Mitral valve regurgitation<br /></br>   • Holosystolic, blowing, loudest at apex, radiates to axilla; no isolated wide pulse pressure.  </p></br><p>D. Mitral valve stenosis<br /></br>   • Opening snap followed by mid-diastolic rumble at apex; often with rheumatic fever, malar flush; NOT decrescendo early-diastolic.  </p></br><p>E.
