Tricuspid valve regurgitation<br /></br>   • Holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign); typically in IV drug use, pulmonary HTN, or Ebstein anomaly.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only aortic regurgitation presents with an early-diastolic decrescendo murmur plus a wide pulse pressure.</p>
88. A 57-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of pain with urination, urinary frequency, and blood-tinged urine. She underwent hysterectomy for endometriosis at the age of 45 years. Current medications include estrogen replacement, digoxin, and warfarin. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F). Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Bleeding disorder secondary to warfarin</br>b. Endometriosis</br>c. Urinary tract infection</br>d. Urogenital cancer</br>e. Urolithiasis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>c. Urinary tract infection (acute uncomplicated cystitis)</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Key phrases that jump off the page</br>   • “2-week history of pain with urination, urinary frequency” = classic irritative voiding symptoms<br /></br>   • “blood-tinged urine” = hematuria can accompany cystitis because inflamed urothelium bleeds easily<br /></br>   • “temperature 36.7 °C (98 °F)” and “Examination shows no abnormalities” = afebrile, no CVA tenderness → favors lower (bladder) rather than upper tract disease<br /></br>   • Postmenopausal woman on estrogen replacement = typical demographic for uncomplicated cystitis<br /></br>2. Pertinent negatives</br>   • No flank/colicky pain → argues against nephrolithiasis<br /></br>   • No painless gross hematuria, weight loss, or systemic signs → argues against malignancy<br /></br>   • On warfarin, but dysuria &amp; frequency are NOT features of an anticoagulant bleed<br /></br>3. Test-taking angle</br>   • Cystitis = “dysuria + frequency ± hematuria, afebrile.”<br /></br>   • Anytime hematuria is accompanied by pain/burning/urgency, think infection first.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1: Identify symptom cluster – dysuria + frequency + hematuria<br /></br>Step 2: Localize – afebrile and no flank pain → lower urinary tract (bladder)<br /></br>Step 3: Apply epidemiology – postmenopausal female, very common for E. coli cystitis<br /></br>Take-home message: Hematuria with irritative voiding symptoms in an afebrile woman is cystitis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Etiology: Ascending infection, usually gram-negative enteric rods (E. coli &gt;80%).<br /></br>• Risk factors (postmenopause): ↓Estrogen → ↓Lactobacilli → ↑vaginal pH → easier colonization. Prior pelvic surgery does not protect.<br /></br>• Mucosal inflammation → micro- or gross hematuria.</p></br><p>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (real-world)</br>• UA: pyuria (≥5 WBC/high power field), positive leukocyte esterase ± nitrites, microscopic hematuria.<br /></br>• Urine culture if complicated or treatment failure.<br /></br>• First-line oral therapy:<br /></br>  – Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID × 5 days<br /></br>  – Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS PO BID × 3 days (if local resistance &lt;20%)<br /></br>  – Fosfomycin 3 g single dose<br /></br>• Encourage hydration; consider vaginal estrogen for recurrent post-menopausal UTIs.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“Dysuria + Frequency, No Fever = think Cystitis.”<br /></br>Mnemonic: F.U.N.<br /></br>F – Frequency<br /></br>U – Urgency / burning (dysuria)<br /></br>N – No Fever (→ lower tract)</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Bleeding disorder secondary to warfarin<br /></br>   • Warfarin can cause gross hematuria, but it would be painless and lack frequency/dysuria. INR would be elevated; question gives no bleeding elsewhere.</p></br><p>b. Endometriosis<br /></br>   • Requires functioning endometrial tissue and cyclical pelvic pain. She is post-hysterectomy, postmenopausal, and symptoms are voiding-related, not cyclical.</p></br><p>d. Urogenital cancer (e.g., bladder CA)<br /></br>   • Classic presentation = painless gross hematuria in an older smoker; irritative symptoms appear late. Cancer is a chronic, not 2-week, process.</p></br><p>e. Urolithiasis<br /></br>   • Presents with acute flank pain radiating to groin, hematuria; frequency/dysuria minimal unless stone at ureterovesical junction. Pain usually severe/colicky; absent here.</p></br><p>By contrasting the presence or absence of dysuria, frequency, pain type, and systemic signs, cystitis is the only choice that matches every detail in the stem.</p>
89. A 62-year-old woman is scheduled for discharge 3 days after admission to the hospital for a small infarction in the distribution of the left middle cerebral artery. Her presenting symptom of mild weakness of the right upper and lower extremities has gradually resolved during the past 3 days. On admission, echocardiography and ambulatory ECG monitoring showed no abnormalities. She has a 5-year history of hypertension treated with hydrochlorothiazide and atenolol. She has no other personal history of serious illness. She has never smoked. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiac examination shows a regular rhythm and no murmurs. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. In addition to aspirin therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy to prevent recurrent cerebral infarction in this patient?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Atorvastatin</br>b. Vitamin B6</br>c. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin)</br>d. Vitamin E</br>e. Warfarin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>Atorvastatin  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 62-year-old woman (ASCVD age group).<br /></br>• Small ischemic stroke in the left MCA territory.<br /></br>• No evidence of cardio-embolic source on echo or ambulatory ECG.<br /></br>• Already receiving antiplatelet therapy (aspirin).<br /></br>• Hypertension = major vascular risk factor.<br /></br>• Neurologically back to baseline → discharge planning = secondary prevention focus.</p></br><p>Step-wise reasoning most test-takers should follow:<br /></br>1. Classify the stroke subtype → non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke.<br /></br>2. Ask: “What therapies reduce recurrent stroke in this group?”<br /></br>   • Antiplatelet (aspirin, clopidogrel, or dipyridamole/aspirin) → already ordered.<br /></br>   • High-intensity statin regardless of baseline LDL (SPARCL trial outcome).<br /></br>   • Blood-pressure control, lifestyle, diabetes management.<br /></br>3. Screen other answer choices for proven benefit; select statin.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On USMLE/NBME, once atrial fibrillation or another cardio-embolic condition is excluded, warfarin usually disappears from the correct column; instead think “antiplatelet + statin” for secondary prevention of ischemic stroke/TIA.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why atorvastatin?<br /></br>• Any patient ≤75 y with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD)—includes ischemic stroke—should receive a high-intensity statin (ACC/AHA guidelines).<br /></br>• Statins lower LDL-C and exert plaque-stabilizing, anti-inflammatory, and antithrombotic effects, all of which reduce recurrent stroke/myocardial infarction and all-cause mortality.<br /></br>• SPARCL trial: Atorvastatin 80 mg daily after recent stroke/TIA reduced 5-year risk of recurrent stroke by ~16% and major coronary events by 35%.</p></br><p>Documentation items in the real world:<br /></br>• Start atorvastatin 40–80 mg nightly.<br /></br>• Counsel on lifestyle modification and follow-up lipid panel in 4–12 weeks.<br /></br>• Document absence of contraindications (e.g., active liver disease).</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke → secondary prevention = antiplatelet + high-intensity statin.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>Ischemic stroke pathways:<br /></br>1. Large-artery atherosclerosis (most common).<br /></br>2. Small-vessel (lacunar).<br /></br>3. Cardio-embolic.  </p></br><p>Statins:<br /></br>• Inhibit HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ hepatic cholesterol synthesis → ↑ LDL receptor expression → ↓ serum LDL.<br /></br>• Pleiotropic effects: improve endothelial function, reduce oxidative stress, decrease vascular inflammation, stabilize atherosclerotic plaque caps, and inhibit platelet aggregation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine)<br /></br>• Lowers homocysteine when deficient, but clinical trials have not shown reduction in cardiovascular or stroke recurrence. No guideline recommends its routine use.</p></br><p>c. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin)<br /></br>• Same rationale as B6; appropriate only if a documented B12 deficiency exists, not for secondary stroke prevention.</p></br><p>d. Vitamin E<br /></br>• Antioxidant hypothesis disproven; large RCTs showed no benefit and possible hemorrhagic stroke risk. Not recommended.</p></br><p>e. Warfarin<br /></br>• Indicated for cardio-embolic sources (atrial fibrillation, mechanical valve, left ventricular thrombus). Echo and ambulatory ECG were normal; thus risks (bleeding) outweigh benefits in non-cardioembolic stroke.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics Review (Exam Pearls)  </p></br><p>Secondary prevention after non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke:<br /></br>1. Antiplatelet: Aspirin 81–325 mg, or clopidogrel, or aspirin-dipyridamole.<br /></br>2. High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg).<br /></br>3. Blood pressure goal &lt;130/80 mm Hg (ACE inhibitor or thiazide often preferred).<br /></br>4. Diabetes control, smoking cessation, diet/exercise.<br /></br>5. Consider carotid imaging if clinical suspicion for extracranial carotid stenosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ASA + STATIN keeps the stroke from happenin’.”<br /></br>Visualize: patient walks out of hospital with two bottles—aspirin in one hand, atorvastatin in the other—forming the shape of an “A” (for Atherosclerosis). Everything else (vitamins, warfarin) drops to the floor—because evidence dropped them.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>After a non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke, evidence-based secondary prevention = antiplatelet + high-intensity statin. Atorvastatin is therefore the best pharmacologic addition for this patient.</p>
90. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of decreased energy, apathy, and difficulty sleeping since being fired from her job 3 weeks ago. Since then, she has awakened at 4 AM daily and has been unable to fall back asleep. She also has crying spells every morning. During the past 6 months, she has had a 3.2-kg (7-lb) weight loss. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Her father committed suicide at the age of 52 years. The patient is 170 cm (5 ft 7 in) tall and weighs 69 kg (152 lb); BMI is 24 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is sad and tearful. Her speech is soft and goal directed. She says that she has not thought about suicide and does not want to die. Laboratory findings, including a complete blood count, serum electrolyte concentrations, and serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration, are within the reference range. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Psychodynamic psychotherapy</br>b. Citalopram therapy</br>c. Lithium carbonate therapy</br>d. Zolpidem therapy</br>e. Electroconvulsive therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>b. Citalopram therapy (an SSRI)</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key positive clues<br /></br>  – Duration ≥2 weeks of depressed mood (sad, tearful, daily crying spells for 3 weeks)<br /></br>  – ≥5 SIGECAPS symptoms: Sleep disturbance (early-morning awakening), loss of Interest/pleasure (apathy), Guilt/worthlessness (fired, feels down), Energy ↓, Appetite/weight ↓ (7-lb loss), Psychomotor changes (soft, slow speech), Suicidal risk factors (family history)<br /></br>  – Total duration of some symptoms ≥6 months (weight loss), indicating a long-standing episode, not just transient job-loss sadness.<br /></br>  – No medical/thyroid explanation (normal labs) and no bipolar history.</p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No current suicidal ideation, intent, or plan<br /></br>  – No psychosis, catatonia, or refusal to eat<br /></br>  – No substance misuse or manic history</p></br><p>Pattern recognition: This is classic, non-psychotic, non-catatonic, moderate-severity Major Depressive Disorder (MDD).</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, run through SIGECAPS once you see “sad, tearful.” If ≥5 symptoms for ≥2 weeks and impacting function, think MDD → first-line = SSRI (or SNRI/bupropion/CBT). Adjustment disorder lasts &lt;6 months AFTER stressor and lacks full SIGECAPS count—here she already had weight loss before losing her job, ruling that out.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1: Confirm MDD criteria met (see above).<br /></br>Step 2: Assess safety—no active suicidality, no need for emergent ECT/hospitalization.<br /></br>Step 3: Choose first-line therapy. Evidence-based guidelines (APA, NICE, USPSTF) recommend an SSRI for moderate–severe episodes. Citalopram is an SSRI with favorable side-effect profile. Psychotherapy can be added, but pharmacotherapy alone is adequate and more evidence-based than psychodynamic therapy alone.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): PHQ-9 score, SIGECAPS review, suicide risk assessment, treatment plan (start SSRI, discuss SEs, follow-up in 2–4 weeks), offer psychotherapy referral.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>MDD is linked to ↓ monoamine (serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine) activity, HPA-axis hyperactivity, and neuroplasticity changes. SSRIs block presynaptic serotonin re-uptake, increasing synaptic 5-HT, gradually reversing neurochemical deficits.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review</br>First-line pharmacologic:<br /></br>• SSRIs – e.g., sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram, fluoxetine, paroxetine.<br /></br>Second-line / augmentation:<br /></br>• SNRIs, bupropion, mirtazapine, atypical antipsychotics, lithium, T3, ECT.<br /></br>Monitoring: Baseline weight, sleep, mood; follow-up in 2–4 weeks to assess response, titrate dose, monitor for suicidality (especially first 2 weeks).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“SIG E CAPS” = Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal thoughts.<br /></br>“SSRI for Sadness”: Anytime you count ≥5 SIGECAPS for ≥2 weeks, think “Start an SSRI unless contraindicated.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Psychodynamic psychotherapy<br /></br>   – Less evidence-based for acute symptom relief in MDD compared with CBT or SSRIs. Acceptable adjunct, but not as first-line monotherapy when full MDD criteria met on exams.</p></br><p>c. Lithium carbonate therapy<br /></br>   – Augmentation for treatment-resistant unipolar depression or mood stabilization in bipolar disorder. No history of mania/hypomania or prior antidepressant failure here.</p></br><p>d. Zolpidem therapy<br /></br>   – Non-benzodiazepine hypnotic for short-term insomnia. Would mask sleep symptom but leave core depressive pathology untreated. Not disease-modifying.</p></br><p>e. Electroconvulsive therapy<br /></br>   – Indicated for severe MDD with psychotic features, high suicide risk, pregnancy requiring rapid response, or failure of multiple medications. Patient is moderate severity, medically stable, and denies suicidality.</p></br><p>Take-Home Message</br>Moderate, non-psychotic MDD without active suicidality is managed first with an SSRI such as citalopram (plus optional psychotherapy).</p>
91. A case-control study is conducted to determine if the relationship between estrogen replacement therapy (ERT) and the risk for breast cancer is affected by estrogen formulation. Cases consist of 705 women with a history of breast cancer; controls consist of 692 randomly selected, age-matched women. All participants range in age from 50 to 74 years. Researchers record the following ERT data from participants: cumulative dosage, type of formulation, and the duration of treatment. Researchers also record patient height and weight, family history of breast cancer, and reproductive history. Results show that the type of estrogen formulation does not affect the risk for breast cancer. Which of the following raises the most concern regarding the validity of this conclusion?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Inadequate number of controls  </br>b. Inclusion of premenopausal women  </br>c. Patient recall bias  </br>d. Pharmacologic differences among estrogen products</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>C. Patient recall bias  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Study design: retrospective case-control (705 breast-cancer cases vs 692 age-matched controls).<br /></br>• Exposure data (ERT dose, formulation, duration) were obtained by “recording” what participants reported.<br /></br>• Key inference: “type of estrogen formulation does not affect risk.” For that statement to be valid, exposure classification must be equally accurate in cases and controls.<br /></br>• In case-control studies, any factor that makes cases remember or report an exposure differently from controls constitutes recall (information) bias. Women who developed breast cancer are more likely to scrutinize and over-report details of past hormone use; controls may under-report or forget. This differential misclassification can obscure a true association between a particular formulation and cancer risk or falsely create one.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime a case-control question tells you exposure history was “recorded” or “obtained by interview/questionnaire,” immediately think “recall bias” as a potential validity threat.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach to judging validity:<br /></br>1. Identify whether misclassification of exposure or outcome is possible.<br /></br>2. Ask: Is that misclassification differential between groups?<br /></br>3. If yes ➜ recall (cases vs controls) or interviewer (observer) bias.<br /></br>Because the outcome (breast cancer) is already known when exposure information is collected, differential exposure recall is highly likely. That is the single biggest threat to concluding there is “no difference” among estrogen formulations.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In case-control studies, differential recollection of past exposures (recall bias) is often the leading threat to validity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)<br /></br>• Estrogen exposure (especially unopposed estrogen) is a known risk factor for hormone-receptor-positive breast carcinoma.<br /></br>• Absolute risk may vary with formulation (conjugated equine estrogen vs estradiol, combined estrogen-progestin, transdermal vs oral) and with duration/dose.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics / Treatments (context)<br /></br>Not the focus of this epi question, but remember:<br /></br>• Women receiving ERT should undergo age-appropriate breast cancer screening (mammography).<br /></br>• Combined estrogen-progestin therapy mitigates endometrial cancer risk, but combined therapy is linked with a higher breast cancer risk than estrogen-alone in women with uterus removed.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“RECALL the exposure? Then recall bias!”<br /></br>Mnemonic: RETROSPECT = Recall Error Threatens Results Of SPECTrum (retrospective) studies.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Inadequate number of controls<br /></br>– 692 controls vs 705 cases is essentially a 1:1 ratio—adequate for power and standard in case-control design. No evidence this threatens validity more than recall bias.  </p></br><p>B. Inclusion of premenopausal women<br /></br>– Participants are 50–74 yr (overwhelmingly post-menopausal). Premenopausal inclusion is not an issue.  </p></br><p>D. Pharmacologic differences among estrogen products<br /></br>– Those differences are precisely what the study sought to evaluate (type of formulation). As long as each product is categorized correctly, differences do not threaten internal validity; they’re part of the research question. The bigger danger is misclassification due to faulty recall, not inherent pharmacologic variation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a retrospective case-control study that relies on patients’ memories for past hormone use, differential recall between women who have cancer (cases) and those who do not (controls) is the most significant threat to concluding that estrogen formulation does or does not influence breast cancer risk.</p>
92. Twenty-four hours after admission to the hospital for a head injury sustained in a motor vehicle collision, an unconscious 23-year-old man has increased urine output. His urine output was 40 mL/h to 60 mL/h during the first 10 hours after admission and has been 450 mL/h during the past 3 hours. On admission, CT scan of the head showed multiple fractures of the facial bones and base of the skull with a minor intracranial hemorrhage. He has no history of serious illness. He is receiving 0.9 percent saline (75 mL/h). His pulse is 96/min, and blood pressure is 103/68 mm Hg. There is no jugular venous distention when supine. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Heart sounds are normal. There is no peripheral edema. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum  </br> Na+  148 mEq/L  </br> K+  4.1 mEq/L  </br> Cl–  102 mEq/L  </br> HCO₃–  28 mEq/L  </br> Glucose 95 mg/dL  </br> Creatinine 1 mg/dL  </br>Urine osmolality 105 mOsmol/kg H₂O  </br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Insertion of a subdural monitor  </br>b. Administration of desmopressin  </br>c. Administration of furosemide  </br>d. Increasing the rate of administration of 0.9 percent saline to 450 mL/h, then administering furosemide  </br>e. Switching from 0.9 percent saline to 5 percent dextrose in water  </br>f. Cerebral decompression</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Administration of desmopressin (DDAVP)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key positive clues<br /></br>   • Severe polyuria, suddenly rising from 40–60 mL/h → 450 mL/h<br /></br>   • Very dilute urine (urine Osm 105 mOsm/kg; normal ≈ 300–900)<br /></br>   • Rising serum sodium (148 mEq/L) despite isotonic IV fluids<br /></br>   • Recent basilar skull fracture → posterior pituitary stalk easily torn  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key negative clues<br /></br>   • No pulmonary crackles, JVD, or peripheral edema → not volume overloaded<br /></br>   • Normal creatinine → kidneys can concentrate if given ADH<br /></br>   • No mention of mannitol, hyperglycemia, or diuretics → osmotic diuresis unlikely  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Head trauma + abrupt high-volume, low-osm urine + hypernatremia” = central diabetes insipidus (DI) from loss of antidiuretic hormone (ADH).</p></br><p>Approach for future questions:<br /></br>   • Always couple “head injury” with “look for DI vs SIADH.”<br /></br>   • Check urine Osm.  &lt;300 mOsm ⇒ DI; &gt;300 mOsm with hyponatremia ⇒ SIADH.<br /></br>   • Rising Na⁺ despite isotonic fluids = free-water loss = DI.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step thought process<br /></br>1. Identify free-water loss: High output, low urine Osm, hypernatremia.<br /></br>2. Localize lesion: Post-trauma → central (neurogenic) rather than nephrogenic DI.<br /></br>3. Immediate goal: Replace ADH to stop water loss and correct Na⁺ safely.<br /></br>4. Drug of choice: Desmopressin (synthetic ADH analogue); acts at renal V₂ receptors → ↑ water reabsorption in collecting ducts.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Post-traumatic central DI is treated acutely with desmopressin while monitoring serum Na⁺ and urine output.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Neurologic status, urine output trends, serum Na⁺ trend, rationale for DDAVP, plan for electrolyte monitoring q2–4 h.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Basilar skull or pituitary stalk injuries shear hypothalamic–posterior pituitary axons → acute ADH deficiency.<br /></br>• Absence of ADH → collecting ducts impermeable to water → inability to concentrate urine → massive dilute diuresis.<br /></br>• Free-water loss → hypernatremia, hypovolemia if untreated.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Immediate:<br /></br>• Desmopressin 1–2 µg IV or 10 µg intranasal; titrate to keep urine output &lt;200 mL/h and serum Na⁺ 135–145.<br /></br>• Concurrent isotonic or hypotonic fluids as needed for volume and Na⁺ correction (but only after DDAVP).<br /></br>Monitoring: q2–4 h urine output, serum Na⁺/Osm; daily weight.</p></br><p>Second-line/adjunct if partial response: Chlorpropamide, carbamazepine, or thiazides.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Head hits → Hypothalamus quits → High volume, Hypo-osm urine → Hit with DDAVP.”<br /></br>Or visualize a leaking water pipe (kidney) missing its faucet handle (ADH); fix with a synthetic handle (DDAVP).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Insertion of a subdural monitor<br /></br>   • For intracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring; no signs of rising ICP (bradycardia, hypertension, papilledema, unequal pupils). Does not address water loss.  </p></br><p>c. Administration of furosemide<br /></br>   • Loop diuretic would further increase free-water loss and worsen hypernatremia.  </p></br><p>d. Increase 0.9 % saline to 450 mL/h then furosemide<br /></br>   • Saline adds sodium; furosemide increases water loss → exacerbates hypernatremia; still does not treat ADH deficiency.  </p></br><p>e. Switch to 5 % dextrose in water (D5W)<br /></br>   • Gives free water, may temporarily lower Na⁺ but polyuria persists; Na⁺ will climb again without ADH replacement. Risk cerebral edema if ADH suddenly returns.  </p></br><p>f. Cerebral decompression<br /></br>   • Indicated for mass effect or refractory high ICP, not for isolated polyuria with normal vitals and neurologic baseline.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Traumatic central diabetes insipidus presents with abrupt, copious dilute urine and rising serum sodium. The definitive acute management is desmopressin.</p>
93. A 37-year-old man comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. He was diagnosed with HIV infection 3 weeks ago. He is asymptomatic and takes no medications. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 100/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities except for erythematous scaling at the hairline and the nasolabial folds. His CD4+ T-lymphocyte count is 160/mm³ (Normal≥500), and plasma HIV viral load is 25,000 copies/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis only</br>b. Three-drug antiretroviral therapy only</br>c. Three-drug antiretroviral therapy and P. jirovecii prophylaxis</br>d. Two-drug antiretroviral therapy only</br>e. Two-drug antiretroviral therapy and P. jirovecii prophylaxis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>C. Three-drug antiretroviral therapy and Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How to “see” it on test day)</br>• “Newly diagnosed HIV … asymptomatic … CD4 160/mm³” → Any CD4 &lt;200 automatically triggers PCP prophylaxis.<br /></br>• “Newly diagnosed” also means the patient is ART-naïve; current guidelines say EVERYONE gets ART, regardless of symptoms or CD4.<br /></br>• “Hairline/nasolabial erythematous scale” = seborrheic dermatitis (common but not management-changing).<br /></br>Key positives: CD4 160 (below 200), no meds yet.<br /></br>Key negatives: No history of sulfa allergy (so TMP-SMX is fine), CD4 &gt;50 (so no MAC prophylaxis yet).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: For every HIV stem, mentally checklist:<br /></br>1. Is ART indicated? (Always YES)<br /></br>2. Which OI prophylaxes are needed (CD4 &lt;200 PCP, &lt;100 Toxo, &lt;50 MAC).<br /></br>Do this before looking at answer choices.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-by-step logic:<br /></br>1. Initiate combination antiretroviral therapy (cART). Standard: 2 nucleoside/nucleotide reverse-transcriptase inhibitors (e.g., tenofovir-emtricitabine) + a third drug (usually an integrase inhibitor such as dolutegravir).<br /></br>2. Because CD4 &lt;200, start PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX 1 double-strength tablet daily.<br /></br>3. No need yet for toxoplasma (&lt;100) or MAC (&lt;50) prophylaxis.<br /></br>Core takeaway: All HIV pts get cART; add OI prophylaxis based on CD4 thresholds.</p></br><p>What would be documented: “Started tenofovir-disoproxil fumarate/emtricitabine + dolutegravir. Initiated PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX 1 DS tab PO daily.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Untreated HIV leads to progressive CD4 decline, predisposing to specific opportunistic infections (OIs) at predictable cutoffs.<br /></br>• PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii) is the most common life-threatening OI when CD4 &lt;200. TMP-SMX inhibits folate synthesis in the organism.<br /></br>• Early universal ART decreases viral load, restores CD4 counts, prevents OIs, and reduces transmission.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</br>First-line ART (one example):<br /></br>• Tenofovir DF + Emtricitabine (NRTIs)<br /></br>• Dolutegravir (INSTI)  </p></br><p>PCP prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS daily (alternatives: dapsone or atovaquone if sulfa allergy).<br /></br>No additional labs needed before starting prophylaxis unless G6PD deficiency suspected (dapsone). Baseline CMP/CBC before ART.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips</br>“2-1-0 Rule”<br /></br>2 NRTIs + 1 other drug = 0 excuses (everyone gets ART).<br /></br>CD4 cutoffs: “2-1-0” again<br /></br>&lt;2-00 PCP, &lt;1-00 Toxo, &lt;0-50 MAC.</p></br><p>Picture a traffic light:<br /></br>Green ≥200 (no OI ppx), Yellow 100-199 (TMP-SMX), Red &lt;50 (add azithro for MAC).</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. PCP prophylaxis only<br /></br>• Ignores universal ART recommendation—would allow unchecked viral replication.</p></br><p>B. Three-drug ART only<br /></br>• Correct ART, but fails to address immediate PCP risk at CD4 160.</p></br><p>D. Two-drug ART only<br /></br>• Dual therapy is reserved for specific maintenance scenarios (e.g., lamivudine + dolutegravir) after prolonged suppression; not for initial treatment and still lacks PCP ppx.</p></br><p>E. Two-drug ART + PCP prophylaxis<br /></br>• Covers PCP but initial dual therapy is sub-standard for naïve patients, risks resistance &amp; failure.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Thinking asymptomatic patients don’t need ART or that prophylaxis waits until symptoms appear—both are incorrect.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line</br>Start full three-drug ART in every newly diagnosed HIV patient and add TMP-SMX for PCP prophylaxis when CD4 &lt;200.</p>
94. A 3-day-old newborn is brought to the physician because of jaundice. The mother states that he was born at home. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 40/min, and palpable systolic blood pressure is 55 mm Hg. Examination shows bilateral eyelid swelling with copious purulent secretions. Administration of which of the following is most likely to have prevented this condition?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Erythromycin</br>b. Gentian violet</br>c. Hepatitis B immune globulin</br>d. Hepatitis B vaccine</br>e. Vitamin K</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Erythromycin ophthalmic ointment</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key clues<br /></br>• “3-day-old newborn … bilateral eyelid swelling with copious purulent secretions” → classic description of neonatal (ophthalmia neonatorum) conjunctivitis caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae.<br /></br>• Onset “3-day-old” (classically 2–5 days after delivery) distinguishes gonococcal from Chlamydia (5–14 days, watery → mucopurulent).<br /></br>• “Born at home” → likely did not receive standard ocular prophylaxis given in the delivery room.<br /></br>• Vital signs otherwise stable; jaundice is incidental and common in newborns, not the focus of this stem.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify time of symptom onset after birth.<br /></br>2. Map onset to most likely pathogens:<br /></br>   – &lt;24 h = chemical irritation (silver nitrate).<br /></br>   – 2–5 d = Neisseria gonorrhoeae (purulent, severe).<br /></br>   – 5–14 d = Chlamydia trachomatis (mucopurulent → pneumonia).<br /></br>3. Ask: what single intervention at birth prevents the 2–5 day form? → Topical macrolide (erythromycin 0.5% ointment).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>All newborns in the USA receive prophylactic 0.5% erythromycin ointment within 1 hour of birth to prevent gonococcal ophthalmia, which can rapidly ulcerate the cornea and cause blindness. Absence of prophylaxis (home birth) → infection appears on day 2–5.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Timing of neonatal conjunctivitis + topical erythromycin prophylaxis prevents gonococcal disease.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Erythromycin ophthalmic ointment 0.5% applied OU within 1 h of birth” and parental consent/refusal.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Neisseria gonorrhoeae acquired as the infant passes through an infected birth canal.<br /></br>• Bacteria adhere to conjunctival epithelium → intense neutrophilic response → purulent discharge, eyelid edema, risk of corneal perforation.<br /></br>• Topical macrolides do not reliably treat established infection but provide bactericidal levels immediately postpartum, preventing colonization.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>Prevention: Single application of erythromycin (or tetracycline, azithromycin) ointment at birth.<br /></br>If infection occurs:<br /></br>1. Hospitalize.<br /></br>2. IV/IM 3rd-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone).<br /></br>3. Saline eye irrigation.<br /></br>Diagnostic confirmation: Gram-negative intracellular diplococci on Gram stain, culture on Thayer-Martin agar.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>High-Yield Memory Tips<br /></br>• “GO N(eisseria) early” – Gonorrhea day 2-5, Onset early, Needs erythromycin Ointment.<br /></br>• Timeline mnemonic: “CHEM-GO-CHLAM”<br /></br>   – CHEMical (&lt;1 d)<br /></br>   – GOnorrhea (2–5 d)<br /></br>   – CHLAMydia (5–14 d)  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Gentian violet – Topical antifungal/antiseptic historically used for oropharyngeal candidiasis; not effective against gram-negative diplococci, not recommended for ocular prophylaxis.<br /></br>c. Hepatitis B immune globulin – Passive antibodies to prevent vertical HBV transmission; unrelated to eye infection timing or presentation.<br /></br>d. Hepatitis B vaccine – Active immunization against HBV; again, does not prevent ophthalmia neonatorum.<br /></br>e. Vitamin K – Prevents hemorrhagic disease of the newborn by repleting clotting factors II, VII, IX, X; no effect on conjunctivitis.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only erythromycin directly targets the organism responsible for purulent conjunctivitis at 2-5 days.</p>
95. A 25-year-old woman comes to the physician because of shortness of breath, marked malaise, weakness, and fatigue for 4 hours. She felt well until 24 hours ago when she developed muscle soreness after an unusually strenuous physical workout; she took ibuprofen for the pain 1 hour before her symptoms began. She has a history of frequent migraines that have been prevented by taking metoprolol (twice daily) over the past month. Her blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows facial flushing and mild periorbital edema. Multiple wheezes are heard throughout the lung fields. Avoidance of which of the following substances is most likely to prevent recurrence of a similar episode?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acetaminophen</br>b. Aspirin</br>c. Meperidine</br>d. Prednisone</br>e. Propoxyphene</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Aspirin  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Sudden‐onset dyspnea, flushing, periorbital edema, diffuse wheezing → suggest acute bronchospasm with mild angioedema (pseudo-allergic “asthmatic” reaction).<br /></br>   • Temporal relationship: Symptoms began 1 hour after she swallowed ibuprofen (a non-selective NSAID).<br /></br>   • Past health otherwise normal; no fever, urticaria, or signs of infection.<br /></br>   • Age &lt;30 and female → typical demographic for aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD).  </p></br><ol></br><li>Reasoning path<br /></br>   a. Ask: “What can ibuprofen do within 30–90 min that causes bronchospasm and flushing without IgE-mediated urticaria?”<br /></br>   b. Answer: Non-allergic COX-1 inhibition → ↓prostaglandin E2 (bronchodilator) and arachidonic-acid shunting → ↑cysteinyl-leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) → bronchoconstriction, nasal congestion, facial edema.<br /></br>   c. Cross-reactivity: Any other COX-1 inhibitor (eg, aspirin, naproxen, indomethacin) can reproduce the episode.<br /></br>   d. Preventive strategy: Strict avoidance of aspirin/other non-selective NSAIDs; acetaminophen (&lt;1 g) or COX-2–selective drugs are generally safe alternatives.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, whenever you see “acute wheeze/angioedema + NSAID within 3 h,” immediately think “aspirin/NSAID-intolerant asthma” and choose the option that removes aspirin/NSAIDs.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1. Identify trigger (ibuprofen).<br /></br>Step 2. Link trigger to mechanism (COX-1 inhibition → leukotrienes).<br /></br>Step 3. Generalize: Any drug that shares the same mechanism (aspirin) must be avoided.<br /></br>Step 4. Rule out other options that do not inhibit COX-1 or are actually treatments, not triggers.  </p></br><p>Take-home: COX-1 inhibitors can provoke pseudo-allergic bronchospasm; avoidance of aspirin class drugs prevents recurrence.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life):<br /></br>• “Suspected aspirin/NSAID-exacerbated respiratory disease; advised complete avoidance of aspirin, ibuprofen, naproxen, ketorolac, etc. Will prescribe acetaminophen for analgesia.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)<br /></br>• Not IgE mediated → skin testing negative.<br /></br>• Pathway: COX-1 blockade → ↓PGE2 → unchecked 5-lipoxygenase activity → ↑Cys-LTs → bronchoconstriction, vascular permeability (edema, flushing).<br /></br>• Classic triad (“Samter’s triad”): Asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis/nasal polyps, and NSAID sensitivity. A patient can present with only the NSAID reaction early.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Nuggets<br /></br>• Acute episode: Inhaled β-agonist ± systemic glucocorticoids.<br /></br>• Chronic management: Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast, zafirlukast) or 5-LO inhibitor (zileuton) can reduce reactions if NSAID absolutely required.<br /></br>• Definitive prevention: Avoid all non-selective NSAIDs; use celecoxib or low-dose acetaminophen instead.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“AAERD” mnemonic for Aspirin-Associated Edema, Respiratory Distress<br /></br>• A – Aspirin/NSAID intake →<br /></br>• A – Arachidonic-acid shunt →<br /></br>• E – Elevated leukotrienEs →<br /></br>• R – Respiratory wheeze/edema<br /></br>Visual: Picture a fire exit (COX pathway) blocked by aspirin; traffic diverts to a narrow alley (5-LO pathway) causing a jam (bronchospasm).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Acetaminophen – Weak COX-1 inhibitor; doses &lt;1 g rarely trigger AERD, so its use is generally safe and would NOT cause recurrence.<br /></br>c. Meperidine – Opioid analgesic; may release histamine but does not inhibit COX-1, so not implicated here.<br /></br>d. Prednisone – Glucocorticoid; treats, rather than provokes, bronchospasm.<br /></br>e. Propoxyphene – Opioid analgesic (withdrawn from US market); same reasoning as meperidine.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only aspirin (and other non-selective NSAIDs) share ibuprofen’s COX-1–blocking property.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</p>
96. A 6-month-old boy is brought for a follow-up examination because of chronic constipation since the age of 1 week. Current treatment with rectal stimulation, glycerin suppositories, and 4 ounces of prune juice produces one firm string-like stool every 4 days. He was breast-fed until 2 weeks of age and then switched to a cow’s milk-based formula. His current diet also includes cereals, fruits, and pureed vegetables. He has no vomiting but does occasionally spit up after meals. Growth and development are appropriate for age. Immunizations are up-to-date. Abdominal examination shows distention with no tenderness. Rectal examination shows no palpable stool in the ampulla. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Switch to a lactose-free formula</br>b. Test of the stool for botulism toxin</br>c. Stool culture</br>d. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>e. Rectal manometry</br>f. Upper gastrointestinal series</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Rectal manometry  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately raise concern for Hirschsprung disease (congenital aganglionic megacolon):<br /></br>• Onset during the first week of life – functional/behavioral constipation almost never starts this early.<br /></br>• Chronic, refractory constipation despite appropriate dietary fiber, prune juice, and stimulation.<br /></br>• Abdominal distention with an EMPTY rectal vault (“no palpable stool in the ampulla”). In Hirschsprung, stool backs up proximally and the distal rectum remains empty.<br /></br>• Normal growth and development – rules out many systemic/metabolic illnesses, keeps focus on a primary colonic motility problem.  </p></br><p>Approach to similar questions:<br /></br>1. Ask, “When did the constipation start?” Early neonatal onset = think Hirschsprung or cystic fibrosis meconium ileus (the latter presents with obstruction at birth).<br /></br>2. Examine the rectal vault description. An empty vault + distention = Hirschsprung until proven otherwise. A rectum full of stool = functional constipation.<br /></br>3. Decide which diagnostic modality is age-appropriate: contrast enema (&lt;1 mo) vs anorectal manometry (&gt;1 mo).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Goal: confirm suspected Hirschsprung disease.<br /></br>• Screening test of choice after 1 month of age = anorectal manometry to look for absence of the recto-anal inhibitory reflex (RAIR).<br /></br>• Positive manometry → rectal suction biopsy for definitive proof of absent ganglion cells.<br /></br>Take-home: In an infant older than 4–6 weeks with classic findings, proceed first to rectal manometry.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real-life): abdominal distention, frequency/character of stools, rectal exam findings, growth parameters, and plan for manometry ± surgical referral.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Hirschsprung)  </p></br><p>• Failure of neural crest cell migration → absence of Meissner &amp; Auerbach plexuses in distal bowel, most commonly rectosigmoid.<br /></br>• Aganglionic segment cannot relax, leading to functional obstruction; proximal colon dilates.<br /></br>• Presentation: delayed passage of meconium (&gt;48 h), chronic constipation, abdominal distention, explosive “squirt” of stool after rectal exam.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>   a. Anorectal manometry (&gt;1 mo) – screening<br /></br>   b. Contrast enema (&lt;1 mo) – screening/road‐mapping<br /></br>   c. Rectal suction biopsy – gold standard  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Management<br /></br>   • Definitive: Surgical resection of aganglionic segment with pull-through anastomosis (e.g., Soave procedure).<br /></br>   • Pre-op: Bowel clean-out, rectal irrigations, nutritional support.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MEGACOLON” mnemonic for Hirschsprung red flags:<br /></br>M – Meconium &gt;48 h<br /></br>E – Empty rectum on DRE<br /></br>G – Growing abdomen (distention)<br /></br>A – Aganglionosis<br /></br>C – Chronic constipation from birth<br /></br>O – Obstructive symptoms<br /></br>L – Lack of RAIR<br /></br>O – Onset neonatal<br /></br>N – Needs pull-through surgery  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Switch to a lactose-free formula<br /></br>   • Lactose intolerance causes watery diarrhea, not firm ribbon-like stools or distention from birth.  </p></br><p>b. Test stool for botulism toxin<br /></br>   • Infant botulism presents with constipation PLUS descending flaccid paralysis (poor suck, weak cry). Neurologic signs absent here.  </p></br><p>c. Stool culture<br /></br>   • Used for infectious diarrhea, not constipation.  </p></br><p>d. Serum TSH<br /></br>   • Congenital hypothyroidism can cause constipation but would also show hypotonia, macroglossia, prolonged jaundice; neonatal screen already done.  </p></br><p>f. Upper GI series<br /></br>   • Evaluates malrotation/volvulus (bilious vomiting), reflux, anatomic obstruction of upper GI tract—not indicated for isolated distal colonic obstruction pattern.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>An infant with constipation since the first week of life, abdominal distention, and an empty rectum should be evaluated for Hirschsprung disease. In a child older than one month, the next best step is rectal manometry to assess for absence of the recto-anal inhibitory reflex.</p>
97. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of progressive shortness of breath during the past 6 months. She now has to rest three or four times when climbing one flight of stairs. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 26 years. She has a chronic nonproductive cough and has wheezing controlled with an albuterol inhaler two to three times weekly. During the past year, her BMI has increased from 45 kg/m2 to 52 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 95/min, respirations are 24/min and shallow, and blood pressure is 140/90 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. The abdomen is distended. There is 1+ edema of the ankles. Arterial blood gas analysis on room air shows:</br>pH             7.37  </br>Pco2           48 mm Hg  </br>Po2            62 mm Hg  </br>O2 saturation  92 percent  </br></br>Her FEV1 is 75 percent of predicted, and total lung capacity is 50 percent of predicted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease  </br>b. Congestive heart failure  </br>c. Interstitial pneumonia  </br>d. Recurrent pulmonary embolism  </br>e. Restrictive lung disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Restrictive lung disease  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key positive clues<br /></br>   • Progressive dyspnea over months<br /></br>   • Morbid obesity (BMI rose from 45 → 52 kg/m²)<br /></br>   • Shallow respirations, hypoxemia (PaO₂ 62 mm Hg) and chronic, mild hypercapnia (PaCO₂ 48 mm Hg) → “obesity hypoventilation” picture<br /></br>   • Lungs clear, no crackles or wheezes at baseline → argues against obstructive disease or interstitial fibrosis<br /></br>   • Spirometry: FEV₁ 75 % predicted (near-normal) with a very low TLC 50 % predicted → classic restrictive pattern (↓ lung volumes with relatively preserved flow rates).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key negative clues (what is NOT present)<br /></br>   • No prolonged expiratory phase, daily bronchodilator use, or low FEV₁/FVC → makes COPD unlikely.<br /></br>   • No rales, S₃, elevated JVP, orthopnea → CHF less likely.<br /></br>   • No inspiratory crackles or radiographic fibrosis → interstitial pneumonia less likely.<br /></br>   • No pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, or acute episodes → recurrent PE unlikely.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, the fastest way to separate obstructive vs restrictive physiology is to look at TLC. Obstructive = normal or ↑ TLC; Restrictive = ↓ TLC. FEV₁ alone can mislead; flow/volume data clinches the diagnosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Classify physiology: Spirometry shows near-normal flows but markedly reduced volume → restrictive.  </li></br><li>Look for cause: In a patient with BMI &gt; 40 kg/m², extrinsic chest-wall restriction from morbid obesity is the single most common explanation (a subtype of restrictive lung disease often grouped under “obesity hypoventilation syndrome”).  </li></br><li>Corroborate with ABG: Chronic hypoventilation from restrictive mechanics leads to mild respiratory acidosis (PaCO₂ high-normal) with compensated pH (7.37) and hypoxemia—exactly what is shown.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: Any process that prevents the lungs from fully expanding—parenchymal fibrosis OR extrinsic causes such as obesity, kyphoscoliosis, or neuromuscular disease—produces low lung volumes with preserved or elevated FEV₁/FVC.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Note BMI, daytime hypercapnia, restrictive PFT pattern, and counsel on weight-loss interventions ± nocturnal non-invasive ventilation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Restrictive lung disease = “can’t get air in.”<br /></br>  – Intrinsic (parenchymal): interstitial fibrosis, sarcoid, pneumoconioses.<br /></br>  – Extrinsic (chest wall/neuromuscular): obesity, scoliosis, myasthenia gravis, ALS.<br /></br>• In obesity, excess adipose tissue over chest and abdomen increases pleural pressure, decreases compliance, and causes rapid, shallow breathing. Chronic alveolar hypoventilation → sustained CO₂ retention and secondary hypoxemia.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (high-yield bullets)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• PFT: ↓ TLC, ↓ FRC, ↓ VC, normal or ↑ FEV₁/FVC.<br /></br>• ABG: chronic hypercapnia with metabolic compensation (↑ HCO₃⁻).<br /></br>• CXR often normal; may show elevated diaphragm.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>1. Weight reduction (bariatric surgery is most effective durable therapy).<br /></br>2. Non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation (NIPPV) at night if hypercapnia or sleep-disordered breathing.<br /></br>3. Supplemental O₂ for resting hypoxemia (&lt; 55 mm Hg).<br /></br>4. Aggressive management of comorbid COPD/asthma if present.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“RESTRICT” mnemonic for restrictive pattern:<br /></br>R = Reduced volumes (TLC, VC)<br /></br>E = Elevated FEV₁/FVC<br /></br>S = Shallow, rapid breaths<br /></br>T = Thick (fibrotic) or Thick patient (obesity)<br /></br>R = Reduced compliance<br /></br>I = Increased elastic recoil<br /></br>C = CO₂ could rise<br /></br>T = Treat with weight loss/NIPPV if obesity  </p></br><p>Visual: Imagine trying to inflate a balloon inside a heavy backpack strapped tight around your chest—hard to expand (↓ TLC) but once the air gets out, airflow is fine (normal FEV₁).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)<br /></br>   • Would show ↓ FEV₁ and ↓ FEV₁/FVC (&lt;0.70) with normal/↑ TLC from air trapping.<br /></br>   • Lung exam usually has prolonged expiration and wheezes; ABG often shows chronic respiratory acidosis but with higher TLC.  </p></br><p>b. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>   • Dyspnea plus edema fits, but you would expect bibasilar crackles, S₃, elevated JVP, cardiomegaly on imaging. Lung volumes are typically normal.  </p></br><p>c. Interstitial pneumonia<br /></br>   • Restrictive volumes yes, but exam usually reveals fine “Velcro” crackles and imaging shows reticular/ground-glass infiltrates. Not suggested here.  </p></br><p>d. Recurrent pulmonary embolism<br /></br>   • Frequently presents with episodic pleuritic pain/hemoptysis, normal PFTs, low PaCO₂ (hyperventilation) rather than chronic hypercapnia.  </p></br><p>e. Restrictive lung disease<br /></br>   • Matches the low TLC with relatively preserved flow rates and the clinical context of morbid obesity—therefore correct.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Low TLC with near-normal flow rates = restrictive physiology. In a morbidly obese patient with chronic hypercapnia and clear lungs, think obesity-related restrictive lung disease (obesity hypoventilation syndrome).</p>
98. A 32-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 1, aborta 1, is brought to the emergency department at 28 weeks' gestation because of uterine contractions for the past 4 hours. Her first pregnancy ended in a spontaneous abortion at 16 weeks' gestation, and her second pregnancy resulted in a delivery at 28 weeks' gestation; the newborn initially had respiratory distress. This pregnancy has been progressing normally. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 28-week gestation. The cervix is partially effaced. Uterine contractions occur every 5 minutes. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Desmopressin</br>b. Ergonovine</br>c. Magnesium sulfate</br>d. Mifepristone</br>e. Misoprostol</br>f. Oxytocin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Magnesium sulfate  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• 28 weeks’ gestation – fetus clearly pre-term (&lt; 37 wk).<br /></br>• Uterine contractions q 5 min + “cervix is partially effaced” – meets definition of preterm labor (regular contractions + cervical change before 37 wk).<br /></br>• Past obstetric hx: prior 28-wk delivery with neonatal respiratory distress → strong RF for recurrent preterm birth.<br /></br>• Normal maternal vitals – infection (chorioamnionitis) unlikely; no indication for delivery.  </p></br><p>Thought process (metacognitive)<br /></br>1. Identify scenario: preterm labor.<br /></br>2. Ask: “What is my immediate goal?” → Temporize labor long enough to give corticosteroids and improve fetal outcome without harming mom.<br /></br>3. Pick the tocolytic/neuroprotective agent that is safe at &lt;32 wk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Magnesium sulfate serves two purposes at &lt;32 wk gestation:<br /></br>1. Tocolysis – Ca²⁺ antagonist at uterine smooth muscle → decreases myometrial contractility, buys 48 h for steroids.<br /></br>2. Fetal neuro-protection – proven to ↓ incidence of cerebral palsy and severe intraventricular hemorrhage when given before anticipated preterm birth.  </p></br><p>Therefore, MgSO₄ is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy right now. (Betamethasone for lung maturity would also be started, but it is not listed.)  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: In preterm labor &lt;32 wk, give magnesium sulfate (± nifedipine/terbutaline/indomethacin) to delay delivery and protect the fetal brain.  </p></br><p>What to document<br /></br>• Contraction pattern &amp; cervical exam confirming preterm labor<br /></br>• Informed consent for tocolysis and maternal side-effects<br /></br>• Plan for antenatal corticosteroids, MgSO₄ dosing, and fetal monitoring  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Preterm labor: activation of prostaglandin &amp; oxytocin pathways → cervical ripening &amp; contractions. Mg²⁺ competes with Ca²⁺ at voltage-gated channels → ↓ intracellular Ca²⁺ → uterine relaxation.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: prior preterm birth, short cervix, multiple gestation, infection, uterine anomalies.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatments &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First-line tocolytics by gestational age<br /></br>• &lt;32 wk: Magnesium sulfate (neuro-protection) ± indomethacin (if &lt;32 wk)<br /></br>• 32–34 wk: Nifedipine<br /></br>• β-agonists (terbutaline) seldom used due to side effects<br /></br>All patients:<br /></br>• Betamethasone 12 mg IM q24 h ×2 for lung maturity<br /></br>• Penicillin G if GBS status unknown<br /></br>• Rule out infection, abruption, fetal distress  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“MAG up the Brain before Week 32”<br /></br>MAG = Magnesium; up = protects; Brain = fetal neuro-protection; &lt;32 wk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Desmopressin – ADH analog; used for central diabetes insipidus &amp; von Willebrand disease, not uterine relaxation.  </p></br><p>b. Ergonovine – Ergot alkaloid causing intense uterine contraction; used for postpartum hemorrhage, would worsen preterm labor.  </p></br><p>d. Mifepristone – Progesterone receptor antagonist used with misoprostol to terminate pregnancy; contraindicated here.  </p></br><p>e. Misoprostol – PGE₁ analog promotes uterine contractions; used for medical abortion, cervical ripening, and postpartum hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>f. Oxytocin – Pituitary hormone that stimulates uterine contractions; used to induce or augment term labor and treat postpartum hemorrhage; contraindicated in preterm labor.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator: Only magnesium sulfate provides uterine relaxation and fetal neuro-protection in a &lt;32-week preterm labor scenario.</p>
99. A 21-year-old man comes to student health services because of anxiety and an inability to concentrate that began 6 weeks ago after he learned that his parents are getting a divorce. He says that he is not feeling like himself and that his academic performance has deteriorated. He has not had changes in sleep or appetite. He reports that he still enjoys sports and spending time with his girlfriend. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, he says that he is hopeful about his future and is looking forward to starting a job in a few months. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation or psychosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acute stress disorder</br>b. Adjustment disorder</br>c. Generalized anxiety disorder</br>d. Major depressive disorder</br>e. Parent-child relational problem</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Adjustment disorder  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key time line<br /></br>   • Symptoms began 6 weeks after learning of parents’ divorce<br /></br>   • DSM-5: Adjustment disorder starts within 3 months of an identifiable psychosocial stressor and lasts ≤6 months once the stressor or its consequences resolve.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Nature of stressor<br /></br>   • Parents’ divorce = a non-life-threatening, but significant, psychosocial stressor typical for adjustment disorder.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Symptom profile<br /></br>   • Anxiety, trouble concentrating, falling grades = emotional/behavioral reaction out of proportion to the stressor.<br /></br>   • No vegetative changes (sleep, appetite) and still enjoys activities → rules out major depressive disorder.<br /></br>   • Hopeful about future, no hopelessness or suicidality.<br /></br>   • No intrusive memories, flashbacks, or avoidance → rules out acute stress disorder/PTSD.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Functional impairment<br /></br>   • “Academic performance has deteriorated” satisfies the DSM requirement that the reaction causes impairment.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Test-taking tip: When you see sub-syndromic mood/anxiety symptoms that start soon after an identifiable stressor and cause functional decline, immediately think “adjustment disorder” unless the full criteria for another disorder are met.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify stressor → parents’ divorce.<br /></br>Step 2: Check timing → symptoms start within 3 months.<br /></br>Step 3: Look for full criteria of other disorders (MDD, GAD, ASD) → not met.<br /></br>Step 4: Note impairment → falling grades.<br /></br>Conclusion: Adjustment disorder.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Adjustment disorder = emotional or behavioral response to a non-life-threatening stressor within 3 months, causing distress/impairment but not meeting criteria for another disorder.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record the specific stressor, symptom type (e.g., “with anxiety”), degree of impairment, and that criteria for other mood/anxiety disorders are not met.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Thought to reflect maladaptive coping rather than a distinct biologic pathology.<br /></br>• Most common in adolescents/young adults facing academic, relational, or occupational stress.<br /></br>• Symptoms can be “with anxiety,” “with depressive mood,” “with mixed anxiety and depressed mood,” or “with disturbance of conduct.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line: Psychotherapy—especially cognitive-behavioral therapy focusing on coping strategies and problem-solving.<br /></br>Pharmacotherapy: Short-term anxiolytics (e.g., hydroxyzine, propranolol) or SSRIs if symptoms are severe or persistent, but usually not necessary.<br /></br>Diagnostics: No labs/imaging required; diagnosis is clinical.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “3-6 Rule”: Starts within 3 months, ends within 6 months after stressor resolves.<br /></br>• Mnemonic ADjust = Acute Distress after stressor.<br /></br>• Visualize a calendar: stressor arrow → 3-month window → “Adjustment box.” Anything beyond 6 months → consider GAD/MDD/PTSD.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Acute stress disorder<br /></br>   – Requires exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence plus intrusive re-experiencing, negative mood, dissociation, avoidance, arousal. Parents’ divorce is not a traumatic event in DSM-5 terms and symptoms are &gt;1 month.  </p></br><p>C. Generalized anxiety disorder<br /></br>   – Needs excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months plus ≥3 somatic symptoms (restlessness, fatigue, muscle tension, etc.). This patient’s anxiety is temporally linked to one stressor and duration &lt;6 months.  </p></br><p>D. Major depressive disorder<br /></br>   – Requires ≥5 of 9 SIGECAPS symptoms for ≥2 weeks, with either depressed mood or anhedonia. He has no depressed mood, maintains interest, normal sleep/appetite, and expresses hope.  </p></br><p>E. Parent-child relational problem<br /></br>   – DSM-5 “Other Conditions” code describing relational discord between parent and child; it is not a mental disorder diagnosis and does not explain his anxiety or functional impairment.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A young adult who develops sub-threshold anxiety/depressed mood within 3 months of a significant life change and whose functioning declines, but who lacks full criteria for another psychiatric disorder, has an adjustment disorder.</p>
100. A 72-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-month history of difficulty maintaining an erection. He has been married for 50 years, and he and his wife have not had any previous difficulty with sexual intercourse. He has a history of angina pectoris and underwent coronary angioplasty 6 years ago. He takes sublingual nitroglycerin as needed. He drinks two alcoholic beverages daily. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation with respect to this patient's sexual activity?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Abstain from alcohol</br>b. Continue sexual activity with his wife but do not have intercourse</br>c. Begin sildenafil therapy</br>d. Take nitroglycerin prior to sexual activity</br>e. No measures are indicated because he has normal age-related changes to sexual function</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Abstain from alcohol</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Sudden‐onset erectile dysfunction (ED) over 1 month rather than a chronic, gradually progressive problem → argue against “normal aging.”<br /></br>2. Takes sublingual nitroglycerin PRN → any phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE-5) inhibitor would be absolutely contraindicated.<br /></br>3. Drinks “two alcoholic beverages daily” → a correctable, common, reversible cause of ED (alcohol is a central nervous system depressant, impairs nitric-oxide–mediated vasodilation, and lowers testosterone with chronic use).<br /></br>4. Physical exam is normal → no clear endocrine, neurologic, or structural abnormality.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see new-onset ED, quickly screen for (A) medications/substances, (B) vascular risk, (C) psychogenic stressors. Fix the reversible factor before jumping to prescriptions.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Rule out normal aging: Abrupt change after decades of normal intercourse ≠ physiologic aging.<br /></br>Step 2 – Identify reversible contributors: Alcohol is the only modifiable risk factor presented that is not otherwise treated.<br /></br>Step 3 – Check for drug interactions: PDE-5 inhibitors + nitrates → life-threatening hypotension, so “start sildenafil” is off the table.<br /></br>Step 4 – Formulate lowest-risk intervention: Trial of alcohol cessation is safe, non-invasive, and addresses a plausible cause.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Always remove reversible lifestyle factors (EtOH, nicotine, stress, meds such as β-blockers or SSRIs) before pharmacologic therapy for ED—especially when nitrates make PDE-5 inhibitors dangerous.</p></br><p>Documentation points for a real encounter<br /></br>• Quantity/frequency of alcohol use and a recommendation for cessation.<br /></br>• Review of medications and contraindications (nitrates).<br /></br>• Counseling provided on ED and follow-up plan.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Ethanol acutely depresses sympathetic outflow and impairs smooth-muscle relaxation in the corpora cavernosa, reducing penile blood inflow.<br /></br>• Chronic use lowers serum testosterone and increases peripheral conversion to estrogen, worsening erectile quality.<br /></br>• Older men have endothelial dysfunction; alcohol amplifies that vascular insufficiency.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First‐line management for ED:<br /></br>1. Address lifestyle/psychogenic factors (smoking, alcohol, obesity, sleep, stress).<br /></br>2. Review offending drugs (thiazides, spironolactone, SSRIs, β-blockers).<br /></br>3. Only then consider PDE-5 inhibitors—unless the patient uses nitrates or riociguat.<br /></br>4. Vacuum erection device or intracavernosal alprostadil if PDE-5 inhibitors contraindicated.<br /></br>No extra labs or imaging needed unless suspicion of endocrinopathy (e.g., morning testosterone if decreased libido or testicular atrophy).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“NAILS” mnemonic for reversible ED causes:<br /></br>N – Nitrates (contraindicate sildenafil)<br /></br>A – Alcohol<br /></br>I – Iatrogenic (meds)<br /></br>L – Lifestyle (obesity, smoking)<br /></br>S – Stress/psychogenic  </p></br><p>Picture a “nail” hammered into penile blood flow—remove the NAILS first!</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Continue sexual activity but no intercourse – Does not correct the underlying problem; unnecessary restriction of intimacy.<br /></br>C. Begin sildenafil therapy – Contraindicated with any form of nitrate use (even PRN) → risk of profound hypotension and syncope.<br /></br>D. Take nitroglycerin prior to sexual activity – Worsens hypotension and does nothing for ED.<br /></br>E. No measures—normal aging – Normal aging causes gradual decline in rigidity &amp; latency, not sudden dysfunction after a lifetime of normal erections.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In older men with new ED, eliminate reversible contributors first. Alcohol reduction is the safest, guideline-concordant initial recommendation—especially when nitrate therapy rules out PDE-5 inhibitors.</p>
101. A 22-year-old man has a temperature of 39.5°C (103.1°F) 1 hour after initiation of a transfusion of packed red blood cells for a hemoglobin concentration of 7 g/dL. Yesterday, he underwent open reduction and internal fixation of a fractured femur sustained in a motor vehicle collision. He received prophylactic cefazolin prior to the operation and is now receiving enoxaparin. He is otherwise healthy and has no known allergies. The remainder of the vital signs is within normal limits. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Sputum and urine cultures</br>b. Venous duplex ultrasonography of the lower extremities</br>c. Ventilation-perfusion lung scans</br>d. Anticoagulant therapy</br>e. Empiric antibiotic therapy</br>f. Discontinuation of the blood transfusion</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>F. Discontinuation of the blood transfusion  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Timing: “1 hour after initiation of a transfusion” → temporally locks the fever to the blood product.  </li></br><li>Manifestation: Isolated temperature 39.5 °C; vitals otherwise normal; no rash, dyspnea, flank pain, hypotension, or oozing.  </li></br><li>Setting: Post-op day 1 after femur ORIF—plenty of alternative fever sources, but the sudden rise during infusion is the biggest clue.  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives: No rigors, hemoglobinuria, hypotension → makes catastrophic acute hemolytic reaction less likely yet still must be ruled out.  </li></br></ol></br><p>NBME/USMLE “pattern”: Any new symptom or sign during a transfusion = STOP THE TRANSFUSION FIRST. Everything else (labs, cultures, meds) comes second.</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions<br /></br>Step 1: Link symptom chronologically to transfusion.<br /></br>Step 2: Immediately halt transfusion (universal first move).<br /></br>Step 3: Then evaluate for acute hemolytic reaction (direct antiglobulin test, plasma free Hb, repeat cross-match) and treat supportively.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• The fever within 1–6 h suggests either:<br /></br>  – Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction (most common)<br /></br>  – Early acute hemolytic reaction (ABO incompatibility)<br /></br>  – Bacterial contamination (esp. platelet transfusion; less likely with PRBCs)  </p></br><p>• Because acute hemolytic reaction can be rapidly fatal, the safest and guideline-supported next step is to discontinue the transfusion immediately.  </p></br><p>Take-home core concept: “New fever/chills during transfusion? → Stop blood, keep IV line open with normal saline, send blood back to bank, draw labs.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Note exact time transfusion stopped, patient’s vitals, amount infused, and notify blood bank.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Febrile non-hemolytic reaction<br /></br>   • Cytokines released from donor leukocytes during storage or recipient anti-HLA antibodies act on donor WBCs.<br /></br>   • Presents with fever/chills within 1–6 h, no hemolysis.<br /></br>   • Prevention: Leukoreduced packed RBCs for future transfusions.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Acute hemolytic reaction<br /></br>   • Type II hypersensitivity (IgM anti-A/B) → complement activation → intravascular hemolysis.<br /></br>   • Triad: fever, flank pain, hemoglobinuria ± hypotension, DIC.<br /></br>   • Management after stopping: aggressive IV fluids, IV mannitol, treat DIC.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment After Stopping  </p></br><p>• Maintain IV access with NS.<br /></br>• Labs: Direct Coombs, plasma free Hb, bilirubin, haptoglobin, repeat type &amp; screen, cultures of patient and blood bag if sepsis suspected.<br /></br>• Antipyretics for febrile non-hemolytic reaction once hemolysis ruled out.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“STOP” mnemonic for any transfusion reaction:<br /></br>S – Stop transfusion<br /></br>T – Type &amp; screen / send blood back<br /></br>O – Observe vitals &amp; urine output<br /></br>P – Provide supportive care (fluids, antipyretics)  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a literal “STOP” sign popping out of the IV pole once the patient spikes a fever.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Sputum and urine cultures<br /></br>   • Post-op infection generally &gt;48 h; no respiratory or urinary symptoms. Cultures come after stopping blood if sepsis still suspected.  </p></br><p>b. Venous duplex ultrasonography<br /></br>   • DVT causes low-grade fever later in the hospital course, not an abrupt spike during transfusion.  </p></br><p>c. Ventilation–perfusion scan<br /></br>   • Pulmonary embolism rarely presents within 24 h of surgery and seldom &gt;39 °C fever. No dyspnea, tachypnea, or chest pain here.  </p></br><p>d. Anticoagulant therapy<br /></br>   • Already on enoxaparin prophylaxis; nothing points to thromboembolism.  </p></br><p>e. Empiric antibiotic therapy<br /></br>   • Indiscriminate antibiotics before identifying a source leads to overtreatment; first priority is to stop the potential offending blood product.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Key Exam Pearl  </p></br><p>Every USMLE/NBME transfusion question:<br /></br>If the patient develops ANY new symptom during transfusion → 1) STOP transfusion, 2) send blood to bank, 3) begin work-up. Everything else follows.</p>
102. A 62-year-old Asian man comes to the physician because of a 10-month history of a dark lesion on the nail of his right big toe. One year ago, he dropped something on the toe, but he is unsure if this accident caused the lesion. Initially, the lesion was a 0.5-cm, longitudinal, black streak near the side of the toenail. During the past 2 months, the lesion has enlarged and has spread to the nail fold. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. A photograph of the right big toe is shown. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acral lentiginous melanoma</br>b. Benign junctional melanocytic nevus</br>c. Cholesterol embolism</br>d. Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation</br>e. Subungual hematoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>A. Acral lentiginous melanoma</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key demographics   – 62-year-old, Asian ethnicity → acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is the most common melanoma subtype in darker-skinned and Asian patients.<br /></br>• Lesion description   – “Longitudinal, black streak” (longitudinal melanonychia) originating in the nail matrix.   – Progressive widening over 10 months with recent rapid growth and “spread to the nail fold” → Hutchinson sign (pigment on peri-ungual skin) = red flag for melanoma.<br /></br>• Trauma history   – Minor trauma a year ago can be a distractor; true subungual hematomas fade or grow out with the nail and do NOT keep widening months later.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives   – No systemic illness, normal exam elsewhere → local primary nail lesion.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step 2/ Shelf questions on nail pigmentation hinge on three cues: (1) longitudinal streak, (2) change over time, (3) extension onto the nail folds. If ALL are present, think melanoma.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Establish the pattern – Longitudinal melanonychia in an older, dark-skinned patient.<br /></br>2. Ask, “stable or changing?” → progressive enlargement.<br /></br>3. Look for Hutchinson sign → peri-ungual spread.<br /></br>4.
