These findings fulfill classic ALM criteria, so biopsy is mandatory.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Any changing, widening longitudinal melanonychia or pigment on the peri-ungual skin = melanoma until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): Describe color, width, borders, peri-ungual extension, and plan for nail-matrix punch or excisional biopsy.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>Acral lentiginous melanoma<br /></br>• Arises from melanocytes in palms, soles, and nail matrix.<br /></br>• Activating KIT mutations common (vs BRAF for superficial spreading).<br /></br>• Spreads laterally (radial phase) before vertical invasion; often diagnosed late → poorer prognosis.<br /></br>Risk factors: Dark skin, chronic mechanical stress, age &gt;50 yr; <em>not</em> UV exposure.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Diagnosis &amp; Management</br>• Diagnostic test: Full-thickness excisional biopsy of nail matrix ± partial nail plate removal.<br /></br>• Staging: Breslow depth, sentinel lymph node biopsy if &gt;1 mm thickness.<br /></br>• Treatment: Wide local excision; adjuvant immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab) or targeted therapy depending on mutation profile.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ALM affects the PALM”:<br /></br>Acral Lentiginous = Palms/soles/nails, Asians, Later-diagnosed, Melanoma.<br /></br>Hutchinson = “Hutch = house → pigment spills out of the nail house onto the skin.”</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. Benign junctional melanocytic nevus<br /></br>• Flat, uniform macule on skin, rarely arises under nails, typically stable. Would not widen or cross onto nail folds.</p></br><p>C. Cholesterol embolism (“blue toe syndrome”)<br /></br>• Sudden painful cyanotic toes after catheterization, livedo reticularis, renal failure. Not a chronic pigmented streak.</p></br><p>D. Postinflammatory hyperpigmentation<br /></br>• Diffuse or patchy brown macules at sites of healed dermatitis or trauma; does not present as a single longitudinal nail band or expand onto folds.</p></br><p>E. Subungual hematoma<br /></br>• Acute dark discoloration after trauma, painful initially. Pigment remains under nail plate and moves distally as nail grows (≈1 mm/week), resolving in weeks-months. No Hutchinson sign, no progressive widening after a year.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>Bottom Line</br>A progressive, widening longitudinal nail streak with peri-ungual pigmentation in an older Asian patient is classic for acral lentiginous melanoma; biopsy is essential.</p>
103. An 18-year-old primigravid woman at 39 weeks’ gestation is admitted to the hospital in labor. Contractions occur every 5 minutes and last 1 minute. Her first prenatal visit was 1 week ago. She was treated for syphilis 1 year ago. Her only current medication is a prenatal vitamin, which she started 1 week ago. She uses cocaine and heroin. She has had sexual intercourse with multiple male partners. They use condoms inconsistently. The patient appears well. Vital signs are within normal limits. On pelvic examination, the cervix is 4 cm dilated and 50 percent effaced. The uterus is consistent in size with a 39-week gestation. No abnormalities are noted. Laboratory studies show:</br>HBsAg                                positive</br>Anti-HBs                             negative</br>Rapid plasma reagin                  negative</br>Fluorescent treponemal antibody assay    positive</br>Herpes simplex virus antibody assay      positive IgG at 1:640</br>HIV ELISA                            positive</br>Vaginal testing for group B streptococcus and cervical testing for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are negative. Intrapartum pharmacotherapy should be administered to prevent which of the following in the newborn?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Cocaine withdrawal</br>b. Hepatitis B transmission</br>c. Herpes simplex virus transmission</br>d. HIV transmission</br>e. Syphilis transmission</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. HIV transmission  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • First prenatal visit only 1 week ago → no prior antenatal treatment.<br /></br>   • Positive HIV ELISA → she is infected.<br /></br>   • She is currently in active labor (4 cm/50 % with q5-min contractions).<br /></br>   • The stem specifically asks for “intrapartum pharmacotherapy,” i.e., a medication given to the mother while she is in labor.  </p></br><ol></br><li>Reasoning pattern<br /></br>   • Ask: “Which maternal infections require a drug started (or continued) DURING labor to cut neonatal transmission?”<br /></br>     – HIV → IV zidovudine (AZT) ± combination ART.<br /></br>     – HSV → not medication but cesarean if active lesions; suppressive acyclovir is started at 36 wks, not intrapartum.<br /></br>     – HBV → passive/active immunization is given to the infant immediately after birth, not drug to the mother in labor.<br /></br>     – Syphilis → penicillin is given antenatally, not during labor.<br /></br>   • Therefore the only infection in this vignette that meets the “give drug NOW” criterion is HIV.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Confirm infection present (positive HIV ELISA).<br /></br>2. Recognize absence of prior prenatal ART → high intrapartum transmission risk (≈25 %).<br /></br>3. Standard of care: start IV zidovudine as soon as possible in labor and continue to delivery; give infant oral zidovudine x 4–6 wk.<br /></br>4. This strategy drops transmission risk to &lt;2 %.<br /></br>Core takeaway: For an HIV-positive woman who presents in unplanned labor without documented viral suppression, immediate intrapartum AZT is mandatory to protect the neonate.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Vertical HIV transmission occurs transplacentally, during exposure to maternal blood/vaginal secretions at delivery, and via breast milk.<br /></br>• Zidovudine is a nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor that crosses the placenta and achieves fetal therapeutic levels, blocking viral replication during the critical peripartum period.  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearl<br /></br>• Intrapartum: IV zidovudine 2 mg/kg load over 1 h, then 1 mg/kg/h continuous infusion until cord clamping.<br /></br>• Newborn: oral zidovudine for 4–6 wk; add additional agents if maternal viral load unknown or high.<br /></br>• Delivery method: If maternal HIV RNA &gt;1000 copies/mL or unknown, schedule cesarean to further lower risk.<br /></br>• Avoid breastfeeding in resource-rich settings.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“AZT at Delivery” → A-Z-T reminds you of “After Ze Transition” (fetus → neonate).<br /></br>Quick table:<br /></br>H = HSV → acyclovir at 36 wk, C-section if lesions<br /></br>B = HBV → Baby gets HBIG + vaccine<br /></br>C = Chlamydia/Gonorrhea → Baby gets erythromycin ointment<br /></br>D = HIV → Drug (AZT) to mom in labor  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Cocaine withdrawal<br /></br>   • No proven intrapartum drug prevents neonatal abstinence from stimulants; management is supportive post-delivery.  </p></br><p>b. Hepatitis B transmission<br /></br>   • Prevention is passive–active immunization of the neonate within 12 h of birth (HBIG + first HBV vaccine dose). No intrapartum medication to the mother is required.  </p></br><p>c. Herpes simplex virus transmission<br /></br>   • Suppressive oral acyclovir is begun at 36 wk, not intrapartum. If active genital lesions are present at labor, the intervention is cesarean section, not medication in labor.  </p></br><p>e. Syphilis transmission<br /></br>   • Adequate maternal penicillin therapy must be completed ≥2 wk before delivery. Her negative RPR suggests prior successful treatment. No intrapartum therapy is indicated.  </p></br><p>Bottom line: The only intrapartum pharmacologic intervention in this scenario that meaningfully reduces neonatal infection is IV zidovudine to prevent HIV transmission.</p>
104. A 92-year-old man is admitted to the hospital because of a 24-hour history of severe abdominal pain and distention, nausea, and vomiting. One week ago, he was discharged from the hospital after repair of a left hip fracture. His postoperative course had been uncomplicated. He has hypertension and cataracts. Medications are hydrochlorothiazide, hydrocodone with acetaminophen, aspirin, and multivitamins. His temperature is 37.7°C (99.9°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 146/80 mm Hg. The abdomen is distended and diffusely tender. Bowel sounds are decreased. Rectal examination shows mucus, scant stool, and no masses. Test of the stool for occult blood is negative. An x-ray of the abdomen shows no free air under the diaphragm; there is marked colonic dilation from the cecum to the anus with stool and air-fluid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Carcinoid syndrome</br>b. Colon cancer</br>c. Colonic pseudo-obstruction</br>d. Fecal impaction</br>e. Irritable bowel syndrome</br>f. Ischemic colitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Colonic pseudo-obstruction (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction, “Ogilvie syndrome”)</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Patient profile<br /></br>   • Very elderly (92 y)<br /></br>   • Recent major surgery → prolonged bed rest, opioid analgesia<br /></br>   • Classic setting for autonomic dysfunction of the colon</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Presentation<br /></br>   • Rapid (24 h) onset of severe abdominal pain, progressive distention, nausea/vomiting<br /></br>   • Diffuse tenderness but no peritoneal signs → favors functional obstruction over perforation</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Physical exam/rectal exam<br /></br>   • Decreased bowel sounds (ileus-like)<br /></br>   • Rectum: only mucus &amp; scant stool, no hard fecal ball or tumor</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key imaging finding<br /></br>   • Upright abdominal film: “marked colonic dilation from the cecum to the anus”<br /></br>   • Entire large bowel is huge, not just proximal to a transition point.<br /></br>   • NO free air → no perforation; NO small-bowel predominance → not generalized ileus.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Pattern to remember:<br /></br>Elderly / postoperative + whole-colon dilation without a mechanical transition point = Ogilvie syndrome.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see the words “entire colon dilated to the rectum,” immediately think functional obstruction; a true mechanical obstruction would decompress distal to the lesion.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Ask, “Is there a transition point/mechanical cause?”<br /></br>– X-ray shows none, and rectal exam is negative for tumor or fecal ball.  </p></br><p>Step 2: Identify risk factors for autonomic imbalance of the colon.<br /></br>– Hip-fracture repair, immobilization, narcotics, electrolyte shifts, underlying chronic disease.  </p></br><p>Step 3: Link findings → acute colonic pseudo-obstruction.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Ogilvie syndrome is a functional paralysis of the colon (likely sympathetic overdrive / parasympathetic suppression) producing massive dilation that can lead to perforation if untreated.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): abdominal girth, serial abdominal exams, NG/rectal tube output, electrolyte panel, plan for neostigmine vs colonoscopic decompression.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Imbalance of autonomic innervation (↓parasympathetic, ↑sympathetic) most often in elderly, postoperative, severe medical illness, or narcotic use.<br /></br>• Result: Functional obstruction beginning at the splenic flexure—colon dilates from cecum distally.<br /></br>• Risk: Cecal perforation when diameter &gt; 12 cm or symptoms &gt; 6 days.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Management (high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Initial conservative therapy<br /></br>   • NPO, IV fluids, stop offending drugs (opioids, anticholinergics)<br /></br>   • NG tube + rectal tube for decompression<br /></br>   • Correct electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺)</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pharmacologic decompression<br /></br>   • IV neostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) if no response in 24–48 h; requires telemetry for bradycardia.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Endoscopic/operative<br /></br>   • Colonoscopic decompression → cecostomy tube if recurrent/persistent<br /></br>   • Emergent surgery if perforation or ischemia.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Mnemonic: “Old GI-LIVELY colon gets ‘Neostimulated’” – Old (elderly), GI (GI surgery), LIVELY (hip fracture immobility) → treat with Neostigmine.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Carcinoid syndrome<br /></br>   • Episodic flushing, wheezing, secretory diarrhea; not acute obstructive picture and x-ray not diagnostic.</p></br><p>b. Colon cancer<br /></br>   • Causes mechanical obstruction with a transition point; dilation proximal, collapsed distal colon/rectum. Rectal exam often reveals empty vault, not stool throughout.</p></br><p>d. Fecal impaction<br /></br>   • Rectal exam would reveal a hard fecal mass; x-ray shows distal fecal load with proximal dilation, not uniform cecum-to-rectum dilation.</p></br><p>e. Irritable bowel syndrome<br /></br>   • Chronic functional disorder with recurrent abdominal pain relieved by defecation; no acute severe distention or radiographic dilation.</p></br><p>f. Ischemic colitis<br /></br>   • Presents with crampy pain and bloody stools, often in watershed areas; CT shows “thumbprinting,” not massive dilation of entire colon.</p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“Ogilvie = Old GI leaves bowel lively but paralyzed.”<br /></br>• Old, post-op, whole-colon dilation<br /></br>• Treat with Neostigmine or colonoscopic decompression if conservative care fails.</p></br><p>Understand the pattern → you’ll nail every Ogilvie question on Step 2 &amp; the surgery shelf.</p>
105. A study is conducted to compare the efficacy of cholesterol-lowering medications X and Y. A total of 500 patients with a serum cholesterol concentration greater than 200 mg/dL are enrolled in the study. Three hundred patients are randomly assigned to receive medication X, and 200 patients are randomly assigned to receive medication Y. Serum cholesterol concentrations are measured for both groups after 6 months of therapy. Which of the following statistical tests will be most helpful in comparing mean serum cholesterol concentrations of both groups?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Analysis of variance</br>b. Chi-square test</br>c. Correlation coefficient</br>d. Mann-Whitney test</br>e. Regression analysis</br>f. Student unpaired t-test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Student unpaired t-test  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key words &amp; phrases<br /></br>   • “Compare the efficacy” → need to compare outcomes between groups.<br /></br>   • “Serum cholesterol concentration” → a quantitative (continuous) variable.<br /></br>   • “300 patients … medication X” vs “200 patients … medication Y” → two separate, independent groups.<br /></br>   • “Measured after 6 months” → one measurement per patient; not paired or repeated.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Pos: Two groups, continuous outcome.<br /></br>   • Neg: No mention of more than two groups (rules out ANOVA), not categorical outcome (rules out χ²), not a before-and-after design (rules out paired t-test).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When Step/NBME questions ask “Which test compares the means of a continuous variable between two independent samples?” immediately think “unpaired (two-sample) t-test.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Statistics)  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Identify type of data → numerical (cholesterol level).<br /></br>2. Identify number of groups → exactly two.<br /></br>3. Identify relationship between groups → independent, not matched.<br /></br>4. Select test → Student unpaired (two-sample) t-test compares the means of a continuous variable between two independent groups assuming approximately normal distribution.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Use an unpaired t-test for mean comparisons of a continuous variable between two unrelated samples.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (if this were a manuscript): “We used a two-tailed Student’s unpaired t-test to compare mean serum cholesterol concentrations between the X and Y groups. A p-value &lt;0.05 was considered statistically significant.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief, for context)  </p></br><p>• Hypercholesterolemia (total cholesterol &gt;200 mg/dL) is a major modifiable risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD).<br /></br>• Lipid-lowering medications reduce LDL-C and total cholesterol, lowering ASCVD events.<br /></br>• Clinical trials must compare average cholesterol reduction to decide which agent is superior.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Tests &amp; Analysis Review  </p></br><p>Student unpaired t-test requirements:<br /></br>1. Dependent variable: continuous, roughly normally distributed (cholesterol often is after log-transform or large n by CLT).<br /></br>2. Independent variable: dichotomous (drug X vs drug Y).<br /></br>3. Independence between observations.  </p></br><p>If normality is violated, the non-parametric alternative is the Mann-Whitney U test (option d).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Two groups, Two (t) test.”<br /></br>• Paired t-test → same patients (Before &amp; After).<br /></br>• Unpaired t-test → Unrelated groups.<br /></br>“ANOVA = 3+ groups ANOthVAriable levels.”<br /></br>“Chi-square checks Categories.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) – correct when comparing means of a continuous variable among three or more independent groups. Only two groups here.  </p></br><p>b. Chi-square test – compares proportions for categorical (nominal) data, not continuous means.  </p></br><p>c. Correlation coefficient – quantifies strength of association between two continuous variables within the same sample (e.g., cholesterol vs BMI), not comparison of two group means.  </p></br><p>d. Mann-Whitney test – non-parametric alternative for two independent samples when data are not normally distributed (compares medians/rank sums). If cholesterol values were heavily skewed or sample size small, this would be appropriate, but with large n and presumed normality, the t-test is preferred.  </p></br><p>e. Regression analysis – models relationship between one dependent variable and one or more independent predictors; appropriate for adjustment or prediction, not simple two-group mean comparison.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: For two independent groups and a continuous outcome, choose the Student unpaired t-test.</p>
106. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of recurring bifrontal headaches and vomiting. The headaches are not relieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She has not had visual changes or other neurologic symptoms. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is alert and fully oriented. She is 157 cm (5 ft 2 in) tall and weighs 104 kg (230 lb); BMI is 42 kg/m2. Her pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 114/72 mm Hg. Funduscopic examination findings are shown. Visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. A CT scan of the head shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. MRI of the brain</br>b. Oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor therapy</br>c. Intravenous corticosteroid therapy</br>d. Lumbar puncture</br>e. Cerebral angiography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Lumbar puncture  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Demographics – 27-year-old woman, obese (BMI 42)<br /></br>• Symptoms – 2-week history of daily, diffuse (bifrontal) headaches + vomiting; analgesics ineffective<br /></br>• Eye findings – photographed optic discs show blurred disc margins → papilledema<br /></br>• Normal visual acuity (20/20) and neurologic exam; no focal deficits<br /></br>• Neuroimaging – Non-contrast head CT is normal  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>“Obese woman of child-bearing age + headache + papilledema + normal neuroimaging” → think Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH, pseudotumor cerebri).  </p></br><p>Step-by-step approach (metacognitive)<br /></br>1. Suspect elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) because of papilledema &amp; vomiting.<br /></br>2. Rule out a space-occupying lesion with neuroimaging (already done → normal CT).<br /></br>3. Next indispensable diagnostic step-–measure opening pressure and analyze CSF → Lumbar puncture. LP is both:<br /></br>   • Diagnostic (opening pressure ≥ 250 mm H2O confirms IIH, CSF contents otherwise normal)<br /></br>   • Therapeutic (headache relief by removing 20-30 mL CSF).  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why LP now?<br /></br>• You cannot diagnose IIH without documenting elevated opening pressure.<br /></br>• Treatment (acetazolamide ± serial LP, optic nerve sheath fenestration, VP shunt) should not begin until diagnosis is confirmed.<br /></br>• MRI/MRV is ideal imaging, but an initial normal CT is considered adequate to rule out mass effect before LP on exams. (If CT were abnormal or venous thrombosis suspected, MRI/MRV would come first.)  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Suspect IIH in an obese young woman with headache &amp; papilledema; after negative imaging, perform a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure before starting therapy.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life):<br /></br>“Normal non-contrast head CT; plan diagnostic LP to obtain opening pressure and CSF analysis to evaluate suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>IIH = chronically elevated ICP with no structural lesion or hydrocephalus.<br /></br>Risk factors: female, 15-45 y, obesity, recent weight gain, vitamin A excess, tetracyclines, OCPs, pregnancy.<br /></br>Mechanism uncertain; likely reduced CSF absorption at arachnoid villi.<br /></br>Manifestations: pulsatile headache, transient visual obscurations, papilledema, horizontal diplopia (CN VI palsy). Vision usually preserved early but risk of permanent loss.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Neuroimaging (MRI ± MRV) first to exclude mass/venous sinus thrombosis.<br /></br>• LP: opening pressure ≥ 250 mm H2O, normal CSF composition.</p></br><p>Treatment (after diagnosis)<br /></br>1. First-line drug: Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor – Acetazolamide (↓ CSF production).<br /></br>2. Add/alternative: Topiramate (also helps weight loss), furosemide.<br /></br>3. Repeated therapeutic LPs for symptom relief.<br /></br>4. Surgical: Optic nerve sheath fenestration or ventriculoperitoneal shunt if vision threatened.<br /></br>5. Weight loss, stop offending drugs.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Fat Female with Full disks and Functional imaging → do a spinal Tap.”<br /></br>• Fat Female = obesity + young woman<br /></br>• Full disks = papilledema<br /></br>• Functional imaging = normal CT/MRI<br /></br>• Tap = LP for opening pressure</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. MRI of the brain<br /></br>• MRI (esp. with MR venography) is better than CT for subtle lesions or venous sinus thrombosis, but this patient already had brain imaging sufficient for exam purposes; next step is to measure ICP, not repeat imaging.</p></br><p>b. Oral carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide)<br /></br>• Appropriate first-line therapy AFTER IIH is confirmed by elevated opening pressure. Premature without diagnostic confirmation.</p></br><p>c. Intravenous corticosteroid therapy<br /></br>• Used short-term for cerebral edema (tumor, abscess) or giant cell arteritis–related vision loss, not for IIH.</p></br><p>e. Cerebral angiography<br /></br>• Invasive study for aneurysm, AVM, vasculitis; unnecessary when non-contrast CT is normal and clinical picture points to IIH.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In suspected idiopathic intracranial hypertension, once imaging excludes a mass lesion, the most appropriate next step is a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure and confirm the diagnosis before initiating treatment.</p>
107. A 22-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of right-sided chest pain. During the past 3 months, she has had temperatures to 38.1°C (100.5°F), marked fatigue, easy bruising, and photosensitivity of the skin, especially over her cheeks. She has had a 7-kg (15-lb) weight loss during this time. She has a 6-month history of aching of her hands and knees. This past winter, she noticed that her fingers became blue on exposure to cold weather and then would become painful and red when she returned indoors. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She has not traveled outside the USA. She is alert and fully oriented. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 92/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 110/60 mm Hg. A photograph of the face is shown. A low-pitched grating sound is heard over the right lateral lung field. The sound is louder on inspiration but is present during expiration. The sound stops when the patient holds her breath. Cardiac and abdominal examinations show no abnormalities. There is no peripheral edema, clubbing, or cyanosis. A chest x-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cefuroxime therapy</br>b. Chest tube thoracostomy</br>c. Ibuprofen therapy</br>d. Levofloxacin therapy</br>e. Pericardiocentesis</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: c. Ibuprofen therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “3-hour history of right-sided chest pain … louder on inspiration” → pleuritic chest pain.<br /></br>• “Low-pitched grating sound … present during inspiration and expiration … stops when the patient holds her breath” → classic pleural friction rub (it disappears when the visceral and parietal pleura are not sliding, i.e., when breath is held).<br /></br>• Constitutional and multisystem clues:<br /></br>  – Recurrent low-grade fever, photosensitive malar rash (picture), easy bruising (possible thrombocytopenia), non-erosive arthralgias of hands/knees, Raynaud phenomenon, weight loss → systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).<br /></br>• Chest radiograph normal → excludes pneumonia, effusion, pneumothorax.<br /></br>Cognitive approach: Once a rub that disappears on breath-holding is identified, localize pain to pleura rather than pericardium; in a young woman with classic SLE stigmata, think “lupus pleuritis,” which is treated first-line with NSAIDs.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify the rub: Disappears on breath-hold → pleural (not pericardial).<br /></br>2. Rule out structural lung disease: Normal CXR.<br /></br>3. Search for systemic disease causing serositis: SLE signs.<br /></br>4. Management of uncomplicated lupus pleuritis = NSAID (e.g., ibuprofen).<br /></br>Take-home: In SLE, the most common pulmonary manifestation is pleuritis; treat with NSAIDs ± glucocorticoids if refractory.<br /></br>Documentation note: record location/quality of pain, pleural rub characteristics, absence of effusion on imaging, and plan for NSAID trial with follow-up.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• SLE is an autoimmune, immune-complex–mediated disease causing inflammation of serosal surfaces (pleura, pericardium, peritoneum).<br /></br>• Pleuritic pain arises from inflamed parietal pleura; friction rub corresponds to inflamed pleural layers rubbing together.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600–800 mg q6-8h) relieve pain and suppress inflammation.<br /></br>Second-line if inadequate/contraindicated: oral glucocorticoids (e.g., prednisone).<br /></br>No antibiotics, chest tube, or pericardiocentesis needed without evidence of infection, pneumothorax, or pericardial tamponade.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Serositis in SLE = S^3”:<br /></br>• Serosal involvement (pleuritis, pericarditis, peritonitis)<br /></br>• Stops with Suspension of breathing → PLEURA<br /></br>• Start with Salicylates/NSAIDs  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Cefuroxime therapy (2nd-gen cephalosporin for CAP): Pneumonia would give fever, cough, infiltrate on CXR—not present.<br /></br>b. Chest tube thoracostomy: Used for large pneumothorax or empyema; CXR is normal, breath sounds present, no hyperresonance.<br /></br>d. Levofloxacin therapy: Also for CAP; same reasoning as (a).<br /></br>e. Pericardiocentesis: Pericardial rub persists when breath is held and pain is relieved by leaning forward; CXR may show enlarged cardiac silhouette if effusion present. Here rub disappears with breath-hold and CXR normal.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: Pleuritic chest pain + pleural friction rub in a patient with systemic lupus erythematosus = lupus pleuritis → treat with NSAIDs (ibuprofen).</p>
108. A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician 2 weeks after noticing a painless lump in her right breast. Mammography 6 months ago showed no abnormalities. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. A 1.5-cm, firm, irregular, nontender mass is palpated in the lower outer quadrant of the right breast. An ultrasonography-guided core biopsy specimen shows infiltrating ductal carcinoma. The patient is concerned about the accuracy of mammography and wishes to know its failure rate in diagnosing breast cancer. Which of the following is the most appropriate test characteristic to determine this answer?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Hazard ratio</br>b. Positive likelihood ratio</br>c. Positive predictive value</br>d. Sensitivity</br>e. Specificity</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Sensitivity  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clinical detail: The patient was screened 6 months ago with a “normal” mammogram yet now has proven infiltrating ductal carcinoma.<br /></br>• Question stem: “wishes to know [mammography’s] failure rate in diagnosing breast cancer.”<br /></br>  – “Failure” here means missing a cancer that is actually present (false-negative results).<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: Proven cancer despite a prior negative test → highlights false-negative concern.  </p></br><p>Cognitive &amp; Metacognitive Approach<br /></br>When a question asks about “missed cases,” “false negatives,” or “failure to detect disease,” think: 1 – Sensitivity (or “false-negative rate”). Sensitivity itself is the proportion of true-positives among all who truly have the disease, so 1 – sensitivity = false-negative rate.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Define what the patient is asking.<br /></br>   – “How often does mammography miss breast cancer?” = false-negative rate.<br /></br>Step 2: Match the statistical term that quantifies this.<br /></br>   – False-negative rate = 1 – Sensitivity, therefore sensitivity is the statistic we need.<br /></br>Take-home message: Sensitivity tells you how good a test is at catching disease when it’s actually there; its complement (1 – sensitivity) is the failure rate the patient cares about.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review (brief)<br /></br>Breast cancer arises when malignant epithelial cells in ducts/lobules proliferate. Screening tests like mammography aim to detect early disease, but factors such as breast density, lesion location, and tumor biology can create false-negative images, leading to the “interval cancers” seen here.  </p></br><p>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>• Screening mammography: Sensitivity ≈ 77–95 % (lower in dense breasts).<br /></br>• Negative imaging + palpable mass always warrants tissue diagnosis (core needle biopsy) because of potential false negatives.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“SIN = Sensitive tests INclude everyone with disease.”<br /></br>– Sensitivity rules IN disease (few false negatives).<br /></br>Therefore, failure = “SIN-cerely missed.” False-negative rate = 1 – Sensitivity.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Hazard ratio – Measures relative risk of an event over time in cohort studies; unrelated to test accuracy.<br /></br>b. Positive likelihood ratio (LR+) – Combines sensitivity &amp; specificity (LR+ = sensitivity ÷ [1 – specificity]) and tells how much a positive test shifts probability, but does not directly quantify missed cases.<br /></br>c. Positive predictive value – Probability that someone who tests positive truly has disease; depends heavily on prevalence and says nothing about missed cancers.<br /></br>e. Specificity – Proportion of true negatives among those without disease; relates to false-positive rate, not false-negative rate.  </p></br><p>Remember:<br /></br>• Ask “Is the concern about missing disease (FN) or falsely labeling disease (FP)?”<br /></br>   – Missing disease → Sensitivity.<br /></br>   – Falsely labeling → Specificity.</p>
109. A 63-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 1-week history of progressive shortness of breath on exertion and swelling of her legs. During this time, she has been using two pillows to sleep. Her pulse is 100/min, respirations are 24/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 93 percent. There is jugular venous distention. Bilateral basilar crackles are heard over the lower lungs. On cardiac examination, a grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur is heard over the apex; an S3 gallop is heard. There is 3+ pitting edema of the lower extremities. Echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 30 percent. In addition to furosemide, which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy at this time?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Hydralazine</br>b. Lisinopril</br>c. Metoprolol</br>d. Nifedipine</br>e. Spironolactone</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Lisinopril  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases pointing to a new-onset acute decompensated systolic heart failure (HFrEF):<br /></br>   • “1-week history of progressive shortness of breath,” “orthopnea (two pillows),” “leg swelling”<br /></br>   • Vital-sign pattern typical for mild pulmonary‐congestion (tachypnea, borderline O₂ sat)<br /></br>   • PE: JVD, basilar crackles, S3, holosystolic murmur (likely functional MR from LV dilation), 3+ edema<br /></br>   • Echo: EF 30 % (≤ 40 % = reduced)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives: Normal BP (not in cardiogenic shock), afebrile (not pneumonia), no wheezing (asthma/COPD less likely).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive Pearl: In NBME questions, once you confirm systolic HF, immediately think “mortality-reducing, neurohormonal blockade (ACE-I/ARB/ARNI) unless contraindicated,” then “add diuretic for symptoms.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise management of HFrEF:<br /></br>1. Rapid symptomatic relief → loop diuretic (furosemide) – already ordered.<br /></br>2. Disease-modifying therapy started as soon as patient is hemodynamically stable → ACE inhibitor (e.g., lisinopril). ACE-Is:<br /></br>   • Block RAAS, decrease afterload &amp; preload<br /></br>   • Slow ventricular remodeling → ↓ mortality, ↓ hospitalization<br /></br>   • First-line for all patients with EF ≤ 40 %, unless serum creatinine is markedly elevated or K⁺ &gt; 5.5 mEq/L.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Every stable patient with symptomatic HFrEF should leave the hospital on an ACE-I (or ARB/ARNI) plus a loop diuretic.  </p></br><p>Documentation items: EF 30 %, NYHA class III, start lisinopril 5 mg PO daily, monitor BMP 1–2 days after initiation.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Chronic systolic HF → ↑ sympathetic tone and RAAS → angiotensin II–mediated vasoconstriction and aldosterone-driven Na⁺/H₂O retention → progressive LV remodeling and worsening EF.<br /></br>• ACE-I interrupts this cascade, limits fibrosis, improves survival.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>First-line mortality benefit in HFrEF (mnemonic “ABS‐D”):<br /></br>A – ACE-I, ARB, or ARNI<br /></br>B – specific β-blockers (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol) AFTER euvolemia<br /></br>S – Spironolactone (EF ≤ 35 % &amp; Cr ≤ 2.5, K⁺ &lt; 5)<br /></br>D – SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin)  </p></br><p>Other add-ons: Hydralazine + nitrates (African-American NYHA III–IV or ACE-I intolerance), loop diuretic for volume, ICD/CRT when criteria met.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ACE the Base” – Always start an ACE inhibitor as the foundational drug once the patient is out of acute shock.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Hydralazine – Reduces afterload but inferior to ACE-I; reserved for patients who cannot take ACE-I/ARB (e.g., severe renal failure, hyperkalemia) or as race-specific add-on.  </p></br><p>c. Metoprolol – Proven mortality benefit, but DO NOT initiate in an actively decompensated or volume-overloaded patient; start after diuresis and stabilization.  </p></br><p>d. Nifedipine – Dihydropyridine CCBs have no mortality benefit in HFrEF and may worsen edema; non-DHP (verapamil, diltiazem) are contraindicated because of negative inotropy.  </p></br><p>e. Spironolactone – Mortality benefit in EF ≤ 35 %, but typically added after ACE-I/ARB and β-blocker. Requires baseline K⁺ ≤ 5 and Cr ≤ 2.5; not first agent to reach for on admission.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Jumping to start β-blocker or spironolactone before instituting (or in place of) an ACE inhibitor in a newly diagnosed, hemodynamically stable HFrEF patient.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Summary  </p></br><p>This patient with new systolic HF has already received a loop diuretic for congestion. The next essential, mortality-reducing step is an ACE inhibitor—lisinopril.</p>
110. A healthy 15-year-old girl is brought to the office because she has never had a menstrual period. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. She is 152 cm (5 ft) tall and weighs 48 kg (105 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 98/55 mm Hg. Breast and pubic hair development is Tanner stage 1. Pelvic examination shows normal-appearing external genitalia and a small uterus. Serum studies show a follicle-stimulating hormone concentration of 56 mIU/mL and luteinizing hormone concentration of 42 mIU/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Gonadotropin-releasing hormone stimulation test</br>b. Karyotype analysis</br>c. Measurement of serum cortisol concentration</br>d. Measurement of serum thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration</br>e. MRI of the pituitary fossa</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Karyotype analysis  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “15-year-old” with “never had a menstrual period”  ➜ primary amenorrhea.<br /></br>• Breast &amp; pubic hair Tanner stage 1 (no secondary sexual characteristics).<br /></br>• Small uterus on pelvic exam.<br /></br>• Markedly elevated FSH (56 mIU/mL) and LH (42 mIU/mL).  </p></br><p>How they narrow the differential<br /></br>1. Absent secondary sexual characteristics + amenorrhea ⇒ estrogen deficiency.<br /></br>2. HIGH gonadotropins ⇒ ovaries are not responding (primary ovarian insufficiency / gonadal dysgenesis).<br /></br>3. Small stature (152 cm) hints at Turner variants, but any form of gonadal dysgenesis (e.g., 45,X; 46,XY Swyer; mosaicisms) is possible.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>Step 1: Decide if the problem is central (low/normal FSH/LH) or peripheral (high FSH/LH).<br /></br>Step 2: In peripheral failure, rule out chromosomal causes because management (e.g., prophylactic gonadectomy if Y chromosome present) depends on karyotype.<br /></br>Step 3: Confirm the diagnosis with the single test that changes management the most: karyotype analysis.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why karyotype is the next step<br /></br>• Hypergonadotropic hypogonadism at age 15 is abnormal; &gt;50% of these cases are chromosomal.<br /></br>• Detects Turner syndrome (45,X) and other forms (46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis, mosaics).<br /></br>• Presence of Y-material mandates bilateral gonadectomy to prevent gonadoblastoma.<br /></br>Take-home: In primary amenorrhea with high FSH/LH, ALWAYS order a karyotype.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls in real practice<br /></br>• “Primary amenorrhea, elevated gonadotropins – suspect gonadal dysgenesis. Will obtain karyotype to guide counseling and need for prophylactic gonadectomy if Y chromosome identified.”  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Turner (45,X) or other gonadal dysgenesis → “streak” ovaries → absent estradiol → no breast development → loss of negative feedback → ↑↑FSH/LH.<br /></br>Short stature, webbed neck, coarctation, horseshoe kidney are classic for Turner, but milder or mosaic cases may present only with primary amenorrhea.<br /></br>46,XY complete gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer) presents the same clinically but requires gonadectomy.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>First test: Karyotype.<br /></br>If 45,X ➜ estrogen replacement + monitor for comorbidities (cardiac, renal, thyroid).<br /></br>If 46,XY ➜ laparoscopic bilateral gonadectomy + hormone replacement.<br /></br>Additional labs (TSH, prolactin) or imaging (pituitary MRI) are reserved for low/normal FSH/LH cases.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “High FSH? First Find the karyotype.”<br /></br>Visual: Picture a traffic light – Red (low FSH), Yellow (normal), Green (high). Green lights mean GO directly to chromosomes.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. GnRH stimulation test<br /></br>• Used when FSH/LH are LOW to differentiate hypothalamic vs pituitary defects. Here FSH/LH are already high; test adds nothing.  </p></br><p>C. Serum cortisol<br /></br>• Adrenal insufficiency can cause fatigue, weight loss, but not isolated elevated FSH/LH primary amenorrhea. No hyperpigmentation, hypotension, or electrolyte issues given.  </p></br><p>D. Serum TSH<br /></br>• Hypothyroidism can delay puberty but usually shows low/normal FSH/LH and systemic symptoms (weight gain, constipation). Elevated gonadotropins make thyroid disease unlikely; even if co-existent, it would not explain the findings.  </p></br><p>E. MRI of the pituitary fossa<br /></br>• Indicated in hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low FSH/LH) to look for adenoma or infiltrative disease. Hypergonadotropic pattern rules this out.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Primary amenorrhea + absent secondary sexual characteristics + high FSH/LH = gonadal dysgenesis → order a karyotype to identify Turner syndrome vs 46,XY dysgenesis and guide life-saving management.</p>
111. A 22-year-old woman is brought to the office by her parents because of a 9-month history of observing her eyebrows in mirrors for up to an hour at a time. She frequently stares at her eyebrows, sometimes separating the hairs with tweezers and examining the skin underneath. She never plucks them. Her behavior has caused her parents to have to remind her to eat meals and go to class. She has fallen behind in her schoolwork and has stopped calling her friends. She appears disheveled. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 57 kg (125 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. On mental status examination, she is alert and fully oriented. She is cooperative and articulate but makes poor eye contact. She describes her mood as "okay." Her affect is flat. Her thought process is linear except when discussing her eyebrows. During the examination, she sees her reflection in a mirror and stares at her eyebrows. She says she is "reading" the hair follicle and direction patterns; she is worried that they might change, which would have dire consequences for her and her family. She says the "reading" may take much time because she often loses track of her progress and must begin again. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Agoraphobia</br>b. Anorexia nervosa</br>c. Body dysmorphic disorder</br>d. Factitious disorder</br>e. Schizophrenia</br>f. Somatic symptom disorder</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Schizophrenia  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Time course: “9-month history” → exceeds the ≥ 6-month criterion for schizophrenia.<br /></br>2. Bizarre content: She believes she can “read” follicle direction patterns and that changes would have “dire consequences” for her family → fixed, non-culture-bound, delusional thinking (positive symptom).<br /></br>3. Repetitive mirror behavior is driven by the delusion, not by appearance dissatisfaction.<br /></br>4. Negative/functional decline: flat affect, poor eye contact, disheveled appearance, social withdrawal, academic failure → fulfills the “functional deterioration” requirement.<br /></br>5. No mood episode predominance, no substance use, no medical cause provided.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: For any psych question, first anchor on the timeline. Next, scan for psychosis (delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/behavior) and for negative symptoms. If ≥ 6 mo + decline in functioning → think schizophrenia-spectrum before OCD, BDD, etc.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify psychotic features: bizarre delusion about eyebrows.<br /></br>2. Check duration: &gt; 6 months.<br /></br>3. Assess impact: marked social/occupational dysfunction.<br /></br>4. Rule out mood or substance causes (not present).<br /></br>Conclusion → DSM-5 diagnosis: Schizophrenia.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Schizophrenia = ≥ 2 psychotic symptoms (at least one of delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech) for ≥ 6 months with functional decline; negative symptoms raise suspicion further.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: Record onset and course, specific psychotic content, negative symptoms, functional impact, absence of mood/substance/medical contributors, and safety assessment.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Dopamine hyperactivity in mesolimbic pathway → positive symptoms.<br /></br>• Dopamine hypoactivity in mesocortical pathway → negative symptoms &amp; cognitive deficits.<br /></br>• Risk factors: genetic loading, obstetric complications, cannabis use, urban upbringing.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Antipsychotics (first-line):<br /></br>   • Atypicals (e.g., risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole).<br /></br>   • Clozapine for treatment-resistant cases or suicidality.<br /></br>2. Psychosocial: CBT-p, social skills training, supported employment, family therapy.<br /></br>3. Monitor: BMI, lipids, glucose, EPS/AIMS, prolactin, QTc.<br /></br>4. Rule-out labs on first episode: CBC, CMP, TSH, B12/folate, RPR, urine tox, HIV, and brain imaging if focal neuro signs.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“6, 2, 1” mnemonic for schizophrenia:<br /></br>• 6 months total duration,<br /></br>• 2 core psychotic symptoms,<br /></br>• 1 must be delusion/hallucination/disorganized speech.  </p></br><p>Visual cue: Imagine a calendar with 6 big boxes; inside two of them are a speech bubble (disorganized speech) and an eye (hallucination) to lock the criteria.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Agoraphobia – Fear of being unable to escape certain situations; no psychotic features.<br /></br>B. Anorexia nervosa – Requires BMI &lt; 18.5 kg/m² or intense fear of weight gain; patient’s BMI = 20 and no weight-/body-image concern.<br /></br>C. Body dysmorphic disorder – Preoccupation with perceived physical flaw; here the concern is not cosmetic but a delusional magical belief. BDD patients try to hide or “fix” flaws; they rarely think the flaw controls destiny.<br /></br>D. Factitious disorder – Intentional production of illness for the “sick role.” No evidence of deception or fabrication.<br /></br>F. Somatic symptom disorder – Excessive anxiety about real physical symptoms; here the preoccupation is psychotic, not somatic, and no physical symptom is described.  </p></br><p>Common Pitfall: Confusing BDD or OCD-type checking with schizophrenia. Look for bizarre, fixed delusional explanations and negative symptoms to separate them.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Point<br /></br>Bizarre delusions + &gt; 6 months + functional decline = Schizophrenia.</p>
112. A 44-year-old woman comes to the office with her husband because of a 2-week history of an increasingly severe tremor in her right hand. She is right-handed. The tremor makes it difficult for her to brush her teeth or hold a coffee cup. She also has had a slowed gait during this time. She says she is afraid that she has Parkinson disease like her father. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She works as an executive at an advertising firm. Her husband says, “My wife is coping as best she can, especially considering how stressful her work is.” Neurologic examination of the patient shows a resting tremor of the right hand. The tremor increases in severity on finger-nose testing. The tremor decreases when she manipulates a coin in her left hand. Her gait is slow, but balance is intact. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. An MRI of the brain shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Body dysmorphic disorder</br>b. Conversion disorder</br>c. Delusional disorder</br>d. Illness anxiety disorder (hypochondriasis)</br>e. Malingering</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Conversion disorder (functional neurologic symptom disorder)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to conversion disorder:<br /></br>1. Acute‐subacute onset of a neurologic symptom (resting tremor, slowed gait) that cannot be explained by neurologic testing (normal MRI, intact balance).<br /></br>2. Symptom inconsistencies:<br /></br>   • Tremor worsens with finger–nose testing (an action tremor component that true Parkinson tremor usually improves with).<br /></br>   • Tremor markedly diminishes when attention is diverted (manipulating a coin with the other hand). Distraction abolishing the symptom is classic for a functional (psychogenic) tremor.<br /></br>3. Recent psychosocial stressor (“stressful work,” 2-week history). Conversion classically appears after stress or conflict.<br /></br>4. Lack of secondary gain or conscious feigning. Patient is worried, not seeking drugs, money, or time off work.<br /></br>5. Physical examination otherwise normal; neurologic disease ruled out by imaging.</p></br><p>Test-taking tip: If a dramatic neurologic sign (tremor, paralysis, seizure) is present but (a) exam or imaging is normal, (b) the symptom fluctuates with distraction or suggestion, and (c) there is a recent stressor, think conversion disorder.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify the primary complaint → new tremor and gait slowing.  </li></br><li>Evaluate for organic disease → normal MRI, balanced gait, inconsistent exam.  </li></br><li>Look for internal psychological factors → high work stress, father’s Parkinson disease (illness model in mind).  </li></br><li>Determine whether symptoms are intentionally produced → no evidence of feigning or external reward.  </li></br><li>Conclusion → functional neurologic symptom disorder (conversion).</li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: Conversion disorder presents with neurologic symptoms that are incompatible with recognized neurologic conditions, often fluctuate with attention, and are linked to psychological stressors.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): note detailed neurologic exam, negative imaging, description of distractibility of tremor, psychosocial stressor, and discussion regarding functional neurologic disorder diagnosis and follow-up plan.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Exact mechanism unknown; thought to involve subconscious conflict or stress manifesting through abnormal cortical–subcortical networks, producing genuine neurologic symptoms without structural lesions.<br /></br>• Symptoms are not “faked” (unconscious production) and therefore are ego-dystonic—patients truly experience them.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Work-up  </p></br><ol></br><li>First-line: Education and reassurance—explain that the symptoms are common and reversible.  </li></br><li>Physical therapy for motor symptoms.  </li></br><li>Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) addresses underlying stress and maladaptive illness beliefs.  </li></br><li>Treat comorbid anxiety/depression when present (e.g., SSRI such as sertraline).<br /></br>No further imaging or labs are needed once a thorough exam rules out organic disease.</li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Conversion = Conflict Converts to symptom.”<br /></br>Distractible tremor + recent stress = think functional.<br /></br>Mnemonic: D.I.S.T.R.A.C.T.<br /></br>• Distractible, Inconsistent, Sudden onset, Trigger (stress), Rule-out organic, Attention dependent, Conversion, Therapy = CBT/PT.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Body dysmorphic disorder – Preoccupation with perceived physical flaw (e.g., nose size), not neurologic symptoms. No incongruent neurologic findings here.  </p></br><p>c. Delusional disorder – Fixed false belief &gt;1 month (e.g., being poisoned). This patient has real tremor; no delusion.  </p></br><p>d. Illness anxiety disorder – Excessive worry about having/ acquiring a disease with minimal or no somatic symptoms. Here there is an overt neurologic symptom.  </p></br><p>e. Malingering – Intentional production of symptoms for external gain (e.g., disability benefits, avoiding work). She is an executive trying to function, no clear external incentive, and symptoms improve when distracted (in malingering patients usually guard consistency).  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing conversion with malingering or factitious disorder. Ask: “Is the patient consciously producing the symptom?” If no → conversion; if yes for external gain → malingering; if yes for internal psychological gain (“sick role”) → factitious.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A distractible tremor that began after stress, lacks objective neurologic findings, and is not consciously produced is classic for conversion disorder.</p>
113. A 4-month-old girl is admitted to the hospital because of a 7-day history of progressive cough. During the past 2 days, the cough has been associated with gagging, difficulty breathing, and perioral cyanosis. Between coughing episodes, she is alert and not in respiratory distress. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery. Her mother has a 3-week history of nonproductive cough treated with over-the-counter cough syrup. The patient receives no medications. She appears well nourished. Her temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 120/min, and respirations are 45/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. The lungs are clear to auscultation, and air movement is good. During an observed coughing episode, the patient’s lips become cyanotic. Pulse oximetry on room air during the episode shows an oxygen saturation of 85 percent. Which of the following is the most likely infectious agent?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adenovirus  </br>b. Bordetella pertussis  </br>c. Haemophilus influenzae  </br>d. Mycoplasma pneumoniae  </br>e. Parainfluenza virus  </br>f. Respiratory syncytial virus</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Bordetella pertussis  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point directly to classic infant pertussis (whooping cough):<br /></br>• Age: 4 months (pre-vaccine primary series is incomplete → high risk).<br /></br>• 7-day prodrome of progressive cough; now severe paroxysms with gagging and perioral cyanosis.<br /></br>• Child looks well between spells (“between coughing episodes, she is alert and not in respiratory distress”)—classic for pertussis.<br /></br>• Maternal “non-productive cough” for 3 weeks → common household source.<br /></br>• Normal lung exam and near-normal baseline SpO₂ (98 %) but large desaturation during paroxysm (85 %)—typical because hypoxia occurs only during the prolonged expiratory effort.<br /></br>• Low-grade fever ≤38 °C (pertussis often afebrile or minimally febrile).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition tip: An afebrile infant with repetitive staccato/paroxysmal cough + apnea/cyanosis but well in between should make you think “pertussis until proven otherwise.”  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>1. Identify age and immunization status.<br /></br>2. Note cough description &amp; pattern.<br /></br>3. Look for well appearance between spells.<br /></br>4. Ask “Who coughed first?”—an ill adult contact supports pertussis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice B is correct)  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Bordetella pertussis toxin produces lymphocytosis and paroxysmal cough by damaging respiratory epithelium and inhibiting the cough-inhibitory pathway.<br /></br>2. In infants, inspiratory “whoop” may be absent, but cyanosis, gagging, or apnea during coughing fits is common.<br /></br>3. Other listed pathogens cause continuous respiratory distress, wheeze, crackles, or significant fever—none match the “paroxysm + well interval” pattern.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Pertussis uniquely causes severe paroxysmal cough with fairly normal exam between episodes.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Immunization history &amp; potential exposure source.<br /></br>• Description of cough paroxysms, apnea/cyanosis.<br /></br>• Need for macrolide therapy and post-exposure prophylaxis of contacts.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Organism: Bordetella pertussis, a fastidious gram-negative coccobacillus.<br /></br>Virulence factors:<br /></br>• Pertussis toxin → ↑cAMP → lymphocytosis, impaired phagocyte function.<br /></br>• Tracheal cytotoxin → ciliated epithelial damage → impaired mucus clearance → cough.<br /></br>Disease phases:<br /></br>1. Catarrhal (1-2 wk): mild URI symptoms, most contagious.<br /></br>2. Paroxysmal (2-6 wk): intense coughing fits, posttussive emesis/apnea.<br /></br>3. Convalescent (wks-mos): gradual recovery.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• Nasopharyngeal PCR or culture (Regan–Lowe charcoal agar) during catarrhal/early paroxysmal phase.<br /></br>• CBC often shows marked lymphocytosis.  </p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>First-line: Macrolide (azithromycin, clarithromycin, or erythromycin).<br /></br>– Azithromycin preferred for infants &lt;1 mo (lowest risk of pyloric stenosis).<br /></br>Additional measures:<br /></br>• Hospitalize young infants for apnea monitoring.<br /></br>• Droplet isolation.<br /></br>• Post-exposure prophylaxis for all close contacts, regardless of vaccine status.  </p></br><p>Prevention: DTaP series at 2, 4, 6, 15-18 mo, 4-6 yr + Tdap booster at 11-12 yr and during each pregnancy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Paroxysms + Post-tussive Problems” = Pertussis<br /></br>• PPP: Paroxysmal cough, Post-tussive emesis/apnea, Periods of wellness.<br /></br>Picture: A “board” (Bordetella) walk that’s only open in bursts (fits) with a baby turning blue, then smiling between rides.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Adenovirus – Usually high fever, conjunctivitis, pharyngitis, or pneumonia; cough is not classically paroxysmal and patient is generally ill even between coughs.  </p></br><p>C. Haemophilus influenzae – Typical cause of epiglottitis or otitis; when pneumonia occurs it presents with higher fever and continuous respiratory distress, not isolated coughing fits with well intervals.  </p></br><p>D. Mycoplasma pneumoniae – School-age/teen pathogen; produces walking pneumonia with persistent dry cough, rales, and extrapulmonary findings (rash, hemolysis). Rare in 4-month-olds.  </p></br><p>E. Parainfluenza virus – Causes croup (laryngotracheitis) with inspiratory stridor and barking cough, often with fever; not paroxysmal cyanotic episodes in an otherwise well baby.  </p></br><p>F. Respiratory syncytial virus – Leading cause of bronchiolitis in infants: wheezing, crackles, tachypnea, diffuse lower airway obstruction; child is not well between episodes and exam is abnormal.  </p></br><p>Key differentiator for all incorrect choices: none cause the hallmark “severe coughing spells with normal exam and appearance between episodes” seen in pertussis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a young, incompletely immunized infant with episodic, violent cough leading to cyanosis but normal baseline status—especially with an adult household contact with chronic cough—think Bordetella pertussis.</p>
114. A metropolitan city has a higher than average rate of obesity-related disability compared with other cities of similar size. The city’s director of public health would like to prevent future obesity-related complications for this city’s residents. Which of the following is the most appropriate strategy for primary prevention of obesity in this population?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Allocate funding to improve the city’s bike lanes and walking trails</br>b. Hire additional diabetic educators</br>c. Measure BMI in all high school students and offer dietary counseling and exercise programs to obese students</br>d. Open a bariatric surgical center in the city hospital</br>e. Sponsor a citywide weight loss initiative and offer prizes for the most weight lost</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Allocate funding to improve the city’s bike lanes and walking trails  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• “Prevent future obesity-related complications” → the question is about prevention before obesity (and therefore its complications) develop.<br /></br>• “Most appropriate strategy for primary prevention” → forces you to pick an intervention that occurs before the disease (obesity) is established.<br /></br>• Population-level framing (“metropolitan city,” “director of public health”) → think community-wide, upstream interventions rather than individual clinical care.  </p></br><p>Exam approach<br /></br>1. Identify which level of prevention is being asked (primary, secondary, tertiary).<br /></br>2. Eliminate choices that target people who are already obese or already have complications.<br /></br>3. Prefer environmental or policy changes when the stem frames the problem at a community level.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Primary prevention = “stop the disease from starting.” For obesity this means:<br /></br>• Increase opportunities for physical activity.<br /></br>• Improve access to healthy foods.<br /></br>• Modify the built environment so that the healthy choice is the easy choice.  </p></br><p>Funding bike lanes and walking trails is an upstream, environmental change that makes physical activity part of daily life for the entire population, thereby reducing the incidence of obesity before it ever develops.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: On NBME/Step-2 questions, primary prevention of lifestyle-related conditions often equals broad policy or environmental modifications, not patient-specific treatments.  </p></br><p>What would be documented in real life?<br /></br>• Community needs assessment data showing high obesity rates.<br /></br>• Plan to allocate X dollars to infrastructure improvements with projected impact on physical activity metrics.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Obesity results from chronic positive energy balance.<br /></br>• The built environment influences energy expenditure—safe, convenient bike lanes/walking paths increase daily non-exercise activity thermogenesis (NEAT) and leisure-time exercise.<br /></br>• Earlier interruption of weight gain trajectories in childhood/adulthood prevents downstream insulin resistance, T2DM, CAD, OA, certain cancers, thus lowering disability rates.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment/Diagnostics Review (context)  </p></br><p>Not applicable—primary prevention occurs before need for medical diagnostics or pharmacologic/surgical therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Primary = PRE-empt”<br /></br>Picture a city map: draw bike lanes and walking trails BEFORE drawing any hospitals—this helps encode that infrastructure comes before disease care.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Hire additional diabetic educators<br /></br>• Tertiary prevention. Targets patients who already have diabetes (a complication of obesity). Does not prevent development of obesity itself.</p></br><p>c. Measure BMI in all high school students and offer dietary counseling/exercise programs to obese students<br /></br>• Secondary prevention (screening &amp; early intervention). You are identifying disease (obesity) that already exists.</p></br><p>d. Open a bariatric surgical center<br /></br>• Tertiary prevention—treats established, often severe obesity to reduce complications.</p></br><p>e. Sponsor a citywide weight-loss competition with prizes<br /></br>• Primarily reaches individuals who are already overweight/obese and focuses on treatment rather than prevention; mixed secondary/tertiary strategy, not primary.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: equating “population-based program” with primary prevention. A program can be citywide yet still be secondary or tertiary if it targets disease that is already present. Always ask: “Does the intervention occur before the first pathologic change develops?”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Primary prevention of obesity at the community level = change the environment so people naturally move more and gain less weight—e.g., fund bike lanes and walking trails.</p>
115. A 57-year-old man with hypertension comes to the office for a routine examination. He feels well. Four years ago, he had basal cell carcinoma on his nose treated with excision. Colonoscopy 5 years ago showed a tubular adenoma. Fifteen years ago, he underwent appendectomy for appendicitis. His medications are hydrochlorothiazide, fish oil supplementation, and a multivitamin. He received the influenza virus vaccine 6 months ago and the tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis vaccine 5 years ago. He does not smoke cigarettes. He drinks 8 to 16 oz of wine nightly and exercises four to five times weekly. He is 183 cm (6 ft) tall and weighs 86 kg (190 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. His pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 128/88 mm Hg. Examination shows scattered pale nevi over the chest and back. No other abnormalities are noted. Results of serum lipid studies from 6 months ago are shown:</br>Cholesterol, total    201 mg/dL</br>HDL-cholesterol        42 mg/dL</br>LDL-cholesterol       120 mg/dL</br>Triglycerides         180 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Administration of diphtheria-tetanus toxoid</br>b. Administration of pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine, 23-valent</br>c. Colonoscopy</br>d. ECG</br>e. Exercise stress test</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. Colonoscopy  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • 57-year-old man: average‐risk patients without prior polyps begin screening at 45–50 yr, but the interval changes once a polyp is found.<br /></br>   • Prior finding: “tubular adenoma” on colonoscopy 5 years ago.<br /></br>   • No high-risk features (no &gt;3 adenomas, none ≥10 mm, no villous histology, no high-grade dysplasia reported).<br /></br>   • Today he is asymptomatic and asks about routine care → question is testing surveillance‐interval knowledge.  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: history of adenomatous polyp → places him in the “low-risk adenoma” surveillance category.<br /></br>   • Negative: no family history of colon cancer, no inflammatory bowel disease, no alarm GI symptoms → no need for earlier or more aggressive surveillance than guidelines dictate.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   a. Identify whether the question is about (i) disease treatment, (ii) risk-factor modification, or (iii) preventive screening.<br /></br>   b. As soon as you see “prior adenoma” + “time since last colonoscopy,” shift to CRC-surveillance algorithms.<br /></br>   c. Recall: 3-yr interval for advanced or multiple adenomas; 5-yr interval for 1–2 small (&lt;10 mm) tubular adenomas.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning (stepwise)<br /></br>1. Classify prior polyp: single tubular adenoma &lt;10 mm = low-risk.<br /></br>2. Guideline: repeat colonoscopy at 5 yr.<br /></br>3. It has been exactly 5 yr → he is due now.<br /></br>Core Take-home: Surveillance intervals after adenoma resection override the standard 10-yr screening interval.</p></br><p>What you would document: “Patient had 1 small tubular adenoma on 2019 colonoscopy; guideline recommends 5-year surveillance—ordered colonoscopy for 2024.”  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Tubular adenomas are premalignant lesions progressing via adenoma-carcinoma sequence (mutation in APC → KRAS → p53).<br /></br>• Risk of progression correlates with size, villous architecture, number, and dysplasia grade.<br /></br>• Removing the polyp resets—but does not eliminate—risk; hence scheduled surveillance.</p></br><p>Treatments and Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Diagnostic: Colonoscopy—the only modality that both detects and allows removal of new adenomas.<br /></br>Therapy if lesions found: endoscopic polypectomy; interval shortened per findings (3 yr for high-risk, 1 yr for &gt;10 adenomas, etc.).  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“1–2 small tubular = ‘Hi-5!’ → High-five your patient goodbye for 5 years.”<br /></br>“Villous is villainous”: villous/large/multiple → come back in 3 years.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Diphtheria-tetanus toxoid (Td): Booster every 10 yr; patient got Tdap 5 yr ago → next Td due in 5 yr.<br /></br>b. PPSV23 (23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide): Given once at 65 yr for immunocompetent adults or earlier only for chronic lung, heart, liver disease, diabetes, alcoholism, smokers, or immunocompromise. Hypertension alone is not an indication.<br /></br>d. ECG: Routine resting ECG is not recommended in asymptomatic adults without cardiac disease.<br /></br>e. Exercise stress test: Indicated for intermediate-risk patients who are symptomatic or ≥10 yr ASCVD risk &gt;7.5–10 % and considering statin/therapy, but not routinely performed in asymptomatic, low-risk individuals.  </p></br><p>Common pitfalls to avoid<br /></br>• Confusing initial CRC screening intervals (10 yr) with post-polypectomy surveillance (3–5 yr).<br /></br>• Ordering PPSV23 before age 65 in healthy adults with no qualifying comorbidities.  </p></br><p>High-Yield Takeaway<br /></br>After resection of 1–2 small (&lt;10 mm) tubular adenomas, repeat colonoscopy at 5 years.</p>
116. A 39-year-old woman, gravida 3, para 2, is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-hour history of moderate to severe abdominal cramps and vaginal bleeding with clots. Her last menstrual period was 9 weeks ago. Menses previously occurred at regular 30-day intervals and lasted 7 days. Her previous pregnancies and deliveries were uncomplicated. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is currently homeless and seeking shelter. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 103/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 130/82 mm Hg. Examination shows tenderness to palpation of the mid lower abdomen. Bowel sounds are normal. The uterus is tender to palpation and consistent in size with a 9-week gestation. Pelvic examination shows normal external genitalia, moderate clots in the vaginal vault, and a closed cervical os. There is no adnexal tenderness or masses. Her hemoglobin concentration is 7.9 g/dL. Pelvic ultrasonography shows an intrauterine pregnancy consistent in size with a 9-week gestation; no fetal heart motion; a small amount of fluid in the cul-de-sac; and a subchorionic hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Administration of ceftriaxone</br>b. Administration of misoprostol</br>c. Dilatation and suction curettage</br>d. Laparoscopy</br>e. Repeat pelvic ultrasonography in 1 week</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Dilatation and suction curettage (vacuum aspiration)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 9-week intrauterine pregnancy with NO fetal heart activity → fetal demise.<br /></br>   • Cervical os CLOSED → products of conception have not been expelled.<br /></br>   • Vaginal bleeding with clots + crampy pain.<br /></br>   • Hgb 7.9 g/dL (symptomatic, significant anemia).<br /></br>   • “Moderate to severe” bleeding and tachycardia (HR 103).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Diagnosis pattern<br /></br>   • Fetal demise + closed cervix = MISSED ABORTION (a type of spontaneous abortion).<br /></br>   • Ongoing, clinically important bleeding makes conservative options less safe.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: Determine intra- vs extra-uterine pregnancy → US shows intra-uterine.<br /></br>   • Step 2: Assess viability → no cardiac motion at 9 wks = non-viable.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Assess cervical os → closed = missed abortion.<br /></br>   • Step 4: Choose management based on stability, bleeding, patient factors.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Three evidence-based choices for first-trimester missed abortion:<br /></br>  1. Expectant management (wait for spontaneous passage).<br /></br>  2. Medical evacuation (misoprostol ± mifepristone).<br /></br>  3. Surgical evacuation (dilation &amp; suction curettage).  </p></br><p>• Indications to move directly to surgery:<br /></br>  – Hemodynamic instability or significant ongoing bleeding.<br /></br>  – Severe anemia.<br /></br>  – Infection.<br /></br>  – Patient preference, need for rapid completion, or limited follow-up (e.g., homelessness).  </p></br><p>This patient has active bleeding, tachycardia, Hgb 7.9, and social factors that make reliable follow-up doubtful. Surgical evacuation rapidly stops bleeding, allows pathologic confirmation, and lets you transfuse if needed. Hence dilation &amp; suction curettage is the most appropriate next step.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In a non-viable first-trimester pregnancy, pick D&amp;C when bleeding is heavy, hemoglobin is low, or follow-up is unreliable.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls<br /></br>• Confirmed absent FHT on US, gestational age criteria met.<br /></br>• Informed consent for surgical evacuation; discuss risks (perforation, Asherman, infection), benefits (bleeding control).<br /></br>• Type &amp; screen; cross-match if transfusion likely.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Early pregnancy loss (&lt;20 wks) affects 10–15 % of clinically recognized pregnancies.<br /></br>• Missed abortion = embryonic or fetal death without cervical dilation or expulsion.<br /></br>• Bleeding occurs as the decidua separates, but the cervix stays closed until uterine contractions expel contents.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><ol></br><li>Rapid stabilization<br /></br>   – IV access, type &amp; screen, possible packed RBCs.  </li></br><li>Suction curettage<br /></br>   – Preferred up to ~14 wks;
