quick, &gt;95 % success, immediate bleeding control.  </li></br><li>Rh(D) immune globulin for Rh-negative women after uterine evacuation.  </li></br><li>Pathology to rule out gestational trophoblastic disease (very low risk but standard).  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC for early loss”<br /></br>A – Assess viability (US)<br /></br>B – Bleeding severity guides choice<br /></br>   • Big bleed → go to OR<br /></br>C – Closed cervix = Missed; open cervix = Incomplete/Inevitable  </p></br><p>Visual: traffic light<br /></br>• Green (minimal bleed) → expectant/medical<br /></br>• Yellow (moderate) → consider misoprostol<br /></br>• Red (heavy, Hb &lt;10, unstable) → suction curettage  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Ceftriaxone<br /></br>   • Empiric antibiotics are for septic abortion or PID. This patient has no fever, purulent discharge, uterine tenderness suggesting endometritis, or adnexal pain.  </p></br><p>b. Misoprostol<br /></br>   • Acceptable for stable patients, but not ideal here given significant bleeding, anemia, and poor follow-up. It can initially INCREASE bleeding and may take days.  </p></br><p>d. Laparoscopy<br /></br>   • Used for ectopic pregnancy or adnexal pathology. Ultrasound confirms an intra-uterine gestation with no adnexal masses—no indication for operative laparoscopy.  </p></br><p>e. Repeat pelvic ultrasonography in 1 week<br /></br>   • Waiting is appropriate in threatened abortion when viability is uncertain. Here viability has already been excluded (absent cardiac motion at 9 wks), and the patient is symptomatic. Delaying treatment risks further hemorrhage and complications.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In a first-trimester missed abortion with significant vaginal bleeding and anemia, immediate surgical evacuation (dilation &amp; suction curettage) is the safest and fastest way to control hemorrhage and complete the miscarriage.</p>
117. Three days after undergoing open fixation of a right hip fracture, a 77-year-old woman has progressive edema of the right lower extremity. She has no history of serious illness. Her medications are oxycodone, enoxaparin, and docusate. Her temperature is 38.0°C (100.4°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 140/80 mm Hg. There is 3+ pitting edema of the right lower extremity from the foot to the hip. Which of the following is the most likely set of findings on venous duplex ultrasonography?</br>Superficial Veins    Deep Veins	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Normal           normal</br>b. Normal           obstructed</br>c. Normal           reflux</br>d. Obstructed       normal</br>e. Obstructed       reflux</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer <br /></br>B. Superficial veins: NORMAL  Deep veins: OBSTRUCTED  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Post-op day 3 after open reduction and internal fixation of a hip fracture – major orthopedic surgery → very high venous thromboembolism (VTE) risk.<br /></br>   • Age 77 – advancing age is an additional VTE risk factor.<br /></br>   • Progressive, unilateral 3+ pitting edema from foot to hip – classic for an acute deep-vein obstruction.<br /></br>   • Low-grade fever is common with DVT; pulse, respirations, and blood pressure are otherwise unremarkable.<br /></br>   • She is receiving only prophylactic–dose enoxaparin (not therapeutic dosing).<br /></br>   Taken together, the pattern is almost textbook for an acute postoperative deep-vein thrombosis (DVT).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>   • ANY unilateral swelling in the first 30 days after major pelvic or lower-extremity surgery → think “rule out DVT first.”<br /></br>   • Quickly rule in or out alternative etiologies (cellulitis = erythema, warmth, pain; lymphedema = non-pitting, chronic; chronic venous insufficiency = skin changes + long history).<br /></br>   • Screening/confirmatory test of choice in symptomatic patients = venous duplex ultrasound.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic the test expects:<br /></br>A. Identify VTE risk factors (surgery, immobility, elderly).<br /></br>B. Recognize unilateral pitting edema → think venous obstruction.<br /></br>C. Deep system is responsible for the bulk of venous return; obstruction here produces dramatic swelling, whereas superficial disease produces much milder findings.<br /></br>D. Therefore duplex US will demonstrate:<br /></br>   • Normal compressibility/flow in superficial veins (great saphenous, small saphenous).<br /></br>   • Loss of compressibility or direct clot visualization in the deep system (femoral, popliteal, iliac).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Acute unilateral swelling after orthopedic surgery = DVT until proven otherwise; duplex shows obstruction in the deep, not the superficial, veins.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life)<br /></br>• Laterality, extent of swelling, calf/ thigh circumference.<br /></br>• Risk stratification (Caprini score).<br /></br>• Diagnostic modality ordered (venous duplex) and therapeutic anticoagulation plan.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Virchow Triad:<br /></br>1. Venous stasis – immobility, limb elevation, venous valves not milked by muscle pump post-op.<br /></br>2. Endothelial injury – surgical manipulation of femoral/iliac vessels.<br /></br>3. Hypercoagulability – acute phase response, tissue factor release, age-related prothrombotic state.  </p></br><p>A thrombus forms in the deep calf veins → can propagate proximally → leg edema; danger is embolization to pulmonary arteries.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests  </p></br><p>• First-line test: Compression duplex US (high sensitivity/specificity for femoral–popliteal DVT).<br /></br>• Labs: D-dimer has limited role post-op (almost always elevated).<br /></br>• Management: switch from prophylactic to therapeutic anticoagulation (e.g., IV unfractionated heparin or therapeutic LMWH such as enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID, then bridge to warfarin or use DOAC like apixaban).<br /></br>• Consider IVC filter only if anticoagulation is contraindicated.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Deep problems run deep.”<br /></br>• BIG swelling = BIG (deep) vein.<br /></br>• “HIP fracture → HIP clot” to link orthopedic hip surgery to high DVT risk.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Superficial NORMAL / Deep NORMAL<br /></br>   – Would be seen if edema were due to lymphedema, CHF, or hypoalbuminemia. No unilateral massive swelling acutely after surgery.  </p></br><p>C. Superficial NORMAL / Deep REFLUX<br /></br>   – Deep venous insufficiency (valvular incompetence), a chronic condition → chronic ankle edema, skin hyperpigmentation, venous ulcers—not an acute post-op phenomenon.  </p></br><p>D. Superficial OBSTRUCTED / Deep NORMAL<br /></br>   – Superficial thrombophlebitis (clot in great or small saphenous). Presents with a tender, palpable cord and mild localized swelling, not entire limb edema. Risk of PE is minimal.  </p></br><p>E. Superficial OBSTRUCTED / Deep REFLUX<br /></br>   – Combination of acute superficial thrombosis with chronic superficial venous insufficiency. Would give focal pain/erythema plus long-standing ankle swelling, not acute massive edema.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A unilateral, markedly swollen leg a few days after hip surgery is most consistent with an acute DVT; duplex ultrasound will show obstruction in the deep venous system while superficial veins remain patent.</p>
118. A 15-year-old girl is brought to the office by her father because of a 2-month history of yellowing of her eyes. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is at the 50th percentile for height, weight, and BMI. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows conjunctival icterus. Serum studies show a total bilirubin concentration of 4 mg/dL and a conjugated bilirubin concentration of 3 mg/dL. Results of other liver function tests are within the reference ranges. Ultrasonography of the liver shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Autoimmune hepatitis</br>b. Cholelithiasis</br>c. Dubin-Johnson syndrome</br>d. Gilbert syndrome</br>e. Hemolytic anemia</br>f. Viral hepatitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Dubin-Johnson syndrome  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clue #1: Isolated hyperbilirubinemia with total bili 4 mg/dL and conjugated (direct) bili 3 mg/dL → predominantly conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.<br /></br>• Key clue #2: AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase all normal → rules out hepatocellular injury and cholestatic obstruction.<br /></br>• Key clue #3: Normal liver ultrasound → no gallstones, mass, or biliary dilation.<br /></br>• Key clue #4: Young, otherwise healthy adolescent with mild, chronic scleral icterus.  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: “Healthy young person + normal LFTs + isolated, predominantly conjugated hyperbilirubinemia + normal imaging = hereditary defect in bilirubin excretion (Dubin-Johnson or Rotor).” On exams, Dubin-Johnson is the prototypic answer. (Rotor gives the same labs but is less commonly tested, and USMLE questions usually pin the diagnosis as Dubin-Johnson unless “darkly pigmented liver” or “MRP2 mutation” is specifically contrasted.)  </p></br><p>Test-taking tip: When bilirubin is conjugated but everything else (enzymes, imaging) is normal, immediately think of Dubin-Johnson/Rotor. When bilirubin is unconjugated with otherwise normal studies, think Gilbert syndrome.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Dubin-Johnson?)<br /></br>1. Genetic defect (ABCC2 gene → MRP2 canalicular transporter) prevents excretion of conjugated bilirubin into bile.<br /></br>2. Conjugation in hepatocytes is normal, so bilirubin that reaches plasma is predominantly conjugated.<br /></br>3. No hepatocellular injury → normal AST/ALT; no cholestasis → normal alkaline phosphatase &amp; GGT.<br /></br>4. No anatomic obstruction → normal ultrasound.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Dubin-Johnson = benign, autosomal-recessive, isolated conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with otherwise normal liver studies.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): chronic benign jaundice secondary to Dubin-Johnson syndrome; counsel on benign course; trigger awareness (illness, pregnancy, OCPs) but no treatment required.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Autosomal-recessive mutation in MRP2 (multidrug resistance–associated protein 2).<br /></br>• Mechanism: Conjugated bilirubin cannot be actively transported into bile canaliculi → refluxes into blood.<br /></br>• Liver grossly appears black due to polymerized epinephrine metabolites, but this is usually seen only at surgery/autopsy—hence “Johnson black.”<br /></br>• Clinical course: Benign, lifelong, fluctuating jaundice, especially during intercurrent illness, stress, OCP use, or pregnancy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• No treatment required.<br /></br>• Labs: persistently elevated direct bilirubin, otherwise normal.<br /></br>• Additional test (not needed here): Oral cholecystographic agent or bromsulphthalein excretion test shows impaired excretion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“Dubin-Johnson = Direct Bilirubin (conjugated) &amp; Dark (black) liver.”<br /></br>“Gilbert gives you a Glowing (unconjugated) yellow when you’re stressed.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Autoimmune hepatitis – Would show markedly elevated AST/ALT, (+) ANA or anti-smooth muscle Ab, and often hypergammaglobulinemia. Not seen here.  </p></br><p>b. Cholelithiasis – Usually causes episodic RUQ pain; if jaundice develops it’s from choledocholithiasis causing cholestasis → elevated alkaline phosphatase and dilated ducts on ultrasound. None of those findings are present.  </p></br><p>d. Gilbert syndrome – Defective UDP-glucuronyl transferase causes mild UNconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (total bili usually &lt;3 mg/dL) precipitated by stress, fasting. Here the bilirubin is predominantly conjugated.  </p></br><p>e. Hemolytic anemia – Produces high UNconjugated bilirubin, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, reticulocytosis, ± anemia/ jaundice. Patient’s bilirubin is conjugated and CBC is not suggestive of hemolysis.  </p></br><p>f. Viral hepatitis – Transaminases typically &gt;10× normal, may have mixed hyperbilirubinemia; patients often have systemic symptoms (fever, fatigue). Normal LFTs and imaging rule this out.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a healthy adolescent with isolated, predominantly conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and otherwise normal laboratory tests and imaging, the diagnosis is Dubin-Johnson syndrome—a benign hereditary defect in hepatic excretion of conjugated bilirubin.</p>
119. A hospitalized 65-year-old woman is prepared for discharge 15 days after undergoing uncomplicated surgical repair of the ventricular septum following an anterior ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. Her postoperative course has been uncomplicated. She has hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Her medications are clopidogrel, lisinopril, metoprolol, atorvastatin, and aspirin. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a clean, dry surgical incision. Echocardiography shows an ejection fraction of 50 percent. In addition to continuation of the medication regimen, which of the following interventions is most likely to decrease this patient's risk for a repeat myocardial infarction?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Antioxidant supplementation</br>b. Following a Mediterranean diet</br>c. Implantation of a cardiac defibrillator</br>d. Limiting physical activity</br>e. Omeprazole therapy</br>f. Warfarin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Following a Mediterranean diet  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 65-year-old woman with a recent ST-elevation MI successfully repaired (ventricular septal rupture closed).<br /></br>• Post-op course is “uncomplicated,” EF = 50 % (i.e., preserved systolic function).<br /></br>• Already on optimal secondary-prevention pharmacotherapy: aspirin, clopidogrel, β-blocker (metoprolol), ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), high-intensity statin (atorvastatin).<br /></br>• Question: “In addition to continuation of the medication regimen, which intervention ↓ risk for repeat MI?”</p></br><p>Cognitive roadmap:<br /></br>1. Recognize she already has all Class I, guideline-directed drugs.<br /></br>2. Ask: What <em>non-pharmacologic</em> or <em>device</em> strategy has proven outcome benefit in secondary prevention?<br /></br>3. Recall evidence hierarchy: cardiac rehab &amp; Mediterranean diet have Level I (randomized controlled trial) data; others on list have no mortality benefit or are harmful.<br /></br>4. Choose Mediterranean diet.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Core concept: Secondary prevention after MI = guideline-directed medical therapy + lifestyle modification with proven outcome data (dietary pattern, smoking cessation, exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation).<br /></br>Mediterranean diet (high fruits/vegetables, legumes, whole grains, nuts, olive oil; moderate fish/poultry; limited red/processed meats &amp; sweets) showed a 70 % relative risk reduction in recurrent MI/cardiovascular death in the Lyon Diet Heart Study and subsequent meta-analyses.</p></br><p>What you’d document: “Patient counseled on Mediterranean dietary pattern as per AHA/ACC secondary-prevention guidelines; educational materials provided; follow-up with cardiac rehab nutritionist arranged.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology &amp; Evidence Review  </p></br><p>• Atherothrombosis recurrence is driven by residual inflammation, dyslipidemia, endothelial dysfunction, and thrombosis.<br /></br>• Mediterranean diet: rich in monounsaturated fats (olive oil), ω-3 fatty acids, antioxidants, and polyphenols → improves lipid profile, reduces oxidative stress &amp; endothelial dysfunction, and lowers CRP levels.<br /></br>• Level of evidence: Class I, LOE B (AHA/ACC 2019 Prevention Guideline).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>First-line secondary prevention already present:<br /></br>1. Antiplatelet(s): aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor.<br /></br>2. High-intensity statin.<br /></br>3. β-blocker (≥3 yrs; lifelong if LV dysfunction).<br /></br>4. ACEI/ARB.<br /></br>Other non-pharm: smoking cessation, BP control, HbA1c &lt;7 % if diabetic, supervised exercise/cardiac rehab.</p></br><p>ICD consideration: wait ≥40 days post-MI; only if EF ≤35 % and NYHA II–III. Her EF 50 % → not indicated.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“M.E.D. after Myocardial infarction”<br /></br>M – Mediterranean diet<br /></br>E – Exercise / cardiac rEhab<br /></br>D – Don’t smoke (tobacco cessation)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Antioxidant supplementation<br /></br>• Multiple RCTs (e.g., HOPE-TOO, ATBC) show no reduction in CV events; some ↑ all-cause mortality.</p></br><p>C. Implantation of a cardiac defibrillator<br /></br>• ICD only if EF ≤35 % ≥40 days post-MI (primary prevention) or documented lethal arrhythmia (secondary). Her EF normal.</p></br><p>D. Limiting physical activity<br /></br>• Opposite of evidence; structured exercise-based cardiac rehab ↓ mortality and reinfarction.</p></br><p>E. Omeprazole therapy<br /></br>• Used to ↓ GI bleed risk with DAPT if high bleeding risk; has no effect on MI recurrence.</p></br><p>F. Warfarin therapy<br /></br>• Anticoagulation not indicated after uncomplicated MI unless LV thrombus, AF, or mechanical valve. Adds bleeding risk without benefit here.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Point  </p></br><p>After optimal medical therapy, the best proven lifestyle change to prevent another MI is adoption of a Mediterranean dietary pattern (plus cardiac rehab and smoking cessation).</p>
120. An 18-year-old woman who is a US Army recruit comes to the troop medical clinic because of a 4-day history of increasingly severe cramping and swelling of her left foot. She began basic training 10 days ago. She had not been physically active before joining the army. Initially, she was able to keep up with the training regimen, but she soon developed pain in her forefoot as the intensity of the exercise increased. Vital signs are within normal limits. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 25 kg/m2. Examination of the left foot shows tenderness to palpation of the dorsal aspect of the fifth metatarsal; there is minimal swelling. Movement of the involved toe elicits pain. X-ray of the left foot shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Bisphosphonate therapy</br>b. Cast immobilization</br>c. Indomethacin therapy</br>d. Use of arch supports in boots</br>e. Reassurance only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Cast immobilization  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • New, abrupt ↑ in weight-bearing activity (basic training).<br /></br>   • Localized pain &amp; point tenderness over the dorsal 5th metatarsal.<br /></br>   • Pain worsens with toe movement (stress is transmitted to the metatarsal).<br /></br>   • Plain radiograph is normal (early stress-fracture films are often negative for 1–3 wk).  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pattern recognition<br /></br>   • “Overuse + focal bony tenderness + normal x-ray” → classic for a stress fracture.<br /></br>   • 5th metatarsal = high-risk location (poor blood supply → non-union risk).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>   • For any overuse bone injury: if x-ray is negative but suspicion is high, treat as a stress fracture; obtain MRI/bone scan only if the diagnosis will change management.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Stepwise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify stress fracture.<br /></br>2. Determine risk level by location:<br /></br>   • Low-risk (2nd–4th metatarsals, calcaneus): stiff-soled shoe or walking boot.<br /></br>   • High-risk (5th metatarsal, navicular, anterior tibia, femoral neck): need more aggressive protection to prevent displacement or non-union.<br /></br>3. Therefore, immobilize with a cast (or non-weight-bearing boot) ± ortho referral.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: 5th-metatarsal stress fractures require cast immobilization.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): focal tenderness, swelling, negative initial radiograph, plan for cast, activity restriction, follow-up in 1–2 wk.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Repetitive sub-threshold loading → micro-trabecular cracks.<br /></br>• Bone remodeling can’t keep pace with the new stress → microfracture coalesces.<br /></br>• Lateral 5th metatarsal has watershed blood supply → higher risk of delayed healing.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management / Diagnostics<br /></br>First-line for high-risk stress fracture<br /></br>1. Immobilize in short-leg cast or CAM boot; non-weight-bearing with crutches.<br /></br>2. Follow-up imaging in 2–3 wk; consider MRI if diagnosis uncertain.<br /></br>3. Gradual return to activity only after pain-free and radiographic healing.<br /></br>(Analgesia: acetaminophen preferred; NSAIDs can impair bone healing.)  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“High FIVE, high risk” → 5th metatarsal stress fracture gets a cast.<br /></br>LSN (Locations of Serious Non-union):<br /></br>L – Lateral (5th) metatarsal<br /></br>S – Scaphoid/navicular<br /></br>N – Neck of femur / anterior tibia<br /></br>All need immobilization or surgery.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Bisphosphonate therapy<br /></br>   • Used for osteoporosis/Paget, not acute fractures; does not aid stress-fracture healing.  </p></br><p>C. Indomethacin therapy<br /></br>   • NSAIDs relieve pain but may delay bone healing; analgesia alone does not address the fracture.  </p></br><p>D. Use of arch supports in boots<br /></br>   • Helpful for plantar fasciitis or low-risk metatarsal stress fractures, but insufficient for a high-risk 5th-metatarsal lesion.  </p></br><p>E. Reassurance only<br /></br>   • Neglects the risk of progression to complete fracture or non-union; would violate standard of care.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>In a recruit with focal 5th-metatarsal pain and a normal x-ray, assume a high-risk stress fracture and place the foot in a cast to protect healing.</p>
121. A 24-year-old woman comes to the physician because of fever, headache, and purulent nasal discharge for 5 days. She has had recurrent sinus infections since the age of 17 years. She has a 3-year history of chronic nasal stuffiness and a morning cough productive of 2 to 3 teaspoons of purulent sputum daily. She was hospitalized for treatment of pneumonia 1 year ago. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 84/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 110/72 mm Hg. Examination shows edema of the nasal mucosa and a creamy yellow nasal exudate. The lungs are clear to auscultation. The spleen tip is palpated at the left costal margin. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the mechanism for the recurrent infections?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Measurement of serum immunoglobulin concentrations</br>b. Sputum culture</br>c. CT scan of the chest</br>d. Liver-spleen scan</br>e. Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct answer (given by user): Measurement of serum immunoglobulin concentrations  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Recurrent bacterial sinopulmonary infections beginning in late adolescence (age 17) → think humoral (antibody) immunodeficiency rather than T-cell or phagocyte disorders.<br /></br>• Current acute bacterial sinusitis (fever, purulent nasal discharge, edematous mucosa).<br /></br>• Daily purulent sputum and a prior hospitalization for pneumonia → encapsulated-organism infections typical of absent/low IgG &amp; IgA.<br /></br>• Splenomegaly → lymphoid hyperplasia/autoimmune complications seen in common variable immunodeficiency (CVID).<br /></br>Key pattern: Young adult + chronic &amp; recurrent sinopulmonary infections + splenomegaly = suspect CVID/antibody deficiency.  </p></br><p>Test‐taking tip: When the stem points to a MECHANISM of recurrent extracellular bacterial infections, first test = quantitative immunoglobulins.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1  Ask “What type of infections?” → bacterial, mucosal, upper &amp; lower airway.<br /></br>Step 2  Age of onset? → late teens/20s = CVID (vs X-linked agammaglobulinemia in infancy).<br /></br>Step 3  Extra clue? → splenomegaly/autoimmune phenomena fit CVID.<br /></br>Step 4  What confirms HUMORAL deficiency? → check IgG ± IgA/IgM levels.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Quantitative serum Ig levels are the screening/confirmatory test for suspected antibody deficiencies such as CVID or selective IgA deficiency.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Plan: order serum quantitative IgG, IgA, IgM to evaluate for common variable immunodeficiency given history of recurrent sinus &amp; pulmonary infections and splenomegaly.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review – Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID)<br /></br>• Etiology: heterogeneous B-cell differentiation defect → low IgG ± low IgA/IgM.<br /></br>• Onset: childhood to early adulthood.<br /></br>• Clinical: recurrent sinopulmonary infections, chronic cough/bronchiectasis, GI Giardia/Chronic diarrhea, autoimmune disease, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, ↑ risk lymphoma &amp; gastric ca.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Diagnosis<br /></br>1. Quantitative serum immunoglobulins (↓ IgG plus ↓ IgA and/or IgM).<br /></br>2. Poor or absent response to protein/polysaccharide vaccines.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>• Lifelong IVIG or SCIG replacement (e.g., IV immune globulin 400–600 mg/kg q3–4 wk).<br /></br>• Aggressive antibiotic therapy for infections.<br /></br>• Monitor for malignancy &amp; autoimmune complications.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“CVID = Cough, (Variable age), Infections (sinopulmonary), Decreased Ig.”<br /></br>Imagine a “C-V-I-D” license plate on a sputum-splattered car parked outside an allergy–immunology clinic. The “plate” reminds you to check Igs!  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Sputum culture – Identifies current pathogen but NOT the underlying immune mechanism. Would not explain years of recurrent infections.<br /></br>c. CT scan of the chest – Might reveal bronchiectasis (a consequence), but still doesn’t establish the immunologic cause.<br /></br>d. Liver-spleen scan – Unnecessary; splenomegaly already noted on PE, and scan does not assess immune function.<br /></br>e. Bone marrow aspiration/biopsy – Reserved for suspected cellular marrow disorders (e.g., leukemia, aplastic anemia). Antibody deficiencies originate in peripheral B-cell differentiation, not usually diagnosed with marrow biopsy.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Jumping to imaging or cultures when question explicitly asks for confirming the MECHANISM of recurrence. Always correlate infection type with the corresponding arm of the immune system and pick the screening test for that arm.</p>
122. A 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of scalp lesions that have been increasing in size during the past week. He went for a nature walk with his day-care class 10 days ago. Examination shows scattered papules with some scaly areas on the scalp; the surrounding hair is broken near the base with mild alopecia. Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Avoidance of wooded areas</br>b. Avoidance of sharing hats</br>c. Use of insect repellent</br>d. Use of a medicated shampoo</br>e. Use of sunscreen</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Avoidance of sharing hats</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases: “4-year-old,” “scalp lesions,” “scaly areas,” “broken hair near the base,” and “mild alopecia.”<br /></br>• Pattern recognition: Painless, scaly patches with hair shafts broken off at the surface (“black-dot” alopecia) is classic for tinea capitis (dermatophyte infection of the scalp).<br /></br>• Incubation: Symptoms began about 1 week after exposure—consistent with the 7- to 14-day incubation period of dermatophytes.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No pruritic linear vesicles (poison ivy), no erythema migrans, no nits or live lice, no mention of painful pustules (bacterial folliculitis).<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: On the exam, assume “scaly patch + broken hairs in a child” = tinea capitis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Diagnosis ⇒ tinea capitis caused most commonly by Trichophyton tonsurans in the USA.<br /></br>2. Mode of transmission ⇒ direct contact with infected hair or via fomites (combs, hairbrushes, hats, pillows).<br /></br>3. Prevention strategy that directly interrupts this transmission chain = do not share headwear or other hair-containing fomites.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Tinea capitis is spread person-to-person or via fomites; therefore avoidance of sharing hats/combs is the most effective preventive measure.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Dermatophytes digest keratin, invading the stratum corneum and hair shaft → perifollicular inflammation → hair becomes fragile and breaks at scalp surface → alopecia with “black dots.”<br /></br>• Risk factors: crowded day care, contact sports, sharing hair accessories, immunosuppression.<br /></br>• Untreated disease can progress to kerion (boggy inflammatory mass) or scarring alopecia.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (for real patients)</br>• First-line therapy: oral griseofulvin (6–8 weeks) or oral terbinafine.<br /></br>• Adjunct: antifungal shampoo (selenium sulfide or ketoconazole) to lower spore burden and reduce household spread—used AFTER infection is present, not for primary prevention.<br /></br>• Diagnosis is clinical; can confirm with KOH prep or fungal culture of plucked hairs.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“Don’t tip your CAP”<br /></br>C – Comb / Caps (hats)<br /></br>A – Animals (pets)<br /></br>P – Pillows / Personal items<br /></br>Avoid sharing these to prevent tinea CAPitis.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Avoidance of wooded areas<br /></br>• Prevents tick-borne diseases (Lyme, RMSF), not a dermatophyte. Would expect erythema migrans or fever, not scaly alopecia.</p></br><p>C. Use of insect repellent<br /></br>• Same logic as A; repellent wards off mosquitoes/ticks, not fungi.</p></br><p>D. Use of a medicated shampoo<br /></br>• Selenium sulfide/ketoconazole shampoo is helpful to limit spread once infection exists, but kids generally do not use it prophylactically. It does not reliably prevent initial infection acquired from an infected classmate’s hat.</p></br><p>E. Use of sunscreen<br /></br>• Prevents sunburn/skin cancer; irrelevant to a fungal scalp infection.</p></br><p>Bottom Line</br>Tinea capitis spreads through direct contact with infected hairs or contaminated fomites. Teaching children not to share hats, combs, or hair accessories is the most effective primary prevention strategy.</p>
123. A 72-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of a 2-week history of increased thirst and frequent urination. She says that her symptoms have been getting worse. She drinks copious amounts of water and now urinates every 1 to 2 hours. She has hypertension controlled with ramipril. She had a myocardial infarction 6 years ago. On arrival, she appears dehydrated. Her temperature is 36°C (96.8°F), pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows dry mucous membranes. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hematocrit 47 percent</br>Serum</br>Na+ 125 mEq/L</br>K+ 3.5 mEq/L</br>Cl- 95 mEq/L</br>HCO3- 24 mEq/L</br>Urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL</br>Glucose 700 mg/dL</br>Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL</br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s serum sodium concentration?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adverse effect of ramipril</br>b. Hyperglycemia</br>c. Increased intake of free water</br>d. Increased renal excretion of sodium</br>e. Increased secretion of ADH (vasopressin)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Hyperglycemia  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key phrases/clues<br /></br>   • “2-week history of increased thirst and frequent urination” → classic osmotic diuresis.<br /></br>   • Serum glucose 700 mg/dL → severe hyperglycemia (Hyper-osmolar hyperglycemic state, HHS).<br /></br>   • Serum Na⁺ 125 mEq/L with clinical dehydration (tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, low BP) → the low sodium seems discordant with obvious water loss.  </li></br><li>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • Hypovolemic exam, NOT edematous or euvolemic → excludes SIADH.<br /></br>   • No diuretics listed, ACE-I only → makes diuretic-induced or drug-induced natriuresis unlikely.<br /></br>   • Large free-water intake is reactive, not primary psychogenic polydipsia.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, whenever you see (Na⁺ 120–130 mEq/L) + (glucose ≥300 mg/dL), immediately calculate a corrected sodium before choosing an SIADH/diuretic answer.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Hyperglycemia → markedly raises extracellular osmolality.  </li></br><li>Water shifts from intracellular → extracellular compartment to equilibrate osmolality.  </li></br><li>This extra water dilutes serum sodium = “translocational” or “hypertonic” hyponatremia.  </li></br><li>Corrected Na⁺ = measured Na⁺ + 1.6–2.0 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL of glucose above 100.<br /></br>   • Excess glucose = 700 – 100 = 600 mg/dL.<br /></br>   • 1.6 × 6 ≈ 9.6 → 125 + 9.6 ≈ 135 mEq/L (normal).  </li></br><li>Therefore the true sodium is normal; the apparent hyponatremia is entirely due to the hyperglycemia.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: In hyperosmolar states, always correct Na⁺ before labeling a patient hyponatremic.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Corrected serum sodium ≈135 mEq/L after adjustment for hyperglycemia; no true hyponatremia.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Hyperglycemia (&gt;600 mg/dL) → plasma osmolality ↑≈2×Na + Glu/18 + BUN/2.8.<br /></br>• Osmotic gradient pulls H₂O out of cells → extracellular dilution of Na⁺.<br /></br>• Concurrent osmotic diuresis (glucosuria) causes polyuria, polydipsia, volume depletion, pre-renal azotemia (BUN 30, Cr 1.8).<br /></br>• Once glucose is treated, water will shift back into cells and serum Na⁺ rises. Rapid correction of glucose without attention to Na⁺/osmolarity may unmask or precipitate hypernatremia or cerebral edema—hence gradual therapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (HHS quick review)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Large-volume isotonic saline first (restore perfusion).  </li></br><li>IV regular insulin drip after initial fluids.  </li></br><li>Replace potassium once K⁺ &lt;5.3 mEq/L (even if serum K⁺ starts “normal” it will fall with insulin).  </li></br><li>Search for precipitating factors (infection, MI).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“1.6 Correction Rule”: Add 1.6 mEq Na for every 100 mg/dL glucose over 100.<br /></br>“High sugar hides sodium.” Visualize glucose molecules crowding plasma pulling water along and pushing Na⁺ apart.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Answers Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Adverse effect of ramipril<br /></br>   • ACE inhibitors cause hyperkalemia, not hyponatremia; hyponatremia via ACE-I would require severe volume depletion + salt wasting, not picture here.  </p></br><p>c. Increased intake of free water (primary polydipsia)<br /></br>   • Would be euvolemic or slightly hypervolemic, not dehydrated; glucose would be normal.  </p></br><p>d. Increased renal excretion of sodium (e.g., diuretics, cerebral salt wasting)<br /></br>   • No diuretic use, natriuresis labs not given; hyponatremia would persist after correcting glucose.  </p></br><p>e. Increased secretion of ADH (SIADH)<br /></br>   • SIADH presents with euvolemic hyponatremia, low serum osmolality, typically normal BUN/Cr. This patient is hypovolemic with high osmolality from glucose.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: The “hyponatremia” in hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state is dilutional; correcting for glucose reveals a normal sodium concentration.</p>
124. A 19-year-old college student comes to the student health center for a physical examination prior to participation in a school intramural basketball league. She has been generally healthy. She says that she is a strict vegetarian and does not eat any meat, eggs, or dairy products. She takes an iron supplement daily. She takes no other medications. She is 162 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 45 kg (100 lb); BMI is 17 kg/m2. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following additional supplements is most appropriate to recommend at this time?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin)</br>b. Vitamin B2 (riboflavin)</br>c. Vitamin C</br>d. Folic acid</br>e. Thiamine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• “Strict vegetarian…does not eat any meat, eggs, or dairy” = vegan diet → eliminates the ONLY reliable natural sources of vitamin B12 (animal products).<br /></br>• BMI 17 kg/m² = underweight, reinforces limited caloric variety but not specific for any one vitamin deficiency.<br /></br>• Already taking iron, so iron is addressed.<br /></br>Key test‐taking cue: In any patient who excludes all animal products, think “B12 supplementation” first.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a nutrition question describes a vegan, quickly scan answer choices for B12; it is frequently the single best answer.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Identify risk: Vegan → high risk of B12 deficiency because plants provide virtually none.<br /></br>Step 2 – Assess stores: Hepatic stores last 2-4 years; a 19-year-old who may have been vegan for years can still become depleted.<br /></br>Step 3 – Recommend prevention: Oral cyanocobalamin is safe, inexpensive, and prevents irreversible neurologic injury.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Vitamin B12 is the only essential vitamin not obtainable from an unfortified plant-based diet; vegans need supplementation.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Counsel patient on daily oral cyanocobalamin 100–250 µg or weekly 1 mg; note dietary counseling and plan for periodic CBC/serum B12 monitoring.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Vitamin B12 is required for methylmalonyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA and homocysteine → methionine reactions.<br /></br>• Deficiency → impaired DNA synthesis → megaloblastic anemia + neurologic findings (peripheral neuropathy, dorsal column &amp; corticospinal tract demyelination, “subacute combined degeneration”) due to methylmalonic acid accumulation.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>1. Prevention: Oral cyanocobalamin or fortified foods (nutritional yeast, fortified cereals/plant milks).<br /></br>2. Symptomatic deficiency: High-dose oral (1–2 mg/day) or IM injections until repleted, then maintenance.<br /></br>3. Laboratory confirmation: Low serum B12, elevated methylmalonic acid &amp; homocysteine.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Vegan? Think V-B12-gan.”<br /></br>or<br /></br>“B12 = Beef, fish, eggs, dairy → remove the animals, remove the vitamin.”</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Vitamin B2 (riboflavin) – Also found in green vegetables, legumes, whole grains; isolated deficiency is rare in vegans alone. Would cause angular cheilosis, glossitis, corneal vascularization—none present.<br /></br>c. Vitamin C – Abundant in fruits &amp; vegetables; vegans often have high intake. Deficiency (scurvy) shows bleeding gums, petechiae, corkscrew hairs—not seen here.<br /></br>d. Folic acid – Leafy greens, beans, citrus supply plenty. Vegans usually have high folate; deficiency presents with anemia but NO neurologic signs. Prophylaxis reserved mainly for pregnancy or certain drugs.<br /></br>e. Thiamine (B1) – Present in whole grains, nuts, legumes; deficiency linked to alcoholism, malabsorption, bariatric surgery. Would produce Wernicke-Korsakoff or beriberi, absent here.</p></br><p>Key difference: Only vitamin B12 is not readily available from plant sources, making it the logical supplement for a healthy vegan college student.</p>
125. A 9-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his mother because of a 1-month history of intermittent nonproductive cough. The cough is exacerbated with exercise and is worse at night. He has not had fever. The patient’s father received the diagnosis of asthma as a child. The patient’s temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), pulse is 90/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 108/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Pulmonary function tests are most likely to show which of the following findings in this patient?  </br>FEV₁           FVC           Total Lung Capacity	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Decreased   decreased     decreased  </br>b. Decreased   increased     decreased  </br>c. Decreased   normal        increased  </br>d. Normal      decreased     normal  </br>e. Normal      increased     normal  </br>f. Normal      normal        increased</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (as provided by the key)</br>C. FEV₁: decreased  FVC: normal  Total lung capacity (TLC): increased</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases:</br>  – “1-month history of intermittent nonproductive cough … exacerbated with exercise and … worse at night.”<br /></br>  – “Father received the diagnosis of asthma.”<br /></br>  – Normal vitals and physical exam.</p></br><p>• Why they matter:<br /></br>  1. Cough that is worse at night + exercise-induced = classic for cough-variant asthma (the most common pediatric presentation of mild intermittent asthma).<br /></br>  2. Positive family history strengthens the pre-test probability.<br /></br>  3. Lack of fever or systemic symptoms makes infection unlikely.</p></br><p>• Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>  – Whenever a vignette lists nocturnal cough plus exercise triggers in a child, move “asthma” to the top of the differential and think “obstructive pattern” on spirometry.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise approach to asthma PFTs:</br>1. Obstructive physiology → airflow out of the lungs is limited → FEV₁ falls.<br /></br>2. FVC is normal or mildly reduced (air eventually comes out with enough time).<br /></br>3. Air trapping/hyperinflation → ↑ residual volume (RV) → ↑ TLC.  </p></br><p>Choice C is the only pattern with decreased FEV₁, normal FVC, and increased TLC—hallmarks of obstructive disease with air trapping.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: In asthma (and any obstructive lung disease) expect ↓FEV₁/normal-to-↓FVC (so the FEV₁/FVC ratio drops) and ↑TLC due to air trapping.</p></br><p>Documentation note (real patient): Record baseline spirometry values, the child’s triggers, nighttime symptoms, and plan for a bronchodilator trial ± methacholine challenge if baseline spirometry is non-diagnostic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Asthma = reversible airway obstruction caused by bronchial hyperresponsiveness and inflammation.<br /></br>• Airway narrowing prolongs expiratory phase → ↓FEV₁.<br /></br>• Trapped air elevates RV and TLC.<br /></br>• Normal diffusion capacity distinguishes it from emphysema.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Snapshot</br>1. Confirm diagnosis: spirometry showing ↓FEV₁/FVC with ≥12% ↑ in FEV₁ after albuterol or +methacholine challenge.<br /></br>2. Mild intermittent (symptoms ≤2×/wk, ≤2 night awakenings/mo): short-acting β₂-agonist (SABA; e.g., albuterol) PRN.<br /></br>3. Escalate with low-dose inhaled corticosteroid if persistent.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“ObstrucTIVe = ‘TIV’ is ↑ TLC, ↑ RV”<br /></br>“FEV₁ Falls, FVC Fair, lungs Full (↑TLC).”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. ↓FEV₁, ↓FVC, ↓TLC – Classic restrictive pattern (e.g., idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis).<br /></br>b. ↓FEV₁, ↑FVC, ↓TLC – Physiologically inconsistent (FVC and TLC usually track together).<br /></br>d. Normal FEV₁, ↓FVC, normal TLC – Suggests restrictive disease with poor effort or obesity hypoventilation, not asthma.<br /></br>e. Normal FEV₁, ↑FVC, normal TLC – Not seen in common lung physiology; doesn’t fit asthma.<br /></br>f. Normal FEV₁, normal FVC, ↑TLC – Would keep FEV₁/FVC ratio normal; seen in some early COPD with air trapping but normal flow rates—doesn’t match symptomatic child with classic triggers.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Equating “normal exam” with normal spirometry. Asthma is often asymptomatic between attacks; objective testing is essential.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Key Take-Home Message</br>Asthma (including cough-variant) shows an obstructive PFT pattern: decreased FEV₁, normal or mildly reduced FVC, and increased TLC from air trapping—precisely the pattern in answer choice C.</p>
126. Thirty-six hours after cesarean delivery because of prolonged labor, a 22-year-old woman has abdominal cramping and nausea and vomiting. Her temperature is 38.8°C (101.8°F), pulse is 98/min, and blood pressure is 110/64 mm Hg. Examination shows diffuse lower abdominal tenderness with some voluntary guarding but no rebound. The incision is clean, dry, and intact. Her leukocyte count is 15,000/mm3. A urinary catheter is in place; urinalysis shows multiple RBCs. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Appendicitis</br>b. Cystitis</br>c. Endometritis</br>d. Intra-abdominal hemorrhage</br>e. Normal findings after cesarean delivery</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Endometritis  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Key Clues<br /></br>1. Timing: 36 hours postpartum (classic window for early puerperal fever).<br /></br>2. Mode of delivery: Cesarean after prolonged labor → highest-risk scenario for postpartum endometritis (bacteria inoculated during many vaginal exams + uterine incision).<br /></br>3. Fever: 38.8 °C (meets ≥38 °C on 2 occasions or &gt;38.3 °C once = postpartum fever definition).<br /></br>4. Abdominal pain: Diffuse lower-abdomen/uterine cramping; mild guarding but no rebound (visceral, not peritoneal).<br /></br>5. Incision: Clean, dry, intact → rules out wound infection.<br /></br>6. Leukocytosis: 15,000/mm³ supports infection but is expected post-op—still helps when coupled with fever/tender uterus.<br /></br>7. UA: Few RBCs only (from Foley irritation) but no pyuria or bacteria → argues against cystitis.  </p></br><p>Approach tip: “Post-cesarean fever within 48 h + uterine tenderness” → think endometritis first; check incision, breasts, urine next.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>• Any postpartum fever → localize source (5 W’s: Wind, Water, Womb, Wound, Walk).<br /></br>• Cesarean = big “Womb” risk.<br /></br>• Incision normal, lungs/BP stable, Foley in place with benign UA → focus on uterus.<br /></br>• Diffuse lower-abd pain = uterine, not peritoneal.<br /></br>Therefore, endometritis is most likely.</p></br><p>Take-home: The womb is the #1 source of fever ≤2 days after a cesarean delivery.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): fever curve, uterine/abdominal exam findings, lochia character, C-section details, IV antibiotics initiated, blood/lochia cultures obtained.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Polymicrobial infection of the decidua/myometrium (anaerobes, Gram-neg rods, GBS, Enterococci).<br /></br>• Ascending vaginal flora gain access when uterine cavity is opened; necrotic tissue and blood = growth medium.  </p></br><p>Risk factors: cesarean (15–30 %), prolonged labor/ROM, multiple digital exams, chorioamnionitis, retained products.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment<br /></br>Diagnostics: Primarily clinical. Cultures (blood, lochia) optional. US only if suspect retained products.  </p></br><p>First-line therapy: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics until afebrile 24 h.<br /></br>• Clindamycin (anaerobes + Gram +) PLUS Gentamicin (Gram –).<br /></br>Add Ampicillin if no clinical response in 48 h (Enterococcus coverage).<br /></br>If vaginal delivery: IV ampicillin-sulbactam or ampicillin/gent/clinda.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“C-section fever? Think the 3 C’s”<br /></br>Cesarean → Contaminated Cavity → Clinda + Gent.  </p></br><p>Or: W-cycle mnemonic (POD timing)<br /></br>POD 1 = Wind (atelectasis)<br /></br>POD 2 = Womb (endometritis, esp after C-section)<br /></br>Helps lock in timing.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. Appendicitis<br /></br>• Pain classically RLQ with rebound/guarding; fever can occur but unrelated to delivery mode.<br /></br>• Postpartum uterus displaces appendix RUQ, making focal tenderness typical—not diffuse uterine cramping.  </p></br><p>b. Cystitis<br /></br>• Foley trauma causes microscopic hematuria, but cystitis needs pyuria, bacteriuria, dysuria. Fever uncommon unless pyelonephritis.  </p></br><p>d. Intra-abdominal hemorrhage<br /></br>• Would present with hemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension), abdominal distention, dropping Hct—not fever/leukocytosis.  </p></br><p>e. Normal findings after cesarean delivery<br /></br>• Low-grade (&lt;38 °C) fever and mild leukocytosis are common. 38.8 °C with uterine tenderness is pathologic.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Concept<br /></br>Post-cesarean fever + uterine tenderness within 48 h is postpartum endometritis—treat promptly with IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (clindamycin + gentamicin).</p>
127. A 67-year-old man with aortic stenosis comes to the physician for prophylaxis before undergoing a dental procedure. He has a history of aortic valve replacement; he is currently asymptomatic. He had an episode of hives several years ago after treatment with penicillin. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows a grade 2/6, systolic ejection murmur; no gallops are heard. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic for prophylaxis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Ampicillin</br>b. Clarithromycin</br>c. Levofloxacin</br>d. Metronidazole</br>e. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole</br>f. Vancomycin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Clarithromycin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key “must notice” clues<br /></br>   • Prosthetic aortic valve (“aortic valve replacement”) → automatic high-risk cardiac condition that requires infective endocarditis (IE) prophylaxis.<br /></br>   • Upcoming dental work (“manipulation of gingival tissue”) → procedure for which IE prophylaxis is indicated in high-risk patients.<br /></br>   • IgE-mediated β-lactam allergy (“episode of hives after penicillin”) → must avoid all penicillins/ampicillin/amoxicillin and most cephalosporins.<br /></br>2. Cognitive pathway<br /></br>   • Step 1 – Does this patient meet an indication?  Yes (prosthetic valve + dental procedure).<br /></br>   • Step 2 – Choose the standard agent: oral amoxicillin 2 g.<br /></br>   • Step 3 – Is amoxicillin contraindicated?  Yes, because of true penicillin allergy (urticaria).<br /></br>   • Step 4 – Pick an oral, non-β-lactam alternative: azithromycin, clarithromycin, or clindamycin.<br /></br>   • Among the given options, clarithromycin is the only one that fits.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On exam, immediately pair “prosthetic valve + dental work” with “need prophylaxis,” then scan answer list for amoxicillin; if allergic, jump to macrolide or clindamycin instead.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Reasoning &amp; Core Takeaway<br /></br>Guideline (AHA/ACC):<br /></br>• Indications for IE prophylaxis = PROSTHETIC valve, prior IE, certain congenital heart lesions, or cardiac transplant with valvulopathy, IF undergoing high-risk procedures (dental work manipulating gingiva or perforating oral mucosa).  </p></br><p>Regimens 30–60 min before procedure<br /></br>• First-line oral: Amoxicillin 2 g.<br /></br>• Unable to take oral: Ampicillin 2 g IV/IM or Cefazolin/Ceftriaxone 1 g IV/IM.<br /></br>• Penicillin allergy, can take oral: Clindamycin 600 mg, Azithro 500 mg, or Clarithro 500 mg.<br /></br>• Penicillin allergy, cannot take oral: Vancomycin 1 g IV or Clindamycin 600 mg IV/IM.  </p></br><p>Clarithromycin therefore satisfies “oral + penicillin allergy.”  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Prophylactic clarithromycin 500 mg PO given 1 h prior to dental extraction for prosthetic aortic valve per AHA guidelines.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Brief<br /></br>Bacteremia during dental manipulation mainly involves viridans group streptococci (e.g., S. sanguinis). In high-risk cardiac lesions these organisms adhere to prosthetic material or damaged endothelium → vegetations → IE. A single prophylactic antibiotic dose vastly reduces bacterial load entering the bloodstream, preventing colonization.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Rundown<br /></br>A. Ampicillin – β-lactam; cross-reacts with penicillin → contraindicated in a patient with IgE reaction (hives).<br /></br>C. Levofloxacin – Fluoroquinolone; not recommended by guidelines, inferior viridans coverage.<br /></br>D. Metronidazole – Anaerobic coverage; little activity against viridans streptococci.<br /></br>E. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole – Mainly gram-negatives &amp; staph; poor viridans coverage; not guideline-endorsed.<br /></br>F. Vancomycin – Acceptable only if patient cannot take oral meds or institutionally high MRSA prevalence; unnecessary IV exposure here because patient can swallow pills.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips<br /></br>“ABCs of dental IE prophylaxis”<br /></br>A – Amoxicillin (1st choice)<br /></br>B – Beta-lactam allergy? → C or C<br /></br>C – Clindamycin / Clarithromycin (oral macrolide/lincosamide)<br /></br>If they “can’t swallow,” give Vancomycin IV.  </p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture a dentist handing an apple (am-apple-cillin) to the patient; if the patient breaks out in hives, the dentist swaps it for a clarinet (claRITHromycin) before the procedure.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home<br /></br>1. Prosthetic valve + dental work = ALWAYS prophylax.<br /></br>2. Penicillin allergy with urticaria → choose oral macrolide or clindamycin.<br /></br>3. Clarithromycin 500 mg PO 30–60 min pre-procedure fulfills guideline criteria in this scenario.</p>
128. A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of progressive shortness of breath during the past week. She now becomes short of breath after walking 10 feet. She says that she thinks she has had fever but has not taken her temperature. She does not think she has been in contact with any ill persons. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years. She does not drink alcohol but has used intravenous heroin for 10 years; she rarely shares or sterilizes needles. She lives in Massachusetts and has not traveled outside of the state in 8 years. She is in mild respiratory distress. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38°C (100.4°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 93 percent. Diffuse crackles are heard bilaterally. There is no peripheral edema. After she walks 50 feet, her oxygen saturation decreases to 83 percent. Her leukocyte count is 4800/mm3 (70 percent segmented neutrophils, 2 percent bands, 26 percent lymphocytes, and 2 percent eosinophils). An x-ray of the chest shows diffuse alveolar infiltrates bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Histoplasmosis</br>b. Mycoplasmal pneumonia</br>c. Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii) pneumonia</br>d. Right-sided endocarditis with septic emboli to the lungs</br>e. Sarcoidosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Pneumocystis jirovecii (formerly P. carinii) pneumonia  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key “buzz” phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>   • Progressive dyspnea over days–1 week, out of proportion to exam<br /></br>   • Exertional desaturation: SpO₂ 93 % at rest → 83 % after a 50-ft walk (classic for PJP)<br /></br>   • Low-grade fever, minimal tachycardia, normal/low WBC (“walking-type” presentation)<br /></br>   • Diffuse bilateral alveolar/interstitial infiltrates on CXR (“ground-glass”)<br /></br>   • IV heroin use → high HIV risk; BMI 18 kg/m² suggests chronic disease/wasting<br /></br>   • Leukocyte count normal–low with relative lymphopenia → possible CD4 depletion  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • NO productive cough, focal lobar process, cavitation, or pleuritic chest pain → argues against typical/atypical bacterial pneumonia or septic emboli<br /></br>   • No travel, bird/bat exposure, or occupational history → makes endemic fungi less likely<br /></br>   • Subacute, not chronic multi-system → sarcoidosis unlikely  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see an afebrile/low-grade fever patient with diffuse infiltrates, marked O₂ drop with minimal exertion, and an HIV risk factor, think “PJP until proven otherwise.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify immunocompromise → IV drug user → possible HIV.<br /></br>Step 2: Match symptom pattern → indolent dyspnea + exertional hypoxemia.<br /></br>Step 3: Imaging pattern → diffuse bilateral, not focal; think opportunistic infections (PJP, CMV).<br /></br>Step 4: Combine → PJP most common AIDS-defining pulmonary infection when CD4 &lt; 200.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: The triad of HIV risk, exertional desaturation, and bilateral diffuse infiltrates = Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real encounter you must note HIV risk factors, offer rapid HIV testing, document oxygen saturation changes with ambulation, and begin empiric PJP therapy promptly.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Organism: Pneumocystis jirovecii (yeast-like fungus).<br /></br>• Host defense: Alveolar macrophages and CD4⁺ T-cells; CD4 &lt; 200 ⇒ high risk.<br /></br>• Pathology: Foamy (“cotton candy”) intra-alveolar exudate → impaired gas exchange → hypoxemia, especially with exertion.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• CXR: Diffuse bilateral “ground-glass” or alveolar infiltrates.<br /></br>• CT chest: Patchy ground-glass opacities (most sensitive).<br /></br>• Definitive: Induced sputum or BAL silver stain or DFA for cysts/trophs.<br /></br>• Lab: ↑LDH (nonspecific but common). A-a gradient usually elevated.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>First-line: TMP-SMX (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) IV or PO, 21 days.<br /></br>Adjunctive steroids (prednisone) if PaO₂ &lt; 70 mm Hg or A-a gradient ≥ 35.<br /></br>Secondary prophylaxis: TMP-SMX until CD4 &gt; 200 × 3 months on ART.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SILVER ground-glass makes you DROP”:<br /></br>• SILVER stain to diagnose<br /></br>• Ground-glass CXR/CT<br /></br>• Oxygen saturation DROPs with minimal exertion  </p></br><p>“PJP = Pretty Junky Patient” → think IV drug user/HIV.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Histoplasmosis<br /></br>   – Midwest/Ohio &amp; Mississippi River valleys, bird/bat guano, caves.<br /></br>   – Hilar/mediastinal LAD, nodular lesions; not rapid exertional desaturation.  </p></br><p>b. Mycoplasmal pneumonia<br /></br>   – Young healthy adults in close quarters; patchy interstitial CXR, bullous myringitis.<br /></br>   – Usually higher WBC, minimal desaturation at rest/exertion, no HIV link.  </p></br><p>d. Right-sided endocarditis with septic emboli<br /></br>   – IV drug user risk, BUT CXR shows multiple nodular/cavitating lesions, not diffuse alveolar pattern.<br /></br>   – Typically high fever, leukocytosis, new murmur, hemoptysis/pleuritic pain.  </p></br><p>e. Sarcoidosis<br /></br>   – Chronic disease, African-American females; bilateral hilar LAD, reticulonodular infiltrates, skin/eye lesions.<br /></br>   – Normal oxygenation early, no fever, subacute course.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>An HIV-risk patient with subacute dyspnea, exertional hypoxemia, low-grade fever, normal-low WBC, and diffuse alveolar infiltrates = Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia. Rapid recognition is vital because prompt TMP-SMX ± steroids dramatically improves survival.</p>
129. A 32-year-old woman, gravida 1, para 1, comes to the physician for a postpartum examination 6 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery at home. She feels well, and her postpartum course has been uncomplicated. During her pregnancy, results of a serum rubella titer were reported as nonimmune. Physical examination today shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Second serum rubella titer today</br>c. Second serum rubella titer when her newborn is vaccinated</br>d. Rubella vaccine today</br>e. Rubella vaccine when her newborn is vaccinated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Rubella vaccine today  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key clues:<br /></br>• “Serum rubella titer … nonimmune” – patient lacks protective IgG antibodies.<br /></br>• “6 weeks after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery” – she is no longer pregnant, so live-virus vaccines are now allowed.<br /></br>• “Feels well … physical exam normal” – no contraindication to vaccination.<br /></br>Testing strategy: On NBME/Step exams, when a woman is identified as rubella non-immune during pregnancy, the automatic next action at the postpartum visit is immediate MMR (measles-mumps-rubella) vaccination before discharge or at the 6-week check, with contraception counseling for at least 1 month.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Identify the problem – absence of rubella immunity places future pregnancies at risk for congenital rubella syndrome (sensorineural deafness, cataracts, PDA).<br /></br>Step 2: Ask “Can I give a live vaccine now?” – Yes, she is postpartum and not pregnant.<br /></br>Step 3: Act now – administer MMR today; do not delay, repeat titers, or wait for infant vaccines.<br /></br>Take-home message: Postpartum MMR for any woman who is rubella-nonimmune during pregnancy. Document vaccine administration and counsel to avoid pregnancy for 28 days.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Rubella virus causes viremia that can cross the placenta. First-trimester infection → up to 85% risk of congenital rubella syndrome. Maternal IgG prevents transmission, hence universal prenatal screening and postpartum immunization. The MMR vaccine is live attenuated; contraindicated during pregnancy but safe during breastfeeding.  </p></br><p>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>Diagnostics: Prenatal rubella IgG screen (already done). No need to “re-check” antibodies; ELISA is highly sensitive/specific.<br /></br>Treatment/Prevention:<br /></br>• First-line: single dose MMR (contains live attenuated rubella virus).<br /></br>• Give before hospital discharge or at postpartum visit.<br /></br>• Provide reliable contraception and document counseling to avoid conception for ≥1 month.<br /></br>• No other routine serologic follow-up is required unless future pregnancy screen is again negative.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Rubella Rule: No baby on board → give the shot on the ward.”<br /></br>Visualize a stop sign labeled “Pregnant?”—if NO, green light to MMR.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Observation only – Misses prevention; leaves patient susceptible to congenital rubella in future pregnancies.<br /></br>B. Second serum rubella titer today – Redundant; the initial screen is definitive. Repeating delays protection.<br /></br>C. Second serum rubella titer when her newborn is vaccinated – Same flaw as B; infant’s vaccine schedule is irrelevant to maternal immunity.<br /></br>E. Rubella vaccine when her newborn is vaccinated – Delays immunization for ~12 months, exposing next pregnancy to risk; immediate postpartum vaccination is standard.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Students over-think live-virus contraindications and forget that they apply only during pregnancy, not during breastfeeding or postpartum period.  </p></br><p>End of explanation.</p>
130. A study is conducted to assess the impact of obesity on the risk for cerebral infarction. The investigator enrolls 100 participants with a history of cerebral infarction and 100 healthy participants. The investigator determines each participant’s weight. The results show:</br>Cerebral Infarction</br>      Yes   No   Total</br>Obesity</br>Yes     40    30    70</br>No      60    70    130</br>Total  100   100   200</br>Which of the following represents the odds ratio for the relationship between obesity and cerebral infarction?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. 30/60</br>b. 60/30</br>c. (30/60)/(70/40)</br>d. (40/60)/(30/70)</br>e. (40/100)/(30/100)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D.  (40/60) ÷ (30/70)</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>1. “Enrolls 100 participants WITH a history of cerebral infarction and 100 HEALTHY participants” → Subjects are selected on the basis of disease status, so this is a case-control study.<br /></br>2. Weight (obesity) is the exposure of interest.<br /></br>3. Question asks for “odds ratio” (OR), the appropriate association measure for a case-control design.</p></br><p>Pertinent numbers (2 × 2 table convention)<br /></br>       Cerebral infarction (+) Cerebral infarction (–)<br /></br>Obese (+)    a = 40       b = 30<br /></br>Obese (–)    c = 60       d = 70</p></br><p>Cognitive road-map for similar items<br /></br>• Step 1: Identify study design (case-control = OR).<br /></br>• Step 2: Label the 2 × 2 table correctly.<br /></br>• Step 3: Plug into OR formula = (a/c)/(b/d) = (a·d)/(b·c).</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Statistic Version)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Odds of exposure (obesity) among cases = a/c = 40/60.  </li></br><li>Odds of exposure among controls = b/d = 30/70.  </li></br><li>OR = (40/60) ÷ (30/70) ≈ (40 × 70)/(60 × 30) = 2800/1800 ≈ 1.6.<br /></br>   • OR &gt; 1 → obesity is associated with higher odds of cerebral infarction.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home: In case-control studies, you cannot calculate risk because incidence is preset by the investigator; you use odds of exposure instead.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (brief, for context)  </p></br><p>Obesity promotes atherosclerosis, hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation—all increase thromboembolic stroke risk. That link is what the investigator is quantifying via the OR.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Treatments / Diagnostics (contextual)  </p></br><p>Not applicable to the statistical calculation, but clinically, obesity modification (diet, exercise, bariatric surgery) is a primary prevention strategy for cerebrovascular disease.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“OR for caSe-control” → the S in “caSe” reminds you of the Slash in a/c and b/d: OR = (a/c)/(b/d).</p></br><p>Cross-product shortcut: OR = ad/bc.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. 30/60<br /></br>   • Ratio of controls exposed to cases unexposed; not a defined measure. Misses both odds and risk concepts.</p></br><p>b. 60/30<br /></br>   • Reverse of a. Simply flips numerator/denominator; still not an OR.</p></br><p>c. (30/60)/(70/40)<br /></br>   • Swaps rows and columns improperly (odds of NON-exposure among cases / odds of NON-exposure among controls). Gives 0.64, the inverse of the true OR—common trap.</p></br><p>e. (40/100)/(30/100)<br /></br>   • Uses ROW totals, effectively calculating the proportion of cases that are obese divided by the proportion of controls that are obese. That would be a risk ratio if this were a cohort study, but you cannot compute risk in a case-control design.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: forgetting that in case-control studies disease status is fixed by design, so risk ratios are inappropriate.</p></br><p>══════════════════════════════════<br /></br>Core Concept “One-Liner”  </p></br><p>In a case-control study, the odds ratio is the cross-product ratio: OR = (exposed cases ÷ unexposed cases) / (exposed controls ÷ unexposed controls) = ad/bc.</p>
131. A previously healthy 3-month-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 24-hour history of increasingly rapid breathing, difficulty feeding, and progressive fussiness. He has not had fever, but he has received acetaminophen to ease his fussiness. He receives no other medications. He is exclusively breast-fed. He is alert and pale and appears anxious. His pulse is 250/min, respirations are 68/min, and blood pressure is 80/50 mm Hg. He is not using accessory muscles of respiration. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 99 percent. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds. Examination shows pink lips and moist oral mucosa. The lungs are clear to auscultation. No murmurs or gallops are heard on cardiac examination. The precordium is hyperdynamic. The abdomen is soft. The liver edge is palpated 3 cm below the right costal margin, and the spleen tip is palpated 2 cm below the left costal margin. Distal pulses are palpable. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin      9 g/dL</br>Hematocrit      27 percent</br>Leukocyte count 8000/mm³</br>Platelet count  212,000/mm³</br>An ECG is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: G</br></br>a. Atrial flutter</br>b. Congenital hemolytic anemia</br>c. Iron deficiency</br>d. Myocarditis</br>e. Pericarditis</br>f. Prolonged QT syndrome</br>g. Supraventricular tachycardia</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: g. Supraventricular tachycardia (SVT)</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>• Red flags: heart rate 250/min (far above the normal infant range of 100-160/min; anything &gt;220/min in an infant is pathognomonic for SVT on exams), hyperdynamic precordium, hepatomegaly (3-cm liver edge) and splenomegaly (signs of right-sided congestion), but</br> – Clear lungs, normal oxygen saturation → not a primary pulmonary problem</br> – No fever → infection less likely</br>• “Increasingly rapid breathing/difficulty feeding/fussiness” in an infant = generic presentation of congestive heart failure (CHF). In babies, CHF can be precipitated either by structural heart disease or by a tachyarrhythmia that severely shortens diastole → ↓ ventricular filling → ↓ stroke volume → compensatory tachypnea &amp; hepatomegaly.<br /></br>• ECG (described, but classic for exam) would show a narrow-complex, regular rhythm at ~250/min with absent or retrograde P waves—classic SVT.<br /></br>Metacognitive pearl: On NBME/Step questions, once the stem gives you a continuous HR &gt;220/min in a well-oxygenated infant, think SVT first, then quickly rule out medication toxicity (e.g., β-agonist) or WPW if delta waves are shown.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step 1: Identify the emergency—A heart rate of 250/min in a 3-month-old is always abnormal.<br /></br>Step 2: Determine if rhythm is likely atrial or ventricular. Regular narrow-complex tachycardia strongly favors supraventricular origin.<br /></br>Step 3: Link physiology to findings. The high rate → ↓ diastolic filling time → ↓ forward flow → hepatic &amp; splenic congestion → hepatosplenomegaly and tachypnea. Clear lungs and normal oxygen saturation indicate that pulmonary edema has not yet developed, so examiners expect you to focus on the rhythm.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Persistent HR &gt;220/min in an infant ± CHF signs = SVT until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Most infant SVT is AV re-entrant (often via a concealed accessory pathway), occasionally AV nodal re-entrant.<br /></br>• Ectopic atrial foci and WPW are other causes.<br /></br>• Consequences: rapid ventricular response ↓ CO → sympathetic activation → tachypnea, diaphoresis, irritability; chronic episodes → cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics</br>Acute management:</br>1. If unstable (poor perfusion, hypotension) → synchronized cardioversion.<br /></br>2. If stable (this infant is borderline but perfusing) → vagal maneuvers (ice to face, carotid massage in older kids) → IV adenosine if vagal fails.<br /></br>Chronic: β-blocker (propranolol) or digoxin; accessory-pathway ablation in refractory cases.<br /></br>Work-up after stabilization: Echocardiogram for structural disease; Holter/event monitor.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>“SVT infants sprint &gt;220.” Picture a baby in a tiny running suit sprinting at 220 beats/min.<br /></br>Diastole = “filling time” → “No fill, no thrill” (↓ stroke volume) → CHF signs without pulmonary edema.</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>a. Atrial flutter<br /></br>• Usually saw-tooth flutter waves around 300/min with variable AV block → ventricular rate rarely exceeds 220 in infants. Would see P-wave morphology; question describes a regular rhythm at 250/min.  </p></br><p>b. Congenital hemolytic anemia<br /></br>• Would explain low Hb but not extreme HR of 250/min. Hemolysis labs (↑ retic, jaundice) absent. Anemia-induced high-output failure produces tachycardia ~160-180, not 250.  </p></br><p>c. Iron deficiency<br /></br>• Causes microcytic anemia at 6–24 mo when diet changes; less common in exclusively breast-fed 3-mo-old. Again, would not cause a narrow-complex tachycardia of 250/min.  </p></br><p>d. Myocarditis<br /></br>• Presents with respiratory distress, muffled heart sounds, S3, crackles, and often fever. ECG shows low voltage or ST-T changes, not rapid regular narrow-complex 250/min.  </p></br><p>e. Pericarditis<br /></br>• Causes muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus, chest pain; ECG with diffuse ST elevation &amp; PR depression. HR mildly elevated, not 250/min.  </p></br><p>f. Prolonged QT syndrome<br /></br>• Baseline HR normal; risk is torsades (polymorphic VT), an irregular wide-complex arrhythmia, not a sustained narrow-complex regular 250/min.  </p></br><p>Document in real life: rhythm strip, vitals, perfusion status, response to vagal maneuver/adenosine, hepatomegaly, discussion with cardiology, parental counseling.</p>
132. A 17-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after he was thrown through a window during an altercation. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. He is alert and fully oriented. He reports moderate pain in his right arm and hand. His pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 2 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows an oxygen saturation of 98 percent. Examination shows superficial abrasions over the right side of the face. A 4 × 4-cm glass shard protrudes from the right upper extremity 2 cm proximal to the medial epicondyle; the extremity is cool and pale, and radial and ulnar pulses are not palpable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Angiography of the right upper extremity</br>b. Duplex ultrasonography of the right upper extremity</br>c. Immediate removal of the foreign body in the emergency department</br>d. MR arteriography of the right upper extremity</br>e. Operative wound exploration</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E.
