Operative wound exploration  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases that tip you off:<br /></br>• “Glass shard protrudes … 2 cm proximal to the medial epicondyle” → penetrating injury directly over the brachial artery.<br /></br>• “Extremity is cool and pale, and radial and ulnar pulses are not palpable” → classic hard signs of acute arterial occlusion/vascular injury (pulselessness, pallor, poikilothermia).<br /></br>• “30 minutes after injury” and vitals are still stable → you still have a limb-threatening vascular emergency rather than imminent exsanguination.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no brisk hemorrhage, no neurologic deficit noted, but the absence of distal pulses is enough to categorize this as a hard sign.</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>Step 1: Does the patient have “hard signs” of vascular injury (pulsatile bleeding, expanding hematoma, bruit/thrill, distal ischemia, absent distal pulses)?<br /></br>– Yes → go straight to the OR.<br /></br>Step 2: Foreign body is still embedded → never pull it outside the OR; it may be tamponading a major vessel.<br /></br>Step 3: Imaging is only for “soft signs” (history of arterial bleeding now quiet, small stable hematoma, proximity to vessel, abnormal but present pulses).  </p></br><p>Bottom line: Hard signs = bypass imaging, do operative exploration and repair.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><ol></br><li>Mechanism: window glass → penetrating trauma over brachial artery.  </li></br><li>Hard signs present → limb is already ischemic (cool, pale, pulseless).  </li></br><li>Foreign body removal outside OR risks exsanguination.  </li></br><li>Definitive management requires vascular control, removal of glass under direct vision, thrombectomy or graft as needed, and fasciotomy if &gt;6 h ischemia.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient):<br /></br>• Time of onset of pulselessness, neuro status, capillary refill.<br /></br>• Doppler signals (none).<br /></br>• Decision making: “Patient displays hard signs of vascular injury; taken emergently to OR for exploration and repair without pre-op imaging.”</p></br><p>Take-home message: In penetrating extremity trauma with hard signs of vascular injury, immediate operative exploration is mandatory; imaging is reserved for indeterminate or soft-sign cases.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (High-Yield)  </p></br><p>• Brachial artery courses medial to the biceps tendon in the antecubital fossa; a laceration/occlusion here eliminates both radial &amp; ulnar pulses.<br /></br>• Ischemia timeline: irreversible muscle/nerve changes begin ≈6 h.<br /></br>• Retained foreign body may tamponade vessel; premature removal → massive hemorrhage.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (Exam Level)  </p></br><p>First-line treatment with hard signs:<br /></br>• OR exploration → vascular control proximal/distal, remove object, repair with end-to-end anastomosis or interposition saphenous vein graft.<br /></br>• Heparinization once proximal control achieved (unless contraindicated).<br /></br>• 4-compartment forearm fasciotomy if prolonged ischemia or high compartment pressures.</p></br><p>No pre-operative angiography/CT/MRA is needed when pulses are absent or there is active bleeding—these tests only delay definitive care.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“5 P’s + Spray” Mnemonic for Hard Signs:<br /></br>1. Pulseless<br /></br>2. Pallor<br /></br>3. Pain (out of proportion)<br /></br>4. Paresthesia / paralysis<br /></br>5. Poikilothermia (cool)<br /></br>“Spray” = spurting/pulsatile bleeding, expanding hematoma, bruit/thrill  </p></br><p>Hard signs = “OR TODAY.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Angiography of the right upper extremity<br /></br>• Appropriate when pulses present but other concerns (soft signs). Here it wastes critical ischemia time.</p></br><p>B. Duplex ultrasonography<br /></br>• Non-invasive screening, helpful for stable patients without hard signs. Would delay limb-saving surgery.</p></br><p>C. Immediate removal of the foreign body in the ED<br /></br>• Dangerous—object may be tamponading the artery; removal without vascular control could cause exsanguination.</p></br><p>D. MR arteriography<br /></br>• Excellent imaging but time-consuming, expensive, needs patient stability; contraindicated in an acute limb-threatening scenario.</p></br><p>E. Operative wound exploration<br /></br>• Correct—provides rapid vascular control, definitive repair, and safe removal of the glass shard.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: Hard signs of vascular injury in an extremity mandate immediate operative exploration; imaging is for soft-sign or equivocal cases only.</p>
133. A 58-year-old woman with osteoarthritis comes to the office because of a 1-week history of fatigue. She also has had difficulty at work climbing stairs and putting away office supplies in cabinets above shoulder level. She says that during this time her arthritis pain has worsened and has spread to her shoulders, hips, and upper legs. She attributes her fatigue to poor sleep because of aching of her arms and legs. During a routine examination 6 months ago, hypertension and hypercholesterolemia were diagnosed and she was started on chlorthalidone, simvastatin, aspirin, vitamin D, calcium, and a multivitamin. Her only additional medication is ibuprofen. The patient smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 30 years but quit 6 months ago in an attempt to improve her health after her sister had “a major heart attack.” At that time, the patient also stopped drinking alcoholic beverages and decreased her coffee consumption from 4 cups to 2 cups daily. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 73 kg (160 lb); BMI is 26 kg/m2. Vital signs are temperature 36.4°C (97.6°F), pulse 84/min, respirations 16/min, and blood pressure 122/78 mm Hg. The patient appears anxious but is not in acute distress. Musculoskeletal examination shows no edema, erythema, or deformity with minimal tenderness of all major joints. Active and passive range of motion is full. During passive range of motion testing, the patient reports pain where her upper arms and upper legs are grasped. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following studies is most likely to establish the diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Determination of erythrocyte sedimentation rate</br>b. Measurement of serum creatine kinase activity</br>c. Muscle biopsy</br>d. Nerve conduction studies</br>e. Serum rheumatoid factor assay</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Measurement of serum creatine kinase (CK) activity  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should immediately narrow your differential:<br /></br>1. Subacute (1-week) onset of diffuse, proximal muscle symptoms – “difficulty climbing stairs” and “putting away office supplies above shoulder level.”<br /></br>2. Generalized muscle tenderness on light palpation (pain when examiner grasps upper arms and thighs).<br /></br>3. Normal joint exam (full active &amp; passive ROM, no swelling or deformity) → points away from a primary arthropathy.<br /></br>4. New medication 6 months ago: simvastatin (a statin).<br /></br>5. No systemic inflammatory findings (normal temperature, no morning stiffness, no true joint swelling).  </p></br><p>Test‐taking “pattern”: middle-aged or older patient on a statin who develops proximal myalgias ± weakness → always think “statin-induced myopathy” → first step = obtain a CK level.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for similar questions:<br /></br>• Step 1: Decide “joint vs muscle vs nerve” origin of symptoms.<br /></br>• Step 2: Scan medication list for statins, corticosteroids, antimalarials, etc.<br /></br>• Step 3: If statin present and time course fits, order CK; if inflammatory features predominate without weakness, think ESR/CRP for polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why CK?<br /></br>• Statin myopathy spectrum: myalgia (normal CK) → myositis (↑CK) → rhabdomyolysis (CK &gt;10,000).<br /></br>• CK is inexpensive, rapid, and immediately guides management (drug discontinuation, possible hospitalization if markedly elevated or renal injury).<br /></br>• A markedly elevated CK would establish a muscle disease rather than joint disease and strongly implicates the statin in this clinical context.<br /></br>Documentation pearl: note muscle pain distribution, strength testing, current medications (dose &amp; duration), and plan to order CK with instructions to hold statin pending results.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: Any new diffuse, proximal muscle pain ± weakness in a patient on a statin → check CK first.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Statin-Induced Myopathy)  </p></br><p>Etiology: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors impair cholesterol synthesis in myocytes, disrupting sarcolemmal membranes and mitochondrial function.<br /></br>Risk factors: age &gt;40, female sex, low BMI, hepatic/renal impairment, hypothyroidism, high statin dose, CYP3A4 inhibitors (eg, macrolides, azoles, grapefruit juice).<br /></br>Clinical manifestations: symmetrical proximal myalgias, weakness, cramps; onset weeks to months after starting therapy.<br /></br>Lab: CK elevation proportional to severity; possible mild transaminase rise; myoglobinuria in rhabdo.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management Snapshot  </p></br><ol></br><li>Confirm CK elevation.  </li></br><li>Stop statin; hydrate aggressively if CK &gt;1000-5000 or myoglobinuria.  </li></br><li>Evaluate for secondary contributors (eg, hypothyroidism, drug interactions).  </li></br><li>Once CK normalizes, consider re-challenging with lower-potency statin or non-statin lipid-lowering therapy (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor).  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “S-tatin → S-ore muscles → S-end CK.”<br /></br>• Picture a STATue (statin) cracking and CK (creatine kinase) leaking out.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate – Useful for PMR, which causes pain &amp; stiffness (not weakness) in shoulder/hip girdles; ESR is typically &gt;40-50 mm/h. The patient’s difficulty climbing stairs likely stems from muscle dysfunction, not joint stiffness.  </p></br><p>c. Muscle biopsy – Gold standard for inflammatory myopathies (polymyositis, dermatomyositis) but invasive and not first-line when an easily reversible drug cause is suspected. Always check CK ± autoantibodies first.  </p></br><p>d. Nerve conduction studies – Evaluate peripheral neuropathies (e.g., diabetic, Guillain-Barré) characterized by distal sensory loss/weakness, not focal proximal myalgia. Exam shows no sensory deficits.  </p></br><p>e. Rheumatoid factor assay – Screens for rheumatoid arthritis, which presents with small-joint symmetric inflammatory arthritis (MCP, PIP, wrists) not diffuse muscle tenderness or proximal weakness.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>In any patient on a statin who develops new, symmetric proximal muscle pain or weakness, measurement of serum CK is the most appropriate initial step to establish statin-induced myopathy.</p>
134. A 37-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after his motorcycle ran into the back of a truck at high speed. He was wearing a helmet. On arrival, his neck is immobilized in a cervical collar, and intravenous fluids are being administered. Paramedics report that at the scene he appeared to be intoxicated. He is alert and talking. His breath smells of alcohol. His pulse is 130/min, respirations are 30/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Heart sounds are normal. The abdomen is distended and tender. There is blood at the urethral meatus. Ultrasonography shows blood in the abdomen. An x-ray of the pelvis shows fractures of the pubic rami and ischial and ilial fractures on the right. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Cystography</br>b. Intravenous pyelography</br>c. Retrograde urethrography</br>d. Placement of a urinary catheter</br>e. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (per key): c. Retrograde urethrography  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>1. High-energy pelvic trauma (motorcycle vs truck) → pelvic ring fractures on x-ray.<br /></br>2. Blood at the urethral meatus + inability to void (no catheter placed yet).<br /></br>3. Pelvic fracture + blood at meatus = classic red flag for posterior urethral injury.<br /></br>4. Key NBME rule: NEVER pass a Foley catheter when urethral injury is suspected; first prove the urethra is intact with retrograde urethrography (RUG).</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test day:<br /></br>• “Pelvic fracture + perineal/urethral blood or high-riding prostate” → think “Stop, do RUG.”<br /></br>• Leads to automatic elimination of “place a catheter” choice.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING (why RUG is next)<br /></br>Step 1: Mechanism &amp; injuries – pelvic ring fractures commonly shear the membranous (posterior) urethra.<br /></br>Step 2: Hard sign – blood at meatus = ≥95 % predictive of urethral injury.<br /></br>Step 3: Passing a Foley blindly can convert a partial tear into a complete disruption and worsen hemorrhage → contraindicated.<br /></br>Step 4: Retrograde urethrography is a quick, contrast-based study obtained in the trauma bay that delineates the urethral lumen.<br /></br>• If contrast flows freely into the bladder: no injury → Foley can be placed.<br /></br>• If contrast extravasates: definitive management is suprapubic catheter + later urethroplasty.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In pelvic trauma, RUG precedes any instrumentation of the urethra.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Blood noted at urethral meatus; suspect urethral injury. Deferred Foley. Ordered STAT retrograde urethrogram.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY HIGH-YIELD<br /></br>• Posterior urethral injury = disruption at the bulbomembranous junction due to pelvic ring shear forces.<br /></br>• Presents with: blood at meatus, inability to void, perineal/scrotal ecchymosis, high-riding prostate on DRE.<br /></br>• Anterior urethral injuries (saddle straddle) present with perineal bruising but usually without pelvic fracture.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY<br /></br>1. Immediate: Retrograde urethrography.<br /></br>2. If intact → Foley catheter, assess urine output.<br /></br>3. If injured → place suprapubic catheter, consult urology for delayed repair.<br /></br>4. Concurrent intra-abdominal bleeding (FAST already positive) will be managed in parallel by trauma surgery; RUG does not delay laparotomy because it is rapid (&lt;5 min).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>Mnemonic “Blood on the Pee Hole = Picture the Pee Hole”<br /></br>• Blood on meatus → Take a picture (RUG) before touching.  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a STOP sign covering the Foley tray whenever you see blood at the meatus in a pelvic fracture vignette.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>INCORRECT ANSWER QUICK REVIEW<br /></br>a. Cystography – Evaluates bladder injury; done only after urethra proved intact (RUG first).<br /></br>b. Intravenous pyelography – Outdated for renal/ureteral trauma; does not evaluate urethra and takes longer.<br /></br>d. Placement of a urinary catheter – Contraindicated until urethral injury ruled out; could worsen tear.<br /></br>e. Diagnostic peritoneal lavage – Helpful for occult intra-abdominal bleeding, but the FAST is already positive and patient is hemodynamically borderline. Pelvic fracture with urethral blood is a more urgent, specific issue to address first to avoid iatrogenic harm.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: In any trauma patient, blood at the urethral meatus mandates retrograde urethrography PRIOR to catheterization.</p>
135. A 25-year-old woman comes to the emergency department because of nausea, diarrhea, and anxiety for 2 days. She also has noticed dizziness and tingling sensations shooting down her forearms. Her sleep and appetite have been normal. She has a 3-year history of social phobia well controlled for the past year with paroxetine. She is attending an out-of-town conference and forgot to pack her medication; her last dose was 3 days ago. She does not drink alcohol or use illicit drugs. She is 163 cm (5 ft 4 in) tall and weighs 80 kg (176 lb); BMI is 30 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 110/75 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no other abnormalities. Mental status examination shows an irritable mood with a full range of affect. Her speech is of normal rate and rhythm. There is no evidence of suicidal ideation or hallucinations. She can recall three objects after 5 minutes. Long-term memory is intact. Serum studies show no traces of paroxetine. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Begin behavioral therapy</br>b. Begin alprazolam therapy</br>c. Begin bupropion therapy</br>d. Begin clomipramine therapy</br>e. Begin gabapentin therapy</br>f. Resume paroxetine therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Resume paroxetine therapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point to SSRI discontinuation syndrome<br /></br>• “Forgot to pack her medication; her last dose was 3 days ago” – abrupt cessation.<br /></br>• Paroxetine specifically – shortest half-life of the common SSRIs and therefore the most likely to cause withdrawal-type symptoms if stopped suddenly.<br /></br>• Symptom cluster: nausea, diarrhea, dizziness, “electric-shock” paresthesias down the arms, anxiety/irritability, and insomnia. These are the classic F-L-U-S-H symptoms (Flu-like, Light-headed, Uneasy sleep, Sensory disturbances, Headache).<br /></br>• Normal vitals and physical exam except for nonspecific neurovegetative complaints → rules out acute infectious, neurologic, or cardiovascular etiologies.<br /></br>• Serum “no traces of paroxetine” confirms she has been off the drug long enough for plasma concentrations to fall below detection, consistent with a half-life of ≈24 hr.  </p></br><p>Cognitive strategy for similar items<br /></br>1. Note an SSRI in the medication list.<br /></br>2. Check timeline since last dose (usually 2–5 days).<br /></br>3. Look for the FLUSH mnemonic symptoms.<br /></br>4. Management = restart the same SSRI (or switch to fluoxetine) and taper slowly.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>• Core concept: SSRI discontinuation syndrome occurs when short half-life SSRIs (paroxetine, sertraline, fluvoxamine, venlafaxine) are stopped abruptly.<br /></br>• First-line management is to resume the SSRI and arrange a gradual taper (or substitute fluoxetine, then taper). This treats symptoms within 24–48 hr and prevents recurrence.<br /></br>• Document: medication non-adherence, withdrawal symptoms, informed discussion about taper plan, follow-up for social phobia control.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Paroxetine has:<br /></br>– t½ ≈ 24 hr, no active metabolites → plasma levels plummet quickly.<br /></br>– Sudden serotonin receptor down-regulation reversal leads to dysautonomia (GI upset), disequilibrium (dizziness), and sensory disturbances (“brain zaps”).<br /></br>Risk factors: high dose, longer duration of therapy, short-acting agent, missed ≥48 hr.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><ol></br><li>Restart paroxetine at the previous full dose; symptoms usually abate in &lt;48 hr.  </li></br><li>Taper by 10–25% every 1–2 wk depending on tolerability.  </li></br><li>If frequent travel/forgetfulness: switch to fluoxetine 20 mg daily (t½ 4–6 days, active metabolite norfluoxetine t½ 9 days) then taper.  </li></br><li>Labs/imaging are generally unnecessary unless another diagnosis is suspected.  </li></br></ol></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PARoxetine PARTS ways poorly” – among SSRIs, paroxetine causes the worst withdrawal.<br /></br>FLUSH mnemonic for discontinuation:<br /></br>• Flu-like symptoms<br /></br>• Light-headedness<br /></br>• Uneasy sleep<br /></br>• Sensory disturbances (“zaps”)<br /></br>• Headache  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Begin behavioral therapy – Helpful long-term for social phobia but does nothing for acute withdrawal symptoms.  </p></br><p>b. Begin alprazolam therapy – A benzodiazepine could transiently relieve anxiety but will not address GI, dizziness, paresthesias, and risks dependence.  </p></br><p>c. Begin bupropion therapy – NDRI lacks serotonergic effect; would not relieve discontinuation syndrome and could exacerbate anxiety.  </p></br><p>d. Begin clomipramine therapy – A TCA with potent serotonergic action but slower onset, higher side-effect/toxicity profile, and unnecessary when the original SSRI can simply be restarted.  </p></br><p>e. Begin gabapentin therapy – Sometimes used off-label for anxiety or neuropathic pain, but it will not correct serotonin withdrawal physiology.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Missing a few doses of short-acting SSRIs such as paroxetine commonly leads to SSRI discontinuation syndrome. The definitive and immediate fix is simply to resume the SSRI and then taper gradually.</p>
136. A 72-year-old man with lung cancer comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of weakness in his legs. He also has had dry mouth, constipation, and tingling of his hands and feet during this period. He received radiation therapy 3 weeks ago and is currently undergoing chemotherapy. He has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 50 years. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 126/84 mm Hg. There is mild bilateral ptosis, which does not worsen after upward gaze for 1 minute. Ocular movements are full. Oral mucosa is dry. There is mild weakness of hip flexion bilaterally. After several attempts, he can rise from a chair with his arms folded across his chest. Deep tendon reflexes are absent. Sensation is intact. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. X-rays of the lumbosacral spine</br>b. Bone scan</br>c. CT scan of the pelvis</br>d. Quadriceps muscle biopsy</br>e. Repetitive nerve stimulation</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Repetitive nerve stimulation</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – how the stem steers you to Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS)</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Malignancy association<br /></br>   • “Lung cancer” (especially small-cell) is the classic neoplasm linked to LEMS.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Proximal, lower-extremity-predominant weakness<br /></br>   • “Weakness in his legs … mild weakness of hip flexion … difficulty rising from a chair” = proximal hip girdle involvement typical of LEMS.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Autonomic dysfunction<br /></br>   • “Dry mouth, constipation” – hallmark autonomic features in LEMS, NOT seen in myasthenia gravis (MG).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Hyporeflexia/areflexia<br /></br>   • “Deep tendon reflexes are absent.” Loss of reflexes is characteristic of LEMS; MG has normal reflexes.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Ocular signs minimal and non-fatigable<br /></br>   • “Mild bilateral ptosis, which does not worsen after upward gaze for 1 minute.” MG classically shows fatigable ptosis; LEMS ocular signs are mild or absent.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Sensation intact<br /></br>   • Rules out peripheral neuropathy or spinal cord pathology.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive tip:  When you see (malignancy + proximal leg weakness &gt; arm weakness + areflexia + autonomic symptoms), think LEMS first.  Reflex status and autonomic clues are the biggest differentials from MG.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning &amp; Why E is Correct</p></br><p>LEMS = antibodies against presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels → ↓ ACh release.<br /></br>Electrodiagnostic hallmark: With repetitive (high-frequency) nerve stimulation you see a progressively <strong>incremental</strong> (&gt;100%) increase in compound muscle action potential amplitude (“facilitation”). This test both diagnoses and distinguishes LEMS from MG (which shows a decremental response).</p></br><p>Thus, repetitive nerve stimulation is the confirmatory bedside/electrophysiologic test.</p></br><p>Take-home core concept: When a paraneoplastic syndrome of proximal weakness and autonomic findings is suspected, the definitive step is electrodiagnostic testing—specifically repetitive stimulation (or single-fiber EMG) demonstrating post-tetanic facilitation.</p></br><p>Documentation if this were a real patient: record distribution of weakness, reflex status, autonomic symptoms, cancer history, and results of electrodiagnostic testing; inform oncology for tumor control.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Brief Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Etiology: Auto-Ab to presynaptic P/Q Ca²⁺ channels (paraneoplastic &gt; idiopathic).<br /></br>• Mechanism: ↓ Ca²⁺ influx → ↓ vesicular ACh release → impaired neuromuscular transmission that <em>improves</em> with repeated activity (Ca²⁺ builds up).<br /></br>• Clinical: Proximal lower-extremity weakness, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth, constipation, impotence), hyporeflexia/areflexia; mild ocular or bulbar signs.<br /></br>• Labs: Anti-VGCC antibodies (supportive).<br /></br>• Electrophysiology: Low baseline CMAP amplitude; &gt;100% increment with rapid stimulation.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls</p></br><ol></br><li>Treat underlying malignancy (chemo/radiation for SCLC) – often improves neuromuscular symptoms.  </li></br><li>Symptomatic:<br /></br>   • 3,4-diaminopyridine (first-line): prolongs presynaptic depolarization → ↑ Ca²⁺ influx.<br /></br>   • Guanidine (less used).<br /></br>   • Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (pyridostigmine) – modest benefit.  </li></br><li>Immunomodulation when cancer absent or controlled: corticosteroids, azathioprine, IVIG, plasmapheresis.</li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“LEMS = Lower-body, Lack of reflexes, Lung cancer, autonomic Loss of moisture.”<br /></br>Mnemonic FACILITATE:<br /></br>F – Facilitates with use<br /></br>A – Autonomic symptoms<br /></br>C – Ca²⁺ channel antibodies<br /></br>I – Incremental response on stim<br /></br>L – Lung cancer (small-cell)<br /></br>I – Initial leg weakness<br /></br>T – Tendon reflexes low<br /></br>A – Ach release low<br /></br>T – Treat tumor<br /></br>E – Electrophysiology confirms</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong</p></br><p>A. X-ray of lumbosacral spine<br /></br>   • Would evaluate structural spine disease causing radiculopathy; would not explain autonomic symptoms or generalized areflexia, and would not confirm LEMS.</p></br><p>B. Bone scan<br /></br>   • Detects osseous metastases or fractures; no relevance for neuromuscular junction disorder.</p></br><p>C. CT scan of the pelvis<br /></br>   • Helpful for pelvic masses but does not confirm a neuromuscular transmission defect.</p></br><p>D. Quadriceps muscle biopsy<br /></br>   • Useful for myopathies (e.g., polymyositis, inclusion-body myositis). LEMS is a <strong>neuromuscular junction</strong> disorder; biopsy would be normal.</p></br><p>Key distinguishing errors: all imaging/biopsy choices look for structural or myopathic etiologies; none test neuromuscular transmission which is the core defect here. Repetitive nerve stimulation uniquely demonstrates the pathognomonic facilitation pattern.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>In an older smoker with new proximal leg weakness, areflexia, and autonomic symptoms, think Lambert-Eaton. Confirm with repetitive nerve stimulation showing post-tetanic facilitation.</p>
137. A 37-year-old man with end-stage Duchenne muscular dystrophy is scheduled for discharge from the hospital after treatment for pneumonia. He has been admitted to the hospital four times over the past 6 months for recurrent pneumonia. Over the past 3 years, he has received mechanical ventilation and has used a wheelchair. His life expectancy is less than 6 months. The patient tells the physician that he is "tired of living like this" and requests a do-not-resuscitate order with no further antibiotics. He has been considering this decision for 3 weeks but has not discussed it with his family. On mental status examination, he shows sadness about his health but has a normal thought process. He expresses no ambivalence about his decision. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Contact the hospital ethics committee</br>b. Determine if the patient wants to discuss his decision with anyone else</br>c. Schedule a meeting with the patient and his family</br>d. Transfer the patient to a skilled nursing care facility</br>e. Obtain psychiatric assessment</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Determine if the patient wants to discuss his decision with anyone else</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “End-stage Duchenne muscular dystrophy … life expectancy &lt; 6 months” – terminal illness.<br /></br>• “He is ‘tired of living like this’ and requests a DNR order with no further antibiotics” – explicit request to limit life-sustaining treatment.<br /></br>• “Considering this decision for 3 weeks … not discussed with his family.” – decision seems deliberate but family is unaware.<br /></br>• Mental-status exam: normal thought process, sadness is appropriate, no ambivalence – establishes decision-making capacity.  </p></br><p>How to approach a similar stem<br /></br>1. Verify decision-making capacity (understands, appreciates, reasons, and communicates).<br /></br>2. Elicit the patient’s goals and preferences.<br /></br>3. Ask whether and how he wants others involved.<br /></br>4. Document and implement the plan.  </p></br><p>The stem confirms Step 1; the correct next step is Step 3 – “Would you like to involve or inform your family or anyone else about this decision?”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Autonomy: A capacitated adult has the right to accept or refuse any treatment, including antibiotics and CPR.<br /></br>• Shared decision-making: Physicians should offer (not impose) the opportunity to include family, clergy, or other supporters.<br /></br>• No further medical, psychiatric, or ethics consultation is needed unless capacity is in doubt or conflict arises.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>When a capacitated patient requests a treatment limitation, honor the request, but first ask whether he wishes others to be involved in the discussion or documentation.</p></br><p>EHR documentation (real-life note)<br /></br>– Capacity assessment (intact).<br /></br>– Patient’s stated wishes: DNR, no antibiotics for future pneumonias.<br /></br>– Offer to involve family/support system; patient’s response.<br /></br>– Plan to complete advance directives/physician orders for life-sustaining treatment (POLST/MOLST).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (why this situation arises)  </p></br><p>Duchenne muscular dystrophy → progressive respiratory muscle weakness → recurrent pneumonias and ventilator dependence → poor prognosis. As quality of life declines, many patients reevaluate aggressive care.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>• No new diagnostics required.<br /></br>• Palliative focus: symptom control, advance care planning, hospice referral once goals are clarified.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CAPA” for end-of-life talks<br /></br>C – Capacity confirmed<br /></br>A – Assess goals (what matters?)<br /></br>P – People to involve?<br /></br>A – Action plan (orders, forms)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>A. Contact the hospital ethics committee<br /></br>   – Ethics consult is for conflicts or uncertainty. Here, the patient’s request is ethically straightforward; no dispute exists.</p></br><p>C. Schedule a meeting with the patient and his family<br /></br>   – Family meetings are helpful, but only if the patient consents. Automatically involving family without asking violates autonomy.</p></br><p>D. Transfer the patient to a skilled nursing facility<br /></br>   – Disposition is separate from his expressed desire to limit life-sustaining treatment and does not address the DNR request.</p></br><p>E. Obtain psychiatric assessment<br /></br>   – Sadness ≠ depression. He demonstrates capacity and no suicidal intent; psychiatric evaluation is unnecessary.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A competent adult’s end-of-life wishes should be respected. After confirming capacity, the physician’s next move is to ask whether the patient wants anyone else included before finalizing directives.</p>
138. A 12-year-old boy is brought to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. He received the diagnosis of chronic renal failure secondary to glomerulonephritis 2 years ago. He has hypertension controlled with an ACE inhibitor. He is at the 10th percentile for height and weight. Examination shows mild edema of the lower extremities. His mother asks about ways to minimize complications of his renal failure. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Increase his intake of fluids</br>b. Increase his intake of potassium</br>c. Restrict his intake of calories</br>d. Restrict his intake of phosphorus</br>e. Begin monthly injections of vitamin B12 (cobalamin)</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Restrict his intake of phosphorus  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>“Chronic renal failure × 2 years” → progressive loss of glomerular filtration → typical metabolic derangements (↑PO₄³⁻, ↓1-α-hydroxylase activity, ↓Ca²⁺, secondary hyperparathyroidism).  </li></br><li>“12-year-old, 10th percentile for height/weight, mild lower-extremity edema” → growth delay and early renal osteodystrophy already beginning.  </li></br><li>Mother asks “ways to minimize complications” → question is about long-term, modifiable measures, not acute therapy.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: In any chronic kidney disease (CKD) stem, scan for clues pointing to the three big chronic complications—(1) bone/mineral disorders, (2) anemia, (3) electrolyte/volume overload—and match each to its preventive intervention (phosphate restriction/binders + vitamin D, EPO/iron, Na⁺/fluid limitation). Here, bone/mineral disorder is highlighted by growth failure.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why phosphorus restriction?)  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. CKD → ↓GFR → phosphate retention.<br /></br>2. Hyperphosphatemia binds calcium and suppresses renal 1-α-hydroxylase → ↓1,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D → hypocalcemia.<br /></br>3. Hypocalcemia + high phosphate → chronic stimulation of parathyroid gland → secondary hyperparathyroidism → renal osteodystrophy (bone pain, fractures, growth retardation).<br /></br>4. First-line preventive measure = dietary phosphate restriction (complemented by oral phosphate binders and active vitamin D analogs).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Controlling serum phosphate is the cornerstone of preventing bone disease in pediatric CKD.  </p></br><p>Documentation note: Record discussion of renal diet emphasizing low-phosphorus foods (limit dairy, colas, processed meats) and plan for periodic serum Ca²⁺/PO₄³⁻/PTH monitoring.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Normal kidney: excretes excess phosphate and converts 25-OH-vitamin D to 1,25-(OH)₂-vitamin D.<br /></br>• CKD: Phosphate retention → high PO₄³⁻. The calcium-phosphate product rises → Ca²⁺ precipitates into tissues, lowering free serum Ca²⁺.<br /></br>• Low Ca²⁺ + low calcitriol → parathyroid hyperplasia and ↑PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism). PTH mobilizes Ca²⁺ from bone → osteitis fibrosa cystica, bone pain, stunted growth in children.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Diet/Lifestyle<br /></br>• Low-phosphorus diet (&lt;800 mg/day in children).<br /></br>• Adequate protein and calories to support growth.  </p></br><p>Pharmacologic<br /></br>• Calcium-based or non-calcium phosphate binders with meals (calcium acetate, sevelamer).<br /></br>• Active vitamin D analogs (calcitriol) to suppress PTH.<br /></br>• Monitor serum Ca²⁺, PO₄³⁻, alkaline phosphatase, and PTH every 3–6 months.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“CKD bones HATE Phosphate”<br /></br>H – High phosphate<br /></br>A – Active vitamin D falls<br /></br>T – Trigger ↑PTH<br /></br>E – Eats away bone  </p></br><p>Therefore, “HATE P” → Hate Phosphate → Restrict phosphate.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Increase fluid intake<br /></br>• CKD patients often require sodium/fluid restriction to control hypertension and edema. Extra fluids would worsen volume overload and edema.  </p></br><p>B. Increase potassium intake<br /></br>• Kidneys excrete K⁺; CKD predisposes to hyperkalemia. Recommendation is to RESTRICT, not increase, dietary potassium.  </p></br><p>C. Restrict caloric intake<br /></br>• This child is already small (10th percentile). Caloric restriction would worsen growth failure and malnutrition. CKD patients actually need adequate (often increased) calories and protein.  </p></br><p>E. Monthly vitamin B₁₂ injections<br /></br>• B₁₂ deficiency is unrelated to renal failure; deficiency arises from pernicious anemia, malabsorption, or strict vegan diet. CKD complications center on mineral bone disease, anemia of EPO deficiency (treated with erythropoietin, not B₁₂), and electrolyte disturbances.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>In pediatric chronic kidney disease, the most effective dietary measure to prevent renal osteodystrophy and growth retardation is restriction of dietary phosphorus, combined with phosphate binders and vitamin D analogs.</p>
139. A 72-year-old woman with a 30-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus comes to the physician because of pain and swelling of her left foot since a blister on the bottom of her left foot broke 2 weeks ago. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F). Examination shows swelling and erythema of the left distal leg, ankle, and foot; there is a 2-cm ulcer at the base of the heel with an overlying black eschar. Peripheral pulses are decreased bilaterally. Capillary refill time is greater than 3 seconds. The ulcer is debrided, and a sterile probe is inserted until the base touches bone. Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Mixed aerobic and anaerobic organisms</br>b. Mixed anaerobic organisms</br>c. Pseudomonas aeruginosa</br>d. Staphylococcus aureus</br>e. Staphylococcus epidermidis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Mixed aerobic and anaerobic organisms  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Long-standing diabetes (30 yrs) → sensory neuropathy + peripheral vascular disease → high-risk foot.<br /></br>• Blister that “broke” 2 wks ago → now ulcer 2 cm with black eschar = chronic, necrotic wound.<br /></br>• Positive “probe-to-bone” test → highly specific for contiguous-spread osteomyelitis.<br /></br>• Swelling/erythema of entire distal leg but T = 37.2 °C → often only low-grade or absent fever in diabetic foot infections.<br /></br>• Decreased pulses &amp; capillary refill &gt;3 s → ischemia → favors polymicrobial growth, incl. anaerobes.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Identify diabetic foot ulcer.<br /></br>2. Ask: hematogenous vs contiguous osteomyelitis? (Here, contiguous from ulcer.)<br /></br>3. Recall causative organisms for each scenario; contiguous = polymicrobial (mixed aerobic + anaerobic).<br /></br>4. Eliminate distractors that fit other classic scenarios (see below).  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Contiguous-focus osteomyelitis from a chronic ischemic/neuropathic ulcer is characteristically polymicrobial because:<br /></br>• Surface is colonized by skin flora (S. aureus, β-hemolytic streptococci).<br /></br>• Repeated dressing changes introduce gram-negative rods.<br /></br>• Ischemic necrosis creates an anaerobic environment favoring Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus, Clostridium spp.<br /></br>Therefore, “mixed aerobic and anaerobic organisms” best matches the microbiology.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>Diabetic foot ulcers → chronic, polymicrobial (aerobic + anaerobic) infection with risk of contiguous osteomyelitis.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record ulcer grade, probe-to-bone result, vascular status, and plan for broad-spectrum empiric antibiotics plus imaging/possible debridement.  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>1. Neuropathy → loss of protective sensation → repetitive trauma/blisters.<br /></br>2. Peripheral arterial disease → poor perfusion → impaired wound healing, promotes anaerobes.<br /></br>3. Chronic open wound → bacterial colonization progresses to deep soft-tissue infection and then bone involvement.  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Review<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Plain radiograph → cortical destruction, but MRI is most sensitive for osteomyelitis.<br /></br>• ESR/CRP often elevated; probe-to-bone positive is highly specific.<br /></br>Treatment<br /></br>• Empiric IV broad-spectrum covering gram-positives (vancomycin), gram-negatives including Pseudomonas (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime), and anaerobes (metronidazole or β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor) until culture-guided.<br /></br>• Surgical debridement/revascularization as needed; glycemic control and off-loading pressure.  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Diabetic FOOT = Full Of Organisms Together” → think mixed flora (aerobes + anaerobes).<br /></br>Quick chart:<br /></br>• Hematogenous kids/adults → S. aureus.<br /></br>• Sickle cell → Salmonella.<br /></br>• Puncture through rubber shoe → Pseudomonas.<br /></br>• Decubitus/diabetic ulcer → Polymicrobial.  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Mixed anaerobic organisms<br /></br>– Pure anaerobes alone are uncommon; aerobes are almost always present.<br /></br>C. Pseudomonas aeruginosa<br /></br>– Classic for puncture wounds through rubber-soled shoes or nail in foot; less common in chronic ischemic ulcers.<br /></br>D. Staphylococcus aureus<br /></br>– #1 overall cause of osteomyelitis but typically hematogenous spread or acute penetrating trauma, not chronic ischemic diabetic ulcers (which are polymicrobial).<br /></br>E. Staphylococcus epidermidis<br /></br>– Associated with prosthetic-device infections or post-operative osteomyelitis, not chronic diabetic foot ulcers.  </p></br><p>–––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</p>
140. A 13-year-old girl is brought to the physician because her mother is concerned that her daughter has never had a menstrual period. She is at the 80th percentile for height and 75th percentile for weight. Breast and pubic hair development is Tanner stage 2. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Reexamination in 1 year</br>b. Pelvic examination</br>c. Karyotype analysis</br>d. Measurement of serum estradiol concentration</br>e. Transvaginal ultrasonography</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Re-examination in 1 year  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• Age 13 y (still within normal range for onset of menses).<br /></br>• Height 80th %ile, weight 75th %ile → normal growth; no short stature or chronic disease clues.<br /></br>• Tanner stage 2 breasts and pubic hair → only EARLY pubertal development (thelarche = first sign, usually stage 2).<br /></br>• No other abnormalities on exam.  </p></br><p>Why these clues matter<br /></br>1. Menarche normally occurs about 2–2.5 years after thelarche, typically at Tanner stage 4.<br /></br>2. Work-up for primary amenorrhea is indicated only if:<br /></br>   • No menses by age 15 y (regardless of Tanner stage) OR<br /></br>   • No menses ≥3 y after thelarche OR<br /></br>   • Lack of any secondary sexual characteristics by 13 y.  </p></br><p>This patient is only one year into puberty (Tanner 2) and is younger than 15 y; therefore she has not yet met criteria for evaluation.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>“Clock + Tanner stage” framework:<br /></br>• Ask: Does she have breasts? Yes → puberty started.<br /></br>• Next: How long since breast buds? &lt;3 y.<br /></br>• Age cutoff 15 y not reached → reassure &amp; observe.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Confirm normal pubertal progression (breast buds present).<br /></br>2. Compare with standard timing of menarche (Tanner 4; avg age 12.5 y).<br /></br>3. Apply guideline triggers for work-up → none met.<br /></br>4. Best management = watchful waiting; schedule follow-up in ~1 year.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Do NOT launch a primary-amenorrhea work-up in a healthy 13-year-old who is still early in pubertal development; simply monitor.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Pubertal development Tanner II, no galactorrhea, thyroid nl, no dysmorphic features. Plan: anticipatory guidance, RTC in 12 mo or sooner if menses begin.”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>Gonadarche (thelarche) → estrogen from maturing ovaries.<br /></br>Adrenarche (pubic/axillary hair) → adrenal androgens.<br /></br>Menarche follows once the HPO (hypothalamic–pituitary–ovarian) axis cycles regularly. This calibration takes ~2 years; early menses would be unexpected at Tanner 2.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>No tests indicated now. If criteria were met later:<br /></br>• First test: pregnancy test.<br /></br>• ± FSH/LH, prolactin, TSH, pelvic US, karyotype depending on breast development &amp; FSH level.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“1-3-5 Rule” for primary amenorrhea<br /></br>1. No secondary sex by 13.<br /></br>3. ≥3 years after thelarche.<br /></br>5. No menses by 15.<br /></br>If none → wait.  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a traffic light. Age 13 &amp; Tanner 2 = yellow light (proceed with caution, not red). Age 15 or 3 y post-thelarche = red light → full work-up.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. Pelvic examination<br /></br>• Indicated for structural anomalies (imperforate hymen, Mullerian agenesis) IF normal secondary characteristics AND age criteria met or cyclic pain. Not needed now; invasive and anxiety-provoking.  </p></br><p>C. Karyotype analysis<br /></br>• Used when gonadal dysgenesis suspected (e.g., Turner 45,X) → would have short stature, streak ovaries, minimal breast development. Not the case here.  </p></br><p>D. Measurement of serum estradiol<br /></br>• Used to assess ovarian estrogen production when amenorrhea with absent or minimal breast development. Her breast buds prove estrogen is present.  </p></br><p>E. Transvaginal ultrasonography<br /></br>• Helpful to visualize uterus/ovaries in suspected Mullerian agenesis or PCOS, but timing is premature and transvaginal approach is inappropriate in non-sexually-active early teen.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A healthy 13-year-old at Tanner stage 2 without menarche warrants reassurance and follow-up, not an immediate diagnostic work-up.</p>
141. A 27-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-week history of intermittent substernal chest pain. She states that the pain is worsened by swallowing solids or liquids. She has a 10-year history of intravenous drug use. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Examination shows poor dentition and multiple 1- to 2-cm lymph nodes in the cervical and inguinal regions. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. PPD skin test</br>b. ECG</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. CT scan of the chest</br>e. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br>f. 24-Hour esophageal pH monitoring</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test‐Taking Strategy</br>1. Key phrases that immediately narrow the differential<br /></br>   • “Substernal chest pain worsened by swallowing solids or liquids” → odynophagia/dysphagia of esophageal origin, not cardiac.<br /></br>   • “2-week history” → acute to subacute process (eg, infectious or inflammatory), not long-standing reflux stricture or malignancy.<br /></br>   • Risk factors: 10-year IV drug use, poor dentition, generalized lymphadenopathy → red flags for immunocompromise (possible HIV) and opportunistic infections such as Candida, HSV, CMV.<br /></br>2. Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Afebrile, normal cardiac exam → less likely endocarditis or myocarditis.<br /></br>   • No exertional component, no radiation to arm/jaw → less likely ischemic heart disease.<br /></br>3. Cognitive road map for “pain on swallowing” questions<br /></br>   a. Decide esophageal vs. cardiac vs. pulmonary.<br /></br>   b. If clearly esophageal: first test is direct visualization (EGD) unless patient only has classic reflux symptoms without alarm features.<br /></br>   c. EGD rules in/out infectious, pill-induced, caustic, erosive, malignant, or structural disease and allows biopsy + culture—crucial in an immunocompromised host.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• Core concept: Odynophagia (pain with swallowing) in an immunocompromised or high-risk patient mandates prompt endoscopic evaluation to visualize and, if necessary, biopsy the esophagus.<br /></br>• Step-wise approach:<br /></br>  1. Identify symptom pattern (pain triggered by swallowing; both solids &amp; liquids from onset → mucosal process vs motility).<br /></br>  2. Recognize risk for infectious esophagitis.<br /></br>  3. Select the diagnostic modality that confirms diagnosis and guides treatment in one session = EGD.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient encounter)<br /></br>• Duration, character, aggravating factors, weight loss, GI bleeding, nutritional status.<br /></br>• Risk factors for HIV, CD4 count (if known), current medications (e.g., steroids).<br /></br>• Plan: “Will obtain EGD with biopsy/culture to evaluate for infectious or erosive esophagitis; HIV testing offered.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review</br>Infectious esophagitis<br /></br>• Candida albicans (most common): white plaques, friable mucosa.<br /></br>• HSV-1: small vesicles → punched-out ulcers.<br /></br>• CMV: linear, shallow ulcers.<br /></br>Risk factors = immunosuppression (HIV, steroids, chemotherapy), severe malnutrition, broad-spectrum antibiotics.</p></br><p>Painful swallowing = mucosal ulceration; involves both solids &amp; liquids because lumen is not mechanically narrowed (contrast with progressive solid-then-liquid dysphagia in cancer).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot</br>1. EGD (preferred): diagnostic &amp; therapeutic (can perform brushing, biopsy, and start topical therapy).<br /></br>2. If Candida visualized: empiric oral fluconazole 200 mg × 1, then 100 mg daily × 14 days while awaiting culture.<br /></br>3. If viral ulcerations: biopsy/brushings with immunostaining; treat HSV with acyclovir, CMV with ganciclovir.<br /></br>4. Additional work-up: HIV testing, CD4 count, blood cultures if systemic infection suspected.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</br>“Pain when you SWALLOW? Go LOOK first.”<br /></br>SWALLOW = Suspected esophageal lesion → LOOK = endoscopy.</p></br><p>Another quick mnemonic for infectious esophagitis appearance:<br /></br>“Can’t Have Cold Smoothies”<br /></br>C = Candida (white)<br /></br>H = HSV (Herpetic punched-out)<br /></br>C = CMV (Circular/linear)<br /></br>S = Smoothies remind you of immuno-Suppressed</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. PPD skin test<br /></br>   • Screens for latent TB; has no role in acute odynophagia work-up.</p></br><p>b. ECG<br /></br>   • Chest pain here is clearly provoked by swallowing, making ischemia unlikely. ECG would not evaluate odynophagia.</p></br><p>c. Echocardiography<br /></br>   • Indicated for suspected endocarditis (IV drug user + fever, murmur). Patient is afebrile, normal cardiac exam, no septic emboli signs.</p></br><p>d. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>   • Helpful for structural mediastinal pathology (mass, perforation) but lower yield for mucosal disease; lacks ability to biopsy or treat.</p></br><p>f. 24-hour esophageal pH monitoring<br /></br>   • Gold standard for GERD when diagnosis uncertain after trial of PPIs, but not first-line with alarm features (painful swallowing, possible infection).</p></br><p>Common Pitfall<br /></br>Students often jump to cardiac tests when they see the words “substernal chest pain.” Always anchor to the modifiers—in this case “worsened by swallowing”—to avoid unnecessary work-up.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Take-Home Message</br>Odynophagia or acute dysphagia in a patient at risk for infection demands immediate esophagogastroduodenoscopy to visualize and biopsy the esophageal mucosa; it is both diagnostic and guides targeted therapy.</p>
142. A healthy 16-year-old girl is brought to the physician for an examination prior to participation in school sports. She has no history of serious illness, and she is doing well in school. Menarche occurred at the age of 12 years; menses occur at regular 28-day intervals. Her mother and maternal grandmother have hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. The patient appears well. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 75 kg (165 lb); BMI is 27 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 96/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no exophthalmos or lid lag. The thyroid gland is moderately enlarged and firm, but no nodules are palpable. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. The extremities are warm and well perfused. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+, but the Achilles relaxation phase is delayed. Serum studies show:  </br>Thyroid-stimulating hormone            56 µU/mL  </br>Thyroxine (T4)                         6 µg/dL  </br>Antithyroid peroxidase antibodies       >1:1000 (N<1:20)  </br>Thyroglobulin antibodies                1:500 (N<1:20)  </br>Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Autoimmune infiltration  </br>b. Euthyroid sick syndrome  </br>c. Iodine deficiency  </br>d. Multinodular goiter  </br>e. Presence of thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibodies</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Autoimmune infiltration (Hashimoto thyroiditis)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis – How to Crack This Question<br /></br>1. Age/Sex: 16-y-o female – autoimmune thyroid disease is common in young women.<br /></br>2. Family history: Mother &amp; grandmother on levothyroxine → strong clue for hereditary autoimmune thyroiditis.<br /></br>3. Exam:<br /></br>   • Moderately enlarged, firm, non-nodular thyroid → typical “rubbery” Hashimoto goiter.<br /></br>   • Delayed Achilles tendon relaxation → classic physical sign of hypothyroidism.<br /></br>4. Labs:<br /></br>   • Very high TSH (56 µU/mL) &amp; low-normal T4 (6 µg/dL) = primary hypothyroidism.<br /></br>   • Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) &gt;1:1000 &amp; antithyroglobulin 1:500 → hallmark antibodies of Hashimoto disease.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive Pearls<br /></br>• Step 1: Check TSH/T4 pattern → primary hypothyroidism.<br /></br>• Step 2: Look for antibodies &amp; exam clues to pick etiology.<br /></br>• Step 3: Recognize that an enlarged, <em>firm, non-nodular</em> gland with +anti-TPO = Hashimoto (autoimmune infiltration).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Hashimoto thyroiditis is caused by autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration of the thyroid, leading to progressive destruction of follicles, decreased T4 production, and feedback elevation of TSH. The firm goiter reflects chronic inflammation and fibrosis. The huge anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin titers seal the diagnosis.  </p></br><p>Take-home core concept:<br /></br>Primary hypothyroidism + markedly positive anti-TPO/anti-TG antibodies = Hashimoto, i.e., autoimmune infiltration of the gland.</p></br><p>Documentation points (real life)<br /></br>• Symptoms/signs of hypothyroidism, exam of thyroid, family history<br /></br>• TSH, free T4, antibody titers<br /></br>• Plan: start levothyroxine, f/u TSH in 6–8 wk  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Mini Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Etiology: Type II/IV hypersensitivity—CD8⁺ cytotoxic T cells &amp; antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity against thyroid peroxidase &amp; thyroglobulin.<br /></br>Risk factors: Female, family history, other autoimmune diseases.<br /></br>Course: Transient hyperthyroid “Hashitoxicosis” possible early; eventual permanent hypothyroidism; ↑ risk of non-Hodgkin (MALT) lymphoma in gland.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment Highlights<br /></br>First-line: Levothyroxine (synthetic T4) 1.6 µg/kg/day; adjust to keep TSH in reference range.<br /></br>Monitoring: TSH every 6-8 wk after dose change, then q6–12 mo.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Hashimoto is HASHing out your thyroid”:<br /></br>H – High anti-TPO/anti-TG<br /></br>A – Autoimmune/family history<br /></br>S – Sluggish reflex relaxation<br /></br>H – Hard (firm) goiter  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>B. Euthyroid sick syndrome – occurs in severe systemic illness; TSH low-normal, low T3, normal/low T4; no antibodies, patient is well.<br /></br>C. Iodine deficiency – causes goiter but usually worldwide malnutrition areas; antibodies negative; TSH elevated but history/setting don’t fit U.S. teen with balanced diet.<br /></br>D. Multinodular goiter – would have palpable nodules &amp; heterogenous gland, not uniformly firm; antibodies absent; thyroid function often normal or hyperthyroid if “toxic.”<br /></br>E. Presence of TSH-receptor-stimulating antibodies – characteristic of Graves disease which causes suppressed TSH and elevated T4 with clinical hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, tremor, exophthalmos), opposite of this vignette.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Takeaway Sentence<br /></br>High TSH + low T4 + very high anti-TPO/anti-TG antibodies in a firm, non-nodular goiter = Hashimoto thyroiditis from autoimmune lymphocytic infiltration.</p>
143. A 67-year-old woman comes to the emergency department 6 hours after she noticed a red rash over her arms and abdomen. She has had nosebleeds during the past week. She has a 1-month history of nightly temperatures to 38.3°C (101°F) with night sweats. Eight years ago, she had ovarian cancer and underwent total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, and omentectomy followed by chemotherapy and filgrastim (GCSF) therapy. She has received conjugated estrogen therapy since the operation. There has been no recurrence of ovarian cancer. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38.3°C (101°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 100/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a diffuse petechial rash over the upper extremities and abdomen. The cervical lymph nodes are shoddy and slightly enlarged. Pelvic examination shows no abnormalities. Her hematocrit is 22 percent, leukocyte count is 30,000/mm3, and platelet count is 30,000/mm3. A blood smear shows immature myeloblasts. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current condition?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Adverse effect of anesthetic agent</br>b. Chemotherapy</br>c. Estrogen use</br>d. Filgrastim (GCSF) therapy</br>e. Recurrence of primary malignancy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Prior chemotherapy</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Presenting picture<br /></br>   • 67-y-o woman with 1-mo history of fevers + night-sweats (“B symptoms”)<br /></br>   • Acute onset petechiae + epistaxis → suggests thrombocytopenia<br /></br>   • Vitals: febrile, tachycardic, mildly hypotensive (sepsis‐like or leukemia‐associated systemic inflammatory response)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Key laboratory clues<br /></br>   • Hct 22 % (anemia)<br /></br>   • WBC 30 000/mm³ with immature myeloblasts on smear<br /></br>   • Platelets 30 000/mm³ (thrombocytopenia)<br /></br>   Pancytopenia with circulating blasts = acute myeloid leukemia (AML) or myelodysplastic transformation.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Important past history<br /></br>   • Ovarian cancer treated 8 yr ago with surgery + chemotherapy.<br /></br>   • Latency of 5–10 yr after alkylating agents or topoisomerase-II inhibitors is classic for therapy-related AML/MDS.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>High-yield cognitive approach:<br /></br>When you see a patient years after cytotoxic chemotherapy who now has pancytopenia with circulating blasts, immediately think “therapy-related AML/MDS” rather than recurrence of the solid tumor.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Identify hematologic malignancy → circulating myeloblasts = acute leukemia.<br /></br>2. Ask “primary vs therapy-related?” → any prior chemo/radiation? Yes (ovarian CA).<br /></br>3. Latency fits (≈8 yr).<br /></br>4. Therefore root cause = mutagenic effect of previous chemotherapy on bone-marrow stem cells.</p></br><p>Core takeaway<br /></br>Alkylating agents (e.g., cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, melphalan) and topo-II inhibitors (etoposide) are leukemogenic; they can induce AML/MDS typically 5–10 years after exposure.</p></br><p>Documentation you’d include: cytopenias, peripheral smear blasts, prior chemo exposure, plan for bone-marrow biopsy &amp; induction therapy.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Alkylating chemotherapy induces DNA cross-linking → chromosomal deletions (esp 5q, 7q) → myelodysplasia → AML.<br /></br>• Therapy-related AML often presents with complex cytogenetics and poorer prognosis than de-novo AML.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: fatigue (anemia), infection (neutropenia), bleeding/petechiae (thrombocytopenia), constitutional symptoms.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics / Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostics to confirm<br /></br>• Bone-marrow biopsy (&gt;20 % myeloblasts confirms AML).<br /></br>• Cytogenetic work-up for prognosis (e.g., monosomy 5/7).  </p></br><p>Initial management<br /></br>• Admit, protective isolation, broad-spectrum antibiotics if febrile neutropenia.<br /></br>• Induction chemo: “7+3” (cytarabine 7 d + anthracycline 3 d) or CPX-351 for therapy-related AML.<br /></br>• Consider allogeneic stem-cell transplant in eligible patients.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Alkylators ALTER marrow later”<br /></br>• Alkylating agents<br /></br>• Lead to Leukemia (AML/MDS)<br /></br>• Appears Later (5–10 yr)</p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a time-bomb labeled “alkylator chemo” planted in the bone marrow; it detonates ~7 years later showering out myeloblasts.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Adverse effect of anesthetic agent<br /></br>   • Anesthetics can cause malignant hyperthermia or hepatotoxicity but not clonal myeloid blasts years later.</p></br><p>c. Estrogen use<br /></br>   • Estrogen therapy ↑risk of thromboembolism, breast/endometrial CA, but no association with AML or pancytopenia.</p></br><p>d. Filgrastim (G-CSF) therapy<br /></br>   • Stimulates neutrophil production; rarely causes transient leukocytosis but not malignant transformation eight years later.</p></br><p>e. Recurrence of primary malignancy<br /></br>   • Ovarian cancer relapse would present with pelvic mass, ascites, CA-125 elevation—not circulating myeloblasts and pancytopenia.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Therapy-related AML/MDS is a classic late complication (5–10 years) of cytotoxic chemotherapy, particularly alkylating agents and topo-II inhibitors. Always consider this in any patient with prior chemo who now has cytopenias and blasts on smear.</p>
144. A previously healthy 42-year-old woman has had generalized weakness, lethargy, and double vision for 2 weeks. Examination shows no other abnormalities. X-rays of the chest show an upper anterior mediastinal mass. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Lymphoma</br>b. Neurogenic tumor</br>c. Parathyroid tumor</br>d. Teratoma</br>e. Thymoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Thymoma  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues and why they matter:<br /></br>1. Diplopia + generalized weakness/fatigability → classic for myasthenia gravis (MG).<br /></br>   • MG presents with fluctuating skeletal-muscle weakness that worsens with use (ocular symptoms &gt; ptosis/diplopia are most common early signs).<br /></br>2. “Previously healthy,” 2-week subacute course, no focal neuro deficits or sensory loss → points away from central nervous system lesion or demyelinating disease.<br /></br>3. Chest x-ray: “upper anterior mediastinal mass.”<br /></br>   • Differential of an anterior mediastinal mass = the “4 T’s”: Thymoma, (Terrible) lymphoma, Thyroid (ectopic/goiter), Teratoma/germ-cell tumors.<br /></br>4. Putting it together: MG + anterior mediastinal mass → think thymic pathology, most classically thymoma (15 % of MG pts) or thymic hyperplasia (∼70 %).  </p></br><p>Approach for similar questions:<br /></br>A. Identify the clinical syndrome (fatigable weakness = MG).<br /></br>B. Recall high-yield associations (MG ↔ thymic neoplasm).<br /></br>C. Match with anatomic location (anterior mediastinum) and pick from the 4 T’s.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic</br>1. Symptom constellation strongly suggests myasthenia gravis.<br /></br>2. MG is paraneoplastic in a sizable minority; the neoplasm is almost always thymic.<br /></br>3. Thymic tumors reside in the anterior mediastinum → radiographic description fits.<br /></br>4. Therefore the most likely diagnosis for THIS mass, given the paraneoplastic clue, is thymoma.</p></br><p>Core takeaway:<br /></br>When you see MG–type weakness accompanied by an anterior mediastinal mass, the answer is thymoma until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-life patient):<br /></br>• Neurologic exam with fatigability signs<br /></br>• Acetylcholine-receptor antibody test ordered<br /></br>• Chest imaging reviewed → anterior mediastinal mass<br /></br>• Plan: CT chest, thoracic surgery referral for thymectomy, pyridostigmine started, evaluate for additional autoimmune disease.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Thymoma<br /></br>• Origin: epithelial cells of the thymus.<br /></br>• Epidemiology: middle-aged adults, equal M/F.<br /></br>• 30–50 % associated with autoimmune disease, most famously MG (anti-AChR or anti-MuSK antibodies).<br /></br>• Other paraneoplastic syndromes: red-cell aplasia, hypogammaglobulinemia.<br /></br>• Pathogenesis of MG link: autoreactive T-cells escape thymic negative selection → antibodies against neuromuscular junction.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnosis &amp; Management (high-yield bullets)  </p></br><p>Diagnosis<br /></br>1. CT chest w/ contrast → defines extent, invasion.<br /></br>2. MG work-up: AChR/MuSK antibodies, edrophonium or ice-pack test, EMG if needed.  </p></br><p>Treatment<br /></br>• Surgical thymectomy = first-line for localized tumors and improves MG symptoms.<br /></br>• Adjunct: pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor), immunosuppression (steroids, azathioprine) if MG severe.<br /></br>• Radiation/chemotherapy for invasive or metastatic thymoma (e.g., cisplatin-based regimens).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic for anterior mediastinal masses: 4 T’s<br /></br>1. Thymoma<br /></br>2. Teratoma (germ-cell)<br /></br>3. (Terrible) T-cell Lymphoma<br /></br>4. Thyroid (goiter, carcinoma)  </p></br><p>“Thymoma Makes You TIRED”<br /></br>T = Thymoma, I = Immune disorders, R = Red-cell aplasia, E = Endocrine (MG), D = Double vision.  </p></br><p>Visual aid: Picture the thymus as a traffic light in front of the sternum; when it malfunctions the “signal” to muscles flickers, causing fatigable weakness.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Lymphoma (Terrible T)<br /></br>• Can appear in anterior mediastinum but usually causes B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) or SVC syndrome, not MG.  </p></br><p>b. Neurogenic tumor<br /></br>• Almost always arises in the posterior mediastinum (e.g., schwannoma, neurofibroma).  </p></br><p>c. Parathyroid tumor<br /></br>• Ectopic parathyroid rests possible but far less common; would present with hypercalcemia (stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones).  </p></br><p>d. Teratoma<br /></br>• Germ-cell tumors (especially mature cystic teratoma) occur in anterior mediastinum; may produce β-hCG or AFP, have calcifications/teeth on imaging, but NOT associated with MG.  </p></br><p>e. Thymoma (correct)<br /></br>• Only choice that simultaneously explains MG symptoms and an anterior mediastinal mass.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Picking lymphoma because it is one of the “4 T’s.” Always integrate paraneoplastic clues to discriminate.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-yield final message:<br /></br>Fatigable ocular or bulbar weakness + anterior mediastinal mass = think myasthenia gravis caused by a thymoma.</p>
145. A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife because of fever and chills for 2 hours. He visited the physician 2 days ago because of burning with urination, and urine cultures were ordered. He was an artillery officer 40 years ago, and his wife says that his hearing is deteriorating. He has a sulfa allergy. He appears moderately ill. His temperature is 38.3°C (101°F). Examination shows left costovertebral angle tenderness. His leukocyte count is 15,000/mm3 with a shift to the left. Urinalysis shows many bacilli. Urine culture and sensitivity show an organism that is sensitive only to nitrofurantoin, gentamicin, amikacin, tobramycin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, and ciprofloxacin. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Amikacin</br>b. Ampicillin</br>c. Ciprofloxacin</br>d. Gentamicin</br>e. Nitrofurantoin</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Ciprofloxacin  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Acute presentation: fever, chills, costovertebral-angle tenderness, leukocytosis with left shift → acute pyelonephritis (systemic infection, not just cystitis).<br /></br>• Culture report: organism is resistant to most drugs except six listed.<br /></br>• Contra-indications spelled out in history:<br /></br>  – “Hearing is deteriorating” + prior artillery noise exposure → high baseline risk for ototoxicity.<br /></br>  – “Sulfa allergy” → cannot use TMP-SMX.<br /></br>  – Need for bactericidal serum and renal-parenchymal levels (rules out agents that only concentrate in urine).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When an NBME stem provides both a short list of susceptible drugs and one or two clear contraindications, immediately cross out those contraindicated drugs and ask, “Which remaining agent achieves adequate serum &amp; tissue levels for this infection?”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why ciprofloxacin?)<br /></br>1. Diagnose systemic UTI (pyelonephritis) → must choose an antibiotic that penetrates renal parenchyma and achieves high serum levels.<br /></br>2. Eliminate TMP-SMX (sulfa allergy).<br /></br>3. Eliminate aminoglycosides (gentamicin, amikacin, tobramycin): high risk of ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity, especially in an elderly patient with pre-existing auditory damage.<br /></br>4. Eliminate nitrofurantoin: adequate only for lower-tract infections (cystitis); achieves poor renal-parenchymal levels and contraindicated in pyelonephritis.<br /></br>5. Remaining drug with proven pyelonephritis efficacy → fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin).  </p></br><p>Take-home message: Fluoroquinolones are first-line oral agents for outpatient or early inpatient treatment of gram-negative pyelonephritis when the organism is susceptible and no contraindications exist.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: note allergy history, baseline hearing status, renal function, and plan for follow-up urine culture.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Pyelonephritis = ascending bacterial infection reaching the renal pelvis and parenchyma → fever, flank pain, systemic inflammatory response.<br /></br>• Most common organisms: Enterobacteriaceae (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus).<br /></br>• Treatment requires drugs that reach bactericidal levels in renal tissue and blood, not just urine.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>First-line (outpatient, stable):<br /></br>• Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID or levofloxacin 750 mg PO daily × 5-7 days (if susceptible).<br /></br>Alternatives (for fluoroquinolone resistance):<br /></br>• Ceftriaxone IV, ertapenem IV, or aminoglycoside single dose followed by oral step-down.<br /></br>• TMP-SMX if susceptible and no sulfa allergy.  </p></br><p>Monitoring:<br /></br>• Repeat culture if not clinically improving in 48-72 h.<br /></br>• Assess creatinine (fluoroquinolone dose adjustment if severe CKD).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“FLOX the Kidney”: Fluoroquinolones (-floxacin) are the oral drugs that FLOX (treat) the kidney parenchyma.<br /></br>“Nitro stays in the bladder; Amino kills your ear &amp; kidney.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Amikacin – Aminoglycoside. Effective against gram-negatives but carries high ototoxic and nephrotoxic risk; contraindicated in a patient with existing hearing loss. Requires IV dosing and drug-level monitoring.<br /></br>b. Ampicillin – Not on susceptibility list → organism resistant.<br /></br>d. Gentamicin – Same aminoglycoside issues as amikacin; additionally, the most notorious for ototoxicity.<br /></br>e. Nitrofurantoin – Excellent urinary concentration but does not reach renal tissue; ineffective for pyelonephritis and contraindicated if CrCl &lt; 30 mL/min (common in elderly).  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: choosing nitrofurantoin for any UTI; remember it is ONLY for uncomplicated lower-tract infections.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>Choose an agent that:<br /></br>1. The bug is sensitive to.<br /></br>2. Achieves therapeutic renal-parenchymal levels.<br /></br>3. Is not contraindicated by patient factors.<br /></br>In this scenario, ciprofloxacin is the sole agent meeting all three criteria.</p>
146. A 19-year-old man comes to the physician because of frequent nosebleeds over the past 3 weeks. He has bipolar disorder currently well controlled with lithium carbonate, bupropion, and valproic acid. Physical examination shows no abnormalities except for dried blood in the nares. Mental status examination shows an anxious mood and slight motor restlessness.
