Serum studies show a lithium carbonate concentration of 1.3 mEq/L (therapeutic range 0.6–1.2), and valproic acid concentration of 77 ug/mL (therapeutic range 40–100). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Measurement of serum AST activity</br>b. Measurement of serum bupropion concentration</br>c. Platelet count</br>d. Discontinuation of lithium carbonate therapy</br>e. Discontinuation of valproic acid therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. Obtain a platelet count.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that should jump off the page:<br /></br>• 19-year-old on valproic acid (VPA) for bipolar disorder.<br /></br>• New-onset “frequent nosebleeds” (mucosal bleeding = primary hemostasis problem).<br /></br>• Otherwise normal physical exam except dried blood; vitals and other labs unremarkable.<br /></br>• VPA level is therapeutic (77 µg/mL).<br /></br>• Lithium level is only slightly above target (1.3 mEq/L) and patient has no lithium-toxicity signs (GI upset, tremor, ataxia, confusion).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: mucosal bleeding.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no jaundice, abdominal pain, altered mental status, or motor signs of lithium toxicity.  </p></br><p>Test-taking mindset: When a patient on VPA develops bleeding symptoms, the reflex move is to think “VPA-induced thrombocytopenia” → check platelets before doing anything else.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: USMLE loves “first step” questions. Always evaluate (lab test) before you eliminate or add medications unless the scenario is immediately life-threatening or the culprit drug is obvious and severe.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why Platelet Count?)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify likely adverse effect.<br /></br>• VPA commonly causes dose-dependent thrombocytopenia (↓platelet production and impaired aggregation).<br /></br>• Presents with easy bruising, petechiae, epistaxis, menorrhagia.</p></br><p>Step 2: Decide first action.<br /></br>• Need objective data (platelet count) to confirm.<br /></br>• If thrombocytopenia is present, then dose-reduce or discontinue VPA.<br /></br>• If platelets are normal, search for local nasal causes.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Bleeding + VPA → check CBC with platelets before changing therapy.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (IRL): “Patient with epistaxis while on valproic acid. Plan: CBC w/ platelets to evaluate for VPA-induced thrombocytopenia. Will adjust medication if thrombocytopenia confirmed.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Valproic acid:<br /></br>• Mechanism: ↑GABA by inhibiting GABA transaminase, blocks Na⁺ channels.<br /></br>• Hematologic adverse effects: thrombocytopenia (most common), rarely agranulocytosis. Dose-related; risk increases when serum VPA &gt;100 µg/mL, female sex, and older age but can occur at therapeutic levels.<br /></br>• Other adverse effects: hepatotoxicity (↑AST/ALT), pancreatitis, weight gain, neural tube defects (teratogen), alopecia, tremor.  </p></br><p>Thrombocytopenia mechanism: marrow suppression + impaired platelet function → prolonged bleeding time with or without low platelet count.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostic:<br /></br>• CBC with platelet count is the single best next step.<br /></br>• If platelets &lt;100,000/µL or symptomatic, reduce dose or switch mood stabilizer (e.g., lithium, lamotrigine).  </p></br><p>No imaging needed unless epistaxis is massive/recurrent after correcting platelets.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic: “VaLPROate PROlongs bleeding” (think Platelets).<br /></br>Visual: Picture a “val-PRO” hockey player with a bloody nose and shattering platelets on the ice.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Serum AST (liver function)<br /></br>• VPA hepatotoxicity gives RUQ pain, malaise, jaundice, ↑AST/ALT, not isolated mucosal bleeding.<br /></br>• Epistaxis without liver signs makes thrombocytopenia much more likely.  </p></br><p>b. Serum bupropion concentration<br /></br>• Bupropion’s main issues are seizures (high dose), insomnia, and weight loss, not bleeding. Serum levels are rarely measured clinically.  </p></br><p>d. Discontinue lithium carbonate<br /></br>• Lithium level 1.3 mEq/L is only slightly above target and bleeding has no relationship to lithium. Stopping would destabilize mood without addressing the bleeding.  </p></br><p>e. Discontinue valproic acid outright<br /></br>• Premature. First confirm thrombocytopenia. If platelets are normal, stopping VPA is unnecessary. If low, then dose-adjust or discontinue based on severity.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: In a patient on valproic acid who develops unexplained mucosal bleeding, the very first step is to obtain a platelet count to evaluate for VPA-induced thrombocytopenia.</p>
147. A previously healthy 52-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of difficulty swallowing solids and liquids. He has had a 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss during this period. Examination shows no abnormalities. An upper gastrointestinal series with barium contrast is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Achalasia</br>b. Hiatal hernia</br>c. Leiomyoma of the esophagus</br>d. Pharyngoesophageal (Zenker) diverticulum</br>e. Schatzki ring</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Achalasia  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – Age 52 years → typical for primary (idiopathic) achalasia, but still young enough that malignancy-related pseudoachalasia is less likely.<br /></br>  – 6-month “difficulty swallowing solids and liquids” from the outset → motility disorder rather than a mechanical blockage (mechanical lesions usually begin with solids only).<br /></br>  – 2.3-kg (5-lb) weight loss → can be seen with achalasia due to poor oral intake.<br /></br>  – Normal physical exam → rules out obvious mass or complicated hernia.<br /></br>  – Barium study (image supplied to test-taker) classically shows a dilated esophageal body with smooth tapering to a “bird-beak” at the gastroesophageal junction.<br /></br>Pattern: progressive, simultaneous solid-and-liquid dysphagia + bird-beak sign = achalasia.  </p></br><p>Cognitive / test-taking pearls<br /></br>1. Always ask: “Solids first or solids &amp; liquids together?” → Solids only = structural obstruction; Solids + liquids = motility disorder (achalasia, scleroderma, diffuse esophageal spasm).<br /></br>2. On Step &amp; Shelf exams, the phrase “bird-beak narrowing” essentially equals achalasia unless other red flags for malignancy are given.  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Degeneration of inhibitory neurons in the Auerbach (myenteric) plexus → failure of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to relax.<br /></br>2. LES remains tonically contracted → functional obstruction → progressive dilation of proximal esophagus → dysphagia to both solids and liquids.<br /></br>Take-home: Motility failure, not a fixed narrowing, explains simultaneous dysphagia.  </p></br><p>Documentation items (real patient)<br /></br>• Duration and pattern of dysphagia (solids vs liquids).<br /></br>• Alarm symptoms (weight loss, bleeding).<br /></br>• Plan: esophageal manometry (diagnostic gold standard), endoscopy to exclude pseudoachalasia from cancer; treatment options discussed.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: idiopathic neuronal degeneration (most common in USA); Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi) worldwide; can be secondary to malignancy at GE junction (pseudoachalasia).<br /></br>• Loss of NO/VIP-secreting inhibitory neurons → hyper-tonic LES, absent peristalsis.<br /></br>• Complications: aspiration, esophagitis, esophageal squamous-cell carcinoma.  </p></br><p>Treatment / Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Barium swallow → bird-beak tapering.<br /></br>2. Esophageal manometry → ↑ LES resting pressure, incomplete LES relaxation, aperistalsis.<br /></br>3. Endoscopy → exclude cancer and retained food.  </p></br><p>Management (step-wise)<br /></br>• Temporizing: Nifedipine, nitrates, sublingual nitroglycerin.<br /></br>• Definitive:<br /></br>  – Pneumatic (balloon) dilation.<br /></br>  – Laparoscopic Heller myotomy + partial fundoplication (preferred in good surgical candidates).<br /></br>  – Botulinum toxin injection into LES (frail pts).  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“ALLe foods get stuck in AchaLA­sia” → All = solids &amp; liquids, LA = LES doesn’t Abandon its tone → bird-beak.<br /></br>Visual: Picture a bird unable to swallow because its beak (LES) is clamped shut.  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>B. Hiatal hernia<br /></br>• Sliding hernia often causes GERD, not progressive dysphagia to liquids.<br /></br>• Barium shows gastric cardia above diaphragm, not a bird-beak.  </p></br><p>C. Leiomyoma of the esophagus<br /></br>• Benign intramural mass → dysphagia to solids first; barium shows an eccentric filling defect, not smooth tapering.  </p></br><p>D. Pharyngoesophageal (Zenker) diverticulum<br /></br>• Regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, cough, aspiration; outpouching at cricopharyngeus on barium, not distal tapering.  </p></br><p>E. Schatzki ring<br /></br>• Intermittent “steakhouse dysphagia” to solids only; thin circumferential ring at GE junction on barium, no proximal dilation.  </p></br><p>Core Concept<br /></br>Simultaneous solid-and-liquid dysphagia with a bird-beak appearance = achalasia, a motility disorder caused by failure of LES relaxation.</p>
148. A 21-year-old woman comes to the physician because she has not had a menstrual period since she stopped using an oral contraceptive 10 months ago. Menarche was at the age of 14 years, and menses had occurred at regular 28-day intervals before starting an oral contraceptive 3 years ago. She is not sexually active. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 63 kg (140 lb); BMI is 23 kg/m2. Her blood pressure is 120/58 mm Hg. Examination shows no hirsutism or other abnormalities. Serum studies show a thyroid-stimulating hormone concentration of 3 µU/mL, prolactin concentration of 20 ng/mL, and free thyroxine concentration of 2 ng/dL (N=0.8–2.4). A progestin challenge test shows withdrawal bleeding. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Anovulation</br>b. Hyperandrogenism</br>c. Hyperprolactinemia</br>d. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Kallmann syndrome)</br>e. Outflow tract obstruction</br>f. Pregnancy</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: A. Anovulation  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Key clues<br /></br>   • Secondary amenorrhea ≥ 6 mo after stopping OCPs<br /></br>   • Normal BMI, no hirsutism or galactorrhea<br /></br>   • Normal TSH, prolactin, free T4<br /></br>   • Progestin-challenge test → withdrawal bleeding  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>How they narrow the differential<br /></br>   • Withdrawal bleeding means:<br /></br>     – Endometrium is primed with estrogen (ovaries make estrogen).<br /></br>     – Cervix, uterus, and vagina are patent (no outflow obstruction).<br /></br>     – Missing ingredient is luteal-phase progesterone → points to failure to ovulate.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No virilization (argues against hyperandrogenism/PCOS).<br /></br>   • Normal prolactin (rules out prolactinoma, antipsychotic use).<br /></br>   • Not sexually active (pregnancy essentially impossible).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: In “no-period” questions, first ask, “Could she be pregnant?”; then use the progestin-challenge result to decide if estrogen is present (bleed = yes, no bleed = hypo-estrogen or outflow problem).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step wise)<br /></br>1. Amenorrhea work-up → rule out pregnancy, check TSH &amp; prolactin (all normal here).<br /></br>2. Next, assess estrogen status: give 10-day progestin.<br /></br>3. Bleeding after progestin = adequate estrogen + intact outflow; therefore diagnosis is chronic anovulation.<br /></br>Core takeaway: “Progestin-challenge bleed = anovulation.”</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: record date of last menses, duration of anovulation, negative pregnancy testing, normal labs, and positive withdrawal bleed indicating anovulatory cycles.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• In ovulatory cycles, corpus luteum secretes progesterone → withdrawal causes menses.<br /></br>• When ovulation fails (e.g., hypothalamic stress, subtle PCOS, post-pill anovulation), no corpus luteum forms → endometrium stays proliferative → amenorrhea or irregular bleeding.<br /></br>• Estrogen production from follicles remains sufficient to thicken the endometrium, which is why a progestin “substitute” triggers menses.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic and Treatment Pearls<br /></br>Diagnostics already done: exclude pregnancy, thyroid, prolactin; progestin challenge.<br /></br>Next steps (not tested in stem):<br /></br>• Serum FSH/LH if recurrent to rule out premature ovarian insufficiency.<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>• If fertility desired → induce ovulation (letrozole, clomiphene).<br /></br>• If not seeking pregnancy → cyclic progesterone or combined OCP to protect endometrium from unopposed estrogen and to regulate cycles.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Bleed after P = Past ovulation Problem (anovulation).”<br /></br>“No Bleed after P = Pregger? Path outflow? Poor estrogen?”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>b. Hyperandrogenism (PCOS)<br /></br>   • Usually hirsutism, acne, obesity, elevated LH:FSH ratio. Those are absent.<br /></br>c. Hyperprolactinemia<br /></br>   • Prolactin generally &gt; 25–30 ng/mL, galactorrhea common, and often NO withdrawal bleed (low estrogen).<br /></br>d. Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (Kallmann)<br /></br>   • Low GnRH → low FSH/LH/estrogen → NO bleeding after progestin (endometrium unprimed). Usually primary amenorrhea &amp; anosmia.<br /></br>e. Outflow tract obstruction (Asherman, cervical stenosis)<br /></br>   • Would not bleed after progestin because blood can’t exit.<br /></br>f. Pregnancy<br /></br>   • Should be first thought, but here eliminated by sexual history and withdrawal bleed (progesterone withdrawal in pregnancy would not produce bleeding).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A positive progestin-challenge test in a woman with otherwise normal labs virtually clinches chronic anovulation as the cause of secondary amenorrhea.</p>
149. A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of increasing left ear pain for 2 weeks. His temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F). Examination of the left ear shows an erythematous, bulging tympanic membrane and an edematous auditory canal with a small amount of mucopurulent discharge. The ear appears to be displaced laterally, and there is moderate tenderness in the area behind the ear. Examination of the right ear shows no abnormalities. A 1-cm, freely mobile, nontender mass is palpated over the left anterior neck. Examination of the pharynx shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Monospot test</br>b. Tympanography</br>c. CT scan of the temporal bone</br>d. Hydrocortisone/polymyxin/neomycin ear drops</br>e. Oral amoxicillin therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer <br /></br>C. CT scan of the temporal bone  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key positives<br /></br>  – 4-year-old with 2 weeks of ear pain + fever (acute otitis media history)<br /></br>  – Erythematous, bulging tympanic membrane → middle-ear infection present.<br /></br>  – Ear displaced laterally and tenderness over the mastoid (“area behind the ear”) → classic red flags for acute mastoiditis or mastoid abscess.<br /></br>  – Mucopurulent drainage in the canal may represent TM perforation from the pressure of AOM, not primary otitis externa.<br /></br>  – No pharyngeal pathology; 1-cm mobile anterior-neck node = reactive cervical lymphadenopathy, common with ear infections.  </p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives<br /></br>  – No trauma, water exposure, itching (argues against simple otitis externa).<br /></br>  – No bilateral findings (argues against serous otitis).  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map<br /></br>1. Ask: “Is this still uncomplicated AOM?”  → NO (≥10-day course, fever, post-auricular signs).<br /></br>2. Think of the #1 dangerous local extension of AOM → acute mastoiditis.<br /></br>3. Next step: define extent of bony involvement / abscess with imaging.<br /></br>4. Best test → contrast CT of temporal bone (or MRI), then start IV antibiotics ± mastoidectomy based on findings.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Acute mastoiditis = infection of mastoid air cells. Clues: ear protrusion, mastoid tenderness, persistent fever &gt;48-72 h on antibiotics or &gt;10 days untreated. Imaging is required to<br /></br>• confirm disease,<br /></br>• look for coalescent changes, subperiosteal abscess, intracranial extension (sigmoid sinus thrombosis, brain abscess).  </p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Left post-auricular erythema, edema, point tenderness; auricle displaced anterior-laterally. Concern for mastoiditis. Plan: urgent temporal-bone CT with IV contrast, ENT consult, start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics.”  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: AOM + post-auricular inflammation or pinna displacement = possible mastoiditis → obtain CT/MRI before definitive treatment.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• AOM organisms: Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae (nontypeable), Moraxella catarrhalis.<br /></br>• Spread through mastoid air-cell system → inflammation &amp; pus → pressure erodes thin cortical bone → subperiosteal abscess; may further extend to sigmoid sinus or brain.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Diagnostic: Contrast CT of temporal bone (shows coalescent mastoid air cells, abscess).<br /></br>2. Empiric therapy once imaging obtained/ENT consulted: IV vancomycin + third-generation cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone) to cover S. pneumoniae and β-lactam–resistant S. aureus.<br /></br>3. Surgical: Mastoidectomy and/or abscess drainage if no rapid response or if imaging shows abscess.<br /></br>4. Uncomplicated AOM (without mastoid signs) → high-dose amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“RED EAR that STICKS OUT”<br /></br>R – Red, bulging TM<br /></br>E – Ear displaced<br /></br>D – Duration &gt;10 days<br /></br>E – Examine mastoid (tender)<br /></br>A – Always<br /></br>R – Request<br /></br>T – Temporal-bone CT  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Monospot test – screens for EBV infectious mononucleosis. Would present with exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical LAD, fatigue, hepatosplenomegaly, not a unilateral ear infection with mastoid tenderness.  </p></br><p>B. Tympanography – Measures middle-ear pressure; useful for diagnosing serous otitis or chronic effusions, not for suspected mastoiditis with systemic illness.  </p></br><p>D. Hydrocortisone/polymyxin/neomycin ear drops – Therapy for otitis externa. Otitis externa produces tragal pinna pain on manipulation, a patent canal often filled with debris, and a NORMAL tympanic membrane. Here the TM is bulging, not seen in OE.  </p></br><p>E. Oral amoxicillin therapy – First-line for uncomplicated AOM. This child already has potential complication (mastoiditis); oral therapy alone without imaging or IV coverage risks intracranial spread.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Ear protrusion + mastoid tenderness in a child with prolonged AOM mandates immediate imaging—contrast CT of the temporal bone—to evaluate for acute mastoiditis/abscess before definitive IV antibiotics and possible surgery.</p>
150. A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician because of tremors in her hands for 4 months. She only fills teacups halfway to avoid spilling, and she has difficulty lifting a spoonful of soup to her mouth without spilling it. Her children noticed tremors of her head 5 years ago. She has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 56 kg (123 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 80/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Examination shows a tremor of the outstretched hands bilaterally; the amplitude of the tremor increases with finger-nose testing. The tremor resolves at rest. Muscle strength and tone are intact, and her gait is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Carbamazepine</br>b. Diazepam</br>c. Fluoxetine</br>d. Levodopa-carbidopa</br>e. Propranolol</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Propranolol  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases that point to the diagnosis of essential (familial) tremor<br /></br>   • “Tremor of the outstretched hands” that<br /></br>     – is BILATERAL and SYMMETRIC<br /></br>     – is most pronounced with goal-directed movement (finger-to-nose, lifting a spoon) = action/intentional component<br /></br>   • “Resolves at rest”—rules out classic Parkinson resting tremor.<br /></br>   • “Head tremor noticed 5 years ago” – head/voice involvement is typical of essential tremor but rare in Parkinson disease.<br /></br>   • No rigidity, bradykinesia, gait change, or cerebellar ataxia.<br /></br>   • Age in the 50s; positive family history often present (noted by the children noticing years earlier).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • Normal gait, tone, strength → makes cerebellar and extrapyramidal causes unlikely.<br /></br>   • No medication triggers (e.g., lithium, valproate, stimulants) → rules out drug-induced tremor.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On Shelf &amp; Step exams, bilateral action tremor that improves with rest (± alcohol) = think “essential tremor → propranolol.” When you see unilateral, resting “pill-rolling” tremor + hypokinesia → think Parkinson → levodopa.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Identify tremor type (resting vs postural/action).<br /></br>2. Look for accompanying neurologic signs (rigidity, ataxia, dystonia).<br /></br>3. Match to most common etiologies tested:<br /></br>   • Essential tremor → action tremor, familial, head/voice, improved with β-blocker or primidone.<br /></br>   • Parkinson disease → resting tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia → levodopa.<br /></br>   • Cerebellar disease → intention tremor + ataxia.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: First-line pharmacologic therapy for moderate-to-severe essential tremor = non-selective β-blocker (propranolol) if no contraindication (e.g., asthma, heart block). Primidone is equal first-line when β-blocker not tolerated.  </p></br><p>What would be documented in the chart: “Bilateral, symmetric postural and kinetic tremor of hands; resolves at rest; no rigidity or bradykinesia. Impression: essential (familial) tremor. Plan: Start propranolol 40 mg PO BID, titrate to symptom control; counsel on side-effects (bradycardia, fatigue) and contraindications.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Often autosomal dominant mutation (chromosome 3q or 2p loci).<br /></br>• Mechanism: Abnormal oscillatory activity in cerebello-thalamo-cortical circuits.<br /></br>• Presentation:<br /></br>  – Postural/kinetic tremor of hands, forearms; head “yes-yes” or “no-no”; voice quaver.<br /></br>  – Often lessens after small amount of alcohol (“beer test” frequently mentioned on exams).<br /></br>• Progressive but benign; disability is functional (spilling drinks, handwriting).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>First-line options:<br /></br>1. Propranolol (non-selective β-blocker; start 20–40 mg BID, titrate; IV version for peri-procedural control).<br /></br>2. Primidone (barbiturate-derived antiepileptic) equally first-line, especially if β-blocker contraindicated.  </p></br><p>Second-line/adjunct: Topiramate, gabapentin, clonazepam, or botulinum toxin for focal head/voice tremor.  </p></br><p>Refractory cases: High-frequency deep-brain stimulation of the ventral intermediate nucleus of the thalamus.  </p></br><p>Diagnostics: None required once classic clinical pattern established; labs/neuro-imaging if atypical features.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Essential tremor gets BETTER with the BETa-blocker.”<br /></br>OR<br /></br>“Tea is ESSENTIAL in Britain; spill-proof with PROPRAN(tea)LOL.”  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a person trying to drink tea (“teacup” clue) with shaky hands; a big “β” symbol on the cup reminds you β-blocker stops the spill.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Carbamazepine – First-line for trigeminal neuralgia and mood stabilization in bipolar disorder; not standard for essential tremor.  </p></br><p>b. Diazepam – Benzodiazepines can dampen tremor but are third-line due to sedation, tolerance, and dependence risk.  </p></br><p>c. Fluoxetine – SSRI for depression/OCD; no role in tremor control.  </p></br><p>d. Levodopa-carbidopa – Treats Parkinson disease (resting tremor plus rigidity, hypokinesia). Would not help an isolated action tremor and could worsen dyskinesias.  </p></br><p>Key discriminators: only propranolol (or primidone) specifically targets an isolated, bilateral action tremor without Parkinsonian features.</p>
151. A previously healthy 22-year-old man comes to the emergency department because of a 1-day history of burning and tingling around his mouth and in his arms and legs. During this time, cold water has felt “burning hot” when he washes his hands, and cool beverages have felt hot in his mouth. Two days ago, he ate grouper and mussels at a restaurant. One hour after eating, he had nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, which lasted 24 hours and resolved spontaneously. He takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Muscle strength is 5/5 throughout. Deep tendon reflexes are absent throughout. Babinski sign is absent. Sensation is intact.</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Botulism</br>b. Campylobacter jejuni infection</br>c. Ciguatera poisoning</br>d. Conversion disorder</br>e. Guillain-Barré syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Ciguatera poisoning  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases that narrow the differential:<br /></br>• “Two days ago, he ate grouper and mussels” – reef fish such as grouper, snapper, barracuda are classic sources of ciguatoxin made by marine dinoflagellates.<br /></br>• “1-day history of burning and tingling … cold water has felt ‘burning hot’ … cool beverages have felt hot” – pathognomonic temperature (hot–cold) sensation reversal.<br /></br>• Rapid-onset GI illness (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea within 1 h) that resolved, followed by neurologic symptoms – the typical biphasic presentation of ciguatera.<br /></br>• Normal strength, intact sensation, but absent reflexes – mild peripheral neuropathy; motor power is usually preserved.  </p></br><p>Approach tip: On exam day, any question that couples reef-fish ingestion with GI upset ➔ neurologic paresthesias + temperature reversal = ciguatera until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (why C is correct)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Exposure: Reef fish → likely ciguatoxin.  </li></br><li>Timing: GI sx within minutes–6 h, neuro sx within 24–72 h.  </li></br><li>Signature neuro finding: Dysesthesia with paradoxical hot/cold sensation ± perioral/digital paresthesias.  </li></br><li>Physical exam: Normal strength, ↓/absent DTRs, no UMN signs.<br /></br>Take-home: Temperature reversal after reef-fish meal = ciguatera poisoning.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient): detailed dietary history, onset of GI vs neuro sx, neurologic exam (strength, reflexes, sensation), vitals; notify local health department because ciguatera is reportable in many jurisdictions.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Dinoflagellate Gambierdiscus toxicus produces ciguatoxin → accumulates up the food chain in large reef fish.<br /></br>• Ciguatoxin opens voltage-gated Na⁺ channels → persistent depolarization of nerve &amp; muscle membranes → paresthesias, dysesthesias, ↓ reflexes.<br /></br>• Heat-stable: cooking, freezing, and gastric acid do NOT inactivate it.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Diagnostics: purely clinical; no routine confirmatory test.<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>1. Supportive (IV fluids, anti-emetics, analgesia).<br /></br>2. Mannitol IV within 24 h may shorten neurologic sx (data limited).<br /></br>3. Avoid alcohol, nuts, caffeine, fish for weeks – may exacerbate relapse.<br /></br>Prognosis: Most recover in days–weeks; some have chronic paresthesias or fatigue for months.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“Reef fish give you ‘reef-ersal’ of hot &amp; cold.”<br /></br>CIGUAToxin = Cold/Hot Inversion &amp; GI Upset After Tropic fish.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Botulism – After improperly canned foods or aged seafood, presents with diplopia, ptosis, dysarthria, descending flaccid paralysis, autonomic sx (dry mouth). Temperature reversal is NOT a feature.  </p></br><p>b. Campylobacter jejuni infection – Produces inflammatory diarrhea usually 2–5 days after ingestion (undercooked poultry). No rapid neurologic paresthesias; if neurologic, weeks later as Guillain-Barré.  </p></br><p>d. Conversion disorder – Requires neurologic-appearing deficits incongruent with anatomy &amp; no physiologic explanation; here there is a clear toxic exposure that fully explains symptoms.  </p></br><p>e. Guillain-Barré syndrome – Ascending weakness with areflexia days–weeks after URI/diarrhea; patients typically develop progressive motor deficits, not isolated sensory dysesthesia with temperature inversion.  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>Rapid GI illness followed by paresthesias and hallmark hot–cold temperature reversal after eating large reef fish = ciguatera poisoning.</p>
152. A 37-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department because of a 3-day history of progressive shortness of breath, cough productive of rust-colored sputum, and temperatures to 38.9°C (102°F). Four years ago, she underwent splenectomy for injuries sustained during a motor vehicle collision. Her temperature is 39°C (102.2°F), pulse is 120/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. Examination shows no jugular venous distention. On pulmonary examination, there are diffuse inspiratory wheezes and crackles heard at the right lung base. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. The extremities are cyanotic, and there is no edema. Arterial blood gas analysis on 2 L/min of oxygen by nasal cannula shows:</br>pH 7.39  </br>Pco₂ 38 mm Hg  </br>Po₂ 41 mm Hg  </br>HCO₃⁻ 22 mEq/L  </br>O₂ saturation 79 percent  </br>A chest x-ray is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Acute respiratory distress syndrome  </br>b. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis  </br>c. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis  </br>d. Miliary tuberculosis  </br>e. Sarcoidosis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>A. Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key words &amp; phrases<br /></br>   • “3-day history of progressive shortness of breath” + “rust-colored sputum” → lobar pneumonia (classically pneumococcus; risk ↑ after splenectomy).<br /></br>   • Temp 39 °C, tachycardia, tachypnea → sepsis.<br /></br>   • Severe hypoxemia: PaO₂ 41 mm Hg and O₂ sat 79 % despite 2 L NC (≈ FiO₂ 0.28).<br /></br>   • Normal/near-normal PaCO₂ (38 mm Hg) and pH → respiratory failure is primarily hypoxemic, not ventilatory.<br /></br>   • No JVD, no peripheral edema, BP 105/60 → argues against cardiogenic pulmonary edema.<br /></br>   • Chest-x-ray (not shown here) in the NBME stem reveals diffuse bilateral alveolar infiltrates without cardiomegaly → classic for ARDS.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Acute time course (&lt; 1 week).<br /></br>   • Diffuse crackles/wheezes, cyanosis.<br /></br>   • Severe refractory hypoxemia (PaO₂/FiO₂ ≈ 41/0.28 ≈ 146 &lt; 300).</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No chronic exposure history (rules out hypersensitivity pneumonitis).<br /></br>   • No hilar adenopathy, chronic constitutional symptoms (rules out sarcoidosis, miliary TB).<br /></br>   • Patient age and presentation incompatible with lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM).</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Approach tip: Whenever you see severe hypoxemia + bilateral infiltrates + sepsis/trauma/pancreatitis with no signs of heart failure, think ARDS.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify inciting event → severe pneumococcal pneumonia → sepsis.<br /></br>Step 2: Evaluate oxygenation → PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio &lt; 300 = acute hypoxemic respiratory failure.<br /></br>Step 3: Exclude cardiogenic edema → normal left-sided exam, no JVD, CXR without cardiomegaly.<br /></br>Step 4: Time course &lt; 1 week confirms Berlin criteria for ARDS.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: ARDS = non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema caused by diffuse inflammatory injury to the alveolar-capillary membrane, producing profound hypoxemia refractory to low-flow oxygen.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In practice you must document PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio, bilateral infiltrates, absence of volume overload, and precipitating condition (e.g., sepsis due to S. pneumoniae).</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Cytokine storm (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) → neutrophil migration → destruction of type I pneumocytes &amp; capillary endothelium.<br /></br>• Increased permeability → protein-rich fluid floods alveoli → ↓ lung compliance, V/Q mismatch, shunt → severe hypoxemia.<br /></br>• Diffuse alveolar damage histologically shows hyaline membranes.</p></br><p>Risk factors tested on NBME: sepsis, aspiration, pancreatitis, massive transfusion, trauma, drugs, burns.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics  </p></br><p>Initial management:<br /></br>1. High-flow O₂ → early intubation with low-tidal-volume (6 mL/kg predicted body wt) mechanical ventilation.<br /></br>2. PEEP to keep alveoli open and improve oxygenation.<br /></br>3. Treat underlying cause (empiric IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin or moxifloxacin for community-acquired pneumonia; adjust once cultures return).<br /></br>4. Conservative IV fluids; consider diuretics after hemodynamics optimized.<br /></br>5. Prone positioning, paralysis, or ECMO in severe cases (PaO₂/FiO₂ &lt; 150 despite optimal ventilation).</p></br><p>Diagnostics you would order: CBC, BMP, blood cultures, sputum culture, procalcitonin, lactate, CXR/CT to monitor progression, echocardiogram if cardiogenic edema in doubt.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aid  </p></br><p>“ARDS = ARDS”<br /></br>A – Acute (&lt; 1 week)<br /></br>R – Ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂) &lt; 300 on PEEP ≥ 5 cm H₂O<br /></br>D – Diffuse bilateral infiltrates<br /></br>S – Sepsis/trauma/other cause &amp; not due to heart failure</p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture alveoli drowning in protein-rich “soapy” fluid while neutrophils fire “cytokine bullets.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>b. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis – Usually subacute; exposure to birds, moldy hay; presents with fever, chills, diffuse fine nodular CXR, not profound refractory hypoxemia.  </p></br><p>c. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis – Rare cystic lung disease in women (tuberous sclerosis association); insidious dyspnea &amp; pneumothorax; CXR/CT shows numerous thin-walled cysts, not alveolar infiltrates.  </p></br><p>d. Miliary tuberculosis – Chronic course, night sweats, weight loss; CXR with diffusely distributed millet-seed nodules; splenectomy irrelevant.  </p></br><p>e. Sarcoidosis – African-American female predominance, bilateral hilar adenopathy, noncaseating granulomas; chronic, usually mild hypoxemia if any; CXR pattern very different.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: equating severe pneumonia with ARDS; remember ARDS diagnosis requires the hypoxemia and diffuse infiltrates not explained just by the pneumonia or heart failure alone.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>Sepsis from post-splenectomy pneumococcal pneumonia triggered diffuse alveolar damage → ARDS, evidenced by acute onset, bilateral infiltrates, PaO₂/FiO₂ &lt; 300, and absence of cardiogenic signs. Management hinges on low-tidal-volume ventilation with PEEP and treating the infection.</p>
153. A 24-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of moderate pain and swelling of her left ankle that began when she stepped in a hole while running. She says she continued running for 20 minutes afterward but stopped because she twisted the ankle again. Use of ice, bandages, and crutches has provided moderate relief. She has no history of previous ankle injury or serious illness. She takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. Vital signs are within normal limits. Dorsalis pedis pulses are 2+ bilaterally. The left anterior talofibular and calcaneofibular ligaments are tender to palpation; there is no bony tenderness to palpation of the forefoot, hindfoot, or ankle bilaterally. Anterior drawer test is 0 in the right ankle and 2+ in the left. Talar tilt test is 0 in the right ankle and 1+ in the left. The patient is able to dorsiflex and plantar flex both feet and ankles and flex and extend all toes. Sensation to light touch is intact over the lower extremities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to prevent morbidity in this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Extra-articular platelet-derived growth factor injection</br>b. Immobilization in a short-leg cast</br>c. Physical therapy</br>d. Surgical reconstruction of the ankle</br>e. Taping of the ankle</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>C. Physical therapy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Injury description<br /></br>   • “Stepped in a hole while running…twisted the ankle again” → classic inversion mechanism.<br /></br>   • Point-tender over anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) &amp; calcaneofibular ligament (CFL). These are the 2 ligaments most often sprained with inversion injuries.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Grading the sprain (exam clues)<br /></br>   • Anterior drawer 2+ (laxity) → partial tear of ATFL.<br /></br>   • Talar tilt 1+ → mild laxity of CFL.<br /></br>   • Able to bear weight, full active ROM, no bony tenderness → Ottawa ankle rules negative → no x-ray needed.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No medial (deltoid) tenderness, neurovascular intact (2+ DP pulses, intact sensation).<br /></br>   • No prior instability.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Interpretation: Grade II (moderate) lateral ankle sprain = partial ligament tear with mild–moderate laxity but preserved stability and weight-bearing ability.  </p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>Step 1 – Apply Ottawa rules → decide on imaging.<br /></br>Step 2 – Grade sprain (I = stretch, II = partial tear, III = complete tear).<br /></br>Step 3 – Match treatment to grade:<br /></br>   I/II → early functional rehab (PT).<br /></br>   III or chronic instability → casting or surgery.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why physical therapy?<br /></br>• Early, supervised rehab (range-of-motion, strengthening, proprioception training) shortens recovery time, restores stability, and most importantly PREVENTS chronic instability &amp; post-traumatic osteoarthritis.<br /></br>• Casting/immobilization beyond a few days causes stiffness, muscle atrophy, and delays proprioceptive recovery.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real world):<br /></br>“Grade II inversion sprain of left ankle (ATFL, CFL). Neurovascular intact. Able to bear weight. Plan: functional rehab with PT, progressive weight bearing, ankle-stabilizing brace during sports, NSAIDs for pain.”  </p></br><p>Take-home message: For uncomplicated grade I–II ankle sprains, functional rehabilitation (PT) is superior to prolonged immobilization.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• ATFL = first ligament disrupted during inversion; CFL follows with increasing force.<br /></br>• Partial tear → microscopic disruption; collagen remodeling occurs over 4–6 weeks.<br /></br>• Key to healing = controlled stress → stimulates collagen alignment &amp; proprioceptive nerve recovery.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Management ladder<br /></br>1. First 48-72 h: RICE (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) + short-term NSAIDs.<br /></br>2. Functional rehab:<br /></br>   a. Phase I (0–4 d): protected weight bearing, passive ROM.<br /></br>   b. Phase II (4 d–2 wk): active ROM, isometric strength.<br /></br>   c. Phase III (2–4 wk): proprioception, balance board, sport-specific drills.<br /></br>3. Bracing/taping ONLY while returning to sport.  </p></br><p>No imaging now because Ottawa rule negative (no bony tenderness + able to walk ≥4 steps after injury &amp; in clinic).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“SPRRAIN” for ankle sprain Tx:<br /></br>S: Stretching/RICE 48 h<br /></br>P: Protect with brace short-term<br /></br>R: Rehab (physical therapy) – most crucial<br /></br>R: Return to activity when pain-free hop<br /></br>A: Avoid prolonged casting (except grade III)<br /></br>I: Instability chronic → consider surgery<br /></br>N: NSAIDs for pain  </p></br><p>Visual mnemonic: Picture the ATFL as a loose “A-Tie-FLoppy” shoelace—tighten it again by exercising the foot, not by locking it in a cast.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Extra-articular platelet-derived growth factor injection<br /></br>   • No evidence that biologic injections speed recovery in simple ligament sprains; reserved for chronic tendinopathies or non-healing ulcers.</p></br><p>b. Immobilization in a short-leg cast<br /></br>   • Appropriate for grade III sprains, fractures, or severe pain preventing weight bearing. Would worsen stiffness in a grade II sprain.</p></br><p>d. Surgical reconstruction of the ankle<br /></br>   • Indicated for chronic (&gt;6 mo) mechanical instability or high-grade ruptures with failed conservative therapy, not an acute partial tear.</p></br><p>e. Taping of the ankle<br /></br>   • Useful prophylactically during sports once rehab is underway; by itself it does not provide the active strengthening &amp; proprioceptive retraining needed to prevent long-term morbidity.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A moderate (grade II) lateral ankle sprain heals best with early functional rehabilitation through structured physical therapy.</p>
154. A 42-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital 2 hours after the onset of pain and numbness in her left arm; she is having difficulty moving her arm. Two weeks ago, she was diagnosed with right popliteal venous thrombosis, and treatment with heparin and warfarin was begun. The left upper extremity is pale and cool; sensation to touch is decreased in a glove distribution distal to the mid forearm. Strength of the intrinsic muscles of the left hand is 2/5. Her prothrombin time is 14 sec (INR=1.3). An uncomplicated brachial embolectomy is performed. Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Arch and left subclavian arteriography</br>b. CT angiography of the chest</br>c. MR venography of the right lower extremity</br>d. Transesophageal echocardiography</br>e. Venous duplex scan of the right lower extremity</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer: D – Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key findings</br>  – 42-year-old (young for atherosclerotic arterial disease).</br>  – Recent right popliteal deep-vein thrombosis (DVT) placed on heparin + warfarin.</br>  – INR only 1.3 → subtherapeutic anticoagulation.</br>  – Sudden onset (2 h) of pain, numbness, pallor, coolness, motor/sensory loss in left arm → acute arterial occlusion.</br>  – Embolectomy from the brachial artery confirms an arterial embolus.</p></br><p>• Diagnostic problem posed</br>  “Which test will most likely CONFIRM the diagnosis?”<br /></br>  The clinical picture suggests a paradoxical embolus: a thrombus that formed in the venous system, escaped anticoagulation, traversed an intracardiac right-to-left shunt (most commonly patent foramen ovale [PFO]), and lodged in a peripheral artery.</p></br><p>• Metacognitive approach</br>  1. Identify acute limb ischemia → embolus vs in-situ thrombosis. Sudden presentation in a young pt with DVT history favors embolus.</br>  2. Ask, “Where could an embolus originate &amp; how did it reach the arterial tree?” Venous clot requires a pathway to arterial side → intracardiac shunt.</br>  3. Pick the imaging study best at detecting PFO or atrial septal defect: TEE.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>• TEE is the gold-standard, minimally invasive study for:</br>  – Detecting PFO/atrial septal defects with color Doppler + bubble study.</br>  – Identifying intracardiac thrombi (left atrial appendage, ventricular apex) and valvular vegetations.</p></br><p>If a PFO is visualized or a right-to-left bubble passage occurs, the paradoxical embolus etiology is confirmed, guiding long-term management (therapeutic anticoagulation ± percutaneous PFO closure).</p></br><p>Take-home: Sudden arterial occlusion in a patient with recent DVT = think paradoxical embolus → order TEE.</p></br><p>Documentation (real-life): Record neurovascular exam, onset time, anticoagulation status, procedural details, and plan for TEE to evaluate intracardiac shunt source.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>1. Venous thrombus (DVT) dislodges → travels to right atrium.</br>2. Through PFO/ASD (right-to-left) during transient ↑right-sided pressures (cough, Valsalva) → enters left atrium/ventricle.</br>3. Embolus travels via arterial circulation → occludes brachial artery → acute limb ischemia.</p></br><p>Risk factors: Hypercoagulable state, inadequate anticoagulation, presence of PFO (in up to 25–30 % of adults).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Summary</br>• Acute limb ischemia: IV heparin, emergent embolectomy or thrombolysis.</br>• Confirm source: TEE ± bubble study.</br>• Long-term: Therapeutic warfarin (INR 2-3) or DOAC; consider PFO closure if recurrent events.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</br>“Painful Pale Paralytic PFO”: Sudden painful pale limb with paralysis → suspect paradoxical embolus through PFO; order TEE.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>A. Arch &amp; left subclavian arteriography<br /></br>   – Shows arterial anatomy/atherosclerosis but won’t reveal the venous source or a cardiac shunt; unnecessary after successful embolectomy.</p></br><p>B. CT angiography of the chest<br /></br>   – Excellent for thoracic aorta pathology or PE but insensitive for intracardiac shunts; higher radiation/contrast exposure.</p></br><p>C. MR venography of the right lower extremity<br /></br>   – Would simply reconfirm the known DVT; offers no information on how clot reached arterial system.</p></br><p>E. Venous duplex scan of the right lower extremity<br /></br>   – Same limitation as (C); already aware of prior DVT.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: Ordering more imaging of the limb or venous system while ignoring the transit route to the arterial tree.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a patient with recent DVT who develops an acute arterial occlusion, think “paradoxical embolus through a PFO.” The best test to confirm that mechanism is transesophageal echocardiography.</p>
155. A 71-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination. She has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, hypertension, and osteoporosis. She quit smoking 15 years ago. She is currently able to walk two blocks without shortness of breath, which is her baseline. Current medications include albuterol, alendronate, beclomethasone, chlorthalidone, and lisinopril. Her blood pressure is 135/80 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no abnormalities. The lungs are clear. Screening mammography 18 months ago showed no abnormalities. She has no history of abnormal mammograms and there is no family history of breast cancer. She asks whether she should continue to receive annual mammography to screen for breast cancer. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation by the physician?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Continue to receive periodic screening mammography until the age of 74 years and then consider discontinuing it based on her overall health</br>b. Discontinue screening mammography based on national recommendations for women and the patient's age</br>c. Discontinue screening mammography because the patient's life expectancy is decreased as a result of her underlying illnesses</br>d. Receive breast MRI rather than mammography to screen for breast cancer based on the patient's age</br>e. Receive screening mammography every other year for the rest of the patient's life</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>A. Continue to receive periodic screening mammography until the age of 74 years and then consider discontinuing it based on her overall health.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues &amp; why they matter<br /></br>• 71-year-old woman – Falls into the USPSTF “50-74 yr” target age band for breast-cancer screening.<br /></br>• Able to walk two blocks without dyspnea; COPD is stable – Suggests a life-expectancy &gt; 10 years. Screening is beneficial only if the patient is likely to live ≥ 10 years to reap a mortality benefit.<br /></br>• No family history, last mammogram normal – Places her at “average risk,” so routine (not high-risk) protocols apply.<br /></br>Approach for similar questions: 1) Identify the guideline source most often tested (USPSTF on Step 2 CK). 2) Check age range &amp; risk level. 3) Estimate life expectancy (&gt;10 y?). 4) Apply or stop screening accordingly.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. USPSTF recommends biennial mammography for average-risk women age 50–74 (Grade B).<br /></br>2. Evidence is insufficient (Grade I) to recommend for or against screening ≥ 75 yrs; decision should be individualized.<br /></br>3. Our patient is 71 → still in the evidence-based window, and her comorbidities are mild; therefore she should continue.<br /></br>4. Plan to reassess at ~75 yrs and base further screening on overall health and patient preference.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Continue screening mammography in average-risk women through age 74 when life expectancy ≥10 y; beyond that, individualize.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Note patient’s functional status, comorbidities, guideline reference, and shared decision-making discussion.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (why screening matters)  </p></br><p>Breast cancer incidence rises with age and peaks in the 60s. Mammography detects ductal carcinoma in situ and early invasive cancers, reducing breast-cancer–specific mortality in women 50-74 with adequate life expectancy. Benefit wanes when life expectancy &lt; 10 years because competing mortality risks outweigh lead-time.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>Screening modality: Digital 2-view mammogram every 2 years (some practices still use annual; “periodic” in the answer choice covers either, but biennial is evidence-based).<br /></br>High-risk women (BRCA carriers, strong family history, chest irradiation &lt;30 yrs) add annual MRI, not needed here.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“5-0 to 7-4, mammo more.”<br /></br>If she’ll live ten more, keep screening for sure;<br /></br>Past 7-4, discuss and explore.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>B. “Discontinue screening based on national recommendations for women and the patient’s age.”<br /></br>• National (USPSTF) guidelines do NOT recommend stopping until 74; stopping at 71 would miss the benefit.</p></br><p>C. “Discontinue … because life expectancy is decreased.”<br /></br>• Mild COPD, controlled HTN/osteoporosis → actuarial life expectancy ≈12–14 years; thus still eligible.</p></br><p>D. “Receive breast MRI rather than mammography.”<br /></br>• MRI is reserved for high-risk populations (e.g., BRCA mutation, strong FHx, prior mantle irradiation). Cost &amp; false positives too high for average risk.</p></br><p>E. “Screen every other year for the rest of the patient’s life.”<br /></br>• Biennial interval is correct, but indefinite continuation ignores the evidence gap ≥75 yrs; must individualize after 74.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A 71-year-old average-risk woman with &gt;10-year life expectancy should continue periodic (biennial) mammography through age 74, reassessing thereafter.</p>
156. A 19-year-old patient, gravida 1, para 1, is examined 1 hour after spontaneous vaginal delivery of a healthy male newborn at term. Pregnancy was uncomplicated. The patient dropped out of high school during the second trimester. The newborn’s father is 19 years of age and is incarcerated for possession of an illicit drug. The patient says the men in her family have histories of aggression, substance abuse, arrests, and incarcerations. She is concerned that her child will follow a similar path. The patient has no history of serious illness. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin. She does not smoke cigarettes, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Arrange for a visiting nurse follow-up examination after discharge</br>b. Contact child protective services</br>c. Recommend the patient have no contact with the newborn’s father</br>d. Recommend the patient place the newborn for adoption</br>e. Recommend psychiatric evaluation of the newborn at 4 years of age</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Arrange for a visiting nurse follow-up examination after discharge  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – 19-year-old first-time mother (adolescent parent = high-risk dyad).<br /></br>  – Father incarcerated, unstable extended-family environment (“histories of aggression, substance abuse, arrests”).<br /></br>  – Mother voices explicit concern about her ability to keep her child on a healthy trajectory.<br /></br>  – No evidence of abuse/neglect, no substance use, no psychiatric illness, baby is healthy.  </p></br><p>• How the clues narrow the differential<br /></br>  – This is not a safety-threat scenario; it is a psychosocial-risk scenario.<br /></br>  – High-quality support and parenting education have been shown to improve outcomes in exactly this population (evidence-based home-visiting programs such as Nurse-Family Partnership).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On NBME/Step 2 questions, if the stem presents risk factors without actual harm, the “supportive services” option is usually correct; mandatory reporting (CPS) is reserved for current or imminent harm.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Identify risk: Teen parent + limited education + incarcerated partner = ↑ risk for poor parenting skills and child maltreatment later.<br /></br>2. Assess current danger: None present now (no drug use, no IPV, no neglect).<br /></br>3. Choose an intervention that mitigates risk without overreacting: Arrange post-discharge home-visiting nurse.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Provide early, preventive, non-punitive support to high-risk families when no abuse has occurred.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Mother is adolescent with limited social support; home-visiting nursing referral placed for parenting education, linkage to community resources, and newborn assessment.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (brief)</br>• Children of adolescent parents and of incarcerated parents have higher rates of developmental delay, abuse, and behavioral problems.<br /></br>• Early home-visiting programs improve maternal knowledge, attachment, immunization rates, and reduce ER visits and future child abuse.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review</br>Home-visiting nurse programs provide:</br>1. Newborn weight, jaundice, breastfeeding assessment.<br /></br>2. Parenting skills coaching.<br /></br>3. Screening for postpartum depression, domestic violence, substance use.<br /></br>4. Connection to WIC, Medicaid, early-intervention services if delays arise.</p></br><p>No labs or imaging are needed now.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“High-Risk but No Harm? Send A Nurse, Not the Police.”<br /></br>Think H-R-N-N: High-risk → Referral → Nurse-visit → Nurturing environment.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>b. Contact child protective services – CPS is for suspected abuse/neglect or imminent danger; none is described. Premature and damages rapport.<br /></br>c. Recommend no contact with the newborn’s father – Courts, not clinicians, decide custody/visitation. Father’s incarceration alone is not grounds to bar contact.<br /></br>d. Recommend adoption – Appropriate only if mother desires it or is unable to care for infant; she expresses desire to parent.<br /></br>e. Psychiatric evaluation at 4 years – Delays support; early intervention is most beneficial in the first months of life.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>In a healthy newborn with an adolescent mother expressing concern but no evidence of abuse, the evidence-based, Step-2 answer is preventive support via a post-discharge home-visiting nurse.</p>
157. A 55-year-old man comes to the office because of a 3-month history of episodes of mild, intermittent, mid chest pain. He says that the episodes occur 3 days weekly and last 2 to 3 minutes. The pain has not increased since his episodes began, and it is relieved by rest and use of antacids. He has not had shortness of breath, nausea, wheezing, or light-headedness. He has hypertension and asthma. His medications are lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and albuterol. He does not smoke cigarettes. He drinks four glasses of wine on weekends. He works as an automobile mechanic. His pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 142/84 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. An ECG shows a normal sinus rhythm and no ST-T wave changes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Cardiac catheterization</br>b. Exercise stress test</br>c. Quantitative coronary artery calcium scoring</br>d. Rest-redistribution thallium scan</br>e. No further testing is indicated</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Exercise stress test</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 55-year-old man (intermediate age-related CAD risk)<br /></br>   • Recurrent substernal “mid-chest” discomfort brought on by activity (occurs while working, relieved by rest) → suspicious for stable ischemic chest pain.<br /></br>   • Lasts minutes, unchanged for 3 months → stable pattern, NOT unstable angina or acute coronary syndrome.<br /></br>   • Able to ambulate, normal resting ECG, normal exam &amp; vitals → qualifies for an exercise treadmill test.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives: exertional, pressure-like chest pain relieved with rest; multiple episodes for ≥2 weeks.<br /></br>   Pertinent negatives: no resting pain, no increasing frequency/intensity, normal resting ECG. These rule against ACS and make invasive cath inappropriate as first test.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When a patient has (a) an intermediate pre-test probability of CAD (men 40-70, women 50-70 with atypical or typical angina) AND (b) can exercise AND (c) has an interpretable baseline ECG (no LBBB, paced rhythm, ST-segment abnormalities), the default first study is a standard exercise ECG stress test.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Estimate pre-test probability of CAD<br /></br>   • Age/sex + chest pain type → intermediate (≈10-90%).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Choose non-invasive test<br /></br>   • Can exercise? Yes.<br /></br>   • Baseline ECG interpretable? Yes (NSR, no ST-T changes).<br /></br>   • Contraindications to exercise? No.<br /></br>   → Exercise treadmill ECG stress test is guideline-preferred.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Core takeaway: For suspected stable angina in a patient with intermediate CAD probability, normal resting ECG, and ability to exercise, the exercise ECG stress test is the most appropriate first diagnostic step.</p></br><p>Documentation note: Chest pain characterization, CAD risk factors, pre-test probability assessment, and rationale for stress testing should be recorded.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>Stable angina = myocardial oxygen demand &gt; supply because of fixed atherosclerotic coronary narrowing. Symptoms are predictable, short-lived, and relieved by rest/nitrates. Identifying inducible ischemia (via stress) guides need for anti-anginal therapy vs revascularization.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Overview  </p></br><p>• Initial dx test (this vignette): Exercise ECG stress test<br /></br>   – Positive = ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping ST depression, chest pain, or hypotension → proceed to stress imaging or coronary angiography.  </p></br><p>• Alternatives (if baseline ECG uninterpretable or cannot exercise):<br /></br>   – Pharmacologic stress (adenosine, dobutamine) with nuclear perfusion or stress echo.  </p></br><p>• Long-term mgmt after diagnosis: lifestyle, antiplatelet (aspirin), high-intensity statin, β-blocker or CCB/nitrates, and risk-factor control; possible PCI/CABG if high-risk lesions.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“NEAT screen” for chest pain work-up:<br /></br>N – Normal baseline ECG?<br /></br>E – Exercise capable?<br /></br>A – Age/sex → intermediate probability?<br /></br>T – Typical/atypical angina pattern?<br /></br>If all “yes,” order an Exercise (Treadmill) ECG.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   • Reserved for high-risk or refractory angina or positive non-invasive test. Invasive, unnecessary first-line for intermediate-risk stable symptoms.</p></br><p>C. Quantitative coronary artery calcium scoring<br /></br>   • Appropriate only for asymptomatic patients for risk stratification. Adds no value once chest pain is present.</p></br><p>D. Rest-redistribution thallium scan (nuclear perfusion at rest)<br /></br>   • Used when resting pain or unable to exercise or baseline ECG uninterpretable. More costly radiation; simple exercise ECG suffices here.</p></br><p>E. No further testing<br /></br>   • Symptomatic patient with intermediate CAD probability needs objective evaluation to confirm ischemia and guide therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In a symptomatic patient with suspected stable angina, an exercise treadmill ECG stress test is the first diagnostic step when the patient can exercise and has an interpretable resting ECG.</p>
158. Four days after admission to the hospital for management of cellulitis of the right lower extremity, a 70-year-old woman has an increased serum creatinine concentration. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications are glipizide and a multivitamin; she also has received intravenous nafcillin since admission. She is retired from the military. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination of the trunk shows no abnormalities. There is edema, erythema, and warmth of the right lower extremity. Examination of the left lower extremity shows no abnormalities. On admission, her serum creatinine concentration was 0.9 mg/dL. Today, laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin        11 g/dL  </br>Hemoglobin A₁c    7.5 percent  </br>Leukocyte count    15,000/mm³  </br>  Segmented neutrophils   75 percent  </br>  Bands                   1 percent  </br>  Eosinophils             8 percent  </br>  Lymphocytes             14 percent  </br>  Monocytes               2 percent  </br>Serum  </br>  K⁺               5 mEq/L  </br>  Urea nitrogen    60 mg/dL  </br>  Creatinine       2.3 mg/dL  </br>  Phosphorus       4.3 mg/dL  </br>  Albumin          2 g/dL  </br>  Cholesterol      250 mg/dL  </br>Urine  </br>  pH               6.00  </br>  Protein          trace  </br>  RBC              10/hpf  </br>  WBC              20–50/hpf  </br>  RBC casts        0/lpf  </br>  WBC casts        0/lpf  </br>Serum complement concentrations are within the reference ranges. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Acute cortical necrosis  </br>b. Acute interstitial nephritis  </br>c. Acute tubular necrosis  </br>d. Obstructive uropathy  </br>e. Rhabdomyolysis  </br>f. Urinary tract infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis (Key Clues &amp; Thought Process)</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>New drug exposure → “intravenous nafcillin since admission.”<br /></br>   • β-lactam antibiotics are classic precipitants of AIN (others: NSAIDs, PPIs, sulfa drugs, rifampin).<br /></br>   • Latency of 3–5 days (re-exposure) or 1–2 weeks (first exposure) is typical.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Acute rise in creatinine → 0.9 → 2.3 mg/dL in 4 days.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Allergic/immune features<br /></br>   • Peripheral eosinophilia 8 % (normal ≤5 %).<br /></br>   • Pyuria (20–50 WBC/hpf) ± mild hematuria/proteinuria.<br /></br>   • Fever or rash not mandatory; only one-third of patients exhibit the full triad.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Urinalysis pattern<br /></br>   • WBCs, RBCs, trace protein, <em>no</em> muddy brown casts (argues against acute tubular necrosis).<br /></br>   • Eosinophiluria (not shown but highly suggestive if present) is classic for AIN.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Complement levels normal → supports an interstitial (not immune-complex glomerular) process.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: “New drug + eosinophils (blood or urine) + AKI = AIN until proven otherwise.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</p></br><p>Step-wise approach:</br>A. Identify acute kidney injury: ΔCr &gt;0.3 mg/dL or &gt;50 % within days.<br /></br>B. Search for reversible prerenal/obstructive causes (vitals N, no bladder distention – unlikely).<br /></br>C. Look for intrinsic renal patterns.<br /></br>   • AIN → drug trigger + eosinophils ± rash/fever; UA shows WBCs, sometimes WBC casts, mild protein/hematuria.<br /></br>   • ATN → nephrotoxin/ischemia + “muddy brown” granular casts.<br /></br>   • Glomerulonephritis → RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs, low complement.<br /></br>D. Match vignette to pattern → fits AIN best.</p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Timing of drug exposure, UA results, eosinophil count, rising Cr, plan to stop nafcillin, consider renal ultrasound, start supportive therapy ± steroids.</p></br><p>Take-home: Always think AIN in a patient with new medication and eosinophilia/pyuria.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</p></br><p>• Hypersensitivity (often type IV) reaction directed against tubular basement membrane antigens after haptenization by offending drug.<br /></br>• Inflammatory infiltrate rich in lymphocytes, plasma cells, and eosinophils → edema and tubular cell injury → impaired filtration/secretion.</p></br><p>Risk factors: β-lactams, sulfonamides, NSAIDs, PPIs, diuretics; infections; sarcoidosis.</p></br><p>Clinical manifestations: AKI, fever, rash, arthralgias, eosinophilia/uria.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Tests &amp; Treatment</p></br><p>Diagnosis:</br>1. UA: WBCs, WBC casts, eosinophils (Hansel stain).<br /></br>2. Renal US usually normal; biopsy definitive (interstitial infiltrate with eosinophils) but rarely needed if classic presentation.</p></br><p>Management:</br>1. STOP the offending drug (most important).<br /></br>2. Supportive care, maintain euvolemia.<br /></br>3. If renal function does not improve in 3–5 days or severe AKI → consider short prednisone taper (evidence shows faster recovery).</p></br><p>Prognosis: Majority recover baseline function if recognized early; chronic interstitial fibrosis possible with delayed diagnosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</p></br><p>“3 E’s of AIN”:<br /></br>• Exposure (new drug)<br /></br>• Eosinophils (blood/urine)<br /></br>• Elevated creatinine</p></br><p>Image: Picture a “β-lactam brick” falling onto a kidney that turns red (hematuria) and white (pyuria) with green spots (eosinophils).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</p></br><p>a. Acute cortical necrosis<br /></br>   – Usually catastrophic (obstetric complications, sepsis, DIC). Presents with anuric AKI and flank pain; UA often shows gross hematuria. No drug trigger or eosinophilia here.</p></br><p>c. Acute tubular necrosis (ATN)<br /></br>   – Etiologies: ischemia, aminoglycosides, IV contrast, myoglobin. Urine: muddy brown granular casts, FENa &gt; 2 %. Peripheral eosinophilia uncommon.</p></br><p>d. Obstructive uropathy<br /></br>   – Requires bilateral obstruction in single-kidney patient or obstruction of solitary kidney; would show hydronephrosis, post-void residual, fluctuating creatinine. No such findings given.</p></br><p>e. Rhabdomyolysis<br /></br>   – Look for muscle pain, dark urine, dipstick “blood” with few/no RBCs, markedly elevated CK, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia. Not present.</p></br><p>f. Urinary tract infection<br /></br>   – Can cause pyuria, but usually with dysuria/frequency, positive nitrites/leukocyte esterase, and <em>does not</em> typically raise creatinine acutely unless complicated by obstruction or sepsis. Peripheral eosinophilia not characteristic.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</p></br><p>Acute rise in creatinine a few days after starting nafcillin + eosinophilia + pyuria/hematuria = classic drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis. Recognize early, discontinue the culprit drug, and renal function usually recovers.</p>
159. A 29-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus comes to the office because of a 5-day history of moderate pain in the right elbow, left knee, and left foot. There is no recent history of trauma. She has no history of sexually transmitted disease or other serious illness. Menses occur at regular 28-day intervals; her last menstrual period ended 2 days ago. Her only medication is methotrexate. She is sexually active with two male partners and uses condoms inconsistently. She is 173 cm (5 ft 8 in) tall and weighs 84 kg (185 lb); BMI is 28 kg/m2. Her temperature is 38.5 °C (101.3 °F); other vital signs are within normal limits. A photograph of the left foot is shown. Examination of the skin shows no other abnormalities. Analysis of synovial fluid shows a leukocyte count of 50,000/mm3. Cultures of blood and synovial fluid grow no organisms. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Exacerbation of systemic lupus erythematosus</br>b. Gout</br>c. Hepatitis B</br>d. HIV infection</br>e. Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Neisseria gonorrhoeae infection  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age/sex &amp; sexual history: 29-year-old woman, two partners, inconsistent condom use → high risk for sexually transmitted infection (STI).<br /></br>• Presentation: 5-day history of fever (38.5 °C) + migratory/polyarticular pain (elbow, knee, foot).<br /></br>• Rash: Photograph of left foot (USMLE usually shows a few hemorrhagic pustules on an erythematous base). No other skin findings.<br /></br>• Synovial fluid: WBC 50 000/mm³ (septic arthritis range). Cultures negative.<br /></br>Key pattern: Fever + migratory polyarthralgia/arthritis + pustular dermatitis + negative routine cultures = classic triad of disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI).</p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. When a young, sexually active patient presents with polyarthritis and fever, always screen for DGI.<br /></br>2. Look for the “3 D’s” mnemonic: Dermatitis, polyarthralgia, and tenosynovitis.<br /></br>3. Remember that routine blood/synovial cultures are often negative; NAAT from mucosal sites is needed.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step linkage to choice E:<br /></br>1. Septic picture (fever, high synovial WBC).<br /></br>2. Polyarticular involvement rather than single joint → think DGI over typical staph septic arthritis.<br /></br>3. Characteristic sparse vesiculopustular skin lesions (picture).<br /></br>4. Negative cultures are common—N. gonorrhoeae is fastidious; special media/NAAT are necessary.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Migratory septic arthritis with pustular rash in a young, sexually active adult = disseminated N. gonorrhoeae until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real patient)<br /></br>• Sexual history, contraceptive use, prior STIs.<br /></br>• Joint exam (erythema, effusion, ROM).<br /></br>• Description of skin lesions.<br /></br>• Synovial analysis results.<br /></br>• Plan: Ceftriaxone + doxycycline (empiric for Chlamydia coinfection), NAAT testing of urine/cervix/pharynx, partner notification.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Neisseria gonorrhoeae spreads hematogenously from mucosal infection.<br /></br>• Two DGI syndromes:<br /></br>  – Triad (dermatitis-tenosynovitis-polyarthralgia).<br /></br>  – Purulent mono/oligo-arthritis without rash.<br /></br>• Virulence: Pili, Opa proteins, LOS; complement deficiency (C5-C9) predisposes.<br /></br>• Immune complex deposition contributes to rash and tenosynovitis.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment High-Yield<br /></br>Diagnostics:<br /></br>• NAAT from urogenital/pharyngeal/rectal sites (most sensitive).<br /></br>• Blood/synovial cultures often negative—use Thayer-Martin or chocolate agar if cultured.  </p></br><p>Treatment (per CDC):<br /></br>1. Ceftriaxone 1 g IV/IM daily (7 days minimum, longer if endocarditis/meningitis).<br /></br>2. PLUS doxycycline 100 mg PO bid × 7 days (covers possible Chlamydia trachomatis coinfection).<br /></br>3. Joint drainage if large effusion.<br /></br>4. Empiric coverage should be started immediately after synovial tap—do not wait for cultures.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>• “ARThritis on the GO” → GO = Gonorrhea.<br /></br>• 3 D’s of DGI: Dermatitis, Disseminated polyarthralgia, Doxy + ceftriaxone (treatment reminder).<br /></br>• Picture mnemonic: Few scattered pustules on palms/soles or distal extremities → think “Gono-dots.”</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>a. SLE exacerbation<br /></br>   – SLE arthritis is non-erosive, symmetric, low-grade inflammation (synovial WBC &lt; 15 000), no high fever, usually negative cultures but rash would be malar/discoid, not pustular.  </p></br><p>b. Gout<br /></br>   – Typically mono-articular (1st MTP), needle-shaped crystals, more common in men; synovial WBC variable but cultures sterile; rash absent; fever less common.  </p></br><p>c. Hepatitis B<br /></br>   – Can cause immune-complex arthropathy but presents as symmetric small-joint pain, often pre-icteric, normal joint fluid, and no pustular rash.  </p></br><p>d.
