HIV infection<br /></br>   – Acute retroviral syndrome causes fever, maculopapular rash, myalgias, but large-joint septic picture with 50 000 WBC and localized pustules is not typical.  </p></br><p>───────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Disseminated Neisseria gonorrhoeae is the prototypical cause of culture-negative, migratory polyarthritis with fever and a sparse pustular rash in a young, sexually active adult. Start ceftriaxone plus doxycycline and obtain NAAT testing.</p>
160. A 7-month-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 1-day history of fussiness. His mother says several children at the patient’s day-care center have been sick during the past week. He has had no change in appetite. His immunizations are up-to-date. He has no history of serious illness and receives no medications. He is at the 10th percentile for length, 15th percentile for weight, and 50th percentile for head circumference. His temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 110/min, and respirations are 32/min. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 97 percent. There are mild intercostal retractions. On pulmonary examination, mild wheezes are heard throughout the lung fields. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. There is no change in examination findings after administration of nebulized albuterol. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Complete blood count</br>b. Oral azithromycin therapy</br>c. Oral prednisolone therapy</br>d. Racemic epinephrine</br>e. No further management is indicated at this time</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. No further management is indicated at this time  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that guide the diagnosis/management:<br /></br>1. Age: 7-month-old (classic age for viral bronchiolitis).<br /></br>2. Season/day-care exposure: “several children … have been sick” → contagious viral illness.<br /></br>3. Presentation: mild fussiness, mild intercostal retractions, diffuse wheezes, normal oxygen saturation (97 %), normal–slightly elevated RR (32/min), afebrile.<br /></br>4. Albuterol trial: “no change in examination findings.” Lack of response favors bronchiolitis over asthma/reactive airway disease and indicates that additional bronchodilators are unlikely to help.<br /></br>5. Growth percentiles and vitals otherwise reassuring.  </p></br><p>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>• Ask: Is the child in respiratory distress (poor feeding, severe retractions, apnea, hypoxemia &lt; 90 %)?<br /></br>   – If YES → consider hospitalization, oxygen, IV fluids ± trial therapies.<br /></br>   – If NO → supportive outpatient care only.<br /></br>• Remember that most interventions (β-agonists, steroids, antibiotics) have not shown clear benefit in uncomplicated bronchiolitis.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic to “No further management”<br /></br>1. Diagnosis: Uncomplicated viral bronchiolitis (most commonly respiratory syncytial virus, RSV).<br /></br>2. Severity assessment:<br /></br>   • SpO₂ 97 % on room air → adequate gas exchange.<br /></br>   • Mild work of breathing, normal feeding/appetite, no apnea → mild disease.<br /></br>3. Evidence-based management:<br /></br>   • Mainstay is supportive care (nasal saline, suctioning, hydration, parent education).<br /></br>   • Guidelines (AAP, CDC) do NOT recommend routine use of bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics, racemic epinephrine, or lab tests in mild cases.<br /></br>   • A single albuterol trial is permissible; if no clinical improvement, it should be discontinued.<br /></br>Therefore, the appropriate “next step” is actually to stop and provide no additional pharmacologic or diagnostic interventions.  </p></br><p>Take-home message:  The vast majority of infants with mild bronchiolitis need nothing beyond reassurance and routine outpatient follow-up.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real-world):  respiratory rate/effort, SpO₂, feeding status, hydration, parental counseling on warning signs (increased work of breathing, decreased intake, cyanosis).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• RSV infects terminal bronchiolar epithelial cells → edema, mucus plugging, airway narrowing → wheezes, crackles.<br /></br>• Peak incidence: 2–6 months; almost all children infected by age 2.<br /></br>• Disease spectrum: mild URI ↔ severe lower-respiratory illness with apnea/respiratory failure (esp. prematurity, chronic lung/heart disease, immunodeficiency).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic Tests &amp; Treatment Review (Brief)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (only if severe/atypical): CXR, viral PCR panel, labs are generally unnecessary.<br /></br>Outpatient treatment:<br /></br>1. Nasal saline + bulb suction.<br /></br>2. Maintain hydration and monitor.<br /></br>3. Educate caregivers; return if SpO₂ &lt; 90 %, poor oral intake, lethargy, apnea, or worsening distress.  </p></br><p>Inpatient criteria: SpO₂ persistently &lt; 90 %, moderate–severe work of breathing, dehydration, underlying high-risk comorbidities, apnea.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Bronchiolitis = Supportive First, Everything Else Last”<br /></br>S – Suction nose<br /></br>U – Use hydration<br /></br>P – Pulse ox monitoring<br /></br>P – Parent teaching<br /></br>O – Oxygen if needed<br /></br>R – Rib retractions/severity dictate admit<br /></br>T – Trial of β-agonist (one time); stop if no response<br /></br>I – Intubate only for apnea/respiratory failure<br /></br>V – Very few meds work<br /></br>E – Every additional test usually Excessive  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Complete blood count<br /></br>   • Routine CBC does not change management in classic mild bronchiolitis; white count poorly predicts bacterial superinfection.<br /></br>   • Would expose infant to painful venipuncture without benefit.  </p></br><p>B. Oral azithromycin therapy<br /></br>   • Macrolides are used for atypical bacterial pneumonia (e.g., Chlamydia, Mycoplasma) or Bordetella pertussis.<br /></br>   • This infant has wheezing without cough paroxysms and normal vitals → viral, not bacterial etiology.  </p></br><p>C. Oral prednisolone therapy<br /></br>   • Corticosteroids do NOT improve outcomes in bronchiolitis unless a co-existing steroid-responsive disease (e.g., asthma) is present, which is very unlikely at 7 months.  </p></br><p>D. Racemic epinephrine<br /></br>   • Racemic epi is primarily indicated for moderate–severe croup (upper airway obstruction, inspiratory stridor).<br /></br>   • Evidence for its use in bronchiolitis is limited; guidelines recommend against routine administration outside a monitored setting for severe disease.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Mild, uncomplicated bronchiolitis in a well-appearing infant with normal oxygen saturation warrants no further pharmacologic or diagnostic intervention beyond supportive outpatient care.</p>
161. A 64-year-old woman comes to the office because of an enlarging, painless mass in her left cheek that she first noticed 2 months ago. She says the mass has doubled in size during this time. She has not sustained any trauma to the area. She was diagnosed with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease 2 years ago. She takes no medications. She has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 50 years. Her pulse is 96/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 136/90 mm Hg. The left cheek at the mandibular angle is larger than the right; a 2 × 2-cm mass fixed to the underlying tissue is palpated. There is no lymphadenopathy. Ultrasonography shows a 2 × 2-cm mass in the superficial left parotid gland. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Excision of the mass</br>b. Left sialography</br>c. MRI of the brain and neck</br>d. PET scan</br>e. Observation only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Excision of the mass (superficial parotidectomy → histopathologic examination)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – “Painless mass” in the parotid region (classic presentation of a salivary-gland tumor).<br /></br>  – “Fixed to the underlying tissue” and “rapidly doubling in 2 months” → red flags for malignancy.<br /></br>  – Age &gt;60 yr and long smoking history (general cancer risk).<br /></br>  – Ultrasound already localized the lesion to the superficial lobe of the parotid.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no facial-nerve palsy and no regional lymph-adenopathy, but fixation + rapid growth still mandate tissue diagnosis.<br /></br>Cognitive approach: When a solid parotid mass is discovered, the priority is obtaining tissue while preserving facial-nerve integrity and preventing tumor spillage. The safest, most definitive way is superficial parotidectomy with the specimen sent to pathology (an “excisional biopsy”).  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1 – Localize: US confirms a 2 × 2 cm superficial-lobe tumor.<br /></br>Step 2 – Assess malignancy risk: fixed, fast-growing mass in older patient → high.<br /></br>Step 3 – Choose diagnostic method:<br /></br>• FNA is popular clinically, but on NBME/Step questions it may not be offered because of 1) false-negative rate and 2) risk of seeding.<br /></br>• Core-needle/open biopsy in situ is contraindicated—may injure the facial nerve and seed tumor cells.<br /></br>Therefore, proceed directly to superficial parotidectomy; this removes the tumor en bloc and yields the histologic diagnosis in one procedure.</p></br><p>Take-home: A suspicious parotid mass requires surgical excision of the superficial lobe for both diagnosis and definitive initial treatment.</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): “Discussed risks/benefits of superficial parotidectomy, including facial-nerve injury, Frey syndrome, hematoma, need for possible completion parotidectomy if carcinoma confirmed.”</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• 80% of parotid tumors are benign (most commonly pleomorphic adenoma), but malignancy likelihood rises with rapid growth, fixation, pain, facial-nerve palsy, or deep-lobe location.<br /></br>• Common malignant salivary tumors: mucoepidermoid carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma, acinic cell carcinoma.<br /></br>• These lesions spread along nerves and can metastasize to lung and bone—hence the urgency of removal.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Superficial parotidectomy (includes facial-nerve dissection).<br /></br>2. If malignancy confirmed, staging CT/MRI ± PET and possible completion parotidectomy + neck dissection ± postoperative radiotherapy.<br /></br>3. Imaging such as MRI or PET is for staging after histologic diagnosis, not before tissue is obtained.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“PAROTID” mnemonic for suspicious features:<br /></br>P – Pain<br /></br>A – Age &gt;50<br /></br>R – Rapid growth<br /></br>O – Overlying skin fixation<br /></br>T – Tumor hard<br /></br>I – Immobile/fixed<br /></br>D – Droop (facial-nerve palsy)<br /></br>If ≥1 of the above, think malignancy → take it out.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>b. Sialography – Used for duct obstruction/sialolithiasis, not solid tumors. Contrast injection is painful and non-diagnostic for cancer.<br /></br>c. MRI of the brain and neck – Helpful for deep-lobe or skull-base spread once cancer is proven. Not first step when tissue diagnosis is lacking and lesion is superficial.<br /></br>d. PET scan – Whole-body staging tool after confirmed malignancy; too premature here.<br /></br>e. Observation only – Rapid growth and fixation contraindicate watchful waiting; delays diagnosis and treatment of possible cancer.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Core Takeaway<br /></br>A fixed, rapidly enlarging parotid mass in an older patient is presumed malignant until proven otherwise. The NBME/Step 2CK “best next step” is superficial parotidectomy for excisional biopsy—obtaining diagnosis while minimizing facial-nerve injury and tumor seeding.</p>
162. A 62-year-old man, who is recovering in the coronary care unit following an acute ST-segment elevation anterior wall myocardial infarction 4 days ago, is being evaluated for the sudden onset of dyspnea and diaphoresis. At the time of admission, tissue plasminogen activator therapy was initiated, and the patient's recovery had been uncomplicated. Medical history otherwise is unremarkable, and he takes no routine medications. Vital signs are temperature 37°C (98.6°F), pulse 128/min, respirations 36/min, and blood pressure 82/58 mm Hg. Pulse oximetry on 100 percent oxygen via nonrebreather face mask shows an oxygen saturation of 89 percent. Jugular venous pressure is 8 cm H₂O (N=5–9). Auscultation of the lungs discloses diffuse crackles halfway up the back. Cardiac examination discloses a hyperdynamic precordium and a grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur that was not present on previous examinations. There is no clubbing, cyanosis, or edema of the extremities. Based on these findings, the patient's condition is most likely attributable to which of the following?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Aortic valvular insufficiency</br>b. Chordae tendineae rupture</br>c. Extension of myocardial infarction</br>d. Left ventricular free wall rupture</br>e. Tricuspid valve insufficiency</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>b. Chordae tendineae rupture (→ acute severe mitral regurgitation)</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Timing: 3–5 days after an acute ST-elevation MI – classic window for mechanical complications due to coagulation-necrosis-induced weakening of intracardiac structures.  </li></br><li>Sudden cardiogenic shock: hypotension (82/58 mm Hg), tachycardia (128/min), tachypnea (36/min), O₂ sat only 89 % despite 100 % FiO₂.  </li></br><li>Pulmonary edema: diffuse crackles halfway up the lung fields.  </li></br><li>NEW holosystolic murmur (grade 3/6) – appeared after the infarct.  </li></br><li>Hyperdynamic precordium, but jugular venous pressure is only high-normal (8 cm H₂O) → favors acute left-sided volume overload, not pericardial tamponade or primary right-sided pathology.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive approach for test day:<br /></br>• Whenever a patient 2–7 days post-MI suddenly decompensates, immediately think “mechanical complication” – papillary muscle rupture, chordae tendineae rupture, interventricular septal rupture, or free-wall rupture.<br /></br>• Pin the diagnosis to the type of murmur, the side of congestion (pulmonary vs systemic), and the JVP.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why option B is correct)  </p></br><p>• Anterior wall MI (LAD territory) can extend to the anterolateral papillary muscle or its chordae → sudden loss of tethering → flail mitral leaflet → acute severe mitral regurgitation (MR).<br /></br>• Acute MR produces:<br /></br>  – Holosystolic blowing murmur best at the apex ± radiating to axilla.<br /></br>  – Flash pulmonary edema (blood regurgitates into low-pressure LA &amp; lungs).<br /></br>  – Cardiogenic shock with relatively normal JVP (no time for chronic right-side backup).<br /></br>• Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) increases the risk of hemorrhagic transformation and mechanical rupture – another board-favorite clue.  </p></br><p>Take-home: A new holosystolic murmur + pulmonary edema + shock 3–5 days post-MI = acute MR due to papillary muscle or chordae tendineae rupture. Document murmur characteristics, hemodynamics, and urgent surgical consult in the chart.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• During the first week after MI, the necrotic myocardium is invaded by neutrophils and macrophages; the wall is soft and friable → structural failure.<br /></br>• Posteromedial papillary muscle (RCA supply) is the single most common muscle to rupture, but any ischemic papillary muscle or its chordae can tear.<br /></br>• Acute MR causes sudden LV forward-stroke-volume loss → ↑LA &amp; pulmonary capillary pressures → pulmonary edema; because LA has not dilated, even small regurgitant volumes cause large pressure rises.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>Immediate work-up:<br /></br>• STAT transthoracic or transesophageal echo – confirms flail leaflet, quantifies MR, and assesses LV function.<br /></br>• Right-heart cath shows giant v-waves in LA/PCWP tracing if done.</p></br><p>Management:<br /></br>1. Afterload reduction to promote forward flow: IV nitroprusside.<br /></br>2. Inotropic support: dobutamine.<br /></br>3. Intra-aortic balloon pump (IABP) as bridge.<br /></br>4. Definitive therapy = emergent surgical repair or replacement of the mitral valve ± coronary artery bypass.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“PICS” for 3-5 day mechanical complications after MI:<br /></br>P – Papillary muscle or chordae tear → acute MR (holosystolic murmur, pulmonary edema).<br /></br>I – Interventricular septal rupture → harsh LSB murmur + biventricular failure.<br /></br>C – “Crackling” lungs in MR vs Clear lungs in tamponade.<br /></br>S – Sudden deterioration, Surgical emergency.  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a parachute (mitral valve) whose cords (chordae) snap three days after a storm (MI) – the parachute flails and air (blood) whooshes backward.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Aortic valvular insufficiency<br /></br>   • Gives an early diastolic, high-pitched, decrescendo murmur best at the left sternal border, not holosystolic.<br /></br>   • Causes bounding peripheral pulses and wide pulse pressure – not present here.</p></br><p>c. Extension of myocardial infarction<br /></br>   • Presents with recurrent chest pain, new ST-segment changes, enzyme rise; no new loud murmur or acute pulmonary edema solely from extension.</p></br><p>d. Left ventricular free wall rupture<br /></br>   • Typically 5–14 days post-MI; produces pericardial tamponade: JVP markedly ↑, muffled heart sounds, pulseless electrical activity, CLEAR lungs (no MR-type pulmonary edema), and no prominent murmur.</p></br><p>e. Tricuspid valve insufficiency<br /></br>   • Holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign).<br /></br>   • Leads to systemic venous congestion: high JVP, hepatomegaly, peripheral edema, clear lungs.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Takeaway<br /></br>In the first week after an MI, a NEW holosystolic murmur with pulmonary edema and shock = acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle/chordae tendineae rupture—an emergent surgical problem.</p>
163. A 17-year-old girl comes to the office for an examination required for sports participation. Medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She is 165 cm (5 ft 5 in) tall and weighs 54 kg (120 lb); BMI is 20 kg/m2. At her last visit 1 year ago, BMI was 23 kg/m2. The patient says she lost the weight by "eating healthy and exercising." She adds, "I would like to lose a little more weight." She began a vegetarian diet 6 weeks ago. Vital signs are normal with a pulse of 64/min; however, her pulse 1 year ago was 80/min. The remainder of the physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Admit the patient to an outpatient treatment program for eating disorders</br>b. Advise the patient to avoid losing any additional weight and schedule a follow-up examination in 1 month</br>c. Counsel the patient to stop exercising and refuse to sign her sports participation form until she returns in 1 week for a follow-up examination</br>d. Praise the patient for her healthy habits and encourage her to continue them</br>e. Recommend adding iron supplementation to the patient's vegetarian diet</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Advise the patient to avoid losing any additional weight and schedule a follow-up examination in 1 month  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues in the stem<br /></br>• 17-year-old female – peak age for onset of eating disorders.<br /></br>• BMI fell from 23 → 20 kg/m² (normal range is 18.5-24.9). Loss ≈ 3 kg/m² (~7 lb).<br /></br>• States she “would like to lose a little more weight” → mild weight/shape preoccupation.<br /></br>• New vegetarian diet (food-group elimination can signal restrictive patterns).<br /></br>• HR 64/min (normal, but down from 80/min – could be athletic conditioning or early physiologic adaptation to caloric restriction).<br /></br>• No physical stigmata of malnutrition, vitals otherwise stable.  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: recent weight loss, desire for further weight loss.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: BMI still normal, no marked bradycardia (&lt;60), no hypotension, dizziness, amenorrhea, lanugo, or orthostatic changes.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test day<br /></br>1. Ask: “Does she meet DSM-5 anorexia criteria (BMI &lt;18.5 or &lt;85 % expected, fear of weight gain, body-image distortion)?” → No.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Are there physiologic red flags that mandate urgent referral/hospitalization (HR &lt;40, BP &lt;80/60, arrhythmia, orthostasis, electrolyte derangement)?” → No.<br /></br>3. Therefore provide anticipatory guidance + close follow-up to monitor for progression.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Why option B is best<br /></br>• Patient is at <strong>risk</strong> but does not yet fulfill diagnostic or severity criteria for anorexia nervosa.<br /></br>• Early, non-stigmatizing intervention: “Let’s keep you where you are, no more weight loss” + set a short-interval re-check (1 month) to monitor weight, vitals, menstrual status, and eating behaviors.<br /></br>• Builds rapport, allows early detection if she continues to lose weight, and can escalate care promptly if needed.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life)<br /></br>– Current weight/BMI trend, dietary changes, exercise habits, vital signs.<br /></br>– Counseling provided: advised no further weight loss and reason.<br /></br>– Plan: repeat weight and vitals in 4 weeks; return sooner if dizziness, missed menses, or further weight drop.  </p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>Adolescents with mild weight loss but normal BMI and no physiologic compromise require counseling against additional loss and <strong>close surveillance</strong>, not immediate specialty referral.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Anorexia nervosa spectrum<br /></br>• Restrictive caloric intake → catabolism, bradycardia (vagal tone ↑), hypotension, amenorrhea (GnRH ↓), osteopenia (estrogen ↓), electrolyte abnormalities.<br /></br>• Severity guides setting of treatment:<br /></br>  – BMI 17-18.5: outpatient counseling ± nutritionist.<br /></br>  – BMI &lt;17 or vitals instability: intensive outpatient or inpatient program.<br /></br>  – HR &lt;40, BP &lt;80/60, K⁺ &lt;2.5 mEq/L, syncope → hospitalize.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (overview)  </p></br><p>Current patient:<br /></br>• No labs or imaging needed now; repeat vitals &amp; weight in 1 month.<br /></br>• If weight ↓ further or vitals decline → order CBC, CMP, phosphate, EKG; refer to multidisciplinary eating-disorder team.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“18-40-60 Rule” for escalation<br /></br>• BMI &lt;18 → consider referral.<br /></br>• HR &lt;40 or BP &lt;60 diastolic → urgent inpatient.<br /></br>Remember: “Under 18 BMI or under 40 beats buys you a bed.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Admit to outpatient eating-disorder program<br /></br>– Indicated when patient meets anorexia criteria or shows physiologic compromise. Her BMI is normal and vitals are stable, so this is premature.  </p></br><p>C. Stop exercising &amp; refuse sports clearance, return in 1 week<br /></br>– Overly punitive, may damage rapport and is unnecessary without signs of exercise purging or significant malnutrition. A 1-month interval is adequate.  </p></br><p>D. Praise and encourage current habits<br /></br>– Reinforces continued weight loss despite her expressed desire to lose more, potentially exacerbating disordered eating.  </p></br><p>E. Begin iron supplementation<br /></br>– Vegetarian diet for only 6 weeks; no signs/symptoms of anemia, no lab confirmation of iron deficiency. Routine supplementation is not evidence-based here.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: A normal-BMI adolescent who has lost modest weight and voices desire for further loss warrants counseling to halt additional weight loss and a short-interval follow-up, not immediate specialty referral.</p>
164. A 52-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police 30 minutes after being found wandering in the park. On arrival, he appears confused and agitated. He is tremulous. He is 1 80 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 59 kg (130 lb); BMI is 18 kg/m2. His temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg. Examination shows telangiectasias over the face and upper and lower extremities. The liver edge is palpated 4 cm below the right costal margin. Muscle bulk is decreased throughout; deep tendon reflexes are 3+ throughout. Babinski sign is absent. His gait is ataxic. On mental status examination, he refuses to answer any questions. Laboratory studies show:</br>Serum</br>Na+    135 mEq/L</br>K+     3.5 mEq/L</br>Cl-    93 mEq/L</br>Glucose    100 mg/dL</br>AST    90 U/L</br>ALT    65 U/L</br>Blood alcohol    90 mg/dL</br>Urine    positive for Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol</br>Intravenous administration of which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy in this patient?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Dextrose followed by potassium and magnesium</br>b. Dextrose followed by vitamin B1 (thiamine) and magnesium</br>c. Potassium and magnesium followed by 0.9 percent saline</br>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine)</br>e. Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin) and potassium followed by 0.9 percent saline</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Vitamin B1 (thiamine) IV</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>Key clues that scream chronic alcohol use + thiamine deficiency risk:<br /></br>• Found wandering, confused, agitated, ataxic gait, tremulous → neurologic dysfunction.<br /></br>• Telangiectasias, hepatomegaly, AST &gt; ALT (90 &gt; 65) → chronic alcoholic liver disease.<br /></br>• BMI 18 kg/m², ↓ muscle bulk → malnutrition.<br /></br>• Blood alcohol 90 mg/dL (still intoxicated) and THC (+).  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives for impending Wernicke encephalopathy (WE) = “CAN” triad:<br /></br>C – Confusion (present)<br /></br>A – Ataxia (present)<br /></br>N – Nystagmus/ophthalmoplegia (may appear next)  </p></br><p>Testing mind-set: anytime a malnourished or alcoholic patient needs glucose, FIRST give thiamine or you can precipitate WE. The question explicitly asks, “Which IV medication prevents Wernicke encephalopathy?” → thiamine alone.</p></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive tip: As soon as you see an alcoholic in the ED, mentally repeat: “Thiamine before glucose or they’ll lose their memory.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify risk (chronic alcohol + malnutrition).<br /></br>2. Recognize early WE signs.<br /></br>3. Understand pathophysiology: glucose infusion raises cellular metabolic rate and rapidly consumes residual thiamine, unmasking or worsening WE.<br /></br>4. Intervention hierarchy:<br /></br>   Step 1 – 100 mg IV thiamine.<br /></br>   Step 2 – THEN give dextrose-containing fluids, electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺), multivitamins, etc.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: In alcoholics, always administer IV thiamine before any glucose load.</p></br><p>Documentation must include: mental status, gait, suspected WE risk, thiamine administration prior to glucose.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Thiamine (vitamin B1) is a cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, and transketolase.<br /></br>• Deficiency → impaired cerebral energy metabolism → focal petechial hemorrhages around the 3rd/4th ventricles (mammillary bodies).<br /></br>• Untreated WE can progress to Korsakoff syndrome (irreversible anterograde amnesia, confabulation).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls<br /></br>Immediate: 100 mg IV thiamine → 5% dextrose + normal saline, replace Mg²⁺/K⁺ (Mg is a cofactor for transketolase, so correct low Mg for thiamine to work).<br /></br>Diagnostics (not needed before thiamine): CT/MRI may show mammillary body changes but WE is a CLINICAL diagnosis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>Mnemonic “Bananas Before Bread”:<br /></br>B1 Before (thiamine first)<br /></br>Bread (glucose)<br /></br>Visualize handing a banana (vit B1) to the patient before a slice of bread (carbs).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Dextrose then K &amp; Mg – No thiamine: glucose first could precipitate or worsen WE.<br /></br>b. Dextrose then thiamine &amp; Mg – Sequence reversed; still risky.<br /></br>c. K &amp; Mg then saline – Omits thiamine entirely.<br /></br>e. Vitamin B12 + K followed by saline – Treats possible pernicious anemia, not WE; B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration, not acute WE.</p></br><p>Beware NBME trap: they often switch the order of glucose and thiamine—always choose thiamine first.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line: Give IV thiamine immediately to any malnourished or alcoholic patient before administering dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.</p>
165. A 35-year-old man with Crohn disease returns to the clinic for follow-up 4 months after undergoing uncomplicated surgical repair of an enterovesical fistula. Medical history otherwise is unremarkable. Trials of 5-aminosalicylic acid, azathioprine, and prednisone prescribed since diagnosis 2 years ago have not controlled his Crohn disease; his only current medication is a daily multivitamin. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 15 years and occasionally drinks alcoholic beverages. The patient is 180 cm (5 ft 11 in) tall and weighs 68 kg (150 lb); BMI is 21 kg/m2. Temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 85/min and regular, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 114/68 mm Hg. Lungs are clear to auscultation. Cardiac examination discloses a grade 1/6 systolic ejection murmur heard best at the upper right sternal border. Abdominal examination shows a well-healed surgical scar. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no tenderness to palpation or percussion of the abdomen. Infliximab therapy is initiated. Which of the following studies is most appropriate to obtain at follow-up examination in 1 month?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Chest x-ray</br>b. Complete blood count</br>c. Echocardiography</br>d. Liver function tests</br>e. Measurement of serum urea nitrogen and creatinine concentrations</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: B. Complete blood count (CBC)</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues: Crohn disease refractory to 5-ASA/azathioprine/steroids → now starting infliximab (anti-TNF-α monoclonal antibody).<br /></br>• Time frame: “follow-up examination in 1 month” (very early safety check).<br /></br>• Clinical goal: choose the single most appropriate study to look for early drug toxicity/complication of infliximab.<br /></br>Metacognitive tip: When you see a biologic agent (infliximab, adalimumab, etanercept), think “infection risk + bone-marrow suppression + liver issues.” Ask yourself which test detects the earliest, most dangerous problem that can appear within the first few weeks.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Anti-TNF agents can cause early, clinically silent leukopenia, neutropenia, or pancytopenia → a CBC will uncover this before a life-threatening infection develops.<br /></br>2. Other adverse effects (hepatotoxicity, heart failure exacerbation, demyelination) usually appear later or are monitored less frequently.<br /></br>3. Therefore, the highest-yield one-month study is a CBC.</p></br><p>Take-home: For patients started on anti-TNF therapy, obtain baseline TB screening &amp; hepatitis serologies, then perform a CBC about 4 weeks after the first dose to detect cytopenias.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• TNF-α is crucial for granuloma maintenance and neutrophil/macrophage activation. Blocking it impairs host defense, occasionally suppresses marrow, and may unmask latent infections.<br /></br>• Reported hematologic toxicities: leukopenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, rare aplastic anemia—all detectable on routine CBC.<br /></br>• Frequency: Most gastroenterology &amp; rheumatology protocols recommend CBC (and LFT) at baseline; CBC again at 4–8 weeks; then every 3 months while on therapy.</p></br><p>Treatments/Diagnostics Summary<br /></br>Baseline before first dose:<br /></br>– Tuberculosis test (PPD or IGRA) ± chest x-ray if positive/unknown TB history.<br /></br>– Hepatitis B/C serologies.<br /></br>– CBC &amp; LFT.<br /></br>Follow-up schedule (common):<br /></br>• 4–8 weeks: CBC (± LFT).<br /></br>• Every 3 mo thereafter: CBC, LFT.</p></br><p>Memorization Tip<br /></br>“TNF-α INHIBIT” =<br /></br>T – TB test first<br /></br>N – Neutrophils (check CBC 1 month)<br /></br>F – Function of liver (LFT q3 mo)<br /></br>A – Avoid live vaccines<br /></br>H – Heart failure caution<br /></br>I – Infections (watch closely)<br /></br>B – Bone-marrow suppression (CBC)<br /></br>IT – demyelinating disease, malignancy</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Chest x-ray – Valuable at baseline to look for latent TB, but not needed routinely 1 month later if baseline screen was negative and no new pulmonary symptoms.<br /></br>C. Echocardiography – Anti-TNF agents can worsen pre-existing class III/IV heart failure, but the vignette patient has no cardiac history or symptoms. Routine echo in 1 month is not standard.<br /></br>D. Liver function tests – Hepatotoxicity is possible, but clinically significant liver injury usually develops later. Guidelines place LFT monitoring at 3-month intervals, not 1 month, making CBC the better early study.<br /></br>E. Serum BUN and creatinine – Anti-TNF agents are not nephrotoxic; no indication for early renal monitoring.</p></br><p>Bottom line: One month after initiating infliximab, the single most appropriate test is a CBC to detect drug-related cytopenias before they become clinically dangerous.</p>
166. A researcher conducts a randomized controlled trial of a new drug to treat chronic hepatitis B infection. A total of 400 patients are needed in order to detect a 10 percent difference in remission rates between the study drug and standard treatment groups. The researcher sets an α level of 0.05 and a β level of 0.20. A total of 500 patients are enrolled in the study, and 480 complete it. Patients are monitored for adverse events and for remission of the disease. The results are shown:</br>Study Drug Group,  percent    Standard Treatment Group,  percent    P-value</br>Remission rate         20                           10      .12</br>Adverse event rate     10                            6      .07</br>Which of the following is the most likely cause of the lack of difference in the adverse event rate?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Lack of clinical response to the study drug</br>b. More patients than needed were enrolled in the study</br>c. Number of patients lost to follow-up</br>d. Type I error</br>e. Type II error</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Type II error (β error)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues<br /></br>  – The trial is powered (β = 0.20 → 80 % power) only to detect a 10 % difference in the <strong>primary outcome</strong> (remission).<br /></br>  – Adverse-event (AE) rate is a <strong>secondary</strong> outcome; the observed difference is 4 % (10 % vs 6 %).<br /></br>  – P = 0.07 (&gt; α = 0.05), so the null hypothesis of “no AE difference” is <strong>not rejected</strong>.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>  – Enrolled/completed &gt; calculated sample size (500/480 vs 400) → adequate for remission but not necessarily for AE detection.<br /></br>  – Only 20 patients lost to follow-up (4 %) → unlikely to alter power substantially.<br /></br>• Metacognitive tip<br /></br>  – Always ask: “Was the study powered for this specific endpoint?” If not, a nonsignificant P-value often reflects limited power (β error) rather than a true lack of effect.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-by-step logic:<br /></br>1. AE endpoint shows a clinically relevant 4 % absolute difference.<br /></br>2. The trial was <strong>not</strong> designed (powered) to detect so small a difference.<br /></br>3. A nonsignificant P-value in an underpowered analysis = higher probability of Type II error (false negative).<br /></br>Take-home: Failure to reach statistical significance can stem from inadequate power, especially for secondary outcomes or smaller effect sizes.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: If this were a real study, you would note that the AE analysis is exploratory and underpowered, so conclusions about safety differences are limited.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Not directly applicable—this is a biostatistics/power question. Remember:<br /></br>• α (Type I error) = probability of false positive.<br /></br>• β (Type II error) = probability of false negative. Power = 1 – β.<br /></br>• Detecting smaller effect sizes requires larger sample sizes.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>No specific clinical interventions are required to understand the statistical concept here.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Beta Blinds” → β error blinds you to a real difference.<br /></br>“Under-Powered = Under-Detects.”</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Lack of clinical response to the study drug<br /></br>   – Would explain remission results, not AE rates; doesn’t address statistical nonsignificance.<br /></br>b. More patients than needed were enrolled<br /></br>   – Extra patients increase, not decrease, power—opposite of what would cause a false negative.<br /></br>c. Number of patients lost to follow-up<br /></br>   – Only 4 %; unlikely to meaningfully alter power.<br /></br>d. Type I error<br /></br>   – Would be a false <strong>positive</strong> (significant result when none exists). Here the result is nonsignificant.<br /></br>e. Type II error <strong>(correct)</strong><br /></br>   – False negative due to insufficient power for the AE endpoint → explains the lack of observed difference.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: The study was not powered to detect the smaller 4 % difference in adverse events, making a Type II error the most plausible explanation for the nonsignificant AE finding.</p>
167. A 13-year-old boy is brought to the office because of a 2-week history of a painless mass in the right thigh. He noticed the mass after a teammate hit him in the same area during a soccer game. He has not had weight loss, fatigue, or fever. He has no history of serious illness and takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the anterior aspect of the right thigh shows a 5-cm firm, nontender mass. Range of motion of the right knee is full and does not produce pain. Femoral and distal pulses are palpable. Sensation is intact. MRI of the right thigh shows a soft-tissue mass in the anterior compartment that is not adjacent to major arteries or nerves. An incisional biopsy specimen shows moderate-grade fibrosarcoma. Which of the following studies is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest and abdomen</br>b. ECG</br>c. Femoral arteriography</br>d. Genetic testing</br>e. Ultrasonography of the scrotum</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selected</br>A. CT scan of the chest and abdomen</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key clues</br>  – “13-year-old boy … painless 5-cm firm, nontender mass in the thigh” → classic presentation for a primary soft-tissue sarcoma (in this case, biopsy-proven fibrosarcoma).</br>  – MRI already obtained → defines local anatomy; shows tumor not invading major neurovascular structures.</br>  – Incisional biopsy has provided the histologic diagnosis and grade (moderate-grade).</p></br><p>• What the examiner is really asking:<br /></br>  “Now that you have a tissue diagnosis of a soft-tissue sarcoma in an extremity, what is the next staging study to look for the most common site of distant spread?”</p></br><p>• Pertinent negatives: no constitutional (“B-”) symptoms, no neurovascular compromise → no need for emergent surgery or vascular imaging.</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip:<br /></br>For any confirmed extremity soft-tissue sarcoma on Step exams, think “Local MRI for extent, then chest ± abdominal CT for distant staging” before definitive limb-sparing resection ± chemo/RT.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Biopsy = diagnosis &amp; grade.<br /></br>2. Next step = staging to guide prognosis and treatment planning.<br /></br>3. Hematogenous spread is vastly more common than lymphatic spread for most soft-tissue sarcomas (lung first, then occasionally liver).<br /></br>4. Therefore CT chest (and often abdomen/pelvis) is the standard initial metastatic work-up.</p></br><p>Take-home message:<br /></br>In an extremity soft-tissue sarcoma, obtain cross-sectional imaging of the lungs (± abdomen) immediately after biopsy confirmation.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl:<br /></br>“CT chest/abdomen ordered for metastatic staging of moderate-grade fibrosarcoma prior to multidisciplinary tumor-board discussion and surgical planning.”</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review (High-yield)</br>• Soft-tissue sarcomas arise from mesenchymal tissue (fibrous, adipose, muscle, etc.).<br /></br>• Metastasize hematogenously via venous drainage; lungs receive first pass → &gt;80% of distant mets.<br /></br>• Lymph-node spread is rare except for rhabdomyosarcoma, synovial sarcoma, epithelioid sarcoma, clear-cell sarcoma.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>• Standard staging:<br /></br>  – MRI of primary site (already done)<br /></br>  – CT chest ± abdomen/pelvis (correct answer)<br /></br>  – Bone scan or PET if high-grade/large tumors</p></br><p>• Definitive therapy: wide local excision with limb-sparing approach + adjuvant radiation; chemo considered for higher-grade/large lesions.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Sarcoma Staging = MLC”</br>M – MRI for local extent<br /></br>L – Lungs look first (CT chest ± abd)<br /></br>C – Cut it out (Wide resection) after staging complete</p></br><p>Visualize the venous blood leaving the leg, traveling straight to the lungs—reminds you why chest CT is mandatory.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>B. ECG<br /></br>• Anthracycline cardiotoxicity monitoring is needed only AFTER chemotherapy is planned/started. No role before staging.</p></br><p>C. Femoral arteriography<br /></br>• Vascular mapping is considered when tumor abuts major vessels (risk to limb perfusion). MRI already showed it is “not adjacent to major arteries or nerves,” making angiography unnecessary.</p></br><p>D. Genetic testing<br /></br>• Some pediatric sarcomas (e.g., Ewing translocation t(11;22)) need molecular confirmation when histology is equivocal. Here, classical fibrosarcoma already confirmed on biopsy; results would not change initial staging/management.</p></br><p>E. Ultrasonography of the scrotum<br /></br>• Would be relevant in suspected paratesticular rhabdomyosarcoma, not in a thigh mass proven as fibrosarcoma.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>After biopsy confirms an extremity soft-tissue sarcoma, the single most important next study is a CT scan of the chest (and typically abdomen) to look for lung metastases before proceeding to definitive local therapy.</p>
168. A 17-year-old boy comes to the urgent treatment center because of a 4-day history of abdominal pain, loss of appetite, nausea, chills, and fever, which has responded to acetaminophen. He has not had headache, ear pain, or sore throat. He is able to tolerate fluids, but eating solid food increases his abdominal pain. Medical history is remarkable for seasonal allergies and lactose intolerance. His only routine medication is as-needed loratadine. He does not drink alcoholic beverages or use illicit drugs. He is monogamous with his girlfriend and engages in oral sex only. He returned home from a trip to Mexico with his family 3 weeks ago. Temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 100/60 mm Hg. The patient appears jaundiced. There is abdominal tenderness in the right upper quadrant without rebound or guarding. Murphy sign is absent. Results of which of the following serologic studies are most likely to confirm the diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a.  Hepatitis A IgG</br>b.  Hepatitis A IgM</br>c.  Hepatitis B IgM</br>d.  Hepatitis B surface antigen</br>e.  Hepatitis C IgG</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Hepatitis A virus (HAV) IgM antibody</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test‐Taking Strategy  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • Returned from Mexico (endemic area, fecal-oral transmission)<br /></br>   • Incubation ≈3 weeks after travel → HAV incubation 2-6 weeks<br /></br>   • Acute prodrome (fever, chills, anorexia, nausea) followed by jaundice &amp; RUQ pain<br /></br>   • No parenteral or high-risk sexual exposures → makes HBV/HCV less likely<br /></br>   • Murphy sign negative → argues against acute cholecystitis<br /></br>   Together these point toward an acute viral hepatitis acquired by the fecal-oral route, i.e., hepatitis A.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: “Is this hepatocellular injury?” → Jaundice + RUQ pain + travel = think viral hepatitis.<br /></br>   • Step 2: “Which virus best fits exposure/incubation?” → Travel/food → HAV.<br /></br>   • Step 3: “Which serology confirms <em>acute</em> HAV?” → Anti-HAV IgM.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: For any patient with jaundice after recent travel or contaminated food/water, default to HAV unless clear parenteral risk factors push you toward HBV/HCV.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Anti-HAV IgM is detectable at symptom onset, peaks in the first few weeks, and disappears within 6 months. It is the gold standard for diagnosing <em>current</em> HAV infection. Anti-HAV IgG appears later and confers lifelong immunity but does <strong>not</strong> distinguish recent from remote infection.</p></br><p>Chart note (real-life): “17-y/o male with acute onset jaundice 3 wks after Mexico travel; plan: check anti-HAV IgM, LFTs, supportive care, notify public health.”</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Suspect HAV after travel/foodborne exposure; confirm with IgM.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology High-Yield Review  </p></br><p>• Virus: Non-enveloped RNA picornavirus, fecal-oral spread via contaminated water/food (e.g., shellfish).<br /></br>• Incubation: 15-50 days.<br /></br>• Disease course: Always acute; no chronicity.<br /></br>• Immunity: Lifelong after infection or 2-dose vaccine.<br /></br>• Treatment: Supportive; avoid hepatotoxins (acetaminophen, alcohol). Post-exposure prophylaxis with HAV vaccine or immune globulin within 2 weeks of exposure.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“Acute = A = IgM”<br /></br>• IgM → “M for <em>M</em>iserable right now” (current infection)<br /></br>• IgG → “G for <em>G</em>one” (past infection or vaccine)</p></br><p>Picture a red “M” stamp on a passport coming back from Mexico = acute HAV.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>A. Hepatitis A IgG<br /></br>   • Indicates past infection or vaccination; not diagnostic of current illness.</p></br><p>C. Hepatitis B IgM core antibody<br /></br>   • Marker of acute HBV, but patient lacks parenteral/sexual risk factors. Incubation 1-4 months; timing and exposure do not match as well as HAV.</p></br><p>D. Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg)<br /></br>   • Present in both acute and chronic HBV; same exposure mismatch and less specific for “acute.”</p></br><p>E. Hepatitis C IgG (anti-HCV antibody)<br /></br>   • Reflects exposure/chronic infection; acute seroconversion may take months. Primary transmission is via blood; no such risk here.</p></br><p>Common pitfall: choosing HBV markers because patient is sexually active. Remember oral sex without blood exchange has very low HBV transmission risk, and HAV travel history is a far stronger clue.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>End of Explanation</p>
169. Screening for colon cancer is started in patients at the age of 45 years instead of at the age of 50 years. Which of the following is the most likely effect on the predictive values?  </br>Positive Predictive Value    Negative Predictive Value	Correct answer: B</br></br>a.  Decrease                decrease  </br>b.  Decrease                increase  </br>c.  Decrease                no change  </br>d.  Increase                decrease  </br>e.  Increase                increase  </br>f.  Increase                no change  </br>g.  No change               decrease  </br>h.  No change               increase  </br>i.  No change               no change</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Positive Predictive Value – DECREASE; Negative Predictive Value – INCREASE  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrase: “Screening … at the age of 45 instead of 50.”<br /></br>• Hidden clue: Moving the same test to a YOUNGER, LOWER-RISK population lowers disease prevalence (pre-test probability).<br /></br>• Testing strategy: Whenever a question changes the population, immediately think “What happens to prevalence, PPV, and NPV?” Sensitivity &amp; specificity are properties of the test itself and do NOT change with prevalence.  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for future questions<br /></br>1. Identify what is being altered: test characteristics (Sn/Sp) vs population (prevalence).<br /></br>2. Recall the prevalence rules:<br /></br>   – Prevalence ↑ → PPV ↑, NPV ↓<br /></br>   – Prevalence ↓ → PPV ↓, NPV ↑<br /></br>3. Plug into answer grid, eliminate distractors that violate the rule.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Colon cancer incidence rises sharply after age 50. By screening at 45, we enter a group with fewer true cases. As a result:<br /></br>• Fewer TRUE POSITIVES per number tested → PPV falls.<br /></br>• More TRUE NEGATIVES among those testing negative → NPV rises.  </p></br><p>Take-home concept: PPV and NPV move in opposite directions when prevalence changes.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real clinic)<br /></br>• Record patient’s age, family history, and guideline used (USPSTF now recommends average-risk screening at 45).<br /></br>• Note counseling on benefits/harms (e.g., false-positive colonoscopy risk).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)<br /></br>• Colorectal cancer arises from adenomatous or serrated polyps via APC/β-catenin or MSI pathways.<br /></br>• Incidence: ≈4% lifetime risk; sharp uptick after 50, hence prevalence lower at 45.<br /></br>Risk factors: age, adenomatous polyps, IBD, FHx, Lynch, diet (low fiber, high red meat), obesity, smoking.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Test Characteristics Refresher<br /></br>• PPV = TP / (TP + FP) = probability disease is present when test is +.<br /></br>• NPV = TN / (TN + FN) = probability disease is absent when test is −.<br /></br>• Sensitivity &amp; specificity depend on the assay itself; PPV &amp; NPV depend on prevalence.  </p></br><p>Memory aid: “P-value for Prevalence”<br /></br> P PV moves with Prevalence (same direction).<br /></br> N PV moves in the Negative (opposite) direction.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a) Decrease / Decrease<br /></br>   NPV should rise, not fall, when prevalence drops.<br /></br>c) Decrease / No change<br /></br>   NPV must change (increase).<br /></br>d/e/f) Any answer with PPV Increase<br /></br>   PPV cannot increase when prevalence decreases.<br /></br>g) No change / Decrease or h) No change / Increase<br /></br>   Test characteristics are unchanged, but PPV &amp; NPV must change with prevalence.<br /></br>i) No change / No change<br /></br>   Ignores prevalence effect entirely.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: Confusing sensitivity/specificity (unchanged) with predictive values (population-dependent).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────<br /></br>Quick Mnemonic<br /></br>“Lower the crowd, PPV bows down, NPV bows up.”  </p></br><p>Use this rule to instantly answer any prevalence-shift question on NBME or Step 2 CK.</p>
170. A 42-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of chronic nosebleeds. He also had an episode of hematuria 3 months ago. His older brother has similar bleeding problems. Examination shows no abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:</br></br>Hemoglobin                   13.7 g/dL</br>Leukocyte count              8000/mm^3</br>Platelet count               280,000/mm^3</br>Bleeding time                15 min</br>Prothrombin time             11 sec (INR=1.0)</br>Partial thromboplastin time  45 sec</br></br>Test of the stool for occult blood is positive. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: H</br></br>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome</br>b. Antithrombin III deficiency</br>c. Disseminated intravascular coagulation</br>d. Factor XI deficiency</br>e. Hemophilia</br>f. Henoch-Schönlein purpura</br>g. Vitamin K deficiency</br>h. von Willebrand disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>h. von Willebrand disease (vWD)  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key “buzz phrases”<br /></br>   • Chronic epistaxis + one episode of hematuria → mucocutaneous bleeding pattern (platelet-type bleeding).<br /></br>   • Positive stool guaiac (occult GI bleeding) → another mucosal‐site bleed.<br /></br>   • Brother with the same problem → inherited disorder; autosomal dominant diseases are most likely in multiple affected generations.<br /></br>   • Normal platelet count but prolonged bleeding time (BT 15 min) → qualitative platelet dysfunction, most classically defective vWF.<br /></br>   • Prolonged aPTT (45 s), normal PT/INR → factor VIII instability from vWF deficiency.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No thrombocytopenia, no schistocytes, and normal PT → argues strongly against DIC, vitamin K deficiency, liver disease.<br /></br>   • No thrombotic history, miscarriages, or lupus anticoagulant → against antiphospholipid syndrome.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive tip: When you see BOTH a prolonged bleeding time and a prolonged aPTT with normal platelet count, “think vWF every time” for exams.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Identify bleeding type.<br /></br>• Superficial mucosal bleeding = platelet function defect rather than clotting-factor deficiency.  </p></br><p>Step 2: Check platelet number vs function.<br /></br>• Platelet count normal → quantitative deficit unlikely; look for qualitative issue (elevated BT).  </p></br><p>Step 3: Look at coagulation studies.<br /></br>• aPTT prolonged, PT normal → intrinsic pathway issue that spares factor VII (PT) but involves factor VIII, IX, XI, XII.<br /></br>• Only one common disorder gives BOTH platelet-type bleeding and intrinsic pathway prolongation: vWD (because vWF is needed for platelet adhesion AND stabilizes factor VIII).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: vWF links platelets to endothelium (GpIb receptor) and protects factor VIII; lose it → ↑BT + ↑aPTT.  </p></br><p>Documentation points if this were a real patient<br /></br>• Detailed bleeding history (dental work, surgeries)<br /></br>• Family pedigree<br /></br>• Ristocetin cofactor assay ordered<br /></br>• Plan to treat/react for invasive procedures (DDAVP administration).  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Genetics: Most cases autosomal dominant with variable penetrance.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Quantitative or qualitative vWF defect →<br /></br>  – Impaired platelet adhesion (↑ bleeding time)<br /></br>  – Rapid degradation of factor VIII (↑ aPTT).<br /></br>• Clinical picture: Recurrent epistaxis, menorrhagia, easy bruising, prolonged bleeding after dental work; deep-tissue/hemarthrosis rare.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Initial: ↑BT, ↑aPTT, normal PT/platelets.<br /></br>• Confirmatory: Ristocetin cofactor activity (failure of platelet agglutination).  </p></br><p>First-line Treatment<br /></br>• Desmopressin (DDAVP) – releases vWF and factor VIII from Weibel-Palade bodies.<br /></br>Second-line/Adjunct<br /></br>• vWF-containing factor VIII concentrates for major surgery or if DDAVP contraindicated.<br /></br>• Antifibrinolytics (e.g., tranexamic acid) for mucosal bleeding episodes.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Quick Memory Aids  </p></br><p>“vWBleeds”:<br /></br>v = vWF defect<br /></br>W = Willebrand<br /></br>B = Bleeding Time ↑<br /></br>leeds → Bleeds mucosa  </p></br><p>OR  </p></br><p>“Two jobs, two tests”: vWF “Works for” (1) platelet adhesion → ↑Bleeding Time, (2) factor VIII → ↑aPTT.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Rundown  </p></br><p>a. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome – Hypercoagulable (thrombosis, pregnancy loss); bleeding uncommon despite prolonged aPTT due to inhibitor, not factor deficiency; BT normal.  </p></br><p>b. Antithrombin III deficiency – Hypercoagulability with normal PT, aPTT, and BT; presents with recurrent DVT/PE, not epistaxis.  </p></br><p>c. Disseminated intravascular coagulation – Thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT &amp; aPTT, low fibrinogen, schistocytes; acute systemic illness, not isolated mucosal bleeds with normal counts.  </p></br><p>d. Factor XI deficiency (Hemophilia C) – Autosomal recessive, prolonged aPTT but normal BT; mucosal bleeding mild, family history less likely autosomal dominant.  </p></br><p>e. Hemophilia A/B – Deep tissue/hemarthroses, X-linked; ↑aPTT only, BT normal; mucocutaneous bleeding uncommon.  </p></br><p>f. Henoch-Schönlein purpura – IgA vasculitis in children with palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain; coag studies normal.  </p></br><p>g. Vitamin K deficiency – Prolongs PT first (factor VII), then aPTT; BT normal; usually due to malabsorption, antibiotics, or warfarin.  </p></br><p>──────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Take-Home Point<br /></br>A normal platelet count with prolonged bleeding time plus prolonged aPTT, especially with mucosal bleeding and an autosomal dominant family history, = von Willebrand disease.</p>
171. A 23-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 2-day history of several small, painful lesions over the vulva. She has no history of medical or gynecologic illness. She has a history of six lifetime sexual partners and became sexually active with her current partner 6 weeks ago; she uses condoms inconsistently. Examination shows ulcerations over the external genitalia that are tender to touch. She is concerned that she might have a sexually transmitted disease and that this might endanger future pregnancies. This patient should be advised that she is at increased risk for which of the following pregnancy outcomes?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Chorioamnionitis</br>b. Fetal growth restriction</br>c. Fetal malformations</br>d. Intrapartum transmission of the infection</br>e. Premature rupture of membranes</br></br></br><p>Correct answer<br /></br>D. Intrapartum transmission of the infection</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key buzzwords: “small, painful lesions,” “ulcerations over the external genitalia that are tender,” recent new sexual partner → classic description of primary genital herpes simplex virus (HSV-2 &gt; HSV-1).<br /></br>• Pertinent positive: pain (helps distinguish HSV from painless syphilitic chancre or chancroid after rupture of fluctuant bubo).<br /></br>• No systemic illness or prior GYN disease points toward a first-episode primary infection.<br /></br>Testing-strategy tip: Whenever a vignette presents painful vesicles/ulcers in the genital area, HSV should be your leading thought. The USMLE almost always couples that with questions about vertical transmission and indications for cesarean delivery.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>1. Identify disorder → primary genital herpes.<br /></br>2. Ask: What are OB implications of maternal HSV?<br /></br>   • Congenital malformations? → No (HSV does not cause teratogenic defects).<br /></br>   • Antepartum complications (chorioamnionitis, PROM, FGR)? → No robust association.<br /></br>   • Biggest risk → neonatal HSV infection acquired when the baby passes through an infected birth canal, particularly if maternal infection is primary and lesions are present at labor.<br /></br>3. Therefore, the correct counseling point is the markedly increased risk of intrapartum (peripartum) transmission, which can lead to neonatal disseminated disease, CNS disease, or localized skin/eye/mouth disease.<br /></br>Take-home: Active genital HSV at delivery = offer cesarean to reduce neonatal transmission.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real chart you must document lesion description, culture/PCR plan, patient counseling about transmission, and plan for suppressive acyclovir starting at 36 weeks if pregnant.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• HSV establishes latency in sacral dorsal-root ganglia.<br /></br>• Primary infection → higher viral load, longer shedding, no maternal IgG yet → highest vertical-transmission risk (30–50% with vaginal delivery if active lesions).<br /></br>• Recurrent infection → lower risk (~1–3%) because maternal IgG offers partial fetal protection.<br /></br>• Transmission can cause neonatal skin/eye/mouth disease, meningoencephalitis, or disseminated multiorgan failure.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Management &amp; Diagnostics (quick review)<br /></br>Non-pregnant patient:<br /></br>• Diagnosis: Viral culture, PCR from swab, or direct fluorescent antibody; serum HSV IgG only tells past exposure.<br /></br>Pregnant patient:<br /></br>• Acyclovir/valacyclovir for treatment of outbreaks and daily suppression from 36 weeks gestation.<br /></br>• Cesarean delivery if prodromal symptoms or active lesions at labor.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Painful = HSV and Haemophilus ducreyi; Painless = syPhilis.”<br /></br>“HERPES hurts and HERPES hands (hands off the baby): do C-section if lesions are present.”<br /></br>Think: HSV = Handover (delivery) hazard.</p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Chorioamnionitis – Typically due to ascending polymicrobial bacterial infection after prolonged membrane rupture or invasive procedures, not from HSV.<br /></br>B. Fetal growth restriction – Common with chronic placental insufficiency or TORCH infections like CMV and toxoplasma, not HSV.<br /></br>C. Fetal malformations – Teratogenic viruses (rubella, CMV, VZV) or drugs cause structural defects; HSV is not teratogenic.<br /></br>E. Premature rupture of membranes – Strongly linked to bacterial vaginosis, STIs like gonorrhea/chlamydia, smoking, but not classically HSV.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Maternal genital HSV primarily threatens the neonate during passage through the birth canal, not by damaging the placenta or fetus in utero.</p>
172. A 62-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of increasing pain in her right thumb, especially with use. She now has difficulty with daily activities such as opening jars. She is right-handed. Ten years ago, she received the diagnosis of breast cancer and underwent mastectomy followed by chemotherapy. Evaluation 2 years ago showed no evidence of recurrence. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus controlled with metformin. Examination shows severe tenderness to palpation at the base of the right thumb. Muscle strength is 3/5 on abduction of the thumb due to pain. Sensation to pinprick is intact over the thumb. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Bony metastases</br>b. Carpal tunnel syndrome</br>c. Cervical radiculopathy</br>d. Degenerative joint disease</br>e. Diabetic neuropathy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. Degenerative joint disease (osteoarthritis) of the first carpometacarpal (CMC) joint  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Age &amp; Sex: 62-year-old woman → classic demographic for hand osteoarthritis (OA), especially of the thumb CMC.  </li></br><li>Time course: Gradual, progressive pain over 6 months. OA is insidious, not sudden or intermittent like tendinitis or acute trauma.  </li></br><li>Location: “Severe tenderness to palpation at the base of the right thumb” → base = 1st CMC (trapeziometacarpal) joint, the single most common site of symptomatic hand OA.  </li></br><li>Functional loss: Difficulty opening jars; pain-limited strength (3/5) on thumb abduction. OA of the CMC compromises pinch and grip strength.  </li></br><li>Neuro exam: Sensation to pinprick intact → rules out neuropathic etiologies (carpal tunnel, diabetic neuropathy, radiculopathy).  </li></br><li>Cancer history: Breast CA 10 yrs ago but no evidence of recurrence 2 yrs ago. A tempting distractor for “bony metastases,” but the local exam points to a joint surface, not bone marrow pain.  </li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive/Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>• Always anchor pain location first (skin, tendon, joint, bone, nerve).<br /></br>• In focal hand pain, check if symptoms worsen with use (mechanical) vs at night (neuropathic/malignant).<br /></br>• OA = older patient + activity-related joint pain + bony tenderness/crepitus at a specific joint.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise reasoning to answer D:<br /></br>1. Focal pain at a single joint (thumb base) → articular pathology.<br /></br>2. Mechanical exacerbation with use + age &gt;60 → top of differential is OA.<br /></br>3. OA favors weight-bearing or high-use joints (knees, hips, 1st CMC, DIP, PIP).<br /></br>4. Absence of sensory changes eliminates nerve compression or neuropathy.<br /></br>Core takeaway: First CMC osteoarthritis presents as activity-related pain and weakness in grip/pinch with localized bony tenderness in middle-aged/older women.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record location, severity, functional limitation (e.g., cannot open jars), neurovascular status, and provocative maneuvers (e.g., CMC grind test).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Degeneration of articular cartilage → subchondral sclerosis, osteophyte formation.<br /></br>• Risk factors: Age, female sex, repetitive thumb use, prior trauma.<br /></br>• Clinical manifestations: Pain with pinch/grip, morning stiffness &lt;30 min, joint enlargement (“squared” thumb base), crepitus, grind test pain.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Management (brief high-yield)  </p></br><ol></br><li>First-line: Activity modification, thumb spica splint, NSAIDs (topical diclofenac or oral naproxen/ibuprofen).  </li></br><li>Second-line: Intra-articular corticosteroid (triamcinolone).  </li></br><li>Refractory: Surgical options (CMC arthroplasty, trapeziectomy).  </li></br></ol></br><p>No imaging is needed for the exam question, but hand x-ray would show joint-space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis/cysts.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “Jar OA” – If an older woman says, “I can’t open jars,” think 1st CMC OA.<br /></br>• Hand OA joints spell “DIP, PIP, CMC” → the ends of the word “OA” touch the ends of the fingers and the base of the thumb.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Bony metastases<br /></br>• Breast cancer metastasis loves axial skeleton (spine, ribs, pelvis), not the thumb.<br /></br>• Metastatic pain is deep, constant, often nocturnal, and not localized solely to a joint surface.<br /></br>• Would not improve/worsen primarily with thumb motion; exam might show swelling but not isolated CMC tenderness.  </p></br><p>B. Carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS)<br /></br>• Compression of median nerve at wrist. Presents with numbness/tingling in palmar thumb, index, middle, and radial half of ring finger, worst at night.<br /></br>• Sensory deficits + positive Tinel/Phalen. Pain is neuropathic, not bony; tenderness is at carpal tunnel, not thumb base.  </p></br><p>C. Cervical radiculopathy<br /></br>• Neck pain radiating along dermatomes (C6 = thumb). Sensory loss, decreased reflexes, possibly weakness across multiple myotomes.<br /></br>• Would not produce point tenderness at the thumb CMC joint.  </p></br><p>E. Diabetic neuropathy<br /></br>• Symmetric “stocking-glove” sensory loss, burning pain, decreased vibration/position sense.<br /></br>• Focal unilateral thumb-base pain with preserved sensation is inconsistent.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line:<br /></br>Localized, activity-related pain and tenderness at the base of the thumb in an older woman = osteoarthritis (degenerative joint disease) of the first carpometacarpal joint.</p>
173. A 42-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of increased drinking and difficulty swallowing because of a dry mouth. She also has noticed swelling of her cheeks and has had more dental cavities than usual during this period. She has no history of serious illness. She takes no medications. Vital signs are within normal limits. Examination shows enlarged parotid glands; slightly red eyes; and dry, cracked lips. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of serum vitamin B12 (cobalamin) concentration</br>b. Serum antinuclear antibody assay</br>c. Oral amoxicillin therapy</br>d. Oral prednisone therapy</br>e. CT scan of the parotid glands</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. Serum antinuclear antibody (ANA) assay  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key phrases: 1-year history, dry mouth (xerostomia), difficulty swallowing, bilateral parotid enlargement, “more dental cavities,” red/dry eyes (xerophthalmia), 42-year-old woman.<br /></br>• Pattern: Middle-aged woman + xerostomia + keratoconjunctivitis sicca + painless symmetric parotid hypertrophy → classic constellation for Sjögren syndrome.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No fever, erythema, purulent discharge (makes acute bacterial parotitis unlikely); no gallstones, no chronic alcohol abuse (argues against pancreatitis-related lipase elevation).<br /></br>• Test‐taking tip: On NBME/Step 2, chronic dryness of mouth/eyes plus parotid swelling should immediately trigger “Sjögren work-up → check autoantibodies (ANA → anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB).”</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Recognize syndrome (autoimmune exocrinopathy).<br /></br>2. Establish diagnosis serologically before prescribing immunosuppression or obtaining invasive imaging.<br /></br>3. First-line screening test = ANA panel; if positive, reflex to anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB.<br /></br>Core takeaway: The appropriate next step for suspected Sjögren syndrome is serologic confirmation with an ANA assay (and specific extractable nuclear antigens).</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Autoimmune lymphocytic destruction of lacrimal &amp; salivary glands.<br /></br>• Associations: Other autoimmune diseases (RA, thyroiditis, SLE).<br /></br>• Antibodies: ANA positive (~80-90%); specific anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB.<br /></br>• Clinical triad: Xerophthalmia, xerostomia, parotid enlargement (“bilateral mumps in a middle-aged woman”).<br /></br>• Long-term risks: Dental caries, oral candidiasis, non-Hodgkin B-cell lymphoma.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review<br /></br>Diagnostic ladder:<br /></br>1. Screening: ANA → ENA panel (anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB).<br /></br>2. Objective tests: Schirmer test (&lt;5 mm wetting in 5 min), ocular surface staining, salivary flow.<br /></br>3. Definitive (if needed): Lip minor salivary gland biopsy (focal lymphocytic sialadenitis).<br /></br>Therapy (after diagnosis):<br /></br>• Symptomatic: Artificial tears, saliva substitutes, sugar-free gum/candy.<br /></br>• Muscarinic agonists: Pilocarpine, cevimeline.<br /></br>• Systemic immunosuppression (hydroxychloroquine, glucocorticoids) only for severe extraglandular disease.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Dry 3’s for Sjögren”:<br /></br>1. Dry eyes<br /></br>2. Dry mouth<br /></br>3. Dry parotids (enlarged)<br /></br>Confirm with “ANA then SSA.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Serum vitamin B12 level: Pernicious anemia can occur with autoimmune disorders but presents with macrocytic anemia, neurologic changes—not predominant xerostomia/keratoconjunctivitis. Not first diagnostic step here.<br /></br>c. Oral amoxicillin: Would treat bacterial parotitis (acute painful unilateral swelling, fever, purulent saliva). Our patient has chronic, painless, bilateral swelling.<br /></br>d. Oral prednisone: Immunosuppression might help systemic Sjögren complications, but treatment should not precede diagnostic confirmation. Starting steroids without a diagnosis is inappropriate.<br /></br>e. CT scan of the parotid glands: Imaging is reserved for suspected tumor, abscess, or sialolithiasis. It does not diagnose Sjögren and adds cost/radiation without changing immediate management.</p></br><p>Take-home Message<br /></br>In a middle-aged woman with chronic dry eyes, dry mouth, and bilateral parotid enlargement, think Sjögren syndrome. The first step is serologic confirmation with an ANA assay (followed by anti-SSA/SSB).</p>
174. A randomized controlled trial shows that a new fecal occult blood test (FOBT) leads to an 80 percent decrease in the relative risk for death due to colorectal cancer. Which of the following additional information is most useful in understanding the impact of this test?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. The nature of the chemical reaction by which FOBT detects cancer</br>b. The number of patients undergoing FOBT in this study</br>c. The relative risk of death among patients who undergo FOBT</br>d. The risk of death due to colorectal cancer among patients in the control group</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>D. The risk of death due to colorectal cancer among patients in the control group  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases<br /></br>• “Randomized controlled trial” – gold standard design; allows valid comparison of outcomes between intervention and control groups.<br /></br>• “80 percent decrease in the relative risk” – gives a RELATIVE measure only (Relative Risk Reduction, RRR = 1 – RR).<br /></br>• “Most useful in understanding the impact” – asks what additional datum best lets us judge the true clinical (absolute) benefit.</p></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>• We already have RRR (80 %).<br /></br>• We are missing the baseline (control) event rate, which determines the Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR) and, therefore, the Number Needed to Screen/Treat (NNS/NNT).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip<br /></br>On exams, when a question provides a relative measure but asks how to judge “impact” or “clinical significance,” mentally ask: “Do I know the baseline event rate?” If not, that is usually the piece you still need.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Clinical decisions hinge on ABSOLUTE benefit (ARR), not just relative.<br /></br>2. ARR = Control Event Rate (CER) – Experimental Event Rate (EER).<br /></br>   • We already know RRR = (CER – EER) / CER = 80 %.<br /></br>3. To turn RRR into ARR we MUST know CER.<br /></br>4. Once ARR is known, we calculate NNS = 1/ARR to understand how many people must be screened to prevent one colorectal-cancer death.<br /></br>5.
