Therefore, learning “the risk of death due to colorectal cancer among patients in the control group” (CER) is the single most informative additional fact.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Relative measures sound impressive but can be misleading; absolute measures drive patient counseling and public-health policy.</p></br><p>What would be documented: Baseline mortality rate in unscreened patients, ARR, and NNS derived therefrom.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (concise)  </p></br><p>Colorectal cancer mortality can be reduced by earlier detection (FOBT, sigmoidoscopy, colonoscopy). FOBT identifies hemoglobin peroxidase activity in stool; earlier-stage cancers have better prognosis, so effective screening lowers death rates. The statistical principle here, not the pathophysiology, is the focus.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Context  </p></br><p>• First-line CRC screening tests (FOBT yearly, FIT yearly, FIT-DNA q3 yrs, sigmoidoscopy q5 yrs, colonoscopy q10 yrs).<br /></br>• Public-health bodies decide between tests based largely on ARR and NNS, plus harms, costs, and adherence – all require the baseline event rate.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“R-R-R rings hollow without C-E-R.”<br /></br>• CER = Control Event Rate.<br /></br>• If you only hear RRR, ask, “Where’s CER so I can get ARR and NNT?”</p></br><p>Visual: Picture an 80 % OFF sale sign without knowing the original price—can’t judge the real savings.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Nature of the chemical reaction of FOBT<br /></br>   • Pertains to test mechanics, not outcome magnitude. Does not help convert RRR into ARR or NNS.</p></br><p>b. Number of patients undergoing FOBT in this study<br /></br>   • Affects statistical power and confidence intervals, but even with perfect precision we still wouldn’t know the absolute benefit without CER.</p></br><p>c. Relative risk of death among patients who undergo FOBT<br /></br>   • That’s the inverse of RRR (RR = 0.20 here). We already possess this information; still can’t gauge absolute benefit.</p></br><p>d. Risk of death in the control group (Correct)<br /></br>   • Supplies CER → enables ARR and NNS → essential for understanding real-world impact.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom line<br /></br>To translate an impressive-sounding “80 % relative risk reduction” into meaningful clinical terms, you must know how common the outcome is without the intervention—the control group’s event rate.</p>
175. A 4-month-old boy is brought to the office by his mother because of a 1-month history of feeding problems. The mother says her son often vomits after feeding and hiccups frequently. She also notes that he sometimes arches his back after feeding and appears to be in pain. The infant’s diet consists primarily of cow milk–based formula, with occasional infant cereal and fruits. He is fed six times daily. Medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. Vaccinations are up-to-date. The patient is at the 10th percentile for length, weight, and head circumference. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination discloses no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely preliminary diagnosis?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cow milk protein intolerance</br>b. Foreign body ingestion</br>c. Peptic ulcer disease</br>d. Pyloric stenosis</br>e. Reflux esophagitis</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer (as provided by user)</br>E. Reflux esophagitis</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Age &amp; chronicity – 4-month-old with 1-month history (subacute, not sudden).<br /></br>• Classic GERD clues – recurrent post-feed emesis, frequent hiccups, and back-arching/neck-turning after feeds (Sandifer behavior = infant trying to relieve esophageal discomfort).<br /></br>• Growth – 10th percentile across weight, length, and head circumference → small but proportionate; no acute dehydration or severe FTT.<br /></br>• Formula-based diet, no blood in stool, no eczema → argues against milk-protein allergy.<br /></br>• Normal physical exam → no abdominal distention, no “olive,” normal vitals.</p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>When an infant (&lt;1 y) presents with effortless post-feed regurgitation, always think “physiologic reflux first; pathologic GERD if there is pain, feeding aversion, back-arching, poor weight gain, or respiratory issues.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise link to the answer:</br>1. Recurrent non-projectile vomiting after feeds.<br /></br>2. Pain behaviors (crying, arching → Sandifer sign) indicate esophageal irritation.<br /></br>3. No red-flag findings for obstruction (bilious emesis, acute distress) or hypertrophic pylorus (projectile, palpable olive).<br /></br>4. Age fits peak prevalence of infant GERD (1–4 mo).<br /></br>Therefore, reflux esophagitis is the preliminarily most likely diagnosis.</p></br><p>Take-home message: Infantile GERD becomes “pathologic” when it causes pain, poor growth, or respiratory complications; Sandifer syndrome is a high-yield buzzword.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: Record feeding history, emesis character, growth parameters, and pain behaviors; note absence of blood or projectile quality.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Immature lower esophageal sphincter (LES) tone + liquid diet + recumbent positioning → frequent reflux.<br /></br>• Repeated acid exposure inflames distal esophageal mucosa → esophagitis → pain → arching.<br /></br>Risk factors: prematurity, neuro impairment, over-feeding.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment (high-yield overview)</br>Most infants do not need invasive work-up initially.</br>1. Conservative first-line: smaller, more-frequent feeds; upright positioning ≥20 min; thickened feeds (1 Tbsp rice cereal/oz).<br /></br>2. Trial of protein-hydrolysate formula if cow-milk allergy is also suspected.<br /></br>3. Pharmacologic (if red-flag or failed conservative): proton-pump inhibitor (eg, omeprazole) or H2 blocker (eg, ranitidine).<br /></br>4. EGD/pH-impedance reserved for refractory or complicated cases.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“Sandifer = GERD Sufferer” – Sandifer posturing (arching/turning) is virtually pathognomonic for infant reflux pain.<br /></br>“3 S’s of infant GERD: Spit-up, Satisfied (usually), Sandifer (if painful).”</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect</br>a. Cow milk protein intolerance – Typically presents with painless hematochezia, eczema, or wheeze; emesis less prominent. Stool blood and atopy absent here.<br /></br>b. Foreign body ingestion – Abrupt onset of choking, drooling, dysphagia; radiographic findings; not a chronic, feeding-related pattern.<br /></br>c. Peptic ulcer disease – Extremely rare in infants; would show hematemesis/melena or severe epigastric pain, often NSAID or stress related.<br /></br>d. Pyloric stenosis – Classically 2–6 weeks old, projectile non-bilious vomiting, palpable olive, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, weight loss. This patient is older, has non-projectile emesis, and normal exam.  </p></br><p>Key differentiators: projectile vs non-projectile, onset timing, presence of blood, systemic signs, and characteristic physical findings.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Incorrect-answer memory aid</br>“PROJECT” for pyloric stenosis (Projectile emesis, 3-week-old, Olive, Emaciation, Contraction alkalosis, Thick pylorus on US). None of these fit our vignette.</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>A 4-month-old with post-feed vomiting, hiccups, and Sandifer posturing most consistently points to reflux esophagitis (symptomatic infant GERD).</p>
176. A 62-year-old woman is brought to the physician by her children 1 week after they noticed a large lesion on her right shoulder. She has lived alone since her husband died 6 months ago, and her family has recently become concerned that she is not caring for herself adequately. She says she thinks she has had the lesion for 1 year and that it has been increasing in size. She underwent renal transplantation 5 years ago and has taken prednisone and cyclosporine since then. She underwent radiation therapy on her face for treatment of acne as a teenager. Her sister died of skin cancer 3 years ago at the age of 65 years. The patient has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 45 years. She was employed as a landscaper for 25 years. She appears disheveled and is poorly groomed. Examination shows a 5-cm lesion on the posterior right shoulder. A photograph of the patient’s back is shown. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the lesion?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cigarette smoking</br>b. Congenital predisposition</br>c. History of radiation therapy</br>d. Inadequate personal hygiene</br>e. Long-term immunosuppression therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Long–term immunosuppression therapy (prednisone + cyclosporine)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Time course/size: “Had the lesion for 1 y … increasing in size … now 5 cm.” A rapidly enlarging, &gt;2-cm nodule on sun-exposed skin should immediately trigger concern for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC).<br /></br>2. Transplant history: “Renal transplantation 5 y ago … prednisone and cyclosporine since then.” Chronic pharmacologic immunosuppression is the single strongest acquired risk factor for cutaneous SCC—risk ↑ 65- to 250-fold vs the general population.<br /></br>3. Location: Posterior shoulder = chronically sun-exposed. UV-B produces pyrimidine dimers in keratinocyte DNA; normally, immune surveillance clears mutated cells—immunosuppression removes that safeguard.<br /></br>4. Other clues (smoking, radiation, sister, poor hygiene) are distractions—common NBME trap of “collecting comorbidities.” The question asks for the MOST likely cause; epidemiologically the transplant-related immunosuppression far outweighs the others.</p></br><p>Cognitive / test-taking pearl: When you see “organ transplant + large/verrucous/rapidly growing skin lesion,” jump to SCC from immunosuppression. The exam loves this pattern.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify lesion type → rapidly enlarging keratotic/ulcerated nodule on sun-exposed skin → SCC.<br /></br>2. Enumerate major SCC risk factors → cumulative UV exposure, chronic immunosuppression, chronic scars/ulcers, ionizing radiation, HPV infection, arsenic.<br /></br>3. Weigh relative risk contribution → Transplant-related immunosuppression dwarfs others listed. Therefore choose E.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Long-term calcineurin inhibitor ± glucocorticoid therapy after solid-organ transplantation markedly increases incidence, multiplicity, and aggressiveness of SCC.</p></br><p>Documentation bullets (real life): size, location, ulceration, induration, regional nodes, immunosuppressive regimen, plan for excisional biopsy &amp; dermatology referral.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) block IL-2 transcription → ↓T-cell activation → impaired immune surveillance for UV-mutated keratinocytes and oncogenic viruses.<br /></br>• Systemic glucocorticoids compound the effect by lymphocyte apoptosis.<br /></br>• Result: early clonal expansion of dysplastic keratinocytes → aggressive SCC that often presents larger and metastasizes earlier than in immunocompetent patients.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Highlights<br /></br>Diagnosis: Punch or excisional biopsy (full-thickness) to confirm SCC and determine histologic subtype (keratoacanthoma-like vs poorly differentiated).<br /></br>Staging: Regional lymph-node exam ± ultrasound/FNA if high-risk features (size &gt;2 cm, depth &gt;2 mm, perineural invasion).<br /></br>Management:<br /></br>• Standard excision with 4-6 mm margins OR Mohs surgery for high-risk lesions.<br /></br>• Consider reducing immunosuppression, switching from calcineurin inhibitor to an mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus) if feasible—shown to lower subsequent SCC incidence.<br /></br>• Photoprotection counseling; dermatology surveillance every 6–12 months.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Transplant = Two-C’s skin cancer”<br /></br>C1: Calcineurin inhibitor<br /></br>C2: Cutaneous Squamous Cell carcinoma  </p></br><p>Or visualize a “Cyclosporine cyclone” stripping away immune guards around sun-battered keratinocytes, letting SCC erupt.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong<br /></br>A. Cigarette smoking – Major risk for lung, bladder, pancreatic, and lip SCC, but not a leading cause of cutaneous SCC; relative risk far lower than immunosuppression.<br /></br>B. Congenital predisposition – One 1º relative with skin cancer at 65 (common age) does not equal a hereditary syndrome (e.g., basal cell nevus syndrome, XP).<br /></br>C. History of radiation therapy – Ionizing radiation raises SCC risk but the therapy was limited to the face, not the posterior shoulder. Also occurred decades ago; still lower risk than chronic immunosuppression.<br /></br>D. Inadequate personal hygiene – Has no proven link to skin malignancy; a distractor leveraging the patient’s disheveled appearance.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message<br /></br>Solid-organ transplant recipients on lifelong calcineurin inhibitors ± glucocorticoids are at the highest acquired risk for aggressive cutaneous SCC. Always prioritize immunosuppression as the primary etiologic factor when such patients develop large or rapidly growing skin lesions.</p>
177. A 27-year-old primigravid woman at 10 weeks' gestation comes to the physician for her first prenatal visit. Examination shows a uterus consistent in size with a 10-week gestation. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL</br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin 27.8 pg/cell</br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration 29 percent Hb/cell</br>Mean corpuscular volume 75 µm³</br>Leukocyte count 10,000/mm³</br>Platelet count 174,000/mm³</br>Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Folic acid deficiency</br>b. Hereditary spherocytosis</br>c. Iron deficiency anemia</br>d. Physiologic anemia of pregnancy</br>e. Sickle cell disease</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Iron deficiency anemia  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues:<br /></br>• 27-year-old pregnant woman (10 weeks) – pregnancy rapidly increases iron demand for expansion of maternal red-cell mass and fetal development.<br /></br>• Low Hgb (10.5 g/dL) – anemia present (Hgb should be ≥11 g/dL in 1st trimester).<br /></br>• Microcytic indices: MCV 75 µm³ (↓), MCH 27.8 pg (↓), MCHC 29 % (↓) → hypochromic, microcytic pattern classic for iron deficiency.<br /></br>• Normal leukocytes &amp; platelets – isolated red-cell abnormality.  </p></br><p>Strategy / metacognition: As soon as you see microcytosis in a pregnant woman, think “iron first.” Always classify anemia by MCV, then match classic patterns (microcytic → TAIL mnemonic: Thalassemia, Anemia of chronic disease, Iron deficiency, Lead/Sideroblastic). Pregnancy + low MCHC = iron deficiency until proven otherwise.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic:<br /></br>1. Identify anemia (Hgb &lt;11).<br /></br>2. Determine microcytic, hypochromic pattern (↓MCV, ↓MCH, ↓MCHC).<br /></br>3. In pregnancy, most common etiology = iron deficiency from increased requirements and often inadequate dietary intake.<br /></br>4. Other microcytic causes are much less common in a healthy 27-year-old with no chronic disease or hemoglobinopathy history.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: Microcytic, hypochromic anemia in pregnancy = iron deficiency anemia.  </p></br><p>Documentation (real patient): Note CBC results, suspected iron deficiency anemia, plan to order ferritin / iron studies, start oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg TID with vitamin C, discuss dietary iron, and schedule follow-up CBC.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Pregnancy expands plasma volume by ~50 %, red-cell mass by ~25 % → relative iron requirement ↑ to ~1000 mg for gestation/lactation.<br /></br>• Inadequate intake or stores → depleted ferritin, ↓serum iron, ↑TIBC, ↓transferrin saturation → impaired heme synthesis → smaller, paler RBCs (microcytic hypochromic).  </p></br><p>Risk factors: Pregnancy, poor diet, heavy menses history, short inter-pregnancy interval.  </p></br><p>Clinical manifestations: Fatigue, pallor, pica; may be asymptomatic early.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment  </p></br><p>Diagnostics (confirmatory):<br /></br>• Serum ferritin (best single test) ↓<br /></br>• Serum iron ↓, TIBC ↑, transferrin saturation ↓<br /></br>Rule out thalassemia with hemoglobin electrophoresis if microcytosis persists despite iron.  </p></br><p>Treatment:<br /></br>1. Oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg TID + 250 mg vitamin C (enhances absorption).<br /></br>2. Counsel on iron-rich foods (red meat, leafy greens).<br /></br>3. Parenteral iron if intolerance or malabsorption.<br /></br>4. Continue through pregnancy &amp; 3 months postpartum.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>• “Pregnancy ironed-out”: Pregnant + microcytic = Iron deficiency.<br /></br>• Low three “M’s” for iron deficiency: ↓MCV, ↓MCH, ↓MCHC (“Many Cells Mini &amp; Colorless”).<br /></br>• TAIL for microcytic causes. Iron is the only one with ↓ ferritin and ↑TIBC.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Folic acid deficiency – Produces MACROcytic (↑MCV) megaloblastic anemia, not microcytic.<br /></br>b. Hereditary spherocytosis – Normocytic or slightly microcytic anemia with ↑MCHC (&gt;36 %), spherocytes on smear, jaundice, splenomegaly. MCHC here is low.<br /></br>d. Physiologic anemia of pregnancy – Dilutional anemia (plasma volume expansion) but indices remain NORMOCYTIC; MCHC normal.<br /></br>e. Sickle cell disease – Chronic normocytic anemia with hemoglobin 6–9 g/dL, peripheral smear shows sickled cells, history of pain crises; MCV usually normal.  </p></br><p>These options do not match the isolated microcytic, hypochromic pattern shown.</p>
178. A previously healthy 4-year-old boy is brought to the physician because of a 3-day history of fever, cough, and runny nose. He has not had wheezing, vomiting, or diarrhea. He is at the 75th percentile for height and 10th percentile for weight. His temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F). The skin is warm and pink. Capillary refill time is 2 seconds. Examination shows clear rhinorrhea. Breath sounds are normal. Cardiac examination is shown. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Congestive heart failure</br>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension</br>c. Pericarditis</br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection</br>e. Ventricular septal defect</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>d. Upper respiratory tract infection (viral “common cold”)</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key words/phrases<br /></br>  – “Previously healthy 4-year-old” – no past cardiac or pulmonary history<br /></br>  – “3-day history of fever, cough, and runny nose” – classic viral prodrome; no dyspnea, wheeze, vomiting, or diarrhea to suggest lower airway or GI involvement<br /></br>  – Afebrile-low grade (37.5 °C/99.5 °F) – typical for a viral URI in children<br /></br>  – Normal perfusion: warm skin, cap refill 2 s – argues against shock or congestive failure<br /></br>  – Exam: clear rhinorrhea, normal breath sounds, no crackles, retractions, or wheeze<br /></br>  – Cardiac exam “shown” – on NBME this image/audio is normal S1 &amp; S2 with no pathologic murmurs, rub, or gallop  </p></br><p>Pertinent positives: mild upper airway symptoms.<br /></br>Pertinent negatives: no tachypnea, retractions, rales, hepatomegaly, pathologic murmur, loud S2, pulsus paradoxus, or muffled heart sounds.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On shelf questions, the most common, benign explanation that fully accounts for the findings is usually correct unless a red flag appears. Always check that vitals, perfusion, and exam are globally reassuring before jumping to exotic cardiac diagnoses.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>1. Identify presenting system: nose/cough—likely respiratory.<br /></br>2. Localize level: symptoms limited to upper airway; lungs clear.<br /></br>3. Screen for danger signs: normal vitals, good perfusion =&gt; no need to invoke heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, or pericardial disease.<br /></br>4. Choose simplest diagnosis that explains everything: viral URI.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: A short history of mild fever, rhinorrhea, and cough with a completely normal chest and heart exam in a preschooler is almost always an uncomplicated viral upper respiratory tract infection.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls if real patient: duration of fever/cough, hydration status, work of breathing, lung &amp; heart exam, anticipatory guidance on fever control and return precautions.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Most pediatric URIs are caused by rhinovirus, coronavirus, RSV, parainfluenza, or adenovirus.<br /></br>• Viruses infect nasal/upper airway epithelium → inflammation → rhinorrhea, congestion, cough due to post-nasal drip.<br /></br>• Self-limited (7–10 days); complications include otitis media or sinusitis.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• No labs or imaging are indicated when exam is benign.<br /></br>• Supportive care: acetaminophen/ibuprofen for fever, saline nasal spray, humidified air.<br /></br>• Educate family to watch for worsening dyspnea, high fever &gt; 3 days, ear pain.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Runny nose + Rubbery vitals = Routine URI.”<br /></br>Think the three R’s: Rhinorrhea, Reassuring exam, Routine viral illness.  </p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Congestive heart failure<br /></br>   – Would show tachycardia, tachypnea, hepatomegaly, S3/gallop, crackles, poor weight gain, possibly diaphoresis with feeds. None are present; cap refill &amp; exam are normal.  </p></br><p>b. Idiopathic pulmonary hypertension<br /></br>   – Presents with exertional dyspnea, fatigue, syncope; exam shows loud, single S2, possible right ventricular heave, sometimes cyanosis. Our patient has normal breath sounds and cardiac auscultation.  </p></br><p>c. Pericarditis<br /></br>   – Acute chest pain relieved by leaning forward, pericardial friction rub, often fever &gt; 38 °C, muffled heart sounds or pulsus paradoxus if large effusion. None of these are described.  </p></br><p>e. Ventricular septal defect<br /></br>   – Classic harsh holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border ± palpable thrill; large VSD may lead to failure to thrive and CHF. The stem implies a normal cardiac exam and the child’s weight is low but stable; absence of a murmur rules it out.  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: seeing “cough” makes students think lower respiratory illness; always correlate with lung exam. If lungs are clear, think URI/post-nasal drip first.</p></br><p>───────────────────</br>Summary Sentence<br /></br>A preschooler with 3 days of low-grade fever, rhinorrhea, and normal heart–lung exam most likely has a simple viral upper respiratory tract infection—no red flags for cardiac or lower airway disease.</p>
179. A 6-year-old girl with a history of delayed motor and language development is brought to the physician by her parents because of three episodes of "staring off" during the past 5 weeks. During the episodes, she turns her head to the left, extends her left arm, and does not respond to questions; each episode lasts 2 minutes. After the episode, she is confused and wants to sleep. She was born at term following an uncomplicated pregnancy and spontaneous vaginal delivery. Examination shows no dysmorphic features. There are a few hypopigmented macules over the abdomen and lower extremities. Language development is at the level of a 3-year-old child. An MRI of the brain is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Linear sebaceous nevus</br>b. Neurofibromatosis</br>c. Sturge-Weber syndrome</br>d. Tuberous sclerosis</br>e. von Hippel-Lindau syndrome</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>D. Tuberous sclerosis</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • “Staring off … turns her head to the left, extends her left arm” → focal impaired-awareness seizure with post-ictal confusion (not typical absence).<br /></br>   • “A few hypopigmented macules over the abdomen and lower extremities” → classic ash-leaf spots (Wood’s lamp–positive).<br /></br>   • Global developmental delay (language = 3-year-old).<br /></br>   • Brain MRI (not shown here) in boards questions usually demonstrates cortical/ subcortical tubers or subependymal nodules lining the lateral ventricles.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No facial capillary malformation (rules out Sturge-Weber).<br /></br>   • No café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas (rules out NF1).<br /></br>   • No linear, waxy, yellow-orange plaque on scalp (rules out linear sebaceous nevus syndrome).<br /></br>   • No retinal/ cerebellar hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma (rules out von Hippel-Lindau).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Approach tip: On exam day, “focal seizures + ash-leaf spots ± cortical tubers on MRI” should immediately trigger tuberous sclerosis.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why choice D is correct)  </p></br><p>Step 1: Classify the seizure → focal onset with motor manifestation and post-ictal state.<br /></br>Step 2: Look for an underlying neurocutaneous syndrome (cutaneous clue provided).<br /></br>Step 3: Match the skin lesion: hypopigmented macules (≥3, &gt;5 mm) → hallmark of tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC).<br /></br>Step 4: Corroborate with neuroimaging → cortical/subependymal hamartomas (“tubers”) commonly cause focal epilepsy.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: TSC = AD mutation in TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) → dysregulated mTOR → hamartoma formation in brain, skin, heart, kidneys, lungs. Epilepsy and developmental delay are the most frequent pediatric presentations.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real note you would record seizure semiology, duration, post-ictal state, skin exam findings (number, size, location of ash-leaf macules), developmental assessment, and imaging results.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Pathophysiology Review of Tuberous Sclerosis  </p></br><p>• Genetics: Autosomal dominant; ~2/3 de novo.<br /></br>• mTOR overactivation → benign hamartomas in multiple organs.<br /></br>• CNS: cortical tubers, subependymal nodules ± subependymal giant-cell astrocytoma (SEGA) → seizures, ID, autism.<br /></br>• Skin: ash-leaf spots, facial angiofibromas (adenoma sebaceum), shagreen patch (connective-tissue nevus), ungual fibromas.<br /></br>• Cardiac: rhabdomyomas (infantile CHF/arrhythmia).<br /></br>• Renal: angiomyolipomas, cysts → bleeding, CKD.<br /></br>• Pulmonary: lymphangioleiomyomatosis (women).<br /></br>Mnemonic: “HAMARTOMAS” – Hamartomas, Angiofibromas/Ash-leaf, Mitral regurg, Ash-leaf, Rhabdomyoma (cardiac), Tuberous sclerosis, autosomal dOminant, Mental retardation, Angiomyolipoma (renal), Seizures/Shagreen patch.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Therapeutic Pearls  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Skin exam + Wood’s lamp.<br /></br>• MRI brain every 1-3 years to monitor for SEGA.<br /></br>• Renal US/CT, echocardiogram (infants), ophthalmologic exam.  </p></br><p>Seizure management<br /></br>• First-line for infantile spasms in TSC: Vigabatrin.<br /></br>• Focal seizures: levetiracetam, oxcarbazepine, etc.<br /></br>• Refractory epilepsy may require ketogenic diet, vagus-nerve stimulator, or surgical resection/laser ablation of tuber.  </p></br><p>Disease-modifying therapy<br /></br>• Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) shrinks SEGA or renal angiomyolipomas and reduces seizure burden.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Aids  </p></br><p>• “Ash-leaf = TSC leaf” – white leaf-shaped macules point to tuberous sclerosis.<br /></br>• Visualize a “tuber” potato with white spots sprouting hamartomas around the body.<br /></br>• Everolimus “EVER-shrinks” the tubers.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>A. Linear sebaceous nevus (epidermal nevus syndrome)<br /></br>   – Lesion = solitary yellow-orange waxy plaque on scalp/face. Seizures possible, but NOT hypopigmented macules and MRI usually normal or with gyral malformations rather than subependymal nodules.  </p></br><p>B. Neurofibromatosis type 1<br /></br>   – Café-au-lait macules, axillary freckling, Lisch nodules, neurofibromas; seizures less common; MRI shows optic gliomas rather than cortical tubers.  </p></br><p>C. Sturge-Weber syndrome<br /></br>   – Facial port-wine stain in trigeminal V1 distribution, leptomeningeal angiomas (tram-track calcifications), glaucoma. Absence of port-wine stain and presence of ash-leaf spots makes this unlikely.  </p></br><p>E. von Hippel-Lindau syndrome<br /></br>   – Cerebellar &amp; retinal hemangioblastomas, pheochromocytoma, renal cell carcinoma in adolescents/adults. Does not present with hypopigmented macules or focal epilepsy in early childhood.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>A child with developmental delay, focal seizures, ash-leaf spots, and tubers on MRI has tuberous sclerosis. Recognizing the skin-brain association is a favorite NBME testable concept.</p>
180. A study is conducted to determine the efficacy of cervical cerclage to prevent preterm birth. Pregnant patients at 16 weeks' gestation are enrolled, and their cervical length is measured transvaginally by ultrasonography. If cervical length is found to be less than 25 mm, the patient is offered cerclage or bed rest. Patients are observed until delivery, and outcome data are collected. Which of the following is the best description of this type of study?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Case-control</br>b. Case-matched</br>c. Cohort</br>d. Descriptive</br>e. Randomized</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Cohort</p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases/clues<br /></br>• “Pregnant patients at 16 weeks’ gestation are enrolled” – subjects are assembled BEFORE the outcome has occurred.<br /></br>• “Cervical length … is measured” – an exposure (short cervix &lt; 25 mm) is ascertained at baseline.<br /></br>• “Patient is offered cerclage or bed rest … observed until delivery, and outcome data are collected” – subjects are then FOLLOWED FORWARD in time to see who develops the outcome (preterm birth).  </p></br><p>Cognitive approach for test day<br /></br>1. Ask: “Were subjects picked because they already had the outcome?” – No → not case-control.<br /></br>2. Ask: “Was the exposure measured first and the group then followed forward?” – Yes → cohort.<br /></br>3. Ask: “Did investigators randomize?” – No → not RCT.<br /></br>If the answer to #2 is yes and #3 is no, the default is an observational cohort study (prospective if done in real time, retrospective if chart review).  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (study-design version)  </p></br><p>Step-wise logic<br /></br>1. Define exposure(s): short cervix (&lt; 25 mm) ± cerclage.<br /></br>2. Enroll all eligible women at 16 weeks (time zero).<br /></br>3. Follow them prospectively until delivery (future point).<br /></br>4. Compare incidence of preterm birth between the exposed vs unexposed groups.<br /></br>Take-home: A cohort study starts with exposure status and measures subsequent disease incidence.  </p></br><p>Documentation note (real world)<br /></br>If this were a real study, the protocol would document inclusion criteria (singleton pregnancy, 16 weeks), exposure classification (cervical length), consent for cerclage or bed rest, follow-up schedule, and definition of “preterm birth.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology/Clinical Pearl  </p></br><p>• Cervical insufficiency = painless cervical dilation during the 2nd trimester; cerclage is placed prophylactically in women with a short cervix or prior mid-trimester losses to reduce preterm birth risk.<br /></br>• Ultrasonographic cervical length &lt; 25 mm at 16–24 weeks is the commonly used cutoff.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review (brief, for context)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>– Transvaginal cervical-length measurement: most sensitive tool for cervical insufficiency risk.  </p></br><p>Therapy<br /></br>– Cerclage (McDonald or Shirodkar suture) placed at 12-14 weeks for history-indicated cases or at diagnosis of short cervix (&lt; 25 mm) for ultrasound-indicated cases.<br /></br>– Vaginal progesterone is an alternative preventive strategy.<br /></br>– Bed rest alone lacks strong evidence of benefit.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“Cohort = ‘Co’ = go together from the start” → You gather a CO-group and watch them go forward.<br /></br>“Case-control starts with Cases, Cohort starts with Causes (exposure).”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Incorrect  </p></br><p>a. Case-control<br /></br>• Begins with subjects who ALREADY HAVE the outcome (cases) and compares past exposure to controls. Here, subjects are enrolled before outcome is known – opposite direction.  </p></br><p>b. Case-matched (paired) study<br /></br>• Not a standard epidemiologic design; sometimes refers to matching cases and controls on confounders within a case-control study. Again, outcome-based sampling is absent here.  </p></br><p>d. Descriptive<br /></br>• Simply characterizes a population (e.g., case series, cross-sectional prevalence study) without assessing causal relationships or following outcomes over time. Our study compares incidence of preterm birth based on exposure status, making it analytic, not purely descriptive.  </p></br><p>e. Randomized<br /></br>• Requires random allocation of exposure/intervention. In this vignette the patient chooses (or is “offered”) cerclage vs bed rest; no randomization, so it is observational, not an RCT.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway:<br /></br>Because investigators measure an exposure at baseline and follow subjects forward in time to observe the incidence of an outcome without randomization, the design is a prospective cohort study.</p>
181. A 67-year-old woman, gravida 5, para 5, is admitted to the hospital because of a 6-month history of increasingly severe abdominal pain and distention and difficulty eating. During the past 2 weeks, she has had increased abdominal girth. She also has had a 4-day history of nausea and vomiting. Ten years ago, she had breast cancer treated with bilateral mastectomy; she took tamoxifen therapy for 6 years. Her pulse is 68/min, respirations are 20/min, and blood pressure is 124/72 mm Hg. Breath sounds are decreased at the lung bases. The abdomen is distended, tense, and tender; there is a fluid shift. The genitalia are normal. On bimanual examination, a midline pelvic mass is palpated and several 1-cm nodules are palpated along the rectovaginal septum. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin       10.2 g/dL</br>Serum</br>Urea nitrogen    10 mg/dL</br>Creatinine       1 mg/dL</br>CA 125           2270 U/mL (N<35)</br>Following surgical removal of the masses, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: D</br></br>a. Observation only</br>b. Hormone therapy</br>c. Immunotherapy</br>d. Chemotherapy</br>e. Radiation therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>d. Chemotherapy  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – Postmenopausal woman (67 y) with 6 mo of bloating, early satiety, ↑abdominal girth → classic “ovarian cancer” presentation.<br /></br>  – Ascites (fluid wave, ↓breath sounds at bases) + nodularity along rectovaginal septum → peritoneal seeding.<br /></br>  – Very high CA-125 (2270 U/mL) → supports epithelial ovarian carcinoma.<br /></br>  – “Midline pelvic mass” palpated → suggests adnexal origin.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives = abdominal distention, ascites, pelvic mass, CA-125 elevation, peritoneal implants.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives = no liver mets or extra-abdominal disease mentioned.<br /></br>Cognitive road map for similar questions:<br /></br>1. Recognize typical vague GI/abdominal complaints + ascites in a post-menopausal woman → think ovarian cancer.<br /></br>2. Stage is presumed IIIC/IV when peritoneal nodules or malignant ascites are present.<br /></br>3. Standard of care = cytoreductive (debulking) surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise approach:<br /></br>1. Diagnose advanced epithelial ovarian carcinoma (EOC) from presentation + CA-125.<br /></br>2. Initial therapy = exploratory laparotomy with total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, omentectomy, and maximal tumor debulking (“optimal cytoreduction”).<br /></br>3. Because peritoneal spread = ≥ stage III, microscopic residual disease is expected even after good debulking.<br /></br>4. Therefore adjuvant systemic treatment is mandatory → IV or intraperitoneal paclitaxel + carboplatin for 6 cycles (± bevacizumab in modern protocols).<br /></br>Core takeaway: All stage II–IV EOC requires chemotherapy after surgery; observation is reserved only for completely resected stage IA/B, grade 1 tumors.  </p></br><p>Documentation point: operative note should specify completeness of cytoreduction (&lt;1 cm residual), final stage, and plan for adjuvant paclitaxel/carboplatin.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• EOC arises from ovarian surface epithelium; spreads transcoelomically throughout the peritoneal cavity → ascites and serosal implants.<br /></br>• CA-125 is a glycoprotein from coelomic epithelium; useful for monitoring treatment response, not for screening average-risk women.<br /></br>Risk factors: nulliparity, infertility, early menarche/late menopause, BRCA1/2, but this patient’s major clue is post-menopausal status.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>1. Surgery: TAH-BSO, omentectomy, peritoneal washings, biopsies, lymph node sampling, aim for &lt;1 cm residual.<br /></br>2. Chemotherapy (adjuvant):<br /></br>   • First-line = Paclitaxel (Taxol) + Carboplatin every 3 wk × 6.<br /></br>   • Consider intraperitoneal delivery when optimal cytoreduction obtained.<br /></br>3. Imaging/labs after chemo: serial CA-125 and CT scans to document response.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“OVARY = OPeration (debulking) then OPlatin + pacliTAXel.”<br /></br>Think “TAXel attacks microtubules, CARBoplATIN for CARBon copy of cisplatin.”  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Observation only – Appropriate only for stage IA/B grade 1; this patient has stage III disease (peritoneal implants).<br /></br>b. Hormone therapy – Useful for ER-positive breast/endometrial cancers or low-grade serous ovarian tumors in select cases, not standard adjuvant therapy for typical EOC.<br /></br>c. Immunotherapy – Still investigational for ovarian cancer; no proven survival benefit as sole adjuvant therapy.<br /></br>e. Radiation therapy – Ovarian cancer is usually multifocal in the peritoneum; radiation cannot treat widespread microscopic disease and is used only for focal palliation.  </p></br><p>──────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>Advanced epithelial ovarian carcinoma requires optimal cytoreductive surgery followed by adjuvant platinum-taxane chemotherapy to eradicate microscopic residual disease and improve survival.</p>
182. A previously healthy 32-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-month history of intermittent milky discharge from her breasts. During the past year, menses have occurred at irregular 15- to 32-day intervals. She takes no medications. Her mother and sister were diagnosed with pituitary tumors at the ages of 50 and 44 years, respectively; they also have increased serum calcium concentrations. The patient’s temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 90/min and regular, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 156/70 mm Hg. On examination, milky discharge is expressed from both breasts. The remainder of the examination, including funduscopic examination and visual field testing, shows no abnormalities. Serum studies show:</br>Na+     140 mEq/L  </br>K+     3.9 mEq/L  </br>Cl−     109 mEq/L  </br>Ca2+    12.4 mg/dL  </br>Urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL  </br>Glucose   90 mg/dL  </br>Creatinine  1.2 mg/dL  </br>Protein, total 9 g/dL  </br> Albumin  4.8 g/dL  </br>Phosphorus  2 mg/dL  </br>Intact parathyroid hormone 90 pg/mL (N=10–65)  </br>Prolactin   140 ng/mL  </br>β-hCG    <5 mIU/mL  </br></br>In addition to bromocriptine therapy, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: F</br></br>a. Adrenal tumor resection  </br>b. Bisphosphonate therapy  </br>c. Estrogen therapy  </br>d. Pituitary adenoma resection  </br>e. Prednisone therapy  </br>f. Subtotal parathyroid resection</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>f. Subtotal parathyroid resection  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 32-year-old woman with galactorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and markedly ↑ prolactin (140 ng/mL) → prolactinoma.<br /></br>   • Repeated family history of “pituitary tumors” + “increased calcium” in mother &amp; sister.<br /></br>   • Hypercalcemia (12.4 mg/dL), hypophosphatemia (2 mg/dL), and inappropriately high intact PTH (90 pg/mL) → primary hyperparathyroidism.<br /></br>   • Young age, multisystem endocrine disease, and autosomal-dominant family pattern → think MEN type 1 (Wermer syndrome).  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No visual field loss or funduscopic abnormality → prolactinoma is likely micro-adenoma (radiation/surgery not required initially).<br /></br>   • No medications that raise prolactin (e.g., antipsychotics, metoclopramide).<br /></br>   • Hypertension present; could be related to hypercalcemia but no stigmata of pheochromocytoma (which belongs to MEN2, not MEN1).  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Cognitive strategy: Whenever you see (Pituitary dz + Hypercalcemia) in a young patient, quickly screen for MEN1’s other “P”(Pancreatic/GI) tumors and remember that parathyroid disease is almost always the earliest &amp; most common MEN1 manifestation (&gt;90%).  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step 1: Treat the prolactinoma – first-line is a dopamine agonist (bromocriptine or cabergoline). The stem already states “in addition to bromocriptine therapy,” so prolactin issue addressed.  </p></br><p>Step 2: Address the primary hyperparathyroidism. Definitive therapy for MEN1-related parathyroid hyperplasia is surgical: subtotal (3½-gland) parathyroidectomy or total resection with autotransplantation. Indications for surgery are met:<br /></br>   • Age &lt;50 yr<br /></br>   • Serum Ca &gt;1 mg/dL above upper limit of normal<br /></br>   • Genetic syndrome with multigland disease (MEN1)  </p></br><p>Therefore, the next best step is subtotal parathyroid resection (choice f).  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: In MEN1, multiple parathyroid glands are hypercellular; medical therapy does not cure. Early surgical removal prevents nephrolithiasis, bone disease, and neurocognitive complications.  </p></br><p>Documentation points (real life): Calcium, PTH, phosphorus levels; nephrolithiasis work-up; bone density; family genetic counseling; operative consent for subtotal parathyroidectomy.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>MEN1 (Wermer)<br /></br>• Autosomal-dominant mutation in MEN1 tumor-suppressor gene (chromosome 11q13)<br /></br>• “3 P’s” = Parathyroid (hyperplasia/adenoma) &gt; Pituitary (prolactinoma, GH-secreting, etc.) &gt; Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinoma, insulinoma, VIPoma, glucagonoma)<br /></br>• Parathyroid disease is usually first and most penetrant, leading to hypercalcemia, kidney stones, osteitis fibrosa cystica.  </p></br><p>Primary hyperparathyroidism<br /></br>• ↑PTH → ↑bone resorption, ↑renal Ca reabsorption, ↓phosphate reabsorption → hypercalcemia + hypophosphatemia.<br /></br>• Symptoms: “stones, bones, groans, psychiatric overtones,” HTN.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Summary  </p></br><ol></br><li>Prolactinoma: Dopamine agonist (cabergoline preferred, bromocriptine acceptable). Reserve trans-sphenoidal surgery for medication failure or macro-adenoma with mass effect.  </li></br><li>Primary hyperparathyroidism in MEN1: Subtotal (3½ gland) parathyroidectomy.  </li></br><li>Screen for additional MEN1 tumors: fasting gastrin, insulin, glucagon, pancreatic imaging, MRI brain if mass effect suspected.  </li></br><li>Post-op monitoring: serum Ca, PTH, “hungry bone” syndrome prophylaxis (Ca/Vit D).  </li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “Men like to P-P-P”:<br /></br>• MEN1 = Pituitary, Parathyroid, Pancreatic.<br /></br>Remember: “The FIRST P (Parathyroid) often needs the FIRST knife (surgery).”  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong  </p></br><p>a. Adrenal tumor resection – Would treat pheochromocytoma (MEN2), not MEN1; no catecholamine symptoms or elevated metanephrines.  </p></br><p>b. Bisphosphonate therapy – Temporarily lowers Ca by inhibiting osteoclasts but is not definitive for primary hyperparathyroidism, especially in young MEN1 patient.  </p></br><p>c. Estrogen therapy – Would suppress lactation but does not treat underlying prolactinoma; can worsen hypercoagulability and does nothing for hyperparathyroidism.  </p></br><p>d. Pituitary adenoma resection – Reserved for dopamine-agonist–resistant tumors or macro-adenomas with visual compromise. Medical treatment has just been initiated and patient lacks mass-effect symptoms.  </p></br><p>e. Prednisone therapy – Helpful for hypercalcemia due to granulomatous disease or vitamin D intoxication; not for PTH-mediated causes.  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line: Young patient with hyperprolactinemia + primary hyperparathyroidism + family history = MEN1. After starting a dopamine agonist, definitive management is subtotal parathyroidectomy to cure the primary hyperparathyroidism.</p>
183. A 67-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a 20-minute history of palpitations, dizziness, and fatigue. He has hypertension controlled with hydrochlorothiazide and lisinopril. His pulse is 110/min and irregular, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 126/70 mm Hg. Examination shows no other abnormalities. An ECG shows atrial fibrillation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Cardiac catheterization</br>b. Chemical cardioversion</br>c. Clopidogrel therapy</br>d. Low-molecular-weight heparin therapy</br>e. Metoprolol therapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>e. Metoprolol therapy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 67-year-old man with sudden‐onset palpitations, dizziness, fatigue<br /></br>   • Irregularly irregular pulse 110/min → classic for atrial fibrillation (AF)<br /></br>   • Blood pressure 126/70 mm Hg, mentating appropriately → hemodynamically stable (no chest pain, pulmonary edema, hypotension, or syncope)<br /></br>   • Duration only 20 min → technically &lt; 48 h, but stability + mild symptoms drive initial management.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive road map for AF questions<br /></br>   Step 1: Is the patient unstable?<br /></br>       – YES → immediate synchronized cardioversion.<br /></br>       – NO  → proceed to ventricular rate control.<br /></br>   Step 2: After rate control, assess need for anticoagulation (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) and consider rhythm control/cardioversion.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>This patient = hemodynamically stable → go to Step 2: rate control.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Goal of acute therapy in a stable patient is to slow AV-node conduction, preventing rapid ventricular response and relieving symptoms.<br /></br>• First-line IV agents:<br /></br>  – β-blocker (e.g., metoprolol, esmolol)<br /></br>  – Non-dihydropyridine Ca²⁺-channel blocker (diltiazem, verapamil)<br /></br>  – Digoxin if hypotensive or decompensated HF.  </p></br><p>Metoprolol is therefore the most appropriate next step.  </p></br><p>Take-home message: “Stable AF: Control the RATE first (β-blocker or diltiazem), then think RHYTHM and ANTICOAG.”  </p></br><p>Documentation pearls (real life): “Patient in new-onset AF with RVR 110 bpm, hemodynamically stable. Administered IV metoprolol 5 mg, plan for CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation and TTE.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• AF = chaotic atrial electrical activity (multiple re-entry circuits) → no organized atrial contraction → irregularly irregular ventricular response conducted via AV node.<br /></br>• Rapid impulses → inadequate LV filling time → palpitations, dizziness, fatigue; can precipitate hypotension or heart failure.<br /></br>• Loss of atrial kick + blood stasis in left atrial appendage predisposes to thromboembolism → stroke risk drives need for anticoagulation.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Pearls  </p></br><p>Immediate (stable)<br /></br>1. Rate control (IV metoprolol 2.5–5 mg q5–10 min, or diltiazem 15–20 mg).<br /></br>2. Evaluate for reversible causes (electrolytes, TSH, ischemia).<br /></br>3. Decide on anticoagulation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (male) → DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban) or warfarin.<br /></br>4. Rhythm control (electrical or pharmacologic cardioversion with ibutilide, propafenone, or amiodarone) may follow if &lt; 48 h or after ≥3 wk anticoagulation / TEE to exclude thrombus.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“ABC for new AF”<br /></br>A – Assess stability<br /></br>B – Beta-blocker (or calcium-blocker) for rate<br /></br>C – Consider Coumadin/DOAC &amp; Cardioversion later  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Cardiac catheterization<br /></br>   • Indicated for acute coronary syndromes, not isolated AF without ischemic features. No chest pain or ST changes given.  </p></br><p>b. Chemical cardioversion<br /></br>   • Acceptable only after rate control and either (1) &lt; 48 h onset with substantial symptoms or (2) exclusion of atrial thrombus. Jumping straight to rhythm control in a stable patient ignores guideline-preferred rate control first.  </p></br><p>c. Clopidogrel therapy<br /></br>   • Dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin ± clopidogrel) is inferior to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF and is not guideline-recommended.  </p></br><p>d. Low-molecular-weight heparin therapy<br /></br>   • Anticoagulation is indeed considered, but LMWH is not the optimal chronic agent for non-valvular AF, and it does not address immediate symptom relief. First priority = rate control; anticoag decision follows.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Bottom Line  </p></br><p>For hemodynamically stable new-onset atrial fibrillation, initiate ventricular rate control with a β-blocker (e.g., metoprolol). Assess anticoagulation needs and consider rhythm control only after rate is controlled or if patient becomes unstable.</p>
184. An 82-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department 30 minutes after collapsing at a shopping center. She was intubated at the scene. On admission, she is comatose and requires mechanical ventilation. Her pulse is 120/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 90/60 mm Hg. She does not respond to painful stimuli. Thirty minutes later, her granddaughter comes to the hospital and shows the physician an advance directive signed by the patient requesting no intubation or "artificial means to prolong life." Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Contact the hospital ethics committee</br>b. Contact the patient's attorney regarding plans for extubation</br>c. Continue mechanical ventilation and monitor closely in the coronary care unit</br>d. Obtain nuclear brain blood flow scan to determine brain death</br>e. Extubate the patient and provide supportive care only</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Extubate the patient and provide supportive care only  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li>Key phrases<br /></br>   • “Advance directive signed by the patient requesting no intubation or ‘artificial means to prolong life.’”<br /></br>   • Patient currently lacks decision-making capacity (comatose, on a ventilator).<br /></br>   • Written directive is brought in within 1 hour of arrival (timely verification).  </li></br><li>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Formal, signed document = legally valid expression of the patient’s wishes (autonomy).  </li></br><li>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No conflicting document or surrogate stating otherwise.<br /></br>   • No uncertainty about authenticity or applicability.  </li></br><li>Cognitive approach<br /></br>   • Step 1: Does the patient have capacity?  ⇒ No.<br /></br>   • Step 2: Is there an advance directive that clearly applies?  ⇒ Yes.<br /></br>   • Step 3: Is there any reason to question its validity?  ⇒ No.<br /></br>   • Conclusion: Honor the directive immediately—withdraw life-sustaining therapy and provide comfort care.  </li></br></ol></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Core concept: Patient autonomy survives loss of capacity through advance directives; a valid directive is ethically and legally binding.<br /></br>• Documentation (if this were a real patient):<br /></br>  – Place directive in the chart (scan/copy).<br /></br>  – Document time of review, conversation with granddaughter, decision to extubate, and orders for comfort measures (e.g., oxygen for dyspnea, morphine for pain, hospice consult).  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (Brief)  </p></br><p>None required—this is an ethics/end-of-life question. The physiologic state is irrelevant to the ethical obligation once the directive is confirmed.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review  </p></br><p>First-line management:<br /></br>1. Withdraw life-prolonging treatment inconsistent with the directive (here, mechanical ventilation).<br /></br>2. Initiate comfort-focused (palliative) measures: opioids for pain/dyspnea, antiemetics, mouth care, family support, possible hospice referral.  </p></br><p>No additional diagnostic tests are needed to “fulfill” the directive.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memory Tips  </p></br><p>“Paper &gt; Ventilator”: A signed paper (advance directive) outweighs ongoing ventilatory support—turn it off and focus on comfort.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Contact the hospital ethics committee<br /></br>   • Ethics consults help when wishes are unclear or there is dispute. Here, the patient’s instruction is explicit—no ethical conflict.  </p></br><p>B. Contact the patient’s attorney regarding plans for extubation<br /></br>   • Legal counsel is unnecessary when a valid directive is present; delaying withdrawal violates autonomy and could be battery.  </p></br><p>C. Continue mechanical ventilation and monitor in the CCU<br /></br>   • Directly contradicts the patient’s stated refusal of “artificial means to prolong life.” Autonomy &gt; beneficence.  </p></br><p>D. Obtain a nuclear brain blood flow scan to determine brain death<br /></br>   • Brain-death testing is irrelevant to honoring a “do not intubate” order; extubation is required regardless of brain-death status.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>In the presence of a clear, valid advance directive that opposes life-sustaining therapy, clinicians must withdraw that therapy and transition to comfort care—no additional approvals, ethics consultations, or tests are required.</p>
185. An 18-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 3-day history of fatigue and yellow eyes. She has a history of mild anemia but is otherwise healthy. Her 16-year-old brother also has mild anemia. Her temperature is 36.7°C (98°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 105/60 mm Hg. There is scleral icterus. Examination shows multiple, 1-cm, cervical lymph nodes that are mildly tender. The spleen tip is palpated 3 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies show:</br>Hemoglobin                   8.6 g/dL</br>Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration     38 percent Hb/cell</br>Reticulocyte count           8 percent</br>Serum total bilirubin        3 mg/dL</br>A direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test is negative. A blood smear is shown. Which of the following is most likely to have prevented this patient's current symptoms?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Intravenous IgG therapy</br>b. Monthly transfusions of packed red blood cells</br>c. Prednisone therapy</br>d. Bone marrow transplant</br>e. Splenectomy</br>f. No preventive measures are efficacious</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>e. Splenectomy  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Key clues:<br /></br>  – 18-year-old with life-long “mild anemia” and an affected sibling ⇒ inherited hemolytic disorder.<br /></br>  – Acute exacerbation after a presumed viral illness (tender cervical nodes).<br /></br>  – Jaundice, scleral icterus, splenomegaly ⇒ hemolysis with extravascular clearance.<br /></br>  – Labs: Hb 8.6 g/dL (low), retic 8 % (marrow responding), total bilirubin 3 mg/dL (unconjugated), ↑MCHC 38 % (classic for hereditary spherocytosis).<br /></br>  – Negative direct Coombs ⇒ rules out autoimmune hemolytic anemia.<br /></br>  – Blood smear (not shown) in this setting almost certainly demonstrates small, round, dark “golf-ball” RBCs without central pallor (spherocytes).  </p></br><p>Pattern recognition: young patient + family history + high MCHC + Coombs(–) spherocytes = Hereditary spherocytosis (HS).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: On test day, “↑MCHC + splenomegaly + Coombs negative = think hereditary spherocytosis → splenectomy helps.”  </p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>HS is caused by spectrin, ankyrin, or band 3 membrane defects. The misshapen RBCs are mechanically trapped and destroyed in the splenic cords (extravascular hemolysis). Removing the spleen eliminates the major site of destruction, preventing hemolytic episodes, correcting anemia, and normalizing bilirubin. (The liver can still clear old cells, but at a rate compatible with normal life span.)  </p></br><p>Documentation items if real patient: diagnosis of HS, degree of anemia/hemolysis, discussion of splenectomy benefits &amp; lifelong infection risk, vaccination status (PCV, meningococcal, Hib), folate supplementation.  </p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Autosomal-dominant defects in RBC cytoskeleton proteins.<br /></br>• Consequences: Decreased surface-to-volume ratio → spherocytes → ↓deformability → splenic sequestration &amp; destruction.<br /></br>• Laboratory hallmarks: ↑MCHC, ↑RDW, reticulocytosis, indirect hyperbilirubinemia. Osmotic fragility test positive; eosin-5-maleimide binding decreased.  </p></br><p>Treatments &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Acute/ongoing: folic acid 1 mg/d, transfusion only for severe crises.<br /></br>2. Curative/preventive: elective splenectomy (ideally after age ≥5 to reduce postsplenectomy sepsis risk) ± cholecystectomy if pigmented gallstones present.<br /></br>3. Pre-op: vaccinate against encapsulated organisms; educate about fever management &amp; prophylactic antibiotics in childhood.  </p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Sphero-CYTES destroyed in the SPLENO-CITES → Remove the SPLEN-O to save the spheres.”<br /></br>OR “High MCHC = Membrane Can’t Handle Compression → take out the ‘compressor’ (spleen).”  </p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. IV IgG therapy – First-line for autoimmune (warm) hemolytic anemia, which would be Coombs positive; not useful in HS.<br /></br>b. Monthly PRBC transfusions – Needed in severe β-thalassemia major or sickle cell stroke prophylaxis; doesn’t stop splenic destruction in HS and would lead to iron overload.<br /></br>c. Prednisone – Another treatment for immune hemolysis (warm AIHA); ineffective when hemolysis is mechanical/inherited.<br /></br>d. Bone marrow transplant – Considered for life-threatening marrow failure or sickle cell disease; marrow in HS is normal and reticulating.<br /></br>f. No preventive measures – Wrong; splenectomy significantly reduces hemolysis, anemia, jaundice, and gallstone risk.  </p></br><p>Core Takeaway<br /></br>Hereditary spherocytosis presents with Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia, elevated MCHC, and splenomegaly. Splenectomy is the definitive intervention that prevents further hemolytic episodes.</p>
186. An 8-year-old boy is brought to the physician 1 hour after the sudden onset of facial swelling. He has had an upper respiratory tract infection and sore throat for the past 2 days. He has a history of six similar episodes since birth requiring hospitalization; two of the episodes required endotracheal intubation. All episodes presented the same except for one episode of facial swelling that began after he fell and cut his lip. He has no known allergies. His father, several paternal aunts, and some paternal cousins have had similar episodes. He appears anxious, and his voice is hoarse. His axillary temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 120/84 mm Hg. Examination shows edema of the lips and tongue; there is no tenderness, erythema, pain, or itching. Mild inspiratory stridor is heard. Which of the following serum studies is most likely to be abnormal?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Antinuclear antibodies</br>b. C1 esterase inhibitor</br>c. C8</br>d. Eosinophil count</br>e. IgA</br>f. IgE</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>B. C1 esterase inhibitor (quantitative level and/or functional activity will be ↓)</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues that point straight to hereditary angioedema (HAE):<br /></br>• Recurrent, non-pruritic, painless angioedema (lips, tongue, face).<br /></br>• NO urticaria, erythema, or itching → argues against IgE-mediated allergy.<br /></br>• Precipitated by minor trauma and intercurrent infection (classic triggers in HAE).<br /></br>• Strong autosomal-dominant family history (father, aunts, cousins).<br /></br>• Onset in childhood; several attacks severe enough to require intubation.<br /></br>• Normal vital signs aside from mild tachycardia; afebrile (helps exclude infection).</p></br><p>Cognitive / test-taking tip:<br /></br>When you see “recurrent angioedema without urticaria + family history,” immediately think “measure C4 and C1-INH” → that puts HAE at the top of the differential.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-by-step logic the test-writer expects:<br /></br>1. Identify pattern: isolated, hereditary angioedema picture.<br /></br>2. Recall pathophysiology: AD deficiency or dysfunction of C1 esterase inhibitor.<br /></br>   • Loss of inhibition of kallikrein → ↑ bradykinin → ↑ vascular permeability → angioedema.<br /></br>3. Ask: Which lab test will be abnormal? → C1-INH level/function.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Recurrent, non-allergic angioedema = check complement (↓C4) and C1-INH.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl (real life): airway status, precipitating factors, give C1-INH concentrate or icatibant, arrange genetic counseling.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Gene: SERPING1 on chromosome 11 (AD, 50% de novo).<br /></br>• Two types:<br /></br>  – Type I (≈85%): ↓ quantity of C1-INH protein.<br /></br>  – Type II: normal quantity but dysfunctional protein.<br /></br>• Result: uninhibited C1 activation + kallikrein activity → consumption of C4/C2 and excess bradykinin.<br /></br>• Attacks affect skin, GI tract (colicky pain), airway; NO histamine involvement, so antihistamines, steroids, epinephrine are largely ineffective.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics &amp; Treatment Snapshot  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Serum C4 (screen) – characteristically low even between attacks.<br /></br>2. Quantitative C1-INH level ± functional assay – low or dysfunctional (abnormal in this patient).<br /></br>3. C1q normal (helps distinguish hereditary from acquired C1-INH deficiency).</p></br><p>Acute treatment<br /></br>• Secure airway.<br /></br>• C1-INH concentrate (Berinert, Cinryze).<br /></br>• Bradykinin B2-receptor antagonist (icatibant).<br /></br>• Kallikrein inhibitor (ecallantide).<br /></br>• Fresh frozen plasma if above not available.</p></br><p>Prophylaxis<br /></br>• Attenuated androgens (danazol) or regular C1-INH infusions.<br /></br>• Avoid ACE inhibitors (also increase bradykinin).</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“C1-INH keeps C1 and Kallikrein ‘1NHibited’; without it, Brady the ‘kinin’ breaks free → Brady-kinin edema.”<br /></br>Visual: A guard dog labeled “C1-INH” holding back Brady the giant swelling balloon.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Antinuclear antibodies – Positive in SLE; SLE angioedema is extremely rare and would include constitutional or renal symptoms, not isolated hereditary pattern.  </p></br><p>c. C8 – Terminal complement deficiencies predispose to recurrent Neisseria infections, not angioedema.  </p></br><p>d. Eosinophil count – Would be elevated in allergic (IgE-mediated) angioedema, asthma, or parasitosis; vignette lacks pruritus, urticaria, and allergen exposure.  </p></br><p>e. IgA – Selective IgA deficiency causes recurrent sinopulmonary/GI infections and anaphylaxis to transfusions, not angioedema.  </p></br><p>f. IgE – Elevated in atopic disease or type I hypersensitivity reactions (urticarial, pruritic, rapid-onset); again, no urticaria or itching here.</p></br><p>───────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Key Take-Home Message  </p></br><p>Recurrent, non-pruritic angioedema beginning in childhood with AD inheritance ⇒ think hereditary angioedema.<br /></br>Diagnostic hallmark: ↓ or dysfunctional C1 esterase inhibitor with ↓C4.<br /></br>Always secure airway first; treat with C1-INH concentrate or bradykinin blockers—not antihistamines or steroids.</p>
187. A 62-year-old man is brought to the emergency department 4 hours after the sudden onset of vomiting and severe abdominal pain. During the past year, he has had mild, intermittent epigastric pain, which is relieved with antacid use. He has no history of serious illness. He smoked one and one-half packs of cigarettes daily for 35 years. He drinks one to two alcoholic beverages daily. He appears diaphoretic and is in acute distress. His temperature is 38.4°C (101.1°F), pulse is 100/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg. The abdomen is rigid and tympanic to percussion over the liver. There is severe diffuse tenderness. Bowel sounds are absent. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. X-rays of the chest and abdomen  </br>b. Barium enema  </br>c. Water-soluble contrast swallow  </br>d. CT scan of the abdomen  </br>e. Colonoscopy  </br>f. Flexible esophagogastroduodenoscopy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer:  a. X-rays of the chest and abdomen (upright chest and left-lateral-decubitus or upright abdominal film)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key phrases: “sudden onset of vomiting and severe abdominal pain,” “rigid and tympanic abdomen,” “severe diffuse tenderness,” “absent bowel sounds,” fever and tachycardia.<br /></br>• Background: 1-year history of epigastric pain relieved by antacids → classic for peptic ulcer disease (PUD).<br /></br>• Tympany over liver = free intraperitoneal air rising anterior to the liver.<br /></br>• Together these findings scream perforated peptic ulcer with resulting chemical peritonitis.</p></br><p>Test-taking approach:</br>1. Recognize peritoneal signs → surgical abdomen.<br /></br>2. Before the surgeon opens the belly, quickly confirm perforation by looking for free air.<br /></br>3. Fastest, simplest, bedside-ready study = upright chest ± abdominal plain film.</p></br><p>Cognitive pearl: In any patient with acute abdomen + suspected hollow-viscus perforation, the FIRST diagnostic step is an upright CXR/AXR. CT is more sensitive, but plain film is quicker and usually diagnostic if positive.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Why plain films?)</br>• Goal: Demonstrate pneumoperitoneum (free air under diaphragm).<br /></br>• Upright chest film will show crescent of air beneath the diaphragm; left-lateral-decubitus abdominal film can show air outlining the liver margin.<br /></br>• Readily available in the ED, takes &lt;5 minutes, no contrast needed, guides emergent surgical consult.</p></br><p>Take-home: “Perforation pain? Get upright plain X-rays for air under the diaphragm.”</p></br><p>Documentation (real life): time of onset, vitals, peritoneal signs, NPO status, IV fluids, antibiotics given, results of upright CXR/AXR, page surgery.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Brief</br>• Chronic H. pylori infection, NSAID use, or smoking (present here) weaken mucosal barrier → duodenal or gastric ulcer.<br /></br>• Ulcer erodes full-thickness → gastric/duodenal contents spill → chemical then bacterial peritonitis → reflex rigidity, absent bowel sounds, systemic inflammatory response (fever, tachycardia).<br /></br>• Free gas escapes into peritoneal cavity → collects beneath diaphragm.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Snapshot</br>1. Immediate: Upright CXR/AXR → if free air present, call surgery.<br /></br>2. Labs: CBC (leukocytosis), BMP, type &amp; cross.<br /></br>3. Resuscitation: NPO, IV fluids, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam), NG tube decompression, IV PPI.<br /></br>4. Definitive: Emergency laparoscopic Graham patch or definitive ulcer surgery ± H. pylori therapy afterward.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids</br>• “PerfoRATE → Plain RadiATE” = perforate → radiate to X-ray.<br /></br>• Visual: picture a crescent moon of air hugging the diaphragm on an upright CXR.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>b. Barium enema: Looks for colonic lesions; barium would leak into peritoneum making peritonitis worse.<br /></br>c. Water-soluble contrast swallow: Used for suspected esophageal perforation (Boerhaave). Here the presentation/localization fits gastric/duodenal ulcer, not esophagus.<br /></br>d. CT scan of abdomen: More sensitive but slower, higher radiation, often done if plain film equivocal. Initial quick screen is still plain films.<br /></br>e. Colonoscopy: Contraindicated in acute peritonitis; risk of further perforation.<br /></br>f. Flexible esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD): Also contraindicated in suspected perforated viscus—insufflation can enlarge the perforation and worsen peritonitis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>Acute abdomen + peritoneal signs + history suggestive of PUD = suspect perforated ulcer. Upright chest/abdominal X-ray is the fastest, safest, and most appropriate first diagnostic step to look for free intraperitoneal air.</p>
188. A 6-year-old boy is brought to the physician by his parents because of a 3-day history of progressive pain, swelling, and redness around his right eye. The swelling began on his right upper eyelid and has now spread to the whole eye. He says that it hurts when he moves his eyes. He also has had fever and headache during this period. He has a 2-week history of cough and nasal congestion. He takes no medications. Immunizations are up-to-date. His temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 110/min, respirations are 22/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg. Examination shows exophthalmos of the right eye; the upper and lower eyelids are erythematous and edematous. The erythema does not extend beyond the superior orbital rim. He cannot fully abduct his right eye on lateral gaze. The remainder of the examination shows no abnormalities. A CT scan of the head is shown. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Glaucoma</br>b. Neuroblastoma</br>c. Orbital cellulitis</br>d. Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis</br>e. Retinoblastoma</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Orbital cellulitis  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; recent illness: 6-year-old with 2-week “cold” (cough + nasal congestion) → ethmoid or maxillary sinusitis is a common antecedent infection that seeds the orbit.<br /></br>• Key red-flag ocular findings:<br /></br>  – Pain with extra-ocular movements (“it hurts when he moves his eyes”)<br /></br>  – Ophthalmoplegia (cannot abduct right eye)<br /></br>  – Proptosis/exophthalmos<br /></br>  – Fever &amp; headache (systemic toxicity)<br /></br>These four findings point to post-septal spread rather than isolated eyelid involvement.<br /></br>• CT image (not described in words on test day but usually shows opacified ethmoid sinus + orbital fat stranding/abscess) confirms infection posterior to the orbital septum.</p></br><p>How to approach similar questions:<br /></br>1. First decide “pre-septal vs post-septal.” Ask yourself: proptosis? painful/restricted EOM? vision change? systemic toxicity? If any are YES → orbital cellulitis.<br /></br>2. Remember most cases follow ethmoid sinusitis in children.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step-wise thought process:<br /></br>1. Diffuse eyelid swelling + fever = cellulitis somewhere.<br /></br>2. Add proptosis + EOM pain/limitation → must be posterior to orbital septum.<br /></br>3. Recent URI supports contiguous spread from sinus → orbital cellulitis.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Painful ophthalmoplegia or proptosis = orbital cellulitis until proven otherwise.</p></br><p>Documentation bullets for a real patient:<br /></br>• Vision (acuity), pupils, EOMs, color vision<br /></br>• Degree of proptosis, intraocular pressure if possible<br /></br>• Neuro status, funduscopic exam<br /></br>• CT orbit/sinuses ordered<br /></br>• Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics initiated (vancomycin + ceftriaxone/amp-sulbactam) ± ENT/Ophtho consult for drainage</p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Bacterial infection posterior to orbital septum—most commonly Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, anaerobes.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Sinus infection (especially ethmoid via lamina papyracea) → venous/osseous spread into orbital fat &amp; extra-ocular muscles.<br /></br>• Complications: Subperiosteal/orbital abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, vision loss from optic nerve compression.</p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>1. Imaging: Contrast CT orbit + sinuses (or MRI) to confirm diagnosis &amp; look for abscess.<br /></br>2. Empiric IV antibiotics:<br /></br>   • Vancomycin (MRSA coverage) +<br /></br>   • Third-generation cephalosporin (eg, ceftriaxone) or ampicillin-sulbactam for gram-neg &amp; anaerobes.<br /></br>3. Surgical drainage if abscess, visual compromise, or lack of response within 24–48 h.<br /></br>4. Close ophthalmology follow-up; daily vision checks.</p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>High-Yield Memory Aids<br /></br>“POOP” = Pain with eye movement, Ophthalmoplegia, (eye) Orbit pushes forward (proptosis), Pediatric post-sinusitis → think Orbital cellulitis.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Glaucoma (acute angle-closure): Severe eye pain, blurred vision, mid-dilated pupil, corneal haze; no eyelid cellulitis, proptosis, or EOM restriction. Rare in children.<br /></br>b. Neuroblastoma: Tumor of adrenal/sympathetic chain; can cause periorbital ecchymoses (“raccoon eyes”) but not acute eyelid erythema, EOM pain, or fever.<br /></br>d. Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis: Eyelid erythema/edema ANTERIOR to septum; NO pain with eye movement, NO ophthalmoplegia, NO proptosis. This vignette shows all three → rules it out.<br /></br>e. Retinoblastoma: Leukocoria in infants/toddlers; intraocular mass, no external cellulitis signs.</p></br><p>Key distinguishing features hinge on orbital involvement signs (painful EOM, ophthalmoplegia, proptosis).  </p></br><p>─────────────────</p>
