189. A 47-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She has a 2-year history of venous insufficiency and notes daily swelling of her ankles that worsens in the evening. She takes no medications. Her pulse is 80/min, and blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. Cardiopulmonary examination shows no abnormalities. Examination of the lower extremities shows 2+ edema and increased pigmentation. Which of the following antihypertensive drugs would most likely exacerbate this patient's swelling?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Atenolol</br>b. Clonidine</br>c. Lisinopril</br>d. Losartan</br>e. Nifedipine</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Nifedipine (dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis &amp; Test-Taking Strategy<br /></br>1. Key phrases<br /></br>   • “2-year history of venous insufficiency … daily swelling of her ankles that worsens in the evening” → baseline peripheral edema.<br /></br>   • “Blood pressure 160/100 mm Hg” → needs an antihypertensive.<br /></br>   • Question stem: “Which drug would most likely exacerbate this patient’s swelling?” → you must know which antihypertensives worsen peripheral edema.</p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives/negatives<br /></br>   • No heart failure, no pulmonary findings → edema is not cardiogenic.<br /></br>   • Edema already present from venous insufficiency; anything that increases capillary hydrostatic pressure will worsen it.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Metacognitive cue<br /></br>   On exam day, when you see “ankle edema” + “dihydropyridine CCB,” think “arteriolar dilation → edema.”</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>• Dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (CCB) such as nifedipine, amlodipine, and felodipine are notorious for dose-dependent peripheral edema.<br /></br>• Mechanism: They selectively dilate precapillary arterioles but leave post-capillary venules relatively unchanged. This increases capillary hydrostatic pressure → transudation of fluid → ankle swelling.<br /></br>• Patients with pre-existing venous disease have impaired venous return, so the edema is even more pronounced.</p></br><p>Core takeaway: Dihydropyridine CCB = “arteriolar only” dilation → ankle edema.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: In a real note you’d record baseline edema, degree (e.g., 2+), chronic venous stasis changes, and caution/contraindication to CCB use.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Venous insufficiency → incompetent valves → elevated venous pressure → chronic edema &amp; hemosiderin pigmentation.<br /></br>• Adding an arteriolar vasodilator (dihydropyridine CCB, minoxidil, hydralazine) increases arteriolar flow into a venous system that can’t cope → worsened edema.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Diagnostic / Therapeutic Review<br /></br>First-line BP options that do NOT worsen edema in this setting:<br /></br>• ACE inhibitors (lisinopril)<br /></br>• ARBs (losartan)<br /></br>• Thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide)<br /></br>• β-blockers (atenolol)<br /></br>If a CCB is necessary, combining with an ACE-I or ARB can decrease the edema by dilating the post-capillary venules as well.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memory Aids<br /></br>“Di-CCB = Di-ankles” → Dihydropyridine CCBs cause Di-lated ankles.<br /></br>Or: “Nife-de-puff” (nifedipine causes puffiness).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Incorrect<br /></br>a. Atenolol (β-blocker)<br /></br>   • Side effects: bradycardia, fatigue, erectile dysfunction; does not typically cause peripheral edema.</p></br><p>b. Clonidine (central α2-agonist)<br /></br>   • Side effects: sedation, dry mouth, rebound hypertension; minimal effect on peripheral edema.</p></br><p>c. Lisinopril (ACE inhibitor)<br /></br>   • May cause cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema (rare but serious); actually can reduce afterload and venous pressure—often improves edema.</p></br><p>d. Losartan (ARB)<br /></br>   • Similar profile to ACE-I without cough; no worsening of peripheral edema.</p></br><p>e. Nifedipine<br /></br>   • Classic, high-yield association with peripheral edema due to selective arteriolar dilation → correct answer.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Exam-Day Pearl<br /></br>Any time the vignette asks about ankle edema + antihypertensive choice, immediately think “dihydropyridine CCBs worsen it; ACE/ARB often offset it.”</p>
190. An asymptomatic 37-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. She has a history of asthma exacerbated by nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. She has smoked two packs of cigarettes daily for 20 years. Her current medications include inhaled isosetharine and an oral contraceptive. She is 152 cm (5 ft) tall and weighs 50 kg (110 lb); BMI is 22 kg/m^2. Her blood pressure is 140/88 mm Hg. Examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation to prevent stroke in this patient?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Initiate an exercise program</br>b. Discontinue the bronchodilator</br>c. Discontinue the oral contraceptive</br>d. Initiate antihypertensive therapy</br>e. Take one aspirin daily</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>c. Discontinue the oral contraceptive  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>   • 37-year-old woman (≥35 years)<br /></br>   • Smokes 2 packs/day (heavy smoking)<br /></br>   • Uses an oral contraceptive (combined estrogen-progestin, unless otherwise specified)<br /></br>   • Question stem: “most appropriate recommendation to prevent stroke” (primary prevention, not treatment)  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives &amp; negatives<br /></br>   • Positive: Two major, synergistic modifiable risk factors for thrombotic stroke (estrogen exposure + heavy smoking ≥15 cigarettes/day in a woman &gt;35).<br /></br>   • Negative: No neurologic symptoms, normal BMI, no diabetes, cholesterol, or atrial fibrillation mentioned—so thromboembolic risk stands out.  </p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Cognitive approach<br /></br>   Step 1 – List modifiable stroke risks in the vignette: smoking, OCP, mild isolated SBP 140 mm Hg.<br /></br>   Step 2 – Rank impact and immediacy: The combination of estrogen-containing OCP plus smoking in a woman &gt;35 carries the highest relative risk for ischemic stroke (RR ≈ 5–13).<br /></br>   Step 3 – Choose the single best action that directly removes that high-impact risk. → Stop OCP.  </p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home “pattern”: Any woman &gt;35 who smokes ≥15 cigarettes a day should not use estrogen-containing contraception; counsel to stop OCP (or switch to progestin-only/IUD) before anything else.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>• Estrogen increases hepatic synthesis of clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X) and fibrinogen while decreasing antithrombin III → hypercoagulable state.<br /></br>• Smoking causes endothelial damage and platelet activation.<br /></br>• Together they markedly increase arterial thrombotic events (stroke, MI).<br /></br>• Eliminating estrogen exposure yields an immediate, substantial risk reduction—superior to starting BP meds in a patient with only a borderline systolic reading.  </p></br><p>Document in chart: “Counseled patient that combined hormonal contraception is contraindicated in smokers ≥35 y. Discussed alternative methods (copper or levonorgestrel IUD, progestin-only pill, barrier). Patient agreed to discontinue current OCP.”  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>Estrogen-containing OCP → ↑ estrogen →<br /></br>1. ↑ hepatic production of clotting factors &amp; plasminogen activator inhibitor → hypercoagulability<br /></br>2. ↓ antithrombin III activity<br /></br>3. Additional vascular effects (↑ triglycerides, mild HTN)  </p></br><p>Smoking (&gt;15 cigarettes/d) →<br /></br>• Oxidative endothelial injury<br /></br>• ↑ Platelet adhesiveness &amp; aggregation<br /></br>• ↑ Hematocrit and fibrinogen levels  </p></br><p>Result: Multiplicative, not just additive, risk for ischemic stroke and MI in women &gt;35.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Diagnostic &amp; Treatment Pearls  </p></br><p>• If contraception still desired: recommend progestin-only pill, injectable depot-medroxyprogesterone, hormonal IUD, or copper IUD.<br /></br>• Obtain serial BP measurements before labeling hypertension. Lifestyle (exercise, ↓ Na⁺) is first-line for isolated 140/88 in otherwise low-risk young adult.<br /></br>• Aspirin for primary prevention is generally reserved for adults 40–59 with ≥10% 10-yr ASCVD risk; at 37 the bleeding risk outweighs benefit.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“35 + 15 = NO ‘E’”:<br /></br>• Woman ≥35 y<br /></br>• Smokes ≥15 cig/day<br /></br>• Absolutely NO Estrogen-containing contraception  </p></br><p>Visual: Picture a pack of cigarettes morphing into a clot blocking a cerebral artery with the number “35” stamped on it—remind yourself to block estrogen before a stroke blocks the artery.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. Initiate an exercise program<br /></br>   • Helpful for long-term BP &amp; lipid control but does not neutralize the imminent clotting risk of combined OCP + heavy smoking.  </p></br><p>b. Discontinue the bronchodilator (isosetharine)<br /></br>   • β₂-agonists carry minimal to no stroke risk; stopping would worsen asthma control.  </p></br><p>d. Initiate antihypertensive therapy<br /></br>   • Single BP of 140/88 = Stage 2 by ACC/AHA 2017, but guidelines still recommend confirming on separate visits and starting with lifestyle modification if ASCVD risk is low. Even if Rx indicated, OCP discontinuation yields greater stroke-risk reduction here.  </p></br><p>e. Take one aspirin daily<br /></br>   • Primary prevention aspirin in a healthy 37-y-old woman is not recommended (USPSTF: usually consider 40–59 with ≥10% ASCVD risk). Bleeding &gt; benefit.  </p></br><p>━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━<br /></br>Core Takeaway  </p></br><p>For women ≥35 who smoke heavily, the FIRST and most effective step to prevent thrombotic stroke is to remove estrogen exposure—stop the combined oral contraceptive (or switch to a progestin-only / non-hormonal method).</p>
191. A 62-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 2, comes to the physician because of loss of urine that has become progressively worse during the past 3 months. She has a 5-year history of occasional incontinence after feeling a strong urge to void; treatment with oxybutynin has provided no relief of her symptoms. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Current medications include metformin, insulin, and metoprolol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination shows no abnormalities. The external genitalia, vagina, and cervix appear normal. The uterus and adnexa are normal to palpation. There is loss of a large amount of urine with mild pressure on Valsalva maneuver. Her postvoid residual volume is 500 mL. Urinalysis shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Overactive bladder with incontinence</br>b. Overflow incontinence</br>c. Stress incontinence</br>d. Urinary tract infection</br>e. Vesicovaginal fistula</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Overflow incontinence  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Vignette Analysis (How the test-taker should dissect the stem)<br /></br>1. “Loss of urine that has become progressively worse during the past 3 months.” – chronic, worsening pattern.<br /></br>2. “Occasional incontinence after feeling a strong urge to void; treatment with oxybutynin has provided no relief.” – oxybutynin (an antimuscarinic) would normally help urge/overactive bladder; lack of response is a clue it is NOT pure urge incontinence.<br /></br>3. “Type 2 diabetes mellitus” – think autonomic neuropathy → neurogenic bladder with incomplete emptying.<br /></br>4. “Large amount of urine with mild pressure on Valsalva maneuver” – suggests bladder is already over-distended; even small increases in pressure force urine out.<br /></br>5. Post-void residual (PVR) = 500 mL – the critical data point. A PVR &gt;300 mL is diagnostic of overflow incontinence.<br /></br>6. Normal urinalysis, normal pelvic exam – rules out infection, fistula, pelvic organ prolapse.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive tip: Anytime the question actually gives you a PVR, pay attention. Large PVR = overflow; normal/low PVR almost always = stress or urge incontinence.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)<br /></br>Step 1: Identify the incontinent pattern. The stem mixes urgency + leakage with pressure → could mislead, but check objective data.<br /></br>Step 2: Evaluate medication response. Oxybutynin failure lowers probability of overactive bladder.<br /></br>Step 3: Look at PVR. 500 mL clinches overflow incontinence.<br /></br>Step 4: Search for causes. Long-standing diabetes → autonomic neuropathy → detrusor underactivity and impaired bladder sensation → chronic retention/overflow.  </p></br><p>Core takeaway: High post-void residual + chronic urinary retention = overflow incontinence, commonly from neurogenic bladder (diabetes, spinal cord lesions) or outlet obstruction.</p></br><p>Documentation (Real-life)<br /></br>• Bladder scan/PVR 500 mL.<br /></br>• Suspected neurogenic bladder secondary to diabetic autonomic neuropathy.<br /></br>• Plan: intermittent catheterization, schedule voiding, ± bethanechol; address glycemic control.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Diabetic autonomic neuropathy damages parasympathetic innervation → detrusor underactivity and decreased sensation of bladder filling.<br /></br>• Bladder fills &gt; overflow point → continuous or episodic dribbling; any minor increase in intra-abdominal pressure expels urine.<br /></br>• High residual urine raises risk of recurrent UTIs and hydronephrosis.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics (high-yield bullets)<br /></br>First-line:<br /></br>• Clean intermittent catheterization (gold standard).<br /></br>Adjuncts:<br /></br>• Scheduled/timed voiding.<br /></br>• Bethanechol (muscarinic agonist) may improve detrusor contraction.<br /></br>Further evaluation:<br /></br>• Urodynamic studies if diagnosis uncertain.<br /></br>• Renal ultrasound to assess for upper-tract dilation when chronic retention is longstanding.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Overflow = Over-Full bladder.”<br /></br>DIabetes → DIstant nerves damaged → DIsastrous retention → overflow.<br /></br>PVR numbers:<br /></br>• &lt;50 mL = normal<br /></br>• &gt;300 mL = overflow pathologic  </p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>A. Overactive bladder with incontinence (urge)<br /></br>• Path: detrusor overactivity.<br /></br>• Key findings: sudden urge, small PVR (&lt;100–150 mL).<br /></br>• Usually improves with antimuscarinics (oxybutynin, solifenacin). Large residual volume and treatment failure make this unlikely.</p></br><p>C. Stress incontinence<br /></br>• Leakage with cough/sneeze/exercise, small volume, normal PVR.<br /></br>• Due to urethral hypermobility or intrinsic sphincter deficiency.<br /></br>• No high residuals; Valsalva-induced leakage here is because bladder is over-full, not weak pelvic floor.</p></br><p>D. Urinary tract infection<br /></br>• Would show pyuria, nitrites, leukocyte esterase, dysuria or suprapubic pain. UA is normal.</p></br><p>E. Vesicovaginal fistula<br /></br>• Continuous painless urine leakage day and night.<br /></br>• Usually occurs after pelvic surgery, radiation, or obstructed labor; exam often shows clear fluid pooling in vagina. PVR would be low/normal because urine bypasses urethra.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––<br /></br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A very high post-void residual in a diabetic patient with progressive leakage that fails antimuscarinic therapy points to overflow incontinence from neurogenic bladder.</p>
192. A 62-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 1-year history of progressive shortness of breath. He has moderate difficulty breathing at rest and has wheezing after walking 20 feet. He has a chronic cough productive of white sputum that occurred intermittently for 10 years and then started to occur daily 1 year ago. He has not had any other symptoms. He has no other history of serious medical conditions and takes no medications. He has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 47 years. His temperature is 37.1°C (98.7°F), pulse is 76/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 130/70 mm Hg while sitting. Pulse oximetry on room air shows an oxygen saturation of 92 percent. Examination shows no jugular venous distention. Expiratory wheezes are heard bilaterally. Cardiac examination shows no abnormalities. Carotid and femoral pulses are 2+. There is no peripheral edema. Spirometry shows a decreased FEV1 and FEV1:FVC ratio. An ECG shows no abnormalities. A chest x-ray shows an increased anteroposterior diameter. The physician counsels the patient about beginning a smoking cessation program. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. CT scan of the chest</br>b. Prednisone therapy</br>c. Theophylline therapy</br>d. Oxygen therapy</br>e. Use of an ipratropium inhaler</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer Selection<br /></br>E. Use of an ipratropium inhaler  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>• 62-year-old, 47-pack-year smoker → classic risk factor for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).<br /></br>• 1-year progressive dyspnea, chronic productive cough → chronic bronchitis phenotype.<br /></br>• Spirometry: ↓FEV1 and ↓FEV1/FVC (&lt;0.70) → confirms airflow limitation.<br /></br>• O2 sat 92% at rest → does NOT yet meet long-term oxygen criteria.<br /></br>• No acute distress, no JVD, no edema → not an acute exacerbation, no cor pulmonale.<br /></br>• First intervention (smoking cessation) already addressed in the stem.  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach<br /></br>1. Recognize COPD by history + spirometry.<br /></br>2. Apply GOLD outpatient management ladder after smoking cessation.<br /></br>3. Ask: “What is the next chronic controller for symptomatic relief?” → short-acting bronchodilator.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning  </p></br><p>Step-wise thought process<br /></br>1. Diagnose COPD (fixed obstruction).<br /></br>2. Assess current symptom burden: dyspnea with minimal exertion (“wheezing after walking 20 ft”) → moderate symptoms.<br /></br>3. No history of exacerbations/hospitalizations → low future risk.<br /></br>4. GOLD Group B (high symptoms, low risk): initial pharmacotherapy = long- or short-acting bronchodilator; short-acting is acceptable starter.<br /></br>5. Among options, ipratropium (short-acting muscarinic antagonist, SAMA) is the only appropriate first-line controller.</p></br><p>Take-home message<br /></br>After smoking cessation, every symptomatic COPD patient should receive an inhaled short-acting bronchodilator (SABA or SAMA) for immediate relief and as-needed use.  </p></br><p>Documentation pearl<br /></br>“Start inhaled ipratropium 2 puffs Q6 h PRN dyspnea; educate on proper inhaler technique.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review (high-yield)  </p></br><p>• Chronic bronchitis = mucus gland hyperplasia → productive cough ≥3 mo/yr for ≥2 y.<br /></br>• Smoking triggers airway inflammation → fixed narrowing, loss of elastic recoil → ↓FEV1.<br /></br>• Anticholinergics inhibit M3 receptors → ↓bronchoconstriction, ↓mucus secretion → improved airflow.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests Review  </p></br><p>COPD outpatient ladder (simplified):<br /></br>1. Smoking cessation &amp; influenza/PPSV23 vaccines.<br /></br>2. Short-acting bronchodilator (SABA: albuterol or SAMA: ipratropium).<br /></br>3. Add long-acting bronchodilator (LABA or LAMA) if persistent symptoms.<br /></br>4. Add inhaled glucocorticoid (ICS) for frequent exacerbations.<br /></br>5. Evaluate for PDE-4 inhibitor, chronic macrolide, or oxygen if advanced.<br /></br>6. Pulmonary rehab, surgery (bullectomy/LVRS), or transplant for severe disease.  </p></br><p>Long-term oxygen indications<br /></br>• Resting PaO2 ≤55 mm Hg or O2 sat ≤88% OR<br /></br>• PaO2 ≤59 mm Hg with cor pulmonale, right-heart failure, or Hct ≥55%.  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“COPD ABCD”:<br /></br>A – Add (stop) smoking<br /></br>B – Bronchodilator (short-acting)<br /></br>C – Controller (long-acting)<br /></br>D – Discuss steroids/exacerbations  </p></br><p>Mnemonic “Ipratropium Improves Inspiration Immediately.”  </p></br><p>─────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>a. CT scan of the chest<br /></br>• Used for lung cancer screening (low-dose CT) or surgical planning, NOT for initial COPD treatment.  </p></br><p>b. Prednisone therapy<br /></br>• Systemic steroids are for acute exacerbations; chronic oral steroids have unacceptable toxicity.  </p></br><p>c. Theophylline therapy<br /></br>• Narrow therapeutic window, many drug interactions; reserved for refractory cases when inhalers unavailable.  </p></br><p>d. Oxygen therapy<br /></br>• Not indicated because resting O2 sat is 92% (&gt;88%) and no evidence of cor pulmonale.  </p></br><p>e. Ipratropium inhaler (correct)<br /></br>• Short-acting anticholinergic; first pharmacologic step after smoking cessation for symptomatic COPD.</p>
193. A previously healthy 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her parents 60 minutes after she had a 2-minute episode of generalized shaking. During the episode, she was unresponsive, and her eyes were rolled back in her head. On arrival, she is awake and cooperative. Her temperature is 39.8°C (103.6°F), pulse is 130/min, respirations are 12/min, and blood pressure is 90/70 mm Hg. Examination shows an erythematous, bulging right tympanic membrane. The neck is supple. Neurologic examination shows no focal findings. Serum electrolyte concentrations are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. CT scan of the head</br>b. Oral antibiotic therapy</br>c. Mastoidectomy</br>d. Intravenous phenobarbital therapy</br>e. Lumbar puncture</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>B. Oral antibiotic therapy (e.g., high-dose amoxicillin for acute otitis media)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>1. Age window: 2 years old (classic age range for febrile seizures is 6 mo – 5 yr).<br /></br>2. Seizure description: generalized, &lt; 15 min, single episode, complete return to baseline, no focal deficits → “simple” febrile seizure.<br /></br>3. Fever source: 39.8 °C + erythematous, bulging TM → acute otitis media (AOM).<br /></br>4. Negatives: normal electrolytes, supple neck (rules out meningitis), no focal neuro findings (rules out intracranial process).  </p></br><p>Metacognitive approach:<br /></br>When a child in the febrile-seizure age range seizes, first decide if it is simple vs. complex. If simple, look for a treatable fever source and manage that; further neuro work-up is unnecessary.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Step 1: Classify the seizure.<br /></br>• Simple febrile = generalized, &lt; 15 min, single in 24 h, return to baseline, no focal signs.<br /></br>This vignette meets all criteria.</p></br><p>Step 2: Identify fever focus.<br /></br>• Otoscopic exam pinpoints AOM.</p></br><p>Step 3: Management.<br /></br>• Treat infection + antipyresis, educate parents. No imaging, antiepileptics, or LP needed.</p></br><p>Take-home message: A simple febrile seizure requires no neurologic work-up; manage the underlying febrile illness.</p></br><p>Documentation pearls: type of seizure, duration, post-ictal return to baseline, physical exam (normal neuro, TM findings), plan for AOM (drug/dose) and parental counseling on febrile seizures.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>Febrile seizure: rapid temperature rise triggers widespread neuronal depolarization in immature brain. Simple type has excellent prognosis—risk of epilepsy only slightly above baseline.</p></br><p>Acute otitis media: viral URI → eustachian tube dysfunction → bacterial superinfection (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis).</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics<br /></br>• First-line AOM: High-dose amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided bid for 5–10 days.<br /></br>• Add antipyretic (acetaminophen/ibuprofen).<br /></br>• No need for CT, MRI, EEG, LP, or long-term anticonvulsants.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Mnemonic / Memory Aid<br /></br>“FAST” for Simple Febrile Seizure:<br /></br>F = Fever focus (treat it)<br /></br>A = Age 6mo-5yr<br /></br>S = Single, Short, generalized Seizure<br /></br>T = Totally back to baseline → no Testing</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Options Are Wrong<br /></br>A. CT scan of the head<br /></br>• Indicated if focal deficits, signs of ↑ICP, trauma, or complex seizure. None present.</p></br><p>C. Mastoidectomy<br /></br>• Surgical treatment for chronic or complicated mastoiditis, not uncomplicated AOM.</p></br><p>D. Intravenous phenobarbital therapy<br /></br>• Reserved for status epilepticus or recurrent seizures unresponsive to benzodiazepines; simple febrile seizures stop spontaneously and do not require prophylaxis.</p></br><p>E. Lumbar puncture<br /></br>• Consider in infants &lt; 6 months, meningeal signs, prolonged altered mental status, or incomplete immunizations. This child is &gt; 6 months, has supple neck, and is fully alert.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Bottom Line<br /></br>A simple febrile seizure in a 2-year-old warrants treatment of the underlying infection—here, acute otitis media—with oral antibiotics; extensive neurologic evaluation or anticonvulsants are unnecessary.</p>
194. A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by police after being found somnolent in an alley. On arrival, he is agitated and confused. When staff approach him, he shouts and tries to get off the stretcher. He appears disheveled. He is malodorous and diaphoretic. He rambles unintelligibly on questioning and persistently picks at his clothes. His pulse is 110/min, and blood pressure is 160/90 mm Hg. Pupils are equal and reactive. He has a mild tremor of both hands. Urine toxicology screening is negative. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Alcohol intoxication</br>b. Alcohol withdrawal</br>c. Cocaine intoxication</br>d. Cocaine withdrawal</br>e. Opioid intoxication</br>f. Opioid withdrawal</br></br></br><p>Correct answer: B. Alcohol withdrawal</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>VIGNETTE ANALYSIS<br /></br>• “Found somnolent in an alley … disheveled, malodorous” – classic set-up for heavy, chronically homeless alcohol use.<br /></br>• “Agitated and confused … shouts … tries to get off the stretcher” – acute delirium.<br /></br>• “Persistently picks at his clothes” – tactile/visual hallucinations (“alcoholic hallucinosis,” often formication).<br /></br>• Autonomic hyperactivity: pulse 110/min, BP 160/90, diaphoretic.<br /></br>• “Mild tremor of both hands.”<br /></br>• Urine tox negative – routine ED panel detects cocaine, amphetamines, opioids, PCP, etc., but NOT ethanol. A negative screen therefore does not exclude alcohol involvement and helps rule out stimulant intoxication.</p></br><p>Pattern: A patient with evidence of chronic alcohol use who is agitated, tremulous, tachycardic, diaphoretic, and hallucinating = alcohol-withdrawal spectrum. This vignette is already beyond simple “the shakes” and is progressing toward delirium tremens (DTs).</p></br><p>Metacognitive tip for future questions<br /></br>1. Ask “intoxication or withdrawal?”  ➜ Autonomic up-regulation (tachycardia, HTN, tremor, diaphoresis) almost always = withdrawal from a CNS depressant (alcohol, benzos, barbs).<br /></br>2. Look for “formication” or picking at clothes – think stimulant intox (cocaine/amphetamines) or DTs. Combine with vital signs and tox screen to decide.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>CLINICAL CRITICAL REASONING<br /></br>Step 1  Identify chronic depressant use clue (homeless, malodorous).<br /></br>Step 2  Match physiologic state: sympathetic surge + tremor + hallucinations = withdrawal, not intoxication.<br /></br>Step 3  Rule out other causes with two key negatives: (a) pupils are normal (not stimulant or opioid intox), (b) tox screen negative for stimulants/opiates.<br /></br>Core takeaway: Autonomic hyperactivity + tremor + hallucinations in a chronic drinker = alcohol withdrawal; must treat early with benzodiazepines to prevent seizures/DTs and document CIWA score, vitals, thiamine, glucose.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>PATHOPHYSIOLOGY REVIEW<br /></br>Chronic alcohol ⟹ ↑NMDA (glutamate) receptors and ↓GABA activity to compensate for ongoing inhibition. Abrupt cessation removes inhibitory tone, producing unopposed CNS excitation: tremor (6-8 h), autonomic instability &amp; hallucinations (12-48 h), seizures (12-48 h), delirium tremens (48-96 h).</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>TREATMENT &amp; DIAGNOSTIC PEARLS<br /></br>1. First-line: Benzodiazepines that undergo hepatic metabolism:<br /></br>   • Diazepam or chlordiazepoxide (long-acting) if liver OK.<br /></br>   • Lorazepam or oxazepam if liver disease suspected (“LOT” – liver-friendly).<br /></br>2. Give thiamine IV before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.<br /></br>3. Supportive care: fluids, electrolytes (Mg, K, PO₄ correction), frequent vitals, seizure precautions.<br /></br>4. Labs: BMP (check electrolytes), LFTs, serum ethanol level, Mg (low Mg precipitates seizures).<br /></br>Documentation must include CIWA-Ar score and response to benzodiazepines.</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>MEMORIZATION TIPS<br /></br>“RULE of 6-12-48-96”<br /></br>• 6 h – Tremor, anxiety.<br /></br>• 12-24 h – Hallucinations.<br /></br>• 24-48 h – Seizures.<br /></br>• 48-96 h – Delirium Tremens (DTs).<br /></br>Mnemonic: “THe SHAKES Have Seized The Drunk.”</p></br><p>––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––––</br>WHY THE OTHER CHOICES ARE WRONG<br /></br>A. Alcohol intoxication – Presents with slurred speech, ataxia, nystagmus, hypotension or normal vitals; no tremor or autonomic overdrive.<br /></br>C. Cocaine intoxication – Would show mydriasis, severe HTN, chest pain, positive urine cocaine metabolite; tox screen here is negative.<br /></br>D. Cocaine withdrawal – Classic “crash”: fatigue, hypersomnolence, depression, hyperphagia, NOT agitation/tremor.<br /></br>E. Opioid intoxication – Triad of miosis, respiratory depression, sedation; vitals often bradycardic/hypotensive.<br /></br>F. Opioid withdrawal – Mydriasis, yawning, lacrimation, diarrhea, piloerection, muscle aches; rarely hallucinations or severe HTN.</p></br><p>Bottom line: Sympathetic surge + tremor + hallucinations in a chronically drinking patient = Alcohol withdrawal.</p>
195. A 16-year-old boy comes to the physician for a routine follow-up examination. He has a 10-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus. During the past 9 months, he has not been compliant with his treatment; he has been missing insulin injections and has not been monitoring his blood glucose concentrations. He has been admitted to the hospital four times during the past 3 years for diabetic ketoacidosis; his hemoglobin A1c during each hospital stay was greater than 10 percent. His pulse is 68/min, and blood pressure is 122/85 mm Hg. Examination shows lipoatrophy at the abdominal injection sites. Funduscopic examination shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?	Correct answer: B</br></br>a. Measurement of serum fructosamine concentration</br>b. Measurement of urine microalbumin concentration</br>c. Liver function tests</br>d. Thyroid function tests</br>e. ECG</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>B. Measurement of urine micro-albumin concentration</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Key facts:</br>  – 16-y/o boy, type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) for 10 years<br /></br>  – Repeated episodes of ketoacidosis and HbA1c &gt; 10% → clearly poorly controlled<br /></br>  – No retinopathy on funduscopy<br /></br>  – Vital signs normal; PE notable only for lipoatrophy at injection sites  </p></br><p>• What the examiner is really asking: “What evidence-based screening test is now due in a teenager who has had T1DM for ≥ 5 years?”</p></br><p>• Pertinent positives/negatives:</br>  – Duration of T1DM ≥ 5 years + age ≥ 10 years → triggers nephropathy screening</br>  – Normal funduscopy means retinopathy screening is under way but not the priority in the stem</br>  – No other symptoms suggesting thyroid disease, liver disease, or cardiac disease</p></br><p>• Metacognitive cue: When you see a T1DM patient with ≥ 5 years of disease, immediately think “micro-albumin test and dilated eye exam” as the two highest-yield screenings.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Chronic complications of diabetes include nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, macrovascular disease.</br>2. Evidence-based guidelines (ADA, ISPAD):</br>   • T1DM: Start urine micro-albumin (spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio) 5 years after diagnosis once patient is ≥ 10 years old, then annually.<br /></br>   • T2DM: Start at diagnosis (because disease duration is unknown).</br>3. This patient meets both duration (10 years) and age (16 years) criteria → nephropathy screening is overdue → order urine micro-albumin test.</p></br><p>Take-home message: In T1DM, micro-albumin screening starts 5 years after diagnosis and allows early ACE-I/ARB therapy to slow progression to diabetic nephropathy.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: “Urine ACR ordered today for annual diabetic nephropathy screening; plan to repeat annually.”</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Chronic hyperglycemia → non-enzymatic glycosylation of glomerular basement membrane (GBM) → ↑ GBM permeability → micro-albuminuria (30–300 mg/day).<br /></br>• Without intervention → mesangial expansion, nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules) → overt proteinuria, ↓ GFR, ESRD.</p></br><p>Risk factors: poor glycemic control, hypertension, genetic predisposition.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests</br>• Screening test: Spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR) or timed 24-h collection.<br /></br>• If micro-albuminuria present:<br /></br>  – Optimize glucose and BP control<br /></br>  – Start ACE inhibitor (e.g., lisinopril) or ARB (e.g., losartan) regardless of BP<br /></br>  – Repeat ACR in 3–6 months</p></br><p>No additional imaging is required unless creatinine rises or ACR persists despite therapy.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“5-10-Kidney-Eye” rule for T1DM:</br>• 5 years after dx, when patient is ≥ 10 years old → Screen Kidney (micro-albumin) and Eye (dilated exam) every year.</p></br><p>Mnemonic: “MAKE” complications</br>M – Micro-Albumin<br /></br>A – ACE-I if positive<br /></br>K – Kidney protection<br /></br>E – Eye exam same schedule</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>A. Serum fructosamine – Reflects average glucose over the preceding 2–3 weeks; useful if HbA1c is unreliable (hemoglobinopathies, recent transfusion). The problem here is screening for chronic complications, not short-term glycemic assessment.</p></br><p>C. Liver function tests – Indicated before starting statins or when suspecting NAFLD. Not routinely required solely because a teen has T1DM.</p></br><p>D. Thyroid function tests – Autoimmune thyroid disease is common in T1DM. Guidelines: check TSH at diagnosis and then every 1–2 years, or sooner if symptomatic. While reasonable, this boy is overdue for nephropathy screening, which carries higher morbidity if missed; micro-albumin testing is the “most appropriate next step.”</p></br><p>E. ECG – Used to evaluate chest pain, syncope, or baseline status in adults with long-standing diabetes (&gt;40 y or multiple risk factors). Not indicated in an asymptomatic 16-year-old.</p></br><p>──────────────────────</br>Bottom Line</br>For any patient with type 1 diabetes who is at least 10 years old and has had the disease for ≥ 5 years, the single most important screening test is annual measurement of urine micro-albumin to detect early diabetic nephropathy.</p>
196. A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department 20 minutes after being stung by a bee on his right arm at summer camp. The site immediately became red, swollen, and hot, and an itchy rash developed and quickly spread to his chest. He has not had shortness of breath, rapid breathing, wheezing, or swelling of his other extremities. On arrival, he appears slightly anxious but is not in acute distress. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 98/min, respirations are 28/min, and blood pressure is 90/65 mm Hg. Examination shows moist mucous membranes. A 3-cm area of erythematous induration surrounds the site of the sting, and the right upper arm is diffusely edematous. There is an urticarial rash on the right upper arm and chest. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Which of the following measures is most likely to decrease this patient's risk for similar reactions in the future?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Use of permethrin spray on clothes before outdoor activity</br>b. Use of topical calamine lotion after a sting</br>c. Use of diphenhydramine before outdoor activity</br>d. Intravenous immune globulin therapy monthly</br>e. Venom immunotherapy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>E. Venom immunotherapy (allergen-specific desensitization to Hymenoptera venom)</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Vignette Analysis</br>• Trigger: Bee sting → almost immediate generalized urticaria plus borderline hypotension (90/65 mm Hg for a 5-year-old).<br /></br>  – These are systemic findings, not just a large local reaction.<br /></br>• Pertinent positives: rapid onset itching, diffuse urticaria beyond the sting site, borderline low BP, mild tachypnea/anxiety.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: no wheeze or airway edema, but you do not need full cardiorespiratory collapse to label a reaction “systemic.”<br /></br>Pattern: IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity (anaphylaxis spectrum) to Hymenoptera venom.<br /></br>Test-taking mindset: Whenever a stem shows systemic reaction to a bee/wasp/yellow-jacket sting, the long-term risk-reduction answer is venom immunotherapy (VIT), not antihistamines or repellents.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>1. Diagnose the reaction: systemic allergic reaction to venom.<br /></br>2. Recognize future risk: 30–60 % of children with systemic reactions will have a similar or worse reaction with the next sting.<br /></br>3. Intervention that alters immune response: Only allergen-specific immunotherapy produces long-term immune tolerance. VIT cuts future severe reaction risk to &lt;5 %.<br /></br>Core takeaway: For patients with systemic (not merely large local) reactions to Hymenoptera stings, venom immunotherapy is the definitive preventive therapy. Document need for referral to an allergy specialist and for an epinephrine autoinjector.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Bee/wasp venom proteins → cross-link pre-existing venom-specific IgE on mast cells → degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes → urticaria, vasodilation, bronchospasm, hypotension.<br /></br>• Venom immunotherapy repeatedly exposes the immune system to standardized small doses of purified venom. Results:<br /></br>  – ↑ venom-specific IgG4 “blocking antibodies”<br /></br>  – ↓ IgE production, mast-cell reactivity<br /></br>  – Cytokine shift from Th2 to regulatory/Th1 phenotype  </p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Notes</br>Acute episode (not asked but test-relevant): intramuscular epinephrine, antihistamine, steroids, airway support.<br /></br>Long-term:<br /></br>1. Venom skin testing or serum-specific IgE to confirm allergen (done by allergist).<br /></br>2. Venom immunotherapy course (build-up over weeks, maintenance 3–5 years).<br /></br>3. Carry 2 epinephrine autoinjectors at all times and educate on use.<br /></br>4. Basic avoidance (wear shoes outside, no open beverages, etc.)—helpful but does not change immune risk like VIT.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Memorization Tips</br>“V-I-T keeps you alive after a sTing.”<br /></br>• V = Venom, I = Immuno, T = Therapy.<br /></br>Think “Bee vaccine” — only option that retrains the immune system.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Why the Other Choices Are Wrong</br>a. Permethrin spray on clothes<br /></br>   • Repels ticks and some insects; bees are not deterred, and it does nothing for IgE antibodies already formed.</p></br><p>b. Topical calamine lotion after a sting<br /></br>   • Soothes local itching; no effect on systemic hypersensitivity or future stings.</p></br><p>c. Diphenhydramine before outdoor activity<br /></br>   • May blunt mild urticaria but cannot prevent mast-cell degranulation or anaphylaxis; not recommended as primary prophylaxis.</p></br><p>d. Monthly intravenous immune globulin (IVIG)<br /></br>   • Used for immunodeficiencies or some autoimmune diseases, not allergic desensitization; no evidence it reduces Hymenoptera anaphylaxis.</p></br><p>────────────────────────────────────────</br>Key Exam Pearl</br>Systemic (anaphylactic) reaction to bee/wasp sting → two prescriptions on discharge:</br>1. Epinephrine autoinjector for emergencies.<br /></br>2. Referral for venom immunotherapy to eliminate or drastically reduce future anaphylaxis risk.</p>
197. A 7-year-old girl is brought to the physician 3 days after her mother noticed a rash on her face. The patient has no other symptoms and has no history of similar skin lesions or recent trauma to the face. She feels well. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). A photograph of the rash is shown. Examination shows no other abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?	Correct answer: C</br></br>a. Topical acyclovir</br>b. Topical clotrimazole</br>c. Topical mupirocin</br>d. Oral acyclovir</br>e. Oral ketoconazole</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>c. Topical mupirocin  </p></br><p>Vignette Analysis<br /></br>• Age &amp; setting: 7-year-old, preschool/early school age = classic demographic for impetigo.<br /></br>• Key descriptor: “rash on her face … no other symptoms … feels well.” Facial rash in a child without fever should immediately trigger the impetigo schema.<br /></br>• Photo (not displayed to you but implied): USMLE/NBME almost always shows honey-colored, adherent crusts around the nares and perioral area.<br /></br>• Pertinent negatives: No pain, pruritus, systemic complaints, or vesicles → rules against herpes (painful clustered vesicles), eczema herpeticum, fungal infection, or cellulitis.<br /></br>How to approach similar questions:<br /></br>1. Ask “Is this rash infectious, inflammatory, or allergic?”<br /></br>2. Note location (face), color/texture (golden crust), absence of systemic illness → pattern recognition = non-bullous impetigo.<br /></br>3. Decide severity (localized vs extensive/systemic) → therapy choice flows directly from that branch point.</p></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning<br /></br>Core takeaway: Localized, non-bullous impetigo in otherwise healthy children is treated first-line with a topical antistaphylococcal/antistreptococcal agent—mupirocin or retapamulin. If lesions are numerous, bullous, or associated with fever, switch to an oral β-lactam (cephalexin, dicloxacillin, or amoxicillin-clavulanate).  </p></br><p>What would go in the note: “Localized non-bullous impetigo on face. No systemic symptoms. Plan: topical mupirocin to affected areas tid × 5 days; return if lesions worsen or spread.”</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review<br /></br>• Etiology: Superficial infection of epidermis by Staphylococcus aureus (most common) or Streptococcus pyogenes.<br /></br>• Lesion evolution: Papule → vesicle → rupture → exudate dries to honey-colored crust.<br /></br>• Lymphatics &amp; dermis spared, so minimal redness, warmth, or pain compared with cellulitis.  </p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Tests<br /></br>• No labs or imaging needed when classic appearance is present.<br /></br>• Localized disease: topical mupirocin 2% ointment or retapamulin 1% ointment tid for 5 days.<br /></br>• Extensive disease/bullous form: Oral agents covering MSSA &amp; GAS—cephalexin, dicloxacillin, or amoxicillin-clavulanate. Reserve clindamycin or TMP-SMX if MRSA risk.<br /></br>• Counsel on hygiene (hand-washing, keep nails short, avoid sharing towels).</p></br><p>Memorization Tips<br /></br>“Honey on the face? Get the BEE-st (Bactroban)!”<br /></br>Honey = non-bullous impetigo; Bee-st → Bactroban (brand name for mupirocin).</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis<br /></br>a. Topical acyclovir<br /></br>• Indication: Herpes labialis or HSV-1 vesicles (painful clusters on erythematous base). Impetigo lesions are crusted, not grouped vesicles, and are painless.  </p></br><p>b. Topical clotrimazole<br /></br>• Antifungal for tinea (annular scaly plaques) or Candida (satellite lesions). Impetigo is bacterial.  </p></br><p>d. Oral acyclovir<br /></br>• Systemic therapy for primary gingivostomatitis, eczema herpeticum, or VZV. Not indicated here; would overtreat and miss bacterial etiology.  </p></br><p>e. Oral ketoconazole<br /></br>• Systemic azole with significant hepatotoxicity; no role in superficial fungal infections in children and certainly not for bacterial impetigo. (Oral ketoconazole is essentially obsolete in the U.S.)  </p></br><p>Common pitfall: confusing honey-colored crusts (impetigo) with painful grouped vesicles (HSV) or scaling (tinea). Remember: golden crust = staph/strep → mupirocin if localized, oral β-lactam if widespread.</p>
198. Four weeks after a flu-like illness, a 40-year-old man develops progressive dyspnea on exertion. Examination shows jugular venous distention, bilateral pulmonary crackles, a diffuse apical impulse, an apical S3, and bilateral pedal edema. Which of the following is the most likely cause?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Dilated cardiomyopathy</br>b. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy</br>c. Restrictive cardiomyopathy</br>d. Endocardial fibroelastosis</br>e. Pericardial tamponade</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>a. Dilated cardiomyopathy  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><p>Key clues<br /></br>• “Four weeks after a flu-like illness” – classic set-up for post-viral myocarditis that can evolve into dilated (congestive) cardiomyopathy.<br /></br>• “Progressive dyspnea on exertion, JVD, bibasilar crackles, bilateral pedal edema” – global, low-output (systolic) heart-failure picture.<br /></br>• “Diffuse apical impulse” – the point of maximal impulse is displaced and enlarged, typical when the LV is dilated.<br /></br>• “Apical S3” – signifies increased end-systolic volume/ventricular dilation and is almost pathognomonic for systolic dysfunction.  </p></br><p>Approach tip: When a question couples (1) recent viral illness + (2) new systolic-HF findings, think “viral myocarditis ➜ dilated cardiomyopathy.”  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify the syndrome: All chambers are volume-overloaded → findings of both left- and right-sided HF.  </li></br><li>Time course: Several weeks after a viral prodrome fits the known latency between myocarditis and overt DCM.  </li></br><li>Physical exam: S3 and displaced PMI point away from purely diastolic problems (restrictive, hypertrophic) or pericardial disease.  </li></br><li>Rule out distractors with classic “must-see” findings they would produce (see Incorrect Answer Analysis).  </li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home concept: Post-viral myocarditis is the most common acquired cause of dilated cardiomyopathy in the United States; it produces global systolic dysfunction with an S3 and volume-overload signs.  </p></br><p>Documentation must-haves (real patient): Severity of HF symptoms (NYHA class), volume status, vitals, rhythm, echo findings, and initiation of guideline-directed medical therapy (e.g., ACE inhibitor, β-blocker, loop diuretic).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Etiology: Coxsackie B, influenza, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, HIV, Chagas, toxins (alcohol, anthracyclines), pregnancy.<br /></br>• Mechanism: Viral infection triggers lymphocytic myocarditis → myocyte necrosis → eccentric hypertrophy with chamber dilation → ↓ ejection fraction.<br /></br>• Hemodynamics: ↑ LVEDV, ↓ contractility, ↓ forward stroke volume → neurohormonal activation (RAAS, SNS) → fluid retention → biventricular HF.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Diagnostics / Treatment (high-yield points)  </p></br><p>Diagnostics<br /></br>• Echocardiogram: Four-chamber dilation, EF &lt; 40%, functional MR/TR possible.<br /></br>• CXR: Cardiomegaly, pulmonary congestion.<br /></br>• Endomyocardial biopsy rarely needed unless atypical.  </p></br><p>First-line Management<br /></br>1. Afterload reduction: ACE inhibitor (lisinopril), ARB (losartan), or ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan).<br /></br>2. Neurohormonal blockade: Evidence-based β-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol) once euvolemic.<br /></br>3. Volume control: Loop diuretic (furosemide).<br /></br>4. Mortality benefit add-ons: Spironolactone or eplerenone, SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin), hydralazine-nitrate (if Black or ACE-intolerant).<br /></br>5. Device therapy: ICD, CRT, LVAD, transplant if refractory.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“DCM = Dilated After Colds (Myocarditis)”<br /></br>• D – Dilated ventricles<br /></br>• A – Afterload ↑ (RAAS)<br /></br>• C – Coxsackie / Cold viruses<br /></br>• M – Myocarditis leads to systolic failure  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a balloon (ventricle) that’s been over-inflated after you blew a virus into it; it’s floppy (S3) and leaks fluid everywhere (edema, crackles, JVD).  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>b. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy<br /></br>• Usually genetic; presents with harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur, syncope, sudden death in young athlete, NO displaced PMI or S3. Small, hyperdynamic LV—not dilated.  </p></br><p>c. Restrictive cardiomyopathy<br /></br>• Amyloid, sarcoid, hemochromatosis, radiation. Stiff ventricles → diastolic HF; S4 more common than S3, PMI normal, no preceding viral illness. Echo shows normal-sized ventricles with biatrial enlargement.  </p></br><p>d. Endocardial fibroelastosis<br /></br>• Rare cause of HF in infants/children, not adults. Results from thick fibroelastic tissue in LV endocardium; vignette age is wrong.  </p></br><p>e. Pericardial tamponade<br /></br>• Triad: hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD + pulsus paradoxus, clear lungs (no crackles), no S3, PMI not palpable. Tamponade impairs diastolic filling but does not produce volume-overload lung findings.  </p></br><p>Common trap: Seeing “JVD” and jumping to tamponade—always look for hypotension, muffled sounds, and clean lungs to seal that diagnosis.  </p></br><p>───────────────────<br /></br>Core takeaway: A middle-aged adult who develops global systolic heart-failure signs a few weeks after a viral illness most likely has post-viral dilated cardiomyopathy.</p>
199. A 52-year-old woman comes to the physician for a routine health maintenance examination. One year ago, she was started on daily hormone replacement therapy (estradiol and medroxyprogesterone) to treat severe vasomotor symptoms. But she has been taking the medroxyprogesterone intermittently because of severe depression and mood changes when taking it. Menses had stopped for the first 6 months on the original regimen, but since she changed her regimen, she has had vaginal spotting two to three times monthly. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: E</br></br>a. Discontinue medroxyprogesterone therapy only</br>b. Increase the dosage of estradiol</br>c. Add an antidepressant to the medication regimen</br>d. Endometrial ablation</br>e. Endometrial biopsy</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer<br /></br>E. Endometrial biopsy  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Vignette Analysis  </p></br><ol></br><li></br><p>Key phrases &amp; clues<br /></br>   • 52-year-old woman (post-menopausal age).<br /></br>   • Had 6 months of amenorrhea on continuous combined HRT → confirms menopause.<br /></br>   • Began taking medroxyprogesterone (the progestin) “intermittently” → periods of unopposed estrogen.<br /></br>   • New-onset “vaginal spotting two to three times monthly.”<br /></br>   • Post-menopausal bleeding (any bleeding ≥12 months after the final menstrual period) is a red-flag for endometrial hyperplasia/cancer until proven otherwise.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent positives<br /></br>   • Estrogen exposure without adequate progestin.<br /></br>   • Post-menopausal bleeding.</p></br></li></br><li></br><p>Pertinent negatives<br /></br>   • No mention of trauma, infection, or anticoagulation that could otherwise explain bleeding.</p></br></li></br></ol></br><p>Metacognitive pearl: The NBME loves the rule “Post-menopausal bleeding → evaluate the endometrium.” Even if you see a tempting management fix (eg, “just add the progestin back”), you must FIRST rule out cancer.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Clinical Critical Reasoning (Step-wise)  </p></br><ol></br><li>Identify the clinical problem: Post-menopausal bleeding.  </li></br><li>Recognize the major risk factor in this vignette: intermittent progestin → unopposed estrogen → possible endometrial hyperplasia/malignancy.  </li></br><li>Apply the algorithm:<br /></br>   • Evaluate the endometrium with biopsy (or TVUS to measure stripe).<br /></br>   • Biopsy is preferred when risk is high (unopposed estrogen, age &gt;45, or prolonged bleeding).  </li></br><li>Therefore, endometrial biopsy is the correct next step.</li></br></ol></br><p>Take-home message: Never “tweak” hormone therapy before excluding endometrial pathology in a post-menopausal woman with bleeding.  </p></br><p>What would be documented in the chart: amount/duration of bleeding, HRT regimen, risk factors for estrogen exposure, counseling regarding biopsy, consent obtained.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Pathophysiology Review  </p></br><p>• Estradiol stimulates endometrial proliferation.<br /></br>• Medroxyprogesterone (progestin) stabilizes &amp; facilitates orderly shedding, preventing uncontrolled growth.<br /></br>• With intermittent/absent progestin, the endometrium continues to proliferate → hyperplasia → potential adenocarcinoma → manifests as post-menopausal bleeding.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Treatment &amp; Diagnostic Test Review  </p></br><p>Diagnostic:<br /></br>• Endometrial biopsy (office pipelle) is ≥99 % sensitive for endometrial cancer.<br /></br>• Alternative: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) to measure endometrial stripe; if ≥4 mm or persistent symptoms, proceed to biopsy. Here, biopsy is warranted immediately due to high-risk history.  </p></br><p>Management after biopsy:<br /></br>• Hyperplasia without atypia → continuous progestin or LNG-IUD.<br /></br>• Atypical hyperplasia or carcinoma → hysterectomy ± BSO depending on stage.<br /></br>• Resume combined continuous HRT only after pathology is benign.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Memorization Tips  </p></br><p>“BLEED = Biopsy”<br /></br>B – Bleeding<br /></br>L – Late (&gt;12 mo after LMP)<br /></br>E – Estrogen exposure (unopposed)<br /></br>E – Endometrial cancer risk ↑<br /></br>D – Do a biopsy  </p></br><p>Visualization: Picture a red STOP sign whenever you see post-menopausal bleeding—first STOP and sample the endometrium before doing anything else.  </p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Incorrect Answer Analysis  </p></br><p>A. Discontinue medroxyprogesterone only<br /></br>• Would leave her on lone estrogen, increasing risk even more. Does not address need to rule out malignancy.</p></br><p>B. Increase the dosage of estradiol<br /></br>• Worsens unopposed estrogen effect; no role in evaluating bleeding.</p></br><p>C. Add an antidepressant<br /></br>• Could treat mood symptoms but does nothing for cancer risk or bleeding evaluation.</p></br><p>D. Endometrial ablation<br /></br>• A definitive treatment for benign AUB but contraindicated before malignancy is excluded. Must biopsy first.</p></br><p>─────────────────────────────────────<br /></br>Core concept: Post-menopausal bleeding + unopposed estrogen = mandatory endometrial sampling (biopsy).</p>
200. A 77-year-old man is brought to the physician by his family because of a 10-week history of decreased appetite that has resulted in a 10.4-kg (23-lb) weight loss. During this time, he also has had a persistent, nonproductive cough, intermittent nausea, and constipation. He reports that he has been sleeping 10 hours daily but does not feel rested. His family states that he had an upper respiratory tract infection prior to the weight loss and that he never "bounced back" from the infection. He had smoked one to two packs of cigarettes daily for 60 years but has recently cut back to one to two cigarettes daily because he no longer enjoys the taste. He appears tired and cachectic. He is 175 cm (5 ft 9 in) tall and weighs 58 kg (129 lb); BMI is 19 kg/m2. His temperature is 36°C (96.8°F), pulse is 86/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. The lungs are clear to auscultation. Abdominal examination shows no tenderness and mild distention. There is trace pedal edema. On mental status examination, he has a sad mood and constricted affect. His speech is slow. He is oriented to person, place, and time. He cannot perform serial sevens. An x-ray of the chest shows no abnormalities. An x-ray of the abdomen shows copious stool. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?	Correct answer: A</br></br>a. Assessment for suicide risk</br>b. Neuropsychological testing</br>c. Bronchoscopy</br>d. Polysomnography</br>e. Placement of a feeding tube</br></br></br><p>Correct Answer</br>Assessment for suicide risk</p></br><p>Vignette Analysis</br>1. Key clues</br>   • Age 77 with 10-week history of ↓ appetite, 23-lb weight loss – physiologic change pointing to medical or psychiatric illness<br /></br>   • Persistent non-productive cough, but CXR is normal → lung cancer less likely now<br /></br>   • Hypersomnia (10 h/d but not rested), anhedonia (cigarettes “no longer enjoyable”), low energy, psychomotor slowing (slow speech), poor concentration (fails serial 7s), sad mood and constricted affect → classic depressive symptoms<br /></br>   • No fever, lungs clear, abdominal film shows stool = functional constipation from inactivity/poor intake<br /></br>   • Cachexia and trace edema represent nutritional neglect, commonly seen in depression (“failure to thrive” in elderly)  </p></br><p>Together these fulfill DSM-5 criteria for a major depressive episode (≥2 wk, ≥5 SIGECAPS symptoms).</p></br><ol></br><li>Cognitive approach</br>   • First decide: organic vs psychiatric? Normal vitals, labs/imaging largely unremarkable; cluster fits SIGECAPS → psychiatric.<br /></br>   • Once depression suspected, ALWAYS ask: “Could this patient harm himself?” Suicide risk assessment is mandatory.</li></br></ol></br><p>Clinical Critical Reasoning</br>Step-wise thought process:</br>1. Recognize major depressive disorder (MDD) in an elderly patient.</br>2. In any new diagnosis or suspicion of MDD, the immediate next step is a safety assessment—determine presence of suicidal ideation, plan, intent, or previous attempts. </br>3. Management (therapy, SSRI, etc.) comes after ensuring safety.</p></br><p>Documentation pearl: If this were a real encounter, the chart must explicitly record responses to suicide-risk questions (ideation, plan, means, intent) and the resulting disposition.</p></br><p>Pathophysiology Review</br>• Late-life depression often follows medical stressors (e.g., URTI) and presents with somatic complaints, weight loss, psychomotor slowing (“depressive pseudodementia”).<br /></br>• Suicide rates are highest in elderly men; firearms and hanging are common methods.</p></br><p>Treatment &amp; Diagnostics Review</br>After suicide risk is addressed:</br>1. Pharmacotherapy: start with an SSRI (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram).<br /></br>2. Psychotherapy: CBT or interpersonal therapy.<br /></br>3. Treat assoc. constipation (bulk fiber, stool softeners).<br /></br>4. Nutrition consult if weight loss severe, but tube feeding only if oral intake impossible and depression refractory.</p></br><p>Memorization Tips</br>SIGECAPS = Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicide.<br /></br>“First S always stands for Suicide assessment.”</p></br><p>Incorrect Answer Analysis</br>b. Neuropsychological testing – Used for dementia; depressive “pseudodementia” improves with mood treatment. Do safety check first; detailed testing can follow if cognition does not improve.</p></br><p>c. Bronchoscopy – Lung cancer work-up starts with CXR → CT. Normal X-ray in chronic smoker lowers pre-test probability; depression explains symptoms better.</p></br><p>d. Polysomnography – Not indicated; patient is underweight, not snoring, and sleepiness is part of depression, not obstructive sleep apnea.</p></br><p>e. Placement of a feeding tube – Invasive, reserved for inability to swallow or severe malnutrition after failing less invasive measures. Root psychiatric cause should be addressed first; may resolve anorexia.</p></br><p>Take-home Message</br>Whenever depression is suspected, especially in an elderly male, the very next action is to assess suicide risk before proceeding with further diagnostics or treatments.</p>
